documents.text:
Discrete mathematics, logic and reasoning
(COMP40018)
Francesco Leofante
Many thanks to Dalal Alrajeh for some of the original material.
– 1 –
Course content
The logic part of the module covers the following topics:
1. Propositional logic:
– Syntax (language) and semantics (meaning)
– English correspondence (translation into and out of logic)
– Arguments and validity
– Checking validity
* truth tables
* direct argument
* equivalences
* proof systems: natural deduction
– 2 –
Course content ctd.
2. Classical first-order (predicate) logic:
– Syntax and semantics
– English correspondence
– Function symbols and sorts
– Many-sorted logic
– Checking validity
* direct argument
* equivalences
* proof systems: natural deduction
Material for the above will taught across two terms:
– 3 –
PMT, tutorials and coursework
Tutorial sheets will be released weekly on Tuesdays and covered in the
tutorial and PMT sessions.
There will befeedback e-submissionsfor questions marked as*PMT*
in the tutorial sheet:

There will be abother coursework submission in Spring term, and an
exam in April/May: A two-part logic question.

Course material
Slides and exercises will be made available onScientia
(https://scientia.doc.ic.ac.uk/signin)
– 5 –
Reading list
The Logic Manual.
Volker Halbach. Oxford University Press, Oxford, United Kingdom.
2010.
Logic in Computer Science: Modelling and Reasoning about Systems.
Michael Huth and Mark Ryan. Cambridge University Press, New
York, NY, USA. 2004.
A Mathematical Introduction to Logic.
Herbert Enderton. 2nd ed. New York: Academic Press. 2001.
Contact details
Please post your questions on Ed to be answered by others, GTAs or
myself.
... and
you may reach me by email at f.leofante@imperial.ac.uk.
Introduction
What is logic?
Etymology Dictionary:
Logic, from Greek
logos: “reason”, “idea”, “word”,
logike: “(the) reasoning (art)”.
Oxford English Dictionary:
Logic, Science of “the forms of thinking in general, and more
especially of inference and of scientific method. Also [ ...]
symbolic techniques and mathematical methods to establish
truth-values inthe physicalsciences, in language, and inphilo-
sophical argument” (OED).
– 8 –
What is logic? ctd.
Logic is the “calculus of computing”: a mathematical foundation for
dealing with information, and reasoning about program behaviour.
In this course, we are concerned with using logic todescribe, specify,
verify, andreason about software.
(We are not concerned with low-level “logic gates” etc in computing
— that is for the Hardware course.)
Multiple different species of logic. E.g., logics for:
– Claims that can be true or false.
– Belief and knowledge.
– Ethics and law.
– Computer systems and how they behave over time.
– 9 –
What is logic? ctd.
A logic usually consists of:
–Syntax — a formal language (like a programming language) used
to express concepts.
–Semantics — provides meaning for the formal language.
–Proof theory— a purely syntactic way of obtaining or identifying
the valid statements of the language. It provides a way of arguing
in the formal language.
We will study two specific logics:
– Propositional logic.
– First-order logic (FOL), also called ‘predicate calculus’ and
‘predicate logic’.
These are foundational for any further logics you might later use. The
former can be seen as a less fine-grained version of the latter.
– 10 –
Why learn logic?
– It provides good training in correct reasoning, and accurate,
unambiguous description.
– Some of the more sophisticated real-world application areas need
and use logic. For example:
–Answer set programmingis a logic programming paradigm
used for building decision support systems, reconfiguring
railway safety, etc.
– TheSQL database language, model checkingand controller
synthesis are based on logic.
– Logic can shed light on “forms of thinking" — relevant to
human-centric, explainable AI, safe AI and AGI.
* Logic-based machine learningenables learning logical
theories that support scientific knowledge discover.
* Planning and schedulinguses logic to formalise a task and
compute solutions for it.

– 12 –
1. Propositional Logic
– 13 –
Main features
Consider the following example (the study of “if-tests”)
if count>0 and not found then
decrement count; look for next entry;
end if
– basic sentences (propositional atoms) — here: ‘count>0’,found
— are true or false depending on circumstances.
– connectives —and, or, not, etc. — used to build more and
more complex test sentences from the atoms.
– the final complex sentence evaluates to true or false.
Propositional logic is not very expressive. FOL (later) can say much
more, e.g., “every student has a tutor”.
– 14 –
Why do propositional logic?
– All logics are based on propositional logic in some form.
– In programming, we want to handle arbitrarily complicated
“if-tests” and study their general features.
* Evaluate complicated “if-tests”.
* Find outwhen two “if-test” mean the same, when one
implies another, whether an “if-test” (or “loop-test”) can
ever be made true or not.
– Propositional logic encapsulates an important class of
computational problems, and so comes in to algorithms,
complexity theory and SAT solving.
– 15 –
1.1 Syntax — The formal language
First, we need to fix a precise definition of the formal language, that
is thesyntax of propositional logic.
It hasthree ingredients:
1. Propositional atoms;
2. Boolean connectives;
3. Punctuation.
– 16 –
1st Ingredient: Propositional atoms
We are not concerned about which facts are being represented
(‘count>0’,found). So long as they can get atruth-value (true or
false), that’s enough. (Quotation marks are used to encapsulate a
single expression.)
So we fix a collection of algebraic symbols to stand for these
statements.
These symbols are calledpropositional atoms.Many people call them
propositional variablesor propositional lettersinstead. For short, we
will usually call thematoms.
They are like variablesx,y,z,...in maths. But because they are
Propositional, we usually use the lettersp, p′, p′′, ... p0, p1, p2, ...,
and alsop, q, r, s, .... (Avoid mixing conventions.)
– 17 –
2nd Ingredient: Boolean connectives
We are interested in the following Boolean connectives
(a.k.a. operator or operations):
•not: written as¬(or sometimes∼or−)
•and: written as∧(or sometimes &, and in old books, ‘·’)
•or: written as∨(in old books,+ or v)
•if-then, or implies. This is written as→(or sometimes⊃, but
not as⇒, which is used differently)
•if-and-only-if: written↔(but not as⇔or≡, which are used
differently)
•truth and falsity: written as⊤,⊥
We’ll discuss themeaning of these connectives later.
– 18 –
Examples
Our test count>0 and not found would be expressed as
‘count>0’ ∧ ¬found, or betterp∧¬q.
Boolean connectives and, or, implies, if-and-only-if take two
arguments and are written in infix form:
p∧q, p∨q, p→q, p↔q.
Negation (not) takes one argument, written to the right of it:
e.g.,¬p, ¬q.
Truth and falsity,⊤,⊥, take no arguments.
They are logicalconstants (likeπ, e in maths).
You will have to learn these new symbols, so you can read logic
books. They are also quicker to write thanand, or, not , etc.
– 19 –
3rd Ingredient: Punctuation
Consider the followingif-test.
‘count>0’ ∧ ¬found ∨‘count>10’
We need brackets to disambiguate it.
This is like terms in arithmetic:1−2 + 3is ambiguous, we can read it
as (1−2) + 3or 1−(2 + 3), and the difference matters.
For example,p∧q∨r might be read as
•(p∧q)∨r,
•p∧(q∨r),
and the difference matters.
So we start by putting all brackets in, and then deleting those that
are not needed.
– 20 –
Propositional formulas
What we have calledif-tests are calledformulas by logicians. (Some
call themsentences, others call themwell-formed formulas,or wffs for
short.)
Informally, apropositional formulais a string of symbols made from
propositional atoms, Boolean connectives, and brackets, in the
appropriate way.
Definition 1.1 (Propositional formula)
– Any propositional atom (p,q,r , etc) is a propositional formula.
–⊤and⊥are formulas.
– Ifϕis a formula then so is(¬ϕ). Note the brackets!
– Ifϕ,ψare formulas then so are(ϕ∧ψ), (ϕ∨ψ), (ϕ→ψ), and (ϕ↔ψ).
– That’s it: nothing is a formula unless built by these rules.
The first two in the above are calledatomic formulas. Greek letters
(ϕ, ψ, ...) are often used as meta-variables, representing formulas.
They are not themselves expressions of the language.
– 21 –
Examples of formulas
The following are formulas:
–p
–(¬p)
–((¬p)∧⊤)
–(¬((¬s)∧⊥))
–((¬p)→(¬((¬q∨r)∧⊤)))
How about these?
–∧p q
–(¬⊥)∧(r→q)
–(p ∨q¬s)
–¬r)
– 22 –
Examples of formulas ctd.
Take the formula((¬p)→(¬((¬q∨r)∧⊤))).
You can see the brackets are a pain already. We need some
conventions to get rid of (some of) them.
What we’ll get are (strictly speaking)abbreviations of genuine
formulas. But most people seem to think of them as real formulas.
We can always omit the final, outermost brackets:
¬p, and (¬p)→(¬((¬p)∧⊤)) are unambiguous.
– 23 –
Binding conventions
To get rid of more brackets, weorder the Boolean connectives
according to decreasing binding strength:
(strongest)¬,∧,∨,→,↔(weakest)
This is like in arithmetic, where×is stronger than+. This means
that 2 + 3×4 is usually read as2 + (3×4), not as(2 + 3)×4.
So:
–p∨q∧r is read asp∨(q∧r), not as(p∨q)∧r.
–¬p∧q is read as(¬p)∧q, not as¬(p∧q).
How about the following?
–p∧¬q→r is read as
(p∧(¬q))→r, rather thanp∧(¬(q→r))
or p∧((¬q)→r).
But don’t take the conventions too far, e.g,p→¬q∨¬¬r∧s↔t
is a mess! USE BRACKETS if it helps readability, even if they
are not strictly needed.
– 24 –
Binding conventions
To get rid of more brackets, weorder the Boolean connectives
according to decreasing binding strength:
(strongest)¬,∧,∨,→,↔(weakest)
This is like in arithmetic, where×is stronger than+. This means
that 2 + 3×4 is usually read as2 + (3×4), not as(2 + 3)×4. So:
–p∨q∧r is read asp∨(q∧r), not as(p∨q)∧r.
–¬p∧q is read as(¬p)∧q, not as¬(p∧q).
How about the following?
–p∧¬q→r is read as
(p∧(¬q))→r, rather thanp∧(¬(q→r))
or p∧((¬q)→r).
But don’t take the conventions too far, e.g,p→¬q∨¬¬r∧s↔t
is a mess! USE BRACKETS if it helps readability, even if they
are not strictly needed.
– 24 –
Binding conventions
To get rid of more brackets, weorder the Boolean connectives
according to decreasing binding strength:
(strongest)¬,∧,∨,→,↔(weakest)
This is like in arithmetic, where×is stronger than+. This means
that 2 + 3×4 is usually read as2 + (3×4), not as(2 + 3)×4. So:
–p∨q∧r is read asp∨(q∧r), not as(p∨q)∧r.
–¬p∧q is read as(¬p)∧q, not as¬(p∧q).
How about the following?
–p∧¬q→r is read as(p∧(¬q))→r, rather thanp∧(¬(q→r))
or p∧((¬q)→r).
But don’t take the conventions too far, e.g,p→¬q∨¬¬r∧s↔t
is a mess! USE BRACKETS if it helps readability, even if they
are not strictly needed.
– 24 –
Repeated connectives
What aboutp→q→r? The binding conventions are no help now.
Should we read it asp→(q→r) or as (p→q)→r?
There probably is a convention here. But I would always put brackets
in such a formula. However,p∧q∧r andp∨q∨r are OK as they are.
As we will see, theirlogical meaningis the same, however we bracket
them. (p∧q)∧r and p∧(q∧r) are different formulas. But we will
see that they always have the same truth value in any situation: they
are logically equivalent.
So we don’t really care how we disambiguatep∧q∧r. (Usually, it is
(p∧q)∧r — from left, and we say in this case the connectives are
left-associative.)
Repeated negations, as with(¬(¬(¬p))), can create no ambiguity:
their brackets can always be removed, e.g., to¬¬¬p.
– 25 –
Special cases
Sometimes the binding conventions don’t work as we would like.
For example, if I saw
p→r∧q→r,
I’d probably read it as(p→r)∧(q→r).
How can I justify this?∧is stronger than→. So shouldn’t it be
p→(r∧q)→r?
Read according to a plausible convention about repeated→, this is
(p→(r∧q))→r. But few people would write it in this way.
So p→r∧q→r seems more likely to mean(p→r)∧(q→r).
Formulas likep→r∧q →r can be misinterpreted without
brackets, even if they are not strictly needed. The lesson: Don’t
write such formulas without brackets. USE BRACKETS if it
helps readability, even if they are not strictly needed.
– 26 –
Special cases
Sometimes the binding conventions don’t work as we would like.
For example, if I saw
p→r∧q→r,
I’d probably read it as(p→r)∧(q→r).
How can I justify this?∧is stronger than→. So shouldn’t it be
p→(r∧q)→r?
Read according to a plausible convention about repeated→, this is
(p→(r∧q))→r. But few people would write it in this way.
So p→r∧q→r seems more likely to mean(p→r)∧(q→r).
Formulas likep→r∧q →r can be misinterpreted without
brackets, even if they are not strictly needed. The lesson: Don’t
write such formulas without brackets. USE BRACKETS if it
helps readability, even if they are not strictly needed.
– 26 –
Parsing: formation tree, logical form
We have shown how to read a formula unambiguously — to parse it.
The information we gain from this can be represented as a tree: the
formation treeof the formula.
For example,¬p∨q→(p→q∧r) has the formation tree:
rq
∧p
→
q
p
¬
∨
→
This is a nicer (but too expensive) way to write formulas.
– 27 –
Principal connective
Note that the connective at the root (top!) of the tree is→. This is
the principal connectiveor main connectiveof¬p∨q→(p→q∧r).
This formula has theoverall logical formϕ→ψ.
Every non-atomic formula has a principal connective, which
determines itsoverall logical form. You have to learn to recognise it.
–p∧q→r has principal connective→.
Its overall logical form isϕ→ψ.
–¬(p→¬q) has principal connective¬.
Its overall logical form is¬ϕ.
–p∧q∧r has principal connective∧(probably the 2nd one!). Its
logical form isϕ∧ψ.
I stress overall as, in general, a formula has more than one
logical form. For example, ¬(p→¬q) has the logical forms:
¬ϕ,¬(ϕ→ψ) — and according to most definitions, also justϕ.
– 28 –
Subformulas
The tree view makes it easy to explain what a subformula is.
The subformulas of a formulaϕare the formulas built in the stages on
the way to buildingϕas in Definition 1.1.
They correspond to the nodes, or to the subtrees, of the formation
tree ofϕ.
The subformulas of¬p∨q→(p→q∧r) are:
r q
q∧r p q
p
¬p
p→q∧r¬p∨q
¬p∨q→(p→q∧r)
There aretwo differentsubformulasp. Andp∨q andp→q are
substrings but NOT subformulas.
– 29 –
Abbreviations
You may see some funny-looking formulas in books:
–⋀
1≤i≤nϕi, ⋀n
i=1ϕi,
These all abbreviateϕ1∧ϕ2∧...∧ϕn.
Example:
⋀
1≤i≤n
pi→q abbreviates p1∧...∧pn→q.
–⋁
1≤i≤nϕi, ⋁n
i=1ϕi,
n⋁
i=1
ϕi,
⋁
1≤i≤n
ϕi,
⋁ ⋁
1≤i≤n
ϕi.
These abbreviateϕ1∨ϕ2∨...∨ϕn.
This is like in algebra:
n∑
i=1
xi abbreviates x1 +x2 +···+xn.
– 30 –
Technical terms for logical forms
To understand logic books, you’ll need some jargon. Learning it is
tedious but necessary.
Definition 1.2
– A formula of the form⊤,⊥or p for an atomp, is (as we know) called
atomic.
– A formula whose logical form is¬ϕis called anegated formula. A
formula of the form¬p,¬⊤, or¬⊥is sometimes callednegated-atomic.
– A formula of the formϕ∧ψis called aconjunction and ϕ, ψare its
conjuncts.
– A formula of the formϕ∨ψis called adisjunction, andϕ, ψare its
disjuncts.
– A formula of the formϕ→ψis called animplication. ϕis called the
antecedent, ψis called theconsequent.
– A formula of the formϕ↔ψis called abidirectional implication.
– 31 –
Technical terms ctd. — literals and clauses
Definition 1.3 (Literals and clauses)
– A formula that is either atomic or negated-atomic is called a
literal.
– Aclause is a disjunction(∨) of one or more literals.
E.g., the formulasp,¬r,¬⊥,⊤are all literals.
The following are all clauses.
p p ∨¬q∨r
¬p p ∨p∨¬p∨¬⊥∨⊤∨¬q
How aboutp∨¬q∧¬p?
Some people allow the empty clause (the disjunction of zero literals),
which by convention is treated the same as⊥.
– 32 –
1.2 Semantics — The meaning
The Boolean connectives (∧and,¬not,∨or,→if-then,↔
if-and-only-if) haveroughly their English meanings.
But English is a natural language, full of ambiguity, subtlety, and
special cases.
We need a precise idea of the meaning of formulas:
– especially as there are infinitely many of them,
– because we may want to implement it.
In propositional logic, our concern is to study good (‘valid’) inferences
which depend just on:
– the truth or falsity of the propositional atoms; and
– the logical form in terms of Boolean connectives.
– 33 –
Atomic evaluation functions
A situation is simply something that determines truth-values,true (tt)
or false (ff), to each propositional atom in the language. (Some write
1, 0 instead.) It is formally given by anatomic evaluation function.
Definition 1.4 (Atomic evaluation function)
LetA be a set of propositional atoms. Anatomic evaluation function
v :A→{tt,ff}assigns truth-values to each propositional atom inA.
There is more than one situation. In a different situation, the
truth-values may be different.
Let v be an atomic evaluation function. The situation in which the
atom p is true is one where we would usev with v(p) = tt.
– 34 –
Atomic evaluation functions
A situation is simply something that determines truth-values,true (tt)
or false (ff), to each propositional atom in the language. (Some write
1, 0 instead.) It is formally given by anatomic evaluation function.
Definition 1.4 (Atomic evaluation function)
LetA be a set of propositional atoms. Anatomic evaluation function
v :A→{tt,ff}assigns truth-values to each propositional atom inA.
There is more than one situation. In a different situation, the
truth-values may be different.
Let v be an atomic evaluation function. The situation in which the
atom p is true is one where we would usev with v(p) = tt.
– 34 –
Evaluation functions
Knowing the situation, we can work out the truth-value of any given
propositional formula — whether it is true or false in this situation.
Definition 1.5 (Evaluation function)
LetA be a set of propositional atoms, andv an atomic evaluation
function forA. The evaluation function|...|v assigns the truth-value
true (tt) orfalse (ff) to formulas as follows.
– Ifϕis an atomp∈A:|p|v = tt iff v(p) = tt
–|¬ϕ|v = tt iff |ϕ|v = ff
–|ϕ∧ψ|v = tt iff |ϕ|v = tt and |ψ|v = tt
–|ϕ∨ψ|v = tt iff |ϕ|v = tt or |ψ|v = tt
–|ϕ→ψ|v = tt iff |ϕ|v = ff or |ψ|v = tt
–|ϕ↔ψ|v = tt iff |ϕ|v =|ψ|v
–|⊥|v = ff −|⊤|v = tt
Note that the definition does not explicitly say when a formula evaluates to
false. We don’t add these since we know a formula is assignedff iff it is not
assigned the truth-valuett.
– 35 –
Evaluation functions — Examples
Suppose thatv(p) = tt and v(q) = ff. Then:
–|¬p|v = ff
–|¬q|v = tt
–|p∧q|v = ff
–|p∨q|v = tt
–|¬p∧¬q|v = ff
–|p→¬q|v = tt
Do not confuse tt, ff (or 1,
0) with⊤,⊥; the latter are
formulas, not truth-values.
Now suppose thatv(p) = ff and v(q) = tt. Then:
–|¬p|v =
–|¬q|v =
–|p∧q|v =
–|p∨q|v =
–|¬p∧¬q|v =
–|p→¬q|v =
–|⊤|v =
We don’t ask whether a for-
mulaistrue. (It’slikeasking
‘is 3x = 7 true?’)
We ask if it is true in a given
situation.
– 36 –
Understanding evaluation functions
Note that ‘∨’ meansinclusive or: if bothϕand ψare true, then so is
ϕ∨ψ. If you want ‘exclusive’ or, write:(ϕ∨ψ)∧¬(ϕ∧ψ).
Consider “If there’s smoke, then there’s fire”. If it’s false that there’s
smoke, does that make the whole if-then claim true?
The semantics of ‘→’ we gave does makeϕ→ψtrue whereϕis false.
(Regardless ofψ, or of any causal relationship betweenϕand ψ.)
Advice: Treatϕ→ψas defined in Definition 1.5. It can often be
used to model if-then claims; but all it asserts is the relationship
between the truth-values ofp and q as stated in the definition.
The evaluation function makes the Boolean connectivestruth
functional: the truth-value of the the whole formula is functionally
determined by the truth-values of the “connected” subformulas.
– 37 –
Truth tables
Our connectives can also have their semantics given usingtruth tables.
These give the same information as Definition 1.5, forevery possiblev.
Truth tables show how the truth-values of complex formulas depend
on the truth-values of their subformulas.
p ¬p
tt ff
ff tt
ϕ ψ ϕ∧ψ ϕ∨ψ ϕ→ψ ϕ↔ψ
tt tt tt tt tt tt
tt ff ff tt ff ff
ff tt ff tt tt ff
ff ff ff ff tt tt
⊤⊥
tt ff
– 38 –
Truth tables
Our connectives can also have their semantics given usingtruth tables.
These give the same information as Definition 1.5, forevery possiblev.
Truth tables show how the truth-values of complex formulas depend
on the truth-values of their subformulas.
p ¬p
tt ff
ff tt
ϕ ψ ϕ∧ψ ϕ∨ψ ϕ→ψ ϕ↔ψ
tt tt tt tt tt tt
tt ff ff tt ff ff
ff tt ff tt tt ff
ff ff ff ff tt tt
⊤⊥
tt ff
– 38 –
Truth tables ctd.
Consider the situation wherep is true (i.e.,v(p) = tt) and q is false
(i.e., v(q) = ff); this fixes what the atomic evaluationv must be.
In this situation, the formulap→q is assigned the truth-valueff
(according to the truth table in in the previous slide.)
p q p∧q p∨q p→q p↔q
. . . . . .
tt ff . . ff .
. . . . . .
Let’s revisit Definition 1.5:
|p→q|v = tt iff |p|v = ff or |q|v = tt
iff tt = ff or ff = tt (in our case)
The RHS is false, so|p →q|v = ff, as the truth table said.
– 39 –
Meanings of other connectives
Any truth table determines a connective. For instance:
ϕ ψ ϕ↑ψ
tt tt ff
tt ff tt
ff tt tt
ff ff tt
↑, as defined here, is called theShef-
fer stroke.
It has a special property: all our
other Boolean connectives can be
defined in terms of it.
Let’s try to express¬p using only the Sheffer stroke operation↑.
p ¬p .↑.
tt ff .
ff tt .
– 40 –
Other connectives ctd.
It is often useful to know how manytruth-functionally, distinct
Boolean connectives of a given arity can be constructed.
There aretwo 0-ary connectives, andfour 1-ary connectives. Why?
One way to see that there are four1-ary connectives is to list the two
truth assignments for an atomp, tt and ff.
Let Θ represent an arbitrary1-ary connective. It can have any of the
following truth tables.
p Θp
tt tt
ff tt
p Θp
tt ff
ff ff
p Θp
tt tt
ff ff
p Θp
tt ff
ff tt
In general, there are22n
distinct n-ary Boolean connectives.
– 41 –
Functional completeness
Definition 1.6 (Functional completeness)
LetC be a set of Boolean connectives.C is functionally complete (for
propositional logic) if any connective of any arity can be defined just
in terms of the connectives inC.
For instance:
The Sheffer stroke is functionally complete for propositional logic.
The set{¬,∧}is functionally complete for propositional logic (i.e.,
any connective, including↑, can also be expressed with∧and¬).
ϕ↑ψcan be expressed as¬(ϕ∧ψ)
– 42 –
1.3 English correspondence
Translating from English to logic is difficult. But we have to do it for
applications.
We can only translate (some)statements; not questions,
commands/invitations, exclamations.
For example,
I am king of the world (‘I am king of the world’)
I bought milk and cookies (‘I bought milk’∧‘I bought cookies’)
There’s no crying in baseball (¬‘There is crying in baseball’)
The answer is yes or no (‘The answer is yes’∨‘the answer is
no’)
Let us consider the following sentence.
The train is delayed and we don’t have a car
– 43 –
Translation in action
The train is delayedand we don’t have a car
(The train is delayedand we don’t have a car)
(‘The train is delayed’and we don’t have a car)
(‘The train is delayed’and it’s not the case that we have a car)
(‘The train is delayed’and (it’s not the case that we have a car))
(‘The train is delayed’and (it’s not the case that ‘we have a car’))
Taked to represent ‘The train is delayed’ andc for ‘we have a car’,
then the above English sentence is translated into the following
propositional logic formula:
(d∧(¬c))
– 44 –
English variants
–‘But’means ‘and’. (So doesyet, although, though.)
E.g., I will go out, but it is raining
Let g be an atom for ‘I will go out’ andr for ‘it is raining’. We get
(g∧r).
–‘Unless’generally means ‘or’.
E.g., I will go out unless it rains
Let g be an atom for ‘I will go out’ andw for ‘it will rain’.(Note
the extra ‘will’.)
We get(g∨w). You could also use ((¬w)→g).
But you may think that ‘I will go out unless it rains’ implies thatif it does
rain then I won’t go out. This is astronger reading of ‘unless’
–Strong ‘Unless’(also called “exclusive or”).
‘I will go out unless it rains’ becomes (g↔(¬w)).
– 45 –
English variants ctd.
–‘Only if’
You will pass only if your aver-
age is at least forty
(‘You will pass’→‘your average is at
least forty’)
–‘Is necessary for’
James’ attending the course is
necessary for his obtaining a cer-
tificate of attendance
(‘James obtains a certificate of atten-
dance’→‘James attends the course’)
–‘Is sufficient for’
Layla’s timely arrival for re-
hearsals is sufficient for her tak-
ing part in the play
(‘Layla arrives on time for rehearsals’
→‘Layla will take part in the play’)
Other variants:provided that, on the condition that, in case, ...
Note: Use symbols for propositional atoms rather than strings in
quotes. (I was lazy!)
– 46 –
From logic to English
Let p be ‘It is raining’ andq be ‘I go out’’,
p∧q translates to It is raining and I go out
¬p translates to It is not the case it is raining
p→q translates to If it is raining then I go out
You might think this is straightforward:
E.g., p→¬q translates to If it is raining then it is not the case I go out.
But there are problems. Complex formulas are hard to render
naturally in English.
– 47 –
From logic to English ctd.
‘→’is a nightmare to translate.
The semantics of→works superbly for logic.
But in English we use ‘if-then’ in many different ways, and not always
carefully.
Don’t read→as ‘causes’ — too strong.
E.g., ‘I play in league 2 football’→‘I play in the Premiere league’ is true here
and now.
But would you sayI play in league 2 football , then I play in the Premiere
league is true?
– 48 –
Modalities (pitfalls)
A modality can be seen as shifting the context of evaluation of an
utterance.
Time:
‘I will be rich and famous’. It can’t be translated as
‘I will be rich’ and ‘I will be famous.’
The formula is true if I get famous only after I lose all my money.
The English probably isn’t: it suggests I’ll be rich and famous at the
same time.
Permission:
‘You can have chicken or fish’. It usually means
‘You can have chicken’∧‘you can have fish’.
Obligation:
‘I must do topics or a foreign language’. It can’t be translated as
‘I must do topics∨‘I must do a language’
(was false — you could choose which to take).
See the 4th year module Modal Logic for Strategic Reasoning in AI.
– 49 –
A really vicious example
This one deserves careful thought. Consider the formulas:
1. p∧q→r
2. (p→r)∨(q→r).
Let p be ‘I throw the rock at the bottle’,q be ‘you throw the rock at the
bottle’, andr be ‘the bottle shatters’.
Then (1) says thatIf we both throw the rock at the bottle,then
the bottle shatters.
And (2) saysIf I throw the rock at the bottleor you throw it then
the bottles shatters.
Right? Would you say these mean the same? I guess not.
But they do!The formulas (1) and (2) are true in exactly the same
situations. They are ‘logically equivalent’! We’ll see this later.
I think the trouble is that in the English version of (2), you read the
‘or’ as ‘and’. You shouldn’t!
– 50 –
A really vicious example
This one deserves careful thought. Consider the formulas:
1. p∧q→r
2. (p→r)∨(q→r).
Let p be ‘I throw the rock at the bottle’,q be ‘you throw the rock at the
bottle’, andr be ‘the bottle shatters’.
Then (1) says thatIf we both throw the rock at the bottle,then
the bottle shatters.
And (2) saysIf I throw the rock at the bottleor you throw it then
the bottles shatters.
Right? Would you say these mean the same? I guess not.
But they do!The formulas (1) and (2) are true in exactly the same
situations. They are ‘logically equivalent’! We’ll see this later.
I think the trouble is that in the English version of (2), you read the
‘or’ as ‘and’. You shouldn’t!
– 50 –
Be mindful ...
1. ‘Arron and Russell are students’.
This could be rewritten as: ‘Arron is a student’and ‘Russell is a
student’.
How about
2. ‘Arron and Russell are friends’.
Is this the same as: ‘Arron is a friend’and ‘Russell is a friend’?
We might argue our way to represent this by rephrasing the English
sentence as
‘Arron is a friend of Russell’and ‘Russell is a friend of Arron’.
What about ‘Arron is a friend of everyone’? FOL deals with these ...
– 51 –
Be mindful ...
1. ‘Arron and Russell are students’.
This could be rewritten as: ‘Arron is a student’and ‘Russell is a
student’.
How about
2. ‘Arron and Russell are friends’.
Is this the same as: ‘Arron is a friend’and ‘Russell is a friend’?
We might argue our way to represent this by rephrasing the English
sentence as
‘Arron is a friend of Russell’and ‘Russell is a friend of Arron’.
What about ‘Arron is a friend of everyone’? FOL deals with these ...
– 51 –
Be mindful ...
1. ‘Arron and Russell are students’.
This could be rewritten as: ‘Arron is a student’and ‘Russell is a
student’.
How about
2. ‘Arron and Russell are friends’.
Is this the same as: ‘Arron is a friend’and ‘Russell is a friend’?
We might argue our way to represent this by rephrasing the English
sentence as
‘Arron is a friend of Russell’and ‘Russell is a friend of Arron’.
What about ‘Arron is a friend of everyone’? FOL deals with these ...

documents.title: (3) lecture 5
documents.text:
Discrete mathematics, logic and reasoning
(COMP40018)
Francesco Leofante
Many thanks to Dalal Alrajeh for some of the original material.
– 1 –
4th method Proof Systems: Natural Deduction
We should be able to establish validity of argument by breaking it
into smaller arguments and showing the validity of these intermediate
ones, i.e., construct aproof.
A proof system is a way of showing formulas to be valid by using
purely syntactic rules—not using semantics at all. A computer
algorithm should be able to apply the rules.
There are many proof systems. We will focus onnatural deduction
(ND), a system invented by F. B. Fitch (and related to work of
Gentzen); it is a major topic and will take some time.
– 2 –
Pandora:
https://www.doc.ic.ac.uk/pandora/newpandora/
Pandora is a lab program that allows you to do natural deduction
proofs as a kind of computer game.
It was originally done by Dan Ehrlich as a 4th-year individual project,
c.1998. It has evolved since then, always developed by students,
supervised by Krysia Broda and (in the past) Gabrielle Sinnadurai.
– 3 –
What is natural deduction?
A formalisation of ‘direct argument’.
Starting perhaps from some given facts—some formulasϕ1,...,ϕn—
we use the rules of the system to reason towards a formulaψ.
(In practice, we reason forwards, backwards, and especially inwards.)
If we succeed, we can write
ϕ1,...,ϕn⊢ψ
ϕ1,...,ϕn are called premises.
ψis called aconclusion.
ϕ1,...,ϕn⊢ψis called asequent.
– 4 –
Assumptions in natural deduction
Proofs in Fitch’s system are not based on axioms expressed in logical
form, as in Hilbert’s axiomatic proof theory.
They sometimes involve making “temporary” assumptions to prove a
conclusion.
“Assume for the sake of argument ...”
An assumption is just a formula, but it’s used in a special way.
We imagine a situation in which a formula is true. Then we derive
some additional formulas that help us make progress towards the
conclusion.
We need to be careful about how we use these assumption. We will
see examples of its use soon.
– 5 –
How natural deduction works
Deduction works by writing down intermediate formulas using
inference rules.
– State the set of premisesϕ1,...,ϕn, and the conclusionψ.
– Intermediate formulas form theproofof ψfrom the givens
ϕ1,...,ϕn.
– Once established, they may be usable later.
– Each step of the proof is avalid argument.
– 6 –
Natural deduction inference rules
Mostly, there are two rules for each connective:
– one forintroducingit (bringing in a formula whose principal
connective is the connective it introduces),
– one forusing it (using a formula already present in a step of the
proof, whose principal connective is the connective it wants to
use).
The rules are based on the semantics for the connectives given earlier.
– 7 –
Rules for conjunction
∧-introduction, or∧I: To introduce a formula of the formϕ∧ψ, you
have to have already introducedϕand ψ.
1 ϕ we proved this
2 ... (other stuff)
3 ψ and this
4 ϕ∧ψ∧I(1, 3)
The line numbers are essential for clarity.
ϕand ψin the above need not be atomic.
– 8 –
Rules for conjunction—ctd.
(∧-elimination, or∧E): If you have managed to write downϕ∧ψ,
you can go on to write downϕand/or ψ.
1 ϕ∧ψ we have this somehow
2 ϕ ∧E(1)
3 ψ ∧E(1)
– 9 –
Rules for conjunction—example.
Prove that the sequent p∧q,r ⊢q∧r is valid.
1 p∧q premise
2 r premise
3 q ∧E(1)
4
q∧r
∧I(3, 2)
– 10 –
Rules for conjunction—example.
Prove that the sequent p∧q,r ⊢q∧r is valid.
1 p∧q premise
2 r premise
3 q ∧E(1)
4
q∧r
∧I(3, 2)
– 10 –
Rules for conjunction—example.
Prove that the sequent p∧q,r ⊢q∧r is valid.
1 p∧q premise
2 r premise
3 q ∧E(1)
4 q∧r ∧I(3, 2)
– 10 –
Rules for implication
→-introduction, or→I: To introduce a formula of the formϕ→ψ,
you assume ϕand then proveψ.
During the proof, you can useϕas well as anything already
established.
But you can’t useϕor anything from the proof ofψfrom ϕlater on
(because it’s based on an extra assumption).
So we isolate the proof ofψfrom ϕ, in a box:
1 ϕ asm
2 ... ⟨the proof⟩hard struggle
3 ψ we made it!
4 ϕ→ψ →I(1, 3)
– 11 –
Boxes in natural deduction
Boxes are used when making additional assumptions.
The first line should always be labelled ‘asm’ (assumption).
The line immediately following the closed box must match the pattern
of the conclusion of the rule that uses the box.
Nothing inside the box can be used later.
– 12 –
Rules for implication—ctd.
→-elimination, or→E: If you have managed to write downϕand
ϕ→ψ, in either order, you can go on to write downψ.
(This is modus ponens.)
1 ϕ→ψ we got this somehow
2
... (other stuff)
3 ϕ and this too
4 ψ →E(1, 3)
– 13 –
Rules for implication—ctd.
Prove that the sequent p,p→q,p→(q→r)⊢r is valid.

2 p→q premise
3 p→(q→r) premise

– 15 –
Using natural deduction to prove equivalence
We say two formulasϕand ψare provably equivalent in natural
deduction (written asϕ⊣⊢ψ) iffϕ⊢ψand ψ⊢ϕ.
Prove that (p∧q)→r⊣⊢p→(q→r).
We first show(p∧q)→r⊢p→(q→r).
1 ( p∧q)→r premise

3 q asm

Prove that (p∧q)→r⊣⊢p→(q→r).

– 17 –
Rules for disjunction
∨-introduction, or∨I: To proveϕ∨ψ, proveϕ, or (if you prefer)
proveψ.
...
3 ϕ proved this somehow
4 ϕ∨ψ ∨I(3)
ψcan be any formula at all!
...
3 ψ proved this somehow
4 ϕ∨ψ∨I(3)
Similarly ϕcan be any formula at all!
– 18 –
Rules for disjunction—ctd.
(∨-Elimination, or∨E): To prove something fromϕ∨ψ, you have to
prove it by assumingϕ, AND prove it by assumingψ.
This is arguing by cases.
1 ϕ∨ψ we got this somehow
2 ϕ asm
...
6 ρ
7 ψ asm
...
9 ρ
we gotρfrom both proofs
10 ρ ∨E(1, 2−6, 7−9)
The assumptionsϕ,ψare not usable later, so are put in (side-by-side)
boxes.
Both boxes must end with the sameρ; ρcan be any formula.
Nothing inside the boxes can be used later, or in the other box.
– 19 –

Prove that the sequent q→r⊢p∨q→p∨r is valid.

– 20 –
Rules for disjunction—ctd.
Prove that the sequent q→r⊢p∨q→p∨r is valid.
1 q→r premise
2 p∨q asm
3 p asm
4 p∨r ∨I(3)
5 q asm
6 r →E(1, 5)
7 p∨r ∨I(6)
8
p∨r
∨E(2, 3−4, 5−7)
9
p∨q→p∨r
→I(2, 8)
– 20 –
Rules for disjunction—ctd.
Prove that the sequent q→r⊢p∨q→p∨r is valid.
1 q→r premise
2 p∨q asm
3 p asm
4 p∨r ∨I(3)
5 q asm
6 r →E(1, 5)
7 p∨r ∨I(6)
8 p∨r ∨E(2, 3−4, 5−7)
9
p∨q→p∨r
→I(2, 8)
– 20 –
Rules for disjunction—ctd.
Prove that the sequent q→r⊢p∨q→p∨r is valid.
1 q→r premise
2 p∨q asm
3 p asm
4 p∨r ∨I(3)
5 q asm
6 r →E(1, 5)
7 p∨r ∨I(6)
8 p∨r ∨E(2, 3−4, 5−7)
9 p∨q→p∨r →I(2, 8)
– 20 –
Rules for single negation
These rules involve the notion ofcontradiction. They treat¬ϕlike
ϕ→⊥.
The formula⊥stands for the contradiction. Examples of
contradiction arep∧¬p and (p∨q)∧¬(p∨q).
As far as truth is concerned, these are all provably equivalent, for
instance
p∧¬p⊢(p∨q)∧¬(p∨q) and (p∨q)∧¬(p∨q)⊢p∧¬p
– 21 –
Rules for single negation ctd.
¬-introduction, or¬I: To prove¬ϕ, you assumeϕand prove⊥.
⊥stands for the contradiction.
As usual, you can’t then useϕlater on, so enclose the proof of⊥from
assumption ϕin a box:
1 ϕ asm
2 ...
3 ⊥
more hard work, oh no
we got it!
4 ¬ϕ ¬I(1, 3)
Idea: If one can prove a contradiction from an assumptionϕ, then one
can conclude that ‘It is not the case thatϕ’.
– 22 –
Rules for single negation ctd.
(¬-elimination,¬E): Fromϕand¬ϕ, deduce⊥.
1 ¬ϕ proved this somehow
2 ...
3 ϕ and this
4 ⊥ ¬E(3, 1)
Note:¬E allows one to introduce a formulaψand discharge all
assumptions of¬ψeven if there aren’t any. We will see this later.
– 23 –
Rules for single negation—Example
Show that p→¬p⊢¬p is a valid sequent.
1 p→¬p premise
2 p asm
3 ¬p →E(1, 2)
4 ⊥ ¬ E(2, 3)
5
¬p
¬I(2, 4)
– 24 –
Rules for single negation—Example

2 p asm

5 ¬p ¬I(2, 4)
– 24 –
Rules for negation—double
¬¬-introduction,¬¬I: Fromϕ, deduce¬¬ϕ
1 ϕ proved this somehow
2 ¬¬ϕ¬¬I(1)
Intuitively, saying that ‘it isnot true that the party isnot fun’ is just
like saying ’the party is fun’
¬¬-elimination,¬¬E: From¬¬ϕ, deduceϕ.
1 ¬¬ϕ proved this somehow
2 ϕ ¬¬E(1)
– 25 –
Rules for negation—double ctd.
Show that p,¬¬(q∧r)⊢¬¬p∧r.
1 p premise
2 ¬¬(q∧r) premise
3 ¬¬p ¬¬I(1)
4 q∧r ¬¬E(2)
5 r ∧E(4)
6

∧I(3, 5)
– 26 –
Rules for negation—double ctd.
Show that p,¬¬(q∧r)⊢¬¬p∧r.

4 q∧r ¬¬E(2)
5 r ∧E(4)
6

Rules for negation—double ctd.
Show that p,¬¬(q∧r)⊢¬¬p∧r.

– 26 –

Show that p,¬¬(q∧r)⊢¬¬p∧r.

4 q∧r ¬¬E(2)
5 r ∧E(4)
6

– 26 –
Rules for negation—double ctd.
Show that p,¬¬(q∧r)⊢¬¬p∧r.

2 ¬¬(q∧r) premise
3 ¬¬p ¬¬I(1)
4 q∧r ¬¬E(2)
5 r ∧E(4)
6
¬¬p∧r
∧I(3, 5)
– 26 –
Rules for negation—double ctd.
Show that p,¬¬(q∧r)⊢¬¬p∧r.
1 p premise
2 ¬¬(q∧r) premise
3 ¬¬p ¬¬I(1)
4 q∧r ¬¬E(2)
5 r ∧E(4)
6 ¬¬p∧r ∧I(3, 5)
– 26 –
Contradiction in natural deduction
Any formula can be derived from a contradiction... This can be
confusing at first.
Suppose
p: Cows can fly.
q: I like going to the theatre.
It is perfectly valid to say
p∧¬p⊢q
⊢tells us what we can infer, given the premises on its left (which may
be always false!). The derivation does not care whether these premises
make sense.
– 27 –
Rules for bottom
You might find the argument above hard to accept. You will not be
alone: it has led some to reject or reformulate classical logic.
I could just say that we are just following proof rules. If the rules
allow us to get from⊥to ϕ, then fine.
Better: the English paraphrase says:
SUPPOSE we are in a situation in which⊥is true... then ϕis true.
(And¬ϕis true too, soϕis false,...)
BUT there is no such situation. So you can’t dispute the crazy things
that follow from this supposition.
– 28 –
Rules for bottom–ctd.
⊥-elimination,⊥E: This encode the fact that a contradiction can
prove anything.
1 ⊥ we got this
2 ϕ ⊥E(1)
⊥-introduction, or⊥I: To prove⊥, you must proveϕand¬A (for
anyϕyou like).
1 ϕ got this somehow
2 ...
3 ¬ϕ and this
4 ⊥ ⊥ I(1, 3)
– 29 –
Rules for bottom—ctd.
The practical point is that there is no situation in which⊥is true, so
you can only prove⊥under (contradictory) assumptions in a box in
the middle of a bigger proof.
You often get such assumptions—e.g., in an argument by cases(∨E).
From⊥you can then deduce anything you want, so the box is no
obstacle to the overall goal. So⊥is a useful formula to aim to prove.
Note that⊥I is the same rule as¬E. (There are two names for
this rule!)
The rules for⊥can actually be obtained from the other rules
already explained.
– 30 –
Rules for bottom—ctd.
Show that ¬p∨q⊢p→q is valid.
1 ¬p∨q premise
2 ¬p asm
3 p asm
4 ⊥ ¬E(3, 2)
5 q ⊥E(4)
6 p→q →I(3, 5)
7 q asm
8 p asm
9 q ✓(7)
10 p→q →I(8, 9)
11
p→q
∨E(1, 2−6, 7−10)
– 31 –
Rules for bottom—ctd.
Show that ¬p∨q⊢p→q is valid.
1 ¬p∨q premise
2 ¬p asm
3 p asm
4 ⊥ ¬E(3, 2)
5 q ⊥E(4)
6 p→q →I(3, 5)
7 q asm
8 p asm
9 q ✓(7)
10 p→q →I(8, 9)
11 p→q ∨E(1, 2−6, 7−10)
– 31 –
Rules for top
⊤-introduction, or⊤I: You can introduce⊤anywhere (for all the
good it does you).
⊤-elimination,⊤E: You can prove nothing new from⊤, sorry!
– 32 –
Examples of natural deduction—Tricky!
Show that ⊢p∨¬p is valid.
1 ¬(p∨¬p) asm
2 p asm
3 p∨¬p ∨I(1)
4 ⊥ ¬E(3, 1)
5 ¬p ¬I(2, 4)
6 p∨¬p ∨I(5)
7 ⊥ ¬ E(6, 1)
8 ¬¬(p∨¬p) ¬I(1, 7)
9 p∨¬p ¬¬E(8)
Make sure you appropriately discharge assumptions in boxes.
– 33 –
Examples of natural deduction—Tricky!
Show that ⊢p∨¬p is valid.
1 ¬(p∨¬p) asm
2 p asm
3 p∨¬p ∨I(1)
4 ⊥ ¬E(3, 1)
5 ¬p ¬I(2, 4)
6 p∨¬p ∨I(5)
7 ⊥ ¬ E(6, 1)
8 ¬¬(p∨¬p) ¬I(1, 7)
9 p∨¬p ¬¬E(8)
Make sure you appropriately discharge assumptions in boxes.
– 33 –

documents.title: 05.02 BoA COver Letter
documents.text:
Dear Bank of America Hiring Manager, 
I hope that after reading this letter, I will have convinced you to give me a spot at the Spring Insight 
Programme at your London office. I am a 1st year studying Mathematics and Computer Science at 
Imperial College London with a predicted 1st. Through this opportunity, I would hope to gain insight 
into the investment management industry and discover what kind of future I could build at BoA.  
What draws me to Bank of America is your approach to thinking generationally and building for the 
long term — not just in markets, but in technology. You invest at a scale and horizon that allows you to 
own and evolve core systems rather than rely on short-term vendor solutions. BoA as a firm 
impresses me massively because it is not only future sighted, but it values technology and hence it 
values technologists. BoA invests at a scale that allows you to own and customise core systems 
rather than relying on inferior vendor solutions. An approach I take in my day to day use of software. 
BoA has tens of thousands of technologists – you are clearly committed to building durable platforms 
in payments, markets infrastructure, cybersecurity and machine learning. I would love to learn more 
about how you achieve this and how engineers think at BoA. 
I am particularly excited by how technology is embedded directly into decision-making and client 
outcomes. Products such as Erica demonstrate how AI and NLP are deployed at real scale, while 
your fraud detection, risk, and payments systems operate on billions of transactions in real time,  
where correctness, latency, and resilience genuinely matter. This is not technology for its own sake, 
but technology used to solve hard, high-impact problems under real-world constraints. 
Your use of proprietary systems is logical to me because in such a regulated environment, reliability of 
software is crucial. I am interested to see how your software implements checks and balance 
rigorously in this space. 
The programme is compelling to me because the description suggests a nice balance between 
learning and exploration – not just a recruitment exercise. I am very interested in the general finance 
side of the spring too! I just want to make it clear that software engineering is also a role that excites 
me in the finance space. 
I also have relevant competences for the industry: at GFG in Sydney, I built LSTM steel models 
(RMSE < 0.03) and cut trucking hours by 17% via geofence analysis. So, I know how to take data and 
create actionable business insight. Moreover, I have experience under pressure – a necessary 
attribute in investment management: I’ve led a team through 14 hours straight of high pressure 
mathematical modelling in the M3 Mathematical Modelling Competition leading to winner ’s 
shortlisting; I know how to maintain analytical focus when the going gets tough. Furthermore, I 
finished top 5 in IMC's coding trading simulation and top 10 in QRT's trading mathematics competition 
both across over 250 Imperial students across all years, demonstrating my interest in financial 
markets. 
Beyond academics, I thrive in collaborative environments where relationships and learning matter. As 
founder and mentor in 2 problem-solving communities with 50,000+ combined members, I have 
experience driving teamwork from sets of diverse contributors. Moreover, as 6th form prefect, elected 
from less than 1% of the student body, I learned how to communicate and organise effectively with 
large groups for prolonged periods of time. In addition, as a Gold DofE awardee and as a top 10 GB 
hockey goalkeeper, I refined my grit and ability to perform under pressure. Finally, as chair of the UK's 
largest Eco Club – I demonstrated long-term commitment to causes beyond immediate personal 
benefit - mirroring Fidelity's focus on managing your impact on society and the environment while 
delivering sustainable investment returns. 
Thank you for considering my application for the Spring Insight Programme. Should I be selected, I 
look forward to contributing in every way possible and discovering what kind of future I could build in 
the investment management industry! 
Yours faithfully, 
Neel Agrawal

documents.title: 07.02.2026 Cover Letter HRT 1
documents.text:
Hudson River Trading 
London Office 
Dear Hiring Team, 
I hope that after reading this, I will have convinced you to give me a spot at Explore HRT this season 
at your London Office. I am a Mathematics and Computing (JMC) student at Imperial College London 
with a predicted First, and I am not only fascinated by quant as an industry but with HRT as a firm. 
Naturally, HRT has been a name I’ve been hearing since coming to university. After going to every 
single one of your events and talks at Imperial, and after chatting to your team at IC-Hack last 
weekend, I’ve come to realise how truly exceptional you are. Firstly, infrastructurally, you spare no 
inch in gaining an edge. Your talks involving your physical infrastructure should not be overshadowed 
by the fantastic tools you support your researchers with — HRTWorker, for instance. 
I desire to work in environments where rigour is paired with an animalistic drive for efficiency. Growth 
in all facets of my life excites me, so much so that I enjoy optimising the rate of growth itself. I know 
that automation and efficiency are core values of yours. Automation is something I implement even on 
menial scales, for example when playing a videogame. HRT has built one of the world’s most 
sophisticated computing environments for research and development; this demonstrates a forward-
thinking culture where I believe I would thrive. 
HRT’s office itself seems enjoyable too. With a UK team on the order of hundreds rather than 
thousands, it strikes the ideal balance: large enough to learn from world-class researchers and 
engineers across disciplines, yet small enough to foster a strong sense of community, ownership, and 
individual responsibility. Environments where the success of my contributions can easily be measured 
and where collaboration feels personal suit me. 
The Explore HRT programme appeals to me primarily for the insight into quantitative research, 
alongside direct interaction with technical team members of a firm as great as HRT. 
During the M3 Maths Modelling Competition, I led my team through high-pressure modelling 
scenarios of the Bristol housing market, demonstrating that I can perform under pressure. Moreover, 
as a British Top 5 finisher at Oxford’s PROMYS Europe, I demonstrate the technical creativity that a 
career in quantitative research demands. Finally, as an intern at GFG Alliance last summer, I built 
LSTM commodity price forecasting models with RMSE < 0.03 and reduced trucking costs by 17% 
through geofencing analysis. This shows that I have experience converting technical results into 
tangible outcomes. I am eager to develop these skills further by learning from HRT’s researchers and 
engineers. 
Beyond academics, I thrive in collaborative environments where relationships and learning matter. As 
founder and mentor in two problem-solving communities with over 50,000 combined members, I have 
experience driving teamwork across diverse contributors. Moreover, as a sixth-form prefect, elected 
from less than 1% of the student body, I learned how to communicate and organise effectively with 
large groups over prolonged periods. In addition, as a Gold Duke of Edinburgh awardee and a top-10 
GB hockey goalkeeper, I refined my grit and ability to perform under pressure — a necessary skill in 
the financial services industry. Finally, during my role as chairman of the largest Eco Club in the UK, 
securing our Green Flag Award for energy optimisations, I demonstrated long-term commitment to 
causes beyond immediate personal benefit, mirroring the socially responsible outlook at HRT. 
Thank you for considering my application. I am excited about the opportunity to participate in Explore 
HRT, learn more about Hudson River Trading’s technical roles, and gain insight into what it is like to 
work at the world’s top quantitative trading firm. I look forward to discussing how I can contribute and 
grow through the programme. 
Yours faithfully, 
Neel Agrawal

documents.title: 07.03.2026 Goldman Sachs Cover Letter
documents.text:
Goldman Sachs 
International Commerce Centre 
1 Austin Road West 
Kowloon, Hong Kong 
Dear Goldman Sachs Hiring Team, 
As a Mathematics and Computer Science student at Imperial College London with a 
predicted 1st, I value this opportunity from Goldman Sachs deeply as a means to deepen my 
understanding of how my studies, which, in theory are extremely relevant to the industry, 
are actually applied. 
I really enjoy applying technical strategies to solve uncertain problems under pressure. So 
trading is naturally of massive interest to me. At Imperial I actively pursue opportunities to 
combine these skills in competitive environments. I placed in the top five in IMC’s trading 
simulation competition across 255 Imperial students and my team finished third from 
Imperial in the Rotman x Trinity Algorithmic Trading Competition - experiences which 
required interpreting noisy signals, managing risk, and forming probabilistic judgement 
under pressure. 
Moreover, I have experience competing in data driven environments – I led a team during 
the M3 Mathematical Modelling Competition, where we developed ARIMA forecasts and 
applied Granger causality analysis. This further strengthened my interest in quantitative 
modelling and decision-making. 
Goldman’s Trader Academy in particular excites me because of its hands on learning and 
mentorship emphasis. I would find great insight in learning how Goldman traders think. 
Hong Kong is also somewhere I would work long term. As a Jane Street intern there, I have 
really fallen in love with the city. 
Thank you for considering my application. I would be delighted to attend the Academy and 
further develop my understanding of trading at Goldman Sachs. 
Yours faithfully, 
Neel Agrawal

documents.title: 10.02 BoA Cover Letter
documents.text:
Dear Bank of America Hiring Manager, 
I hope that after reading this letter, I will have convinced you to give me a spot at the Spring Insight 
Programme at your Dublin office. I am a 1st year studying Mathematics and Computer Science at 
Imperial College London with a predicted 1st. Through this opportunity, I would hope to gain insight 
into what sort of future I could build in SWE in finance. 
What draws me to Bank of America is your approach to thinking generationally and building for the 
long term — not just in markets, but in technology. You invest at a scale and horizon that allows you to 
own and evolve core systems rather than rely on short-term vendor solutions. BoA as a firm 
impresses me massively because it is not only future sighted, but it values technology and hence it 
values technologists. BoA invests at a scale that allows you to own and customise core systems 
rather than relying on inferior vendor solutions. An approach I take in my day to day use of software. 
BoA has tens of thousands of technologists – you are clearly committed to building durable platforms 
in payments, markets infrastructure, cybersecurity and machine learning. I would love to learn more 
about how you achieve this and how engineers think at BoA. 
I am particularly excited by how technology is embedded directly into decision-making and client 
outcomes. Products such as Erica demonstrate how AI and NLP are deployed at real scale, while 
your fraud detection, risk, and payments systems operate on billions of transactions in real time, 
where correctness, latency, and resilience genuinely matter. This is not technology for its own sake, 
but technology used to solve hard, high-impact problems under real-world constraints. 
Your use of proprietary systems is logical to me because in such a regulated environment, reliability of 
software is crucial. I am interested to see how your software implements checks and balance 
rigorously in this space. 
The programme is compelling to me because the description suggests a nice balance between 
learning and exploration – not just a recruitment exercise. I am very interested in the general finance 
side of the spring too! I just want to make it clear that software engineering is also a role that excites 
me in the finance space. 
I also have relevant competences for the industry: at GFG in Sydney, I built LSTM steel models 
(RMSE < 0.03) and cut trucking hours by 17% via geofence analysis. So, I know how to take data and 
create actionable business insight. Moreover, I have experience under pressure – a necessary 
attribute in investment management: I’ve led a team through 14 hours straight of high pressure 
mathematical modelling in the M3 Mathematical Modelling Competition leading to winner’s 
shortlisting; I know how to maintain analytical focus when the going gets tough. Furthermore, I 
finished top 5 in IMC's coding trading simulation and top 10 in QRT's trading mathematics competition 
both across over 250 Imperial students across all years, demonstrating my interest in financial 
markets. 
Beyond academics, I thrive in collaborative environments where relationships and learning matter. As 
founder and mentor in 2 problem-solving communities with 50,000+ combined members, I have 
experience driving teamwork from sets of diverse contributors. Moreover, as 6th form prefect, elected 
from less than 1% of the student body, I learned how to communicate and organise effectively with 
large groups for prolonged periods of time. In addition, as a Gold DofE awardee and as a top 10 GB 
hockey goalkeeper, I refined my grit and ability to perform under pressure. Finally, as chair of the UK's 
largest Eco Club – I demonstrated long-term commitment to causes beyond immediate personal 
benefit - mirroring Fidelity's focus on managing your impact on society and the environment while 
delivering sustainable investment returns. 
Thank you for considering my application for the Spring Insight Programme. Should I be selected, I 
look forward to contributing in every way possible and discovering what kind of future I could build in 
the industry! 
Yours faithfully, 
Neel Agrawal

AuDHD Psychiatry

support@audhdpsychiatry.co.uk

To Whom It May Concern,
Re:  Invoice 2133 for Neel Agrawal 
I am writing in my capacity as a Consultant Psychologist specialising in ADHD assessments to con2rm that Mr Neel Agrawal
has undergone a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and has been formally diagnosed by AuDHD Psychiatry.  
The assessment consisted of the following: 
Part 1 - Autism Diagnostic Interview on 10th December 2025 with Dr Darren O'Reilly 
Part 2 - Autism Informant Interview on 19th December 2025 with Dr Darren O'Reilly 
Part 3 - ADHD Diagnostic Interview on 20th January 2026 with Dr Darren O'Reilly 
Part 4 - feedback on 30th January 2026 with Dr Darren O'Reilly 
The total cost for this Combined ADHD and Autism Assessment was £2,195 which has already been paid in full by Neel
Agrawal.  
Neel Agrawal has been given a formal diagnosis of both ADHD and Autism as a result of this assessment.  
 
Should you have any further questions or require additional information, please do not hesitate to contact me.
Kind regards,

Dr Darren O'Reilly

•Smart Lamp - iLoomi- Led team of four in the full stack creation of a smart-lamp system with sensors, micro-controllers and
an app. This got us shortlisted.
•Hospital Routing Optimiser - CareNow– Built a Flask web app that optimises hospital selection using probabilistic
wait-time predictions combined with live travel times, presented via a Google-Maps-style UI.
A wards and Competitions
•SchoolRank 1for each of Mathematics/Computer Science/Physics. Overall rank 1 across 500 students.
•ICHack2026 Bending SpoonsHonourable Mentionand IMC Math ChallengeT op Scorer
•Oxford University Coding Competition/Bebras: 2x Gold (top 0.5%), 1x Distinction (top 0.1%), 2x Best-In-School Award.
•British Mathematics Olympiads: 4x Gold (top 8%), 2x Distinction BMO (top 0.25%), and 1x BPhO Gold.
•National Shine Award - 2x winning for publishments in Mathematics and Computer Science.
•Top 5 in IMC’s trading simulation competition across 255 Imperial students from all years.
•3rd placefrom Imperial in Rotman x Trinity College Algorithmic Trading Competition
•Top 5% in QRT Trading Mathematics Competition, Top 5% in QRT Hackathon
Relevant Skills
•Experienced: Python, SQL, Haskell, Pandas, NumPy, MatplotLib, Pytorch, MatLab, Tensor-Flow, Assembly LMC, Excel
•Founder/Mentor of 2 Online Problem-Solving Communities which have member counts of over 50,000.
•Elected Prefect from 1% of student body - spent 4 hours per week making decisions, distributing comms and organising events.
•Chairman for Imperial Housing (£50K budget) and Year Rep for Imperial Mathematics
•TedX speaker/King’s College London Tech Summit speaker - discussing PROMYS Mathematics solution set.
Hobbies
•Chairman of the largest Eco Club in the UK through 9 years of service, green flag certified for energy optimisations.
•Music: Percussion for Professional Theatre; Winning national singing competition at RAH; top 10 GB hockey; county
table-tennis; backpacking (Gold DofE)

documents.title: 29.01.2026 Cover Letter TF 1
documents.text:
Dear Hiring Manager at Treefera,  
I hope that after reading this letter I will have convinced you to choose me as one of your SWE 
Interns this summer. I am a 1st-year studying Mathematics and Computer Science at Imperial College 
London with a predicted 1st.  
I, like much of the computing world enjoy problem solving for problem solving’s sake, yet, I am 
driven by solving problems that actually matter. At GFG in Sydney, I built LST steel models from 
scratch, experimenting with architectures and parameters using fundamental factors to achieve RMSE 
< 0.03. I didn’t just copy some github repo, I learnt that there are factors, one cannot (easily) pull data 
for, for instance, I web scraped to get info on building operations in Australia due to the Olympics. I 
also have experience with data pipelines themselves. Furthermore, at CMT, I developed a full-stack 
emissions analyser using an LLM API and Pandas to classify descriptions by natural language with a 
9% uncertainty. Moreover, I have experience making sure stuff works as fast as possible! At Morgan 
Dental, I used SQL to reduce report generation by 40% across a 10 000 item database. I am proficient 
in Python, Haskell, PyTorch, TensorFlow and other relevant ML tools. My Kotlin knowledge is good 
too – aided by my current module in Kotlin at Uni for which I am averaging a 1st. Beyond work, I 
code for fun! I am currently prototyping an LSTM RNN-based reinforcement learning agent for a 
Python platformer.  
My CV demonstrates some of my technical competences, but it foregoes some of my soft skills, so I’ll 
go into those now. On a leadership level, I have experience as a school prefect; 1 of 4 treasurers in 
Imperial College; Chairman of the UK’s largest eco club after 9 years of participation; Orchestral lead 
for two years; and hockey captain for two years at a top 10 UK level. On a recreational level, I enjoy 
hiking (evidenced by my Gold DofE), table tennis (evidenced by my position in the county table 
tennis team) and playing poker (not that I’m any good). 
I’m also dedicated to the environment, which is why Treefera’s mission resonates with me so deeply. I 
love nature and have spent years translating that passion into action. I served as Chairman of the UK’s 
largest Eco Club through 9 years of service. This wasn't just a title; I led initiatives to plant a 
significant number of trees, including landscaping projects at local care homes and establishing eco-
trails to engage the community. We also focused on "first-mile" impact by achieving 'Green Flag' 
certification for energy optimizations, directly reducing our CO2 emissions. I understand the effort 
required to protect nature on the ground, and I want to apply my technical skills to help verify and 
scale that protection from space. 
 
Furthermore, I love collaborative environments. As founder and mentor of two online problem-
solving communities with over 50,000 members combined, I've learned how to coordinate diverse 
groups with different thinking methods. I believe the best solutions come from bouncing ideas off 
people who approach problems differently than you do.  
I look forward to hearing from you.  
Yours faithfully,  
Neel Agrawal
