Theorem 10.2.Assumef ′′(a)exists andfhas a local minimum ata. Thenf ′′(a)≥0.
Proof.We show it by contradiction. Assume:
(1)ais a local minimum;
(2)f ′′(a)<0.
Since a is a local minimum and f ′(a) exists, we must have f ′(a) = 0, i.e. a is a critical point.
Together withf ′′(a)<0, the previous theorem implies thatais a local maximum.
Therefore a is both a local minimum and a local maximum, so f is locally constant near a. In
particularf ′′(a) = 0, which contradictsf ′′(a)<0.□
11.Convexity
A good way to think about convexity is as a global version of the idea “the function bends
upward”. For C2 functions, this is exactly what the condition f ′′ ≥ 0 means. However, convexity
makes sense (and is extremely useful) even when no second derivative exists: it is ageometric
condition on the graph off.
11.1.Convex sets.
22
Definition 11.1(Convex set).A set U⊂R 2 is calledconvexif for every two points p, q∈U , the
straight line segment joining p and q is also contained in U. Equivalently, for every t∈ [0, 1] we have
(1−t)p+tq∈U.
It is often convenient to parametrise the segment fromptoqby
γ: [0,1]→R 2, γ(t) = (1−t)p+tq.
Example 11.2.The closed unit disk D = {x∈R 2 : |x|≤ 1} is convex: given p, q∈D , the entire
segment joining them lies in D. However, if we remove the origin, the set D\ { 0} isnotconvex.
Indeed, take p = (1/2, 0) and q = (−1/2, 0). Then p, q∈D\ { 0}, but the segment between them
contains the midpoint 0 = p+q
2 , which is not inD\ {0}.
11.2.Convex functions and three equivalent viewpoints.Let I⊂R be an interval and
f:I→R.
Definition 11.3(Epigraph).Theepigraphoffis the set
epi(f) :={(x, y)∈I×R:y≥f(x)} ⊂R 2.
Definition 11.4(Convex function: geometric definition).We say that f isconvexon I if epi(f) is
a convex set inR 2.
This definition is geometric: the region above the graph has no “dents”. Unwinding it yields two
concrete inequalities which are often easier to use.
Lemma 11.5(Jensen inequality).Letf:I→R. The following are equivalent:
(1)fis convex onIin the sense of Definition 11.4.
(2)For everya < binIand everyt∈[0,1],
(1)f((1−t)a+tb)≤(1−t)f(a) +tf(b).
Proof.Assumefis convex (epigraph definition). Consider the two points on the graph
P= (a, f(a)), Q= (b, f(b))∈epi(f).
Convexity of epi(f) implies the whole segment betweenPandQlies in epi(f). But
(1−t)P+tQ= ((1−t)a+tb,(1−t)f(a) +tf(b)),
so the y–coordinate on that segment must lieabovethe graph of f at x = (1 −t )a + tb. This is
exactly (1).
Conversely, assume (1). Take any two points ( x1, y1), (x2, y2) ∈epi (f), so y1 ≥f (x1) and
y2 ≥f(x 2). Then fort∈[0,1],
(1−t)y 1 +ty 2 ≥(1−t)f(x 1) +tf(x 2)≥f((1−t)x 1 +tx 2),
where the last inequality is (1). Hence (1 −t )(x1, y1) + t(x2, y2) ∈epi (f), so the epigraph is
convex. □
These concrete viewpoint using Jensen’s inequality is:the graph lies below each chord.
23
Definition 11.6(Chord line).Given a < b in I, we denote by ℓa,b the affine function whose graph
is the straight line through (a, f(a)) and (b, f(b)):
ℓa,b(x) := f(b)−f(a)
b−a (x−a) +f(a).
Lemma 11.7(Chord inequality).Letf:I→R. The following are equivalent:
(1)fis convex onI.
(2)For everya < binIand everyx∈[a, b],
(2)f(x)≤ℓ a,b(x).
Proof.Assume (1). Ifx∈[a, b], writex= (1−t)a+tbwitht= x−a
b−a ∈[0,1]. Then (1) becomes
f(x)≤(1−t)f(a) +tf(b) =ℓ a,b(x),
which is (2). Conversely, (2) implies (1) by the same change of variables.□
Remark(How to remember convexity).A function is convex if its graph liesbelowevery chord
joining two points of the graph. Equivalently, the regionabovethe graph is convex.
Example 11.8.The functions x7→x 2 and x7→e x are convex on R. The function x7→ |x| is also
convex onR(even though it is not differentiable at 0).
Exercise 11.9.Show directly from (1) that if f and g are convex on I, then so is f + g. Show also
that ifλ≥0, thenλfis convex.
Exercise 11.10.Letf, gbe convex onI. Prove thatx7→max{f(x), g(x)}is convex onI.
11.3.Strict convexity.
Definition 11.11(Strict convexity).We say that f : I→R isstrictly convexon I if for every
a < binIand everyt∈(0,1),
f((1−t)a+tb)<(1−t)f(a) +tf(b).
Equivalently, for everya < binIand everyx∈(a, b),
f(x)< ℓ a,b(x).
Remark.Strict convexity says the graph liesstrictlybelow every chord, except at the endpoints.
This is the reason strictly convex functions have very rigid minimisation behaviour (we will return
to this later).
11.4.A calculus criterion: monotonicity of f ′.Here is a simple mechanism that produces
convexity.
Theorem 11.12(Increasing derivative ⇒ convexity).Let I be an interval and let f : I→R be
differentiable.
(1)Iff ′ is increasing onI, thenfis convex onI.
(2)Iff ′ is strictly increasing onI, thenfis strictly convex onI.
24
Proof.Fixa < binIand consider the chord lineℓ a,b. Define
g(x) :=f(x)−ℓ a,b(x).
Thengis differentiable and
g(a) = 0, g(b) = 0, g ′(x) =f ′(x)−ℓ ′
a,b(x),
where ℓ′
a,b is the constant slope f(b)−f(a)
b−a . If f ′ is (strictly) increasing, then g′ is (strictly) increasing
as well.
We claim that g(x) ≤ 0 for all x∈ [a, b] (and g(x) < 0 for x∈ (a, b) in the strict case). Suppose
for contradiction that g takes a positive value on ( a, b). Since g is continuous on [ a, b], it attains a
maximum at some x0 ∈ [a, b], and that maximum is positive, so necessarily x0 ∈ (a, b). Then x0 is
an interior maximum, henceg ′(x0) = 0.
Because g′ is increasing, we have g′(x) ≤ 0 for all x < x 0 and g′(x) ≥ 0 for all x > x 0. In
particular,gis non-increasing on (a, x 0), so
g(x0)≤g(a) = 0,
contradicting g(x0) > 0. This proves g(x) ≤ 0 on [ a, b], i.e. f(x) ≤ℓ a,b(x) for all x∈ [a, b], which is
convexity.
In the strict case, the same argument shows g cannot attain 0 at an interior point unless g≡ 0 on
[a, b], which is impossible wheng ′ is strictly increasing. Henceg(x)<0 for allx∈(a, b), i.e. strict
convexity. □
Remark.This is the calculus reason behind the slogan “ f ′′ ≥ 0 means convex”: if f ′′ exists and
f ′′(x)≥0 onI, thenf ′ is increasing, hencefis convex by Theorem 11.12.
12.Some consequences of convexity
Throughout this section,I⊂Ris an interval andf:I→Ris convex.
12.1.The chord extension inequality.Convexity says that on [ a, b] ⊂I the graph liesbelow
the chord ℓa,b. Less obvious (and extremely useful) is that outside [ a, b] the inequality reverses: the
graph liesabovethe extension of the same chord.
Proposition 12.1(Chord extension inequality).Let f : I→R be convex and fix a < b in I. Then
ℓa,b(x)≤f(x)for allx∈I\[a, b].
Proof. We prove the case x > b (the case x < a is analogous). We proceed by contradiction. Assume
there exists x > b such that ℓa,b(x) > f (x), and consider the chord line ℓa,x through the two points
(a, f(a)) and (x, f(x)).
Since b∈ [a, x], convexity on [a, x] implies that the graph of f lies below the chord from a to x,
hence f(b) ≤ℓ a,x(b). On the other hand, the inequality ℓa,b(x) > f (x) can be rewritten geometrically
as ℓa,b(x) > ℓ a,x(x), because ℓa,x(x) = f(x). Now, both lines ℓa,b and ℓa,x pass through the common
point (a, f(a)), and at the point x > b > a the line ℓa,b lies strictlyabove ℓa,x. Therefore ℓa,b must
have strictly larger slope than ℓa,x. To see this, note that at x we have ℓa,b(x) = αa,b · (x−a ) + f(a)
25
and f(x) = ℓa,x(x) = αa,x ·(x−a )+ f(a). Since ℓa,b(x) > f (x) = ℓa,x(x) we conclude that αa,b > α a,x,
which implies thatℓ a,b is strictly larger thatℓ a,x on points larger thana.
In particular at b—the value of ℓa,b is strictly larger: ℓa,b(b) > ℓ a,x(b). But ℓa,b(b) = f(b) by
definition of the chord line through ( a, f(a)) and ( b, f(b)). Thus f(b) = ℓa,b(b) > ℓ a,x(b), which
contradicts the earlier inequalityf(b)≤ℓ a,x(b).
This contradiction shows that our assumption was false, and therefore ℓa,b(x) ≤f (x) for all
x > b. □
Exercise 12.2.Draw a picture that convinces you Proposition 12.1 must be true. Then prove
carefully the casex < a.
Exercise 12.3(Chord extension property implies convexity).Let I⊂R be an interval and let
f:I→Rbe a function. Assume that for everya < binI, ifℓ a,b denotes the chord line
ℓa,b(x) := f(b)−f(a)
b−a (x−a) +f(a),
thenfsatisfies thechord extension inequality
f(x)≥ℓ a,b(x)for allx∈I\[a, b].
Prove thatfis convex onI(i.e. show thatf(x)≤ℓ a,b(x)for everyx∈[a, b]).
Remark.An important consequence of the proposition above, is that convex functions are always
squeezed between chords:
f(x)≤ℓ a,b(x) forx∈[a, b], f(x)≥ℓ a,b(x) forx∈I\[a, b].
This “one chord, two inequalities” viewpoint is a very efficient tool.
12.2.Continuity of convex functions.
Theorem 12.4(Convex functions are continuous).Let f : I→R be convex. Then f is continuous
at every interior point ofI.
Proof.Letabe an interior point ofI. Chooseδ >0 such that [a−δ, a+δ]⊂I.
Left limit.Fixx∈[a−δ, a]. By convexity on [a−δ, a] we have
f(x)≤ℓ a−δ,a(x).
By the chord extension inequality (Proposition 12.1) applied to the chord from a to a + δ, we also
have
ℓa,a+δ(x)≤f(x),
becausex≤alies outside [a, a+δ]. Hence for allx∈[a−δ, a],
ℓa,a+δ(x)≤f(x)≤ℓ a−δ,a(x).
Both chord lines are continuous and satisfy ℓa,a+δ(a) = ℓa−δ,a(a) = f(a). Therefore, by the squeeze
theorem,
lim
x→a−
f(x) =f(a).
26
Right limit.The argument is identical: forx∈[a, a+δ] we have
ℓa−δ,a(x)≤f(x)≤ℓ a,a+δ(x),
and lettingx→a + yields limx→a+ f(x) =f(a).
Thusfis continuous ata.□
Remark.The proof is worth remembering: near an interior point a, convexity traps the graph
between two straight lines that meet at ( a, f(a)). An important subtlety of the proof is that the
hypothesis of a being interior was crucial so that we could extend the chords. Because of that the
argument does not work at the end points. Indeed, as the next exercise shows, the result is not true
at the boundary of an interval.
Exercise 12.5(A convex function with a discontinuity at an endpoint).Find an example of
a convex function f : [0, 1] →R which isnotcontinuous at0(in the sense that the right limit
limx→0+ f(x)does not equalf(0)), or which isnotcontinuous at1(i.e.lim x→1− f(x)̸=f(1)).
12.3.Monotonicity of secant slopes.
Proposition 12.6.Letf:I→Rbe convex. Then, for anya < b < cinI, we have
f(b)−f(a)

Therefore, for allx∈[b, c],

We now write both lines in terms ofx−b(this makes the comparison transparent):
ℓa,b(x) = f(b)−f(a)

Since ℓa,b(x) ≤ℓ b,c(x) for all x∈ [b, c] and x−b≥ 0 on this interval (with x−b > 0 as soon as

Example 12.8(A convex function with a corner).The function f(x) = |x| is convex on R. Its
derivative does not exist at 0, but Proposition 12.6 still applies: secant slopes increase as you move
to the right. This is one reason convexity is useful even without differentiability.
12.4.Consequences for derivatives and supporting lines.
Corollary 12.9(Derivatives of convex functions are increasing).Let f : I→R be convex. If f is
differentiable at two pointss < tin the interior ofI, then
f ′(s)≤f ′(t).
Proof. Apply Proposition 12.6 to the triples s−h < s < t and s < t < t + h (for h > 0 small), to get
f(s)−f(s−h)
h ≤ f(t)−f(s)
t−s ≤ f(t+h)−f(t)
h .
Lettingh→0 + yieldsf ′(s)≤f ′(t).□
A particularly nice geometric consequence is that at a differentiability point, the tangent line lies
below the graph.
Proposition 12.10(Supporting line inequality).Let f : I→R be convex and assume f is
differentiable at an interior pointx 0 ∈I. Then for everyx∈I,
f(x)≥f(x 0) +f ′(x0)(x−x 0).
Proof.Fixx̸=x 0. Apply Proposition 12.6 to the triple min{x, x 0}< x 0 <max{x, x 0}to obtain
f(x)−f(x 0)
x−x 0

which rearranges to the desired inequality.□
Remark.So convexity can be rephrased as:every tangent line (where it exists) is a global under-
estimate for the function.
Exercise 12.11(Uniqueness of critical points).Let f : I→R be strictly convex and differentiable.
Show that f has at most one critical point on I. Moreover, if f ′(c) = 0for some c∈I , then c is the
unique global minimiser offonI.
Remark.Geometrically, strict convexity says the graph lies strictly below every chord. This is why
minimisation for strictly convex functions is so rigid: there is at most one place where the slope can
vanish, and when it does, it must be the global minimum.
Exercise 12.12(Bounded convex functions have a limit).Let f : [0 ,∞ ) →R be convex and
bounded. Prove thatlim x→∞ f(x)exists and is finite.
Exercise 12.13(Derivative at infinity).In the setting of Exercise 12.12, assume in addition that f
is differentiable on(0,∞). Prove thatlim x→∞ f ′(x) = 0.
28
13.The Inverse Function Theorem
When working with functions on the real line, it is common to encounter functions which are
locally bijective. This occurs, for example, if f : (a, b) →R is differentiable and satisfies f ′(x) ̸= 0
for all x∈ (a, b). In this section, we study when the continuity and differentiability of f imply the
same properties for its inversef −1.
Definition 13.1.Let f : (a, b) → (c, d) be a bijection. We say that a function g : (c, d) → (a, b)
is its inverse if f(g(x)) = x for all x∈ (c, d) (equivalently, g(f(y)) = y for all y∈ (a, b)). In this
situation, we writeg=f −1.
Remark.The notation f −1(u)does notmean the same as 1
f(u) ; it denotes the inverse function
in the sense of mappings between sets.
As mentioned earlier, monotone functions provide natural examples of bijections between intervals.
In fact—when continuity is assumed—these are theonlybijective functions between intervals. This
follows from the Intermediate Value Theorem and is encapsulated in the following exercise.
Exercise 13.2(This is a good exercise to discuss with your personal tutor).Show that if f is
continuous and injective on an interval( x, y), then f is either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing
on that interval.
First, we check that continuity is preserved.
Theorem 13.3.Let f : (a, b) → (c, d)be a continuous bijection, then f −1 is also continuous on
(c, d).
Proof. By the previous theorem we can assume that f is strictly monotone on ( a, b). Moreover,
replacing f by −f in the argument if necessary, we can assume that f is strictly increasing on ( a, b).
Fix y0 ∈ (c, d). We denote by g := f −1 the inverse of f and x0 = g(y0). Clearly f(x0) = y0.
We want to show that given ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that if y satisfies |y−y 0|< δ , then
|g(y) −g (y0)|< ε . In other words, we want to find δ > 0 such that y∈ (y0 −δ, y 0 + δ) implies
g(y)∈(g(y 0)−ε, g(y 0) +ε) = (x 0 −ε, x 0 +ε).
By the previous exercise, f is monotone on ( a, b). Therefore it is enough to choose δ such that
(y0 −δ, y 0 + δ) is contained inside of the image of the interval ( x0 −ε, x 0 + ε). We claim that
δ = inf{f (x0 + ε) −y 0, y0 −f (x0 −ε ))} does the work. First, the monotonicity of f implies δ > 0.
Second, it also implies that if f(x0 −ε) ≤y 0 −δ < y < y 0 + δ = f(x0 + ε) then x0 −ε < g (y) < x0 + ε,
which is what we wanted to show.
□
Theorem 13.4.Let f be continuous and injective on an open interval I. Assume that f is
differentiable at x0 ∈I with f ′(x0) ̸= 0. Then f −1 is differentiable at f(x0)and( f −1)′(f(x0)) =
1/f ′(x0).
Proof.Lety 0 =f(x 0). We want to prove that the limit whenhgoes to 0 of
f −1(y0 +h)−f −1(y0)
h
29
is 1/f ′(x0). From the injectivity of f we know that for each small h there exists a unique k = k(h)
such that y0 + h = f(x0 + k). Moreover, the continuity of f −1 proven in the last theorem, means
that limh→0 k(h) = 0, since
y0 +h=f(x 0 +k) =⇒f −1(y0 +h) =x 0 +k.
At this stage we can rewrite the quotient in terms off:
f −1(y0 +h)−f −1(y0)
h = f −1(f(x 0 +k))−f −1(f(x 0))
f(x 0 +k)−y 0
= (x0 +k)−x 0
f(x 0 +k)−y 0
= k
f(x 0 +k)−f(x 0) .
Finally, since limh→0 k= 0 and lim k→0
f(x 0+k)−f(x 0)
k =f ′(x0)̸= 0 we obtain:
lim
h→0
f −1(y0 +h)−f −1(y0)
h = 1
f ′(x0) .
□
Theorem 13.5(The Inverse Function Theorem).Let I an open interval. Assume f : I→R is a
C1(I)function, i.e. f is differentiable on I and its derivative is continuous on I. Then, if f ′(a) ̸= 0
for a∈I , there exists δ > 0such that f : (a−δ, a + δ) →R is invertible and its inverse f −1 is
differentiable with(f −1)′(f(t)) = 1/f ′(t).
Marco A. M. Guaraco - Imperial College London

About these lecture notes
These LATEX notes are a living document and will be updated weekly as the course
develops. If you wish to consult a full version of the material, you can read the
handwritten notes of Fall 2024 available on Blackboard.
1.Limits of functions of a real variable
In this section, we discuss the behaviour of functions f : J→R , where J⊂R indicates an open
interval of the real line.
Definition 1.1.Given x0 ∈J and α∈R , we say that limx→x0 f(x0) = α, if for all ε > 0, there
existsδ=δ(ε)>0, such that
∀xwith 0<|x−x 0|< δ(ε) =⇒ |f(x)−α|< ε.
Exercise 1.2.Show that limx→x0 f(x) = α, if and only if, for every sequence {xn}n∈N ⊂J , with
xn ̸=x 0,
lim
n→∞
xn =x 0 =⇒lim
n→∞
f(x n) =α.
Proof of( =⇒). Let {xn}n∈N, be such thatlimn→∞ xn = x0. We want to show thatlimn→∞ f(xn) =
α. Fix ε > . From the definition of limit of a real function we know there is δ > 0 such that
|x−x 0|< δ implies |f(x) −α|< ε . On the other hand, from the definition of limit of a sequence,
there is N = N(δ) ∈N such that |xn −x 0|< δ as long as n > N . Summarizing, for each fixed ε > 0
we have selectedδandN(both depending on the fixedε) such that
n≥N=⇒ |x n −x 0|< δ=⇒ |f(x n)−α|< ϵ.
This proves that lim n→∞ f(x n) =α.□
Proof of(⇐=). We proceed by contradiction. Assume that limx→x0 f(x) is not α. In other words,
there exist ε > 0 such that for all δ > 0, there is x with |x−x 0|< δ and |f(x) −α|> ε . Since we are
free to choose δ > 0, we can define a sequence in the following way: for every n∈N , choose δ = 1/n,
and let xn be a real number such that |xn −x 0|< 1/n but |f(xn) −α|> ε . Notice, that on one hand
limn→∞ xn =x 0, howeverf(x n) is always far away fromα(sinceϵis fixed), then lim n→∞ f(x n) is
notα, which contradicts our hypothesis.□
Although the definition of limit of a function of a real variable is different than the definition of
limit of sequences, they do have many common properties.
Exercise 1.3.Let f : J→R and g : J→R , with x0 ∈J , limx→x0 f(x) = α and limx→x0 g(x) = β.
(1)Show thatlim x→x0(f+g)(x) =α+β.
(2)Show thatlim x→x0(f g)(x) =αβ.
(3) Show that if β̸= 0then there is an open interval J ′ ⊂J , such that x0 ∈J ′ and |g(x)|> β/2,
for allx∈J ′.
(4)Show that ifβ̸= 0, thenlim x→x0 ( f
g )(x) = α
β .
(5)Iflim x→x0|f(x)−g(x)|= 0thenα=β.
(6)Iff≤g, thenα≤β.
3
(7)The Squeeze Theorem: Show that iff≤h≤gandα=β, thenlim x→x0 h(x) =α=β.
Proof.
(6) Proceed by contradiction. Assuming α > β , let ε = (α−β )/2. Applying the definition of
limit to f, there is a δ > 0 such that f((x0 −δ, x 0 + δ)) ⊂ (α−ε, α + ε). Similarly, applying
the definition of limit to g, there is a δ′ > 0 such that g((x0 −δ ′, x0 + δ′)) ⊂ (β−ε, β + ε).
Let δ′′ = min{δ, δ′}. Then, for any x∈ (x0 −δ ′′, x0 + δ′′) we must have f(x) ∈ (α−ε, α + ε)
and g(x) ∈ (β−ε, β + ε). It follows that for this choice of x, f(x) > α−ε = (α + β)/2 =
β+ε > g(x), which contradicts our assumptionf≤g.
□
Exercise 1.4.Letlim x→x0 f(x) =y 0 andlim y→y0 g(y) =β. Show that
lim
x→x0
g(f(x)) =β.
Exercise 1.5.
(1)IfP:R→Ris a polynomial thenlim x→x0 P(x) =P(x 0).
(2)Definelim x→x0 f(x) = +∞.
(3) Show that if limx→x0 f(x) = +∞ then1 /f(x)is well defined in an open interval J ′ around
x0 andlim x→x0 1/f(x) = 0.
(4) If f : (a, b) →R is bounded from above and monotone increasing then limx→x0 f(x)exists
for allx 0 ∈(a, b).
(5)Show thatlim x→0 sin(1/x)does not exist.
(6)Show thatlim x→0 xsin(1/x)exists and is equal to zero.
(7)Show that, ifm∈N, then
lim
x→a
xm −a m
x−a =ma m−1.
(Hint: Try factoring the numerator.)
(8) Let f : (−1, 1) →R , defined by cases as f(x) = (−1)nn, if |x|∈ [ 1
n+1 , 1
n)and f(0) = 0. Show
thatlim x→0 f(x)does not exist.
(9) Let f : R→R , defined by cases as f(x) = 1, if x∈Q and f(x) = 0if x /∈Q. Show that for
allx 0 ∈R,lim x→x0 f(x)does not exist.
Some proofs and hints.
(5) Hint: Draw the graph of the function f. Then, study the sequence xn = ( π
2 + nπ)−1 and its
image byf.
(6) Hint: We know that limx→0 x = limx→0 −x = 0. Now notice −x≤f (x) ≤x . Can you use
part (7) of the previous exercise?
(8) Let us show that no α∈R is the limit of f(x) as x tends to 0. Let ε = 1 and δ = 1/n,
where n∈N is a natural number to be determined. From the definition of f it follows that
if x∈ (−δ, δ) then |f(x)|≥n . Now, choose n so that n >|α| +1. Then, for all x∈ (−δ, δ),
we must have ε = 1 <||f (x)|−|α||≤ |f (x) −α| . In particular, α is not the limit of f as x
goes to 0.
4
□
Definition 1.6.We say that a real functionf:J→Ris (monotone)
(1) non-decreasing: iff(x)≤f(y), for allx < y∈J.
(2) non-increasing: iff(x)≥f(y), for allx < y∈J.
(3) strictly increasing: iff(x)< f(y), for allx < y∈J.
(4) strictly decreasing: iff(x)> f(y), for allx < y∈J.
Exercise 1.7.Show that iffis strictly monotone inJ, thenfis injective.
2.One-sided limits
In this section, we discuss functions f : J→R , where J⊂R indicates an open interval of the
real line.
Definition 2.1.Givenx 0 ∈Jandα∈R, we say that
αis the limit offasxconverges tox 0 from the right (or from above),
and write lim x→x+
0
f(x) =α, if∀ε >0, there existsδ >0 such that
x∈(x 0, x0 +δ) =⇒ |f(x)−α|< ε.
Similarly,
Definition 2.2.Givenx 0 ∈Jandα∈R, we say that
αis the limit offasxconverges tox 0 from the left (or from below),
and write lim x→x−
0
f(x) =α, if∀ε >0, there existsδ >0 such that
x∈(x 0 −δ, x 0) =⇒ |f(x)−α|< ε.
Then the definition of limit can we summarized as
Exercise 2.3. limx→x0 f(x)exists if and only if both limx→x+
0
f(x)and limx→x−
0
f(x)exist and
coincide.
Exercise 2.4.Letf:J→Rbe a monotone function. Show that
(1)∀x 0 ∈J, bothlim x→x+
0
f(x)andlim x→x−
0
f(x)exists.
(2)Give an example of a monotonef, such thatlim x→x+
0
f(x)̸= lim x→x−

f(x)for somex 0.
3.Continuous Functions
Letf:J⊂R→R, whereJis an open interval, andx 0 ∈J.
Definition 3.1(Continuity V.1).We say thatfis continuous at the pointx 0 if
lim
x→x0
f(x) =f(x 0).
5
Definition 3.2(Continuity V.2).We say that fis continuous at the pointx 0 if ∀ε > 0, there is
δ=δ(ε)>0 such that
|x−x 0|< δ=⇒ |f(x)−f(x 0)|< ε.
Exercise 3.3.Show that the two definitions of continuity offatx 0 given above, are equivalent.
Exercise 3.4.Show that if f is continuous at x0 ∈J , and f(x0) ∈ (α, β), then there is δ > 0such
thatf(x)∈(α, β)for allx∈(x 0 −δ, x 0 +δ).
Proof. Let ε = min{ β−f(x 0)
2 , f(x 0)−α
2 }. Then ( f(x0) −ε, f (x0) + ε) ⊂ (α, β). On the other hand,
since the function is continuous, there exists a δ > 0 such that f(x0 −δ, x0 + δ) ⊂ (f(x0) −ε, x 0 + ε).
This proves the claim.□
Definition 3.5.We say thatf:J→Ris continuous iffis continuous at every point ofJ.
Theorem 3.6(Intermediate value theorem).Let f : [a, b] →R be a continuous function on[ a, b],
such thatf(a)< f(b). Iff(a)< η < f(b), there existsα∈(a, b)such thatf(α) =η.
Proof. Define A = A(η) = {x∈ [a, b] : f(x) < η}. Notice A̸=∅ since a∈A , and A is bounded from
above by definition (for example,bis an upper bound forA). Therefore, there existsα= supA.
Applying the same logic as in Exercise 3.4 at a and b, there exist δ > 0 such that f(x) < η for
x∈ [a, a + δ) and η < f (x) for x∈ (b−δ, b ]. This implies α must in the interior of the interval, i.e.
α∈(a, b).
The rest of the strategy is to show that f(α) = η. We can prove this by contradiction using
trichotomy. Firstly, assume that f(α) < η. In this case, again by Exercise 3.4, there exists δ > 0,
such that f(x) < η for x∈ [α, α + δ), which means [ α, α + δ) ⊂A contradicting α = supA . If
instead we assume that η < f (α), then the result from the exercise implies the existence of δ > 0
such that η < f (x) for x∈ (α−δ, α ]. Hence, A⊂ (−∞, a−δ ], meaning a−δ is an upper bound
forAsmaller thanα= supA, which is a contradiction. Finally, by trichotomy the only remaining
option is thatf(α) =η.□
Exercise 3.7.Show that every polynomial of odd degree must have a real root.
Exercise 3.8.Show that every polynomial P (x) = xn + an−1xn−1 + · · ·+ a1x + a0, where n∈N even
must have a global minimum, i.e. if there isx 0 ∈Rsuch thatP(x 0)≤P(y), for ally∈R.
Exercise 3.9.Show that if f : R→R is continuous at x0 and f assumes both positive and negative
values on every open interval containingx 0, thenf(x 0) = 0.
Theorem 3.10(Continuous functions are bounded on closed intervals).Let f : J→R be continuous.
If[a, b]⊂J, then there isM∈R, such thatf(x)≤Mfor allx∈[a, b].
Proof. Assume that f is not bounded in [ a, b], then there is a sequence xn ∈ [a, b], such that
limn→∞ f(xn) = + ∞. By Bolzano-Weierstrass xn has a convergent subsequence xnk, we have
limk→∞ xnk = x0 ∈ [a, b]. However, since f is continuous at x0, it follows limk→∞ f(xnk) = f(x0),
which contradicts limn→∞ f(x n) = +∞. Therefore,fmust be bounded in [a, b].□
Exercise 3.11.Show that a continuous function does not need to be bounded on an open interval.
6
Definition 3.12.Givenf:A→R, we say thatfhas a maximum value inA , (or also “fattains
its maximum inA”), if there isa∈Asuch thatf(a)≥f(a ′), for alla ′ ∈A.
Theorem 3.13(Extreme Value Theorem - Continuous functions have a maximum value on
closed intervals).Let f : J→R be continuous. Given[ a, b] ⊂J , there exists x∈ [a, b]such that
f(x) = max [a,b] f, i.e.f(x)≥f(y)for ally∈[a, b].
Proof. The proof is an exercise for you. First argue that there must be a sequence xn ∈ [a, b], which
is convergent and such that lim n→∞ f(x n) = sup{f(y) :y∈[a, b]}.□
Exercise 3.14.
(1)The sum of two continuous functions is continuous.
(2)The product of two continuous functions is continuous.
(3) If a function is continuous at a point and its value is not zero, then its multiplicative inverse
is continuous at the same point.
(4)The composition of two continuous functions is continuous.
Definition 3.15.We say that f: [a, b]→Ris continuous on [a, b] , if it is continuous on ( a, b) and
at the extremes we have lim x→a+ f(x) =f(a) and lim x→b− f(x) =f(b).
Exercise 3.16.Show that if f : [a, b] →R is continuous and injective, then f is monotone on[ a, b].
Proof. Since f is injective then either f(a) < f (b) or f(a) > f (b). Without loss of generality, assume
f(a) < f (b) (the proof for the case f(a) > f (b) is analogous). Notice that we must have f(a) < f (y),
for all y∈ (a, b). Otherwise, a < y < b but f(y) < f (a) < f (b) and by the intermediate value
theorem, the would be z∈ (y, b) such that f(z) = f(a). In addition, since a /∈(y, b) it follows that
z̸=a , contradicting that f is injective. By a symmetric argument we also have f(x) < f (b), for all
x∈(a, b).
Now, assume by contradiction that given x < y∈ (a, b) we have f(x) > f (y). By the previous
paragraph we have f(a) < f (y) < f (x) < f (b). Since a < x < y < b , by the intermediate value
theorem, there must be z∈ (y, b) such that f(z) = f(x). However, x /∈(y, b) so z̸=x , contradicting
thatfis injective.
We conclude that if f(a) < f (b), then f is actually strictly monotone increasing on all of [ a, b]. □
Exercise 3.17.Let f : [ a, b] → [f(a), f(b)]continuous and bijective, then its inverse f −1 :
[f(a), f(b)]→[a, b]is also continuous.
Proof. Since we are assuming f(a) < f (b), by the previous exercise we know that f is strictly
monotone increasing. Therefore, f −1 is also strictly monotone increasing. Given y0 ∈ (f(a), f(b)),
we will show thatf −1 is continuous aty 0.
Fix ε > 0. To simplify the notation denote x0 = f −1(y0). The function f is monotone increasing
so f(x0 −ε ) < y 0 < f (x0 + ε). We claim that choosing δ = min{f(x0 + ε) −y 0, y0 −f (x0 −ε )},
gives us
y∈(y 0 −δ, y 0 +δ) =⇒ |f −1(y)−f −1(y0)|< ε.
In fact, from the choice ofδ,
f(x 0 −ε)< y 0 −δ < y 0 +δ < f(x 0 +ε).
7
Therefore, given y∈ (y0 −δ, y 0 + δ), we have f(x0 −ε ) < y < f (x0 + ε). Since f −1 is monotone
increasing it follows that x0 −ε < f −1(y) < x0 + ε. From x0 = f −1(y0), this reads f −1(y0) −ε <
f −1(y)< f −1(y0) +ε, which is the same as
|f −1(y)−f −1(y0)|< ε.
Therefore,f −1 is continuous aty 0 ∈(a, b).
Continuity at y0 = a or y0 = b follows the same argument but checking only the corresponding
one sided limits. □
Exercise 3.18.
(1) Let f : (a, b) →R be continuous. Show that if f(x) ̸= 0, for all x∈ (a, b), then the values of
fare either all positive or all negative.
(2) Let f : [a, b] →R and g : [a, b] →R be continuous functions. Show that if g(a) < f (a)and
g(b)> f(b), then there isx 0 ∈(a, b)such thatf(x 0) =g(x 0).
4.Continuous functions and the topology of the Real Line
Definition 4.1.We say that a subset A⊂R is open, if for all a∈A , there exists δ = δ(a) > 0
such that (a−δ, a+δ)⊂A.
Exercise 4.2(Easy).Prove that a set A is open if and only if it is the union of a family of open
intervals{I α}α∈Γ, i.e.
A=
[
α∈Γ
Iα.
(Note that in the statement the set of indicesΓmight really large, e.g. it could be uncountable
infinite.)
Exercise 4.3(Challenging).It is possible to improve the conclusion of the previous exercise: prove
that every open set is the countable union of open intervals.
Definition 4.4.We say that a subset B⊂R is closed if its complement is open, i.e. B is closed if
and only ifR\Bis open.
Remark.By convention we assume that the empty set is both open and closed.
Exercise 4.5(Easy).
(1)Show that an open interval(a, b)is an open set.
(2)Show that a set containing exactly one point is a closed set.
(3)Show that a closed interval[a, b]is a closed set.
(4)Show that an interval of the form[a, b)is neither open or closed.
(5)Show that the setRis both open and closed.
In practice, it is often easy to recognise when a set is open or closed. For example:
Exercise 4.6.Let f : R→R be a continuous function and A⊂R be an open set. Prove that
f −1(A)is also open.
Exercise 4.7.Letf:R→Rbe a continuous function andp∈R. Prove thatf −1(p)is closed.
8
5.Differentiable Functions
We will begin the lecture with an informal discussion on the motivation behind the concept of
differentiable functions. Please refer to the handwritten notes for more details. Here, we begin
directly with the formal definition:
Definition 5.1.Let f : J→R , where J is an open interval. We say thatfis differentiable atx 0 ∈J ,
if the limit
f ′(x0) = lim
x→x0
f(x)−f(x 0)
x−x 0
exists. In such a case, we say thatf ′(x0) is the derivative offatx 0.
We could also phrase the definition of derivative as:
Exercise 5.2.Show that it is equivalent to define
f ′(x0) = lim
h→0
f(x 0 +h)−f(x 0)
h .
One of the most fundamental properties of differentiation is that it is, in a strict sense, a stronger
condition than continuity. In other words:
Proposition 5.3.Iffis differentiable atx 0, thenfis continuous atx 0.
Proof. From the definition of continuity it is enough to show limx→x0

follows from the algebra of limits

functions. In practice, we think of differentiable functions as behaving better than continuous ones;
for example, we often describe a differentiable function as being “smoother” or “more regular” than
a merely continuous function.

6.The Growth of a Function and Its Derivative
Interpreting the derivative as both the rate of change of a function and the slope of its tangent
line suggests a connection between the sign of the derivative and the behaviour of the function itself.
In this section, we make this connection precise in several settings: (1) when f ′(c) > 0 at a single
pointc∈R, (2) whenf ′(x)>0 for allxin an intervalI, and (3) whenf ′(c) = 0 at a pointc.
6.1.Local comparison when f ′(c) > 0at a single point.Let f : I→R and assume that f
is differentiable at a point c∈I with f ′(c) > 0. Note that we are not assuming f is differentiable
anywhere else on I. In this situation, the most we can conclude is a local comparison of the values
offnearc. More precisely:
12
Lemma 6.1.If f ′(c) > 0, then there exists δ > 0such that for every x∈ (c−δ, c )and y∈ (c, c + δ)
we have
f(x)< f(c)< f(y).
Proof.Sincef ′(c)>0, we have
lim
h→0
f(c+h)−f(c)
h =f ′(c)>0.
By the definition of the limit, there existsδ >0 such that
f(c+h)−f(c)
h >0
for all h∈ (−δ, δ) \ {0}. If h > 0, then f(c + h) −f (c) > 0, so f(c + h) > f (c); if h < 0, then
f(c + h) −f (c) < 0, so f(c + h) < f (c). Writing x = c + h for h < 0 and y = c + h for h > 0 gives
the result. □
Remark.Note that f ′(c) > 0 at a single point DOES NOT imply monotonicity on an interval
(c−δ, c + δ). In other words, on a sufficiently small neighbourhood of c, values of f to the left of c lie
below f(c), while values of f to the right lie above f(c). This shows that a positive derivative at a
point forces the function to “tilt upward” through that point, even without assuming differentiability
elsewhere.
In this lecture we collect a few ways in which the derivative carries information about a function.
The guiding idea is simple: the sign (and size) of f ′(x) tells us how f is behavingnear x, and—under
stronger hypotheses—this can be promoted to global information.
6.2.Local comparison at a single point.(Here we donotassume any differentiability away
from the pointc.)
Lemma 6.2(Local comparison when f ′(c) > 0).Let f : I→R and assume that f is differentiable
at a point c∈I with f ′(c) > 0. Then there exists δ > 0such that for every x∈ (c−δ, c )and
y∈(c, c+δ)we have
f(x)< f(c)< f(y).
Proof.Sincef ′(c)>0, we have
lim
h→0
f(c+h)−f(c)
h =f ′(c)>0.
By the definition of the limit, there existsδ >0 such that
f(c+h)−f(c)
h >0
for all h∈ (−δ, δ) \ {0}. If h > 0, then f(c + h) −f (c) > 0, hence f(c + h) > f (c). If h < 0, then
f(c + h) −f (c) < 0, hence f(c + h) < f (c). Writing x = c + h for h < 0 and y = c + h for h > 0
gives the result. □
Remark.Intuitively, f ′(c) > 0 forces the graph to “tilt upward” as it passes through c, even
if the function misbehaves elsewhere. However, note that a positive derivative at a single point
DOES NOT imply that f is monotone on any interval around that point. What we do get is a clean
13
one-sided comparison: sufficiently close to c, values to the left lie below f(c) and values to the right
lie abovef(c).

6.3.The derivative can indicate global growth.The previous lemma was purely local. Now
we assume differentiability on an interval and ask for a global conclusion.
Proposition 6.6.Letf: (a, b)→Rbe differentiable and assume that
f ′(t)>0for allt∈(a, b).
Thenfis strictly increasing on(a, b).
Proof. Fix x∈ (a, b). We want to show that for every y∈ (x, b) we have f(x) < f (y). Suppose not.
Then there existsy 0 ∈(x, b) such thatf(y 0)≤f(x).
Define
A:={y∈(x, b) :f(y)≤f(x)}.
By assumptionAis nonempty (it containsy 0) and it is bounded below byx. Let
α:= infA.
Claim 1: f(α) ≤f (x).Since α = infA , there exists a sequence an ∈A with an →α . Since f is
differentiable atα, it is continuous atα, hence

n→∞
f(a n)≤f(x),
because eacha n ∈A. This finishes the proof of Claim 1.
14
Claim 2: x < α .Since f ′(x) > 0, the local comparison lemma gives a δ > 0 such that f(y) > f (x)
for all y∈ (x, x + δ). In particular, no point of ( x, x + δ) belongs to A, so α≥x + δ > x . This
finishes the proof of Claim 2.
Finally, to get a contradiction, we apply the local comparison lemma at α (since f ′(α) > 0): there
existsη >0 such that for everyy∈(α−η, α) we have
f(y)< f(α)≤f(x).
Hence every y∈ (α−η, α ) with y > x belongs to A. But those y are strictly smaller than α,
contradicting the fact that α is a lower bound for A (indeed, it is the greatest lower bound). This
contradiction shows our initial assumption was false, so f(y) > f (x) for all y > x . Therefore f is
strictly increasing. □

6.4.The derivative helps locate maxima and minima.One of the most useful applications of
derivatives is tonarrow downwhere extrema can occur.
Definition 6.10(Local extremum).We say that f attains alocal maximumat c if there exists
δ >0 such that
f(c)≥f(x) for allx∈(c−δ, c+δ).
Similarly,fattains alocal minimumatcif there existsδ >0 such that
f(c)≤f(x) for allx∈(c−δ, c+δ).
Iffhas a local maximum atc, then forx > cclose tocwe havef(x)−f(c)≤0, hence
f(x)−f(c)
x−c ≤0 (x > c).
Forx < cclose tocwe havef(x)−f(c)≤0 but nowx−c <0, so dividing flips the inequality:
f(x)−f(c)
x−c ≥0 (x < c).
So the difference quotients approachcfrom opposite sides with opposite signs.
Theorem 6.11(Fermat’s theorem).If f attains a local maximum or a local minimum at c and f
is differentiable atc, then
f ′(c) = 0.
Proof. Assume f has a local maximum at c (the minimum case is analogous). For x→c + we have
f(x)−f(c)
x−c ≤0,
15
and forx→c − we have
f(x)−f(c)
x−c ≥0.
Iffis differentiable atc, then both one-sided limits exist and coincide withf ′(c). Therefore
0≤f ′(c)≤0,
hencef ′(c) = 0. □
Remark.Be careful: although maxima and minima of differentiable functions satisfy f ′(c) = 0, the
converse is false. For instance, for f(x) = x3 we have f ′(0) = 0, but 0 is neither a local maximum
nor a local minimum (it is a “saddle point”).
Definition 6.12(Critical point).A pointcis called acritical pointoffiff ′(c) = 0.
Remark.In optimisation problems, candidates for maxima/minima are often among the set of
critical points. (If you’re curious: this point of view sits behind theleast action principlein physics,
and even behindgradient descentmethods in machine learning.) Of course, after locating critical
points one typically needs additional information to decide whether they are maxima, minima, or
neither.
6.5.A historical example: The law of light refraction.We finish with a historically important
example showing how “minimising something” naturally leads to an equation involving a derivative.
Statement 1(Snell’s law).Let M1 and M2 be two media separated by a straight line (e.g. the
interface between air and water). Assume the speed of light is v1 in M1 and v2 in M2. If a light ray
travels from a point P∈M 1 to a point Q∈M 2 and crosses the interface making angles θ1 and θ2
with the normal, then
sinθ 1

Remark(Fermat’s insight).Fermat derived Snell’s law from a general variational principle: light
chooses the path that minimises travel time.
Statement 2(Fermat’s principle).The path light takes to travel between two points P and Q is
the path of least time.

7.Existence of critical points
Let f : [a, b] →R be continuous on [ a, b] and differentiable on ( a, b). We proved (Extreme Value
Theorem) that continuous functions on compact sets always attain a maximum and a minimum.
A natural question is:can we use this to guarantee the existence of a critical point?
We have to be careful: local minima and maxima are critical pointsonly when the point is interior.
At the endpoints, there is no reason for the derivative to vanish.
Example 7.1.Let f : [a, b] →R be given by f(x) = x. Then f(a) is the minimum and f(b) is the
maximum on [a, b], but there is not∈(a, b) withf ′(t) = 0 (in factf ′(x) = 1 everywhere).
Nevertheless, in several situations wecanguarantee the existence of critical points. The cleanest
one is when the function starts and ends at the same height.
7.1.Critical points whenf(a) =f(b).
Theorem 7.2(Rolle’s Theorem).Let f : [a, b] →R be continuous on[ a, b]and differentiable on
(a, b). Iff(a) =f(b), then there existst∈(a, b)such that
f ′(t) = 0.
Proof.By the Extreme Value Theorem, there existst∈[a, b] such that
f(t) = max{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
Ift∈(a, b) is an interior point, thentis a local maximum, hencef ′(t) = 0 (Fermat’s theorem).
Iftis not interior, thent=aort=b. But sincef(a) =f(b), we must have
f(a) =f(b) = max{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
Apply the Extreme Value Theorem again to obtainx∈[a, b] such that
f(x) = min{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
Ifx∈(a, b), thenxis an interior local minimum and againf ′(x) = 0.
Ifxis not interior, thenx=aorx=b, so
f(a) = min{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}andf(a) = max{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
This means min[a,b] f = max[a,b] f, so f is constant on [a, b]. In that case f ′(t) = 0 for every t∈ (a, b),
and we are done. □
Remark.The picture to keep in mind is: if f(a) = f(b), then as you slide a horizontal line down
from above, it will touch the graph for the first time at some interior point, producing a horizontal
tangent.
17
7.2.Critical points when f ′(x) > 0 > f ′(y).There is another common mechanism that forces a
critical point: the derivative being positive at one point and negative at another. In this case, the
picture to keep in mind is that of a dome (or bowl depending on the signs).
Proposition 7.3.Let f : (a, b) →R be differentiable on( a, b). Assume x < y in( a, b)are such that
f ′(x)>0> f ′(y).
Then there existst∈(x, y)such thatf ′(t) = 0.
Proof. Since f ′(x) > 0, the local comparison lemma implies that there exists δ > 0 (small enough
so thatx+δ < y) such that for allp∈(x, x+δ) we have
f(p)> f(x).
In particular,fdoes not attain its maximum on [x, y] at the left endpointx.
Similarly, since f ′(y) < 0, there exists δ′ > 0 (small enough so that x < y−δ ′) such that for all
q∈(y−δ ′, y) we have
f(q)> f(y).
Sofdoes not attain its maximum on [x, y] at the right endpointyeither.
On the other hand, f is continuous on [ x, y] (because it is differentiable on ( a, b)), so by the
Extreme Value Theorem there existst∈[x, y] such that
f(t) = max{f(s) :s∈[x, y]}.
Since the maximum cannot occur at x or y, we must have t∈ (x, y). Thus t is an interior maximum
and Fermat’s theorem givesf ′(t) = 0.□
8.Two important applications
Even when f(a) ̸=f (b) we can still say something useful about the derivative. The key idea is:
subtract the straight line through (a, f(a)) and (b, f(b)) to reduce to Rolle’s theorem.
8.1.Mean Value Theorem.
Theorem 8.1(Mean Value Theorem).Let f : [a, b] →R be continuous on[ a, b]and differentiable
on(a, b). Then there existst∈(a, b)such that
f ′(t) = f(b)−f(a)
b−a .
Proof.Define the affine function (the secant line through the endpoints)
g(t) := f(b)−f(a)
b−a (t−a) +f(a).
Thengis continuous on [a, b], differentiable on (a, b), and satisfiesg(a) =f(a) andg(b) =f(b).
Therefore the functionh:=f−gis continuous on [a, b], differentiable on (a, b), and
h(a) =f(a)−g(a) = 0, h(b) =f(b)−g(b) = 0.
18
By Rolle’s theorem, there existst∈(a, b) such thath ′(t) = 0. But
h′(t) =f ′(t)−g ′(t), g ′(t) = f(b)−f(a)
b−a ,
soh ′(t) = 0 is exactly
f ′(t) = f(b)−f(a)
b−a .
□
Corollary 8.2.Iff ′(t)>0for allt∈(a, b), thenfis strictly increasing on(a, b).
Proof. Choose x < y in (a, b). Apply Cauchy’s Mean Value Theorem to f on [x, y] to find t∈ (x, y)
such that
f(y)−f(x)
y−x =f ′(t).
Since f ′(t) > 0 and y−x > 0, we get f(y) −f (x) > 0, hence f(x) < f (y). This holds for every
x < y, sofis strictly increasing.□
Exercise 8.3.Let f : J→R be a differentiable function. Show that if f ′(x) = 0, for all x∈J ,
then f is constant on J. (Note that this is the converse of Exercise 5.7 - Part (2). Hint: Use the
mean value theorem.)
8.2.The Intermediate Value Theorem for derivatives (Darboux).One might expect the
derivative to be continuous. This isfalsein general. However, a surprising feature remains true:
derivatives cannot jump over values.
Theorem 8.4(Intermediate Value Property for derivatives).Let f : (a, b) →R be differentiable
on( a, b). Then f ′ satisfies the intermediate value property: for every x < y in( a, b)and every real
numbercbetweenf ′(x)andf ′(y), there existst∈(x, y)such that
f ′(t) =c.
Remark.This holds even whenf ′ is not continuous.
Proof.Fixx < yand letcbe betweenf ′(x) andf ′(y). Consider
φ(t) :=f(t)−ct.
Thenφis differentiable on (a, b) and
φ′(t) =f ′(t)−c.
Since c lies between f ′(x) and f ′(y), the numbers φ′(x) and φ′(y) have opposite signs (or one is 0
already). Ifφ ′(x) = 0 orφ ′(y) = 0 we are done. Otherwise,
φ′(x)>0> φ ′(y) orφ ′(x)<0< φ ′(y).
Applying the previous proposition to φ on (x, y) gives a point t∈ (x, y) such that φ′(t) = 0. Hence
f ′(t)−c= 0, i.e.f ′(t) =c.□
Exercise 8.5.Find an example of a function that satisfies the Intermediate Value Property but it
is not continuous.
19
Corollary 8.6(Nonvanishing derivative implies strict monotonicity).Show that if f : (a, b) →R is
differentiable and
f ′(t)̸= 0for allt∈(a, b),
thenfis strictly monotone on(a, b).
Proof.By the Intermediate Value Theorem for derivatives,f ′ has the intermediate value property.
If f ′ took both positive and negative values, then it would also take the value 0 somewhere in
between. Since f ′(t) ̸= 0 everywhere, this cannot happen. Therefore either f ′(t) > 0 for all t or
f ′(t)<0 for allt.
In the first case, the corollary to Cauchy’s Mean Value Theorem shows that f is strictly increasing;
in the second case, applying the same result to−fshows thatfis strictly decreasing.□
Exercise 8.7(Functions with unit-speed derivative).Let J⊂R be an interval and let f : J→R
be differentiable. Assume that
|f ′(t)|= 1for allt∈J.
Prove that there exists a constantc∈Rsuch that either
f(t) =t+cfor allt∈J,
or
f(t) =−t+cfor allt∈J.
This fact will be used to show that an arc-length parametrisation of a curve is unique, up to a
translation of the parameter (and possibly reversing orientation).

10.1.What does it mean to sayf ′′(x)exists?When we say “f ′′(x) exists”, we are assuming:

10.2.Second derivative test.
Theorem 10.1.Letabe a critical point off, i.e.f ′(a) = 0.
(1)Iff ′′(a)>0thenfhas a local minimum ata.

(2)Iff ′′(a)<0thenfhas a local maximum ata.

Theorem 10.2.Assumef ′′(a)exists andfhas a local minimum ata. Thenf ′′(a)≥0.
Proof.We show it by contradiction. Assume:
(1)ais a local minimum;
(2)f ′′(a)<0.

Therefore a is both a local minimum and a local maximum, so f is locally constant near a. In

11.Convexity
A good way to think about convexity is as a global version of the idea “the function bends
upward”. For C2 functions, this is exactly what the condition f ′′ ≥ 0 means. However, convexity
makes sense (and is extremely useful) even when no second derivative exists: it is ageometric
condition on the graph off.
11.1.Convex sets.
22
Definition 11.1(Convex set).A set U⊂R 2 is calledconvexif for every two points p, q∈U , the
straight line segment joining p and q is also contained in U. Equivalently, for every t∈ [0, 1] we have
(1−t)p+tq∈U.
It is often convenient to parametrise the segment fromptoqby
γ: [0,1]→R 2, γ(t) = (1−t)p+tq.
Example 11.2.The closed unit disk D = {x∈R 2 : |x|≤ 1} is convex: given p, q∈D , the entire
segment joining them lies in D. However, if we remove the origin, the set D\ { 0} isnotconvex.
Indeed, take p = (1/2, 0) and q = (−1/2, 0). Then p, q∈D\ { 0}, but the segment between them
contains the midpoint 0 = p+q
2 , which is not inD\ {0}.
11.2.Convex functions and three equivalent viewpoints.Let I⊂R be an interval and
f:I→R.
Definition 11.3(Epigraph).Theepigraphoffis the set
epi(f) :={(x, y)∈I×R:y≥f(x)} ⊂R 2.
Definition 11.4(Convex function: geometric definition).We say that f isconvexon I if epi(f) is
a convex set inR 2.
This definition is geometric: the region above the graph has no “dents”. Unwinding it yields two
concrete inequalities which are often easier to use.
Lemma 11.5(Jensen inequality).Letf:I→R. The following are equivalent:
(1)fis convex onIin the sense of Definition 11.4.
(2)For everya < binIand everyt∈[0,1],
(1)f((1−t)a+tb)≤(1−t)f(a) +tf(b).
Proof.Assumefis convex (epigraph definition). Consider the two points on the graph
P= (a, f(a)), Q= (b, f(b))∈epi(f).
Convexity of epi(f) implies the whole segment betweenPandQlies in epi(f). But
(1−t)P+tQ= ((1−t)a+tb,(1−t)f(a) +tf(b)),
so the y–coordinate on that segment must lieabovethe graph of f at x = (1 −t )a + tb. This is
exactly (1).
Conversely, assume (1). Take any two points ( x1, y1), (x2, y2) ∈epi (f), so y1 ≥f (x1) and
y2 ≥f(x 2). Then fort∈[0,1],
(1−t)y 1 +ty 2 ≥(1−t)f(x 1) +tf(x 2)≥f((1−t)x 1 +tx 2),
where the last inequality is (1). Hence (1 −t )(x1, y1) + t(x2, y2) ∈epi (f), so the epigraph is
convex. □
These concrete viewpoint using Jensen’s inequality is:the graph lies below each chord.
23
Definition 11.6(Chord line).Given a < b in I, we denote by ℓa,b the affine function whose graph
is the straight line through (a, f(a)) and (b, f(b)):
ℓa,b(x) := f(b)−f(a)
b−a (x−a) +f(a).
Lemma 11.7(Chord inequality).Letf:I→R. The following are equivalent:
(1)fis convex onI.
(2)For everya < binIand everyx∈[a, b],
(2)f(x)≤ℓ a,b(x).
Proof.Assume (1). Ifx∈[a, b], writex= (1−t)a+tbwitht= x−a
b−a ∈[0,1]. Then (1) becomes
f(x)≤(1−t)f(a) +tf(b) =ℓ a,b(x),
which is (2). Conversely, (2) implies (1) by the same change of variables.□
Remark(How to remember convexity).A function is convex if its graph liesbelowevery chord
joining two points of the graph. Equivalently, the regionabovethe graph is convex.
Example 11.8.The functions x7→x 2 and x7→e x are convex on R. The function x7→ |x| is also
convex onR(even though it is not differentiable at 0).
Exercise 11.9.Show directly from (1) that if f and g are convex on I, then so is f + g. Show also
that ifλ≥0, thenλfis convex.
Exercise 11.10.Letf, gbe convex onI. Prove thatx7→max{f(x), g(x)}is convex onI.
11.3.Strict convexity.
Definition 11.11(Strict convexity).We say that f : I→R isstrictly convexon I if for every
a < binIand everyt∈(0,1),
f((1−t)a+tb)<(1−t)f(a) +tf(b).
Equivalently, for everya < binIand everyx∈(a, b),
f(x)< ℓ a,b(x).
Remark.Strict convexity says the graph liesstrictlybelow every chord, except at the endpoints.
This is the reason strictly convex functions have very rigid minimisation behaviour (we will return
to this later).
11.4.A calculus criterion: monotonicity of f ′.Here is a simple mechanism that produces
convexity.
Theorem 11.12(Increasing derivative ⇒ convexity).Let I be an interval and let f : I→R be
differentiable.
(1)Iff ′ is increasing onI, thenfis convex onI.
(2)Iff ′ is strictly increasing onI, thenfis strictly convex onI.
24
Proof.Fixa < binIand consider the chord lineℓ a,b. Define
g(x) :=f(x)−ℓ a,b(x).
Thengis differentiable and
g(a) = 0, g(b) = 0, g ′(x) =f ′(x)−ℓ ′
a,b(x),
where ℓ′
a,b is the constant slope f(b)−f(a)
b−a . If f ′ is (strictly) increasing, then g′ is (strictly) increasing
as well.
We claim that g(x) ≤ 0 for all x∈ [a, b] (and g(x) < 0 for x∈ (a, b) in the strict case). Suppose
for contradiction that g takes a positive value on ( a, b). Since g is continuous on [ a, b], it attains a
maximum at some x0 ∈ [a, b], and that maximum is positive, so necessarily x0 ∈ (a, b). Then x0 is
an interior maximum, henceg ′(x0) = 0.
Because g′ is increasing, we have g′(x) ≤ 0 for all x < x 0 and g′(x) ≥ 0 for all x > x 0. In
particular,gis non-increasing on (a, x 0), so
g(x0)≤g(a) = 0,
contradicting g(x0) > 0. This proves g(x) ≤ 0 on [ a, b], i.e. f(x) ≤ℓ a,b(x) for all x∈ [a, b], which is
convexity.
In the strict case, the same argument shows g cannot attain 0 at an interior point unless g≡ 0 on
[a, b], which is impossible wheng ′ is strictly increasing. Henceg(x)<0 for allx∈(a, b), i.e. strict
convexity. □
Remark.This is the calculus reason behind the slogan “ f ′′ ≥ 0 means convex”: if f ′′ exists and
f ′′(x)≥0 onI, thenf ′ is increasing, hencefis convex by Theorem 11.12.
12.Some consequences of convexity
Throughout this section,I⊂Ris an interval andf:I→Ris convex.
12.1.The chord extension inequality.Convexity says that on [ a, b] ⊂I the graph liesbelow
the chord ℓa,b. Less obvious (and extremely useful) is that outside [ a, b] the inequality reverses: the
graph liesabovethe extension of the same chord.
Proposition 12.1(Chord extension inequality).Let f : I→R be convex and fix a < b in I. Then
ℓa,b(x)≤f(x)for allx∈I\[a, b].
Proof. We prove the case x > b (the case x < a is analogous). We proceed by contradiction. Assume
there exists x > b such that ℓa,b(x) > f (x), and consider the chord line ℓa,x through the two points
(a, f(a)) and (x, f(x)).
Since b∈ [a, x], convexity on [a, x] implies that the graph of f lies below the chord from a to x,
hence f(b) ≤ℓ a,x(b). On the other hand, the inequality ℓa,b(x) > f (x) can be rewritten geometrically
as ℓa,b(x) > ℓ a,x(x), because ℓa,x(x) = f(x). Now, both lines ℓa,b and ℓa,x pass through the common
point (a, f(a)), and at the point x > b > a the line ℓa,b lies strictlyabove ℓa,x. Therefore ℓa,b must
have strictly larger slope than ℓa,x. To see this, note that at x we have ℓa,b(x) = αa,b · (x−a ) + f(a)
25
and f(x) = ℓa,x(x) = αa,x ·(x−a )+ f(a). Since ℓa,b(x) > f (x) = ℓa,x(x) we conclude that αa,b > α a,x,
which implies thatℓ a,b is strictly larger thatℓ a,x on points larger thana.
In particular at b—the value of ℓa,b is strictly larger: ℓa,b(b) > ℓ a,x(b). But ℓa,b(b) = f(b) by
definition of the chord line through ( a, f(a)) and ( b, f(b)). Thus f(b) = ℓa,b(b) > ℓ a,x(b), which
contradicts the earlier inequalityf(b)≤ℓ a,x(b).
This contradiction shows that our assumption was false, and therefore ℓa,b(x) ≤f (x) for all
x > b. □
Exercise 12.2.Draw a picture that convinces you Proposition 12.1 must be true. Then prove
carefully the casex < a.
Exercise 12.3(Chord extension property implies convexity).Let I⊂R be an interval and let
f:I→Rbe a function. Assume that for everya < binI, ifℓ a,b denotes the chord line
ℓa,b(x) := f(b)−f(a)
b−a (x−a) +f(a),
thenfsatisfies thechord extension inequality
f(x)≥ℓ a,b(x)for allx∈I\[a, b].
Prove thatfis convex onI(i.e. show thatf(x)≤ℓ a,b(x)for everyx∈[a, b]).
Remark.An important consequence of the proposition above, is that convex functions are always
squeezed between chords:
f(x)≤ℓ a,b(x) forx∈[a, b], f(x)≥ℓ a,b(x) forx∈I\[a, b].
This “one chord, two inequalities” viewpoint is a very efficient tool.
12.2.Continuity of convex functions.
Theorem 12.4(Convex functions are continuous).Let f : I→R be convex. Then f is continuous
at every interior point ofI.
Proof.Letabe an interior point ofI. Chooseδ >0 such that [a−δ, a+δ]⊂I.
Left limit.Fixx∈[a−δ, a]. By convexity on [a−δ, a] we have
f(x)≤ℓ a−δ,a(x).
By the chord extension inequality (Proposition 12.1) applied to the chord from a to a + δ, we also
have
ℓa,a+δ(x)≤f(x),
becausex≤alies outside [a, a+δ]. Hence for allx∈[a−δ, a],
ℓa,a+δ(x)≤f(x)≤ℓ a−δ,a(x).
Both chord lines are continuous and satisfy ℓa,a+δ(a) = ℓa−δ,a(a) = f(a). Therefore, by the squeeze
theorem,
lim
x→a−
f(x) =f(a).
26
Right limit.The argument is identical: forx∈[a, a+δ] we have
ℓa−δ,a(x)≤f(x)≤ℓ a,a+δ(x),
and lettingx→a + yields limx→a+ f(x) =f(a).
Thusfis continuous ata.□
Remark.The proof is worth remembering: near an interior point a, convexity traps the graph
between two straight lines that meet at ( a, f(a)). An important subtlety of the proof is that the
hypothesis of a being interior was crucial so that we could extend the chords. Because of that the
argument does not work at the end points. Indeed, as the next exercise shows, the result is not true
at the boundary of an interval.
Exercise 12.5(A convex function with a discontinuity at an endpoint).Find an example of

limx→0+ f(x)does not equalf(0)), or which isnotcontinuous at1(i.e.lim x→1− f(x)̸=f(1)).
12.3.Monotonicity of secant slopes.
Proposition 12.6.Letf:I→Rbe convex. Then, for anya < b < cinI, we have

Therefore, for allx∈[b, c],

Example 12.8(A convex function with a corner).The function f(x) = |x| is convex on R. Its
derivative does not exist at 0, but Proposition 12.6 still applies: secant slopes increase as you move
to the right. This is one reason convexity is useful even without differentiability.
12.4.Consequences for derivatives and supporting lines.
Corollary 12.9(Derivatives of convex functions are increasing).Let f : I→R be convex. If f is
differentiable at two pointss < tin the interior ofI, then
f ′(s)≤f ′(t).
Proof. Apply Proposition 12.6 to the triples s−h < s < t and s < t < t + h (for h > 0 small), to get

A particularly nice geometric consequence is that at a differentiability point, the tangent line lies

Exercise 12.11(Uniqueness of critical points).Let f : I→R be strictly convex and differentiable.
Show that f has at most one critical point on I. Moreover, if f ′(c) = 0for some c∈I , then c is the

Remark.Geometrically, strict convexity says the graph lies strictly below every chord. This is why
minimisation for strictly convex functions is so rigid: there is at most one place where the slope can

13.The Inverse Function Theorem
When working with functions on the real line, it is common to encounter functions which are
locally bijective. This occurs, for example, if f : (a, b) →R is differentiable and satisfies f ′(x) ̸= 0
for all x∈ (a, b). In this section, we study when the continuity and differentiability of f imply the
same properties for its inversef −1.
Definition 13.1.Let f : (a, b) → (c, d) be a bijection. We say that a function g : (c, d) → (a, b)
is its inverse if f(g(x)) = x for all x∈ (c, d) (equivalently, g(f(y)) = y for all y∈ (a, b)). In this
situation, we writeg=f −1.
Remark.The notation f −1(u)does notmean the same as 1
f(u) ; it denotes the inverse function
in the sense of mappings between sets.
As mentioned earlier, monotone functions provide natural examples of bijections between intervals.
In fact—when continuity is assumed—these are theonlybijective functions between intervals. This
follows from the Intermediate Value Theorem and is encapsulated in the following exercise.
Exercise 13.2(This is a good exercise to discuss with your personal tutor).Show that if f is
continuous and injective on an interval( x, y), then f is either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing
on that interval.
First, we check that continuity is preserved.
Theorem 13.3.Let f : (a, b) → (c, d)be a continuous bijection, then f −1 is also continuous on
(c, d).
Proof. By the previous theorem we can assume that f is strictly monotone on ( a, b). Moreover,
replacing f by −f in the argument if necessary, we can assume that f is strictly increasing on ( a, b).
Fix y0 ∈ (c, d). We denote by g := f −1 the inverse of f and x0 = g(y0). Clearly f(x0) = y0.
We want to show that given ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that if y satisfies |y−y 0|< δ , then
|g(y) −g (y0)|< ε . In other words, we want to find δ > 0 such that y∈ (y0 −δ, y 0 + δ) implies
g(y)∈(g(y 0)−ε, g(y 0) +ε) = (x 0 −ε, x 0 +ε).
By the previous exercise, f is monotone on ( a, b). Therefore it is enough to choose δ such that
(y0 −δ, y 0 + δ) is contained inside of the image of the interval ( x0 −ε, x 0 + ε). We claim that
δ = inf{f (x0 + ε) −y 0, y0 −f (x0 −ε ))} does the work. First, the monotonicity of f implies δ > 0.
Second, it also implies that if f(x0 −ε) ≤y 0 −δ < y < y 0 + δ = f(x0 + ε) then x0 −ε < g (y) < x0 + ε,
which is what we wanted to show.
□
Theorem 13.4.Let f be continuous and injective on an open interval I. Assume that f is
differentiable at x0 ∈I with f ′(x0) ̸= 0. Then f −1 is differentiable at f(x0)and( f −1)′(f(x0)) =
1/f ′(x0).
Proof.Lety 0 =f(x 0). We want to prove that the limit whenhgoes to 0 of

that limh→0 k(h) = 0, since

At this stage we can rewrite the quotient in terms off:

Finally, since limh→0 k= 0 and lim k→0

Theorem 13.5(The Inverse Function Theorem).Let I an open interval. Assume f : I→R is a
C1(I)function, i.e. f is differentiable on I and its derivative is continuous on I. Then, if f ′(a) ̸= 0
for a∈I , there exists δ > 0such that f : (a−δ, a + δ) →R is invertible and its inverse f −1 is
differentiable with(f −1)′(f(t)) = 1/f ′(t).
Marco A. M. Guaraco - Imperial College London

Chapter 1
Limits of functions of a real variable
In this section, we discuss the behaviour of functions f : J→R , where J⊂R indicates an open interval
of the real line.
Definition 1.0.1.Given x0 ∈J and α∈R , we say that limx→x0 f(x0) = α, if for all ε > 0, there exists
δ=δ(ε)>0, such that
∀xwith 0<|x−x 0|< δ(ε) =⇒ |f(x)−α|< ε.
Exercise 1.0.2.Show that limx→x0 f(x) = α, if and only if, for every sequence {xn}n∈N ⊂J , with
xn ̸=x 0,
lim
n→∞
xn =x 0 =⇒lim
n→∞
f(x n) =α.
Proof of( =⇒). Let {xn}n∈N, be such that limn→∞ xn = x0. We want to show that limn→∞ f(xn) = α.
Fix ε >. From the definition of limit of a real function we know there is δ > 0 such that |x−x 0|< δ implies
|f(x) −α|< ε . On the other hand, from the definition of limit of a sequence, there is N = N(δ) ∈N such
that |xn −x 0|< δ as long as n > N . Summarizing, for each fixed ε > 0 we have selected δ and N (both
depending on the fixedε) such that
n≥N=⇒ |x n −x 0|< δ=⇒ |f(x n)−α|< ϵ.
This proves that lim n→∞ f(x n) =α.
Proof of(⇐=). We proceed by contradiction. Assume that limx→x0 f(x) is not α. In other words, there
exist ε > 0 such that for all δ > 0, there is x with |x−x 0|< δ and |f(x) −α|> ε . Since we are free to
choose δ > 0, we can define a sequence in the following way: for every n∈N , choose δ = 1/n, and let xn
be a real number such that |xn −x 0|< 1/n but |f(xn) −α|> ε . Notice, that on one hand limn→∞ xn = x0,
however f(xn) is always far away from α (since ϵ is fixed), then limn→∞ f(xn) is not α, which contradicts
our hypothesis.
Although the definition of limit of a function of a real variable is different than the definition of limit of
sequences, they do have many common properties.
Exercise 1.0.3.Letf:J→Randg:J→R, withx 0 ∈J,lim x→x0 f(x) =αandlim x→x0 g(x) =β.
1. Show thatlim x→x0(f+g)(x) =α+β.
2. Show thatlim x→x0(f g)(x) =αβ.

x∈J ′.

6. Iff≤g, thenα≤β.
7. The Squeeze Theorem: Show that iff≤h≤gandα=β, thenlim x→x0 h(x) =α=β.
Proof.
(6) Proceed by contradiction. Assuming α > β , let ε = (α−β )/2. Applying the definition of limit to f,
there is a δ > 0 such that f((x0 −δ, x 0 + δ)) ⊂ (α−ε, α + ε). Similarly, applying the definition
of limit to g, there is a δ′ > 0 such that g((x0 −δ ′, x0 + δ′)) ⊂ (β−ε, β + ε). Let δ′′ = min{δ, δ′}.
Then, for any x∈ (x0 −δ ′′, x0 + δ′′) we must have f(x) ∈ (α−ε, α + ε) and g(x) ∈ (β−ε, β + ε).
It follows that for this choice of x, f(x) > α−ε = (α + β)/2 = β + ε > g (x), which contradicts our
assumptionf≤g.

x0 ∈R,lim x→x0 f(x)does not exist.
Some proofs and hints.

of the previous exercise?

(8) Let us show that no α∈R is the limit of f(x) as x tends to 0. Let ε = 1 and δ = 1/n, where
n∈N is a natural number to be determined. From the definition of f it follows that if x∈ (−δ, δ)
then |f(x)|≥n . Now, choose n so that n >|α| +1. Then, for all x∈ (−δ, δ), we must have
ε= 1<||f(x)|−|α||≤ |f(x)−α|. In particular,αis not the limit offasxgoes to 0.
Definition 1.0.6.We say that a real functionf:J→Ris (monotone)
1. non-decreasing: iff(x)≤f(y), for allx < y∈J.
2. non-increasing: iff(x)≥f(y), for allx < y∈J.
3. strictly increasing: iff(x)< f(y), for allx < y∈J.
4. strictly decreasing: iff(x)> f(y), for allx < y∈J.
Exercise 1.0.7.Show that iffis strictly monotone inJ, thenfis injective.

In this section, we discuss functionsf:J→R, whereJ⊂Rindicates an open interval of the real line.

Chapter 3
Continuous Functions
Letf:J⊂R→R, whereJis an open interval, andx 0 ∈J.
Definition 3.0.1(Continuity V.1).We say thatfis continuous at the pointx 0 if
lim
x→x0
f(x) =f(x 0).
Definition 3.0.2(Continuity V.2).We say that fis continuous at the pointx 0 if ∀ε > 0, there is
δ=δ(ε)>0 such that
|x−x 0|< δ=⇒ |f(x)−f(x 0)|< ε.
Exercise 3.0.3.Show that the two definitions of continuity offatx 0 given above, are equivalent.
Exercise 3.0.4.Show that if f is continuous at x0 ∈J , and f(x0) ∈ (α, β), then there is δ > 0such
thatf(x)∈(α, β)for allx∈(x 0 −δ, x 0 +δ).
Proof. Let ε = min{ β−f(x 0)
2 , f(x 0)−α
2 }. Then ( f(x0) −ε, f (x0) +ε) ⊂ (α, β). On the other hand, since the
function is continuous, there exists a δ > 0 such that f(x0 −δ, x 0 + δ) ⊂ (f(x0) −ε, x 0 + ε). This proves
the claim.
Definition 3.0.5.We say thatf:J→Ris continuous iffis continuous at every point ofJ.
Theorem 3.0.6(Intermediate value theorem).Let f : [a, b] →R be a continuous function on[ a, b], such
thatf(a)< f(b). Iff(a)< η < f(b), there existsα∈(a, b)such thatf(α) =η.
Proof. Define A = A(η) = {x∈ [a, b] : f(x) < η} . Notice A̸=∅ since a∈A , and A is bounded from
above by definition (for example,bis an upper bound forA). Therefore, there existsα= supA.
Applying the same logic as in Exercise 3.0.4 at a and b, there exist δ > 0 such that f(x) < η for
x∈ [a, a + δ) and η < f (x) for x∈ (b−δ, b ]. This implies α must in the interior of the interval, i.e.
α∈(a, b).
The rest of the strategy is to show that f(α) = η. We can prove this by contradiction using trichotomy.
Firstly, assume that f(α) < η . In this case, again by Exercise 3.0.4, there exists δ > 0, such that
f(x) < η for x∈ [α, α + δ), which means [ α, α + δ) ⊂A contradicting α = supA . If instead we assume
that η < f (α), then the result from the exercise implies the existence of δ > 0 such that η < f (x) for
x∈ (α−δ, α ]. Hence, A⊂ (−∞, a−δ ], meaning a−δ is an upper bound for A smaller than α = supA ,
which is a contradiction. Finally, by trichotomy the only remaining option is thatf(α) =η.
Exercise 3.0.7.Show that every polynomial of odd degree must have a real root.
Exercise 3.0.8.Show that every polynomial P (x) = xn + an−1xn−1 + · · · + a1x + a0, where n∈N even
must have a global minimum, i.e. if there isx 0 ∈Rsuch thatP(x 0)≤P(y), for ally∈R.
5
6CHAPTER 3. CONTINUOUS FUNCTIONS
Exercise 3.0.9.Show that if f : R→R is continuous at x0 and f assumes both positive and negative
values on every open interval containingx 0, thenf(x 0) = 0.
Theorem 3.0.10(Continuous functions are bounded on closed intervals).Let f : J→R be continuous.
If[a, b]⊂J, then there isM∈R, such thatf(x)≤Mfor allx∈[a, b].
Proof. Assume that f is not bounded in [a, b], then there is a sequencexn ∈ [a, b], such thatlimn→∞ f(xn) =
+∞. By Bolzano-Weierstrass xn has a convergent subsequencexnk, we havelimk→∞ xnk = x0 ∈ [a, b]. How-
ever, since f is continuous at x0, it follows limk→∞ f(xnk) = f(x0), which contradicts limn→∞ f(xn) = +∞.
Therefore,fmust be bounded in [a, b].
Exercise 3.0.11.Show that a continuous function does not need to be bounded on an open interval.
Definition 3.0.12.Given f : A→R , we say that fhas a maximum value inA , (or also “ f attains its
maximum inA”), if there isa∈Asuch thatf(a)≥f(a ′), for alla ′ ∈A.
Theorem 3.0.13(Extreme Value Theorem - Continuous functions have a maximum value on closed
intervals).Let f : J→R be continuous. Given[ a, b] ⊂J , there exists x∈ [a, b]such that f(x) = max[a,b] f,
i.e.f(x)≥f(y)for ally∈[a, b].
Proof. The proof is an exercise for you. First argue that there must be a sequence xn ∈ [a, b], which is
convergent and such that lim n→∞ f(x n) = sup{f(y) :y∈[a, b]}.
Exercise 3.0.14.
1. The sum of two continuous functions is continuous.
2. The product of two continuous functions is continuous.
3. If a function is continuous at a point and its value is not zero, then its multiplicative inverse is
continuous at the same point.
4. The composition of two continuous functions is continuous.
Definition 3.0.15.We say that f: [a, b]→Ris continuous on [a, b] , if it is continuous on ( a, b) and at
the extremes we have lim x→a+ f(x) =f(a) and lim x→b− f(x) =f(b).
Exercise 3.0.16.Show that iff: [a, b]→Ris continuous and injective, thenfis monotone on[a, b].
Proof. Since f is injective then either f(a) < f (b) or f(a) > f (b). Without loss of generality, assume
f(a) < f (b) (the proof for the case f(a) > f (b) is analogous). Notice that we must have f(a) < f (y), for
all y∈ (a, b). Otherwise, a < y < b but f(y) < f (a) < f (b) and by the intermediate value theorem, the
would be z∈ (y, b) such that f(z) = f(a). In addition, since a /∈(y, b) it follows that z̸=a , contradicting
thatfis injective. By a symmetric argument we also havef(x)< f(b), for allx∈(a, b).
Now, assume by contradiction that given x < y∈ (a, b) we have f(x) > f (y). By the previous paragraph
we have f(a) < f (y) < f (x) < f (b). Since a < x < y < b , by the intermediate value theorem, there must
bez∈(y, b) such thatf(z) =f(x). However,x /∈(y, b) soz̸=x, contradicting thatfis injective.
We conclude that iff(a)< f(b), thenfis actually strictly monotone increasing on all of [a, b].
Exercise 3.0.17.Let f : [a, b] → [f(a), f(b)]continuous and bijective, then its inverse f −1 : [f(a), f(b)] →
[a, b]is also continuous.
Proof. Since we are assuming f(a) < f (b), by the previous exercise we know that f is strictly monotone
increasing. Therefore, f −1 is also strictly monotone increasing. Given y0 ∈ (f(a), f(b)), we will show that
f −1 is continuous aty 0.
7
Fix ε > 0. To simplify the notation denote x0 = f −1(y0). The function f is monotone increasing so
f(x 0 −ε)< y 0 < f(x 0 +ε). We claim that choosingδ= min{f(x 0 +ε)−y 0, y0 −f(x 0 −ε)}, gives us
y∈(y 0 −δ, y 0 +δ) =⇒ |f −1(y)−f −1(y0)|< ε.
In fact, from the choice ofδ,
f(x 0 −ε)< y 0 −δ < y 0 +δ < f(x 0 +ε).
Therefore, given y∈ (y0 −δ, y0 +δ), we have f(x0 −ε) < y < f (x0 +ε). Since f −1 is monotone increasing it
follows that x0 −ε < f −1(y) < x0 + ε. From x0 = f −1(y0), this reads f −1(y0) −ε < f −1(y) < f −1(y0) +ε,
which is the same as
|f −1(y)−f −1(y0)|< ε.
Therefore,f −1 is continuous aty 0 ∈(a, b).
Continuity at y0 = a or y0 = b follows the same argument but checking only the corresponding one sided

Chapter 6
The Growth of a Function and Its
Derivative
Interpreting the derivative as both the rate of change of a function and the slope of its tangent line
suggests a connection between the sign of the derivative and the behaviour of the function itself. In this
section, we make this connection precise in several settings: (1) when f ′(c) > 0 at a single point c∈R ,
(2) whenf ′(x)>0 for allxin an intervalI, and (3) whenf ′(c) = 0 at a pointc.
6.1 Local comparison whenf ′(c)>0at a single point
Let f : I→R and assume that f is differentiable at a point c∈I with f ′(c) > 0. Note that we are not
assuming f is differentiable anywhere else on I. In this situation, the most we can conclude is a local
comparison of the values offnearc. More precisely:
Lemma 6.1.1.If f ′(c) > 0, then there exists δ > 0such that for every x∈ (c−δ, c )and y∈ (c, c + δ)
we have
f(x)< f(c)< f(y).
Proof.Sincef ′(c)>0, we have
lim
h→0
f(c+h)−f(c)
h =f ′(c)>0.
By the definition of the limit, there existsδ >0 such that
f(c+h)−f(c)
h >0
for all h∈ (−δ, δ) \ {0}. If h > 0, then f(c + h) −f (c) > 0, so f(c + h) > f (c); if h < 0, then
f(c + h) −f (c) < 0, so f(c + h) < f (c). Writing x = c + h for h < 0 and y = c + h for h > 0 gives the
result.
Remark.Note that f ′(c) > 0 at a single point DOES NOT imply monotonicity on an interval (c−δ, c +δ).
In other words, on a sufficiently small neighbourhood of c, values of f to the left of c lie below f(c), while
values of f to the right lie above f(c). This shows that a positive derivative at a point forces the function
to “tilt upward” through that point, even without assuming differentiability elsewhere.
In this lecture we collect a few ways in which the derivative carries information about a function. The
guiding idea is simple: the sign (and size) of f ′(x) tells us how f is behavingnear x, and—under stronger
hypotheses—this can be promoted to global information.

6.2 Local comparison at a single point
(Here we donotassume any differentiability away from the pointc.)
Lemma 6.2.1(Local comparison when f ′(c) > 0).Let f : I→R and assume that f is differentiable at
a point c∈I with f ′(c) > 0. Then there exists δ > 0such that for every x∈ (c−δ, c )and y∈ (c, c + δ)
we have
f(x)< f(c)< f(y).
Proof.Sincef ′(c)>0, we have

f(c + h) −f (c) < 0, hence f(c + h) < f (c). Writing x = c + h for h < 0 and y = c + h for h > 0 gives the
result.
Remark.Intuitively, f ′(c) > 0 forces the graph to “tilt upward” as it passes through c, even if the
function misbehaves elsewhere. However, note that a positive derivative at a single point DOES NOT
imply that f is monotone on any interval around that point. What we do get is a clean one-sided
comparison: sufficiently close to c, values to the left lie below f(c) and values to the right lie above f(c).

6.3 The derivative can indicate global growth
The previous lemma was purely local. Now we assume differentiability on an interval and ask for a global
conclusion.
6.4. THE DERIV ATIVE HELPS LOCATE MAXIMA AND MINIMA15
Proposition 6.3.1.Letf: (a, b)→Rbe differentiable and assume that
f ′(t)>0for allt∈(a, b).
Thenfis strictly increasing on(a, b).
Proof. Fix x∈ (a, b). We want to show that for every y∈ (x, b) we have f(x) < f (y). Suppose not. Then
there existsy 0 ∈(x, b) such thatf(y 0)≤f(x).

Finally, to get a contradiction, we apply the local comparison lemma at α (since f ′(α) > 0): there exists

6.4 The derivative helps locate maxima and minima
One of the most useful applications of derivatives is tonarrow downwhere extrema can occur.
Definition 6.4.1(Local extremum).We say that f attains alocal maximumat c if there exists δ > 0
such that

Similarly,fattains alocal minimumatcif there existsδ >0 such that
f(c)≤f(x) for allx∈(c−δ, c+δ).

Theorem 6.4.2(Fermat’s theorem).If f attains a local maximum or a local minimum at c and f is
differentiable atc, then
f ′(c) = 0.
Proof.Assumefhas a local maximum atc(the minimum case is analogous). Forx→c + we have

Remark.Be careful: although maxima and minima of differentiable functions satisfy f ′(c) = 0, the
converse is false. For instance, for f(x) = x3 we have f ′(0) = 0, but 0 is neither a local maximum nor a
local minimum (it is a “saddle point”).
Definition 6.4.3(Critical point).A pointcis called acritical pointoffiff ′(c) = 0.
Remark.In optimisation problems, candidates for maxima/minima are often among the set of critical
points. (If you’re curious: this point of view sits behind theleast action principlein physics, and even
behindgradient descentmethods in machine learning.) Of course, after locating critical points one
typically needs additional information to decide whether they are maxima, minima, or neither.

Chapter 7
Existence of critical points
Let f : [a, b] →R be continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b). We proved (Extreme Value Theorem)
that continuous functions on compact sets always attain a maximum and a minimum.
A natural question is:can we use this to guarantee the existence of a critical point?
We have to be careful: local minima and maxima are critical pointsonly when the point is interior. At
the endpoints, there is no reason for the derivative to vanish.
Example 7.0.1.Let f : [a, b] →R be given by f(x) = x. Then f(a) is the minimum and f(b) is the
maximum on [a, b], but there is not∈(a, b) withf ′(t) = 0 (in factf ′(x) = 1 everywhere).
Nevertheless, in several situations wecanguarantee the existence of critical points. The cleanest one is
when the function starts and ends at the same height.
7.1 Critical points whenf(a) =f(b)
Theorem 7.1.1(Rolle’s Theorem).Let f : [a, b] →R be continuous on[ a, b]and differentiable on( a, b).
Iff(a) =f(b), then there existst∈(a, b)such that
f ′(t) = 0.
Proof.By the Extreme Value Theorem, there existst∈[a, b] such that
f(t) = max{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
Ift∈(a, b) is an interior point, thentis a local maximum, hencef ′(t) = 0 (Fermat’s theorem).
Iftis not interior, thent=aort=b. But sincef(a) =f(b), we must have
f(a) =f(b) = max{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
Apply the Extreme Value Theorem again to obtainx∈[a, b] such that
f(x) = min{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
Ifx∈(a, b), thenxis an interior local minimum and againf ′(x) = 0.
Ifxis not interior, thenx=aorx=b, so
f(a) = min{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}andf(a) = max{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
This means min[a,b] f = max[a,b] f, so f is constant on [ a, b]. In that case f ′(t) = 0 for every t∈ (a, b),
and we are done.
Remark.The picture to keep in mind is: if f(a) = f(b), then as you slide a horizontal line down from
18
7.2. CRITICAL POINTS WHENf ′(x)>0> f ′(y) 19
above, it will touch the graph for the first time at some interior point, producing a horizontal tangent.
7.2 Critical points whenf ′(x)>0> f ′(y)
There is another common mechanism that forces a critical point: the derivative being positive at one
point and negative at another. In this case, the picture to keep in mind is that of a dome (or bowl
depending on the signs).
Proposition 7.2.1.Letf: (a, b)→Rbe differentiable on(a, b). Assumex < yin(a, b)are such that
f ′(x)>0> f ′(y).
Then there existst∈(x, y)such thatf ′(t) = 0.
Proof. Since f ′(x) > 0, the local comparison lemma implies that there exists δ > 0 (small enough so that
x+δ < y) such that for allp∈(x, x+δ) we have

In particular,fdoes not attain its maximum on [x, y] at the left endpointx.
Similarly, since f ′(y) < 0, there exists δ′ > 0 (small enough so that x < y−δ ′) such that for all
q∈(y−δ ′, y) we have
f(q)> f(y).
Sofdoes not attain its maximum on [x, y] at the right endpointyeither.
On the other hand, f is continuous on [ x, y] (because it is differentiable on ( a, b)), so by the Extreme
Value Theorem there existst∈[x, y] such that
f(t) = max{f(s) :s∈[x, y]}.
Since the maximum cannot occur at x or y, we must have t∈ (x, y). Thus t is an interior maximum and
Fermat’s theorem givesf ′(t) = 0.

8.2 The Intermediate Value Theorem for derivatives (Darboux)
One might expect the derivative to be continuous. This isfalsein general. However, a surprising feature
remains true: derivatives cannot jump over values.
Theorem 8.2.1(Intermediate Value Property for derivatives).Let f : (a, b) →R be differentiable on
(a, b). Then f ′ satisfies the intermediate value property: for every x < y in( a, b)and every real number c
betweenf ′(x)andf ′(y), there existst∈(x, y)such that
f ′(t) =c.
Remark.This holds even whenf ′ is not continuous.
Proof.Fixx < yand letcbe betweenf ′(x) andf ′(y). Consider
φ(t) :=f(t)−ct.
Thenφis differentiable on (a, b) and
φ′(t) =f ′(t)−c.
Since c lies between f ′(x) and f ′(y), the numbers φ′(x) and φ′(y) have opposite signs (or one is 0 already).
Ifφ ′(x) = 0 orφ ′(y) = 0 we are done. Otherwise,
φ′(x)>0> φ ′(y) orφ ′(x)<0< φ ′(y).
Applying the previous proposition to φ on ( x, y) gives a point t∈ (x, y) such that φ′(t) = 0. Hence
f ′(t)−c= 0, i.e.f ′(t) =c.

Proof. By the Intermediate Value Theorem for derivatives, f ′ has the intermediate value property. If f ′
took both positive and negative values, then it would also take the value 0 somewhere in between. Since
f ′(t)̸= 0 everywhere, this cannot happen. Therefore eitherf ′(t)>0 for alltorf ′(t)<0 for allt.
In the first case, the corollary to Cauchy’s Mean Value Theorem shows that f is strictly increasing; in the
second case, applying the same result to−fshows thatfis strictly decreasing.

A good way to think about convexity is as a global version of the idea “the function bends upward”. For
C2 functions, this is exactly what the condition f ′′ ≥ 0 means. However, convexity makes sense (and is
extremely useful) even when no second derivative exists: it is ageometriccondition on the graph off.
11.1 Convex sets
Definition 11.1.1(Convex set).A set U⊂R 2 is calledconvexif for every two points p, q∈U , the
straight line segment joiningpandqis also contained inU. Equivalently, for everyt∈[0,1] we have
(1−t)p+tq∈U.
It is often convenient to parametrise the segment fromptoqby
γ: [0,1]→R 2, γ(t) = (1−t)p+tq.
Example 11.1.2.The closed unit disk D = {x∈R 2 : |x|≤ 1} is convex: given p, q∈D , the entire
segment joining them lies in D. However, if we remove the origin, the set D\ { 0} isnotconvex. Indeed,
take p = (1/2, 0) and q = (−1/2, 0). Then p, q∈D\ { 0}, but the segment between them contains the
midpoint 0 = p+q
2 , which is not inD\ {0}.

Throughout this section,I⊂Ris an interval andf:I→Ris convex.
12.1 The chord extension inequality
Convexity says that on [ a, b] ⊂I the graph liesbelowthe chord ℓa,b. Less obvious (and extremely useful)
is that outside [a, b] the inequality reverses: the graph liesabovethe extension of the same chord.
Proposition 12.1.1(Chord extension inequality).Letf:I→Rbe convex and fixa < binI. Then
ℓa,b(x)≤f(x)for allx∈I\[a, b].
Proof. We prove the case x > b (the case x < a is analogous). We proceed by contradiction. Assume there
exists x > b such that ℓa,b(x) > f (x), and consider the chord line ℓa,x through the two points ( a, f(a))
and (x, f(x)).
Since b∈ [a, x], convexity on [a, x] implies that the graph of f lies below the chord from a to x, hence
f(b) ≤ℓ a,x(b). On the other hand, the inequality ℓa,b(x) > f (x) can be rewritten geometrically as
ℓa,b(x) > ℓ a,x(x), because ℓa,x(x) = f(x). Now, both lines ℓa,b and ℓa,x pass through the common point
(a, f(a)), and at the point x > b > a the line ℓa,b lies strictlyabove ℓa,x. Therefore ℓa,b must have
strictly larger slope than ℓa,x. To see this, note that at x we have ℓa,b(x) = αa,b · (x−a ) + f(a) and
f(x) = ℓa,x(x) = αa,x · (x−a ) + f(a). Since ℓa,b(x) > f (x) = ℓa,x(x) we conclude that αa,b > α a,x, which
implies thatℓ a,b is strictly larger thatℓ a,x on points larger thana.
In particular at b—the value of ℓa,b is strictly larger: ℓa,b(b) > ℓ a,x(b). But ℓa,b(b) = f(b) by definition of
the chord line through ( a, f(a)) and ( b, f(b)). Thus f(b) = ℓa,b(b) > ℓ a,x(b), which contradicts the earlier
inequalityf(b)≤ℓ a,x(b).
This contradiction shows that our assumption was false, and thereforeℓ a,b(x)≤f(x) for allx > b.
