Exercise 12.4.3(Uniqueness of critical points).Let f : I→R be strictly convex and differentiable. Show

minimisation for strictly convex functions is so rigid: there is at most one place where the slope can
vanish, and when it does, it must be the global minimum.

Chapter 13
The Inverse Function Theorem
When working with functions on the real line, it is common to encounter functions which are locally
bijective. This occurs, for example, if f : (a, b) →R is differentiable and satisfies f ′(x) ̸= 0 for all x∈ (a, b).
In this section, we study when the continuity and differentiability of f imply the same properties for its
inversef −1.
Definition 13.0.1.Let f : (a, b) → (c, d) be a bijection. We say that a function g : (c, d) → (a, b) is its
inverse if f(g(x)) = x for all x∈ (c, d) (equivalently, g(f(y)) = y for all y∈ (a, b)). In this situation, we
writeg=f −1.
Remark.The notation f −1(u)does notmean the same as 1
f(u) ; it denotes the inverse function in the
sense of mappings between sets.
As mentioned earlier, monotone functions provide natural examples of bijections between intervals. In
fact—when continuity is assumed—these are theonlybijective functions between intervals. This follows
from the Intermediate Value Theorem and is encapsulated in the following exercise.

and injective on an interval( x, y), then f is either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing on that interval.
First, we check that continuity is preserved.

Chapter 14
The Integral
The concept ofareais a simple and intuitive one, yet formalising it presents some subtle challenges. For
a rectangle we define its area as b×h . But for a curved region (say, the disk) the idea “counting how
many unit squares fit inside” is not so straightforward.
The notion ofintegralformalises the idea ofsigned areabetween the graph of a function and the x-axis.
Here “signed” means: regions above the x-axis count as positive area, while regions below count as
negative area.
14.1 Lower and upper sums
The basic idea is to approximate the area by rectangles. Some rectangles sitbelowthe graph (under-
estimates), others sitabovethe graph (over-estimates). Intuitively, ifs we refine our subdivision, these
approximations become better and better.
Definition 14.1.1(Partition).Apartitionof [ a, b] is a finite collection of points in [ a, b] containing both
endpoints. We usually write a partition as
P:a=t 0 < t1 <· · ·< t n =b,orP={t 0, t1, . . . , tn}.
Definition 14.1.2(Lower and upper sums).Let f : [a, b] →R be a bounded function and let P =

mi := inf{f(x) :x∈[t i−1, ti]}, M i := sup{f(x) :x∈[t i−1, ti]}.
Thelower sumoffwith respect toPis

Remark.It follows immediately from the definition that for any partitionP,

Definition 14.1.3.Given two partitions P and P ′ of an interval [ a, b], we say that P ′ is a refinement of
PifP⊂P ′.

14.2. A KEY COMPARISON LEMMA37
The next lemma formalises the intuitive idea that refining a partition can only improve the approximations.
Lemma 14.1.4(Refinement lemma).Let P and P ′ be partitions of an interval[ a, b]. If P ′ is a refinement
ofPthen
L(f, P)≤L(f, P ′)andU(f, P ′)≤U(f, P).
Proof. It is enough to prove L(f, P ) ≤L (f, P ′) in the special case where P ′ is obtained by adding exactly
one new point to P . This is because any refinement P ′ of P can be obtained as a sequence of refinements
P=P 0 ⊂P 1 ⊂. . .⊂P ℓ =P ′ in which each stepP k ⊂P k+1 consists in exactly one point.
So assume
P={t 0, . . . , tk−1, tk, . . . , tn}, P ′ ={t 0, . . . , tk−1, u, tk, . . . , tn},
witht k−1 < u < t k. Let
mk = inf{f(x) :x∈[t k−1, tk]}, m ′ = inf{f(x) :x∈[t k−1, u]}, m ′′ = inf{f(x) :x∈[u, t k]}.
Since
[tk−1, u]⊂[t k−1, tk] and [u, t k]⊂[t k−1, tk],
we havem ′ ≥m k andm ′′ ≥m k. Therefore
L(f, P ′)−L(f, P) =m ′(u−t k−1) +m ′′(tk −u)−m k(tk −t k−1)
≥m k(u−t k−1) +m k(tk −u)−m k(tk −t k−1)
= 0.
Hence L(f, P ) ≤L (f, P ′). The proof of U(f, P ′) ≤U (f, P ) is similar, using suprema instead of infima.
14.2 A key comparison lemma
The next lemma is less obvious, but it is the reason why the integral definition makes sense. It says that
any lower sum is less or equal than any upper sum, i.e. even if the partitions are different.
Lemma 14.2.1.Given any two partitionsPandQof[a, b], we have
L(f, P)≤U(f, Q).
Proof.LetPandQbe arbitrary partitions of [a, b]. Consider the refinement
R:=P∪Q
which contains every point from both partitions, so P⊂R and Q⊂R . By the previous lemma and the
remark after the definition of lower and upper sums, we get
L(f, P)≤L(f, R)≤U(f, R)≤U(f, Q),
which gives the desired inequalityL(f, P)≤U(f, Q).
14.3 Integrability
Lemma 14.2.1 tells us thateverylower sum is less or equal thaneveryupper sum. In particular,
sup{L(f, P) :Ppartition of [a, b]} ≤inf{U(f, P) :Ppartition of [a, b]}.

Chapter 18
Taylor’s Theorem
The guiding principle behind this section is that polynomials are easier to evaluate than functions such
as exp, log, sin, cos, etc. Taylor’s theorem tells us that, under suitable differentiability assumptions, a
general function can be approximated near a point by a polynomial whose coefficients are determined by
the derivatives of the function at that point. Moreover, it gives us several ways of measuring the error
between the original function and the polynomial approximation.

Chapter 19
Taylor Series
19.1 From Taylor polynomials to Taylor series
One pleasant feature of Taylor polynomials is that they are builtincrementally: the polynomial of order
n + 1 is obtained from the polynomial of order n by adding exactly one more term. More precisely, fixing
a pointaand writing
Pn,a(x) =
nX

It is sometimes helpful to write out the first few Taylor polynomials explicitly:
P0,a(x) =f(a),
P1,a(x) =f(a) +f ′(a)(x−a),
P2,a(x) =f(a) +f ′(a)(x−a) + f ′′(a)
2! (x−a) 2,

3! (x−a) 3,
and so on. Each step keeps all the previous information and adds one new piece of data: the next
derivative ata.
This naturally raises a few questions. If we keep adding terms forever, we are led to theformalseries
∞X

which is called theTaylor series offata(when the series converges). The big question is then:
When does the Taylor series converge, and when does it convergeto the original function f?
Many of the functions you already know and love are completely determined by their Taylor series. For
example, as we will prove later, on suitable intervals the exponential function, the trigonometric functions,
and the logarithm can all be recovered from their Taylor series.
However, it is important to know that this isnot automatic. There are smooth functions whose Taylor
series at a point converges but doesnotrecover the function anywhere except at that point.

actually define well-behaved functions: in particular, we need tools to prove not only convergence but
also continuity, differentiability, and to show that the resulting functions satisfy the identities we expect
(for instance (exp) ′ = exp or sin ′ = cos). We will develop some these tools in the next section.
Chapter 20
Convergence of functions
As we saw in the previous section, in many situations we encounter asequence of functions
fn :I→R,
and we would like to understand whether (and in what sense) fn approaches a limiting function f.
Moreover, we are also interested in the following (important) question: if each fn has a desirable property
(for instance, being continuous, differentiable, integrable), does the limitfinherit that property?
As it turns out, there are many natural notions of convergence one can consider. In this section we learn
about pointwise convergence and uniform convergence.

Definition 20.1.1(Pointwise convergence).We say that fn : I→R converges pointwiseto a function

20.2 Uniform convergence
Definition 20.2.1(Uniform convergence).We say thatf n converges uniformlytofonIif
∀ε >0,∃N∈Nsuch that∀x∈I, n > N=⇒ |f n(x)−f(x)|< ε.
Remark.The only difference with pointwise convergence is the order of quantifiers: for uniform
convergence, the index N = N(ε) workssimultaneously for all x∈I . In particular, uniform converges
implies pointwise convergence but not the other way around.

21.2 Weierstrass M-test
Theorems 21.1.2 and 21.1.3 are the basic “exchange of limit with integral/derivative” results for series:
they tell us that once we have uniform convergence (and, for differentiation, uniform convergence of the
derivative series together with convergence at one point), we are allowed to treat the infinite series much
like a finite sum.
In practice, however, it is often inconvenient to verify uniform convergence directly from the definition.
The Weierstrass M-test in Theorem 21.2.1 provides a simple sufficient criterion: if each term (or each
derivative term) can be dominated by a numerical series that converges, then the relevant uniform
convergence follows automatically.

Beyond the WeierstrassM-test
One should also remember that the M-test is only a sufficient condition. Uniform convergence may still
hold if no summable sup–norm bound exists. For instance, two different mechanisms that might imply
uniform convergence are:
• geometric structurethe functions in the sum have (almost) disjoint supports, so that at each point
only few functions are “active”; or
•cancellation(e.g. alternating signs), where large terms cancel in the partial sums.
The next two examples illustrate these two phenomena.

22.3 Inverse trigonometric functions
The functions sin and tan are strictly increasing on ( −π/2, π/2) (since sin′ = cos> 0 there and tan′ > 0

•Define arccos : [−1,1]→[0, π] to be the inverse of cos restricted to [0, π].

Chapter 23
A nowhere differentiable function
Up to now, many of our examples have suggested an optimistic picture: continuous functions often look
“smooth” when drawn, and the operations we like (limits, integration, differentiation) frequently behave
well under suitable hypotheses (uniform convergence, uniform convergence of derivatives, etc.)). This
section is a reality check:continuity alonedoes not force any differentiability at all. In fact, we will build
a function that is continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere.
The construction is also a good illustration of the power of uniform convergence: we will define our function
as a uniformly convergent series of simple “triangular waves”. Uniform convergence will immediately give
continuity, while a careful look at difference quotients will show that derivatives fail everywhere.
23.1 Construction of the Takagi Function

Proof.SinceTis 1-periodic, it is enough to prove non-differentiability on [0,1].

23.2. REGULARITY OF THE TAKAGI FUNCTION73
By Exercise 23.1.5, if T were differentiable at x, then these symmetric secant slopes would have to
converge. HenceTis not differentiable at any non-dyadicx.
(ii) Dyadic points.Now letx∈[0,1] be dyadic, sayx=k/2 N0. ForN≥N 0 set

Remark.The Takagi function is built from extremely simple pieces, yet it is as far from differentiable
as possible. It shows thatcontinuity is genuinely a lot weaker that differentiability, and it highlights
why our additional hypotheses (like uniform convergence of derivatives) are not technicalities but real
mathematical content.
23.2 Regularity of the Takagi function
As we saw, the Takagi function T is continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere. In terms of
regularity, it sits in between those two worlds: it is more regular than an arbitrary continuous function (it
has an explicit H¨ older modulus of continuity1 as the exercise below shows), but still far from differentiable
(since it is nowhere differentiable).

1.Limits of functions of a real variable
In this section, we discuss the behaviour of functions f : J→R , where J⊂R indicates an open
interval of the real line.
Definition 1.1.Given x0 ∈J and α∈R , we say that limx→x0 f(x0) = α, if for all ε > 0, there
existsδ=δ(ε)>0, such that
∀xwith 0<|x−x 0|< δ(ε) =⇒ |f(x)−α|< ε.
Exercise 1.2.Show that limx→x0 f(x) = α, if and only if, for every sequence {xn}n∈N ⊂J , with
xn ̸=x 0,
lim
n→∞
xn =x 0 =⇒lim
n→∞
f(x n) =α.
Proof of( =⇒). Let {xn}n∈N, be such thatlimn→∞ xn = x0. We want to show thatlimn→∞ f(xn) =
α. Fix ε > . From the definition of limit of a real function we know there is δ > 0 such that
|x−x 0|< δ implies |f(x) −α|< ε . On the other hand, from the definition of limit of a sequence,
there is N = N(δ) ∈N such that |xn −x 0|< δ as long as n > N . Summarizing, for each fixed ε > 0
we have selectedδandN(both depending on the fixedε) such that
n≥N=⇒ |x n −x 0|< δ=⇒ |f(x n)−α|< ϵ.
This proves that lim n→∞ f(x n) =α.□
Proof of(⇐=). We proceed by contradiction. Assume that limx→x0 f(x) is not α. In other words,
there exist ε > 0 such that for all δ > 0, there is x with |x−x 0|< δ and |f(x) −α|> ε . Since we are
free to choose δ > 0, we can define a sequence in the following way: for every n∈N , choose δ = 1/n,
and let xn be a real number such that |xn −x 0|< 1/n but |f(xn) −α|> ε . Notice, that on one hand
limn→∞ xn =x 0, howeverf(x n) is always far away fromα(sinceϵis fixed), then lim n→∞ f(x n) is
notα, which contradicts our hypothesis.□
Although the definition of limit of a function of a real variable is different than the definition of
limit of sequences, they do have many common properties.
Exercise 1.3.Let f : J→R and g : J→R , with x0 ∈J , limx→x0 f(x) = α and limx→x0 g(x) = β.
(1)Show thatlim x→x0(f+g)(x) =α+β.
(2)Show thatlim x→x0(f g)(x) =αβ.
(3) Show that if β̸= 0then there is an open interval J ′ ⊂J , such that x0 ∈J ′ and |g(x)|> β/2,
for allx∈J ′.
(4)Show that ifβ̸= 0, thenlim x→x0 ( f
g )(x) = α
β .
(5)Iflim x→x0|f(x)−g(x)|= 0thenα=β.
(6)Iff≤g, thenα≤β.
(7)The Squeeze Theorem: Show that iff≤h≤gandα=β, thenlim x→x0 h(x) =α=β.
Proof.
(6) Proceed by contradiction. Assuming α > β , let ε = (α−β )/2. Applying the definition of
limit to f, there is a δ > 0 such that f((x0 −δ, x 0 + δ)) ⊂ (α−ε, α + ε). Similarly, applying
3
the definition of limit to g, there is a δ′ > 0 such that g((x0 −δ ′, x0 + δ′)) ⊂ (β−ε, β + ε).
Let δ′′ = min{δ, δ′}. Then, for any x∈ (x0 −δ ′′, x0 + δ′′) we must have f(x) ∈ (α−ε, α + ε)
and g(x) ∈ (β−ε, β + ε). It follows that for this choice of x, f(x) > α−ε = (α + β)/2 =
β+ε > g(x), which contradicts our assumptionf≤g.
□

for allx 0 ∈(a, b).

(8) Let us show that no α∈R is the limit of f(x) as x tends to 0. Let ε = 1 and δ = 1/n,
where n∈N is a natural number to be determined. From the definition of f it follows that

we must have ε = 1 <||f (x)|−|α||≤ |f (x) −α| . In particular, α is not the limit of f as x
goes to 0.

Definition 1.6.We say that a real functionf:J→Ris (monotone)
(1) non-decreasing: iff(x)≤f(y), for allx < y∈J.
(2) non-increasing: iff(x)≥f(y), for allx < y∈J.
4
(3) strictly increasing: iff(x)< f(y), for allx < y∈J.
(4) strictly decreasing: iff(x)> f(y), for allx < y∈J.

In this section, we discuss functions f : J→R , where J⊂R indicates an open interval of the

3.Continuous Functions
Letf:J⊂R→R, whereJis an open interval, andx 0 ∈J.
Definition 3.1(Continuity V.1).We say thatfis continuous at the pointx 0 if
lim
x→x0
f(x) =f(x 0).
Definition 3.2(Continuity V.2).We say that fis continuous at the pointx 0 if ∀ε > 0, there is
δ=δ(ε)>0 such that
|x−x 0|< δ=⇒ |f(x)−f(x 0)|< ε.
Exercise 3.3.Show that the two definitions of continuity offatx 0 given above, are equivalent.
Exercise 3.4.Show that if f is continuous at x0 ∈J , and f(x0) ∈ (α, β), then there is δ > 0such
thatf(x)∈(α, β)for allx∈(x 0 −δ, x 0 +δ).
5
Proof. Let ε = min{ β−f(x 0)

2 }. Then ( f(x0) −ε, f (x0) + ε) ⊂ (α, β). On the other hand,
since the function is continuous, there exists a δ > 0 such that f(x0 −δ, x0 + δ) ⊂ (f(x0) −ε, x 0 + ε).
This proves the claim.□
Definition 3.5.We say thatf:J→Ris continuous iffis continuous at every point ofJ.
Theorem 3.6(Intermediate value theorem).Let f : (a, b) →R a continuous function on( a, b),
such thatf(a)< f(b). Iff(a)< η < f(b), there existsα∈(a, b)such thatf(α) =η.
Proof. Define A = A(η) = {x∈ (a, b) : f(x) < η} . Notice A̸=∅ since a∈A , and A is bounded
from above by definition (for example, b is an upper bound for A). Therefore, there exists α = supA
and α∈ (a, b). By Exercise 3.4 we must have α /∈A, otherwise we could find an element in A larger
than α (Why?), which contradicts that α = supA , i.e. f(α) < η cannot happen. If f(α) > η then,
again by Exercise 3.4, we would find an upper bound for A smaller than α (Why?). Therefore, by
trichotomy,f(α) =η.□

Theorem 3.10(Continuous functions are bounded on closed intervals).Let f : J→R be continuous.
If[a, b]⊂J, then there isM∈R, such thatf(x)≤Mfor allx∈[a, b].
Proof. Assume that f is not bounded in [ a, b], then there is a sequence xn ∈ [a, b], such that
limn→∞ f(xn) = + ∞. By Bolzano-Weierstrass xn has a convergent subsequence xnk, we have
limk→∞ xnk = x0 ∈ [a, b]. However, since f is continuous at x0, it follows limk→∞ f(xnk) = f(x0),
which contradicts limn→∞ f(x n) = +∞. Therefore,fmust be bounded in [a, b].□

Exercise 3.16.Show that if f : [a, b] →R is continuous and injective, then f is monotone on[ a, b].
Proof. Since f is injective then either f(a) < f (b) or f(a) > f (b). Without loss of generality, assume
f(a) < f (b) (the proof for the case f(a) > f (b) is analogous). Notice that we must have f(a) < f (y),
for all y∈ (a, b). Otherwise, a < y < b but f(y) < f (a) < f (b) and by the intermediate value
theorem, the would be z∈ (y, b) such that f(z) = f(a). In addition, since a /∈(y, b) it follows that
z̸=a , contradicting that f is injective. By a symmetric argument we also have f(x) < f (b), for all
x∈(a, b).
Now, assume by contradiction that given x < y∈ (a, b) we have f(x) > f (y). By the previous
paragraph we have f(a) < f (y) < f (x) < f (b). Since a < x < y < b , by the intermediate value
theorem, there must be z∈ (y, b) such that f(z) = f(x). However, x /∈(y, b) so z̸=x , contradicting
thatfis injective.
We conclude that if f(a) < f (b), then f is actually strictly monotone increasing on all of [ a, b]. □
Exercise 3.17.Let f : [ a, b] → [f(a), f(b)]continuous and bijective, then its inverse f −1 :
[f(a), f(b)]→[a, b]is also continuous.
Proof. Since we are assuming f(a) < f (b), by the previous exercise we know that f is strictly
monotone increasing. Therefore, f −1 is also strictly monotone increasing. Given y0 ∈ (f(a), f(b)),
we will show thatf −1 is continuous aty 0.
Fix ε > 0. To simplify the notation denote x0 = f −1(y0). The function f is monotone increasing
so f(x0 −ε ) < y 0 < f (x0 + ε). We claim that choosing δ = min{f(x0 + ε) −y 0, y0 −f (x0 −ε )},
gives us
y∈(y 0 −δ, y 0 +δ) =⇒ |f −1(y)−f −1(y0)|< ε.
In fact, from the choice ofδ,
f(x 0 −ε)< y 0 −δ < y 0 +δ < f(x 0 +ε).
Therefore, given y∈ (y0 −δ, y 0 + δ), we have f(x0 −ε ) < y < f (x0 + ε). Since f −1 is monotone
increasing it follows that x0 −ε < f −1(y) < x0 + ε. From x0 = f −1(y0), this reads f −1(y0) −ε <
f −1(y)< f −1(y0) +ε, which is the same as
|f −1(y)−f −1(y0)|< ε.
Therefore,f −1 is continuous aty 0 ∈(a, b).
Continuity at y0 = a or y0 = b follows the same argument but checking only the corresponding
one sided limits. □

6.The Growth of a Function and Its Derivative
Interpreting the derivative as both the rate of change of a function and the slope of its tangent
line suggests a connection between the sign of the derivative and the behaviour of the function itself.
In this section, we make this connection precise in several settings: (1) when f ′(c) > 0 at a single
pointc∈R, (2) whenf ′(x)>0 for allxin an intervalI, and (3) whenf ′(c) = 0 at a pointc.
6.1.Local comparison when f ′(c) > 0at a single point.Let f : I→R and assume that f
is differentiable at a point c∈I with f ′(c) > 0. Note that we are not assuming f is differentiable
anywhere else on I. In this situation, the most we can conclude is a local comparison of the values
offnearc. More precisely:
Lemma 6.1.If f ′(c) > 0, then there exists δ > 0such that for every x∈ (c−δ, c )and y∈ (c, c + δ)
we have
f(x)< f(c)< f(y).
Proof.Sincef ′(c)>0, we have
lim
h→0
f(c+h)−f(c)
h =f ′(c)>0.
By the definition of the limit, there existsδ >0 such that
f(c+h)−f(c)
h >0
for all h∈ (−δ, δ) \ {0}. If h > 0, then f(c + h) −f (c) > 0, so f(c + h) > f (c); if h < 0, then
f(c + h) −f (c) < 0, so f(c + h) < f (c). Writing x = c + h for h < 0 and y = c + h for h > 0 gives
the result. □
12
Remark.Note that f ′(c) > 0 at a single point DOES NOT imply monotonicity on an interval
(c−δ, c + δ). In other words, on a sufficiently small neighbourhood of c, values of f to the left of c lie
below f(c), while values of f to the right lie above f(c). This shows that a positive derivative at a
point forces the function to “tilt upward” through that point, even without assuming differentiability
elsewhere.
In this lecture we collect a few ways in which the derivative carries information about a function.
The guiding idea is simple: the sign (and size) of f ′(x) tells us how f is behavingnear x, and—under
stronger hypotheses—this can be promoted to global information.
6.2.Local comparison at a single point.(Here we donotassume any differentiability away
from the pointc.)
Lemma 6.2(Local comparison when f ′(c) > 0).Let f : I→R and assume that f is differentiable
at a point c∈I with f ′(c) > 0. Then there exists δ > 0such that for every x∈ (c−δ, c )and
y∈(c, c+δ)we have
f(x)< f(c)< f(y).
Proof.Sincef ′(c)>0, we have
lim
h→0
f(c+h)−f(c)
h =f ′(c)>0.
By the definition of the limit, there existsδ >0 such that
f(c+h)−f(c)
h >0
for all h∈ (−δ, δ) \ {0}. If h > 0, then f(c + h) −f (c) > 0, hence f(c + h) > f (c). If h < 0, then
f(c + h) −f (c) < 0, hence f(c + h) < f (c). Writing x = c + h for h < 0 and y = c + h for h > 0
gives the result. □
Remark.Intuitively, f ′(c) > 0 forces the graph to “tilt upward” as it passes through c, even
if the function misbehaves elsewhere. However, note that a positive derivative at a single point
DOES NOT imply that f is monotone on any interval around that point. What we do get is a clean
one-sided comparison: sufficiently close to c, values to the left lie below f(c) and values to the right
lie abovef(c).

6.3.The derivative can indicate global growth.The previous lemma was purely local. Now
we assume differentiability on an interval and ask for a global conclusion.
Proposition 6.6.Letf: (a, b)→Rbe differentiable and assume that
f ′(t)>0for allt∈(a, b).
Thenfis strictly increasing on(a, b).
Proof. Fix x∈ (a, b). We want to show that for every y∈ (x, b) we have f(x) < f (y). Suppose not.
Then there existsy 0 ∈(x, b) such thatf(y 0)≤f(x).
Define
A:={y∈(x, b) :f(y)≤f(x)}.
By assumptionAis nonempty (it containsy 0) and it is bounded below byx. Let
α:= infA.
Claim 1: f(α) ≤f (x).Since α = infA , there exists a sequence an ∈A with an →α . Since f is
differentiable atα, it is continuous atα, hence
f(α) = lim
n→∞
f(a n)≤f(x),
because eacha n ∈A. This finishes the proof of Claim 1.
Claim 2: x < α .Since f ′(x) > 0, the local comparison lemma gives a δ > 0 such that f(y) > f (x)
for all y∈ (x, x + δ). In particular, no point of ( x, x + δ) belongs to A, so α≥x + δ > x . This
finishes the proof of Claim 2.
Finally, to get a contradiction, we apply the local comparison lemma at α (since f ′(α) > 0): there
existsη >0 such that for everyy∈(α−η, α) we have
f(y)< f(α)≤f(x).
Hence every y∈ (α−η, α ) with y > x belongs to A. But those y are strictly smaller than α,
contradicting the fact that α is a lower bound for A (indeed, it is the greatest lower bound). This
contradiction shows our initial assumption was false, so f(y) > f (x) for all y > x . Therefore f is
strictly increasing. □

f(x)−f(c)
x−c ≤0 (x > c).
Forx < cclose tocwe havef(x)−f(c)≤0 but nowx−c <0, so dividing flips the inequality:
f(x)−f(c)
x−c ≥0 (x < c).
So the difference quotients approachcfrom opposite sides with opposite signs.
Theorem 6.11(Fermat’s theorem).If f attains a local maximum or a local minimum at c and f
is differentiable atc, then
f ′(c) = 0.
Proof. Assume f has a local maximum at c (the minimum case is analogous). For x→c + we have
f(x)−f(c)
x−c ≤0,
and forx→c − we have
f(x)−f(c)
x−c ≥0.
Iffis differentiable atc, then both one-sided limits exist and coincide withf ′(c). Therefore
0≤f ′(c)≤0,
hencef ′(c) = 0. □
Remark.Be careful: although maxima and minima of differentiable functions satisfy f ′(c) = 0, the
converse is false. For instance, for f(x) = x3 we have f ′(0) = 0, but 0 is neither a local maximum
nor a local minimum (it is a “saddle point”).
Definition 6.12(Critical point).A pointcis called acritical pointoffiff ′(c) = 0.
15
Remark.In optimisation problems, candidates for maxima/minima are often among the set of
critical points. (If you’re curious: this point of view sits behind theleast action principlein physics,
and even behindgradient descentmethods in machine learning.) Of course, after locating critical
points one typically needs additional information to decide whether they are maxima, minima, or
neither.
6.5.A historical example: The law of light refraction.We finish with a historically important
example showing how “minimising something” naturally leads to an equation involving a derivative.
Statement 1(Snell’s law).Let M1 and M2 be two media separated by a straight line (e.g. the
interface between air and water). Assume the speed of light is v1 in M1 and v2 in M2. If a light ray
travels from a point P∈M 1 to a point Q∈M 2 and crosses the interface making angles θ1 and θ2
with the normal, then
sinθ 1
sinθ 2
= v1
v2
.
Remark(Fermat’s insight).Fermat derived Snell’s law from a general variational principle: light
chooses the path that minimises travel time.
Statement 2(Fermat’s principle).The path light takes to travel between two points P and Q is
the path of least time.
Exercise 6.13(Derive Snell from Fermat).Assume Fermat’s principle. Show that it implies Snell’s
law.
Hint.Choose coordinates so that the interface is the liney= 0, with
P= (0, h)∈M 1, Q= (a,−b)∈M 2,
where a, b, h > 0are fixed. Let the ray cross the interface at( x, 0)with x∈ (0, a). Write the
distances
d1(x) =
p
x2 +h 2, d 2(x) =
p
(a−x) 2 +b 2,
and uset 1 =d 1/v1,t 2 =d 2/v2 to write the total time
T(x) = d1(x)
v1
+ d2(x)
v2
.
Now differentiate and set T ′(x) = 0, and interpret the resulting equation geometrically in terms of
sinθ 1 andsinθ 2. Finally, conclude that the critical point you found is a global minimum.
7.Existence of critical points
Let f : [a, b] →R be continuous on [ a, b] and differentiable on ( a, b). We proved (Extreme Value
Theorem) that continuous functions on compact sets always attain a maximum and a minimum.
A natural question is:can we use this to guarantee the existence of a critical point?
We have to be careful: local minima and maxima are critical pointsonly when the point is interior.
At the endpoints, there is no reason for the derivative to vanish.
Example 7.1.Let f : [a, b] →R be given by f(x) = x. Then f(a) is the minimum and f(b) is the
maximum on [a, b], but there is not∈(a, b) withf ′(t) = 0 (in factf ′(x) = 1 everywhere).
16
Nevertheless, in several situations wecanguarantee the existence of critical points. The cleanest
one is when the function starts and ends at the same height.
7.1.Critical points whenf(a) =f(b).
Theorem 7.2(Rolle’s Theorem).Let f : [a, b] →R be continuous on[ a, b]and differentiable on
(a, b). Iff(a) =f(b), then there existst∈(a, b)such that
f ′(t) = 0.
Proof.By the Extreme Value Theorem, there existst∈[a, b] such that
f(t) = max{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
Ift∈(a, b) is an interior point, thentis a local maximum, hencef ′(t) = 0 (Fermat’s theorem).
Iftis not interior, thent=aort=b. But sincef(a) =f(b), we must have
f(a) =f(b) = max{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
Apply the Extreme Value Theorem again to obtainx∈[a, b] such that
f(x) = min{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
Ifx∈(a, b), thenxis an interior local minimum and againf ′(x) = 0.
Ifxis not interior, thenx=aorx=b, so
f(a) = min{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}andf(a) = max{f(s) :s∈[a, b]}.
This means min[a,b] f = max[a,b] f, so f is constant on [a, b]. In that case f ′(t) = 0 for every t∈ (a, b),
and we are done. □
Remark.The picture to keep in mind is: if f(a) = f(b), then as you slide a horizontal line down
from above, it will touch the graph for the first time at some interior point, producing a horizontal
tangent.
7.2.Critical points when f ′(x) > 0 > f ′(y).There is another common mechanism that forces a
critical point: the derivative being positive at one point and negative at another. In this case, the
picture to keep in mind is that of a dome (or bowl depending on the signs).
Proposition 7.3.Let f : (a, b) →R be differentiable on( a, b). Assume x < y in( a, b)are such that
f ′(x)>0> f ′(y).
Then there existst∈(x, y)such thatf ′(t) = 0.
Proof. Since f ′(x) > 0, the local comparison lemma implies that there exists δ > 0 (small enough
so thatx+δ < y) such that for allp∈(x, x+δ) we have
f(p)> f(x).
In particular,fdoes not attain its maximum on [x, y] at the left endpointx.
17
Similarly, since f ′(y) < 0, there exists δ′ > 0 (small enough so that x < y−δ ′) such that for all
q∈(y−δ ′, y) we have
f(q)> f(y).
Sofdoes not attain its maximum on [x, y] at the right endpointyeither.
On the other hand, f is continuous on [ x, y] (because it is differentiable on ( a, b)), so by the
Extreme Value Theorem there existst∈[x, y] such that
f(t) = max{f(s) :s∈[x, y]}.
Since the maximum cannot occur at x or y, we must have t∈ (x, y). Thus t is an interior maximum
and Fermat’s theorem givesf ′(t) = 0.□
8.Two important applications
Even when f(a) ̸=f (b) we can still say something useful about the derivative. The key idea is:
subtract the straight line through (a, f(a)) and (b, f(b)) to reduce to Rolle’s theorem.
8.1.Mean Value Theorem.
Theorem 8.1(Mean Value Theorem).Let f : [a, b] →R be continuous on[ a, b]and differentiable
on(a, b). Then there existst∈(a, b)such that
f ′(t) = f(b)−f(a)
b−a .
Proof.Define the affine function (the secant line through the endpoints)
g(t) := f(b)−f(a)
b−a (t−a) +f(a).
Thengis continuous on [a, b], differentiable on (a, b), and satisfiesg(a) =f(a) andg(b) =f(b).
Therefore the functionh:=f−gis continuous on [a, b], differentiable on (a, b), and
h(a) =f(a)−g(a) = 0, h(b) =f(b)−g(b) = 0.
By Rolle’s theorem, there existst∈(a, b) such thath ′(t) = 0. But
h′(t) =f ′(t)−g ′(t), g ′(t) = f(b)−f(a)
b−a ,
soh ′(t) = 0 is exactly
f ′(t) = f(b)−f(a)
b−a .
□
Corollary 8.2.Iff ′(t)>0for allt∈(a, b), thenfis strictly increasing on(a, b).
Proof. Choose x < y in (a, b). Apply Cauchy’s Mean Value Theorem to f on [x, y] to find t∈ (x, y)
such that
f(y)−f(x)
y−x =f ′(t).
Since f ′(t) > 0 and y−x > 0, we get f(y) −f (x) > 0, hence f(x) < f (y). This holds for every
x < y, sofis strictly increasing.□
18
Exercise 8.3.Let f : J→R be a differentiable function. Show that if f ′(x) = 0, for all x∈J ,
then f is constant on J. (Note that this is the converse of Exercise 5.7 - Part (2). Hint: Use the
mean value theorem.)
8.2.The Intermediate Value Theorem for derivatives (Darboux).One might expect the
derivative to be continuous. This isfalsein general. However, a surprising feature remains true:
derivatives cannot jump over values.
Theorem 8.4(Intermediate Value Property for derivatives).Let f : (a, b) →R be differentiable
on( a, b). Then f ′ satisfies the intermediate value property: for every x < y in( a, b)and every real
numbercbetweenf ′(x)andf ′(y), there existst∈(x, y)such that
f ′(t) =c.
Remark.This holds even whenf ′ is not continuous.
Proof.Fixx < yand letcbe betweenf ′(x) andf ′(y). Consider
φ(t) :=f(t)−ct.
Thenφis differentiable on (a, b) and
φ′(t) =f ′(t)−c.
Since c lies between f ′(x) and f ′(y), the numbers φ′(x) and φ′(y) have opposite signs (or one is 0
already). Ifφ ′(x) = 0 orφ ′(y) = 0 we are done. Otherwise,
φ′(x)>0> φ ′(y) orφ ′(x)<0< φ ′(y).
Applying the previous proposition to φ on (x, y) gives a point t∈ (x, y) such that φ′(t) = 0. Hence
f ′(t)−c= 0, i.e.f ′(t) =c.□
Exercise 8.5.Find an example of a function that satisfies the Intermediate Value Property but it
is not continuous.
Corollary 8.6(Nonvanishing derivative implies strict monotonicity).Show that if f : (a, b) →R is
differentiable and
f ′(t)̸= 0for allt∈(a, b),
thenfis strictly monotone on(a, b).
Proof.By the Intermediate Value Theorem for derivatives,f ′ has the intermediate value property.
If f ′ took both positive and negative values, then it would also take the value 0 somewhere in
between. Since f ′(t) ̸= 0 everywhere, this cannot happen. Therefore either f ′(t) > 0 for all t or
f ′(t)<0 for allt.
In the first case, the corollary to Cauchy’s Mean Value Theorem shows that f is strictly increasing;
in the second case, applying the same result to−fshows thatfis strictly decreasing.□
Exercise 8.7(Functions with unit-speed derivative).Let J⊂R be an interval and let f : J→R
be differentiable. Assume that
|f ′(t)|= 1for allt∈J.
19
Prove that there exists a constantc∈Rsuch that either
f(t) =t+cfor allt∈J,
or
f(t) =−t+cfor allt∈J.
This fact will be used to show that an arc-length parametrisation of a curve is unique, up to a
translation of the parameter (and possibly reversing orientation).
9.Convexity
A good way to think about convexity is as a global version of the idea “the function bends
upward”. For C2 functions, this is exactly what the condition f ′′ ≥ 0 means. But convexity makes
sense (and is useful) even when a second derivative does not exist.
9.1.Convex sets.
Definition 9.1(Convex set).A set U⊂R 2 is calledconvexif for every two points p, q∈U , the
straight line segment joining p and q is also contained in U. Equivalently, for every t∈ [0, 1] we have
(1−t)p+tq∈U.
It is often convenient to parametrise the segment fromptoqby
γ: [0,1]→R 2, γ(t) = (1−t)p+tq.
In coordinates, ifp= (p 1, p2) andq= (q 1, q2) then
γ(t) = ((1−t)p 1 +tq 1,(1−t)p 2 +tq 2).
9.2.Convex functions.
Definition 9.2(Convex function).Let I⊂R be an interval and let f : I→R . We say that f is
convexonIif the regionaboveits graph is convex, i.e. if the set
epi(f) :={(x, y)∈I×R:y≥f(x)}
is convex inR 2.
Unwinding the definition gives a very concrete inequality.
Lemma 9.3(Jensen inequality for convex functions).Let I be an interval and f : I→R . Then f
is convex onIif and only if for alla < binIand allt∈[0,1],
(1)f((1−t)a+tb)≤(1−t)f(a) +tf(b).
Proof.Assume first thatfis convex. Consider the two points on the graph
P= (a, f(a)), Q= (b, f(b)).
The line segment between them is given by
(1−t)P+tQ= ((1−t)a+tb,(1−t)f(a) +tf(b)).
20
Since the epigraph of f is convex and contains P and Q, it contains the whole segment. In particular,
at the x–coordinate (1 −t )a + tb the corresponding y–coordinate on the segment must lieabovethe
graph off, which is exactly (1).
Conversely, assume (1) holds. Take any two points ( x1, y1), (x2, y2) ∈epi (f), so y1 ≥f (x1) and
y2 ≥f(x 2). Fort∈[0,1] we have
(1−t)y 1 +ty 2 ≥(1−t)f(x 1) +tf(x 2)≥f((1−t)x 1 +tx 2),
where the last inequality is (1). This shows (1 −t )(x1, y1) + t(x2, y2) ∈epi (f), hence the epigraph is
convex and so isf. □
9.3.The “graph lies below the chord” viewpoint.Fix a < b in I. The straight line through
(a, f(a)) and (b, f(b)) is the affine function
ℓa,b(x) := f(b)−f(a)
b−a (x−a) +f(a).
Note thatℓ a,b(a) =f(a) andℓ a,b(b) =f(b).
Lemma 9.4(Chord characterization).Let f : I→R . Then f is convex on I if and only if for all
a < x < binIwe have
(2)f(x)≤ℓ a,b(x).
Proof. Assume (1). Given a < x < b , write x = (1 −t )a + tb with t = x−a
b−a ∈ (0, 1). Then (1)
becomes
f(x)≤(1−t)f(a) +tf(b) =ℓ a,b(x),
which is (2). The converse is the same argument backwards.□
Exercise 9.5.Check carefully that(1)and(2)are equivalent.
9.4.Convexity and growth.In the last section we saw that if f is convex on an interval I,
then given a < b∈I we must have ℓa,b(x) ≤f (x) for all x∈ (a, b). A natural question is asking
what happens with f and ℓa,b outsideof the interval ( a, b). The answer, explained in the following
proposition, is that outside of (a, b) the inequality must reserve:
Proposition 9.6.Let f be convex on an open interval I. Then, given a < b∈I , we have
f(x)≥ℓ a,b(x)for allx∈I\(a, b).
Proof. We can argue by contradiction. Assume the inequality does not hold for some c∈I with
b < c(the casec < ais analogous), i.e.
f(c)< ℓ a,b(c).
Note that if this is the case, then ℓa,c(x) < ℓ a,b(x) for all x > a . This is because the linear functions
ℓa,b and ℓa,c intersect at most one point, which in this case is ℓa,b(a) = f(a) = ℓa,c(a). By the
Intermediate Value Theorem it follows that the difference ℓa,b(x) −ℓ a,c(x) is either positive for all
x > a or all negative for all x > a , but we are assuming ℓa,c(c) = f(c) < ℓ a,b, so the difference must
be always positive onx > a.
21
Finally, evaluating this inequality at x = b∈ (a, c) we obtain a f(b) = ℓa,b(b) > ℓ a,c(b), which
contradicts the convexity offon (a, c).□
9.5.Strict convexity.
Definition 9.7(Strict convexity).We say that f : I→R isstrictly convexon I if for all a < b in
Iand allt∈(0,1),
f((1−t)a+tb)<(1−t)f(a) +tf(b).
Equivalently, for alla < x < binI,
f(x)< ℓ a,b(x).
9.6.A useful criterion: monotonicity of f ′.Here is a simple and important mechanism that
produces strict convexity.
Theorem 9.8(Increasing derivative ⇒ strict convexity).Let I be an interval and let f : I→R be
differentiable. Iff ′ is strictly increasing onI, thenfis strictly convex onI.
Proof.Fixa < binIand consider the chord functionℓ a,b. We want to show that
f(x)< ℓ a,b(x) for allx∈(a, b).
Set
g(x) :=f(x)−ℓ a,b(x).
Thengis differentiable onI, and
g(a) = 0, g(b) = 0, g ′(x) =f ′(x)−ℓ ′
a,b(x) =f ′(x)− f(b)−f(a)
b−a .
Sincef ′ is strictly increasing andℓ ′
a,b is constant, it follows thatg ′ is strictly increasing as well.
We now claim that g(x) < 0 for every x∈ (a, b). Suppose not. Then there exists x∈ (a, b)
with g(x) ≥ 0. Since g(a) = g(b) = 0 and g is continuous, g attains a maximum on [ a, b], say at
x0 ∈ [a, b]. Our assumption forces the maximum to be nonnegative, hence it cannot occur only at
both endpoints unlessg≡0. So either:
Case 1:x 0 ∈(a, b). Thenx 0 is an interior maximum, henceg ′(x0) = 0.
Apply the Mean Value Theorem togon [a, x 0]. There existsx 1 ∈(a, x 0) such that
g′(x1) = g(x0)−g(a)
x0 −a = g(x0)
x0 −a ≥0.
Butx 1 < x0 andg ′ is strictly increasing, sog ′(x1)< g ′(x0) = 0, a contradiction.
Case 2:g≡0 on [a, b]. Then g′(x) = 0 for all x∈ (a, b), contradicting the fact that g′ is strictly
increasing.
In all cases we reach a contradiction, henceg(x)<0 for everyx∈(a, b). Therefore
f(x)−ℓ a,b(x) =g(x)<0 for allx∈(a, b),
which is exactly strict convexity.□
Remark.This is the calculus reason behind the slogan “ f ′′ > 0 means convex”: if f ′′(x) > 0 on I
(andf ′′ exists), thenf ′ is strictly increasing onI, hencefis strictly convex.
22
9.7.Why convexity matters.
Theorem 9.9.Letf:I→Rbe convex. Prove thatfis continuous onI.
Proof. Let a∈I and δ > 0 such that [ a−δ, a + δ] ⊂I . Fix x∈ [a−δ, a ]. By Proposition 9.6 we
haveℓ a,a+δ(x)≤f(x) and by the definition of convexity we havef(x)≤ℓ a−δ,a(x). It follows that
x∈[a−δ, a] =⇒ℓ a,a+δ(x)≤f(x)≤ℓ a−δ,a(x).
Since both ℓa,a+δ and ℓa,a+δ are continuous with ℓa−δ,a(a) = ℓa,a+δ(a) = f(a), by the Squeeze
Theorem for limits we conclude
lim
x→a+
f(x) =f(a).
The proof of
lim
x→a−
f(x) =f(a)
is similar. □
Exercise 9.10.Let f : I→R be strictly convex and differentiable. Show that f has at most one
critical point onI. Moreover, iff ′(c) = 0for somec∈I, thencis aglobalminimiser offonI.
Remark.Geometrically, strict convexity says the graph lies strictly below every chord. This is why
minimisation problems for strictly convex functions are so rigid: there is at most one place where
the slope can vanish, and when it does, it must be the unique global minimum.
10.The Inverse Function Theorem
When working with functions on the real line, it is common to encounter functions which are
locally bijective. This occurs, for example, if f : (a, b) →R is differentiable and satisfies f ′(x) ̸= 0
for all x∈ (a, b). In this section, we study when the continuity and differentiability of f imply the
same properties for its inversef −1.
Definition 10.1.Let f : (a, b) → (c, d) be a bijection. We say that a function g : (c, d) → (a, b)
is its inverse if f(g(x)) = x for all x∈ (c, d) (equivalently, g(f(y)) = y for all y∈ (a, b)). In this
situation, we writeg=f −1.
Remark.The notation f −1(u)does notmean the same as 1
f(u) ; it denotes the inverse function
in the sense of mappings between sets.
As mentioned earlier, monotone functions provide natural examples of bijections between intervals.
In fact—when continuity is assumed—these are theonlybijective functions between intervals. This
follows from the Intermediate Value Theorem and is encapsulated in the following exercise.
Exercise 10.2(This is a good exercise to discuss with your personal tutor).Show that if f is
continuous and injective on an interval( x, y), then f is either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing
on that interval.
First, we check that continuity is preserved.
23
Theorem 10.3.Let f : (a, b) → (c, d)be a continuous bijection, then f −1 is also continuous on
(c, d).
Proof. By the previous theorem we can assume that f is strictly monotone on ( a, b). Moreover,
replacing f by −f in the argument if necessary, we can assume that f is strictly increasing on ( a, b).
Fix y0 ∈ (c, d). We denote by g := f −1 the inverse of f and x0 = g(y0). Clearly f(x0) = y0.
We want to show that given ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that if y satisfies |y−y 0|< δ , then
|g(y) −g (y0)|< ε . In other words, we want to find δ > 0 such that y∈ (y0 −δ, y 0 + δ) implies
g(y)∈(g(y 0)−ε, g(y 0) +ε) = (x 0 −ε, x 0 +ε).
By the previous exercise, f is monotone on ( a, b). Therefore it is enough to choose δ such that
(y0 −δ, y 0 + δ) is contained inside of the image of the interval ( x0 −ε, x 0 + ε). We claim that
δ = inf{f (x0 + ε) −y 0, y0 −f (x0 −ε ))} does the work. First, the monotonicity of f implies δ > 0.
Second, it also implies that if f(x0 −ε) ≤y 0 −δ < y < y 0 + δ = f(x0 + ε) then x0 −ε < g (y) < x0 + ε,
which is what we wanted to show.
□
Theorem 10.4.Let f be continuous and injective on an open interval I. Assume that f is
differentiable at x0 ∈I with f ′(x0) ̸= 0. Then f −1 is differentiable at f(x0)and( f −1)′(f(x0)) =
1/f ′(x0).
Proof.Lety 0 =f(x 0). We want to prove that the limit whenhgoes to 0 of
f −1(y0 +h)−f −1(y0)
h
is 1/f ′(x0). From the injectivity of f we know that for each small h there exists a unique k = k(h)
such that y0 + h = f(x0 + k). Moreover, the continuity of f −1 proven in the last theorem, means
that limh→0 k(h) = 0, since
y0 +h=f(x 0 +k) =⇒f −1(y0 +h) =x 0 +k.
At this stage we can rewrite the quotient in terms off:
f −1(y0 +h)−f −1(y0)
h = f −1(f(x 0 +k))−f −1(f(x 0))
f(x 0 +k)−y 0
= (x0 +k)−x 0
f(x 0 +k)−y 0
= k
f(x 0 +k)−f(x 0) .
Finally, since limh→0 k= 0 and lim k→0
f(x 0+k)−f(x 0)
k =f ′(x0)̸= 0 we obtain:
lim
h→0
f −1(y0 +h)−f −1(y0)
h = 1
f ′(x0) .
□
Theorem 10.5(The Inverse Function Theorem).Let I an open interval. Assume f : I→R is a
C1(I)function, i.e. f is differentiable on I and its derivative is continuous on I. Then, if f ′(a) ̸= 0
24
for a∈I , there exists δ > 0such that f : (a−δ, a + δ) →R is invertible and its inverse f −1 is
differentiable with(f −1)′(f(t)) = 1/f ′(t).
Marco A. M. Guaraco - Imperial College London

Spring Programme Overview 
3
Programme Details
The 2026 Deutsche Bank Spring Programme is scheduled to 
take place from Monday 30 March – Thursday 2 April in 
London.
The four-day orientation programme offers insight into the 
world of Deutsche Bank, including the following four 
divisions: Investment Banking & Capital Markets, Fixed 
Income & Currencies, Corporate Bank and Technology, Data 
and Innovation. Visit The Unofficial Guide to Banking to 
learn more.
You will learn how financial markets work from expert 
trainers, build your professional skills through interactive 
case studies, simulations, panel discussions and senior 
speaker sessions. 
These activities are designed to provide you with a 
comprehensive understanding of the industry and help you 
develop the competencies needed for a career in finance.

Final Round Live Video Interview
4
The final round interview will be conducted by video
What to expect
A 30-minute interview which includes:
— Competency / skills-based interview
— Motivation question
— Business acumen questions
This interview will be conducted via MS Teams (link will be provided).
Please also ensure you are in a quiet area where you will not be disturbed. 
Please also take a moment to ensure your internet connection, camera and 
microphone are working properly. 
If you are successful after the final video interview, you will then be 
offered a place on the programme. The decision will be communicated 
within two weeks.

Interview Preparation Tips
5
Preparation is key to any successful interview and to help you to 
prepare we have included a few hints and tips below:
- Know your CV. Your interviewer wants to get to know you and hear about 
your experience and areas of interest outside of your academics.
- Think about at least one or two examples against each of the key 
competencies and try answering the questions in a logical and structured 
manner (e.g. STAR: situation, task, action, results).
- Think about the transferable skills you can bring into the business division 
you have applied for. We are not expecting you to have relevant 
experience but are keen to understand your passions and motivations for 
the industry.
- Keep up to date with current affairs/news and understand how these can 
impact the industry and Deutsche Bank specifically. Even better, if you 
can link this to the business area you are applying to.
- Make sure to do your research before the interview. Have a browse of our 
website as we share a number of articles and updates here.

Video Interview Etiquette
Bring your personality. 
We want to know about you too. Don’t forget to be 
professional – think about the business etiquette.
Ensure that you are aware of your confirmed interview time 
and do not schedule any other appointments around this 
time. 
We would encourage you to join 5 mins before your 
interview start time to make sure your laptop (or alternative 
device) has sufficient charge and your internet, camera and 
microphone are all working.
Make sure you are in a quiet environment during the 
interview to avoid distractions/background noise.
First impressions count

Welcome
These are lecture notes for the second part of Calculus and Applications first year
module at the Department of Mathematics, Imperial College London. The notes
are split into three parts on Fourier Transform, Ordinary Differential Equations
and Introduction to Multivariate Calculus. Please refer to course Blackboard
for additional materials recommended text books for further reading.
These lecture notes are adobted from existing courses in our department. Part
I of the course is based on the old M2AA2 course (Andrew Walton) and Part II
and III are based on the old M1M2 course (Frank Berkshire, Mauricio Barahona,
Andrew Parry). Some examples and ideas from the old mechanics course M1A1
is included as well.
These notes are produced with accessibility in mind and I hope it meets your
requirements. You can experiment with the controls in the toolbar at the top of
the html version of the notes. You can search for a word, adjust typeface, font
size, font and background color. You can also download a copy of these notes
in differnt formats, if you wish for offline use. I hope you enjoy this course and
let me know if you have any comments or questions by email.
© Vahid Shahrezaei (2021) These notes are provided for the personal study of
students taking this module. The distribution of copies in part or whole is not
permitted.
5
6 CONTENTS
Part I: Fourier Transforms
7

Chapter 1
Fourier Transforms
Last term, we saw that Fourier series allows us to represent a given function,
defined over a finite range of the independent variable, in terms of sine and co-
sine waves of different amplitudes and frequencies. Fourier Transforms are the
natural extension of Fourier series for functions defined over ℝ. A key reason for
studying Fourier transforms (and series) is that we can use these ideas to help
us solve differential equations as seen in this course regarding ordinary differen-
tial equations and more extensively next year in relation to partial differential
equations. There are also many other applications for Fourier transforms in
science and engineering, particularly in the context of signal processing.
1.1 Fourier’s integral formula
We can represent a function 𝑓(𝑥)defined over the interval [−𝐿, 𝐿]using the

of different functions. After discussing some basic properties, we will discuss,
convolution theorem and energy theorem. Finally, we introduc Dirac delta
function.

Introduction to ordinary

Differential equations are very important in science and engineering. In this
course, we focus on a specific class of differential equations called ordinary dif-
ferential equations (ODEs). Ordinary refers to dealing with functions of one in-
dependent variable. We initially focus on scaler functions of the form 𝑓 ∶ ℝ → ℝ .
Later in the course, we discuss systems of ODEs and consider vector functions
of the form 𝑓 ∶ ℝ → ℝ 𝑛. We assume 𝑓(𝑥)is differentiable upto order 𝑘. An ODE
is an equation for the function 𝑓(𝑥)that involves the function, its derivatives
and the independent variable. A general form for an ODE of order 𝑘 is

degree of the ODE is the power of highest derivative (when fractional powers
have been removed). The ODE is called linear if G is a linear function of 𝑓(𝑥)
and its derivatives. This form for the ODE is the so called implicit form. In
an explicit form for an ODE, the highest order derivative is given as function of
the lower derivatives:

Example 3.2 (Second Newton Law) .
Mechanics: A very short introduction
Kinematics is a branch of mechanics that describes the motion of points (objects)
without considering the forces that cause them to move. In one dimension 𝑥(𝑡)
denotes the position of a particle at time 𝑡. Then 𝑑𝑥
𝑑𝑡 = ̇ 𝑥 = 𝑣 is defined as

can be generalised to higher dimensions using vectors of location, velocity and
acceleration.
Dynamics is the branch of mechanics concerned with the study of forces and
their effects on motion. Isaac Newton came up with the fundamental physical
laws, which govern dynamics in physics:
-First law an object not acted upon by any force either remains at rest or
continues to move at a constant velocity
-Second law the vector sum of the forces 𝐹 on an object is equal to the mass
𝑚 of that object multiplied by its acceleration 𝑎: 𝐹 = 𝑚𝑎

This is a second order ODE for the position of the object 𝑥(𝑡).
-Third law when one body exerts a force on a second body, the second body
exerts a force equal in magnitude and opposite in direction on the first body.

Example 6.1 (Predator-prey systems) .
These models can be used to predict the dynamics of predator and prey systems
such as rabbits ( 𝑥(𝑡)) and foxes ( 𝑦(𝑡)). A classic model is Lotka-Volterra model
(1925/26) that can exhibit a periodic solution of the population of predator and
preys as one goes up and the other goes down.

Example 6.4 (SIR model of an epidemic) .
One of the simplest but influential models of an epidemic is the compartmental
SIR model. In this model, disease propagates between the population compart-
ments through susceptible individuals ( 𝑆) becoming infected ( 𝐼) after encoun-
tering other infected individuals with rate 𝛽 and finally moving to a recovered
population ( 𝑅) with rate 𝛾. The following simple system of ODEs describes the
dynamics of this compartmental model.

This model and its variations are the basis of a lot of mathematical modelling
that has been performed for predicting the effect of different interventions in
the Covid-19 world-wide pandemic. If you like to learn a bit more about the
SIR ODE system above check out this video. Also, the SIR model can be
modeled using a so-called agent-based stochastic approach, where the individuals
and their random interactions are specifically followed. You can check out this
interesting video from the 3blue1brown series to see some cool exploration of
this approach to SIR models.

Chapter 7
Qualitative analysis of
ODEs
So far we have focused to obtain analytical solution to ODEs, but this is not
always possible. Even, when it is possible, it is not always very insightful. In
this section, we will focus on qualitative analysis of ODEs and as before we
mostly focus on linear ODEs. We’ll discuss asymptotics behavior, fixed points
(and their stability) and phase plane analysis.
7.1 asymptotic behaviour
In qualitative analysis of an ODE, asymptotic behaviour of solution 𝑦(𝑡)as
𝑡 → ∞ is one aspect of solutions we look at.

Stability of fixed points
Informally, a fixed point is stable if whenever the initial state is near that point,
the state remains near it, perhaps even tending toward the equilibrium point as
time increases. Formally, we have two types of stability as described below.
Lyapunov stability
A fixed point ⃗ 𝑦∗ is said to be Lyapunov stable , if for every 𝜖 > 0 , there exists a
𝛿 > 0 such that, if ‖ ⃗ 𝑦(0) − ⃗ 𝑦∗‖ < 𝛿 , then for ∀𝑡 ≥ 0, we have ‖ ⃗ 𝑦(𝑡) − ⃗ 𝑦∗‖ < 𝜖 .
Intuitively, it means the solution does not blow up but also does not necessarily
approach to the fixed point.
Asymptotic stability
7.2. PHASE PLANE ANALYSIS 63
A fixed point ⃗ 𝑦∗ is said to be Asymptotically stable , if it is Lyapunov stable and
there exists a 𝛿 > 0 such that, if ‖ ⃗ 𝑦(0) − ⃗ 𝑦∗‖ < 𝛿 , then we have
lim
𝑡→∞
‖ ⃗ 𝑦(𝑡) − ⃗ 𝑦∗‖ = 0.
Intuitively, in this case the solution does approach to the fixed point over long
times.
7.2 Phase plane analysis
The general solution of systems of ODEs is given by the family of parametric
curves, specified by the initial condition:
⃗ 𝑦(𝑡; 𝑐1, ⋯ , 𝑐𝑛) ∈ ℝ𝑛.
These solutions represent trajectories in ℝ𝑛 for a system of 𝑛 dimensional ODEs.
For a 2 dimensional systme the family of solutions starting from different initial
conditions can be represented in a so called phase plane as illustrated in Figure
7.1.
Figure 7.1: Illustration of phase plane for a 2 dimensional system. Two solutions
starting at different initial conditions are shown (blue). Also, some representa-
tive velocity vectors from the vector field are drawn (red).
One can interpret the phase plane in terms of dynamics. The solution ⃗ 𝑦(𝑡)
corresponds to a trajectory of a point moving on the phase plane with velocity
𝑑 ⃗ 𝑦
𝑑𝑡(𝑡). For a system of first order ODEs of the form:
𝑑 ⃗ 𝑦
𝑑𝑡 = 𝐹 ( ⃗ 𝑦),
64 CHAPTER 7. QUALITATIVE ANALYSIS OF ODES
where, there is no explicit dependence on the independent variable (time) on
the right hand side, the velocity is a vector defined at every point of the phase
plane and is tangent to the trajectory. This is called the vector field. See Figure
7.1.
Uniqueness of solutions of ODEs
Solutions of ODEs are uniquely defined by initial conditions except at some
special points in the phase plane (no proof now, you will see in the second year
rigorous proof). Trajectories in the phase plane cannot cross (except at some
special points) as this would be equivalent of non-uniqueness of solutions. The
special points are fixed points or singular points where trajectories start or end.
Phase plane analysis for the linear systems of first order ODEs
For linear systems, the vector field has some very nice properties.

7.4 Phase plane analysis for 2D nonlinear sys-
tems
Phase plane analysis is a very useful tool for 2 dimensional nonlinear systems and
the insights obtained from the 2D linear case is highly relevant. One obtains the
special points (fixed points) and could linearise the equations in the vicinity of
these points to get insight from the analysis of the corresponding linear system.
Vector field and asymptotic behaviour is useful to identify the trajectories in
the phase plane.
Example 7.7 (Synthetic Biology: Genetic Toggle Switch) .
This model of a simple synthetic genetic network was proposed in a pioneering
paper by Gardener, Cantor and Colins, Nature 403:339-342 (2000). Consider
two genes 𝑢 and 𝑣, which inhibit expression of one another. The following
system of nonlinear ODEs characterises the dynamics between the two genes.

The approach directly generalises for the linear systems as the solutions are

these directions and grow or decay depending on the sign of the corresponding
eigenvalue.
Stability of linear 𝑛 dimensional systems For the 2 dimensional case we
had stability where 𝜏 ≤ 0 and Δ ≥ 0 . In terms of eigenvalue characterization
this meant the real part of the eigen values are negative. Similarly, for the
general 𝑛 dimensional linear systems of ODEs we have stability if the real part
of all the eigenvalues are negative.

More complex dynamics in phase planes are possible. Lorenz proposed a system

3 and 𝜌 = 28),
exhibits a complex non-periodic dynamics that is an example of chaos. This

Introduction to Bifurcations
Bifurcations in a dynamical system (system of ODEs) describe the qualitative
change in behavior under a variation or change of some parameters of the system.
Parameters are constants that are tunable.

8.2.3 Pitchfork bifurcation
This kind of bifurcation is common in physical systems that have a symmetry.
There are two subtypes of pitchfork bifurcation.
Supercritical pitchfork bifurcation
The prototypical example of supercritical pitchfork bifurcation is given by:
𝑑𝑦
𝑑𝑡 = 𝑟𝑦 − 𝑦3 = 𝑓(𝑦; 𝑟)
Note that the equation is invariant under the change of variable 𝑦 → −𝑦, which
signifies the symmetry. The system has up to three fixed points 𝑦∗ = 0 and
𝑦∗ = ±√𝑟. Figure 8.8 using the vector field, illustrates number and stability of
these fixed points for different values of 𝑟.
For 𝑟 < 0 we have 1 stable fixed point, at 𝑟 = 0 we have still one stable fixed
point and for 𝑟 > 0 we have three fixed points (two stable and a middle one
that is unstable). At the bifurcation point 𝑟 = 0 an exchange of stabilities takes
place between the fixed points. The changes in the number and stability of the
fixed points is summarised in the bifurcation diagram (see Figure 8.9).

8.2.5 Impossibility of oscillations for one dimensional sys-
tems
Fixed points dominate the dynamics of first-order systems. The trajectory in
one dimension phase plane never reverses direction and the approach to equi-
librium is always monotonic, hence there is no over-shoot, damped oscillations
or periodic solutions. This is a topological constraint, if you flow monotonically
on a line, you’ll never come back to your starting point. Of course, if we were
moving on a circle rather than a line, we could eventually return to starting
point. For example the following ODE 𝑑𝜃
𝑑𝑡 = 𝜔 for the angle 𝜃 has the following
periodic solution 𝜃 = 𝜔𝑡 + 𝜃0.

ℎ(𝑥, 𝑦). One should be aware of this notational ambiguity.
9.6 Implicit functions
First, a reminder about the explicit form for an ordinary function:
𝑦 = 𝑓(𝑥); 𝑥 ∈ ℝ
The implicit form for an ordinary function is
𝐹 (𝑥, 𝑦) = 0.
Trivially, if we have the explicit form we also have an implicit form:
𝐹 (𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑦 − 𝑓(𝑥) = 0.

𝑧 = 𝑧(𝑥, 𝑦)
And implicit form:
𝐹 (𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧) = 0

Example 9.3 (Obtain the partial derivatives of 𝑧 using the explicit and implicit
forms).
Let 𝑧(𝑥, 𝑦) = 𝑥2 + 𝑦2 − 5.

Using the implicit form 𝐹 (𝑥, 𝑦, 𝑧) = 𝑥2 + 𝑦2 − 5 − 𝑧, we have:
𝑑𝐹 = 2𝑥𝑑𝑥 + 2𝑦𝑑𝑦 − 𝑑𝑧 = 0.
Which results to the same total derivative for 𝑑𝑧, that we obtained above from
the explicit form.

Chapter 10
Applications of

In Chapter 9, we introduced multivariable functions and notions of differentia-
tion. In this chapter, we present several applications of multivariate calculus.
10.1 Change of Coordinates

could be a nonlinear transformation. In this section, we focus on the familiar
example of the change of coordinates from polar coordinates ( ⃗ 𝑥 = (𝑟, 𝜃)) to
cartesian ( ⃗ 𝑥 = (𝑥, 𝑦)) and vice versa to illustrate the concepts. We generalise

documents.title: Culture&Policies—AGreatSpooner—CompanyWebsite
documents.text:
© Bending Spoons S.p.A. All rights reserved. 
A Great Spooner 
Introduction 
With this document, we detail the qualities that make for a great Spooner. Nobody can embody all of these 
qualities ﬂawlessly—each one of us falls short of the ideal in one way or another, and that’s OK. Still, we 
hope that by reading (and re-reading) what follows, a Spooner is inspired to aim higher than ever and work 
tirelessly toward becoming the very best version of themselves. 
We recommend that a Spooner take full responsibility for their own growth and approach it with a can-do 
attitude, believing they’re capable of achieving anything they set their mind to. To maximize their chances 
of success, a Spooner should make a 
ﬁrm commitment to put in the necessary eﬀort, learn from others, 
and stick at it with discipline over the long term. 
Lastly, we hope this document—and, most importantly, the example of a Spooner striving every day to 
embody the qualities it describes—helps us re
ﬁne and strengthen our company culture, with the ambition 
of making it ever more distinctive and powerful. 
The qualities of a great Spooner 
Extreme ownership 
A great Spooner feels profoundly responsible for their work, their team, and the company at large, and 
acts accordingly. They continually look for ways to make a positive impact on Bending Spoons with a 
holistic, long-term view. They don’t wait to be told what to do, but rather approach every day with an 
entrepreneurial outlook, proactively spotting and seizing opportunities. They understand the relevant 
objectives, and keep them top of mind at all times. They speak up, even when it’s tough to do so. They get 
their hands dirty. They’re tenacious in the face of a challenge. They’re a thorough 
ﬁnisher: While they’re 
quick to abandon an unpromising project or task, they don’t let a high-priority one fall through the cracks 
or remain incomplete, instead making sure it’s suitably taken care of (including when that involves pushing 
other stakeholders). 
This is the trait upon which a Spooner’s greatness depends the most. 
____________________________________________________________________________________________________ 
1 

© Bending Spoons S.p.A. All rights reserved. 
Pragmatism and sense of urgency 
With each project and task, a great Spooner identiﬁes and follows the approach that lends itself to making 
an impact at the highest possible rate—whether that approach is scrappy, pristine, or somewhere in 
between. Rather than being hasty, simplistic, or short-term oriented, they’re thoughtful and keep a holistic, 
long-term view, yet they push things forward with pace and a healthy dose of impatience. When in doubt 
as to the appropriate balance between speed and sophistication, they favor the former. 
Selﬂess team-play 
A great Spooner acts with the company’s best interests at heart. They’re an altruistic team member. Where 
useful, they eagerly carry out projects and tasks they 
ﬁnd unappealing and pick up skills they wouldn’t 
otherwise be interested in acquiring. They’re sensitive to the needs of their colleagues and generous in 
helping out. They keep the relevant individuals informed of their objectives and progress. They’re honest 
and transparent. They’re reliable. After voicing their disagreement with a decision, they set their 
skepticism aside and execute with full commitment. 
Good judgment 
A great Spooner makes well-informed, logically sharp, and radically rational decisions, optimizing for the 
whole company and for the long term. Rather than mindlessly jumping into execution mode, they diligently 
prioritize and architect their e
ﬀorts ﬁrst. Then, they stay focused on the identiﬁed priorities, making prompt 
adjustments should the boundary conditions change. Far from being overly academic, they root their 
thinking 
ﬁrmly in reality. They identify areas where they lack critical knowledge, then they acquire it rapidly. 
They weigh rewards and risks appropriately. They pay keen attention to the details, yet they never lose 
sight of the big picture. They support the best idea, not their idea. While they value consensus, they don’t 
compromise on making the right decision just to keep the peace. They assess their decisions in retrospect 
and update their priors as a consequence. 
Relentless simpliﬁcation 
A great Spooner ﬁghts unnecessary complexity, including by placing the burden of proof on those in favor 
of adding any and by seeking to eliminate that which already exists (no matter when and by whom it was 
____________________________________________________________________________________________________ 
2 

© Bending Spoons S.p.A. All rights reserved. 
introduced). Their resolute pursuit of simplicity spans the gamut of what we do at Bending Spoons, be it 
with a piece of software, a policy, a process, a product, a team, or something else entirely. 
Endless invention 
A great Spooner questions the status quo. They imagine how things could be, unconstrained by how 
things are. They foster a creative, think-big environment. They strike a good balance between looking for 
10x opportunities while relentlessly seeking incremental improvement. 
Deep knowledge 
A great Spooner possesses the detailed, extensive knowledge they need to be an eﬀective, hands-on 
contributor in their position and similar ones. 
Positive energy 
A great Spooner enhances the morale of those around them. While not being blindly optimistic, they tend 
to see the bright side of things. They confront adversity with resilience, a growth mindset, and a smile on 
their face. They receive all feedback with gratitude. They’re informal and approachable. They never act 
superior or entitled. They don’t act defensively, complain unconstructively, gossip, or make disrespectful 
comments. 
Sky-high standards 
A great Spooner sets and upholds exceedingly high standards. They hold themselves and their colleagues 
accountable for meeting these standards. They’re unequivocally intolerant of a lack of ambition or eﬀort, 
and don’t accept a substandard contribution just to avoid a diﬃcult conversation. They see to it that a 
shortcoming—be it their own or someone else’s—is overcome as quickly as possible. 
Eﬀective coaching 
A great Spooner prioritizes helping their colleagues improve on all of the above. They personalize their 
coaching style depending on the speci
ﬁcs of the colleague, project, and task. They provide actionable, 
____________________________________________________________________________________________________ 
3 

© Bending Spoons S.p.A. All rights reserved. 
radically candid, and high-quality feedback—promptly and often. They’re an active, demanding, and 
supportive teacher. They document knowledge and deﬁne guidelines where it’s eﬃcient to do so. 
Conclusion 
We’ve spent a lot of time identifying and describing the qualities that contribute to being a great Spooner. 
When it comes to these qualities, we don’t expect perfection across the board—every individual has their 
own strong and not-so-strong suits, and progress inevitably comes more easily with some than with 
others. Still, a Spooner who makes a deliberate e
ﬀort to develop along the dimensions outlined above is 
destined to, over time, increase their impactfulness. Just as importantly, the more a Spooner strives for 
greatness here, the more powerful and distinctive our company culture gets. And the higher our chances 
become of succeeding in our ambitions. 
____________________________________________________________________________________________________ 
4

documents.title: diagnosis 1
documents.text:
9 February 2026
 
 
 
 
 
 
9 February 2026
AuDHD Psychiatry
Third Floor, 3 Hill Street
New Town
Edinburgh
EH2 3JP
support@audhdpsychiatry.co.uk
Tel: 0131 516 1656
 
To Whom It May Concern,
Re:  Application for Exceptional Circumstances
I am writing in my capacity as a Consultant Psychologist specialising in ADHD and Autism assessments to con2rm that Mr
Neel Agrawal has undergone a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and has been formally diagnosed by AuDHD Psychiatry
with 
Attention De	cit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) and Autism
, as per DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, guided by NICE
guidelines.
These diagnoses were made following two structured clinical interviews:
"DIVA" (Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in Adults), completion of validated ADHD rating scales, and corroborating
information from multiple informants.  
"ACIA" (Autism Clinical Interview for Adults), DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, completion of validated Autism rating scales
and corroborating information from multiple informants.  
The assessment identi2ed clinically signi2cant dif2culties, with symptoms evident across multiple settings since childhood
and persisting into adulthood.
 
Impact on Academic Performance in Relation to ADHD 
 
Neel’s ADHD symptoms have had a substantial and ongoing impact on their academic functioning.  These include:
Severe dif	culties initiating and completing assignments
, often requiring last-minute efforts and overnight work to
meet deadlines, which has affected the quality and consistency of their submissions.
Pronounced challenges with sustained attention
 during lessons/lectures, leading to dif2culty retaining information,
missing key instructions, and falling behind during in-class teaching.
Marked organisational impairments
, resulting in missed deadlines, misplaced items essential for study, and frequent
disruptions to study routines.
Disrupted sleep patterns
, often linked to hyperactivity and dif2culty winding down, which further exacerbate
difficulties with concentration, time management, and daily academic demands.
 Email : support@audhdpsychiatry.co.uk
Executive functioning impairments
, including poor working memory and dif2culty prioritising tasks, which limit their
ability to plan and pace their workload across a term.
Despite Neel's intellectual potential and strong motivation to succeed, these ADHD-related impairments have placed them
at a distinct disadvantage in a self-directed academic environment that lacks the external structure found in earlier
educational settings.
 
Impact on Academic Performance in Relation to Autism 
 
Neel’s autistic profile has had a substantial and ongoing impact on their academic functioning.  These include:
Signi	cant dif	culties with initiating and completing assignments 
- often due to challenges with task initiation,
uncertainty about how to begin, or becoming overwhelmed by the steps involved.  This may result in delayed work,
inconsistent output, or reliance on last-minute efforts once the task becomes clearer or urgency increases.
Challenges with sustaining attention during lessons/lectures 
- particularly when the material is not of strong personal
interest or when sensory distractions are present.  This leads to reduced retention of information, missed instructions,
and difficulty keeping pace with the flow of teaching.
Marked organisational differences 
- including dif2culty managing multiple tasks, tracking deadlines, and maintaining
necessary study materials.  These challenges can lead to missed or late submissions and disruptions to established
study routines.
Sleep disturbances 
- often associated with sensory sensitivities, anxiety, or dif2culty disengaging from focused
interests.  These disrupted sleep patterns further affect Neel’s daytime concentration, cognitive stamina, and ability to
manage academic demands.
Executive functioning differences 
- including reduced working memory, dif2culty shifting between tasks, and
challenges with planning and sequencing.  These factors limit Neel’s ability to break tasks into manageable steps,
prioritise effectively, and distribute workload evenly across a term.
Despite Neel's intellectual potential and strong motivation to succeed, these Autism-related impairments have placed them
at a distinct disadvantage in a self-directed academic environment that lacks the external structure found in earlier
educational settings.
 
Clinical Summary and Recommendation
 
Under the Equality Act 2010, neurodivergent individuals, 
including those with ADHD and Autism, are protected as 'disabled'
where the condition 
has a substantial and long-term impact on daily function—triggering a
 duty to implement reasonable
adjustments.
 
Given the above, it is my professional opinion that Neel's academic performance has been negatively and substantially
impacted by untreated ADHD symptoms and Autism, particularly during periods of assessment and work deadlines.  These
challenges are not reAective of a lack of effort or academic ability, but rather of the disabling nature of ADHD when
unrecognised and unsupported and Autism.
 
Now that a formal diagnosis has been established, Neel is eligible to access support and potentially pharmacological
treatment.  However, it is important to note that the dif2culties to date occurred 
prior to diagnosis and without any
reasonable adjustments or interventions in place
.
 
In light of this, I strongly support Neel’s application for 
retrospective exceptional circumstances
 to acknowledge the
disadvantage they have experienced and to ensure they are not unfairly penalised for the functional impairments associated
with their condition.  Neel would likely have bene2ted from earlier recognition and support, and I would be happy to provide
further clinical documentation if required.
 
I also recommend that a meeting is set up to consider which adjustments will be most helpful and if any referrals need to be
made to other services or for additional support.  Please formalise any agreed adjustments in writing and have regular
reviews to assess impact and adapt as needed.
 
Should you have any further questions or require additional information, please do not hesitate to contact me.
Kind regards,
 Email : support@audhdpsychiatry.co.uk
Dr Darren O'Reilly
Consultant Psychologist
Doctorate in Counselling Psychology | Chartered Psychologist | HCPC Registered Practitioner Psychologist | HCPC:
PYL041661
 Email : support@audhdpsychiatry.co.uk

1 Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs
Drossopoulou & Wheelhouse (DoC) COMP40006: Reasoning about Programs 46 / 331
Slide 46
35
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs
Part I – Outline
2 Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs
Mathematical Induction
Strong Induction
Structural induction over Haskell data types
Induction over any recursively defined structures
Drossopoulou & Wheelhouse (DoC) COMP40006: Reasoning about Programs 47 / 331
Slide 47
36
1.1 Mathematical Induction
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs Mathematical Induction
Motivation: The game of frogs
With thanks to Bartosz D. Wozniak
The game of frogs takes place on a plank of n meters length, where n : N.
It starts with m frogs at different positions on the plank (where m : N).
The frogs move from reft to right or from right to left. They all have
different, non-zero speeds. When they collide, the frog with the highest
speed continues his journey, while the rest flip direction.
When a frog reaches the edge of the plank, it falls off the plank, and
morphs into a prince.
Is there some initial configuration for which in all future steps there will be
at least one frog left on the plank?
Drossopoulou & Wheelhouse (DoC) COMP40006: Reasoning about Programs 48 / 331
Slide 48
“In the ‘Game of Frogs’, you swim or die.”
For all initial configurations eventually all frogs will have been turned to princes. This
can be proven by mathematical induction over the number of frogs on the plank.
37
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs Mathematical Induction
Motivation-2: The beetle and the cactus
A beetle eats the leaves of a cactus, but when it does so, the cactus grows
back. The question is whether the beetle will consume the cactus.
In more detail: The cactus corresponds to a tree. When the beetle eats
one of the leaves of that tree, then the leaf ( lf ) is removed from the tree.
Moreover, if lf is not a child of the root, then, the cactus will grow back
as follows:
We call the stub the subtree with root parent(lf) from which lf has been
removed. Add two copies of the stub under parent(parent(lf)).
The cactus is consumed when it consists of the root only.
Is there a cactus which no beetle can consume?
Can the beetle avoid ever consuming the cactus?
Drossopoulou & Wheelhouse (DoC) COMP40006: Reasoning about Programs 49 / 331
Slide 49
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs Mathematical Induction
Motivation-3: The beetle and the super-cactus
A variation on previous: When the super-cactus grows back, it can make
an arbitrary number of copies of the stub.
In more detail: The cactus corresponds to a tree. When the beetle eats
one of the leaves of that tree, then the leaf ( lf ) is removed from the tree.
Moreover, if lf is not a child of the root, then, the cactus will grow back
as follows:
We call the stub the subtree with root parent(lf) from which lf has been
removed. Add k copies of the stub under parent(parent(lf)), where k is
an arbitrary natural number.
The cactus is consumed when it consists of the root only.
Is there a super-cactus which no beetle can consume?
Can the beetle avoid ever consuming the super-cactus?
Drossopoulou & Wheelhouse (DoC) COMP40006: Reasoning about Programs 50 / 331Slide 50
The super-cactus is a more general question than the cactus.
38
For the first question, ne can show that for all possible cacti, the beetle has a strategy
whereby to consume the whole cactus. For this you need to show that the beetle has a
strategy whereby it decreases the depth of the tree, and if the tree has depth 1, then the
beetle can consume it – this is a more sophisticated proof by induction.
For the second question, the answer is that no matter what the beetle does, it cannot keep
the cactus alive. The proof requires a more sophisticated form of structural induction,
and we shall probably not have time to discuss it in the course. But you can find out
more about this under Hydra and Hercules.
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs Mathematical Induction
Induction in general
Induction can be used to prove statements of the form
∀x :S.P(x)
where S is an enumerable set, and P⊆S.
Examples of properties of enumerable sets.
∀n : N.(7n + 5 is exactly divisible by 3)
∀n : N.∀m : N.Given a plank of length n and with m frogs,
eventually there will only be princes left.
∀xs:[a].∀ys:[a].
length(xs ++ ys) = length(xs) + length(ys)
Drossopoulou & Wheelhouse (DoC) COMP40006: Reasoning about Programs 51 / 331
Slide 51
P ⊆ S means that P is a property of of elements of the set S. For example, pos ⊂ Z.
Examples of enumerable sets are the natural numbers ( N), sequences, strings, or Haskell
data structures such as lists, trees, etc. R is not an enumerable set.
39
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs Mathematical Induction
Principle of mathematical induction
For any P⊆N :
P (0) ∧ ∀k : N.[P (k)→P (k + 1) ] −→ ∀n : N.P(n)
Drossopoulou & Wheelhouse (DoC) COMP40006: Reasoning about Programs 52 / 331
Slide 52
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs Mathematical Induction
Principle of mathematical induction – as in Discreet Structures
For any P⊆N :
P (0) ∧ ∀k : N.[P (k)→P (k + 1) ] −→ ∀n : N.P(n)
Drossopoulou & Wheelhouse (DoC) COMP40006: Reasoning about Programs 53 / 331Slide 53
40
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs Mathematical Induction
Mathematical induction principle for ∑n
i=0 i = n∗(n+1)
2

Drossopoulou & Wheelhouse (DoC) COMP40006: Reasoning about Programs 54 / 331
Slide 54
Part I: Reasoning About Haskell Programs Mathematical Induction
Proof schema for ∀n : N.∑n
