Group Theory 2025
Marie-Amelie Lawn, edited by Alexei Skorobogatov
based on notes by Matthew Towers
Some good references for this course are:
• A concise introduction to pure mathematics , Martin Liebeck (510 LIE in the Central
Library level 3). Mainly for the section on permutations.
• Sets and groups , J.A. Green (512.54 GRE, 510.22 GRE).
• A book of abstract algebra , C.C. Pinter. Available as an ebook through the Imperial
library website.
There are lots of introductory group theory textbooks: try a few and find one you like. Visual
group theory by Nathan Carter is very different to standard textbooks and is worth looking
at if you like to think geometrically.
Contents
1 Basic definitions and examples 2
1.1 Binary operations and groups . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 2
1.2 Some consequences of the axioms of a group . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 6
1.3 Modular arithmetic and the group Z/n . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 8
2 Cyclic groups 10
3 The symmetric groups 12
3.1 Permutations . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 12
3.2 Cycle notation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 14
4 Subgroups 16
4.1 Basic definitions . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 16
4.2 Cyclic subgroups . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 17
5 Cosets and Lagrange’s Theorem 18
5.1 Basic definitions and properties . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 18
5.2 Lagrange Theorem and Applications . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 20
6 Normal subgroups and quotient groups 22
6.1 Normal subgroups . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 22
6.2 Quotient groups . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 27
1
2
7 The first Isomorphism Theorem 28
7.1 Group Homomorphisms . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 28
7.2 Kernel and Image of a homomorphism . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 31
7.3 The First Isomorphism Theorem . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 32
1 Basic definitions and examples
1.1 Binary operations and groups
Definition 1. Let G be a set. A binary operation on G is a function
⋆ : G × G → G
Let (g, h) be an element of G × G. We will denote the value of the function on the pair (g, h)
by g ⋆ h.
Recall that the Cartesian product G × G is defined as the set of ordered pairs ( a, b) with
a and b elements of G. Note that there are infinitely many binary operations possible on an
infinite set.
Example 1. 1) Let Z be the set of integers. The usual addition + is a binary operation.
Another possible binary operation on Z is the multiplication ×. But we can also define
something less familiar like g ⋆ h := gh for all g, h in Z.
2) The usual substraction − is not a binary operation on the set of strictly positive whole
numbers N, because it doesn’t always output an element of N.
3) The usual substraction − is a binary operation on the set of complex numbers C.
4) There are many more arbitrary seeming examples. For example, let G be a set, then we
could simply define g ⋆ h = h for all g, h ∈ G: to “combine” two elements, you always
pick the second one. Another example is a “constant” binary operation: for a nonempty
set G, choose once and for all an element c ∈ G, and define g ⋆ h = c for all g, h ∈ G.
Remark 1. 1) Note that since binary operations have inputs in the cartesian product G ×
G, they are in general not commutative. In fact, for g, h two elements of G, the two
ordered pairs (g, h) and (h, g) are not equal and hence in general g ⋆ h and h ⋆ g are not
equal either.
2) If G is a finite set with n elements, i.e G = g1, . . . , gn, then a binary operation on G
can be described by a multiplication table or Cayley table:
⋆ g1 g2 . . . gn
g1 g1 ⋆ g1 g1 ⋆ g2 . . . g1 ⋆ gn
... ...
gn gn ⋆ g1 gn ⋆ g2 . . . gn ⋆ gn
Hence the number of different binary operations on G is nn2
.
We will now consider some special binary operations with nice properties.
3
Definition 2. A binary operation ⋆ on a set G is called associative if it satisfies
g ⋆ (h ⋆ k) = (g ⋆ h) ⋆ k,
for all g, h, k in G.
Example 2. 1) The usual addition + and the usual multiplication · on the sets Z, R and
C are associative.
2) − on R is not associative. In fact for example 2 − (3 − 4) ̸= (2 − 3) − 4.
3) g ⋆ h = gh is not associative as (2 ⋆ 3) ⋆ 2 = 23 ⋆ 2 = 82 = 64 and 2 ⋆ (3 ⋆ 2) = 512.
Definition 3. A binary operation ⋆ on a set G is called commutative if it satisfies
g ⋆ h = h ⋆ g,
for all g, h in G.
Example 3. 1) The usual addition + and the usual multiplication · on the sets Z, R and
C are commutative, the usual substraction − is not as 5 − 7 = −2, 7 − 5 = 2.
2) On R, the operation defined by g ⋆h := 1+ g·h is commutative as the usual multiplication
is, but it is not associative. In fact let g = 0, h = 1, k = −1, then
(g ⋆ h) ⋆ k = 1 ⋆ −1 = 0, g ⋆ (h ⋆ k) = 0 ⋆ 1 = 1.
Definition 4. Let G be a set with a binary operation ⋆. An element e of G is called left
(respectively right) identity (element) if for all g in G
e ⋆ g = g (respectively g ⋆ e = g).
Note that there might be many left (or right) identities, and that there might also be
none.
Example 4. 1) Consider the operation g ⋆ h := g on a set G. By definition any h is a
right identity. Assume now e is a right identity. Then e ⋆ h = h, for all h in G. But
e ⋆ h = e by definition of the operation. Hence e = h and e has to be different for each
h if G has more than one element, therefore there is no left identity element.
2) The operation g ⋆ h = 1 +g · h hast no left or right identity element. In fact assume e is
a right identity, then especially for g = 1, we have 1 = 1 ⋆ e = 1 + 1· e, hence e = 0. But
now for g = 2, we should have 2 = 2 ⋆ e = 2 ⋆ 0 = 1 + 2· 0 = 1, which is a contradiction.
This is annoying. We therefore want to know under which condition we can have ”nicer”
identity elements. This is given by the following propositions
Proposition 1. Let G be a set with a binary operation ⋆ that has both a left identity e1 and
a right identity e2. Then
e1 = e2 =: e.
Proof. Let e1 be the left identity, then for any element g in G, e1 ⋆ g = g. In particular if
g = e2, the right identity, we have e1 ⋆ e 2 = e2. Similarly e1 ⋆ e 2 = e1, as e2 is the right
identity, which proves the result.
4
In this case e is called the (two-sided) identity element. Note that different operations on
a set might have different identities. For examples ( R, +) has identity 0, whereas ( R, ·) has 1
as identity.
Proposition 2. Let G be a set with an identity element e. Then e is unique.
Proof. By definition e ⋆ g = g ⋆ e = g for all g in G. Assume e′ is another identity. Then
e′ ⋆ g = g ⋆ e ′ = g for all g in G as well. In particular
e = e′ ⋆ e = e ⋆ e′ = e′.
The notion of identity leads us naturally to the notion of inverses.
Definition 5. Let G be a set with a binary operation ⋆ and an identity element e. Let g be
an element of G. An element h in G such that
h ⋆ g = e (resp. g ⋆ h = e)
is called left (resp. right) inverse of g.
Note that an element g in G can have no left or right inverse.
Example 5. Consider the set of natural numbers N together with the usual multiplication ·.
Except the number 1, no element has an inverse.
The right inverse of an element is not necessarily equal to its left inverse and vice versa.
We want to explore conditions under which they are actually equal.
Proposition 3. Let G be a set with associative binary operation ⋆ and an identity element
e. If h1 is a left inverse and h2 is a right inverse of an element g in G, then
h1 = h2 =: g−1.
Proof. Since h1 is a left inverse we have by definition h1 ⋆ g = e, and since h2 is a right inverse
g ⋆ h 2 = e. Hence, since the operation is associative
h2 = e ⋆ h2 = (h1 ⋆ g) ⋆ h2 = h1 ⋆ (g ⋆ h 2) = h1 ⋆ e = h1,
which proves the result.
In this case we call g−1 a (two-sided) inverse.
Proposition 4. Let G be a set with associative binary operation and identity element e. If
g is an element of G and g has an inverse, then g−1 is unique.
Proof. Let ˜g−1 be another inverse of g. Then ˜g−1 ⋆ g = g ⋆ ˜g−1 = e. Moreover g−1 ⋆ g =
g ⋆ g −1 = e. Hence since ⋆ is associative
g−1 = e ⋆ g −1 = (˜g−1 ⋆ g) ⋆ g−1 = ˜g−1 ⋆ (g ⋆ g −1) = ˜g−1 ⋆ e = ˜g−1.
5
Note that the condition on the binary operation to be associative cannot be removed!
Example 6. We define the following operation given by its Cayley table
⋆ g1 g2 g3 g4
g1 g1 g2 g3 g4
g2 g2 g3 g2 g1
g3 g3 g1 g3 g2
g4 g4 g4 g4 g4
Clearly g1 is the identity element. g4 is a right inverse of g2 while g3 is its left inverse, g4
has no right inverse, but g2 as left inverse. Note that the operation is not associative.
We are now ready to give the definition of a group, which is simply the data of a set
together with a binary operation satisfying (some of) the nice properties we studied above.
It is defined by a list of axioms as follows.
Definition 6. A group (G, ⋆) is a set G together with a binary operation ⋆ on G such that
1) ⋆ is associative.
2) ⋆ has an identity element.
3) For all elements g in G, there exists an inverse g−1 in G.
The binary operation ⋆ is called the group operation.
Example 7. 1) (Z, +) is a group. In fact the usual addition is clearly associative, 0 is the
identity and for each element a in Z −a is an inverse. (R, +) and (C, +) are groups as
well, but not (N, +) since for an element a in N, −a is not in N.
2) (Z, −) is not a group since the usual substraction is not associative.
3) (C, ·), where · is the usual multiplication of complex numbers is not a group since the
element 0 has no inverse. But (C∗, ·) is, and so is (R∗, ·).
4) Let G := {e}, a one element set, and let ⋆ be the binary operation on G defined by
e ⋆ e = e. Recall that there is only one binary operation on a one element set). Then
(G, ⋆) is a group, called the trivial group.
Note that all examples above are sets of numbers but there are many other ways to get
groups. Some examples are groups of special matrices, permutation groups, symmetry groups.
We will study some of these examples later in the course.
Definition 7. Let (G, ⋆) be a group. If G is a finite set with exactly n different elements we
write |G| = n and say G has order n, and that G is a finite group. Otherwise we say that
(G, ∗) is an infinite group.
Example 8. Let G = {1, −1, i, −i} a subset of C, and · the usual multiplication of complex
numbers. You can check as an exercise that (G, ·) is a finite agroup.
Definition 8. Let (G, ⋆) be a group. If ⋆ is commutative, G is called an Abelian group.
6
All groups seen so far are Abelian since the usual addition and multiplication are com-
mutative on Z, R and C. But it is not always the case.
Example 9. Let G be the set of invertible real functions and ◦ the composition of func-
tions.The identity on G is the identity function id : R → R, x 7→ x. All functions have an
inverse by assumption, and the composition of function is associative, hence (G, ◦) is a group.
But ◦ is not commutative (in general f(g(x)) ̸= g(f(x)).
1.2 Some consequences of the axioms of a group
Let (G, ⋆) be a group.
Remark 2. By our previous considerations about binary operations and from the axioms of
groups we deduce immediately that
1) The identity element is unique by Proposition 2.
2) For all elements g of G, the inverse g−1 is unique by Proposition 4.
We have moreover the following
Proposition 5. Let (G, ⋆) be a group., g an element of G. Then
1) If either g ⋆ h = e or h ⋆ g = e for some h in G, then h = g−1.
2) The inverse of g−1 is g (i.e (g−1)−1 = g)
Proof. 1) This follows immediately from Proposition 3 and from the existence of inverses
for any element g. In fact if g ⋆ h = e, h is a right inverse (and a left inverse in the other
case), hence h = g−1.
2) g ⋆ g −1 = e by definition. The result follows immediately from part 1).
Remark 3. There are only two ways to bracket a product of three elements a, b, c of a group:
a ∗ (b ∗ c) or (a ∗ b) ∗ c
and the associativity axiom tells you that these are equal, so that the product a ∗ b ∗ c is
unambiguous. As a consequence we obtain
(a ∗ b) ∗ (c ∗ d) equal to ((a ∗ b) ∗ c) ∗ d
For the proof, write x = a ∗ b and apply the associativity axiom to x, c, d. This works for a
product of any length: any bracketing you use to work out a product like g1 ∗ · · · ∗ gn gives
the same result. You can prove this by induction: one such proof is given in the book by J.A.
Green in the suggested reading.
Proposition 6. Let (G, ∗) be a group and g, h ∈ G. Then (g ∗ h)−1 = h−1 ∗ g−1.
Proof. We are going to show that (h−1 ∗ g−1) ∗ (g ∗ h) = e. We are free to bracket the product
on the left in whichever way we like as discussed in the previous remark, so
(h−1 ∗ g−1) ∗ (g ∗ h) = h−1 ∗ (g−1 ∗ g) ∗ h = h−1 ∗ e ∗ h = h−1 ∗ h = e. (1)
In a group, if x ∗ y = e then multiplying on the right by y−1 gives x ∗ y ∗ y−1 = e ∗ y−1 so
x = y−1. Applying that to (1) gives that h−1 ∗ g−1 = (g ∗ h)−1 as required.
7
There is some special notation for what happens when you multiply a group element by
itself some number of times.
Definition 9. Let n ∈ Z, let ( G, ∗) be a group and let g ∈ G. Then we define gn as follows:
gn =



g ∗ g ∗ · · · ∗ g n > 0
g−1 ∗ g−1 ∗ · · · ∗ g−1 n < 0
e n = 0
where in the first case there are n copies of g in the product and in the second there are −n
copies of g−1, so that gn = (g−1)−n.
These exponents behave exactly how you would expect them to.
Proposition 7. Let n, m ∈ Z and let G, ∗ be a group. Then
1. gn ∗ gm = gn+m.
2. (gn)m = gnm.
Proof. 1. This result is clear if either n or m is zero, and follows directly from associativity
of (G, ∗) if n and m have the same sign. So suppose n > 0 and m < 0. The proof is by
induction on n, and when n = 1,
g ∗ gm = g ∗ (g−1 ∗ · · · ∗ g−1
| {z }
−m
) = (g ∗ g−1) ∗ gm+1 = gm+1
as required.
Now for n > 1 we have
gn ∗ gm = g ∗ gn−1 ∗ gm = g ∗ gm+n−1
by induction, and using either the base case (if m + n − 1 < 0) or the comment at the
start of the proof, this equals gm+n as required.
The case n < 0 and m > 0 is similar.
2. For m positive or zero this follows immediately from the definition, so suppose m < 0.
We do the case n > 0. The first part of this proposition implies ( gn)−1 = g−n, so
(gn)m = g−n ∗ · · · ∗ g−n
with −m copies of g−n appearing. This is the product of −mn copies of g−1, so equals
gmn by definition.
The case when n < 0 is similar.
Very often we’ll write the product g ⋆ h of two group elements simply as gh, especially
when the group operation ⋆ is some kind of multiplication.
8
1.3 Modular arithmetic and the group Z/n
There are some nice examples of finite abelian groups that show up when we think about
modular arithmetic. You should have met modular arithmetic before, but let’s recall the
definitions.
Fix a positive integer n.
Definition 10. For a, b ∈ Z, we say that a is congruent to b modulo n (or just mod n) if
a − b is divisible by n. In this case we write
a ≡ b mod n.
For example 22 ≡ 4 mod 9, as 22 − 4 = 18 and 9 |18.
We recall the following
Proposition 8. Let a, b, c be in Z. Then
1) a ≡ a mod n (reflexivity).
2) If a ≡ b mod n, then b ≡ a mod n (symmetry).
3) If a ≡ b mod n and b ≡ c mod n, then a ≡ c mod n (transitivity).
The last proposition means exactly that congruences define an equivalence relation on Z,
and for any integer a we will write
[a] = {b ∈ Z, b ≡ a mod n}
for the equivalence class containing a, so [ a] = [ a′] if and only if the integers a and a′ are
congruent mod n. Every integer is congruent to exactly one of the numbers 0 , 1, . . . , n− 1, so
there are exactly n equivalence classes. We write
Z/n = {[0], [1], ...,[n − 1]}
for the set of equivalence classes.
We now want to define operations on this new set. In order to do this we recall the
following
Lemma 1. Suppose a, a′, b, b′ ∈ Z are integers such that [a] = [a′] and [b] = [b′]. Then:
1. [a + b] = [a′ + b′].
2. [ab] = [a′b′].
Proof. This is an easy check that you should have seen in M1F.
This lemma means that if we choose integers a and b and calculate a + b or ab, and then
you choose two other representatives a′ ∈ [a] and b′ ∈ [b] and calculate a′ + b′ or a′b′ we get
the same element of Z/n. We can therefore define the following well-defined addition and
multiplication on Z/n:
+ : Z/n × Z/n → Z/n, ([a]n, [b]n) 7→ [a + b]n
· : Z/n × Z/n → Z/n, ([a]n, [b]n) 7→ [ab]n.
9
Proposition 9. (Z/n, +) is an Abelian group.
Note that (Z/n, +) is a finite group, of size n.
Proof. The element [0] n is an identity element, any element [ a]n has an inverse [ −a]n, and
the operation is associative and commutative (because addition on Z is associative and com-
mutative).
Now what about the set ( Z, ·)?
Example 10. 1) (Z/3, ·) is associative since the usual multiplication is associative and
therefore
([a] · [b]) · [c] = [ab] · [c] = [(ab)c] = [a(bc)] = [a] · ([b] · [c]).
Similarly we can show that the operation is commutative. The identity is the element
[e] such that [a] = [a] · [e] = [e] · [a] = [ae] = [ea], hence [e]=[1]. As for the inverses we
find them looking at the Cayley table of Z/3.
· 0 1 2
0 0 0 0
1 0 1 2
2 0 2 4 ≡ 1 mod 3
Hence the inverse of [1] is [1], the inverse of [2] is [2], but [0] does not have an inverse,
hence (Z/3, ·) is not a group.
But (Z/3 − {[0]}, ·) is an Abelian group.
2) For (Z/4, ·) the same consideration as above show that the operation is commutative
and associative, and that [1] is the identity. But similarly we can show that [0] and [2]
do not have inverse elements, whereas [1] and [3] do ([1]−1 = [1] and [3]−1 = [3]).
Hence in general ( Z/n, ·) is not a group. Nevertheless it is possible to correct this in the
following way.
Lemma 2. For each [a] ∈ Z/n, there exists [b] ∈ Z/n such that [a][b] = [1] if and only if a is
coprime to n.
We use the notation ( a, n) = 1 for the greatest common divisor of a and n is 1, i.e. a is
coprime to n.
Proof. Recall that ( a, n) = 1 if and only if we can find integers b, c such that
ab + nc = 1
(this follows from Euclid’s algorithm). Suppose that ( a, n) = 1. Then we can find b, c such
that ab + nc = 1, and then ab ≡ 1 mod n so [ a][b] = [1]. Conversely, suppose there is a
[b] ∈ Z/n such that [a][b] = [1]. This means ab ≡ 1 mod n, so ab − 1 = nc for some integer c,
and then ab + nc = 1.
Definition 11.
(Z/n)∗ = {[a] ∈ Z/n|∃[b] ∈ Z/n such that [a][b] = [1]}
of elements in Z/n which have inverses.
10
By Lemma 2 this is the set of [ a] such that ( a, n) = 1.
Proposition 10. The set ((Z/n)∗, ·) is a finite Abelian group with the operation of multipli-
cation.
Proof. The only thing we really have to prove is that the set is closed under our multiplication.
But if a and b are both coprime to n then ab is also coprime to n, so multiplication really
does define a binary operation on ( Z/n)∗. The element [1] ∈ (Z/n)∗ is an identity, and
associativity and commutativity follow immediately from associativity and commutativity in
(Z, ·). Finally if [ a] ∈ (Z/n)∗ then by definition there is a [ b] ∈ Z/n such that [a][b] = [1], but
then [a] is the inverse to [ b] so [ b] must be in ( Z/n)∗. So ( Z/n)∗ has inverses.
Notice that if n is a prime number then
#(Z/n)∗ = n − 1
but if n is not prime then it’s not immediately obvious how big ( Z/n)∗ is.
2 Cyclic groups
Definition 12. Let G be a group, and let g ∈ G. The order of g is the smallest positive
integer n such that
gn = e.
If no such n exists we say that g has infinite order.
Example 11. In the group (C∗, ·) the element −1 ∈ C∗ has order 2, and the element i ∈ C∗
has order 4. The element 10 ∈ C∗ has infinite order.
Remark 4. Do not confuse the order of a group, i.e. the size of a (finite) group ( e.g. “the
group Z5 has order 5”) with the order of an element.
Lemma 3. Let G be a finite group. Then every element of G has finite order.
Proof. Let g ∈ G, and consider the elements
g, g2, g3, . . .
of G. Since G is finite and this list is infinitely long, the elements of the list cannot all be
different: we must have ga = gb for some a < b . Then gb−a = e, that is, some positive
power of g equals the identity element. Thus the order of g is some number which is at most
b − a.
Lemma 4. Suppose G is a group and g ∈ G has order n. Then the elements e = g0, g, g2, . . . , gn−1
are all different.
Proof. Suppose gi = gj for some 0 ≤ i < j ≤ n − 1. Then we can write j = i + k for some
0 < k < n . So gi = gi+k = gigk. Multiplying both sides by ( gi)−1 = g−i we get e = gk. But
this contradicts n being the smallest positive power of g which equals the identity.
Corollary 1. If G is finite of size n, and g ∈ G, then the order of g is at most n.
11
Definition 13. A group G is called cyclic if there is a g ∈ G such that
G = {gn, n ∈ Z}.
Any g ∈ G with this property is called a generator of G.
Example 12. The group (Z, +) is cyclic, and the element 1 ∈ Z is a generator (remember
that in this group ‘ gn’ means the result of adding g to itself n times). There is exactly one
other generator for this group namely −1.
Example 13. For any n, the group (Z/n, +) is cyclic and [1] is a generator.
Lemma 5. Cyclic groups are abelian.
Proof. Let G be a cyclic group and g be a generator. Take any two elements h, k ∈ G, then
we have h = gi and k = gj for some i, j ∈ Z. Hence
hk = gigj = gi+j = gjgi = kh.
Many abelian groups are not cyclic.
Example 14. (Q, +) is abelian but is not cyclic. We can prove this by contradiction: if
q ∈ Q was a generator, then we could write any rational number as nq for some integer n.
But the number q/2 (for example) cannot be written in this form.
Lemma 6. Let G be a finite group of size n. Then G is cyclic if and only if G contains an
element of order n.
Proof. Suppose G is cyclic and g ∈ G is a generator. Since G is finite, g must have finite
order, d say. Then G = {e, g, g2, ..., gd−1}, and by Lemma 4 these elements are all distinct,
so d = n.
Conversely suppose thatG contains an elementg of order n. Then the set {e, g, g2, ..., gn−1}
has size n so it is the whole of G.
Example 15. In the group ({1, −1, i, −i}, ×) the elements i and −i both have order 4, so
this group is cyclic, and either of these elements is a generator.
Lemma 7. Let G be a finite group. If G is cyclic, it has at most one element of order 2.
Proof. Since G is finite, say of size n, there exists an element g in G of order n. Hence gn = e.
Any other element is of the form gi, with 0 < i ≤ n − 1 as G is cyclic. Assume now that gi
is of order 2. Then
e = (gi)2 = g2i, hence 2i = n,
and i = n
2 , which is only possible for one element in the case where n is even. If n is odd
there is no element of order 2.
Example 16. G = (Z/15, ·) is not cyclic. In fact G has 8 elements (the number of classes
of numbers which are coprime to 15), i.e.
Z/15 = {[1], [2], [4], [7], [8], [11], [13], [14]}.
Hence it is a finite group of size 8. But [4]2 = [1] and [14]2 = [−1]2 = [1], hence it has two
elements of order 2. By our previous lemma it cannot be cyclic.
12
3 The symmetric groups
This family of examples is important enough to deserve its own section. See also Chapter 20
of Liebeck A concise introduction to pure mathematics .
3.1 Permutations
Recall the following definition from M1F
Definition 14. A function f from a set X to a set Y is called
• one-to-one or injective if f(x1) = f(x2) implies x1 = x2 for any x1, x2 ∈ X.
• onto or surjective if for every y ∈ Y there is an x ∈ X such that f(x) = y.
• a bijection if it is both injective and surjective.
Furthermore, f is a bijection if and only if there is an inverse function g : Y → X such
that g ◦ f is the identity function on X and f ◦ g is the identity function on Y .
Definition 15. A permutation (on n symbols) is a bijection
σ : {1, ...., n}f→{1, ..., n}.
If we want to write down a permutation, one option is to use two-row notation. We write
down the numbers 1 to n, and underneath each number i we write down the number that σ
sends i to:     
1 2 . . . n
σ(1) σ(2) . . . σ (n)
     .
Because σ is a bijection, the bottom row of the table consists of the numbers 1 , 2, . . . , n in
some order. So a permutation is a ‘re-ordering’ of the numbers 1 to n.
Example 17. Let σ and τ be the functions:
σ : {1, 2, 3} → { 1, 2, 3} and τ : {1, 2, 3} → { 1, 2, 3}
1 7→ 1 1 7→ 2
2 7→ 3 2 7→ 3
3 7→ 2 3 7→ 1
These are both bijections, so they are permutations (on 3 symbols). In two-row notation we
would write them as
σ =
    
1 2 3
1 3 2
     andτ =
    
1 2 3
2 3 1
     .
Definition 16. The set
Sn =
 
σ : {1, ..., n}f→{1, ..., n}
	
of all permutations on n symbols is called the symmetric group (on n symbols).
13
Notice that Sn is not a set of numbers but a set of functions.
We are going now to justify why we called Sn the symmetric group. Define the binary
operation on Sn as the composition of functions
(σ, τ) 7→ σ ◦ τ.
This makes sense because if σ and τ are bijections from {1, ..., n} to {1, ..., n} then their
composition
σ ◦ τ : {1, ..., n} → { 1, ..., n}
is also a bijection. We are now ready to prove the following
Proposition 11. (Sn, ◦) is a group.
Proof. The composition of functions is associative. The identity function Id on the set
{1, ..., n} is an element of Sn, and it satisfies Id ◦ σ = σ ◦ Id = σ for any permutation
σ ∈ Sn, so it is an identity element. Any σ ∈ Sn is a bijection, so it has an inverse bijection
σ−1 ∈ Sn which satisfies σ ◦ σ−1 = σ−1 ◦ σ = Id.
Example 18. In Example 17 we wrote down two elements σ and τ of the group S3. Let us
compute their products, σ ◦ τ and τ ◦ σ.
Two-row notation is quite convenient for this. Take the table for τ, and look at its bottom
row: in the ith column, we have the number τ(i). Now create a third row, where underneath
each number τ(i) we write the number σ(τ(i)) that we get by applying σ (this can be read off
from the table for σ). In this case we get:
      
1 2 3
2 3 1
3 2 1
      
and we can conclude that
σ ◦ τ =
    
1 2 3
3 2 1
     .
In a similar way we can compute that τ ◦ σ =
    
1 2 3
2 1 3
    . Note that σ ◦ τ is in general not
equal to τ ◦ σ. Hence (Sn, · is not an Abelian group.
Proposition 12. #Sn = n!.
Proof. Define an element σ ∈ Sn by specifying the outputs
σ(1), σ(2), ..., σ(n)
one-by-one. There are n possibilities for σ(1). Then the number σ(2) cannot be equal to
σ(1) (because σ has to be a bijection) so there are n − 1 possibilities for σ(2). Similarly σ(3)
cannot equal σ(1) or σ(2), so there are there are n − 2 possibilities for it. Altogether this
gives
n × (n − 1) × · · · × 2 × 1 = n!
elements of Sn.
14
3.2 Cycle notation
There is a another way to write down elements of Sn, called cycle notation.
Definition 17. A permutation σ ∈ Sn is called a cycle if there is a sequence of distinct
numbers a1, ..., ak ∈ {1, ..., n} such that
σ(a1) = a2, σ (a2) = a3, ..., σ (ak−1) = ak, σ (ak) = a1
and σ(i) = i for every number not in this sequence.
The number k is called the length of the cycle, and we often abbreviate ‘cycle of length
k’ to ’ k-cycle’.
Example 19. Look at the following two permutations in S4:
σ =
    
1 2 3 4
2 3 1 4
     and τ =
    
1 2 3 4
2 1 4 3
     .
The first one is a 3-cycle, it rotates the numbers 1, 2, 3 and fixes 4. The second one is not a
cycle: no numbers are fixed, so if it was a cycle it would have to be a 4-cycle, but it is not.
Note that a k-cycle is just a particular element of the group ( Sn·), and therefore it makes
sense to speak of its order.
Proposition 13. The order of a k-cycle is k.
Proof. By definition σk = Id, hence the order of σ is at most k. We need to prove that it is
not less than k. Assume the order is d < k . Then we get immediately from the definition of
cycle that σd(a1) = ad ̸= a1, hence σd ̸= Id.
In cycle notation, we write
(a1, a2, ..., ak)
to mean the cycle which sends a1 7→ a2 7→ ... 7→ ak 7→ a1 and fixes all other elements. This
only makes sense if the numbers a1, ..., ak are all distinct (or this permutation would not be
a cycle).
Example 20. In cycle notation, we would write the 3-cycle σ from Example 19 as (1, 2, 3).
Remark 5. 1. The cycle notation is ambiguous and you need to precise which Sn you are
in.
2. There are several different ways to write the same cycle, for example (1, 2, 3), (2, 3, 1)
and (3, 1, 2) are all the same permutation. The usual convention is to put the smallest
number first.
3. A cycle of length one has to be the identity permutation. So the 1-cycles (1), or (3), or
(9), all denote the identity. The usual convention is to use (1), and this makes sense in
any Sn.
4. Cycles only make sense if all elements are distinct.
15
Example 21. The permutation τ ∈ S4 from Example 19 is not a cycle, but it’s easy to see
that it can be expressed as the composition
τ = (3, 4)(1, 2)
of two 2-cycles.
Definition 18. We say that two cycles (a1, ..., ak) and (b1, ..., bm) are disjoint if no ai is
equal to any bj.
Disjoint cycles commute if these two cycles are disjoint (exercise). i.e.
(a1, ..., ak)(b1, ..., bm) = (b1, ..., bm)(a1, ..., ak).
Lemma 8. Let σ ∈ Sn be a permutation.
1. For any i ∈ {1, ..., n}, there is a positive integer d such that σd(i) = i.
2. If d is the smallest positive integer such that σd(i) = i, then the numbers i, σ(i), σ2(i), ..., σd−1(i)
are all distinct.
3. If j ∈ {1, ..., n} is not in the set {i, σ(i), ..., σd−1(i)}, then neither is σ(j).
Proof. 1. Since Sn is finite (recall that it has n! elements), all elements have finite order,
therefore σd = Id for positive integer d, so certainly σd(i) = i.
2. Suppose σk(i) = σl(i) for two integers 0 ≤ k < l < d . Then σl−k(i) = i, contradicting
the fact that d > l − l is the smallest integer with this property.
3. If σ(j) = σk(i) for some k then j = σk−1(i) and j is in the set whenever σ(j) is which
is a contradiction.
Proposition 14. Any permutation can be expressed as a product of disjoint cycles.
Proof of Proposition 14. The proof is given by an explicit algorithm. Pick any σ ∈ Sn.
Step 1: Pick any number i ∈ { 1, ..., n}. By Lemma 8(1)) there exists an integer d
such that σd(i) = i. Take the smallest such d. Since by Lemma 8(2)) the elements
i, σ(i), σ2(i), ..., σd−1(i) are all distinct, we cand form the cycle
�
i, σ(i), ..., σd−1(i)

Step 2: Pick any j ∈ {1, ..., n} which does not occur in this cycle, and apply Step 1 to j
to form another cycle. By Lemma 8(3), this new cycle is disjoint from the previous one.
Step 3: Pick any k ∈ {1, ..., n} not occuring in any of our previous cycles. Applying Step
1 produces a new cycle, disjoint from each of our previous ones. Repeat this step until
all numbers in {1, ..., n} occur in one of our cycles.
The permutation σ will be the product of our list of cycles.
16
The choices we make in this algorithm don’t affect which cycles appear in our list, only
the order in which they appear (and since disjoint cycles commute this is not important).
Once we’ve factored σ into disjoint cycles γ1γ2...γr we can record the lengths ( k1, k2, ..., kr)
of the cycles that occur; this list is called the cycle-type of σ.
Example 22. Let’s run this algorithm on the permutation
σ =
    
1 2 3 4 5 6 7
4 1 3 2 6 7 5
     .
At the first step we choose the number 1. We have σ(1) = 4 , σ2(1) = σ(σ(1)) = 2 , σ3(1) =
σ(2) = 1. So the first cycle is (1, 4, 2).
Not all of the numbers 1, 2, . . . ,7 appear in this cycle, so we go back to step one and pick
one that doesn’t appear, say 3. Since σ(3) = 3, the cycle that we add is just (3), so we now
have (1, 4, 2)(3).
We still haven’t covered all the numbers, so pick a number that hasn’t appeared yet, say
5. We have σ(5) = 6, σ2(5) = σ(6) = 7, σ3(5) = σ(7) = 5, and so the cycle we add is (5, 6, 7)
and we now have
(1, 4, 2)(3)(5, 6, 7).
Since all seven numbers now appear, the algorithm stops. The permutation σ is the product
of these three cycles, but the 1-cycle (3) is the identity permuation, so we can just write
σ = (1, 4, 2)(5, 6, 7).
The cycle-type of σ is (3, 3) (we can leave out the 1’s from this list, they’re not important).
4 Subgroups
4.1 Basic definitions
We have seen before that a group can be a subset of another group, for example ( R, +) lives
inside of ( C, +). This consideration yields the concept of a subgroup.
In the following for more convenience we will write gh instead of the product g ⋆ h for two
elements of a group.
Definition 19. Let (G, ⋆) be a group and let H ⊆ G be a subset. We say that H is a
subgroup of G if:
1. For any g, h ∈ H we have gh ∈ H.
2. The identity element e ∈ G lies in H.
3. If g ∈ H then g−1 ∈ H.
We denote a subgroup H of G by H ≤ G.
Note that the first property guarantees that ⋆ defines a binary operation on H, and then
the other two axioms guarantee that (H, ⋆) is a group (associativity holds in G, and therefore
automatically in H). So we can always think of a subgroup as being a group in its own right.
The second property guarantees that the subgroup H is non-empty. Conversely if we
assume that G is non-empty we can remove the second property, since then there exists an
element g in H, and its inverse g−1 is in H as well by property 3. But by the closure of H
under multiplication gg−1 = e is in H.
17
Example 23. 1) Let G = {1, −1, i, −1} ⊂ C∗ and let H ⊂ G be the subset H = {1, −1}.
Then G is a subgroup of (C∗, ·), and H is a subgroup of G. But the subset {1, i} does
not form a subgroup as i · i = −1, which is not an element of the subset.
2) Let n be a fixed integer and nZ := {nz|z ∈ Z}. Then (nZ, +) is a subgrou of (Z, +).
3) Not every subset of a group which is closed under the binary operation is necessarily a
subgroup. For example (N, +) ⊂ (Z) is closed under the addition but has no inverses.
4) Let J := {2n + 1|n ∈ Z}, the set of odd integers. (J, +) is not a subgroup as the sum of
two odd numbers is even.
5) (Q∗, ·) is a subgroup of (R∗, ·), which is itself a subgroup of (C∗, ·).
6) (Z, +) ≤ (Q, +) ≤ (R, +) ≤ (C, +).
Example 24. Any group G has two obvious subgroups: The subset with only element the
identity {e} ⊆ G and the group itself G ⊆ G.
Definition 20. Let G be a group. The subgroup {e} ⊆ G is called the trivial subgroup.
A subgroup is called proper if it is not equal to G, and non-trivial if it is not equal to the
trivial subgroup.
Proposition 15. (Subgroup test) Let (G, ⋆) be a group and H be a non-empty subset of G
such that if x, y are elements of H, then x ⋆ y −1 is in H.
Then H is a subgroup of G.
Proof. We prove that H is indeed a group.
H contains the identity e: we know that if x, y are elements of H, then x ⋆ y −1 is in H. Let
x = y. Then e = x ⋆ x−1 ∈ H.
H contains inverses: Let x be an element of H. We saw that e is in H, hence x−1 = e ⋆ x−1
is in H.
H is closed under the binary operation: Let x and y be in H, then y−1 is H as we saw, and
therefore x ⋆ y −1−1 is in H.
Example 25. We says that a permutation σ ∈ Sn such that σ(i) = i fixes the element i. Let
H be the subset of Sn consisting of all permutations fixing 1. We’ll show that H is a subgroup
using the subgroup test.
Firstly, H is non-empty, since it certainly contains the identity permutation which fixes
everything. Secondly, suppose σ1, σ2 ∈ Sn fix 1. Then σ1(1) = 1 , so σ−1
1 (1) = 1 . Therefore
(σ−1
1 ◦ σ2)(1) = σ−1
1 (σ2(1)) = σ−1
1 (1) = 1. It follows σ−1
1 ◦ σ2 ∈ H, and so H is a subgroup of
Sn by the subgroup test.
4.2 Cyclic subgroups
Definition 21. Let (G, ∗) be a group, let g ∈ G, and let ⟨g⟩ = {gi : i ∈ Z}. Then ⟨g⟩ is called
the cyclic subgroup generated by g.
The following lemma tells us that this set is indeed a group.
Lemma 9. ⟨g⟩ ≤ G.
18
Proof. Apply the subgroup test: ⟨g⟩ contains g so it is not empty, and if gi, gj are elements
of ⟨g⟩ then gi ⋆ (gj)−1 = gi ∗ g−j = gi−j ∈ ⟨g⟩. So the subgroup test implies ⟨g⟩ ≤ G.
Lemma 10. If g has order n, then ⟨g⟩ has order n.
Proof. We know from Lemma 4 that the elements e, g, g2, . . . , gn−1 of ⟨g⟩ are all different, so
the size of ⟨g⟩ is at least n. We’ll show that it is exactly n by showing that any gi ∈ ⟨g⟩ equals
one of these elements.
We can write i = qn + r where q, r ∈ Z and 0 ≤ r < n by the division algorithm. So
gi = gqn+r = gqn ∗ gr = (gn)q ∗ gr = eq ∗ gr = e ∗ gr = gr.
Thus any element of ⟨g⟩ equals one of e, g, . . . , gn−1, and we’re done.
Example 26. Consider ⟨(1, 2)⟩ ≤ S3. The permutation (1, 2) has order two, so ⟨(1, 2)⟩ has
size two, and its elements are {e, (1, 2)}.
Note that obviously if the group is not of finite size, then an element g in the group does
not necessarily have finite order, and therefore ⟨g⟩ does not necessarily have finite size:
Example 27. Consider the group (Z, +), n an integer. Then ⟨n⟩ = nZ.
Example 28. The order of [2] ∈ Z6 is 3, because [2]2 = [2] + [2] = [4] and [2]3 = [2] + [4] =
[6] = [0], which is the identity element of (Z6, +). Thus the cyclic subgroup generated by [2]
is ⟨[2]⟩ = {[0], [2], [4]} ≤ Z6.
5 Cosets and Lagrange’s Theorem
5.1 Basic definitions and properties
Definition 22. Let (G, ∗) be a group, H a subgroup of G, and g an element of G.
1) The left coset of H by g is gH := {g ⋆ h : h ∈ H}.
2) The right coset of H by g is Hg := {h ⋆ g : h ∈ H}.
We write G : H for the set of left cosets of H by elements of G so G : H = {gH : g ∈ G}.
Similarly H : G is the set of right cosets of H by elements of G.
Usually right cosets are not equal to left cosets for a particular subgroup H. Note that
this is true if G is Abelian, but the converse is not true (this fact yields the study of normal
subgroups).
Example 29. The 3-cycle (1, 2, 3) ∈ S4 has order 3, so H := ⟨(1, 2, 3)⟩ is equal to
{e, (1, 2, 3), (1, 2, 3)2} = (1, 3, 2).
Then
(1, 2)H = {(1, 2), (1, 2)(1, 2, 3), (1, 2)(1, 3, 2)}
= {(1, 2), (2, 3), (1, 3)}
H(1, 2) = {(1, 2), (1, 2, 3)(1, 2), (1, 3, 2)(1, 2)}
= {(1, 2), (1, 3), (2, 3)}.
So in this case, (1, 2)H = H(1, 2).
19
Example 30. The 2-cycle (1, 2) ∈ S3 has order 2, so H := ⟨(1, 2)⟩ is equal to {e, (1, 2)}.
Then
(2, 3)H = {(2, 3), (2, 3)(1, 2)} = {(2, 3), (1, 3, 2)}
H(2, 3) = {(2, 3), (1, 2)(2, 3)} = {(2, 3), (1, 2, 3)}
So in this case (2, 3)H ̸= H(2, 3).
Example 31. Consider the group (Z, +). The subset of even integers H = 2Z ⊂ Z is, as we
saw, a subgroup. The subset J = {2z + 1|z ∈ Z} ⊂ Z of odd integers is not a subgroup, but it
is closely related to the subgroup H. For instance, we can describe J as the set
J = {1 + n, n ∈ H}.
In fact, if a is any odd integer then we have
J = {a + n, n ∈ H}.
Obviously J is the coset of H by a. Notice that we get the same coset when we start from any
odd integer, and then add on all possible even integers. A concise notation for this is to write
J = a + H
(for some odd integer a). Also notice that every integer lies in either H or J, but not both.
Example 32. Consider again the group (Z, +), but now consider the subgroup H = 3Z ⊂ Z
of all multiples of 3. The coset
1 + H = {1 + n, n ∈ 3Z}
is exactly the set of numbers that are equal to 1 modulo 3 ( i.e. it is the class [1] in Z/3).
Again the number 1 can be replaced by any integer a such that a ≡ 1 mod 3 then
a + H = {a + n, n ∈ 3Z} = 1 + 3Z
so these two cosets are the same. Similarly, if b is any integer such that b ≡ 2 mod 3 then
the coset
b + H = {b + n, n ∈ 3Z} = 2 + 3Z
is the set of integers that equal 2 modulo 3. There is a third case; if c is an integer such that
c ≡ 0 mod 3 then
c + H = 3Z
is the set of integers that are zero modulo 3. Notice that every integer lies in exactly one of
these three cosets. Note that the coset a + H does not depend on the element a as long as a
is in the coset.
20
5.2 Lagrange Theorem and Applications
We have seen in all our last examples that the cosets we computed were all disjoint. This is
actually a general fact.
Proposition 16. Let (G, ⋆) be a group, and H as subgroup of G. For two elements g1 and
g2, the cosets g1H and g2H are the same if and only if g2 is an element of g1H.
Proof. Assume that g1H = g2H. Then since e is in H, g2 = g2e is in g2H, hence g2 is in g1H
as well.
Conversely, suppose g2 ∈ g1H, which by definition means there is some h ∈ H such that
g2 = g1h. We will show firstly that g2H ⊂ g1H, and secondly that g1H ⊂ g2H, hence the
two subsets are identical.
Any element in g2H is of the form g2k for some k ∈ H, and then
g2k = (g1h)k = g1(hk).
The product hk lies in H (because H is a subgroup), so g1(hk) is an element of g1H. This
proves that g2H ⊂ g1H.
Now take an element in g1H, it must be of the form g1l for some l ∈ H. Then
g1l = (g2h−1)l = g2(h−1l).
The element h−1l lies in H (because H is a subgroup) so this is an element of g2H. This
proves that g1H ⊂ g2H.
Corollary 2. Every element g ∈ G lies in exactly one of the left cosets of H.
Proof. We know that g ∈ gH. If g ∈ g′H for some g′ then the previous proposition tells us
that the coset g′H is exactly the same subset as gH.
The left cosets form a partition of G, they are a collection of subsets g1H, g2H, ... ⊂ G
such that
G =
[
giH
and the intersection of any two of these subsets is empty.
Example 33. Consider the group (Z6, +), and the cyclic subgroup
H = ⟨[3]⟩ = {[0], [3]}.
The cosets of H are
[0] + H = H = {[0], [3]} = [3] + H
[1] + H = {[1], [4]} = [4] + H
[2] + H = {[2], [5]} = [5] + H
(this group is abelian so there is no distinction between left and right cosets). Notice that all
three cosets have the same size.
Lemma 11. Let G be a group and let H ⊂ G be a finite subgroup. Then all left cosets of H
have the same size, i.e.
#gH = #H
for any g ∈ G.
21
Proof. Fix any g ∈ G. Now define a function
α : H → gH (2)
h 7→ gh. (3)
This function is an injection, because if α(h1) = α(h2) then gh1 = gh2, and multiplying on
the left by g−1 shows that h1 = h2. It is also a surjection, because any element in gH is of
the form gh = α(h) for some h ∈ H.
So α is a bijection, and hence the sets gH and H must have the same size.
Remark 6. If H is infinite (like in our earlier Examples 31 and 32) then the function α is
still a bijection from H to gH, but the question of whether H and gH have ‘the same size’ is
a bit more delicate.
Theorem 1. (Lagrange Theorem) Let G be a finite group and H be a subgroup, then
#G = #H · #(G : H),
in particular the order of H divides the order of G.
Proof. We saw that the cosets of H form a partition of G, i.e. G =S giH. But # gH = #H,
hence each piece of the partition has size the size of H, hence the size of G is the number of
cosets times the size of H.
Definition 23. Let G be a groups and H a subgroup of G. Then the size of the set of left
cosets #(G : H) is called the index of H.
Example 34. The group (Z10, +) has size 10, hence it has no subgroup of size 3, 4, 6, 7, 8 or
9.
Corollary 3. Let G be a finite group and let g ∈ G. Then the order of g divides the size of
G.
Proof. The order of g equals the size of the cyclic subgroup ⟨g⟩ ⊂ G. By Lagrange’s theorem
this divides # G.
In particular, if g ∈ G is any element of a finite group, we must have
g#G = e.
Corollary 4. Let G be a finite group of size p, where p is a prime number. Then G is cyclic.
Proof. Pick any g ∈ G not equal to e. Consider the cyclic subgroup ⟨g⟩ ⊂ G. Its size divides
p by Lagrange’s theorem, so it is 1 or p (since p is prime). It cannot be 1 since ⟨g⟩ contains
both g and e, so it must be p and therefore
⟨g⟩ = G.
So G is cyclic and g is a generator.
Corollary 5 (Fermat’s Little Theorem). Let a ∈ Z and let p be a prime number. If a ̸≡ 0
mod p then
ap−1 ≡ 1 mod p.
22
Proof. The group ( Z∗
p, ×) has size p − 1, so the element [ a] ∈ Z∗
p satisfies
[a]p−1 = [1].
Another way to say this is that
ap ≡ a mod p
and this is still true if p does divide a.
6 Normal subgroups and quotient groups
6.1 Normal subgroups
We have seen before that the left cosets gH are in general not equal to the right cosets by
the same element Hg. They are equal for example when the group is Abelian, but we also
saw that the converse is not true. This yields the next definition.
Definition 24. Let G be a group, and H a subgroup of G. Then H is called a normal
subgroup if Hg = gH.
The notation for normal subgroups is H ⊴ G.
Example 35. 1. Let G be a group. If G is Abelian, of course all subgroups are normal
2. The centre of a group G is the set defined as
Z(G) := {z ∈ G|gz = zg, for all g ∈ G}.
It can easily be checked that Z(G) is a subgroup of G and Z(G) is a normal subgroup
by definition.
3. Let G = S3 and H = ⟨(1 2 3)⟩, the cyclic subgroup generated by (1 2 3). We have showed
before that (2 3)H ̸= H(2 3). Therefore H is not a normal subgroup.
In general it is not easy to find normal subgroups. In the rest of this section we are going
to study ways to determine if a subgroup is normal and to classify at least in some cases
normal subgroups inside of a group.
Proposition 17. Let G be a group and H a subgroup of G. Moreover let #(G : H) = 2 .
Then H is normal.
Proof. Let H be a subgroup of G with index 2. This means that H has only two distinct
left cosets. Now take an element g ∈ G. If g ∈ H, then gH = H = Hg. If g is not in H,
necessarily gH ̸= H and we have a partition of G by H and gH, but also for the same reason
by the right cosets H and Hg. Therefore G = H ⊔ gH = H ⊔ Hg, which yields the result.
23
Example 36. Let G = S3 and H = ⟨(1 2 3)⟩. Since (1 2 3) is a 3-cycle, it has order 3, and
H = {id, (1 2 3), (1 2 3)2} = {id, (1 2 3), (1 3 2)}.
Therefore the size of H is 3 and since the size of G is 3! = 6, by Lagrange Theorem
#(G : H) = |G|
|H| = 2.
Therefore H has to be normal by the previous proposition.
This is of course very restrictive as it limits our studies to subgroups of index 2. But here
is a more general way to find normal subgroups.
Lemma 12 (Conjugacy Criterion). Let G be a group, H a subgroup of G. H is normal if
and only if for all g ∈ G and h ∈ H, ghg−1 ∈ H.
Proof. By definition H is normal if and only if gH = Hg for all g ∈ H. But this is equivalent
to the fact that for all h ∈ H, and g ∈ G, gh = h′g for some h′ ∈ H (note that h′ and h do
not have to be equal). Or in other words, for all h ∈ H, and g ∈ G, ghg−1 = h′ for some
h′ ∈ H. This shows the result.
Note that this statement is equivalent to the fact that H is a normal subgroup if and only
if gHg −1 = H.
Example 37. 1. Let G be a group. Then the trivial subgroup is a normal subgroup since
e = gg−1e = geg−1 ∈ { e}, for all g ∈ G. Similarly the group G itself is a normal
subgroup.
2. Let G = GLn(R) the group of invertible matrices together with matrix multiplication,
and
SLn := {A ∈ GLn(R)| det A = 1}.
It is left as an exercise to show that G is indeed a group and that H is a subgroup of
G. We are going to use the conjugacy criterion in order to show that the subgroup H
is normal. Let A ∈ GLn(R) and B ∈ SLn(R) (which means that det B = 1), then
det(ABA−1) = det A det B det(A−1) = det(AA−1) = 1,
and therefore ABA−1 ∈ SLn(R). This means that SLn(R) is a normal subgroup inside
of the group of invertible matrices.
Definition 25. Let G be a group and g, x be fixed element in G. The element gxg−1 is called
the conjugate of x by g.
The map
c : G → G,
x 7→ gxg−1
is called the conjugation.
Another way to formulate the conjugacy criterion is to say that a subgroup is normal if
and only if it is invariant by conjugation.
Conjugation allows us to define an equivalence relation on the group G, by saying that
two elements are related if they are conjugate.
24
Theorem 2. Let G be a group. Then for all x1, x2 ∈ G, the relation defined by
x1 ∼ x2 if and only if x2 = gx1g−1 for some g ∈ G
is an equivalence relation on G.
Proof. We start by proving the reflexivity. We have x = exe = exe−1, therefore we have
found an element g := e ∈ G such that x = gxg−1 hence x is conjugate to itself.
Now assume that x ∼ y for some x, y ∈ G. Then x = gyg −1 for some g ∈ G. But this
is equivalent to y = g−1xg = g−1x(g−1)−1, therefore y = ˜gx˜g−1 where ˜g := g−1, and y˜x and
the relation is symmetric.
Finally assume that x ∼ y and y ∼ z. Then we have y = g1xg1−1 and z = g2yg−1
2 for
some g1, g2 ∈ G. Then
z = g2(g1xg−1
1 )g−1
2 = (g2g1)x(g1g2)−1 =: g′xg′−1,
where g′ = g1g2 ∈ G. Consequently x ∼ z and the relation is transitive.
Definition 26. Let G be a group. The relation ∼ is called the conjugacy of G. The equivalence
classes
Ca := {gag−1|g ∈ G}
is called the conjugacy class of a.
The conjugacy criterion means exactly that all conjugacy classes Ch of elements of a
normal subgroup H remain inside of the subgroup H.
Example 38. If G is an Abelian group, then there are as many conjugacy classes as elements.
Example 39. Let now G = S4. We are going to compute all conjugacy classes of S4. Note
that |S4| = 24, which are quite a lot of elements . We will use the fact that permutations can
be written as a product of disjoint cycles in order to simplify our study. Note that there are
five possibilities for the cycle decomposition of an element σ in S4: either σ is the identity,
or a 2-cycle, or a 3-cycle, or a 4-cycle, or a (2,2)-cycle, and these are the only possibilities.
Case 1: σ = id. Then τ στ−1 = id for all τ ∈ S4 and therefore the only element conjugate to id
is id itself, so Cid = {id}.
Case 2: σ = ( i1 i2 i3 i4) is a 4-cycle. Applying τ στ−1 to τ(ik), k ∈ { 1, 2, 3, 4}, we get that
τ(i1 i2 i3 i4)τ −1 = (τ(i1) τ(i2) τ(i3) τ(i4)). Hence any conjugate of a 4-cycle is a 4-cycle.
Conversely if (i1 i2 i3 i4) and (j1 j2 j3 j4) are two 4-cycles, define τ ∈ S4 such that τ(ik) =
jk. Then similarly to our previous computation we get that
τ(i1 i2 i3 i4)τ −1 = (τ(i1) τ(i2) τ(i3) τ(i4)) = (j1 j2 j3 j4)
and two 4-cycles are necessarily conjugate. Therefore we have just proved that the
conjugacy class of a 4-cycle is the set of 4-cycles
C(i1 i2 i3 i4) = {σ ∈ S4|σ = (j1 j2 j3 j4)}.
25
Case 3: σ = (i1 i2 i3) is a 3-cycle which leaves i4 fixed. We can prove similarly to Case 2 that
τ(i1 i2 i3)τ −1 = (τ(i1) τ(i2) τ(i3)) and that moreover τ(i4) is left fixed by the transfor-
mation τ στ−1. Conversely two 3-cycles are always conjugate and the conjugacy class
of a 3-cycle is therefore the set of 3-cycles.
C(i1 i2 i3 = {σ ∈ S4|σ = (j1 j2 j3)}.
Case 4 and 5: By the same reasoning one gets immediately that the conjugacy class of a 2-cycle is the
set of all 2-cycles and that the class of a (2,2)-cycle is the set of (2,2)-cycles.
C(i1 i2 = {σ ∈ S4|σ = (j1 j2)}, C (i1 i2)(i3 i4) = {σ ∈ S4|σ = (j1 j2)(j3 j4)}.
Consequently there are five conjugacy classes in S4 corresponding respectively to the five
possible cycle types in S4.
The previous example actually works in general for any symmetric group Sn.
Note that the five conjugacy classes we just computed for S4 are all disjoint and moreover
the form a partition of the group. This is a general fact which we are going to prove now.
Proposition 18. Let G be a group. Then any two conjugacy classes in G are either disjoint
or equal.
Proof. Assume that Ca and Cb are two conjugacy classes in G, such that Ca ∩ Cb = ∅. Then
there exists an element c ∈ Ca ∩ Cb. Then by definition of a conjugacy class, we have
c = gag−1 = ˜gb˜g−1, for some g, ˜g ∈ G.
Then a = (g−1˜gb(˜g−1g)) = ( g−1˜g)b(g−1˜g)−1, which means exactly that a is conjugate to b.
Hence Ca = Cb.
Corollary 6. The conjugacy classes of a group G form a partition of G.
Proof. Every element g ∈ G belongs to one of the conjugacy classes of G. Moreover by the
previous proposition, different conjugacy classes have to be disjoint.
Therefore
G = ⊔iCgi.
and we have now learned two different partitions for the group G: the partition by left (or
right) cosets, and the partition by conjugacy classes. Unlike the partition with cosets, the
conjugacy classes do not all have the same size. This is clear from the previous example.
As we saw before, conjugation, and therefore conjugacy classes are intimately related.
Theorem 3. Let G be a group, H a subgroup of G. Then H is normal if and only if for all
conjugacy classes Gg of G either Cg ⊆ H or Cg ∩ H = ∅.
Proof. Assume that H is normal and that Cg ∩ H ̸= ∅. We have to show that Cg is a subset
of H. Since the intersection is not empty, there exists h ∈ Cg ∩ H, and then h ∈ Cg ∩ Ch,
since h is of course in its own conjugacy class. But then Cg = Ch by Proposition 18 and
Ch ⊆ H since H is normal and h ∈ H, by the Conjugacy Criterion. Hence finally Cg ⊆ H,
which proves the result. The converse can be proved in a similar way and we leave it as an
exercise.
26
Since all conjugacy classes are disjoint, and for a normal subgroup conjugacy classes stay
either inside of the subgroup, or not in the subgroup, last theorem tells us that any normal
subgroup is a union of some conjugacy classes of the group G! One can therefore use the
following method to find normal subgroups for a given group G:
i) Compute all conjugacy classes inside of the group G.
ii) Since the size of a subgroup H has to divide the size of G, find all possible partitions of
H in terms of conjugacy classes. Do not forget that the trivial conjugacy class Ce has
to be be in the partition, since e has to be in the subgroup H.
iii) Check if the possible partition do form a subgroup. Indeed Theorem 3 does not tell us
that an arbitrary union of conjugacy classes (even involving Ce) is necessarily a group.
Example 40. We are going to find all normal subgroups of S4. By Example 39, there are
five conjugacy classes in S4. We can easily compute their size: #Ce = 1, #C(i1 i2)(i3 i4) = 3,
#C(i1 i2) = 6, #C(i1 i2, i3) = 8, #C(i1 i2, i3, i4) = 6 (indeed this adds up to 24). The possible
combinations containing Ce and with size dividing the size of G are
1. H0 = #Ce = 1,
2. H1 = #Ce = 1 + #C(i1 i2)(i3 i4) = 4,
3. H2 = #Ce = 1 + #C(i1 i2)(i3 i4) + #C(i1 i2, i3) = 12,
4. The sum of all conjugacy classes which is the group S4.
This are therefore the only possible normal subgroups, and H1 and H2 are the only proper
such subgroups. It is not hard to check that these are indeed subgroups, and we therefore get
a complete classification of normal subgroups in S4.
A conjugacy class is obtained from the conjugation of one element of a group by all the
elements of the group. But it is also possible to conjugate a subgroup by an element of the
group.
Definition 27. Let G be a group, g a fixed element of G, and H a subgroup. The set
gHg −1 := {ghg−1|h ∈ H}
is called the conjugate subgroup of H.
It remains to show of course that conjugate subgroups are indeed subgroups.
Proposition 19. Let G be a group and H a subgroup of G. For any element g in G the set
gHg −1 is a subgroup of G.
Proof. We use the subgroup test. First we have to show that conjugate subgroups are not
empty. This is clearly the case as e ∈ H and e = gg−1e = geg−1 ∈ gHg −1. Let now
x, y ∈ gHg −1. Then x = gh1g−1 and y = gh2g−1 for some h1 and h2 in H. But then
xy−1 = gh1g−1(gh2g−1)−1 = (gh1g−1)(gh−1
2 g−1) = g(h1h−1
2 )g−1 ∈ gHg −1.
27
There can be of course many conjugate subgroups for a particular subgroup H, in fact
theoretically as many as elements in the group G. The case of normal subgroup is very
particular as it can be shown that there exists only one conjugate subgroup, namely the
subgroup itself.
Corollary 7. Let G be a group, H a normal subgroup of G. Then the unique conjugate
subgroup to H is the group H itself.
Proof. This is immediate from our previous considerations: H is normal if and only if
gHg −1 = H, for all g in G.
6.2 Quotient groups
We worked in the last sections with the group of (left) cosets G : H and we have seeing that
in general there is no nice structure on this set, i.e. it is not a group. But, the case where H
is normal is very special and does allow us to improve the situation. The first set is to define
a binary operation on the set.
Definition 28. The binary operation on the set of cosets
· : G : H × G : H → G : H, (gH, g′H) 7→ gH · g′H := gg′H.
is called the coset multiplication.
As we know, the relation of one element being in the same left coset than another is an
equivalence relation, and as usual in this type of situation, we have to check that the operation
we have just defined does not depend on the representative of the class. In other words, we
have to check that if ˜g ∈ gH and ˜g′ ∈ g′H, then gH · g′H = ˜gH · ˜g′H.
Proposition 20. Coset multiplication is well-defined if and only if H is a normal subgroup.
Proof. Let ˜g ∈ gH and ˜g ∈ g′H. Then ˜g = gh1 and ˜g′ = g′h2 for some h1 and h2 in H. By
definition of coset multiplication we have therefore
˜gH · ˜g′H = ˜g˜g′H = gh1g′h2H = gh1g′H ∈ gHg ′H.
So we need to show that the set gHg ′H is actually the set gg′H. This is equivalent to the fact
that for all g ∈ G, h1, h 2 ∈ H there exists an element h3 in H, such that gg′h3 = gh1g′h2.
And this expression is the same as g′H ∋ g′h3h−1
2 = h1g′ ∈ Hg ′, which can be rewritten in
terms of cosets as gH = Hg, for all g in G. This is exactly the condition that H is a normal
subgroup.
This proposition tells us that in the case of a normal subgroup, the set of cosets is endowed
with a nice well-defined binary operation. And actually we get more than that!
Definition 29. Let G be a group, H a normal subgroup of G. The set G/H := (G : H, ·) is
called the quotient group (or factor group) of G by H.
Of course we still need to show that G/H is a group as for now we only have its closure
under coset multiplication.
28
Proposition 21. Let G be a group, H a normal subgroup of G. The quotient group G/H is
a group under coset multiplication.
Proof. We have proved already that the coset multiplication is well-defined and that the quo-
tient group is closed under this operation. Associativity follows trivially from the associativity
of the group G. We need now to find an identity. Let E := eH (note that since we are dealing
with the set of coset, the identity has to be a coset). We have
E · gH = eH · gH = egH = gH = gH · E.
For the inverse note that gHg −1H = gg−1H = eH = E, hence the inverse of g−1H has to be
the inverse of gH for all gH.
Note that by Lagrange theorem, since H is a subgroup of G, we have |G/H| = |G|
|H|, which
motivates the name of quotient of factor group.
Example 41. 1. We have seen before that G is a normal subgroup of itself. We can
therefore consider the quotient G/G. The cosets are the elements gG with g ∈ G.
Therefore there is only one coset, namely the coset eG = G. This is also clear from the
size of the group |G/G| = 1, and this means that G/G is or rather has to look like the
trivial subgroup {e}.
2. The trivial subgroup {e} is also a normal subgroup of any group G. We can therefore
consider G/{e}. The cosets are the elements g{e} ̸= g′{e} for any g ̸= g′. This means
that there are as many cosets as elements in G. Therefore |G/{e}| = |G| and G/{e}
has the same structure as the group G.
3. Let G = R(8) = {e
iπk
4 |k ∈ {0, 1, . . .7}}, the set of 8-th roots of unity, and H = R(4) =
{e
iπk
2 |k ∈ { 0, 1, . . .3}, the subgroup of 4-th roots of unity. Clearly H is a subgroup of
G. Moreover G is Abelian, hence H is a normal subgroup. eH = {e
i2πk
4 |k ∈ 0, . . .3}
has to be in the group G/H as it is the identy. Now pick an element of G, which
is not in eH, for example e
iπ
4 and compute the coset of H by this element. We get
e
iπ
4 H = {e
i(2k+1)π
4 |k ∈ { 0, . . .3}} and G = eH ⊔ e
iπ
4 H, so this are the only two cosets
and R(8)/R(4) is the group with only two elements. So it looks like Z/2.
Until now we have seen many times that groups might ”look like” some other groups. The
purpose of the next section is to make this notion more precise and be able to compare group
structures in a proper way.
7 The first Isomorphism Theorem
7.1 Group Homomorphisms
Our goal is to define maps between groups which have the property of preserving the structure
(i.e. the group operation).
Definition 30. Let (G, ⋆) and (G′, ·) be groups. A group homomorphism is a map f : G → G′
such that
f(g1 ⋆ g2) = f(g1) · f(g2),
for all g1, g2 ∈ G.
29
As usual we will omit to write the group operations if they are clear from the context.
Example 42. 1. Let G = (R, +) and G′ = (R≥0, ·). Then
f : (R, +) → R≥0, ·) x 7→ ex
is a group homomorphism as f(x1 + x2) = ex1+x2 = ex1 · ex2 = f(x1) · f(x2)
2. Let G be a group, H a normal subgroup. Then
π : G → G/H, g 7→ gH
is a homomorphism, as π(g1g2) = g1g2H = g1H · g2H = π(g1) · π(g2) by definition of
coset multiplication.
Note that the additional condition on the map f exactly means that we are preserving
the binary operation (which is what defines the group structure). Homomorphisms have
properties which are very similar to linear maps between vector spaces. As a matter of fact a
linear map between vector spaces is often called a vector space homomorphism: the linearity
properties just mean that the map preserves the axioms. We are going to prove now some of
these properties for group homomorphisms.
Proposition 22. Let G and G′ be groups, f : G → G′ be a group homomorphism. Then
1. f(eG) = eG′, where eG and eG′ are the identities on G and G′ respectively.
2. f(g−1) = f(g)−1.
Proof. 1. f(eG) = f(eGeG) = f(eG)f(eG), since f is a homomorphism. But then taking
f(eG)−1, which exists since f(eG) is an element of the group G′ yields the result.
2. Let g ∈ G. By the first part eG′ = f(eG) = f(gg−1), for all g in G. But since f is a
homomorphism this means that eG′ = f(g)f(g−1) and therefore f(g−1) has to be the
inverse of f(g) by definition.
For the next proposition we recall the following general definition for sets.
Definition 31. Let S and S′ be two sets, and f : S → S′ a map. The preimage of the set S′
under f is the set defined by
f −1(S′) := {s ∈ S|f(s) ∈ S′}.
Note that the notation f −1 should not be confused with the inverse of the function f
which might not even exist as the function is not necessarily bijective.
Proposition 23. Let G and G′ be groups, f : G → G′ be a group homomorphism. Then
1. If H ≤ G, then f(H) ≤ G′,
2. If H ′ ≤ G, then f −1(H ′) ≤ G′,
3. If H ′ ⊴ G′, then f −1(H ′) ⊴ G.
30
Proof. 1. We have to check the three axioms of subgroups for the set f(H). Since H ≤ G,
eG ∈ H. But we proved that f(eG) = eG′ ∈ f(H). Next we prove the closure. If x, y ∈
H, then f(x), f(y) ∈ f(H). Since f is a homomorphism, f(x)f(y) = f(xy) ∈ f(H), as
xy ∈ H since H is a subgroup. And finally if x ∈ H, it inverse x−1 is also in H and
f(x) ∈ f(H). But by properties of homomorphism f(x)−1 = f(x−1) ∈ f(H).
2. Similarly we check the axioms for the preimage f −1(H2). Since H ′ is a subgroup of
G′, eG′ ∈ H ′. But as previously, f(eG) = eG′, hence eG′ ∈ f −1(H ′). To check the
closure, pick x and y in f −1(H ′). Then f(x), f(y) ∈ H ′ and since H ′ is a subgroup
H ′ ∋ f(x)f(y) = f(xy), since f is a homomorphism. Therefore xy ∈ f −1(H ′). And
finally let x ∈ f −1(H ′). Then f(x−1) = f(x)−1 ∈ H ′ and x−1 ∈ f −1(H ′).
3. We just showed that f −1(H ′) is a subgroup. It remains to prove that this subgroup
is normal. Using the conjugacy criterion, this is equivalent to showing that gxg−1 ∈
f −1(H ′) for all g ∈ G and x ∈ f −1(H ′). But f(gxg−1) = f(g)f(x)f(g)−1 ∈ H ′, since f
is a group homomorphism. Therefore gxg−1 has to be in the subgroup, which completes
the proof.
In general the image of a normal subgroup under a homomorphism is not normal! This is
actually true if and only if the homormorphism is surjective.
Example 43. Let f : S2 → ⟨(1, 2)⟩ ⊂ S3, such that f(idS2 = idS3) and ((1 2)) = (12) ∈ S3. f
is a homomorphism, and S2 is obviously normal in S2, but f(S2) is not a normal subgroup
in S3.
Definition 32. Let G and G′ be subgroups. A bijective homomorphism f : G → G′ is called
a group isomomorphism.
Notation: G ≃ G′.
Example 44. 1. Let G = (Z, +) and G′ = (Z/n, +). Then
f : (Z, +) → (Z/n, +), z 7→ [z]n
is a homomorphism since f(z1 + z2) = [z1 + z2]n = [z1]n + [z2]n = f(z1) + f(z2). But it
is not injective as f(z) = f(˜z) whenever ˜z = z + kn for any k ∈ Z.
2. Let G = (Z, +) = G′. Then
f : (Z, +) → (Z, +), z 7→ 2z
is obviously a group homomorphism, but not an isomorphism, since there exists no
z ∈ (Z, +), such that f(z) = 3 for example, so it is not surjective.
3. We saw earlier that f : (R, +) → (R≥0, ·) is a group homomorphism. It can be easily
checked that it is actually an isomorphism.
4. Let Cn = {e, g, g2, . . . gn−1} be a cyclic group of order n. Then
f : Cn → (Z/n, ·), g i 7→ [i]n
is a group isomorphism.
31
7.2 Kernel and Image of a homomorphism
Like in the case of vector space homomorphism (or linear maps), we can define a notion of
kernel and image of a homomorphism, and we will se that this subsets have indeed similar
properties to what we have seen in linear algebra.
Definition 33. Let G and G′ be groups, f : G → G′ be a group homomorphism.
The kernel of f is the set
ker f := {g ∈ G|f(g) = eG′}.
The image of f is the set
Imf := {g′ ∈ G′|f(g) = g′, for some g ∈ G}.
Obviously the kernel ker f is a subset of the group G whereas the image is a subset of the
group G′. But they are actually more than subsets.
Proposition 24. Let G and G′ be groups, f : G → G′ be a group homomorphism. Then f is
injective if and only if ker f = {eG}.
Proof. Assume first that f is injective and take g ∈ ker f. We know that since f is a
homomorphism, we have necessarily f(eG) = e′
G) Then f(g) = eG′ = f(eG), but then eG = g
since f is injective.
Conversely assume now that ker f = {eG}. Let g, g′ be in G and f(g) = f(g′). Then
multiplying f(g′)−1 on both sides we have eG′ = f(g)f(g′)−1 = f(gg′−1), hence gg′ is in ker f
and gg′−1 = eG by assumption, which yields g = g′ and f is injective.
Proposition 25. Let G and G′ be groups, f : G → G′ be a group homomorphism. Then
ker f ≤ G and Im ≤ G′.
Proof. To check that ker f is a subgroup of G, we use the subgroup test. We know that
f(eG) = e′
G. Therefore eG ∈ ker f and ker f is not empty. Assume now that x and y are in
ker f. Then since f is a homomorphism f(xy−1) = f(x)f(y)−1 = eG′eG′ = eG′ and therefore
xy−1 ∈ ker f, therefore ker f ≤ G.
Now we prove that Imf is a subgroup of G′ using the axioms of subgroups. Again the
identity e′
G ∈ Imf since f(eG) = eG′. Assume now that x, y ∈ Imf . Then there exists u
and v in G such that f(u) = x and f(v) = y. Then xy = f(u)f(v) = f(uv), since f is a
homomorphism, and xy is in the image of f. We therefore have closure. Finally assume that
x = f(u) ∈ Imf , for some u ∈ G. Then x−1 = f(u)−1 = f(u−1), since f is a homomorphism
,and x−1 is in the image of f, which finishes the proof.
Proposition 26. Let G and G′ be groups, f : G → G′ be a group homomorphism. Then
ker f ⊴ G.
Proof. We already know that ker f is a subgroup of G. We also know that {eG′} is a normal
subroup of G′. But then by Proposition 23 f −1({eG′}) is a normal subgroup of G′ and
f −1({eG′}) = ker f by definition, which yields the result.
32
Example 45. Let G be a group and H a normal subgroup of G. We have seen in example
42 that the map
π : G → G/H, g 7→ gH
is a group homomorphism. The kernel of π is
ker π = {g ∈ G|π(g) = eG/H = eH} = H.
The map π is very important for the following. It is called the canonical projection of G
onto G/H. Obviously it is not a isomorphism as the kernel is H, hence it is not injective.
But it is surjective.
Corollary 8. Let G be a group, H a subset of G. Then H ⊴ G if and only if there exists a
group G′ and a homomorphism f : G → G′, such that ker f = H.
Proof. Assume H ⊴ G. Then the canonical projection π : G → G/H, g 7→ gH is such a
homomorphism by the previous example. Conversely if f : G → G′ is a homomorphism, such
that ker f = H, we know by Proposition 25 that ker f ⊴ G .
Last corollary is very useful to study quotient groups.
Example 46. We consider the two matrix groups GLn(R) = {A ∈ Mn×n(R)|A is invertible },
SLn(R) = {A ∈ GLn×n(R)| det A = 1 }. We showed previously that SLn(R) is a normal
subgroup of GLnR using the conjugacy criterion. We can recover this result considering
the map f : GLn → (R∗, ·), A 7→ det A is a homomorphism since f(AB) = det( AB) =
det A det B = f(A)f(B). Then
ker f = {A ∈ GLn(R)| det A = 1} = SLnR ⊴ GLn(R).
7.3 The First Isomorphism Theorem
We finally have all the ingredients to state a theorem which is very important in group theory.
This result will allow us in particular to study quotient groups and their structures without
having to go back to working with cosets and only considering group homorphisms and their
properties.
Theorem 4 (First Isomorphism Theorem). Let G and G′ be groups, f : G → G′ be a group
homomorphism. Then
Imf ≃ G/ ker f
.
Proof. We recall that G/ ker f is well-defined as ker f is a normal subgroup of G. We define
the map
φ : G/ ker f → Imf, g ker f 7→ φ(g ker f) := f(g).
We first have to show as usual that φ is well-defined, i.e that it does not depend on the
representative of the coset. We assume therefore that g1 ker f = g2 ker f and we want to
show that f(g1) = f(g2). But f(g1) = f(g2) is equivalent to f(g2)−1f(g1) = eG′ by taking
f(g2)−1 on both sides. And this means that g−1
2 g1 is in the kernel of f, or in other words
g−1
2 g1 ker f = ker f. Hence finally f(g1) = f(g2) if and only if g1 ker f = g2 ker f. Note
33
that this also shows that f is injective since it is an equivalence. And it is surjective by
construction.
Next we have to show that φ is a homomorphism. Let g1 ker f and g2 ker f be two cosets
in G/H. By the definition of coset multiplication, and since f is a homomorphism, we have
φ(g1 ker f · g2 ker f) = φ(g1g2 ker f) = f(g1g2) = f(g1)f(g2) = φ(g1 ker f)φ(g2 ker f),
which proves the result.
Example 47. 1. Let G be a group. We know that G ⊴ G and we saw that G/G looks like
{e}. We will formulate this better. The map f : G → { e}, g 7→ e is a homomorphism
as
f(g1g2) = e = e · e = f(g1)g(g2).
Obviously the image of f is just the trivial group {e} and the kernel is therefore the
entire group G. Then by the First Isomorphism Theorem G/G ≃ {e}.
2. Similarly let G be a group, and consider the map id : G → G, g 7→ g. This is already
an isomorphism, with Im(id) = G, and ker id = {e}. Therefore G/{e} ≃ G, which is
what we expected since we saw that this quotient group looked like G.
3. Consider the map f : GLnR → R∗, A 7→ det A. We proved already that this map is a
homomorphism with kernel the group SLn(R). Clearly the image is the entire group R∗.
Therefore GLn(R)/SLn(R) ≃ R∗, as expected.
4. Let f : (C∗, ·) → (R>0, ·), z 7→ |z|. It is easy to check that (R>0, ·) is a group. This map
is a homomorphism as
f(z1z2) = |z1z2| = |z1||z2| = f(z1)f(z2)
and it is surjective. We have ker f = {z||z| = 1} =: S1, also called the circle group (the
group of complex numbers with norm one form the unit circle). Then C∗/S1 ≃ R>0.
Corollary 9. Let f : G → G′ be a group homomorphism and G a finite group. Then |Imf |
divides the size of G.
Proof. Since G is finite, Imf is a finite subgroup of G′ (whereas G′ which is not necessarily
finite). Now by the First Isomorphism Theorem and Lagrange Theorem
|Imf | = |G/ ker f | = |G : ker f | = |G|
| ker f | ,
and therefore |Imf || ker f | = |G| and the size of the image of f divides the size of G.
Example 48. We want to know how many homomorphisms f : (Z/4, +) → S3 there exist.
We know that necessarily the size of Imf has to divide |Z/4| = 4. Then the image of Imf has
size 1 or 2. If |Imf | = 1, Imf = {e} necessarily and f is the trivial homomophism sending
everything to the identity f : Z/4 → S3, [n]4 7→ id.
Now assume that the size of Imf is 2, or in other words that Imf is a subgroup of order 2.
Remember that the identity in Z/4 has to be sent to the identity in S3. There are 3 possible
subgroups of order 2 in S3 which are ⟨(1 2)⟩, ⟨(1 3)⟩ and ⟨(2 3)⟩ and we get therefore three
additional homomorphism
f1 : [n]4 7→ (1 2)n, f 2 : [n]4 7→ (1 3)n, f3 : [n]4 7→ (1 2)n.

High  level  approach  For  background,  our  team  was  composed  of  five  2nd-year  undergraduates  studying  Mathematics,  
Computer
 
Science,
 
Computer
 
Engineering,
 
and
 
Economics.
 
We
 
all
 
had
 
different
 
backgrounds
 
and
 
relative
 
strengths,
 
so
 
for
 
each
 
round
 
we
 
generally
 
had
 
two
 
or
 
three
 
team
 
members
 
working
 
on
 
manual
 
trading
 
and
 
the
 
others
 
working
 
on
 
algorithmic
 
trading.
 
We
 
found
 
that
 
working
 
in-person
 
was
 
extremely
 
helpful
 
for
 
morale
 
and
 
ideation,
 
as
 
it
 
allowed
 
us
 
to
 
bounce
 
ideas
 
off
 
each
 
other
 
and
 
motivate
 
one
 
another
 
if
 
someone
 
got
 
tired.

information
 
on
 
GitHub
 
to
 
prepare
 
for
 
this
 
half
 
of
 
the
 
competition.

I
 
recall
 
correctly,
 
the
 
PnL
 
from
 
this
 
side
 
is
 
weighted
 
more
 
heavily
 
in
 
ranking
 
than
 
manual
 
trading
 
PnL.

from
 
last
 
year.
 
While
 
this
 
part
 
isn’t
 
weighted
 
as
 
heavily
 
for
 
ranking,

a
 
lot
 
of

will
 
underprepare
 
for
 
this
 
compared

algorithmic
 
part.
 
Study
 
things
 
like
 
Colonel
 
Blotto
 
and
 
game
 
theory.

entire
 
tutorial
 
round
 
and
 
had
 
to

It

three

very
 
rushed
 
throughout

duration
 
of
 
the
 
competition.
 
 -  In  one  of  the  later  rounds  we  weren’t  very  confident  in  one  of  our  strategies.  At  that  point  
we
 
were
 
ranked
 
2nd
 
in
 
the

from

gains.
 
  What  I  would  do  differently  -  We  relied  heavily  on  Jasper’s  backtester  and  visualizer,  but  I  think  it  would  have  been  
helpful
 
to
 
make
 
our
 
own
 
infra
 
and
 
tools.
 
This

of

able

with
 
custom

 -  A  lot  of  our  time  was  spent  trying  every  strategy  we  could  think  of  and  seeing  which  
worked
 
in
 
the
 
backtester/dashboard.
 
However,
 
I

What  to  do  before  the  competition  -  Study  all  of  the  openly  available  past  IMC  Prosperity  writeups  on  GitHub.  Figure  out
