2
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
Notes by Ryan Ling, Co-President, Imperial College Investment Society 2022/23.
Introduction
A Simplistic Example of the Stock Market
A security is a tradable asset or certificate that has monetary value. What do I mean by this? Well imagine
your friend has started a company and is looking for money. As a good friend and self-proclaimed opportunistic
investor, you decide to invest £100 of your hard earned money into his company. In exchange, you receive a
certificate saying that you now own 1% of his company. Now, you can do 1 of 2 things:
1.Keep your share of the company.You invested in thecompany because you thought it was a good
investment at £100 for a 1% share of the company. As your friend expands and grows his company,
other investors may look at it and want to invest in it. The higher demand for shares will mean that
investors will have to pay more for the same 1% stake. As such, the “market value”, i.e. how much
people are willing to pay for your share of the company will have increased. Similarly, if people don’t
believe in what your friend's company is doing, the lack of demand will mean the ‘market value’ of your
1% share will drop as fewer people are willing to buy your share or are only willing to buy in at a lower
price.
2.Sell off your share of the company (i.e. trade it).If you don’t believe in what your friend’s company is
doing you can try to sell off your share of the company. However, depending on what other people think
of your friend’s company you may or may not have a hard time finding someone else willing to buy your
share of the company.
What is clear from this example, is that the certificate you are currently holding, guaranteeing you 1% of the
company has a monetary value. This is what is meant by asecurity.
Asset Classes
In general, there are 4 main types of investments that are tradable in markets. Each classification is known as
an asset class made up of groups of investments that share common characteristics:
Equities
These are shares of companies that are traded on public exchanges. These exchanges are market places that
facilitate the buying and selling of shares between 2 parties. In the example above, we briefly discussed what
the market value of a security means. Well, it is the job of the stock exchange to keep up to date with what the
market value of a share currently is for companies that are listed on the exchange, which in effect is how much
other investors are willing to pay a share of a company and how much you would have to pay to purchase that
share. If you ever decided to sell the shares of your friend’s company, the stock exchange would help you to
find a buyer and the price the sale would take place at, based on investor demand for your friend’s company’s
shares, acting like a middle man in the transaction. Examples of these include the New York Stock Exchange
(NYSE), London Stock Exchange (LSE), NASDAQ (predominantly comprised of tech stocks). Owning a share
of a company represents owning a fraction of that company so if, say Company X has 10,000,000 shares
outstanding and you buy 1 share of that company, then you own 0.00005% of the company. Depending on the
3
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
number of people that trade them, these stocks can also trade Over-The-Counter. This means that the trade is
settled between the two parties, without the supervision of an exchange.
Fixed Income (Bonds)
Bonds are debt securities that are issued by both corporations and governments. These are essentially
IOUs”(“I Owe yoU”), where companies and governments borrow money (the total amount borrowed is called
the principal) from lenders for a fixed period of time (e.g. up to 3, 5 or even 10 years) at a fixed coupon rate
(the amount of yearly interest the borrower will pay the lender). At the end of the contract, i.e. at maturity, the
principal amount is paid back to the lender. Therefore, if a lender holds a bond until maturity, they will receive
both their principal back and interest that they have earned from the borrower for lending out their money.
Investors holding bonds do not have to hold them to maturity, and can trade them either on public exchanges
or Over-The-Counter (OTC). Do not worry about the technical details discussed and how bonds are valued -
this will be covered in Lecture 6. The important point here is to note that bonds are one of the main securities
that can be traded in markets.
As an example, let’s say you lend your friend, Bobby, £20,000 (principal) over 5 years to buy a car and who
agrees to pay 1% (coupon rate) of the principal back to you every year. So every year, Bobby pays you £200,
until the end of year 5 (maturity), when he returns you the original £20,000. One thing to note is that you could
have sold the bond before maturity.
Foreign Exchange (FX)
The Foreign Exchange market is the largest market in the world worth US$1.93 quadrillion. Each day, US$5.3
trillion is traded. More than 85% of the market(1)features transactions between the 7 major currency pairs
which are (EUR/USD, USD/JPY, GBP/USD, AUD/USD, NZD/USD, USD/CAD, USD/CHF). What are currency
pairs? Currency pairs allow us to compare the value of one currency to another - the first currency listed is
called the base currency and the second currency listed is the quote currency. The base currency is the
currency you want to buy, while the quote currency is the currency you want to sell. Essentially, say you are
holding JPY but believe that the USD will increase in value. Why? Several factors may come into play such as
politics where the country might be undergoing elections, or an important piece of economic data has just been
released detailing that the economy of the USA is growing better than expected. You then go to your broker
who will give you a 2-way quote on the USD/JPY currency pair (note that USD is the base currency, i.e. the
one you want to buy), sell him/her your JPY and receive USD in exchange. If you’re confused by currency
pairs… Don't worry! Our Lead Lecturer Ahmad will be guiding you through the entirety of FX in Lecture 2, but
I’ve given you an introductory scope of the topic and what you’ll be finding out soon when he takes over next
time.
The FX market is considered an OTC market in the sense that there is no centralised exchange that oversees
the trading of the currency pairs in circulation, and the exchange runs 24h a day for 5 days a week.
Commodities
These include physical resources such as Oil & Gas, precious metals such as Gold, Silver, metals such as
Lithium, Copper that are essential for electric vehicles. Even wheat, grain, coffee and beans are considered
commodities. As highlighted by the supply chain shocks due to the war in Russia and Ukraine, the prices of
these resources are determined by the supply-demand dynamics which are affected by factors such as
4
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
weather, geopolitical tensions, trading routes etc (Will be covered more in Lecture 2). One way for investors to
invest in this asset class is to buy financial instruments called futures, which are contracts where the investor
has agreed to buy/sell a specific amount of the commodity at a predetermined price at a specific time in the
future. These are traded on futures exchanges. Again, do not worry this will be covered more in-depth in
Lecture 7 by Joseph.
Over recent years, the emergence of cryptocurrencies have prompted debates about where it sits as an asset
class i.e. whether it should be considered a commodity or an asset class on its own given its role as both a
store of value and use as currency. We’ll leave you to decide where you would class them!
Industry Players
Although it might seem confusing at first, the finance industry can be split broadly into 4 separate categories –
of which we will go into more detail below:
Sell-Side Buy-Side
Regulators Central Bank
Sell-side
These are predominantly the investment banks that you are probably familiar with. Think Goldman Sachs, JP
Morgan, Morgan Stanley etc. These banks are classified as financial services companies that act typically as
advisors to clients from the buy-side and take a fee from the services they provide, but do not invest any of
their own funds i.e. from their balance sheet into the market. Think of them as the gate-keepers to the financial
markets, helping companies raise money or facilitate acquisitions/mergers between companies.
Buy-side
As its name suggests, firms on the buy-side invest their own money or clients’ money into different asset
classes (i.e. they buy assets usually by enlisting the help of the sell-side). What they invest in, and how long
they choose to invest defines the type of company they are classified as. This includes companies such as
Private Equity Funds, Asset Managers, Venture Capitalists and Hedge funds.
Regulators
This third group is involved in ensuring that the market participants are operating fairly to prevent market
manipulation. Market manipulation refers to when a market participant (a bank, hedge fund, investors,
company chairmen etc.) artificially tries to increase or decrease the stock price of a company under false
pretences. A great example of this is the “Pump-and-Dump” scheme that you might have heard of that
featured in The Wolf of Wall Street. Essentially, Jordan Belfort led a stock broking firm that held a large amount
of shares in questionable penny stocks - stocks that are not traded around as much (we call this low liquidity).
The firm would call up potential investors and feed them false stories about these companies to make them
buy shares in the penny stocks the firm owned, causing the share prices of these companies to rise (the
pump), before exiting their positions (the dump) and leaving investors facing losses.
In the USA, the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is in charge of enforcing securities law to prevent
cases like this from occurring. In the UK, this is the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA).
5
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
The Central Bank
Typically. each country has its own central bank – the USA has the federal reserve or the ‘Fed’, while the UK
has the Bank of England. The banks do not function like how a normal bank does – they exist as a separate
entity from the government and act with the primary goal of controlling inflation (i.e. the total amount of money
in circulation in the economy) by adjusting interest rates on the Federal Funds Rate. This is the rate that
commercial banks lend to each other (mostly overnight). These banks have to borrow from each other as they
must all have enough reserves (the cash they are holding) to make up a percentage of customer deposits they
hold. Why does this matter? This is the base rate that all other rates are determined from, so government
bonds, company bonds, lending rates including mortgages are all adjusted based on this rate. If the Federal
Funds Rate is increased, this will mean that banks will also raise their own interest rates when they lend out
their money.
It is very important to control inflation as if more money is in circulation in the economy, the prices of goods
and services will increase resulting in a rising cost of living. The interest rate is the single most important
number in the world of finance as all other lending rates depend on it. If the Fed sets a higher interest rate, it
means borrowing costs will go up for corporations as well as consumers. As a result, corporations are less
likely to borrow money and subsequent business and economic activity will decrease resulting in a fall in the
total amount of money in circulation. We have seen especially this year that the Fed has been very
aggressively trying to raise interest rates in order to combat inflation, so now you know why they have to do
this! We will cover this topic much more in depth in Macroeconomics in lecture 2.
This place is left blank for you to write your own notes.
Scroll on over to the next page to find out more about the sell-side.
6
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
The Sell-Side
Now we will go into detail on the Sell-side. As mentioned above, the sell-side consists of investment banks.
Structure of a typical investment bank
An investment bank is typically split into 3 main areas. The Front Office which features the Investment Banking
and Markets divisions, the Middle Office, which includes Risk Management and Compliance, and Back Office,
which includes other support functions such as Human Resources and Technology.
The Front Office is further split into two sides - the public facing side, featuring the markets division and equity
research teams and the private side, which features the Investment Banking division. Very often, Sales &
Trading is classified as an Investment Banking activity but falls under the markets side of the bank. Typically,
Investment Banking refers to the advisory (with respect to raising capital or a transaction between two
companies) and underwriting services (left) that banks provide to their clients. We will go into this in more
detail below.
Figure 1: Structure of a BB Investment Bank
Investment Banking
This typically includes the bank’s advisory side of the business. This is known as theprivateside ofthe bank
because you are dealing with deals that are not yet known to the market. On this side of the business you will
find the following divisions:
M&A deal teams
These teams do what is most commonly perceived as Investment Banking. The teams typically cover different
sectors such as Healthcare, Technology, Media and Telecommunications (TMT), Infrastructure, Consumer and
7

Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
Retail and advise on both buy-side and sell-side deals i.e. advising the buyer in a deal or advising a seller on a
sale.
For example, imagine you are a managing director at Jefferies in the Healthcare sector. From your research
you have identified that Pfizer could be in the market to acquire a smaller pharmaceutical company
specialising in immuno-oncology treatments. You then analyse potential acquisition targets by analysing their
business and valuing them. You pitch this to Pfizer and then execute the deal. This is a simple example of a
buy-side deal. On the opposite side, there will be another investment bank acting as advisors to the smaller
oncology drugs company who will be facilitating its sale.
Equity Capital Markets (ECM)
ECM focuses on servicing companies that are interested in raising capital through equity – to put it simply, this
means it services companies who want to raise money by issuing new shares to the stock markets. What is
Capital? Well, capital is nothing more than money that companies raise in order for them to either fund their
strategy by maybe building more factories, investing in research and development or even acquiring another
company. So in effect, when you buy these new shares, you are directly providing funding for these companies
to carry out their specific expansion plans!
This is typically done in 3 different ways:
Initial Public Offerings (IPO)
This is the most well-known process of raising capital. When a private company (i.e. owned by only a select list
of investors including founders) has grown to a certain size, they will want to raise capital to continue funding
their expansion plans. They therefore decide to sell a proportion of their company to the public markets where
the company’s stock will be traded. They will of course retain a majority shareholding so that they can retain
control of company decisions.
Typically in an IPO, a group of banks (usually 4-5) will work together to carry out the transaction. This group is
known as the syndicate, and their primary job is to find investors to invest in the IPO company. Very often, the
syndicate will underwrite the deal. What does this mean?  In every IPO process, the banks will work together
to value the IPO company. For example, let’s say Uber wants to IPO and is valued at £1bn.Underwritingthis
means that the syndicate of banks will guarantee that Uber will receive £1bn of capital regardless of whether
the banks find enough investors to invest £1bn in total. So the banks take on the risk of the IPO not raising
enough money and will have to pay Uber the remaining amount of money should they not be able to find
enough investors for the IPO.
Of the syndicate, 1-2 of these banks will act as Global Coordinators who take a principal role in sorting out the
logistics of the deal including the legal processes that must be carried out for the company to be listed, and
take on a larger amount of the risk from underwriting as highlighted above.The other banks are known as
bookrunners play a more secondary role and only focus on finding investors for the transaction. The process of
finding investors is calledbookbuilding.
Rights Issue (RI)
This is for companies that are already public who want to raise capital – either because they need to fund their
capital expenditure plans or because they are in financial trouble and need to pay down their debts. The
company typically issues new shares at a discounted price that is offered to existing shareholders to avoid
8
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
dilution. (Since creating more shares will mean that all existing shareholders will own a smaller proportion of
the company).
Rights Issues typically run over a few weeks as investors are typically given a week to decide if they want to
subscribe to new shares or sell-off their right to buy shares in the market and face their percentage of
ownership decreasing (diluted).
Accelerated Book Building (ABB)
ABBs are similar to Rights Issues – they are an alternative method for companies to raise capital. As
mentioned in the IPO section, a bookbuild is when banks find investors to invest in the company in question -
combining this with the word “accelerated” and the name of this transaction immediately makes sense. There
are two main types: Primary ABBs and Secondary ABBs. In a Primary ABB, the company simply issues new
shares and it is the bank's job to find investors that are willing to invest in these shares. In a Secondary ABB,
the company in question wants to sell off shares it has in another company or a subsidiary – it is a secondary
transaction because no new shares are being created.
ABBs are very fast processes that do not require much paperwork, generally taking a few hours. For this
reason, there are rules put in place where the maximum number of new shares issued can only be a maximum
of 20% of the current number of shares outstanding. In contrast, in a Rights Issue, typically a maximum of 50%
the current number of shares outstanding can be issued to the market. So for instance if Uber has 100 million
shares in total before the rights issue, it can only issue a maximum of 50 million new shares.
Debt Capital Markets (DCM)
Similar to ECM, DCM is the part of the investment bank that looks to help companies raise capital but through
the issuance of debt. DCM can be split into Investment Grade bonds and High Yield bonds (aka junk bonds).
The difference between IG bonds and High Yield bonds lies in their rating. Each bond is rated by a rating
agency (E.g. Moody’s, S&P) based on the ability of the borrower to return the amount borrowed with interest.
These ratings range from AAA to C or even D. The lower a bond is rated, the more likely the borrower will
default on its repayment obligations and hence the higher the returns the investor will demand resulting in a
higher coupon rate. Anything under BBB (S&P) or Baa (Moody’s) is considered a junk bond.
Like ECM, analysts in this area have to be very up-to-date with the market in order for them to price the
coupon rates of the bonds issued appropriately.
Leveraged Finance
Leveraged Finance is the part of the IB that services Private Equity firms, also known as financial sponsors. As
we will see below, Private equity firms seek to acquire companies through a mixture of debt and equity,
meaning that they both borrow money and invest their own money when acquiring another company. Their
goal is to improve the operations of the target company over a period of time and use the earnings generated
by the company to pay down the debt they have taken on to purchase the company.
In this division, analysts create Leveraged Buy-Out (LBO) models in order to predict the cash flows of the
company that is to be acquired in order to determine how much they are willing to lend to the financial sponsor
and the interest rate they will charge. Typically, there is a consortium of banks called a syndicate that provide
the loans in order to spread the risk of lending to the sponsor.
9
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
Depending on the Investment Bank, this division could cover high-yield bonds that companies can use to raise
capital. High yield bonds simply mean that the interest the company has to pay on them is typically higher due
their perceived riskiness. Factors such as the amount of debt a company has taken on, how much profit they
are making and how well people think the company will do are all taken into account.
Project Finance
Project Finance (PF) is very similar to leveraged finance – the only difference is that instead of dealing with the
acquisition of companies, PF deals with real-assets that are typically in the renewable/infrastructure finance,
for example, offshore wind farms. Clients can be either financial sponsors or developers, and projects can be
built from scratch (greenfield) or re-developed (brownfield).
Types of Investment Banks
Bulge Brackets (BB)
These banks are the biggest players in the industry, rank the highest in the IB league tables servicing the
largest clients and working on the largest deals.
e.g. Goldman Sachs, JP Morgan, Morgan Stanley, UBS, Credit Suisse.
Middle Market Players
These banks are structured in the same way as BBs but typically service smaller clients. They are typically
Universal Banks – banks that provide the whole spectrum of services ranging from retail banking (where you
store your money), commercial banking (providing loans to clients), investment banking, asset management
and private banking.
e.g. HSBC, Standard Chartered
Elite Boutiques
These are pure play investment banks that focus only on advising clients. These firms do not underwrite their
transactions, and do not possess a Markets Division (i.e. Sales and Trading). This is meant to make their
advice less biased as they will only receive fees for advising and not from underwriting and selling products to
the market.
e.g. Lazard, Rothschild, PWP, Evercore.
Markets
The markets division at an Investment Bank encompasses the following roles:
1.Sales
2.Trading
3.Structuring
4.Equity Research
10
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
Contrary to the private side where the Investment Banking division sits, the markets division sits on thepublic
side as the information they deal with is all public which everyone has access to. This division between the
public and private sides of the business is called ‘The Chinese Wall’ meaning that there can be no
communication between both sides of the bank. This is very tightly regulated by compliance in the bank as
there is a big conflict of interest between the public and private sides of a bank. As you can imagine, if any
private-side information from the investment banking side is leaked to the sales and trading desk, the sales
and trading desk will be able to profit based on an unfair advantage compared to other investors in the market.
Sales & Trading
Sales and trading share what we call a symbiotic relationship - each one depends on the other in order to best
fulfil the client’s needs.
People working in Sales must have comprehensive and detailed knowledge of the products the bank offers, for
example swaps, options (which we will cover in Lecture 4) etc.. So in effect, the salesperson is the person on
the phone to, say, Brevan Howard, to discuss an order Brevan Howard would like to be placed. In order to
effectively communicate the bid-ask spreads to Brevan Howard, they will have to be in constant contact with
the trader to get this information and relay it back to the client.
There are 2 main types of trading:
1.Agency Trading
2.Market-making
Fun fact:After the Volcker Rule was passed in 2010by then Fed chairman Paul Volcker, proprietary trading
was banned in all banks as a result of the 08-09 Global Financial Crisis to prevent banks from using their own
accounts to make speculative investments that might lead to similar crises. Originally, in 1933, the government
introduced the Glass-Steagall act which was aimed at preventing commercial banks from investing clients’
deposits into the market. This was repealed in 1999, but all it took was 10 years before it had to be
reintroduced.
Agency Trading
In agency trading, you are servicing clients on the buy-side (typically hedge funds) by executing orders and
trading on behalf of them. Why is this needed? Because typically these clients put in orders that are big
enough to cause drastic changes in a given company’s share price. As a trader, it is your responsibility to
execute the order in a way that the stock price remains steady so that your client can fill their order at the
lowest possible price.
For example, imagine you are an agency trader at BNP Paribas. Your sales person comes to you one day
saying that Brevan Howard (a hedge fund) would like to place a big order of 500 shares of Tesla (its stock
ticker – how it is recognised on the stock exchange – is TSLA) stock. Because the order amount is much
bigger than a normal retail investor, it would be difficult to purchase the shares at one go without moving the
share price upwards. For example, if at the time you received the order, TSLA was trading at $700. What I
mean is it is impossible for you to buy all 500 shares at $700 as, due to the size of the order, every time you
buy say 100 shares, the price of TSLA will be pushed upwards, meaning your client would have to pay a
higher price. It is therefore your job as an agency trader to ensure that the order is executed in the most
efficient way possible so Brevan Howard pays the best price (lowest) for their order.
11
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
Market-Making
If you have ever purchased or sold shares on the market, have you ever wondered how your order is always
seamlessly and immediately filled? Imagine this, say right now you decide to buy 10 shares of TSLA. If you go
to your brokerage account on Trading 212, you will realise that all you need to do is click “buy” and you will
then immediately be the owner of 10 TSLA shares. This is the same vice versa. How is this possible?
If you think about it, that implies that there must be a buyer on the other end who has bought the shares you
are selling and vice versa if you are selling.
This is not the case in reality.
The first thing you need to know about market-making is the bid-ask spread.
Whenever a trade is to be placed at the sales desk of the bank, the salesperson will always quote a bid/ask.
The Bid-Ask is always seen from thebank’s perspective.Bid in this case is the amount that the bank willbid
to buy your shares of TSLA for when you wish to sell it, and Ask would be the amount the bank wouldaskyou
to pay for them to sell shares of XX company to you. The difference between both quotes is called the bid-offer
spread. A spread is typically the difference between two things - in this case, it is the difference between the
bid and ask prices.
Bid-Offer spread = Bid – Ask
The SPOT price, which is the actual price of the security, will lie in between the bid and ask prices. Why? If you
think about it, no middle-man in a deal is going to buy at exactly at the bid price because they want to be
compensated for the service they have provided by acting as market maker to allow you to sell your shares
immediately. Therefore, naturally and normally, the SPOT price will lie somewhere in between the bid and ask
prices so that the bank always buys at a lower price and sells at a higher price than the SPOT.
Bid Spot Ask
JPM 92.49 92.53 92.55
GS 92.54 92.60 92.72
BNPP 91.70 92.00 92.40
It is the job of the salesperson to communicate this with the client effectively, and the job of the trader to
analyse the current market conditions to provide a competitive bid-offer spread. There are tons of banks on the
street that are competing for the same business, so as a trader you really want to deliver a price that is good
for the bank in terms of profit, but also for the bidding client to make them feel like they have got the best deal
and will not go to a competitor.
As you might have guessed, the trader profits via the bid-offer spread. In an ideal world the trader will remain
delta neutral i.e. they will profit regardless of the direction the security is trading in, but only through the
spread.
12
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
Let’s go through an example so this is clearer:
Say you are a trader at a hedge fund and you want to sell 1000 shares of AAPL. You call a salesperson at the
bank, Bobby. Bobby communicates with a trader, Sophie who quotes a two way price. You have already
enquired about other banks and their spreads and have decided that this is the best deal for you (i.e. the
tightest spread), so you sell your shares to the bank. Now, Sophie does not want to take any directionality on
her position, but finds herself with 1000 shares of AAPL. He will then need to work together with the
salesperson to both hedge (i.e. manage the risk of holding the 1000 shares - this will be covered in Lecture 4)
and unwind (i.e. sell off) his position to avoid making a loss with his current position.
A Challenging Case Study – EUR/GBP Currency Pair
Hopes are high on the GBP since the markets believe a Brexit deal will soon be approved in parliament.
Jasmine, a broker at Morgan Stanley, wants to beshorton this pair unless James is willing to pay a higher
price. This means Jasmine would much rather sell EUR to get GBP as opposed to buying EUR with GBP.
James likes to think of himself as a successful day trader, and firmly believes that GBP will rise, meaning that
he islongon GBP.
To avoid confusion, I want to clarify that both Jasmine and James share the same view that GBP will rise
against the EUR which effectively means that they are bothshorton the EUR/GBP pair as they expectthe
value of EUR to drop against GBP.
On Feb 1, 2019, Jasmine gives James the following quote:
Bid (James sells EUR for GBP)Market Ask (James buys EUR with GBP)
£0.892 £0.900 £0.901
We can see that because Jasmine is long on GBP, she has set her bid-ask spread to be a lot more attractive to
her receiving EUR - there is only a 0.001 difference between the market price and the ask price, making it
much more attractive for people to sell their GBP to her. This is in contrast to the Bid price which is 0.008 away
from the market price which makes it much less attractive for people to sell their EUR for GBP. As mentioned
above, James is long on GBP so he sells his EUR where €1 and gets £0.892.
Two months later, the U.K. leaves the E.U. with a good Brexit deal advantageous for the economy, including a
single market agreement. The market is now reflecting this outcome, so Jasmine is nowneutralon the pair.
On Apr 2, 2019, James now wants to sell his GBP for EUR to make a profit, since the price of EUR has
dropped relative to GBP. He goes to Jasmine and receives the following bid-ask spread.
Bid (James sells EUR for GBP)Market Ask (James buys EUR with GBP)
£0.889 £0.892 £0.896
This means if he were to sell his £0.892, he would get back £0.892/£0.896 = €0.996. This is €0.004 less than
what he started with (€1) even though he took the correct position in believing that GBP would rise. In fact, this
difference is Jasmine’s profit as a market-maker!
13
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
As seen by this example, although as a trader you do not profit from the direction of the security that you are
trading, you aim to use your knowledge of the market to create a bid-ask spread that maximises your profit
while remaining competitive to the market.
Structuring
Structuring is the process of creating bespoke products for clients’ complex needs and views on markets.
What does bespoke mean here? Well, in general this involves the combination of 2 or more ‘vanilla’ (vanilla
refers to the most basic version of a financial instrument as opposed to exotic, which represents a more
complicated instrument that combines a few vanilla products) products that produce a pay-off profile (i.e. how
much you can profit and how much you can lose in different scenarios - do not worry, we will be covering this
in Lecture 4) that suits what a company needs. In general there are 2 types of structuring products:
1.Interest rate structuring
2.Equities derivatives structuring
The main clients of interest rate structuring are pension funds while the main clients of the equities derivatives
side are hedge funds. Typically these companies come to you looking for ways to mitigate the risk of future
market conditions (for example if the Fed raises interest rates) or simply to mitigate the risks of their unique
investment strategies. They will usually have an idea about what this product should do, but don’t have access
to it through the Sales & Trading desks or the product might not exist at all. Examples of structuring products
include combining products such as bonds with call options (we will cover options in Lecture 4) or multiple
call/put options together.
For example, let’s say you are an analyst covering credit structuring and a pension fund comes to you. They
know that in the future they will have to pay out a certain amount of money to pensioners,  however they do
not know what the market conditions will be like (such as inflation) up to the time they make that payout.
Therefore, it is your job as a structuring analyst to create a product through the combination of other products
such as bonds and options and accurately price them that fits the specific situation. In general the role of a
structuring analyst is a mix of getting exposure to the clients, the market and quantitative skills (for pricing).
Equity Research
Equity Research is the part of Markets that focuses on analysing companies in different sectors, and sells their
research to investors such as hedge funds and investment banks that want access to their research to help
with their investment ideas or to examine potential deals they want to explore. Examples of these sectors
include Renewables, Healthcare, Oil & Gas, Technology, Media & Telecommunications (TMT) etc. Typically,
these teams will all operate separately and cover their own sectors, and follow a specific few companies in that
sector.
The role of a research analyst is to follow a particular company (or group of companies) very closely - this
includes attending all of the company’s announcements, speaking to their management, and attending their
earnings reports every quarter. The lead analyst of the team (Managing Director) will form an opinion of the
company’s news and update their view on the company. Equity research analysts will split their day following
these companies, speaking to investors who buy their research and pay to talk to these analysts, and writing
research reports on the industry and companies. They will also be required to attend investor roadshows for
IPOs which are marketing events investment banks hold with potential investors to educate them about the
IPO company in order to bookbuild as we covered before.
14
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
It is very important to note that Equity Research sits on the public side. This is because their coverage of their
companies should be unbiased. What do I mean? Imagine Joe is an equity research analyst at Goldman
Sachs specialising in the transport sector and covering Deliveroo. One day one of his colleagues in the ECM
team in the Investment Banking division of Goldman Sachs comes to him saying that he is currently working
on the Deliveroo IPO. Now since this is a company Joe is covering he will be tasked to attend the investor
roadshows to educate investors on the company to drum up interest in the company. There is a potential
conflict of interest here as while Joe is meant to be unbiased in his opinion of the company, having very good
remarks could sway investors to invest in the IPO, which would benefit the ECM team working on the deal.
This is unethical and there are very strict procedures in the bank to avoid this information from crossing the
private side to the public side.
The Buy-Side
Asset Management (AM)
AM services institutional investors such as pension funds, mutual funds, insurance companies by managing
their funds by investing across different asset classes. AM strategies are typically Long-only and no short
positions are taken.
It is common for Investment Banks such as Goldman Sachs and JP Morgan to have asset management
divisions, but these typically exist as separate entities from their sell-side businesses due to potential conflicts
of interest. There are also numerous well-known Asset Managers such as BlackRock and Fidelity.
Hedge Funds
Hedge funds can be seen as a more ‘extreme’ version of AM. Instead of only taking Long-only views on
different asset classes, hedge funds tend to trade on shorter time-frames and take both long and short views
on the security of interest.
What do I mean by short? In general, the sentiment of going short on a stock means you believe that you
believe TSLA’s share price is going to drop. In order to profit from this drop, I go to my broker and borrow
shares of TSLA and sell them on the market. Assuming all goes well and the stock price drops, I can then buy
back the TSLA’s share price at a lower price and then return the stock back to my broker – in effect profiting on
the difference in share price. This process is called shorting a stock.
While this might seem like a good strategy in practice, it is inherently riskier than simply going long on a
company because if things don't pan out as you hope and the stock price rises, there is no limit as to how high
the stock can rise. A good example is the meme stock rally that happened with Gamestop. Basically, a group
of reddit traders realised that many hedge funds had decided to short Gamestop stock (Ticker: GME) as
explained above. They then decided to team up to buy large quantities of GME which caused the share price
to spike. Now, because these hedge funds had borrowed the shares at a specific price without actually owning
the stock, they ran the risk of getting “short squeezed” if the share price went above the price they chose to
borrow it at. As a result of the spike, many hedge funds had to buy the stock to return it to their broker at a loss
- which again caused the share price to continue rising by adding upwards pressure on the stock. As you might
have figured out by now, it can be seen that shorting a stock is much riskier because there is no ceiling/limit to
how high the share price can rise, so theoretically your loss is unlimited. This is contrary to if you simply owned
a stock where the maximum amount you could lose is the money you invested originally, assuming the stock
price goes to £0.
15
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
There are many different kinds of hedge funds and each Portfolio manager, the chief manager of a pool of
money, at the same hedge fund operates differently. There are long/short equity hedge funds, hedge funds that
only invest in a particular industry or with a certain investment theme, and hedge funds that take a
discretionary/systematic approach. A discretionary approach lets the portfolio manager decide whether or not
to buy or hold a given security while a systematic approach utilises a set of predefined rules and price limits
set by the portfolio manager that determine whether or not to buy or sell a security. To highlight the difference
between both approaches let’s look at an example:
Let’s say Apple (stock ticker: APPL) has reported huge sales growth over the past year of its iPhones and
services. Jane is a systematic portfolio manager while Adam is a discretionary portfolio manager. Both of them
decide to buy APPL at a price of $100 and believe that at that price, APPL is a good investment. Jane sets a
lower price limit to sell APPL if it drops below $85 to cut losses and an upper price limit of $120 to sell and take
profit, while Adam believes from his analysis, APPL will rise to $120. A week later, the Central Bank
announces that it will be raising interest rates so, as explained earlier, the cost of borrowing for corporates is
increasing. This results in a widespread correction in the market (i.e. stock prices drop) and the price of APPL
plummets to $80. At this point, Jane has decided to sell APPL because the stock has crossed Jane’s cut loss
price of $85. Adam, on the other hand, still believes that AAPL is still the same company fundamentally and
will still rise to $120 - its management is still top notch, business opportunities are still the same and the
company has enough cash on hand to weather the higher interest rate environment. He therefore continues
holding the stock in the hope it will reach $120 even though the price has dropped to $85.
Hedge funds can also either be family offices, where a small group of traders typically trade the founder’s
money or they could be non-family offices, trading on investor’s money – in this case, because of their
aggressive strategies, hedge funds typically ask for a 2% base fee along with 20% commission on profits (2
and 20 rule), so for example, if an investor has £8,000 originally invested that has grown to £10,000 with the
hedge fund, the hedge fund will take a base fee of £200 (2%) and a further 20% commission on any profits
made, leaving 80% to the investor.
Private Equity Funds
PE funds, also referred to as sponsors, are companies that look to acquire another company through a mixture
of debt and equity (i.e. through taking loans, issuing bonds and using investor’s money) and improve the
business to generate earnings to pay off the debt. Typically, 50-70% of the acquisition is paid for through debt.
Like all funds, these companies pull money from investors – these investors could be pension funds, wealthy
individuals etc, and invest these funds in companies (equity). The rest of the capital needed to acquire a
company is raised through debt – a mixture of loans from banks and the release of bonds to public markets.
The key metric PE funds look at is their Internal Rate of Return (IRR), the rate at which the firm's invested
capital is expected to grow each year that the fund owns the company. Typically the return has to be at least
20-30% over five years. After this period, the PE fund can exit their position by either:
1.Selling the company to another PE fund.
2.Selling the company to a strategic buyer (which would be another corporate).
3.IPO-ing the company onto a public exchange.
Examples of prominent PE funds include Blackstone, KKR, CVC, Carlyle Group etc.
16
Imperial College Investment Society |Securities EducationCertiﬁcate
Bibliography
1.Taylor, Marcus. Forex Trading Industry Statistics, Published 10 June 2018.
https://brokernotes.co/forex-trading-industry-statistics
17

5 
Accounting 
Notes by Ahmad Danesh 
Lead Lecturer, Securities Education Certificate 2022/23. 
Founder, Queen’s Tower Capital (2021 –). 
The main purpose 
It’s often a grave misconception that accounting is all about finding out how much money a business is making 
in terms of profit. I’d like to take this immediate opportunity to say that is NOT what accounting is about. I don’t 
know where this idea has come from over the past 100 years but it’s definitely not the main purpose of 
accounting. It is, however, one of the metrics that accountants use to measure the performance of a company 
but that is not the preliminary reason in the first place that accounting takes place.  
 
Accounting actually answers a different question.. 
 What is the operational worth/strategy of the business in question? 
 
What do I mean by this? Essentially, the profitability of a business isn’t my main interest when I’m looking at a 
business’s accounts. When you are doing accounting, you’re essentially trying to track the spending of the 
company that you’re looking at in order to figure out what it’s up to at different points in time. What is it 
spending its money on over the course of a specific month? What revenue streams exist to help money come 
through the door? How much am I being charged for taxes from His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs? These 
are the sorts of questions that accountants answer because they, er… account for the money that’s coming in 
and out of the business over a specific time period.  
 
When I read financial statements, I want to gain an understanding of what the business is up to. Whether or 
not the business itself is making money is rather irrelevant as long as it’s spending money in the right places at 
the right time. Of course, I’d be alarmed if I was running a venture capital firm and I found out that the founders 
of the next big social network I’d sponsored had falsely squandered half of the capital on “general expenses” 
when in fact they’d been seen on social media showcasing their lifestyle in Ibiza surrounded by illegal 
paraphernalia in the back of a yacht.  
 
This is what accounting does: it shows a total breakdown of the operations of the business and tries to put 
numbers behind it. Profits and loss figures are only a by-product of all the work done to account for everything 
that a business does, NOT its end goal entirely.  
 
So, now that I got that out of the way, let’s discuss some basic concepts in accounting that you’ll need to be 
familiar with. 
Cash vs Accrual basis 
Cash-based accounting 
As I said above, the money-making aspect of a business isn’t entirely relevant when you’re doing accounting. 
Instead, you’re looking at what the business is doing in terms of its operations. This may not make sense to 
you right now so we’re going to try and plug an example instead! 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
6 
 
Consider Oscar and I to be running a nice small bakery in the pretty countryside of Devon. We’ve set up a 
bakery, QT Bakery and we’ve started running it from the 1st of January so that our fiscal year counts as 
January 2022 – January 2023. Because Oscar’s a great baker given his 12.5% maternal Swiss background, 
we’ve received our first order from a customer today (the 29th of October) to supply £1,000 worth of pastries to 
their event next week – namely, the birthday of Oscar’s mum. However, Oscar’s dad (who organised the 
event) says that he can’t pay us until March next year in 2023 because he’s waiting for his year-end bonus to 
come through.  
 
Oscar and I think that we can and will fulfil the order. As we don’t have the money right now from Oscar’s dad 
to pay for the necessary £500 ingredients, we’ve also promised our suppliers that we’ll pay those costs the 
month after Oscar’s dad pays us back, which is in April 2023.  
 
So far, so good. This is pretty much standard transactional behaviour in business and nothing shady is 
happening at all. We are promised money for our work in delivering the pastries and our suppliers are 
promised money for their supplies once me and Oscar get paid.  
 
But (and this is where you need to realise the problem)... how would this look in accounting if we wrote down 
the transactions we did? The logical starting point would be to write down what we did in terms of making the 
pastries and buying our goods alongside how much money you received or gave away for doing those two 
jobs: 
 
Type of transaction Value Transaction Date Date of payment 
Sale of pastries £1,000 29th October March 2023 
Purchases of ingredients £500 29th October April 2023 
 
So far, so good. We need to account for this in some way. The common idea would be to look at how much 
money moved in and out of the business at the time of the transaction date. If we tried this, however, 
something wouldn’t look right… 
 
For the Fiscal Year (FY) January 2022 – January 2023 CASH-BASED 
Account Value (£) 
Sales 0 
Costs 0 
Profit 0 
 
This isn’t a mistake! Remember, we don’t get paid until next year. So, in terms of the cash moving in and out of 
the business, we haven’t received any cash from sales or paid for any costs yet, hence our profit of £0. This is 
otherwise known as cash-based accounting as I’m purely accounting for the bakery’s operations based on the 
movement of cash in and out of the business.  
 
But, think about it. If you read that tabulated breakdown above and had no idea of the inner workings of our 
bakery, you’d believe that nothing was happening in the business, right? You’d think that me and Oscar have 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
7 
done absolutely nothing at all in that entire year since we haven’t made any sales and spent absolutely nada 
on procuring any ingredients – that’s literally that the statement tells you, right? And yet, we have just secured 
a deal to supply £1,000 worth of pastries on the 29th of October to Oscar’s dad and been able to procure £500 
worth of ingredients from our suppliers as long as we pay them later! 
 
This is the issue with cash-based accounting: it does not account for the operations of the business – only the 
cash moving in and out of the company. Cash does NOT represent what is going on in the business although 
it is a metric that can be used to measure the business’s activity WHEN APPLIED CORRECTLY! If you have 
situations where a business is delaying payment, cash-based accounting is going to mess up the true image of 
the business’s operations in a way that an average accountant or shareholder would think you’ve done nothing 
at all in the first place while you’ve been working unbelievably hard to fulfil orders and the like.  
 
So, we need an alternative system. Enter accrual-based accounting! 
Accrual-based accounting 
Let’s instead account for the operations of the business as they happen rather than when we transfer the 
money for the transactions in question.  
 
For the Fiscal Year (FY) January 2022 – January 2023 ACCRUAL-BASED 
Account Value (£) 
Sales 1000 
Costs 500 
Profit 500 
 
Ah, this is much more like it. We don’t receive the £1000 yet from Oscar’s dad nor do we pay the £500 costs of 
the ingredients but we account for it through an accrual-based system, which in essence just means that we 
take note of it when it happens (i.e. when it is ‘recognised’) and not when it is earnt in terms of the cold hard 
cash. That way, when someone looks at the business’s accounts, they can see the work we’ve done in 
supplying the pastries for Oscar’s birthday and realise that me and Oscar have actually done something 
instead of mis-interpreting the lack of cash flowing in or out of the bakery as a sign of inactivity.  
 
We do NOT care when the cash comes in if we are doing accrual-based accounting: we care about when the 
business’s operations have taken place! That’s the key difference between cash and accrual-based 
accounting and I insist that this is clearly understood irrespective of whatever you learn in future accounting 
classes as it forms the basis of how to read and understand financial statements to the best of your abilities!  
 
If you are interested as to when the cash comes in, you’d be more interested in the ideas of account 
receivables in the balance sheet which store information about the incoming money coming into the business 
alongside the business’s cash flow statement that tells you exactly what money is flowing in and out of the 
business at specific times of the year.  
Linking the three key financial statements 
In essence, you can conclude the work of the three financial statements through its three purposes: 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
8 
 
➔ The balance sheet is a snapshot of the business’s assets in terms of what it owns and its liabilities in 
terms of what it owes to any of its debtholders and its owners.  
➔ The income statement links two balance sheets to show how the change in the value of its total assets 
was achieved between two different time periods.  
➔ The cash flow statement links two balance sheets to show how the change in the total value of the 
business’s cash reserves was achieved.  
 
By keeping those three points in mind, you’ll be able to read any financial statement and ensure that you can 
invest appropriately, write effective yet insightful equity research reports and deliver DCF models with intuition 
and clear clarity since you’ll know exactly what you’re doing in terms of the three key financial statements and 
why they work the way they do.  
Technicals 
Some of you who are reading this definitely attended Justin’s accounting workshop the other week over the 
course of the two sessions. While I have to admit that it was a great workshop, there were those amongst you 
who seemed a bit confused about some of the terminology (not to mention the technical issues that Huxley 
and the college’s I.T. department seem to continuously exhibit whenever a large event is being run in South 
Kensington). 
 
I thought it would be useful to cover a few of these terms for the interests of those of you who are a bit stuck 
on what they mean and how they may make more sense by thinking of them from an alternative perspective. 
First, a discussion on EBIT and its sister, EBITDA.  
EBIT and EBITDA 
Consider a simple income statement like the one you see below: 
 
Income Statement (£000s) 
Revenue 1000 
Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) (800) 
GROSS PROFIT 200 
SG&A (90) 
Depreciation (10) 
OPERATING PROFIT 100 
Interest (10) 
INCOME BEFORE TAXES 90 
Tax (30%) (27) 
NET PROFIT 63 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
9 
 
Here, the net profit of the business from the income statement above was £63,000. This is how much the 
company earns after every single possible cost has been deducted from the revenue.  
 
EBITDA is simply the earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation. The formula you normally 
get given is something along the lines of: 
 
EBITDA = Net income (profit) + interest + taxes + depreciation + amortisation 
 
Here, all we’re doing is we’re going from the bottom of the income statement (i.e. the net profit) and we’re 
working our way back up the income statement to get the earnings of the company before the ITDA deductions 
were accounted for. It’s pretty simple: 
 
EBITDA = 63 + 27 + 10 + 10 (+ 0) = 110 (i.e. £110,000). That’s basically the value of the earnings after we’ve 
deducted SG&A from Gross Profit. That’s it, honestly! 
 
EBIT is even simpler. Forget the depreciation and amortisation section: 
 
EBIT = Net Income (profit) + interest + taxes = 63 + 10 + 27 = 100 (i.e. £100,000). That’s interesting… 
That's literally what the operating profit signifies. So really, EBIT is basically operating profit if we work our way 
from the bottom of an income statement and travel upwards, reversing several deductions that we did from the 
top to end at the value of net income.  
 
Now, why do people care about EBITDA and EBIT so much? Well, think about it. Operating profit tells you a 
rough sense of how much money the business is generating before you worry about the more irrelevant stuff 
including taxes and interest. The latter two aren’t really going to stain the quality of the business’s operations 
are they? So, operating profit gives us an idea of how much raw hard cash the business is making.  
 
However, when Justin alluded to the mechanics of depreciation and amortisation, all we’re doing is spreading 
out the cost of an asset’s purchase over the time that we use the said asset. We’ve already spent all our hard 
earned cash buying that asset but we’re doing an accounting trick to show that it’s being used over a fixed 
period of its useful life. That does NOT mean that we’re losing money through depreciation at all! All we’re 
doing is saying “We’ve bought this car for £20,000 cash but we’re going to say in our accounting books that 
the car cost £5,000 each year for 4 years since that’s how long we use it for and that’s how much value it loses 
every year.” The car’s already used up £20,000 of your cash stores back when you first bought it – you’re not 
losing any more money on its purchase over the time that you actually use it!  
 
For that reason, we add depreciation and amortisation costs as the business isn’t actually losing any money 
after the purchase on the asset itself – it’s simply spreading out the cost appropriately to account for its cost as 
a function of how long it’s going to be useful before it gets thrown away. To really add to the picture, you may 
see depreciation being included as a Cost of Goods Sold (CoGS) so be careful as to where you see 
depreciation in the income statement that you’ve got in front of you.  
 
As for EBIT, it only helps us understand how the business is performing without considering costs unrelated to 
the goods or services that it sells. Remember, interest is dependent on the amount of money raised by the 
business through debt and taxes are dependent on where the business is located. Neither of the two have 
anything to do with the business’s main good/service-selling operations so EBIT and EBITDA show the 
business’s earning potential only through its main operations in a clearer sense.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
10 
Enterprise Value 
This term isn’t as hard as it looks. Essentially, when you’re looking at a business, you’re aware that the 
business itself has assets that help it perform its operations. A business can raise money in two ways: 
1. Issue debt. It can e.g. take out a loan to go and buy its equipment and all the material it will need to 
produce its goods and services.  
2. Issue shares to sell equity. It can sell shares of the company to interested people so that they can 
become shareholders. NOTE even if you launch your own company with no other shareholders, you’ve 
still issued shares to yourself in the company (i.e. all 100% of the company’s shares) in return for your 
own money, so the definition holds true even for a sole proprietor. 
 
So, the combination of debt + total value of shares (i.e. market cap) is how the company sources its funds to 
run its operations.  
 
However, here’s a hypothetical for you to think about. If a company takes out a loan of £1 million and sells 
shares to the tune of £1 million, it raises £2 million in total. If it stores that entire £2 million as cash which sits in 
the company’s vaults, that £2 million in cash isn’t actually contributing to the company’s operations. If the 
company’s main planned operations is building high-end computers and all it has so far is just a cash reserve 
of £2 million, that £2 million in cash is literally completely useless to its main aim of building high-end 
computers. As a result, the company’s value of operations is literally zero. Hell, if the CEO actually spent all £2 
million of it to build factories and source material, then its value of operations is exactly £2 million as that’s 
literally how much it cost to get the business’s main task of building computers actually going.  
 
What enterprise value really measures is the value of the company’s operations. To do this, it combines the 
value of the shares it’s sold alongside the debt it’s raised and removes any cash that doesn’t play an active 
part in its operations. Hence, the formula for Enterprise Value (EV) is: 
 
Enterprise Value = Market Capitalisation + Total Debt - Cash.  
 
Alternatively, you might see some people mention EV as a way of seeing the amount of money it takes to buy 
out the business entirely. This is also intuitive through a similar analysis: you have shareholders who want to 
be paid the market rate for their shares (market capitalisation) and people who are owed money due to the 
company’s debt (total value of debt). Once you paid them both off, you then have the company to yourself and 
you’re rewarded with some of the cash reserves to offset the size of your purchase. The two explanations 
(although different) provide the same final formula in terms of what enterprise value actually stands for.   
Market Capitalisation 
This is, er… a badly explained term normally in financial academia. It’s actually quite simple to understand so 
I’m not sure why people get this term so badly confused. Market capitalisation is the value that the market 
gives to the value of shareholder’s equity. This is achieved by taking the market price of each share and 
multiplying it by the number of shares that the company has issued.  
 
Market Capitalisation = Share price × No. of issued shares 
 
Why is this relevant, you might ask? Companies get their accountants to value the assets that they own, which 
in turn calculates the value of equity that shareholders are entitled to. However, accountants may value the 
business’s assets at a value greater than or less than what the market values it at. Market capitalisation is the 
valuation of the shareholders’ equity (and in turn, the assets of the business) from the perspective of market 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
11 
players. If the market values the shareholders’ equity at a value greater than the business does, there might be 
an intangible asset e.g. brand value that the accountants haven’t been able to account for. If the market value 
of shareholders’ equity is less than the business’s valuations, the accountants have clearly overvalued their 
business and something weird is going on inside the business’s accounting and valuation process. 
 
Simple, really. Nothing too complicated there.   
The WACC 
Let me first spell out what it means: the Weighted Average Cost of Capital. The problem with teaching this 
formula is that practically no-one (and I mean literally no-one) that I’ve met has a single clue what it means. 
Often, you’ll see this formula: 
 
(E × e) + (D × d × (1-T)) 
Where: 
 
E → cost of equity 
e → market value of equity 
D → cost of Debt 
d → market value of debt 
T → tax rate 
 
That’s all fun and games but it means absolutely nothing at all if you don’t know where it comes from. 
Evidently, this formula wasn’t developed by some imaginary maths fairies in Canary Wharf or on Wall Street.  
 
So, since I’m a chemistry student and chemists care about where formulae and theory come from rather than 
just blindly apply it (as you might blow something up), let’s try and derive it.  
 
First, let’s discuss the idea of a weighted average.   
What does the Weighted Average actually mean? 
Let’s calculate the mean of 5 numbers: 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. I’m sure that given most of you did GCSE maths, this 
won’t be too hard: 
 
 
 
Thanks to the properties of fractions, we can write this in an alternative form: 
 
 
 
Here, we’ve basically multiplied each value by ⅕ and totalled the result. One way you can consider this is that 
we’ve weighted each number by ⅕ and calculated their total sum. This is a weighted average. Each number 
in the list has a weight of ⅕ that we use to multiply each number and add together to calculate the value of the 
mean.  

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
12 
 
However, we might wish to change the weights if we have a scenario where one number has more importance 
than another e.g. with exam marks.  
 
I take three exams, both out of 60 marks. I scored 30, 40 and 50 in all three exams. If all the exams had 
equal importance (i.e. 33⅓% each), I averaged 40/60.  
 
However, the third exam (the one that I scored 50/60) actually counts for 50% of the weight of my 
overall mark, with the other two exams only counting 25% each. This means that the third exam is 
more important than the first two exams. How do I calculate my weighted average mark? 
 
This isn’t actually that hard. We know the total percentage is 100%, of which 50% is allocated towards the 
second exam. Adapting the example above, we get: 
 
 
 
So, I actually ended up scoring 42.5/60 thanks to the fact that the exam which counted more (i.e. the third one) 
actually contributed more to my final mark, an extra 2.5 marks more. Fantastic! 
 
Similarly, if I know what the total sum of the weights are (in this case, 100), I can calculate decimal values 
instead of writing a fraction: 
 
 
 
There is also a mathematical description using summation to help me simplify the notation of the weighted 
average but since I’m not a mathematician, I’m not really bothered.  
The relevance towards the WACC 
I’ve already mentioned the idea of the weighted average (i.e. the ‘WA’ of the WACC).  
 
What do I mean by the cost of capital? Do you remember how companies raise funds to kickstart their 
operations? They issue shares to potential shareholders, which is the company’s cost of equity and they also 
take on debt from third-parties, which is their cost of debt. Both these two contribute to the total cost of 
capital, which is how much it costs the business to run its operations. There’s another definition as well but 
we’ll come onto that momentarily.  
 
Remember, the cost of capital isn’t just the normal mean of the cost of equity and cost of debt because they 
may not be allocated in equal amounts. Think of it, if 25% of the company’s capital comes from shareholders 
buying shares and the other 75% comes from raising debt, it’s a pretty silly idea to then work out the average 
cost of capital by simply adding the two numbers up and dividing by 2 (we divide by 2 because there’s only 2 
ways of raising capital) since we’d be weighting both costs of capital by 50% each (100 / 2 = 50), essentially 
saying that the cost of equity counts more and the cost of debt counts less towards our final average.  
 
So, if we want to work out the average cost of capital, we’d take a weighted average.  
For the example above, we’d do: 
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
13 
 
 
More generally, we could work out our final weight by adding both the cost of equity CE and cost of debt CD. 
Then, We could weight each term accordingly based on how much they contribute to the total sum of CE and 
CD. Writing this out, we get: 
 
 
 
We’re getting closer! Sometimes, the combined value of equity E and debt D gets fused together to give 
another term, V. So, sometimes you see: 
 
 
 
Some of you might have wondered ‘isn’t E and CE the same? They’re both the cost of equity, aren’t they? 
Aren’t D and CD the same thing here?’ And if you thought that, you’d be right! Sadly, because some academic 
people in finance do things their own way, they’ve sort of forgotten the rules of maths and because of these 
rules (which I’m discussing in the next paragraph), I’ve had to separate the two terms accordingly because I’m 
doing to do something of a Legendre-transform-esque magic trick.  
 
There’s one thing I haven’t explained to you yet… What is the cost of equity? What does the cost of selling 
shares mean? What is the cost of taking on debt? For the latter, you’d know what that is… it’s the interest 
rate! It’s a percentage of the loan that you have to pay back on top of the original loan because of the fact that 
you’ve rented money from a third-party. Read this bit very carefully: 
 
The cost of selling shares is that shareholders expect a percentage return on the money they invested 
in buying your shares! The cost of taking on debt is that the debtholders expect a return on the 
amount of money they’ve given you, which is the interest rate.  
 
The shareholders might want to buy a share of your company and expect you to pay back around, say… 5% a 
year of their investment. Likewise, the people who you owe debt to might expect you to grow the company in a 
way that they expect you to pay back around, say… 7% a year on their loan that they gave you. What’s the 
average rate of return from both the shareholders and the debtholders? Is it slap bang in the middle at 6%? 
No. Why? Because you have a disproportionate amount of cash raised from equity shareholders and 
debtholders in your company. In our example, 25% of the capital raised came from shareholders whereas 75% 
came from debtholders so it’ll be closer to 7% than 5% (it’s 6.5% if you do a weighted average). 
 
For this reason, the cost of equity and the cost of debt are the percentage returns that the shareholders and 
the debtholders expect from buying your shares or lending you a loan. So, the WACC actually gives you a 
proportionate value of the expected return from your business. I prefer to now complete the job by changing CE 
to Re (return on equity) and changing CD to Rd (return on debt) to match what’s often taught on the CFA Level I 
exam: 
 
 
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
14 
Sometimes, you’ll see a version of the formula that includes the tax rate, t: 
 
 
 
It seems fairly strange that the tax has a part to play in the calculation for the WACC. What do taxes have to 
do with this (if anything at all)? 
 
Recall the income statement earlier: 
 
Income Statement (£000s) 
Revenue 1000 
Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) (800) 
GROSS PROFIT 200 
SG&A (90) 
Depreciation (10) 
OPERATING PROFIT 100 
Interest (10) 
INCOME BEFORE TAXES 90 
Tax (30%) (27) 
NET PROFIT 63 
 
Tax is paid after the interest is paid for. So, if you have a lot of debt, the amount of interest you pay increases 
BUT the amount you pay through tax decreases! In essence, debt reduces the amount of tax you owe by 
lowering the income before taxes. We say that interest is a tax-deductible expense as it is deducted from the 
total amount of tax that you have to eventually pay! Sometimes, you might read this term being named ‘tax 
shield’, which is what the WACC accounts for. In essence, debt actually does have a benefit! 
 
Here’s a classic example to get things in perspective. Consider that I earn a salary of £100,000 (very 
hypothetical and unlikely, but it’ll do). If the flat tax rate for my salary is around 30%, I end up having to pay 
£30,000 in taxes to Her Majesty’s government and I get to take home £70,000.  
 
What if I had also taken out a loan of £200,000 and have to pay 10% interest on it every year? Well, I have to 
pay £20,000 in interest. BUT, what do I pay in taxes? I don’t get taxed on the £100,000 that I take home 
– remember the income statement above. Interest is tax-deductible as it reduces the net profit I get to take 
home.  
 
If I have to pay £20,000 interest from my £100,000 salary, I am only taxed on the remaining £100,000 - 
£20,000 = £80,000. As a result, I pay 30% on £80,000, which is £24,000.  
 
Wow.  

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
15 
 
By taking on some debt, I’ve actually reduced the amount of tax that I pay from £30,000 to £24,000. Yes, I 
would only be taking home £56,000 but that’s an entirely different story altogether. Here, I’m bothered about 
the benefit of the debt and not how much profit I’m making. In essence, the debt of £200,000 has actually 
saved me £6,000 a year! As one great quote I read on this subject read: ‘money saved is money earned’. By 
saving £6,000, the debt has helped me actually earn £6,000! So, really, the debt I’ve taken out hasn’t cost me 
£20,000 overall but instead actually costs me £20,000 - £6,000 (cost - gain) = £14,000.  
 
So, the interest cost is actually £20,000 - 30% (i.e. 30% on the £20,000) = £20,000 × [1 - 0.3] = £14,000.  
 
More holistically, if a debt costs me 10% of my returns but the tax rate is 25%, the actual cost of my tax is: 
 
10% × [1 - 0.25] = 7.5% 
 
Phew. That’s a very hard concept to understand and often one that is rarely mentioned. I hope that does add a 
clear image on the WACC and where it comes from so that in future, you know exactly what you’re trying to 
calculate when you’re doing DCF calculations, for example.  
Capital Asset Pricing Model 
There might be situations where you won’t have the value of equity to hand or you might have to calculate it by 
hand. How do financial academics do this? They have a model (just like they do for literally anything) called 
the Capital Asset Pricing Model. 
 
It’s a bit hard to explain this bit without explaining what the government bond is. So, sometimes you’ll hear 
some terms that you’ll familiarise yourself with in Lecture 6. For now, I’m just going to pre-emptively mention a 
few of them with a little bit of context so that you can understand the next few points. Once you’ve completed 
Lecture 6, you can return to these notes and fully understand the ideas that I’ve discussed.  
 
The trouble with buying stocks is that it’s risky. Ah, great – another term you’ll find more about next lecture. If 
something in finance is risky, it means that it can make you lose money. If I buy a share of a company, I might 
lose money as the value of that share can go down to £0 just as much as it could go up in value. The safest 
thing that I could buy into would be a government bond because which government could never pay back its 
bondholders? If it runs out of money, it could easily increase taxes to recoup the money owed.  
 
In economics, if I invest in something with my money, I’ve lost out on other opportunities to invest into other 
things. I can measure my loss in opportunities by measuring the opportunity cost. When buying a stock, this 
opportunity cost is the value of a government bond that I could have invested in. The riskier the stock that I 
buy, the greater the potential returns. This comes down to whether the stock price follows the pattern of the 
entire stock market. If the stock is very risky, it behaves erratically and unpredictably BUT this could also mean 
I could make a lot of money while everything else behaves predictably – this is the essence of stock trading: 
finding stocks that could be very risky compared to its competitors but also provide huge potential returns.  
 
In theory, I could define my risk in terms of my opportunity cost through a value denoted as β, where the 
higher the β, the higher the risk. I could define a zero-risk investment (i.e. β = 0) as an investment in a 
government bond and define a straight line relationship between the riskiness of my investment and the 
potential returns I get. This is the Capital Asset Pricing Model.  
 
Putting it in equation form (just like y = mx + c), it gives us: 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
16 
Ra = Rf + β(Rm – Rf) 
where: 
Ra = Expected return on an asset (i.e. the share) in question GIVEN BY THE MODEL 
Rf = Return on a risk-free investment 
β = The ‘riskiness’ of the asset (i.e. the share) in question 
Rm = Return given by the market 
 
Normally, some economists like to mention that the Rm – Rf term is the ‘market-risk premium’, which 
essentially just stands for how much you’re willing to pay more in the market for the risk you’re taking on as a 
share buyer.   
 
Here’s a nice little illustration of the CAPM to show you how this looks: 
 
 
 
If I’m honest, I’ve never really liked the CAPM. Primarily, my issue is that the maths tries to predict the future 
through using past data. However, past information is never indicative of future success. Just because 
Barcelona won 4 Champions League titles in the last 18 years does NOT mean that they’ll win one in the next 
10 or 20 years. Maths does use probability to do its analysis, although probability is something that will never 
be able to predict events with absolute certainty i.e. a probability of 1. Of course, mathematicians have very 
little choice in the data they can use otherwise so they’re forced to use historical data. But, the strange yet 
blind belief that quantitative modellers know precisely how the market behaves and have developed 
safeguards in their models is a complete fallacy that shouldn’t be believed at any cost! 
 
There are other professional issues that the CAPM struggles with. Firstly, the idea of a risk-free rate. We 
define the risk-free return rate as the rate given by government bonds with zero risk. This does NOT hold true! 
If a government can (and sometimes has) collapsed, the ‘risk-free’ returns don’t become ‘risk-free’ any longer 
as you could lose money by realising that the government can’t pay you back.  
 
I haven’t mentioned this but β is actually based on the historical risk of a stock in relation to the market. For a β 
of 1, the stock behaves just like any other stock you could find on the market hence why we denote the market 
return Rm for a β of 1. For a β of 0, the stock does not behave like anything on the market and is essentially 
mimicking the risk-less nature of a government bond.  
 
Historically, some markets have behaved normally BUT this does not mean that they’ll continue to behave the 
same in the future. Just like a gambler’s fallacy is that they think they can carry on winning whilst they’re on a 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
17 
streak before they lose the next game and it’s all over for them, people in markets are reminded that ‘black-
swan’ style events are very possible and if they’re not factored appropriately in mathematical models, it’s very 
easy to ruin yourself by not hedging the risk of losing money in an appropriate fashion.
