Equities 
Notes by Adam Snelling 
Lecturer, Securities Education Certificate 2022/23. 
Senior Research Analyst, Imperial Blockchain Group 
Cryptocurrency Analyst, QT Capital (2021-22) 
 
Asset Classes 
An asset is any item of economic value, this can range from anything as simple as a car or a watch, to 
something as complex as an options contract. 
 
Asset classes are ways in which investors group different assets that have a similar financial structure and are 
subject to similar laws and regulations. 
 
Focusing on different asset classes can make it easier for investors to diversify their portfolios and spread their 
risk.  
What Are the Most Popular Asset Classes? 
There are many options when it comes to investing in different asset classes. Historically, the most popular 
asset classes have been equities (shares in companies), fixed income (bonds) and cash equivalents. 
However, as the financial world has become more complex and automated, we have more complex 
investment options. 
 
These range from financial derivatives (futures, forwards etc) to more recently, cryptocurrencies. Hence there 
is a plethora of choice for the opportunistic investor. Typically, each asset class also has a level of risk 
associated with it, for example: 
 
Fixed Income (e.g. 
Bonds) 
Equities Commodities Cryptocurrencies 
Low Risk Medium Risk High Risk High Risk 
 
We will be explaining why Equities are seen as a relatively risky investment, but also different ways investors 
trade in the stock market to reduce that risk. 
What are equities? 
Equity represents ownership in a company. Let’s assume you go to the stock market and buy shares in a 
company called ABC. Once you have executed that trade you are now a part-owner of ABC. 
 
What does this mean? Well, if the value of ABC’s shares go up you can sell your particular share back on the 
stock market for a profit. Likewise, if the shares go down, you will sell at a loss. You might also be entitled to 
dividends (we will go through what this means later) and depending on what percentage of all shares you own 
you could have influence on the company’s operations. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
19 
What affects equities? 
If I could tell you exactly what affects the value of a company’s shares, I probably wouldn’t be writing these 
notes. Anyway, what you need to know is just like any investment vehicle, equities are affected by a large 
number of complex factors that make it difficult for any individual or institution to predict.  
 
Like any asset, the price of a company’s shares depends on supply and demand for that stock at the time. 
However, there are ways in which people try to assign a value to a company and its stock and we will have a 
look at this later. 
Private Equity 
Private equity refers to company stock that is not publicly listed on a stock exchange. Investing in private 
companies is quite different from public companies as there is usually a high barrier of entry due to the large 
sums of money required to invest in these companies.This is a popular investment class for high net-worth 
individuals due to the possibility of high returns. However, there are ways for individual investors to get 
involved with private equity. 
 
Private equity firms specialise in investing in private companies before they go public. They can earn money 
by charging management and performance fees from investors in a fund. Private equity capital can be utilised 
to fund new technology in emerging markets, make acquisitions of other companies, and expand working 
capital for a business.  
Public Equity 
Public equity is much easier for individual investors to invest in as you can just purchase the company’s shares 
on a stock exchange. There is no limit on how much you can invest, and there is greater liquidity than private 
equity, as you can sell your shares back onto the stock market whenever you like.  
 
Both public and private equity have advantages and disadvantages for companies and investors. 
 
One of the biggest differences between private and public equity is that private equity investors are generally 
paid through distributions rather than stock accumulation. Distributions are just ways of returning investment 
gains amongst investors, whilst stock accumulation refers to increasing the amount of stock someone has in a 
particular company. 
Stock Exchange 
Now, with all this talk about public and private equity, you might be wondering how you actually purchase 
equity for yourself. Well, let me introduce to you the stock exchange. 
 
An exchange is just a place where buyers and sellers meet to exchange goods. In the past this used to be a 
physical place where buyers would place their bids for a good, and sellers would place their offers for said 
good, hence creating a market where buyers and sellers can agree on prices. 
 
This philosophy is maintained in modern exchanges, only with this market-making process being a lot more 
automated and efficient due to the use of computers. Publicly-listed companies are traded in the UK mainly via 
the London Stock Exchange (LSE) and it is likely where you would go to purchase company stock. 
 
Let’s assume that you would like to purchase shares in ABC, in the past you might have been able to just walk 
into the LSE and start bidding your price, however with larger swathes of investors and heightened security, 
this is not possible. Hence, you must place your order through what is known as a broker. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
20 
 
A broker has financial and legal right to trade in the London Stock Exchange, and hence will execute a trade 
on your behalf. The broker buys the shares from the exchange using the money you have supplied them and 
then returns the shares to the account you have with the broker.  
Understanding the London Stock Exchange (LSE) 
You might have heard of something called the "Big Bang". This refers to the government's deregulation of the 
London stock market in 1986, an event that led to a modernised electronic trading system and opened up the 
LSE to capital markets worldwide. 
 
The immense deregulation of the financial markets was introduced in a bid to establish London as a hub of 
financial services and encourage free-market competition.  
 
This enabled the London Stock Exchange to become one of the most international stock exchanges, rivalling 
other stock exchanges around the world such as the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE).  
IPO 
An initial public offering (IPO) is the process by which a private company becomes publicly-listed on a stock 
exchange, allowing anyone to trade its shares. The companies must meet requirements specified by that 
specific exchange. 
 
IPOs allow companies to gain further capital investment by offering their shares to a wider audience of 
potential buyers. It can also be seen as an exit strategy for the company’s founders as well as those who 
invested when the company was still private. Realising the profit from their investment. 
How an Initial Public Offering (IPO) Works 
Before an IPO, a company is considered private. As a pre-IPO private company, the business has grown with 
a relatively small number of shareholders including early investors such as the founders along with 
professional investors such as venture capitalists. 
 
Before the IPO the company will release what is called a Prospectus. In this document the company reveals all 
the details relating to the release of their shares onto the secondary market (stock market). This is usually 
done with the aid of an investment bank, where the number of shares and price of each share are included, as 
well as any other business information.  
 
Investors can then apply to buy the shares while the offer period is open. Once investors have been allocated 
their shares, the stock is now free to trade on the stock market the company is listed on. 
 
One of the main reasons for issuing an IPO is the raising of capital. As a result of offering shares in the 
company to the public, capital can be accumulated at a faster rate which can lead to greater growth in the 
company if invested properly. 
 
There are also other legal obligations that come with being a public company as compared to a private one. 
This is mainly to do with consumer protection regulations. As a result of this greater transparency, companies 
are likely to experience more favourable borrowing terms due to less risk on the behalf of the creditor, leading 
to a lower cost of debt. 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
21 
However, there are still some unpleasant circumstances that come with becoming a public company. The most 
prominent of these are the increased costs that come with an IPO, most of them unrelated to the cost of doing 
business. This can include legal and accounting fees on required processes before the company can be listed. 
However, by far the greatest of these costs are the underwriting fees, where an investment bank will 
underwrite the value of the shares about to be listed on the stock market. Charging up to 7% of the value of 
the shares being listed.  
 
Another potential disadvantage could be the distraction of the price of the company’s stock, which could be 
influenced more by factors outside of the company’s control. However, this could lead to fragmentation in the 
company’s goals and objectives if the wrong things are prioritised. 
The context of accounting 
Balance Sheet 
A balance sheet is a financial statement that reports a company's assets, liabilities, and shareholder equity. 
You might be asking what a liability is, well it is crucial to understanding how businesses. Liabilities should not 
be confused with expenses, expenses are the costs a company generates from its operations, whilst liabilities 
are the debts the company owes.  
 
Say ABC just received a loan from a bank of £1000 to help them get up and running. An example of their 
expenses would be their employees wages, but a liability would be the debt plus any interest they owe to the 
bank. 
 
Fundamental analysts use balance sheets to calculate financial ratios that can help give an idea of what a 
company is worth. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
22 
 
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
23 
How Balance Sheets Work 
The purpose of a balance sheet is to provide a snapshot of a company’s financial position at the time. 
However it can be used in different ways by different parties. 
 
When a key stakeholder in the company or an employee reviews the company’s balance sheet it gives an idea 
on whether the company is succeeding or failing to meet its targets. However, when the balance sheet is 
reviewed externally, for example by potential investors, it gives insight as to the health of the company and 
allows them to assess whether the company could be a good investment opportunity. 
 
The balance sheet adheres to the following accounting equation, with assets on one side, and liabilities plus 
shareholder equity on the other: 
 
Assets = Liabilities + Shareholders’ Equity 
 
In other words, the balance sheet must always be balanced. This formula is intuitive. That's because a 
company has to pay for all the things it owns (assets) by either borrowing money (taking on liabilities) or taking 
it from investors (issuing shareholder equity). 
Assets 
We already know what assets are, but we can see how they are shown on a balance sheet.  
  
They are listed from top to bottom in order of their liquidity. This is the ease with which they can be converted 
into cash. They are divided into current assets, which can be converted to cash in one year or less; and non-
current or long-term assets, which cannot. 
 
Current assets can include: 
● Short-term investments 
● Advance payments 
Non-current assets include the following: 
● Long-term investments 
● Intangible fixed assets (such as a patent) 
● Tangible fixed assets (such as property or equipment) 
Liabilities 
Now that we know what a liability is, we can see that on a balance sheet liabilities are divided into similar 
sections as assets, with current liabilities being those due within one year, and long-term or non-current 
liabilities are debts due after one year. 
 
Current liabilities might include: 
● Taxes due within a year  
● Short-term loans 
● Money owed to suppliers 
 
Long-term liabilities could include: 
● Long-term loans 
● Bonds payable  
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
24 
Bonds payable refers to the amount of money the company will owe people that have bought their issued 
bonds. 
Shareholder Equity 
Shareholder equity refers to how much of the company is owned by shareholders once liabilities have been 
subtracted from assets. It is essentially the money belonging to the business shareholders as a result of 
investing in the company, note this includes business owners as well. 
Limitations of a Balance Sheet 
There are some issues that come with using a balance sheet, similar to all other financial statements. One 
thing to watch out for is the valuation assigned to certain assets. These can sometimes be quite inaccurate as 
the valuation could be quite old and hence not represent current market value. Therefore, it might be worth 
doing your own personal research into said market, rather than taking the value on the balance sheet as said. 
 
Not only might values be unintentionally inaccurate, but some companies might deliberately alter the values on 
the balance sheet in order to make the company look more favourable to potential investors. This is 
particularly true for intangible assets where they are more difficult to quantify their value. For example, a 
company might include some fictitious assets such as discounts in costs of goods to inflate the value of the 
company. 
Cash Flow Statement 
A cash flow statement is a document that records aspects of a company’s operations. It is usually released at 
the end of every quarter for the previous quarter (three months) and does exactly what it says on the tin: 
describes the flow of cash. 
 
What does this mean in practice? Well we can break down the cash flow statement into main components: 
1. Cash flow from operating activities 
2. Cash flow from investing activities 
3. Cash flow from financing activities 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
25 
 
Cash from Operating Activities 
Let’s return to our company ABC and suppose they are a retail business selling clothing. The cash flow from 
operating activities can be seen as the net cash position of the company arising out of the core business.  
 
It reflects revenue coming into the company, in the case of ABC this could be from the sales of clothing, and 
costs coming out of the company, for example rent on the store in which ABC sells their clothing. 
Other operating activities that might be included in the cash flow statement: 
● Interest payments 
● Income tax payments 
● Payments made to suppliers of goods and services used in production 
● Salary and wage payments to employees 
● Depreciation 
● Change in investment portfolio 
Cash from Investing Activities 
Investing activities include any uses of the company’s cash to purchase an asset or the cash coming in from 
the sale of an asset.  
 
Say for example that ABC owns some property from which they lease out to other companies to use. They 
might decide to sell this asset for £1000 and purchase an £800 building in another area. Hence, the net cash 
flow from ABC’s investing activities is £1000 - £800 = £200. 
 
Other investing activities include: 
● Any payments relating to mergers and acquisitions (M&A) of other companies 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
26 
● Loans the company makes to suppliers 
 
Cash from Financing Activities 
This part of the cash flow statement includes all cash that passes from investors into the company, as well as 
cash out of the company to its shareholders. 
 
A company could at the end of every quarter issue a dividend to its shareholders, this would represent cash 
out of the company through financing. They could also receive cash flows into the company through the sale of 
its stock. 
 
Other financing activities include: 
● Payments for stock repurchases by the company 
● Repayment of debt to banks (from potential loans the company might require) 
● Issuance of a corporate bond 
 
Now we will look at how to calculate cash flow, there are two methods: the direct method and the indirect 
method. 
Direct Cash Flow Method 
This is a very straightforward way of calculating the cash flow of a company. You essentially just add up all the 
cash coming in and subtract all the cash going out to find the company’s net position over a certain period. 
 
By going through each aspect of the cash flow statement this is relatively easy and is more common amongst 
smaller businesses as it provides great insight as to the current position of the company and the best actions 
to move it forward. 
Indirect Cash Flow Method 
The other accounting method is indirect cash flow. This is different from the direct method in that you begin 
with the net income generated over a period (from the income statement), and this value is then added or 
subtracted based on the assets and liabilities located in the balance sheet. 
The main difference between the direct and indirect method is when it comes to cash flows from operating 
activities. In the direct method this is treated as normal cash flows in and out of the business, but in the indirect 
method you start with net income and then change the cash flow based on the change in assets and liabilities. 
Both methods should yield similar results, although most accountants prefer the indirect method as it can be 
simpler to prepare. 
Limitations of the Cash Flow Statement 
The cash flow statement is very useful in understanding the movement of cash within a company, however it 
can be misleading when being used to compare companies within the same industry.  
As cash flow statements don’t normally show a breakdown of the costs and revenues of a company, it can be 
difficult to interpret the profitability of said company. Hence, comparing cash flows of two different companies 
to determine which one is more profitable can be a mistake. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
27 
Also, since the cash flow statement only shows the cash position of the company at the end  of a period, it is 
not a true reflection of the company’s liquidity, as it could own some liquid assets not displayed in the 
statement. 
Income Statement 
An income statement is a financial report that details a company’s income and expenses usually over each 
quarter. 
 
The income statement is defined by the following equation: 
  
Net Income = (Total Revenue + Gains) – (Total Expenses + Losses) 
 
The purpose of the income statement is to show where a company makes its revenues and incurs its 
expenses, and is vital in analysing the health of a company.  
  
The revenue can be further divided into operating and non-operating revenue, this depends on the specific 
business model of the company, but dividing the revenue like this can provide greater clarity on where the 
company is generating its revenue and where it can potentially expand.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
28 
  
Operating Revenue 
This is revenue gained by the company through its primary activities. Let’s take our example of our retail 
company ABC. Their operating revenue would largely consist of revenue gained from the sales of their 
clothing. 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
29 
  
Non-operating Revenue 
This is revenue gained through secondary activities from the company. If we look at ABC, this could include 
things like interest earned on business capital in a bank account, or rent from other properties that ABC owns. 
Gains 
This is subtly different from non-operating revenue, in that gains usually represent non-business activities that 
are quite irregular. For example, ABC might be upgrading their delivery system for online orders and could be 
upgrading their transport vans. The revenue received from selling their old vans would be seen as a gain. 
  
Similar to revenue, expenses are split into operating expenses and non-operating expenses, where operating 
expenses are those incurred from the primary activities of the business. Non-operating expenses are those 
that come about from alternative business activities. 
Operating Expenses 
An example of this for ABC could be the cost of purchasing clothing goods from their supplier. 
Non-operating expenses 
In the case of ABC, this could be the cost of interest that needs to be paid on a loan from a bank. 
Losses 
This is sometimes included in non-operating expenses although can be slightly different. Just like gains these 
are irregular, usually one-off losses accrued by the company. An example of a loss for ABC could be the cost 
of changing the location of some of their business operations. 
Limitations of the income statement 
The income statement is an incredibly useful document, however it is by no means the sole document in 
determining a successful company. Something to beware about income statements is they tend to vary widely 
over different time periods. This is because income statements will include one-off transactions that could 
drastically change the net income for that period, but is not reflective of the long-term net income of the 
company. 
 
Also, the income statement does not provide any detail on the actual business model. How the company 
generates revenue and tries to cut costs. It is for this reason that when trying to valuate companies, it is 
important to look at not just the quantitative data as derived from financial statements, but also the qualitative 
aspects. 
 
PE ratio 
The PE ratio is one of the fundamental metrics used to analyse companies as a possible investment 
opportunity. 
It is the price-to-earnings ratio and it is a ratio between a company’s share price and its earnings per share. 
Now, what do these values actually mean? A company’s share price is the price of one share on the stock 
market. If we go back to our example of ABC, its share price is the current price of one share on the London 
Stock Market.  
  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
30 
Earnings per share is a bit more difficult to obtain. To calculate this you need the earnings the company has 
generated (usually over the past 12 months) and the total amount of shares issued by the company. ‘Earnings’ 
is just another word for net income, so you can find the total earnings of a company by looking at their income 
statement as previously discussed.  
To find the total number of shares issued, most financial websites should have this available on their website. 
Note, some companies might report their earnings per share themselves on their income statement, however it 
is still good to know how to calculate this yourself.  
  
For example, say ABC reported earnings of £1 million over the past 12 months and they have 100,000 shares 
outstanding. Then their earnings per share is £1,000,000
100,000 = £10. Now suppose the current price of one share is 
£20 on the stock market, then ABC’s P/E ratio is £20
£10 = 2. 
  
This is known as a trailing PE ratio, where earnings are based on previous earnings of the company. 
  
Another form of PE ratio is forward PE ratio, where instead of using past earnings, you use projected earnings 
that the company might generate. 
  
Why use PE ratio? 
PE ratio can be seen as determining the value of a stock, and is usually compared to the company’s previous 
PE ratios, or the PE ratios of other similar companies. If the PE ratio is unusually low, an investor may see the 
stock as being undervalued and hence might decide to long the stock. On the other hand if the PE ratio is 
unusually high then the stock might be overvalued and an investor might want to short the stock.  
  
Limitations of PE ratio 
Like all financial analysis, using PE ratios is not foolproof and there are particular limitations with using it. One 
is that it can’t be used for companies that generate negative earnings, which is quite common especially for 
younger companies. Also, share prices are incredibly volatile, which could incredibly skew that value of your 
PE ratio. When using a trailing PE ratio, there is no guarantee that past performance will be an indicator of the 
company’s future results. When using a forward PE ratio, there is a possibility that a company will not meet its 
forecasted earnings. 
  
PS ratio 
Similar to PE ratio, the PS ratio is another metric used to analyse companies and their profitability. It is the 
price-to-sales ratio and compares the price of one share of a company to its sales per share, usually over the 
past 12 months. 
  
Again, you can find a company’s share price on the exchange that it is listed on, and ‘sales’ is just another 
word for revenue generated. Hence, you can find the company’s sales per share by taking the revenue 
generated on the income statement and dividing it by the total number of shares issued.  
  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
31 
For example, say ABC reported revenues of £2.5 million over the past 12 months and they have 100,000 
shares outstanding. Then their sales per share is  £2,500,000
100,000 = £25. Now suppose the current price of one 
share is £20 on the stock market, then ABC’s PS ratio is  £20
£25 = 0.8. 
  
Similar to PE ratio, this is a trailing PS ratio, where we used past revenue. We could also use forward PS ratio, 
where we use forecasted sales over the next year. 
  
Why use PS ratio? 
PS ratio is another metric that can help allude to the financial health of a company.  Similar to PE ratio, a low 
value of PS ratio compared to other companies in its sector could be a signal of an undervalued stock, and a 
high PS ratio could show a company is overvalued. However, one benefit of PS ratio over PE ratio is that it can 
be used for all companies, as it doesn’t take into account costs and hence whether a company actually makes 
any profit. 
  
Limitations of PS ratio 
However, this can lead to one great inaccuracy of PS ratio, in that it doesn’t take into account what costs a 
company is generating. So if a company is projected to see an increase in sales, but its costs are projected to 
increase at a greater rate, then it might not be a good investment opportunity, however the PS ratio will not 
take this into account.  
  
EV 
Enterprise Value (EV) is a measure of a company’s total value, including all the assets that it owns. It is 
important to know that this is different from equity value, which is just a measure of a company’s market value 
in equity. This is calculated by taking the current share price and multiplying it by the total number of shares 
outstanding: 
  
𝐸𝑞𝑢𝑖𝑡𝑦 𝑣𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒 = 𝑝𝑟𝑖𝑐𝑒 𝑝𝑒𝑟 𝑠ℎ𝑎𝑟𝑒 × 𝑛𝑢𝑚𝑏𝑒𝑟 𝑜𝑓 𝑠ℎ𝑎𝑟𝑒𝑠 𝑜𝑢𝑡𝑠𝑡𝑎𝑛𝑑𝑖𝑛𝑔 
 
This is also known as market capitalisation. 
  
Enterprise value on the other hand is calculated by the following: 
  
𝐸𝑛𝑡𝑒𝑟𝑝𝑟𝑖𝑠𝑒 𝑣𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒 = 𝑚𝑎𝑟𝑘𝑒𝑡 𝑐𝑎𝑝 + 𝑑𝑒𝑏𝑡 – 𝑐𝑎𝑠ℎ 𝑜𝑟 𝑐𝑎𝑠ℎ 𝑒𝑞𝑢𝑖𝑣𝑎𝑙𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑠 
  
What does EV tell you? 
When looking at Enterprise value for the first time it may just seem like some arbitrary calculation that would 
not be particularly useful to anyone. However, it is important to look at this from the perspective of a potential 
investor trying to evaluate what the real value of a company is. 
  
We have already established what the market cap (equity value) is. Debt is what the company owes to its 
creditors (people that have loaned money to the company). Cash or cash equivalents are just the amount of 
cash or very liquid assets the company owns. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
32 
  
Suppose John would like to purchase ABC. In order to develop a fair value of the company he may try to 
determine the enterprise value of the company. Part of what he would have to pay for the company would be 
its equity value, as we already discussed. However, another cost he would have to pay for is the debt the 
company owes. We subtract cash or cash equivalents because this would reduce what John has to pay for 
ABC as he can use the cash to pay off some of the debt, for example. 
  
EV is very useful as it takes into account not only market cap but also the debt a company owes and its cash 
in reserves, both of which can be found on the balance sheet document we already discussed.  
EV can also be used for useful metrics such as EV/EBITDA and EV/Sales to aid in gaining better accuracy on 
the value of a company. 
  
EBITDA 
EBITDA stands for earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation and is a widely used metric 
for determining the profitability of a company.  
 
We know earnings means the net income of a company. Interest refers to the payments a company is required 
to make on the debt it owes, when calculating EBITDA, we do not include the cost of these interest payments. 
Taxes are pretty self-explanatory; we do not include the cost of any taxes the company has to pay. 
Depreciation refers to the loss in value of physical (tangible) assets, such as machinery or vehicles, usually 
due to their use overtime in the company’s business activities. Amortisation is similar to depreciation, except it 
refers to the decrease in value of intangible assets, such as patents on company products or software the 
company has.  
  
𝐸𝐵𝐼𝑇𝐷𝐴 = 𝑒𝑎𝑟𝑛𝑖𝑛𝑔𝑠 + 𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑡 + 𝑡𝑎𝑥𝑒𝑠 + 𝑑𝑒𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 + 𝑎𝑚𝑜𝑟𝑡𝑖𝑠𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 
  
For example, let’s say ABC has a net income of £1 million over the past 12 months. This is its earnings and 
can be obtained from the income statement. We will then add to this the costs of interest, taxes, depreciation 
and amortisation, as all these costs are subtracted from revenue in the income statement to obtain the 
earnings of £1 million. Suppose ABC paid £100,000 in taxes, £100,000 in interest on debt and lost £50,000 
due to devaluation of its clothing machinery in its factories. Lastly, suppose ABC paid £200,000 for a patent on 
some of its manufacturing processes for 10 years, hence over the last year, it has incurred an amortisation 
cost of £200,000
10  = £20,000. 
  
So the EBITDA of ABC is £1,000,000 + £100,000 + £100,000 + £50,000 + £20,000 = £1,270,000. 
  
Why use EBITDA? 
This may seem like an odd metric, as we are just ignoring some of the costs incurred by the company. 
However, it is useful as it measures the company’s value, minus any costs associated with tax and financing 
decisions, and how the depreciation of assets is scheduled. It is seen as having a loose similarity to cash flow 
and can be a good estimator of the profitability of a company. It is also useful for business owners in 
determining how much costs they can afford with relation to their company’s structure and still remaining 
profitable. 
  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
33 
EBITDA Disadvantages 
Although, some investors reject the use of EBITDA as it can greatly alter the perception of a company’s 
profitability. This is particularly stark in asset-intensive industries such as car manufacturing, where capital 
costs have an enormous effect on the business but are omitted in EBITDA. 
Time Value of Money 
The time value of money is an essential concept in understanding the idea of cash as an asset and evaluating 
potentially profitable investments. It is also intertwined with the idea of opportunity cost.  
  
The time value of money is the idea that money in the present can increase in value in the future when it is 
invested into some sort of asset. As a result of this, most investors prefer to receive money in the present than 
in the future, as it gives you greater time, and hence a better opportunity to invest that money. Let’s look at a 
common example to illustrate this. 
  
Say you have the choice between receiving £100 now, or £130 in three years’ time. The average person would 
choose the £130 in three years, as they see this as more money than the £100. However, the savvy investor 
should choose the £100 now, as they have three years to invest that money. Say they invest that £100 into an 
asset that returns 10% of their capital a year (not as unreasonable as you may think), then after three years 
their £100 would be worth = £133.10. Which is £3.10 more than the £130. This is the time value of money. 
  
We can see from the example above that calculating the time value of money must include the initial amount of 
money to be invested, the rate of return over a fixed time period and the total time period over which the 
money is being invested. We see the future value of money can be calculated as such 
 
𝐹𝑉 = 𝑃𝑉 × (1+𝑖)𝑛 
  
Where FV = future value, PV = present value, i = interest / return rate per year and n = number of years. So, 
using this formula in our example 
 
𝐹𝑉 = £100 × (1+0.1)3 = £133.10 
  
This is also illustrating the powerful effect of compound interest, where the interest / return gained after each 
period is increased, as the total capital being invested is now greater than in the period before. For example, 
£100 is invested so  
  
After year 1, the total value of money is now £100×1.1 = £110 = £100 + £10. 
After year 2, £110 is invested, so the total value of money is now £110 ×1.1=£121=£110+£11. 
After year 3, £121 is invested, so the total value of money is now £121×1.1=£133.10=£121+£12.10. 
  
So, we can see the interest payments increase each year.

Discounted Cash Flow 
Further developing the idea of the time value of money, discounted cash flow analysis helps determine the 
current value of an asset based on its future cash flows.  
  
Just like how we can use the time value of money to determine the value of money in the future given present 
value, we can also work backwards and determine the present value of money depending on the future value 
and the rate of return. 
Suppose I wish to buy some property for my rental business, and based on some analysis this particular 
building will be worth £100,000 next year. I am also hoping to get a 5% return on my investment in this 
property, which can be based on forecasted growth rates in the property market. In order to determine what I 
should pay for this property I do the following calculation

This is the key concept to DCF analysis.  
  
Now let’s look at a more complex example. Say John is looking to purchase 20% of ABC shares. By looking at 
its cash flow statement you see that it generated £1 million in cash flow, so 20% of this would cost £200,000.

after one year its cash flow will be  £200,000×1.1=£220,000.After 2 years it will be £220,000×1.1=
£242,000 and so on. 
  
Now suppose you wish to obtain a 15% compound return on your investment for five years of owning 20% of 
ABC. That is for each future year of owning ABC you wish to earn a 15% return on that year. To determine 
how much you should pay now for that return, you do what we showed in the example, so for the first year you 
should pay  and for the second year you should pay

Free Cash Flow 
There are two types of free cash flow: levered and unlevered. Both these types of cashflow appear on the 
balance sheet but have a slight difference. Unlevered free cash flow, also known as free cash flow to the firm 
(FCFF), is cash flow before any interest payments on debt obligations. Levered free cash flow also known as 
free cash flow to equity (FCFE) and is calculated after a company meets its debt obligations. The company can 
these use this money to pay dividends to shareholders, buy back stock, or invest in new business projects. A 
company that uses a lot of debt to finance its operations will have large amounts of unlevered free cash flow, 
therefore it must be compared to levered free cash flow to see how much free cash flow is being used to pay 
interest expenses. For a DCF, unlevered free cash flow is used as it can be very difficult to predict a company’s 
future debt. From this point onward when free cash flow is mentioned assumed this is unlevered free cash flow. 
 
Free cashflow is simply the cash available to a company to pay dividends or to repay creditors.  
Free cashflow cab be calculated by taking net income and subtracting investments made to maintain the 
company’s assets and production: 
 
𝐹𝑟𝑒𝑒 𝐶𝑎𝑠ℎ 𝐹𝑙𝑜𝑤=𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒+𝑁𝑜𝑛 𝐶𝑎𝑠ℎ 𝐸𝑥𝑝𝑒𝑛𝑠𝑒𝑠−𝐶ℎ𝑎𝑛𝑔𝑒 𝑖𝑛 𝑁𝑊𝐶−𝐶𝑎𝑝𝑖𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝐸𝑥𝑝𝑒𝑛𝑑𝑖𝑡𝑢𝑟𝑒 
 
𝐹𝑟𝑒𝑒 𝐶𝑎𝑠ℎ 𝐹𝑙𝑜𝑤
=𝐸𝐵𝐼𝑇∗(1−𝑡𝑎𝑥 𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑒)+𝐷𝑒𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 𝑎𝑛𝑑 𝐴𝑚𝑜𝑟𝑡𝑖𝑧𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛−𝐶ℎ𝑎𝑛𝑔𝑒 𝑖𝑛 𝑁𝑊𝐶
−𝐶𝑎𝑝𝑖𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝐸𝑥𝑝𝑒𝑛𝑑𝑖𝑡𝑢𝑟𝑒 
 
 
Net Working Capital 
Net working capital is the difference between the company’s current short-term assets and its current short-term 
liabilities. This can be calculated by taking current assets from the balance sheet and subtracting current 
liabilities also on the balance sheet. However, to make this more accurate we will take some items from the cash 
flow statement too: 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
37 
 
𝐶ℎ𝑎𝑛𝑔𝑒 𝑖𝑛 𝑁𝑊𝐶=𝐶ℎ𝑎𝑛𝑔𝑒 𝑖𝑛 𝐴𝑐𝑐𝑜𝑢𝑛𝑡𝑠 𝑅𝑒𝑐𝑒𝑖𝑣𝑎𝑏𝑙𝑒𝑠+𝐶ℎ𝑎𝑛𝑔𝑒 𝑖𝑛 𝐼𝑛𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑜𝑟𝑦−𝐶ℎ𝑎𝑛𝑔𝑒 𝑖𝑛 𝐴𝑐𝑐𝑜𝑢𝑛𝑡𝑠 𝑃𝑎𝑦𝑎𝑏𝑙𝑒 
Capital Expenditure 
Capital expenditure is funds used by the company to acquire, upgrade, and maintain physical assets. This can 
often be found on the company’s cashflow statement but if it cannot be found we can also calculate it by the 
change in PP&E (Property, Plant, & Equipment) and subtracting depreciation during the current period 
 
𝐶𝑎𝑝𝐸𝑥=𝑃𝑃𝐸(𝑐𝑢𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝑦𝑒𝑎𝑟)−𝑃𝑃𝐸 (𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑣𝑖𝑜𝑢𝑠 𝑦𝑒𝑎𝑟)−𝐷𝑒𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 
 
By using the equation outlined above we have free cash flow which is the first step of our discounted cash flow 
model. Free cash flow is then projected over the forecasting period using various assumptions we make as 
analysts. In this lecture we will not cover how exactly this free cash flow is projected, but we will assume that we 
have projected free cash flow over the forecast period. The exact methods on how to project free cash flow will 
be covered in the next lecture. 
 
Terminal Value 
The next step in our discounted cash flow model is to calculate the terminal value of the company at hand. The 
terminal value is simply the value of the company beyond the forecasted period when future cash flows can be 
estimated. Since you can’t keep forecasting cash flows forever, at some point an assumption needs to be made 
about the cash flows beyond the final explicit forecast year by estimating a lump sum value of the business past 
this period. This lump sum is called the terminal value (TV) and can be calculated in 2 main ways. 
Perpetuity Growth Rate 
We assume that cash flows will grow at a stable rate forever starting at some point in the future (final year of 
projection). The terminal value is therefore calculated by dividing the last cash flow forecast by the difference 
between the discount rate and terminal growth rate: 
 
𝑇𝑉=𝐹𝐶𝐹𝑛 ×(1+𝑔)
𝑑−𝑔  
 
In this formula 𝐹𝐶𝐹𝑛 represents the free cash flow for the last forecast period, g represents the perpetuity growth 
rate and d represents the discount rate. The perpetuity growth rate is a constant growth rate forecasted for the 
company after the final free cash flow period. In general, it is best to use the 10-year US government bond yield 
(also the Risk-Free Rate) and is currently at 3.6%. You can also take into account historical inflation (2-3%) and 
historical GDP growth rate (4-5%) to sense check the value you use. If you assume a perpetuity growth rate 
higher than 5%, you are saying that you expect the company’s growth rate to outpace the economy’s growth 
rate. Which, after a long period of time, ends up as the company being larger than the world economy! In regard 
to, 𝑑, the discount rate, we will cover what this means and how to calculate it below. 
Exit Multiple  
The exit multiple is another method in which you can calculate the terminal value of a company. This method 
assumes that the business will be valued at the end of the projection period based on public market valuations. 
It is typically calculated by applying an appropriate multiple (generally EV/EBITDA, EV/EBIT etc) to the relevant 
statistic projected for the last projected year. This specific multiple is generally found using comparable company 
analysis and multiplied by the projected statistic to arrive at your enterprise value at the end of the forecasting.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
38 
The equation to calculate terminal value using an exit multiple is shown below: 
 
𝑇𝑉=𝐿𝑎𝑠𝑡 12 𝑀𝑢𝑙𝑡𝑖𝑝𝑙𝑒 ×𝐸𝑥𝑖𝑡 𝑀𝑢𝑙𝑡𝑖𝑝𝑙𝑒 
 
A more specific equation for TV using EBITDA may look like  
 
𝑇𝑉=𝐹𝑖𝑛𝑎𝑙 𝑌𝑒𝑎𝑟 𝐸𝐵𝐼𝑇𝐷𝐴 ×𝐸𝑉/𝐸𝐵𝐼𝑇𝐷𝐴 𝑀𝑢𝑙𝑡𝑖𝑝𝑙𝑒 
 
The exit multiple does use relative valuation in an inherently absolute valuation technique but for industries well 
covered by analysts it can yield a more reliable terminal value when compared to the perpetuity growth method. 
The assumptions when using an exit multiple can generally be easier to defend and better explained but this is 
reliant on the fact that data for the exit multiple is available. 
Discount Rate 
So now that we have our projected free cash flow and our terminal value at the end of the forecasting period, 
we now need to come up with a rate to discount all these future cash flows to the present. The discount rate 
represents the expected return on an investment based on its risk profile (meaning that the discount rate is a 
function of how risky the cash flow is). It can also be considered as the minimum threshold return of an 
investment based on comparable investments with similar risks. A higher discount rate makes a company’s 
cashflows less valuable, as it implies the investment carries a greater amount of risk.  In order to calculate this 
rate, we used something called the weighted average cost of capital, or WACC for short. 
Benefits of DCF 
Discounted Cash Flow is a really useful analysis tool, as it ignores all market perceptions about the value of a 
stock and instead tries to predict the future value of a company based on objective data. It is also really good 
for estimating the long-term returns of an investment. 
  
Disadvantages of DCF 
DCF can be very inaccurate if used in isolation, just like all other analysis tools. This is mainly because DCF 
might not take into account the state of the economy or in particular the sector in which the company operates 
in.   
 
Gordon Growth Model 
The Gordon Growth Model (GGM) is similar to DCF analysis but instead uses the dividends per share issued 
by the company. The GGM assumes that a company’s shares are worth the sum of all its future dividends 
discounted back to their current value. This is not too far fetched, because a company can only issue 
dividends if it has surplus profit, hence a company can only issue greater dividends if its profits increase, likely 
leading to an increase in share price. It also assumes a constant rate of growth for dividends per share that are 
issued. 
  
You can find the dividends issued by a company on the cash flow statement and hence the dividends per 
share by dividing this value by the current number of shares outstanding. Suppose the dividends per share of 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
39 
ABC is predicted to grow at a constant rate of 5% per year, however you want a return of 10% a year when 
investing in this company.  
  
So, to find the intrinsic value of one share suppose the share is currently trading at £180 with a dividend per 
share of £10. If I purchase the share now then I will expect in a year to receive a dividend payout of 
£10×1.05 = £10.50. This is because I expect the dividends per share to increase by 5%, but I then have to 
discount this by my discount rate to find what the present value of this dividend payment is, similar to the 
discounted cash flow. So the present value of £10.50 in a year is £10.50
1.1=£9.54. This is what I should pay now 
for my dividend payment in a year’s time.  
 
Now after two years the dividend payment will increase to £10×1.052 = £11.025, and to discount this to 
present day value according to the rate of return I want, I get £11.025
1.12 =£9.11. 
 
So in return for 2 years worth of dividend payments I should pay now  
 
£10×1.05
1.1 + £10×1.052
1.12 = £9.54 + £9.11 = £18.65 
 
Some of the more mathematically inclined reading this would have noticed this is a geometric series, but more 
importantly that this is a geometric series that tends to a limit, as each successive addition becomes smaller. 
 
Now suppose we are going to own ABC shares for an infinite amount of time, this means we are going to 
receive an infinite amount of dividend payments. We can find what the present value of these dividend 
payments are by calculating the following series 
 
PV = £10×1.05
1.1  +£10×1.052
1.12  + £10×1.053
1.13  + … 
                     = 
£10×1.05
1.1
1− 1.05
1.1
 (according to the sum to infinity formula) 
                     = £190.91 
 
Now, going back to our assumption that the sum of all future dividend payments discount to the current share 
value, then the intrinsic value of ABC is £190.91 per share, which is greater than the current market price of 
£180 per share. There the GGM suggests ABC is undervalued and could be a good investment.  
So is the Gordon Growth Model actually useful? 
The great thing about the GGM is that compared to a lot of other financial models it is not very complicated 
and is relatively easy to understand and apply to many different businesses. It is pretty useful when applied to 
more mature businesses who are likely to issue dividends and increase the amount of dividend they issue as 
well.  
 
However, there can be some pretty serious issues when using the GGM and the most obvious of these is that 
it cannot be used on companies that don’t issue any dividends, making it completely useless for growth stocks.  
 
Also, the assumptions made when using it can be pretty detrimental, one it assumes constant dividend growth, 
and the accuracy of this dividend growth depends heavily on the analysis used to reach this figure.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
40 
 
Another issue is if the rate of return required by the investor is not greater than the expected growth rate of the 
dividends, then the common ratio is greater than 1, meaning our series of dividend payouts will tend to infinity, 
giving the company an infinite value. In practice, this is meaningless and so we cannot use the GGM in this 
case. 
Economic Cycle 
The economic cycle is the theory that all economies around the world go through cyclical periods in terms of 
their growth rate. The economic cycle can be split up into four stages: expansion, slowdown, contraction and 
recovery. This is important in the world of equities, as the value of equities can broadly follow the growth rate 
of an economy. 
 
 
 
There are different ways of measuring an economy’s growth with the most popular being GDP (Gross 
Domestic Product). This is a measure of the total value of goods and services produced by an economy over a 
fixed period of time. Many factors affect GDP such as interest rates, inflation, unemployment rate and much 
more. However, depending on what stage in the economic cycle we are can sometimes determine what 
sectors are likely to perform well. During periods of high economic growth, sectors such as tech tend to 
perform strongly as these companies are usually leading economic growth due to their high innovation. During 
recessions or periods of economic decline, sectors such as water and electricity usually perform better as 
demand for more risky stocks decreases and investors move into more stable companies.  
  
We could also look at seasonal cycles, where months such as September and January are historically the 
worst performing for stocks, but the run-up to Christmas seems to be a particularly profitable time to enter the 
stock market. 
How to make money from equities 
There are two main ways to make money from equities. This is not accounting for all the different strategies an 
investor or trader might employ depending on the company, its sector or the period we are in in the economic 
cycle. Instead, this looks at what the investor is looking for in terms of return from his/her investment.  
Growth stocks 
Growth stocks are stocks that are predicted to experience high levels of growth in its share price. This is the 
standard way you would invest in any asset. Buy the shares now in the expectation that in the future you can 
sell it for more than you bought it for. 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
41 
  
There are particular stocks which tend to have a higher growth rate than other sectors. In more recent years 
these have been healthcare and tech stocks for example. These stocks usually come with a higher risk of the 
company not performing as expected, but this is balanced with a greater potential return on your investment. 
Your investment could be based on some of the ratios and investment models we have previously discussed.  
Dividend stocks 
Dividend stocks are a form of fixed income investment where you receive income at regular time intervals. A 
dividend is a distribution of profits generated by a company to its shareholders, hence if you hold shares in a 
company and they decide to issue shares, then you are entitled to those profits. Where younger companies 
might be more interested in investing any surplus profits back into the company to stimulate growth, more 
mature companies tend to distribute those profits in the form of dividends to maintain demand for their stock. 
These are usually distributed at the end of every quarter. 
  
Dividend yield is calculated by dividing the dividend received per share divided by the price of one share. It 
can be interpreted as the return in dividends for every £1 invested in the company. For example, say ABC 
distributes £0.50 per share every quarter, so over a year if you owned one share you would receive £2 in 
dividends (dividend yield is usually calculated over a year). Suppose the current share price is £20, then the 
dividend yield for the past year would be £2
£20=0.1 or 10%. So, for every £1 invested in ABC you would receive 
£1×0.1=£0.10 in dividends per year.  
  
Dividend stocks are usually seen as less risky as growth stocks due to the more regular periods of income, 
and hence are likely to produce less return. One important thing to note about dividend stocks is a dividend 
yield that is too high could be a bad thing, as this could lead to depreciation in the share price. Therefore, the 
amount of dividend received could be cancelled out by the drop in value of the stock. 
Funds 
A fund is a collection of different stocks in a portfolio. Different types of funds can suit different types of 
investors in terms of their risk tolerance and what they expect in terms of return, as well as their personal 
interests. Funds don’t have to only contain stocks, but can also contain other assets such as cryptocurrencies, 
bonds and commodities. 
 
The usual benefit of a fund is that it diversifies your portfolio across different sectors, geographical areas or 
perhaps even assets, spreading your risk and diversifying your portfolio compared to just investing in one 
stock. 
Actively-Managed Funds 
Actively-managed funds are managed by a fund manager. The fund manager is responsible for choosing the 
allocation of different securities within the overall fund. A fund might have a certain profile or objective that the 
fund manager is trying to obtain. The fund might be specialising in aviation stock across the world, or emerging 
markets in Europe. It could even just focus on real estate in Brazil. It is up to the fund manager and up to the 
investor whether this fund is in alignment with what you want to invest in. 
 
Actively-managed funds typically have higher costs associated with them as you are paying for the fund 
manager to alter the funds allocations based on market conditions and other factors. Not only are you betting 
on the fund, but also the manager to make the correct decisions to essentially ‘beat the market’. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
42 
Passive/Index Funds 
Passive funds are the opposite to actively-managed funds. Rather than having a fund manager alter the 
allocations of the fund, passive funds usually track some sort of stock market index.  
One of the most popular indexes is the S&P 500 which tracks the 500 largest funds in the USA based on 
market capitalisation. Another is the FTSE 100 which are the 100 largest companies in the UK again based on 
market capitalisation. The purpose of index funds is to essentially track a certain market, trying to match its 
returns. For this reason, index funds are seen as a lower risk option compared to actively-managed funds.  
  
Passive/Index funds also tend to have lower costs as it is essentially just an algorithm tracking an index, hence 
there is no need to have a fund manager. Index funds can also be quite diverse, ranging from different 
geographic areas to different sectors. 
Exchange-traded funds 
An exchange-traded-fund (ETF) can be an active or passive fund. What defines an ETF is how it is traded. 
ETFs are like any other stock and are listed on the stock market. Hence, they can be traded at any time during 
trading hours and hence their price fluctuates throughout the day. 
 
You can have ETFs that are either index funds or actively-managed funds, however it is more common to have 
them as index funds as the value of the index changes throughout the day and the lower costs associated with 
ETFs. 
Mutual funds 
These are the opposite of ETFs. Typically, index mutual funds can only be traded at certain points in a day, so 
whilst you might initiate a trade at some point during the day, it will only be executed at a specific time, usually 
based on the day’s closing price. Mutual funds are controlled by a broker or an investment company, and it is 
here that you trade mutual funds. 
  
You can also have mutual funds as either index or actively-managed funds. However, it is more likely to have 
them as actively-managed funds as the investment company pools funds from many individual investors to 
create the fund, giving the fund manager greater control.

documents.text:
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
2 
 
 
 
Table of Contents 
 
Macroeconomics 3 
Introduction 3 
Microeconomics 3 
Types of Economies 3 
Supply and Demand 5 
Market Equilibrium 9 
Macroeconomics 10 
Aggregate Demand (AD) 10 
Aggregate Supply (AS) 11 
Fiscal and Monetary Policy 15 
Economic Indicators 19 
Currencies 21 
Foreign Exchange 23 
Foreign EXchange Basics 23 
Characteristics of the FX Markets 24 
Exchange rates 25 
Currency rankings 27 
Margin, Carry, and Trade Mechanics 29 
Types of FX markets 32 
Spot exchange markets 32 
Forward exchange markets 33 
Pricing FX Forwards 34 
Exchange Rate Systems 36 
Floating Exchange Rate Systems 36 
The issue of floating exchange rate mechanisms 39 
FX Rate Drivers 40 
Purchasing power parity 40 
Other types of exchange rate systems 41 
Fixed exchange rate systems 41 
Managed exchange rate systems 43 
Fact Sheet 47 
Reading List 52 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
3 
 
 
Macroeconomics 
Notes by Himanish Joshi and Olasoji Ajayi 
 
Introduction 
The field of economics is divided into 2 sections, namely microeconomics and macroeconomics. 
 
Microeconomics considers economics at an individual and business level, looking at decisions made that 
affect markets for singular products or services. Its areas of focus include resource allocation, price 
mechanisms, market failure and government intervention. 
 
Macroeconomics however considers the economy at a country and international level, looking at policy 
decisions made by governments and central banks. Typical topics involve: inflation, interest rates, economic 
growth and employment. 
 
Overall, microeconomics takes a “bottom-up” approach to analysing the economy whilst macroeconomics 
takes a “top-down” approach. Both areas are incredibly important in the field of finance, from private equity to 
global markets, from accounting to asset-management. In many ways, economics underpins many financial 
practices. To understand the rationale behind macroeconomics we will introduce some basic microeconomic 
theory such as supply and demand. 
 
Microeconomics 
 
Types of Economies 
 
Fundamental concepts 
Before we look at different types of economy, we must first introduce some very fundamental concepts. They 
are so fundamental in fact it can be summarised as the “Fundamental Economic Problem”. This is the idea that 
everyone has unlimited wants but only limited resources which is called scarcity. Unlimited wants refer to there 
being theoretically no end to the quantity or quality of goods that people would want to consume. However, 
there is only a finite supply of resources (raw materials) to fulfil those wants. This leads to choices. Decisions 
must be made: What goods should be produced? How should they be produced? How many should be 
produced? Who should consume them? Which allocation of resources is the most efficient? These decisions 
are economic decisions. 
 
Another key concept closely related to scarcity is that of opportunity cost. Technically this is “the value of the 
next best alternative foregone”. Essentially this means the value of a choice that is not taken due to another 
choice being taken instead (a trade-off). Note that often, opportunity cost ≠ financial value. For example, by 
taking time to read these notes your opportunity cost is the time spent on something else you could be doing 
e.g. playing a sport, meeting friends or sleeping! 
 
Now for the big questions: Who makes the decisions? Who determines the opportunity costs? You may think 
about a wide variety of groups: consumers, firms and governments… and you would be right! (but not all the 
time). The answer is: it depends on the type of economy. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
4 
 
 
 
 
 
Free Market 
In a free market economy (sometimes called market economy), supply and demand (market forces) guide the 
allocation of resources. These forces determine the quantity and price of goods and services that are 
produced and consumed. Private businesses make economic decisions with minimal government intervention. 
This of course assumes that the market forces are the best mechanism through which to allocate resources in 
the economy. Theoretically, in a true-free market, there is no government intervention. In practice however, 
there is always some involvement by the government, meaning there are no real-world examples of true 100% 
free-market economies. 
 
So, how do these “market-forces” lead to a theoretically efficient allocation of resources? It’s through price 
discovery, which we will discuss in the next section. Essentially, buyers and sellers through supply and 
demand reach a price for every good/service known as equilibrium. Consumers drive choices through their 
decisions on goods and services – they influence the price set. Also, producers need to find a price by 
balancing their profit with customer affordability. The minimum government intervention allows competition in 
the market, which leads to efficiency. If one producer is charging too much for a product, another firm can enter 
the market and undercut them, driving the equilibrium price in the market lower. The competition is driven by a 
profit motive where businesses aim to engage in practices that increase profits by reducing costs and 
increasing revenues whilst remaining competitive in the market. Knock-on effects of competition include 
increased productivity and innovation. 
 
However, there are drawbacks to free markets. For example, market failures can occur when supply and 
demand create prices that fail to reflect the social cost or social benefit of producing/consuming the product. 
Petrol/Diesel production is often quoted as a “negative externality” market failure. The argument is that the 
price consumers pay in a free market does not account for the cost of damages to the environment etc. and 
leads to a misallocation of resources. This leads to governments having to intervene through imposing fuel 
taxes to alter the price to a more “socially optimal equilibrium”. Another disadvantage of free markets relates to 
risks of the profit motive. To increase their profits, producers may cut costs through unethical means such as 
reduced health and safety for workers or damaging environmental practices. Finally, some economists believe 
that free-market practices lead to inequality. 
 
Command Market 
Completely opposite to the free market is a command economy. This is where resources are owned by the 
state or central government agency, and they decide how to allocate these resources without any market 
forces. The government sets production targets, salaries and growth rates according to its own view of 
people’s wants. Advocates of command economies suggest that the central planning maximises social welfare 
and resources are allocated to reduce inequality and unemployment. This comes at the cost of less 
competition and innovation in the economy. 
 
Most economies are mixed economies i.e. they share characteristics from free-market and command 
economies. Often, they allow market forces to determine the allocation of resources but do have government 
intervention to correct market failures. Some resources are owned by the public sector and some owned by 
the private sector. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
5 
 
 
 
Supply and Demand 
 
Demand 
How much do consumers want a good/service and their willingness to pay a price for them is encompassed in 
the idea of demand. More specifically, demand is the quantity of a good/service that consumers are willing and 
able to buy at a range of given prices during a given time period. Importantly “willing and able”, we are only 
interested in “effective demand” i.e. we only care about consumers who want something but are supported by 
an ability to pay for that thing. If you can’t pay for it, you have no effective demand. Throughout these notes, 
when we refer to demand, it will implicitly mean effective demand. 
 
To analyse markets, supply and demand curves are drawn to better represent market prices and 
characteristics. However prior to drawing these curves we need to develop some theory into why they are 
shaped as such. 
 
The price is what a buyer pays for one unit of a specific good/service. The number of units purchased at this 
price is the quantity demanded. The law of demand describes an inverse relationship between price and 
quantity demanded. For every extra unit demanded by a consumer they will demand a lower per-unit price. 
 
There are 3 main reasons as to why this is the case. 
 
1) Diminishing Marginal Utility: The consumer gains less additional utility from consuming a product the 
more of it is consumed. For every extra unit, a consumer is willing to pay less sue to the less utility 
(personal gain) they receive. 
 
To illustrate this point, consider your demand for a Mars bar - assuming you like chocolate. Suppose 
there was 1 Mars bar and you would pay £1 for it. Now, consider there are 2 Mars bars. It’s likely that 
the first bar will give you the most utility (i.e. pleasure). The second bar will also give you some utility 
but probably less than the first bar. This is due to the diminishing marginal utility. Would you still 
demand the 2 bars at a per-unit price of £1, knowing that the first bar was more “valuable” to you from 
a utility perspective? Rationally speaking, no. For example you may offer £0.8 per unit for the 2 bars. 
Now consider your demand for 10 bars. Its extremely unlikely that the 8th, 9th or 10th bar will give you 
much utility at all. Hence, you might rationally demand £0.2 per unit for the 10 bars. 
 
2) Income Effect: Consumers have fixed budgets to pay for the goods/services they consume. As the 
prices of goods increase, consumers can buy fewer units of the goods with the same income. 
 
3) Substitution Effect: If goods become too expensive, consumers will find alternatives for it. 
 
The aforementioned reasons lead to a “demand schedule” - a table which illustrates the law of demand. From 
this we can plot a demand curve with price on the y-axis and quantity on the x-axis: 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
6 
 
 
 
 
 
   
Demand curves can look different for various goods/services as they may have different gradients and 
curvatures. However, they all exhibit the fundamental property of sloping down from left to right due to the law 
of demand: as price increases , the quantity demanded decreases. We can simplify all demand curves as a 
linearly downward sloping curve. The gradient of the demand curve has an interesting property called 
“elasticity of demand” that will not be explained here but those interested should read into this. N.B. sometimes 
quantity can be referred to as ‘output’. 
  
 
 
A very niche exception to the downward sloping demand curve is the notion of “snob goods” which lead to an 
upward sloping demand curve. These occur when the goods are valued highly purely because their price is 
high and consumers want other people to see that they own these goods. Consider luxury items such as 
jewellery, Rolex watches, yachts etc. This concept of “snob goods” only applies to these luxury goods and will 
be ignored in future references to the concept of demand. 
 
So, demand curves seem useful but their true power is reflected when we consider changes of the curve. 
There are 2 types of changes to consider: movements along the curve and shifts of the curve. 
 
A movement along a demand curve can only be caused by a change in the price. If 
the price decreases from P1 to P2 this is accompanied by a rise in quantity from Q1 
to Q2. This movement is the red arrow and is described as an “extension in 
demand”. Conversely a “contraction in demand” (the purple arrow) shows a situation 
where price has increased, and output has decreased.

A shift of the demand curve is more interesting. It occurs when the quantity 
demanded changes for every price along the curve, which happens when any 
factor other than price changes the demand for a good/service. The green arrow 
shows an outward shift of the curve which is an increase in demand. The red 
arrow shows an inward shift of the curve representing a decrease in demand. 
 
You may be wondering what would cause a shift in demand. Many reasons in fact 
can result in changes in demand. 
 
Firstly, changes in income. If people have more money to spend, they will likely be “willing and able” to 
demand a good/service at higher prices thus resulting in a shift out of demand. An increase in population can 
result in increased demand. Another interesting cause is a change in the price of substitute goods. If the price 
of KitKats is reduced, then by the law of demand consumers will demand more KitKats. Therefore, they have 
less money to spend on Mars bars thus resulting in a shift inward (decrease) in the demand of Mars. The 
following diagrams illustrate this scenario. 
 
You may be wondering how analysing these microeconomic supply and demand diagrams will aid us in 
understanding global macro. As we will see later on, macro diagrams will be very similar and follow the exact 
same principles! 
 
Supply 
Now that you’ve understood the concept of demand, we will look at supply. 
 
The law of demand was largely driven by consumers. Supply, however, is explained by looking at producers 
(i.e. firms). Supply is the quantity of a good/service that firms are willing and able to produce at a range of 
prices in a given period. In free-market economies, firms are driven by a profit motive. They make more profit 
as prices of the goods/services they sell rise. This suggests that firms are willing to supply a greater quantity of 
a good as its price increases thus causing a positive relationship (upward sloping curve) between price and 
quantity supplied. This is known as the law of supply. 
 
Going back to the example of Mars bars. Suppose that the price of the bars increased, well then its rational to 
assume that Mars will want to produce more bars, to take advantage of higher prices and make more profits. 
This may lead to the following Supply schedule and curve: 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
8 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
Supply curves also have different shapes but all exhibit the fundamental property of the law of supply, a 
positive relationship between price and quantity. We can simplify such supply curves as a linear straight line 
with positive gradient. As with demand, the gradient of the curve is called the “elasticity of supply” and has 
properties that relate to the type of good/service. 
 
 Like demand curves, the supply curves can also change through movements along the curve or shifts of the 
whole curve. 
 
A movement along a supply curve can only be caused by a change in the price. If 
price decreases from P1 to P2 (left) this is accompanied by a decrease in quantity 
from Q1 to Q2. This movement is the purple arrow (left) and is described as a 
“contraction in supply”. Conversely, an “extension in supply” (the red arrow, left) 
shows a situation where price has increased, and output has increased. 
 
A shift of the supply curve occurs when the quantity 
supplied changes for every price along the curve, 
which happens when any factor other than price changes the supply for a 
good/service. The green arrow (right) shows an outward shift of the curve which is 
an increase in supply. The red arrow (right) shows an inward shift of the curve 
representing a decrease in supply. 
 
Factors that cause shifts in supply curves are largely related to what determines a 
firm’s profits. Anything that increases a firm’s costs of production will reduce supply as the profit per unit

decreases and firms will be less willing to produce the good/service. For instance, a rise in rents, wages, raw 
material costs etc. will all increase costs of production and lead to a decrease of supply (shift from S to S2). 
Changes in technology can help increase supply e.g. new machinery that makes production lines more 
efficient and reduces production costs per unit. Government policy can also shift supply curves via taxes and 
subsidies. Subsidies given to solar panel manufacturers help to reduce the firm’s costs leading to an outward 
increase in supply (shift from S to S1). On the other hand, taxes on the production of goods lead to reductions 
in supply as they increase costs for firms. For example, carbon taxes on oil production are designed to 
encourage firms to reduce their output. 
 
Market Equilibrium 
Now that we have seen both demand and supply curves, it makes sense to plot them together. By doing so we 
can visually show the market of any good/service. 
 
 
Market equilibrium is defined as the price and quantity at which the quantity demanded by consumers is 
exactly balanced by the quantity that firms wish to supply. 
 
On the graph above, this is shown as the intersection of the supply and demand curves. P* and Q* are the 
symbols used to depict the equilibrium price and quantity. You may be wondering ‘why is it that the market 
equilibrium must be the intersection?’ Well, to explain this, we can consider what happens when the market is 
not at equilibrium. 
 
Assume this is the market for Mars bars as shown earlier. If we were operating at PH (£0.8 per unit) then the 
quantity demanded by consumers is 2 units. However, the suppliers are willing to supply 9 units. There are 7 
units of excess supply in the market which is an inefficiency. The supplier will be faced with depreciation costs 
and cannot make any profit from the extra 7 units they hold. Market forces, supply and demand, dictate that 
the price must be lowered to reach a more optimum output for both consumers and firms. 
 
Conversely, if the market operated at PL (£0.12 per unit) then the quantity demanded is 9 units, but the quantity 
supplied is only 2 units. This time, we have excess demand of 2 units. There is once again an inefficiency in 
the market that can be corrected by raising price and output, enabling more consumers to consume the goods 
and firms to make more profits via higher prices. This convergence of price and quantity to the equilibrium is 
the price mechanism (“market forces”) by which free-markets aim to operate efficiently. Note that for this 
example, it turns out that P* is around £0.30 and Q* is between 5 and 6 units. 
 
Let’s now transition from the microeconomics world to the macroeconomics world.
