Macroeconomics 
 
Aggregate Demand (AD) 
Aggregate Demand (AD) is the total amount of spending on goods and services produced in an economy 
during a period of time. It can be thought of as Gross Domestic Product (GDP) which is a measure of the 
market value of all the final goods and services produced and sold by a country in a specific period. 
 
In microeconomics we considered the demand for one good/service. Here we are just scaling the concept up: 
instead of one good/service, we consider all the goods/services in the economy. Once again, the law of 
demand applies, and the AD curve is shaped exactly like an ordinary demand curve. This time the x-axis 
represents total output, and we use the symbol Y. Total output is also correlated with the level of employment 
in the economy. The y-axis represents price-level and loosely speaking “inflation” and we use the symbol P. 
 
AD can be broken down into separate components: consumption, investment, 
government spending and net trade. We can express this conveniently as: 
 
AD = C + I + G + (X-M) 
 
where X represents exports and M represents imports. A change in any one of 
these components will result in a shift of the AD curve either outwards (increasing 
AD) or inwards (decreasing AD). 
 
The largest component of AD is Consumption, C (which typically makes up 
around 70% of total AD). Consumption largely depends on disposable income. 
Higher levels of disposable income led to greater consumption as individuals 
can afford to buy higher quantities of goods/services at higher prices. This 
would lead to a shift out of AD to AD 1. 
 
Another important factor affecting consumption is interest rates. In 
macroeconomics, interest rates refer to the cost of borrowing i.e. how 
expensive it is to take out a loan or a mortgage. It also refers to saving i.e. how 
high are the rewards for saving money? Interest rates are managed by central 
banks and are crucial to managing the economy and will be explored later when we discuss monetary policy. 
 
Theoretically, interest rates can be thought of as the opportunity cost of holding money. If you store money 
under your bed (which you really shouldn’t), you are forgiving the interest rate of the return on any other asset, 
noting that return can be negative or positive. Anyway, the important thing to note here is that interest rates 
should be thought of as the cost of borrowing. So, high interest rates would mean a higher cost of borrowing, 
meaning that credit becomes expensive and saving more attractive leading to a decrease in consumption and 
a decrease in AD to AD 2. 
 
Investment refers to capital expenditure undertaken by firms to add to their capital inventory (machinery, 
factories or new technologies). Government spending largely depends on the condition of the economy 
(business cycle). During times of recession e.g. during the Covid lockdowns, the government was forced to 
increase its welfare spending on unemployment benefits (e.g. furlough), grants and bailouts. In addition to 
welfare spending, there is public sector service spending e.g. increase in funds to the health service. These 
types of spending programs by the government aren’t entirely funded by taxes. Often, it would involve 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
11 
 
 
 
monetary policy such as quantitative easing (which we’ll explain later). Finally, net trade affects AD. This 
depends on numerous factors including exchange rates, the state of the world economy, degrees of 
protectionism (tariffs etc.) and consumer demand. 
 
Aggregate Supply (AS) 
We can extrapolate the supply we looked at earlier to encompass every good/service in the economy and we 
would get Aggregate Supply (AS). Remember that supply is looked at from a firm perspective NOT the 
consumer. So, AS is the total value of output that firms are willing and able to supply at a given point in time. 
 
Unlike AD, there are 2 types of AS curves: SRAS (Short Run Aggregate Supply) and LRAS (Long Run 
Aggregate Supply). This is because the ability of firms to change production levels differs when we consider 
different timeframes. 
 
Suppose you own a car firm. In the short-run, your ability to change the number of cars you produce would 
largely depend on the costs of production. If energy prices increased overnight, it is likely your supply may 
slightly decrease. On the other hand, if energy prices went down it is unlikely that you would be able to 
increase your production by a meaningful quantity. Hence for SRAS, the ability for firms to vary output is 
limited and SRAS can only shift due to a change in cost of production. Suppose energy prices went down and 
you used that excess capital to invest in a new technology. Over the long run, this may lead to your factory 
being more efficient and the quantity of cars you produce would increase noticeably. Hence for LRAS, the 
ability of firms to vary output is flexible. This difference leads to 2 differently shaped AS curves that both still 
follow the law of supply. 
 
Interestingly, there are multiple types of LRAS curves and the interested reader should Google about Classical 
and Keynesian views of AS. As macroeconomic policy is largely associated with the long-run, we will mainly 
be considering the LRAS. Once again, the y-axis represents the price level and the x-axis represents total 
output/employment.  
Shifts in LRAS are caused by anything that increases the quality or quantity of resources used to produce 
goods/services. Technological advances can increase productivity of supply chains and help decrease the cost 
per unit whilst also attracting new firms into the market. Education and skills improvements can make workers 
more efficient and increase the quality of the labour force thus enabling more goods/services to be produced 
and increasing the AS in the economy. Finally (and perhaps most importantly), changes in government policy. 
This can include fiscal policy such as taxes and welfare spending or monetary policy such as interest rates and 
financial market interventions. 
 
To help explain how price level (inflation), output and employment are all related we will consider a series of 
situations. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
12 
 
 
 
 
  
Here we are representing changing macroeconomic environments through increasing AD curves. FE denotes 
full employment and it is the maximum possible output of the economy if all resources are used and the LRAS 
is constant. 
 
Consider AD1 first. In this situation, the equilibrium point creates an output of Y1 and price level of P1. We can 
see that we are operating far from full employment. This means there is a lot of spare capacity in the economy. 
When I mention employment, I refer to the fact that more resources can be used, more people can be hired 
and more goods can be produced – NOT just employment in terms of people and job opportunities. 
 
As we shift to AD2 we move closer to FE and some of the unused spare capacity has been used up, which has 
enabled an increase in output and in employment. At the same time, we see the price level of the economy 
increasing from P1 to P2. Why is that? Well those extra employees need to be paid and to gather the extra 
resources costs money. Overall, costs of production for firms have increased. This is inflation. The increase in 
price however is minimal and this is overall healthy for the economy. 
 
Now suppose AD continues to shift to AD 3. Now we are practically at full employment and there is no spare 
capacity left. The cost of acquiring additional resources has gone up exponentially. As there is so little extra 
resource, firms must pay much more to acquire it. This translates to higher costs of production and much 
greater prices in the economy. This has led to very high inflation as price levels have risen from P2 to P3. Now 
that we have reached peak output of the economy you may be wondering how is it possible that economies 
continue growing? Well this is achieved through increasing (shifting out) the LRAS (notice that in this example, 
we assumed LRAS was fixed). We have seen earlier that very high inflation can be disastrous for the economy 
and in many ways the point P3 is indicative of global economies today. Government intervention is required, 
and this will be discussed in the next section. 
 
Inflation 
If you were not sleeping under a rock, you may have noticed that the price of almost everything has increased. 
Food, fuel, transportation, entertainment and rent have all significantly risen in price. Undoubtedly, you must 
have felt that a pound today does not stretch as far as a few years ago. This is inflation – the sustained rise in 
general price level over time. 
 
Very high rates of inflation do indeed “make everyone poorer” as the cost-of-living increases and the 
purchasing power of money decreases. Another negative consequence of high inflation is that it disrupts 
business planning by discouraging capital investment and degrading business confidence. One of the most 
important consequences of inflation and what is extremely topical is the concept of a wage-price spiral. As 
inflation rises (currently between 8-10% since August in the UK), workers demand higher wages. For example, 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
13 
 
 
 
transport staff have been striking in demand for higher wages. If employers do indeed decide to increase 
wages, this can lead to inflation spiralling out of control as higher incomes will further increase consumption 
and demand in the economy putting further pressure on prices (a shift out of AD) 
 
However, some inflation is a good thing. To illustrate this, we can consider the opposite of inflation: deflation, 
which is a decrease in price level over time. The most notable example of this is Japan, which has had 
decades of extremely low or negative rates of inflation. If prices of goods/services were continuously 
decreasing, you may think this is good right? Everything is getting cheaper! But this is disastrous for the 
economy, as you as a consumer are likely to delay your spending choices. 
 
For example, that TV you were thinking of buying. Well… why not wait a couple of months and you could get it 
at a lower price? This can be extrapolated to firms wanting to make investment decisions e.g. building new 
factories or researching new technologies. Delayed spending decisions decrease the aggregate demand in the 
economy and reduce overall output (i.e. GDP). Firms are less likely to invest into new technologies and the 
economy stagnates. Overall, low and stable inflation is a positive for the economy. In the UK, the target 
inflation rate set by the government is 2% and it is managed by the Bank of England. Low and stable inflation 
is often a consequence of sustainable growth and a healthy macroeconomic environment. 
 
The most quoted measure of inflation is the % change in CPI (Consumer Price Index). CPI measures price 
levels in the economy by considering the price changes of a basket of goods/services. What goods are in the 
basket are determined by a national agency, in the UK this is the ONS (Office for National Statistics). A slightly 
more detailed explanation of the calculation is below: 
1) A base year is chosen where the average price of the basket is measured, and a corresponding index 
value is set to 100. 
2) Each subsequent quarter or year, the changes of prices of goods in the basket is determined and a 
new index value is calculated. We can estimate inflation by looking at the % changes of the index value 
from the base value of 100. 
3) The goods/services in the basket are weighted to reflect the relative importance of that good to a 
typical household. Around 700 goods are used to calculate the CPI. 
 
You may be wondering that it is subjective which goods should be in the basket and how they should be 
weighted. You are correct! One of the limitations of the CPI is that it is difficult to define typical consumption 
(since different households can have drastically different spending patterns). Another drawback of CPI is that it 
doesn’t include mortgage interest payments (the biggest monthly expenditures of many households) and that 
the CPI is slow to react to new inclusions in the basket. 
 
To resolve this, there is another measure of price-levels called RPI (Retail Price Index) which does include 
mortgage payments and is based on a different basket of goods. Furthermore, the RPI covers a smaller range 
of households (e.g. it excludes pensioners/high income earners), meaning it aims to target the average 
household. Having said this, the CPI is a more useful indicator to measure policy effectiveness due to its tried 
and tested calculation methods and its worldwide adoption making it helpful in performing international 
comparisons. 
 
A very important extension of CPI is Core CPI. The normal CPI reading (often called ‘headline inflation’) 
includes all parts of the economy which experience inflation and is not adjusted to remove volatile readings 
that are not caused by changes in economic conditions i.e. food and energy prices which can exhibit extensive 
volatility due to climate variances, political turmoil, natural disasters etc. Core CPI removes these volatile 
components (food and energy) from the basket of goods/services. This is deemed as a better measure of 
economic conditions and is used by central banks as a barometer for policy decision-making.

The graph above shows CPI inflation (blue) and Core CPI inflation (red) in the US over the past 20 years. We 
can see that the headline reading (which includes food and energy) is much more volatile than the core 
reading. Other key things to note include the huge fall in inflation during and after the global financial crisis 
from 2007-2009. So much so that inflation even went negative (i.e. prices were falling!), but as discussed this 
is a bad thing. 
 
We can use the aggregate supply and demand diagrams to illustrate the causes of inflation. The first type is 
demand-pull inflation where AD has shifted out raising prices and output of the economy. 
 
Demand-pull inflation 
 
 
In the above diagram, we can see that AD has shifted outward (increased) from AD 1 to AD2. Remember that 
AD shifts when anyone of its components in the equation C+I+G+(X-M) changes. The diagram above is a 
good example of the recovery after the Covid lockdowns. When restaurants, entertainment and travel opened 
again, there were big increases in consumer spending (consumption) for these goods/services. Furthermore, 
as consumption is the largest component of AD, it is likely that there would be a significant increase in AD. At 
the same time when we had an increase in consumption, we also had increases in government spending. The 
government was forced to increase welfare payments (through furloughs, unemployment benefits etc.) to 
ensure people could survive despite not going to work. This excessive government spending creates more 
credit (i.e. “money”) in the economy and further increases AD. 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
15 
 
 
 
Cost-push inflation 
The second cause of inflation is called cost-push inflation. Here, the root cause is on the supply-side and more 
specifically, decreasing supply. This is often due to rising costs of production. This might be indicative of the 
current situation where the war in Eastern Europe has led to surging commodity prices and raw material costs 
for firms. As these costs increase, firms are restricted in the quantity of goods/service they can provide and 
hence AS decreases. Rising energy prices are NOT the only reason that AS has been decreasing. Supply side 
problems are present across the world due to many reasons such as lockdowns in Asia, the inability of firms to 
find workers in the transportation/shipping industry in the US and rising interest rates. 
 
You may be wondering ‘aren’t we currently experiencing scenario one (demand-pull) and scenario two 
(cost-push) inflation?’ Yes, we are! We can plot both these on the same diagram to truly understand the 
current macroeconomic environment. 
 
 
Combining demand-pull and cost-push inflation 
 
 
The above diagram is a very good representation of many global economies. The important things to note are 
an increase in AD and a decrease in AS. This has caused a huge increase in prices as reflected by globally 
high inflation levels with a moderate increase in output and employment. This makes sense as the economies 
are recovering from the pandemic where GDP and employment fell tremendously. Currently the big problem is 
high inflation. To tackle this, we will look at government policy. 
 
Fiscal and Monetary Policy 
We have seen that AD and LRAS curves can be used to effectively portray macroeconomic environments. The 
market forces on their own dictate several important economic factors such as: GDP, employment and 
inflation. These factors in turn have huge knock-on effects to our everyday lives via the quantity/quality of 
goods we can consume, cost of living, quality of life and household incomes. If left to their own devices, market 
forces can sometimes create situations that are unfavourable to the economy (think high inflation or low 
employment etc.). So, how can economies artificially “control” AD and LRAS to ensure that situations like 
these (market failures) are minimised? The answer is Government Policy. More specifically, a government can 
intervene to influence the markets via fiscal policy and monetary policy. 
 
Fiscal Policy 
Fiscal policy is the manipulation of AD in the economy via changes in taxation or government spending. It can 
also have supply side effects and be used to improve education, health or the redistribution of income. To see 
this in action, we can consider the fiscal policy response of the UK government to the Covid pandemic. In 
March 2020, the government implemented several policies that included social distancing, shutting down of 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
16 
 
 
 
shops, travel restrictions etc. that led to a serious downturn in the UK economy (e.g. 19.5% drop in QoQ* GDP 
for Q2 2020 and an overall contraction of 9.8% in 2020). This was a situation where AD had severely 
decreased, people weren’t buying much so consumption decreased, businesses weren’t investing and overall 
the economy was contracting. Consequently, a policy needed to be adopted that increased AD. 
 
We need to think of the components of AD i.e. C+I+G+(X-M), remembering that consumption accounts for 
around 70% of total AD. Naively speaking, we need a policy that can get more money into the hands of people 
hoping that they will spend it and in turn increase economic activity (AD). The UK Government attempted to do 
this by increasing welfare payments (e.g. furlough payments at an estimated cost of £70bn, an increase in 
Universal Credit and grants to businesses). This policy aimed to increase the C and G components of AD. 
Also, the government pledged to spend £1bn on grants for firms committing to innovation, aiming to increase 
the I component. These measures (among others) are examples of fiscal policy trying to influence the market 
and boost AD. 
 
*QoQ = Quarter-on-Quarter 
 
 
You’ve already seen this graph so we can use this to explain the fiscal policy that the government tried to 
pursue during the pandemic. Note that fiscal policy has a time-lag, meaning there is a delay between when 
policy is introduced and when its effects can be seen in the market. Furthermore, there can be unintended 
consequences. For example, what if the extra credit in the economy was not spent on goods/services but was 
instead saved for future use? This concept is called the “multiplier” and interested readers should look up 
“marginal propensity to save / marginal propensity to consume”. 
 
In my opinion, the more interesting intervention is monetary policy. Whilst sometimes governments play a role 
in this, largely monetary policy is driven by central banks. In the UK this is the Bank of England (BoE), whilst in 
the U.S. it is the Federal Reserve (Fed) and in Europe it is the European Central Bank (ECB). The primary tool 
behind monetary policy is interest rates. As discussed earlier, interest rates should be thought of as the cost of 
borrowing. 
 
But, how do interest rates affect AD and LRAS and consequently the overall economy? Well… in many ways! 
Firstly, we will consider the impact of decreasing interest rates on AD. Low interest rates encourage consumer 
borrowing – as the saying goes: “money is cheap''. Every form of loan becomes cheaper, from car loans to 
bank loans to credit card loans. Overall, there is more credit in the economy and disposable incomes for 
households increase. In turn, consumption of goods/services increases. At the same time, saving becomes 
less attractive, further increasing consumption. 
 
Simultaneously, as interest rates fall, mortgage payments decrease, and this can trigger housing booms as 
more people can afford the monthly payments and demand for housing increases. From a business 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
17 
 
 
 
perspective, borrowing costs decrease. This encourages investment as loans for big capital projects (new 
factories, research and development, mergers etc.) come at lower premiums. 
 
Finally, usually (but not always) a fall in interest rates typically leads to a depreciation in a country’s currency in 
FX markets. This means imports become more expensive whilst exports seem cheaper. Overall, interest rates 
have a wide-ranging effect on the economy, and this is what makes monetary policy so powerful. Unlike fiscal 
policy which largely has targeted effects, interest rates affect so many different groups of the economy through 
many different channels and can lead to a more coordinated response when intervening in the markets. 
 
Interest rates also have supply side effects and can influence LRAS. For example, low and stable interest 
rates may encourage firms to invest through big capital projects as they can obtain relatively cheaper 
financing. In the long run, this can lead to improved efficiency and lower costs for firms meaning that LRAS 
can increase (shift outwards). Here, we explained the situation where interest rates were decreasing, and this 
typical policy undertaken during times of economic slowdown e.g. during the COVID pandemic. 
 
 
The graph above illustrates the drop in interest rates to historic lows of 0.1% by the BoE to try and boost 
economic activity during the pandemic 2020. Interestingly, there are a series of spikes from early 2022 this 
year where rates have suddenly shot up and are currently at 2.25%. Why have interest rates gone up so much 
and why so quickly? Well to answer this we can consult the earlier diagram we drew about the current 
macroeconomic conditions (see below). 
 
 
The current macro environment is dominated by high inflation (8.6% CPI in the UK, 8.3% CPI in the US and 
10% in the eurozone). This is due to the demand-pull and cost-push factors that were explained earlier. To 
reduce inflation, central banks have been raising (hiking) interest rates at unprecedented speed. This is a form 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
18 
 
 
 
of contractionary monetary policy where the aim is to tighten financial conditions by making money more 
expensive to borrow. In theory, this has the reverse effect of those outlined earlier leading to a decrease in AD 
and reducing inflationary pressures. 
 
Another form of monetary policy is quantitative easing (QE). This is performed in times of economic downturn 
and typically occurs in conjunction with a decrease in interest rates. Here, a central bank starts buying up 
bonds issued by the government (which you’ll cover in greater detail in lecture 6 with Don-Angelo). Do you 
remember the increased welfare spending we mentioned earlier? Well, think about it: how does the 
government obtain the money to execute such large spending programs? It is debt. More specifically, 
government bonds (rates – a fixed income instrument) which are issued by institutions in return for coupon 
payments and return of the principal at some maturity date in the future. 
 
So in QE, central banks buy up these government bonds, in turn driving the price of the bonds higher. As the 
prices of these bonds increase, their yield (coupon payment as % of price) decreases. This “yield” can be 
thought of as an interest rate and is often the benchmark interest rate used on many loans. Sounds like earlier 
now, right? These lower yields feed down through the economy and seek to decrease the cost of borrowing 
and hence increase spending/consumption in the economy. For perspective, as part of the response to the 
Covid pandemic the BoE adopted a QE program worth up to £450bn. 
 
The reverse of QE is Quantitative Tightening (QT) where central banks reduce their bond holdings. Expectedly, 
this has the opposite effect of QE. This time, bond prices drop and yields rise, having the ripple effect of higher 
interest rates and reducing the amount of spending in the economy. Currently, in response to higher inflation 
and a need to reverse the extensive QE programs during Covid, many central banks have been forced to start 
QT and this has led to falling bond prices across the world. 
 
In conclusion, by reading these notes you should have a good understanding of the mechanics behind 
demand and supply. Moreover, you should better understand macroeconomic concepts such as inflation, AD 
and LRAS. Finally, you will now appreciate the importance of interest rates and monetary policy in influencing 
macroeconomic environments.

Economic Indicators

 In 2002, Argentina faced a default largely owing to its debt being chiefly denominated in dollars. 
When the government couldn’t uphold the currency peg that year, the Argentine Peso plummeted 
by 75%. This drastic devaluation meant that a significantly larger sum of pesos was now required 
to settle the same dollar-denominated debt. Consequently, the government defaulted. Currency 
pegs, in many cases, are maintained by utilising foreign exchange (FX) reserves, often stored in 
U.S. dollars due to its high liquidity. This suggests that currency exchange rates aren’t solely 
determined by market forces like supply and demand, but can also be influenced by central 
banks. Maintaining pegs for stronger currencies can be challenging, necessitating large FX 
reserves to assure markets of the peg’s credibility. 
 The fluctuation in the value of a currency pair indicates only the relative value between those two 
currencies, not against all other currencies. To gauge the overarching strength or weakness of a 
currency, we often use “Trade Weighted Baskets.” These indices assess the aggregate value of a 
currency against its primary trading partners, with major partners carrying more weight. 
 The “Trade-Weighted Dollar” index, established by the Federal Reserve, tracks the U.S. dollar’s 
(USD) value based on its competitive standing against major international trading currencies. 
Starting at a benchmark of 100, the index’s ascent to nearly 150 reflects the strength of the dollar 
during President Reagan’s tenure, a period often associated with his “Reaganomics” policies, 
which emphasised deregulation and a lean government approach.

Foreign EXchange Basics 
If you’re sitting here thinking this has anything to do with Forex scams, you’d be completely right! Well, 
no. Some of the concepts you will be studying here are the same ones that those dodgy rental 
Lamborghini-driving, rental mansion-living, rental Gucci-wearing 25-year-old snake oil salesmen try to 
use in order to lure you in to exchanging your hard-earned money for the false promise of a return 
based off some ‘mArKeT sEnTiMeNt’. In the SEC, we’ll be discussing the ways that Foreign Exchange 
(FX) can be used as a way for well-established market players including hedge funds and other 
institutional investors. 
So, why would institutions care about foreign exchanges? First of all, what is a foreign exchange? 
When you hear the word ‘foreign exchange’, the first idea you’ll need to be aware of is a foreign 
exchange rate. This is simply how much money you need to convert from one currency to another one. 
For instance, an exchange rate of £1 = $1.21 means that to get a £1 coin using dollars, you need to 
cough up exactly $1.21. The place where you do this exchange of currency is at a foreign exchange 
market. This doesn’t seem too trivial if you go on holiday. After all, if you travel from the USA to the 
UK, you won’t be able to walk into a Tesco and pay for a £10 bottle of vodka using a $10 note, a $2 
and 10 cents – it’s actually an enforced law that shops only accept Great British Pounds (GBP), which 
is why you walk into a Bureau de Change at Heathrow or wherever to swap your currency for the local 
one.  
Now, why do institutions care about foreign exchanges? Well, often in this world of globalisation, a 
customer (whether that might be an individual, a company or an institution) might need to pay for a 
product that’s not made in the country where the customer themselves are located. Apple don’t make 
the electronics for the iPhone in the US – it’s made in the Foxconn plant in Chennai, India. Practically 
speaking, they don’t pay the plant for their labor in US dollars (USD) since the bosses and the workers 
aren’t going to be able to go to the local shops and services and exchange the USD for the product, so 
they’ll be asked to pay in Indian Rupees (INR), which is the national Indian currency. Now, Apple’s 
accountants (god forbid they aren’t the same ones Enron used) would have to sit and work out the 
exchange rate from USD to INR, exchange their USD to INR and then pay the plant the exact amount 
before they agree to start building their phones. However, the USD to INR exchange rate isn’t fixed... it 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
24 
changes. If one day, 1 USD can buy you 80 INR and the next day 1 USD can buy you 90 INR, you get 
10 more INR available to you. If you were Apple, you’d be incredibly happy since you use up less USD 
to pay, say the 8 billion INR bill to make 1 million units of the next iPhone. If the exchange rate falls to 
70 INR, then by simple division Apple is going to need to give away more USD to pay the bill and Tim 
Cook won’t be a happy CEO when it comes to the next Q4 shareholders’ meeting.  
 
Characteristics of the FX Markets 
Foreign Exchange markets have a couple of distinct features which seem to morph FX traders into 
high intensity individuals (based on the few that I now live in fear of). These features include:  
Global Liquidity 
These markets enable countries all over the world to trade with each other by allowing different 
currencies to be exchanged. With the ability to convert currencies, countries can trade in a foreign 
currency and always be able to convert their profits back into the home tender. Liquidity refers to the 
ease with which an asset can be traded. FX markets are highly liquid meaning that there are lots of 
buyers or sellers at any one time and competition to give a fair conversion rate is high.  
Fast Paced 
As there is lots of liquidity (lots of traders buying and selling at any one time), FX markets are a perfect 
candidate for algorithmic trading – both from market makers supplying the market and those 
interacting with them. This makes the markets full of super-fast algorithms leaving very little room for 
arbitrage. Arbitrage is the process of making a ‘guaranteed profit’ by taking advantage of mispricing. 
Computers are very quick to react to a mispricing, therefore quickly closing any arbitrage gap.  
Spot and Forward Market, (and many more such as swaps) 
Spot markets in this instance describe the instantaneous swapping of two currencies. Instead, FX 
forwards describe an exchange of two currencies at some point in the future at an exchange rate 
decided on at the time the forward contract is written. Of course, you can also find just about any 
complex derivative on FX markets also, including options, swaps, … 
Largest Over the Counter Market 
FX markets are used by a broad range of players including banks, forex dealers, commercial 
companies, central banks, investment management firms, hedge funds, retail forex dealers, and 
investors.  
Most FX transactions are executed OTC (Over the Counter) meaning that trades are tailored for clients 
via a network of banks. This is because most FX transactions are functional for companies or 
institutions who need specific contract details which may not be available on an exchange. The FX 
OTC market is by far the largest financial market in the world and runs 24 hours per day except 
weekends. Exchange traded FX is typically for smaller market participants, such as retail traders 
(someone like you and I rather than a bank, company, …).

The first column gives the bids. A bid is the exchange rate at which the broker that you are trading with 
will ‘buy’ the currency pair for (they will give you the second currency in the pair in exchange for the 
first currency at the given bid FX rate). We will look at the mechanics of this in the next section but just 
remember that a brokers bid is the exchange rate at which the retail trader is ‘selling’ for.  
Bid = exchange rate at which broker buys = rate at which retail trader sells 
The second column gives the asks. This is the exchange rate at which the broker will ‘sell’ the 
currency pair (they will give you the first currency in the pair in exchange for the second currency at 
the given ask FX rate) to the retail trader for who in this case is the ‘buyer’.  
Ask = exchange rate at which broker sells = rate at which retail trader buys 
I use quotation marks here because, how can you possibly buy or sell an exchange rate between two 
currencies! We will cover what is meant by this next.  
  
Exchange rates  
Right, now that we’ve discussed the purpose of FX, let’s go on and discuss how exchange rates are 
quoted. We’re going to repeat this point again and again, so make a note of this point:  
Any trade can be simplified to two features.

What do I mean by this? Well, when we trade a currency, we have a currency that we buy and a 
currency that we sell. So, there’s only two currencies involved in a trade, depending on if we are 
buying or selling them. For this reason, we will be trading in currency pairs.  
Let’s take an example of Apple having to pay Foxconn chennai 8 billion INR (Indian Rupee) to pay for 
its manufacture of the iPhone in India. Taking an exchange rate of 1 USD = 80 INR, we can assess 
Apple’s cash flow by drawing a table:

In this Scenario, Foxconn is selling the currency pair because they are selling their USD (the first 
currency in the pair – the base currency) and buying INR (the second currency in the pair – the quote 
currency). So, they’ve lost 100 million USD to convert it to 8 billion INR, which they’ve then had to 
transfer to Foxconn to pay the bills.  
One way to think about the exchange rate is the ratio of value of one currency to another. Here, we’ve 
specified that the value of 1 USD is 80 INR – that’s how much it costs to be able to buy USD by selling 
INR. Unsurprisingly, there is a standardised notation for this sort of thing to save traders time and 
make sure everyone talks the same way, which is what we’re going to talk about.  
We often write currency pairs using the currency codes and separate them with a backward slash, e.g. 
USD/INR – 80.0 
 
The first currency you specify, USD in this case, is known as the base currency. This is always given a 
value of 1 in the ratio between the pair. In this currency pair, this ratio refers to 1 USD for 80.0 INR 
(Indian Rupees).  
The second currency you specify, INR in this case, is known as a quote currency. This is not fixed to 
any value, so it can take any value it wants. Why is this the case?  
The base currency is given the name ‘reference element’. This is because the quote must be read as 
such:  
How much you have to pay in the quote currency to be able to buy 1 unit of the base currency.  
Here, the USD/INR is a currency pair that specifies how many INR you need to sell (or buy) in order to 
buy (or sell) 1 USD.

Currency rankings  
To save you from the history lesson that I could give about this, there is a specific order that you write 
the order of currencies REGARDLESS OF IF YOU ARE BUYING OR SELLING one of them.  
The order is based off a rankings list of currencies called the majors. I like to think of them the same 
way I think about the ATP Tennis Rankings, or the F1 Driver Championship rankings. There’s a 
convention in tennis that the first name on a tennis match scoreboard is the player that’s higher 
ranked. For instance, if Novak Djokovic (No. 6) faces John Isner (No. 33), you’ll see the notation of a 
game with Djokovic’s name first on the scoreboard, followed by Isner’s score:  
We do the same with currencies: Let’s start with the rankings before the notation.  
Here’s the ranking of currencies for the majors:  
1. Euro, EUR (€)  
2. Great British Pound Sterling, GBP (£)  
3. Australian Dollar, AUD ($ or A$)  
4. United States Dollar, USD ($)  
5. Canadian Dollar, CAD ($ or C$)  
6. Swiss Franc, CHF (CHF)  
7. Japanese Yen, JPY (¥)

popularity for reference:

What are exotics and cross currency pairs?  
But wait, there are more than 7 currencies in the world. What about the INR? Or even, what about the 
Chinese Yuan (CNY)? Russian Rouble (RUB)? These are known as exotics! Why are they called 
exotics? Essentially, no one really stores money as CNY or RUB unless they’re making transactions 
within those countries e.g. China and Russia respectively. It’s more business minded to hold your 
money as USD or EUR as it’s more geopolitically stable and more people can buy or sell USD and 
EUR with you compared to CNY or RUB. They’re unique currencies to be holding i.e. they’re exotic.  
So, what do we do with CNY or RUB? Well, since they’re not on the majors list, we pair them up with 
one of the currencies on the majors list and they’ll always take the place of the quote currency e.g.  
CAD/CNY 
JPY/RUB 
Conveniently, the Americans in the US have a special name for currencies that don’t involve the USD 
as either the base or quote currency – cross currency pairs. For instance, the AUD/CHF pair is a 
cross currency pair. Even better, any currency pair that involves the EUR is given the term euro 
crosses. The EUR/GBP is a very common euro cross that you’ll encounter given how much it’s traded 
(known as its ‘volume’). 
Right, how to sell currency pairs. Let’s get back to that for a bit now. We wanted to sell USD and buy 
INR. The previous quote we had was:

USD/INR = 80.00 
 
which meant that to buy 1 USD, we had to sell 80 INR. You’ll remember that this was by convention 
since USD was the base currency and INR was the quote currency. To buy INR and sell USD, you sell 
1 USD and in exchange receive 80 INR (i.e. buy 80 INR). However, we still use the same quote but 
just read it the other way!  
When trading exotics, there may be issues with low liquidity so it’s important for traders to 
execute trades carefully to avoid large slippage. To take a layman’s definition, liquidity is a 
measure of how easy it is to buy or sell something. Cash is very easy to sell... It's cash. You can go 
into any supermarket and sell your cash for a loaf of bread (that’s what we mean by ‘selling’ cash). A 
house, however, is illiquid. It’s harder to sell a house and get money in return for it to spend on other 
things.  
If lots of people are willing to sell and lots of people are willing to buy, liquidity is high. If either of the 
two don’t exist (or exist in low numbers), then there’s low liquidity. Since few people would wish to 
trade in their CHY directly into RUB, it would be most likely that the liquidity is low and as a result, the 
bid-ask spread might be ridiculously huge because market makers do not have to be very competitive 
to get your business as there are fewer of them.  
 
Margin, Carry, and Trade Mechanics 
To sell 1 USD in the above exotics example, it is not necessary that we own USD in the first place. 
Instead, we can borrow 1 USD (and pay some interest on it of course) and then exchange it with 80 
INR (buying INR). As you can see, during this trade not a single dollar leaves our account, and we 
have access to INR at the USD borrowing rate.  
This is the basis of a carry trade, a very famous trading technique where you borrow in a currency with 
low interest rates (e.g., Japan’s current -0.1% and exchange into a currency with high interest rates 
such as USD at 5.5%). This carry trade is profitable if the exchange rate stays roughly the same or 
increases – as was the case with USD/JPY during the golden carry trade days because so many 
people were getting in the trade, buying USD and selling JPY in huge scales. This example also 
introduces the idea of carry – earning income on a trade if even nothing happens. This is favorable for 
a trader and a very important topic when pitching a trade, as I found out the hard way after pitching a 
negative carry bull steepener on US Treasuries.  
But there is a limit to how much notional (the amount we borrow) we can put on this trade. To make 
sure the bank that we trade with doesn’t take on too much risk, the speculator making the trade will 
typically have to give the bank 10% of the trade value (this is called margin) in case the trade starts 
losing money. If we close the trade and we haven’t lost more money than the margin amount, we get 
what is left back. To summarize, to access leverage (a total trade size larger than the money we need 
to open the trade), we put down some margin to a broker or bank so that they are protected if we start 
losing money – the banker can just keep the margin amount and close the trade in the worst case. We 
will revisit this in later lectures so don’t worry if this is too confusing for now.

the USD that we owe. We get to keep any USD in excess of the amount owed if the USD/INR 
exchange rate falls.

A quick revisit to what a bid-ask spread is in FX. Remember the MetaTrader screenshot in Figure 1 
above, it showed a bid and an ask exchange rate. The reason that we have two rates is because you 
can’t always buy and sell something at the same price – if this were the case, how would the people 
providing the prices make any money? Therefore, there is both a bid and an ask exchange rate… 
As a reminder, you buy a currency pair when you buy the first currency in the pair using the second 
currency in the pair – this equivalent to being Long. You sell a currency pair when you sell the first 
currency in the pair in exchange for the first currency in the pair – this is being short.  
 
Bid = exchange rate at which broker buys = rate at which retail trader sells 
So for a bid on EUR/USD of 1.2136 (see Figure 1), the market maker will buy 1 Euro and sell to you 
1.2136 US Dollars and be ‘long’ (long means you think something will increase) the currency pair. And 
for the ask exchange rate, 
Ask = exchange rate at which broker sells = rate at which retail trader buys 
Here, the EUR/USD bid of 1.2137 means that the market maker will sell 1 Euro in exchange for 1.2137 
USD and be ‘short’ (short means you think something will decrease) the currency pair.  
So if you are a bank, you could firstly buy a Euro for 1.2136 dollars, and sell if for 1.2137 dollars, 
making a guaranteed profit of 0.0001. As you can imagine, they have to trade millions and millions of 
euros to make a plump Christmas bonus.

Right, now that’s exchange rates and currency pairs covered from its fundamentals. What’s next? Ah, 
yes. The types of exchange rates!  
 
Types of FX markets  
So far, we’ve spoken about why exchange rates exist, how you write them using currency pairs and 
how you can buy and sell from one currency to another. Now, we’re going to go up a level and talk 
about the different types of exchange rates that exist.  
Currencies are traded on the foreign exchange market. You’ll know this already but just for 
clarification, a market is an area (either one that exists in the real world or digital) where buyers and 
sellers can meet to buy and sell goods respectively. On the foreign exchange market, currencies are 
bought and sold. We’ve already mentioned two reasons above – the ability to trade internationally 
outside of your domestic country, being able to invest in different countries that use different 
currencies. Another reason people trade currencies is the exact reason that people trade stocks... 
They think a currency will go up OR DOWN in value soon and they can profit from the difference in 
price between now and a point in the future. This third reason is called speculation i.e. the idea that 
they think they’ll benefit from a change in price. Often, there is a misconception that speculation is the 
idea of prices only going up. This is not the case – prices can go down so you’d benefit from shorting 
an asset (in this case, the asset being a currency).  
 
Right. Now we’ve cleared that up and out of the way. There are several types of products that you can 
find inside FX exchange markets:  
a. Spot exchange markets  
b. Future exchange markets  
c. Forward exchange markets 
d. FX swaps 
e. Cross currency swaps 
f. FX options, FX CFDs, and many others 
 
We will discuss Spot, Futures and Forwards here but please feel free to research the others if you 
fancy yourself as an FX trader.  
 
Spot exchange markets  
Spot markets are where you can trade currency right now i.e. on the spot. You would theoretically buy 
the currency right now and have it in your hands the same second you pay the asking price for it. It’s 
just like walking into a shop, paying the shopkeeper and walking out with the goods all on one go.  
We tend to say (thanks to financial lingo) that you take immediate delivery of the asset which actually 
just means the same thing as walking into the shop and walking out with what you want in your hand 
all in one go. This doesn’t just apply to currencies (as you will find out in Lecture 4) – spot markets 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
33 
apply to pretty much anything you can think of whether that be oil, gas, feedstock etc. We specify in 
this case that we’re discussing spot exchange markets to refer to the idea of currency being traded.  
You’ll have encountered spot exchange markets in real life when you visit a Bureau de Change. You 
walk in and exchange your EUR to GBP (or vice versa) and you get the currency you want on the very 
same day. In financial markets, companies will do much the same process through FX markets to be 
able to convert currencies at a specified exchange rate, however the typical convention is that a spot 
transaction is settled at T+2 (i.e. in two days’ time from the date of execution) so the currencies are 
exchanged slightly after a deal is made.  
What if you don’t want to exchange currencies now but instead do the exchange in, say... a month’s 
time (far forward in the future) while locking in an exchange rate right now? That’s what forward 
exchange markets do!  
 
Forward exchange markets  
We only have so long each lecture to cover things so we’ll briefly cover forward exchange markets 
from a high-level overview before we discuss them in greater depth later on in a few weeks’ time.  
With both FX futures and forwards (two types of financial instrument), an agreement is made between 
a buyer and seller (e.g. a hedge fund and a bank) to exchange currencies at an agreed-upon rate at a 
set date in the future. E.g. I will exchange $1 million for Euros at a EUR/USD rate of 1.5 in 1 year from 
now. In general, futures are forwards are an agreement to exchange at a point in the future something 
(shares, commodities, …) at a price agreed today.  
Futures trade on an exchange, where everyone can see the bids and asks and trading is largely 
electronic, as seen for retail traders in Figure 1. You can think of an exchange as a marketplace where 
any two people can trade and give bids and asks and everyone else can see the trades as they 
happen. Instead, forwards are traded OTC (over the counter). This means that a company or hedge 
fund would contact their trader at a bank over the chat function or the phone, ask for a bid/ask spread 
and receive a personalized quote based on their needs and trade size. This process is 1 to 1 rather 
than many to many as with futures.  
Take again the example of Apple. I briefly pointed out that if the exchange rate for USD/INR falls from 
80.00 to 70.00 over the course of, say, 6 months or something, they’d lose out on an extra 10 INR per 
USD. Of course, it would be very ideal for Tim Cook and his accountants to lock-in today’s exchange 
rate of 80.00 and use that to pay off Foxconn in 6 months’ time if that’s the payment structure that 
they’ve planned instead of the 70.00 INR per USD as they would have to cough up $1.125 per 80.00 
INR rather than $1. So, Apple buys a forward contract (over the counter from a bank) to have the 
currency rate fixed for one day in the future at 80.00 INR if they expect that it’ll fall in half a year to 
70.00 INR. It is possible that USD/INR goes up to 90.00 (which we often say as ‘the USD getting 
stronger against the rupee’) and they miss out on profits as less USD is needed to pay the INR 
required. However, the point is that Apple may not think they can predict the exchange rate well and 
don’t want to risk it moving against them over the next 6 months.

Pricing FX Forwards 
We cover derivative pricing more thoroughly in later lectures so some of this may be a bit confusing for 
now, it is more a taste of things to come.  
Some of the most traded types of forwards and futures contract are based on currency pairs (e.g., 
EUR/USD). As currencies don’t require storage (because they’re electronic, unless you want to take 
delivery of bank notes or coins) and provide no income such as dividends, the futures price is 
impacted only by the interest rates which apply to each currency.  
As we found previously, buying a currency pair with no prior capital requires borrowing the second 
(quote) currency and using it to buy the first currency (base), e.g., for a long position in EUR/USD, you 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
35 
would borrow USD and exchange it for EUR. Therefore, we earn interest on our EUR and pay interest 
on our USD.  
Now imagine that we want to buy a 2 year forward for GBP/USD, how would we price it? We find that 
the current spot price of GBP/USD is 1.18, i.e., 1-pound exchanges for 1.18 dollars, and lets imagine 
the annual US interest rate is 2.50% and the UK rate is 1.75% (FYI the interest rates at the time of 
writing are actually 5.5% in the US and 5.25% in the UK, Oct 2023).  
A futures price is always the future value of the underlying asset if it was bought today. Using this, we 
can calculate the future outcome of buying this currency pair today given that the exchange rate 
doesn’t move.  
We will be buying pounds and so, at the current interest rate, £1 now will be worth (1 + 1. 75%)2 × 1 = 
£1.0353 in 2 years’ time (because we can invest it risk free at 1.75% for 2 years). Conversely, we will 
be borrowing dollars and so, at the current interest rate, $1.18 borrowed now will be (1 + 2. 5%)2 × 1. 
18 = $1. 2397 owed in 2 years. Therefore, our debt in 2 years will be worth $1.2397 and our assets will 
be worth £1.0353. To avoid any arbitrage opportunity, this is what we must therefore pay for the 1-year 
maturity forward contract.  
This leaves our quoted exchange rate as $1.2397 / £1.0353  = 1.1974 = the number of dollars we 
receive per pound in 2 years’ time.  
It is common to find this kind of thought process confusing so one way of finding the price of a forward 
or future can be to find the future value of the underlying asset as if you had instead bought the asset 
at spot value, i.e., today on the market and waited until the maturity of the forward/futures contract.

Exchange Rate Systems  
In an earlier lecture, we introduced the simple yet fundamental concept of supply and demand 
economics. You can draw a line for both the supply and demand for a good by plotting the price the 
good is valued at against the amount of the good that people are prepared to pay (i.e. the demand). 
Just to be clear, a good (noun, n.) is just an item of any description, whether that be a loaf of bread, a 
barrel of oil etc. Cash is a type of good as well! You can use it in exchange for other goods when you 
complete a trade with a seller (not so bad, huh?).  
So, you could ask “can we draw supply and demand curves for currencies?” Yes, we can. When we 
draw supply and demand curves for currencies, the axes we use actually have specific meanings this 
time compared to the usual ‘price’ and ‘quantity’ that you saw earlier. Take the EUR/GBP currency 
pair. Our x-axis this time is ‘quantity in EUR, € millions (m) per day’ (since currencies are traded on a 
daily basis) and the y-axis is ‘price, GBP £ per EUR €’. If you are a bit confused, remember we are 
fixing the unit of EUR to 1 and we’re looking at how many GBP we need to pay for 1 EUR.  
 
Floating Exchange Rate Systems

and cheap hence the quantity of EUR demanded is going to be high. For this, we’re going to draw a

Demand curves  
Now, let’s think about the sellers. At a low price, very few sellers are going to be prepared to supply 
the market with EUR as they think the price that they have to sell at (based on the market price for 
EUR/GBP) is too low and hence they won’t make a reasonable margin or profit. However, if the price 
of the EUR/GBP is much higher, they’ll be more incentivised to pull their reserves of EUR and supply it 
on the market for people to buy. For this reason, at low EUR/GBP, the quantity of EUR supplied will be 
low but at higher EUR/GBP, the quantity of EUR supplied will be high.

Combining supply and demand curves together  
Now, this is interesting. What if we go one step further... what if we draw both ones, one on top of each 
other (assuming both axes are the same scale)? What would happen here?  
 
We get a complete supply-demand curve for the EUR/GBP! Notice that since we have two straight 
lines here, they’re going to intersect at one given point. This point is critical, it’s what we know to be 
the equilibrium point. Think of the equilibrium point as the point on the graph where the flow of 
supply and demand are equal i.e. in equilibrium (just like forces in mechanics or one-step reactions in 
chemistry). In the context of macroeconomics here, it means that the EUR is being sold as quickly as 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
39 
it’s being supplied into the market. If the price of the EUR/GBP is set to £0.80, a seller would be 
guaranteed a sale as they would be supplying €1,800 million per day and finding it being sold all by the 
close of business.  
If the price by sellers was artificially set higher e.g. to £0.90, there would be a difference between the 
quantity supplied and the quantity demanded so the sellers would find some EUR left over at the end 
of the day since there aren’t enough buyers interested in purchasing EUR at £0.90 but instead at lower 
prices (as you can see on the demand curve). At £0.90 per EUR, there’s only demand to buy €1,500 
million but suppliers would be supplying €2,100 million, leaving €600 million unsold – a seller’s 
nightmare. For this reason, they want to make sure they’re as close to the equilibrium price possible to 
ensure all of their supply of EUR is sold out.  
 
Here, the exchange rate quite obviously (in the graph on the previous page) was EUR/GBP = 0.80. 
This type of exchange rate has a special name: floating exchange rate system. I like to think of it as 
a boat that’s sitting on water with a tidal wave passing by. The boat will move up and down in sync 
with the passing waves while it stays afloat, without any external interference. The reason I mention 
‘external interference’ is that the exchange rate of most currency pairs is not influenced by any 
country’s government – it is left purely to the market’s ‘free market forces’ of demand and supply to 
reach some sort of an equilibrium point at, say, 0.80 GBP and everyone trades the currency pair at 
that price.  
The issue of floating exchange rate mechanisms  
However, if you are Rishi Sunak, having your own country’s currency priced quite low is a bit of an 
issue. Of course, it looks terrible on economic policy since the rest of the world can buy your 1 GBP at 
1.25 EUR (that’s 1 EUR / 0.8 GBP, for those wondering) meaning that the pound is low in value, but it 
also means that for factories and industries based in Britain, the price of imports is quite high. If the 
Pound were to appreciate against the Euro to e.g., EUR/GBP = 0.5 (£0.5 buys €1, rather than £0.8 
buys €1), UK importers can cough up fewer GBP to pay for something in EUR and are inclined to 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
40 
import more. On the other hand, other countries will have to pay more in their local currency to import 
UK goods. Hence, net importers may want a strong currency while next exporters may not.  
Economics is a balancing act in essence based on the government policy that’s being implemented 
any one time: should the GBP be ‘expensive’ e.g., EUR/GBP = 1.50, or should the GBP be cheaper, 
so imports are easier to buy e.g. EUR/GBP = 0.60.  
So, how can countries intervene to adjust their country’s exchange rate? For that, there are several 
other classes of exchange rate systems, which we’ll get onto next – after a discussion on purchasing 
power parity!  
 
FX Rate Drivers 
There are a number of reasons why floating FX rates move around, and many traders have both 
gained and lost fortunes trying to understand the different factors at play. Trading currencies is 
typically based on the careful combination of fundamental and technical analysis. Technical analysis 
my seem foolish, but there are plenty of people out there trading using technicals, I’ve even seen it in 
major banks! Here are some of the major reasons for markets to move… 
 
Technical Analysis: Chart patterns, Correlations between currencies 
Fundamentals: Purchasing power parity, Inflation differentials, Interest rate differentials 
Economics and Stats: Measures of economic growth (GDP), Measures of inflation (CPI), Balance of 
payments (trade flows), Financial statistics (government debt, fiscal measures) 
Politics: Public policy preferences, Current and planned economic policy (e.g. Lizz Truss crashing 
markets with mini budget) 
Central Banks: Market operations (fiscal policy), Interest rates (fiscal policy) 
Sentiment: World news (e.g. wars), confidence and stability (‘bull’ market vs. ‘bear’ market) 
Unfortunately, we don’t have time to dive deep on the above, however we will take a quick look at 
Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) as a key economic idea for valuing currencies.  
Purchasing power parity  
Purchasing power is basically how much you can buy for 1 unit of currency. You might have heard 
back in the old days that a Freddo would cost around 10p in 2000 although it’s now around 25p in 
2022. Conveniently, there even exists a humorous ‘Freddo index’ in its name to track its price over the 
last 20+ years. Back to the main point. Well, in 2000, you could walk into a shop with £1 and buy 10 
Freddos (100p / 10p). 20 years later, you could only buy 4 Freddos (100p / 25p). That means that the 
purchasing power of a unit GBP with respect to freddos has gone down in the last 20 years i.e. the 
amount of freddos that you can get with a single coin has gone down over time.  
Now, think of this: you do your weekly shopping at Sainsbury’s which includes food, drink, meat 
(unless you don’t eat meat), washing-up liquid, toiletries etc. and that comes to around £30. Now, 
imagine walking into a Walmart store in America and picking up the exact same list of groceries in your 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
41 
list (and I mean, literally the exact same) and it comes to around $45. That means that by some simple 
maths, £1 in England (in theory) buys you $1.50 worth of goods in the USA. That’s an actual exchange 
rate for GBP/USD of 1.50.  
What’s going on here? Why is there an exchange rate for 1.21 in the exchange markets for GBP/USD 
but 1.50 when we actually compare the same sample shopping list? This is because the exchange 
markets reflect what we call ‘short-term speculations’ whereas the grocery lists may reflect longer-term 
effects of inflation that exist in both the UK and USA economies. Exchange market exchange rates are 
called nominal exchange rates as they’re just determined by the equilibrium price from buyers and 
sellers in a market. In reality, the GBP/USD exchange rate is much higher at 1.50 if we look at a 
specific basket of goods, which we call a real exchange rate. There’s actually a theory which states 
that changes in exchange rates for a currency pair in the long-term are due to the differences in 
inflation rates between the two countries – this is conveniently called the purchasing power parity of 
exchange rates. The term ‘parity’ is a reference to the equality between how much you can buy when 
you compare the USA to the UK, which comes from the idea of how ‘equal’ one thing is to another. 
We’re not comparing currencies based on their exchange rate, but instead on how much you can buy 
in one country compared to another.  
You won’t come across purchasing power parity too often but it’s an important topic in 
macroeconomics that we thought would be useful to cover. Right, onto other types of exchange rate 
systems.  
 
Other types of exchange rate systems  
Fixed exchange rate systems  
Sometimes, a government might wish to be directly in charge of making sure an exchange rate that 
involves its own currency is fixed at a specific value. These types of exchange rate systems are fixed 
exchange rate systems. Just to be clear here, this DOES NOT mean that it’ll always stay precisely at 
one specific number. As you’ll see momentarily, this used to be the case up until around the 1970s 
when a certain Richard Nixon did something that changed economies and finance forever.  
 
Gold standard  
One example of a fixed exchange rate system was the gold standard. For hundreds of years, any 
currency whether that be the pound, or the dollar was linked to a specific weight of gold in the 
country’s central bank. This meant that e.g. if you were in America, you would be able to walk into the 
Federal Reserve and be able to exchange a $10 note for nearly 15 grams of gold from the 1870s to 
1914, and again from the early 1920s to 1932. In England, the same 15 grams of gold could be 
exchanged for £2.06, which implied an exchange rate of GBP/USD ≈ $4.87 The same could be done 
in France, Germany, England as well. However, there was a problem. Since there was a direct link 
between the weight of gold that a central bank stored and the amount of paper money it could issue, it 
was hard for England and other countries to issue more paper notes in order for the Government to 
pay for services and other stuff such as imports – the exchange rates were fixed and so the rates 
themselves determined the amount of paper currency in the country’s money supply. One way to solve 
this was to mine for more gold but that would take time, be a waste of effort for the little reward and 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
42 
also be difficult to find areas of land that have gold (unless you could invade, say, another country... 
but no one really did that – much, anyway).  
 
Bretton Woods  
This whole gold standard business changed slightly after World War 1, the intermittent Great 
Depression and World War 2. In 1944, another system came about called the Bretton Woods system. 
Essentially, 44 countries sent delegates to, er... Bretton Woods in New Hampshire, USA. A new 
financial system was needed that was less rigid than the old gold standard but still standardised 
enough so it was under control, without it falling apart. The Americans at this point had managed to 
hoard over 66% of the world’s gold reserve from travelling through other countries across Europe, so it 
decided to be the main country in charge of a new type of gold standard where the USD would be 
linked to its reserves of gold, and in turn all other countries including England, France, Australia etc. 
would fix their currencies to the dollar at a near-fixed exchange rate, with some very small fluctuations 
allowed. This was a huge win for the USA as it would essentially be in charge of the world’s 
economies through the dollar by manipulating its own gold reserves. Unsurprisingly, just as many 
American policies have shown post-WW2, the Bretton Woods system didn’t work. Most countries were 
facing their own economic problems and decided to take back some of the gold that belonged in the 
Federal reserve, most notably France who even sent a battleship to transport their gold back from the 
USA.  
 
Fiat currency  
As a result, Richard Nixon (USA President) found a battle of trying to print more money in the face of 
his country’s stagflation but also facing dwindling supplies of gold. He fiddled with the gold to US 
exchange rate from 1971 up until 1973 when he decided to decouple the dollar from the gold. Now, the 
dollar and every other currency in the world was essentially ‘backed’ by nothing and the dollar was 
‘floated’ so it couldn’t be exchanged for gold anymore. No one could exchange it for a specific value of 
gold anymore that was guaranteed by a central bank. People now believe that a dollar note gives you 
the right to purchase something for a dollar based on what it says on paper. This is what we know now 
to be a fiat currency.  
Nowadays, a few countries fix their exchanges to another currency, namely the dollar. To keep the 
exchange rate fixed, the country’s central bank dabbles in buying or selling its own currency in order to 
keep the e.g. USD/CNY currency pair as constant as possible, if we were talking about the Bank of 
China. Just to be clear, China does NOT have a fixed rate exchange system with the USD – we just 
used it here as an example. If the USD/CNY moved from the intended fixed rate of 6.50 to 7.00, it 
would imply that the CNY weakened against the USD as 1 USD gives you more CNY, so the Chinese 
government would buy CNY in foreign exchange markets to lower its supply and shift the exchange 
rate back to 6.50, which is known as making the CNY ‘stronger against the dollar’. Likewise, if the 
USD/CNY fell to 6.00, it would mean that the USD became weaker against the CNY (or the CNY got 
stronger against the USD – notice the opposite effect on the other side), so the Chinese government 
would sell some of its CNY reserves to restore the price back upwards to 6.50 by increasing the 
supply. The overriding idea is this: the government of the country in question wants to make sure its 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
43 
currency is fixed as much as possible at a specific value (or as close as possible) by selling or buying 
its own currency to affect its supply in the foreign exchange market.  
 
Managed exchange rate systems  
Realistically, governments have other jobs to do than to sit down all day and look at an exchange rate 
e.g. establish policy, liaise with trade partners, get exposed by an undercover journalist in the 
newspapers – you know, the usual stuff (just kidding). An alternative sort of exchange rate system is a 
managed exchange rate system. They’re also called hybrid systems. Why is that? Well, in these 
sorts of systems, there’s two mechanisms at play. The first is that the market is free to set the price 
through the forces of demand and supply. However, sometimes the government might want to step in 
and sort things out directly to push the exchange rate either up or down in price through selling its 
central bank’s own gold and foreign currency reserves. An alternative way that the government can 
push exchange rates in a certain direction is through an indirect means of fiddling with the interest rate 
– either increasing or decreasing it until the right effect is achieved. Managed exchange rate systems 
can be broken down into different types, although be careful as they are NOT fixed exchange systems 
(as you will see momentarily).  
 
Adjustable peg systems  
An alternative way of saying that a currency rate is ‘fixed’ is to say it’s ‘pegged’, a bit like when you put 
your laundry on a washing line – you fix it on the line to make sure it doesn’t move when the wind 
blows through it. Another term that’s used is “anchored” but I think it’s easy to visualise why that term 
is also appropriate. So, we got the idea of a peg out of the way. What’s an adjustable peg? In fact, if 
you think back to the washing line analogy, this idea isn’t too hard to understand. In much the same 
way that you can slide an item of laundry up and down the washing line until you find a point that’s just 
right, currency rates can also be moved up and down in value until the central bank feels it’s just at the 
right price.  
Normally, we peg two currencies e.g. the USD and the Saudi Arabian Rial (SAR) against each other 
so that they stay fixed in value in the short term. In the long term however, the Saudi central bank 
might wish to adjust the value of the currency i.e. ‘move the peg’ up or down in response to economic 
activity such as an oncoming recession or to spark a growth phase in the Saudi economy. The Bretton 
Woods system was a good example of an adjustable peg as all currencies were pegged against the 
USD in the short term. In the long term however, countries would take measures to make sure their 
daily exchange rate with the USD fell within a certain narrow, pre-established limit. If the price of its 
currency rose, it would take measures to sell its currency in FX markets to increase supply and 
decrease its price, but also buy the currency if the price of the currency went down with respect to 
USD.  
You can actually represent this using an extension of the supply and demand curves that you’re 
familiar with from earlier. Let’s first draw our usual supply and demand curves, noting the equilibrium 
price. We’ll draw some pre-established limits in red so that we can show where we expect the 
equilibrium price to hover around. We assume that demand and supply are once again linear and draw 
the usual axes that we mentioned earlier for the USD/SAR currency pair.
