Crawling peg system  

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
46 
To think about a crawling peg, let’s think first about two types of systems we know so far. We know 
already that an adjustable peg system allows the price of a currency to fluctuate between two hard 
limits we set artificially such that if the price falls outside of the limits specified, the central bank will 
undergo some buying or selling of its own currency in order to bring it back within the tolerance we 
want. Also, we know that in a floating exchange rate system, we simply let the free market supply and 
demand forces dictate the price of the currency itself.  
A crawling peg system is a bit of a hybrid between an adjustable peg system and a floating exchange 
rate system. It still is a peg system (like we mentioned above) but we move the limits slowly to allow 
the central bank to buy or sell the currency over time in response to economic sentiment. If the price of 
the currency is increasing over the last 3 months, we move the limits up by a small amount (hence the 
‘crawl’) and establish a new set of limits that the currency has to live in so that it reflects the recent 
market and economic momentum. Afterwards, the central bank then sells the currency in question so it 
fluctuates around a new, lower price.  
China actually does this with the Chinese Yuan (also known as the ‘Renminbi’), where the government 
pegs the Yuan to different currencies but also can get involved in buying and selling the Yuan itself by 
first moving the limits up or down respectively and then completing the necessary fiscal policies of 
buying and selling its currency to meet its objectives.  
 
Managed float  
Remember what a ‘float’ meant in FX? It simply just meant letting the currency price be dictated by 
supply and demand in the free market, a bit like a boat on the sea with the tides going past to rock it 
up and down. A managed float is similar in FX – it has all the features of a floating exchange rate 
system but with one specific difference... the government can intervene and buy or sell the currency in 
order to change its price!  
This actually isn’t too different to the crawling peg, but instead the price isn’t fixed by the government 
and the central bank in question yet instead it’s left to the free market. This is generally the most 
common type of exchange rate system you’ll find in financial markets, with the USD and GBP being 
prime examples of a ‘managed float’. Often, you might find it called a dirty float as it’s not exactly a 
pure floating exchange system – it has features of it being fixed by central banks to suit its economic 
policies when it needs to, although its interventions are few and far between (by that, I mean it rarely 
happens).  
Analysing a managed float is much the same as a crawling peg, but just remember that the equilibrium 
price isn’t as harshly fixed by the central bank – it has a lot more freedom to move around to its liking.  
 
 
 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
47 
Fact Sheet 
 
 
 
 
1. Foreign Exchange Basics 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
48 
 
An exchange rate of £1 = $1.21 means that to get a £1 coin using dollars, you need to cough up 
exactly $1.21. The place where you do this exchange of currency is at a foreign exchange market.  
 
Well, when we trade a currency, we have a currency that we buy and a currency that we sell. So, 
there’s only two currencies involved in a trade, depending on if we are buying or selling them. For this 
reason, we will be trading in currency pairs.  
 
USD/INR – 80.0 
  
The first currency you specify, USD in this case, is known as the base currency. This is always given a 
value of 1 in the ratio between the pair. In this currency pair, this ratio refers to 1 USD for 80.0 INR 
(Indian Rupees). The second currency you specify, INR in this case, is known as a quote currency. 
This is not fixed to any value, so it can take any value it wants. The quote can be read as: ‘How much 
you have to pay in the quote currency to be able to buy 1 unit of the base currency’.  
 
To convert from the base currency (first in pair) to the quote currency (second in pair), we multiply the 
base amount by the FX rate. To convert from the quote currency to the base currency, we divide the 
quote currency amount by the given FX rate. 
 
 
2. Rankings 
 
Here’s the ranking of currencies for the majors:  
 
1.     Euro, EUR (€)  
2.     Great British Pound Sterling, GBP (£)  
3.     Australian Dollar, AUD ($ or A$)  
4.     United States Dollar, USD ($)  
5.     Canadian Dollar, CAD ($ or C$)  
6.     Swiss Franc, CHF (CHF)  
7.     Japanese Yen, JPY (¥)  
  
The higher ranked currency first as the base currency, followed by the lower ranked currency as the 
quote currency.  
 
 
3. FX Markets 
 
Spot and Forward Markets, (and many more such as swaps) 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
49 
Spot markets are where you can trade currency right now i.e. on the spot. You would theoretically buy 
the currency right now and have it in your hands the same second you pay the asking price for it. In 
practice, the typical convention is that a spot transaction is settled at T+2 (i.e. in two days’ time from 
the date of execution) so the currencies are exchanged slightly after a deal is made.  
 
With both FX futures and forwards (two types of financial instrument), an agreement is made between 
a buyer and seller (e.g. a hedge fund and a bank) to exchange currencies at an agreed-upon rate at a 
set date in the future. E.g. I will exchange $1 million for Euros at a EUR/USD rate of 1.5 in 1 year from 
now. Futures trade on an exchange, where everyone can see the bids and asks and trading is largely 
electronic. Forwards are traded OTC (over the counter).  
 
FX markets are used by a broad range of players including banks, forex dealers, commercial 
companies, central banks, investment management firms, hedge funds, retail forex dealers, and 
investors. These markets enable the ability to trade internationally outside of your domestic country as 
well as being able to invest in different countries that use different currencies. Another reason people 
trade currencies is speculation – they think a currency will go up or down in value soon and they can 
profit from the difference in price between now and a point in the future.  
 
 
 
4. Bids/Asks 
 
As a reminder, you buy a currency pair when you buy the first currency in the pair using the second 
currency in the pair – this equivalent to being Long. You sell a currency pair when you sell the first 
currency in the pair in exchange for the first currency in the pair – this is being short.  
 
Bid = exchange rate at which broker buys = rate at which retail trader sells 
(Bank will give you the second currency in the pair in exchange for the first currency at the given bid 
FX rate).  
 
Ask = exchange rate at which broker sells = rate at which retail trader buys 
(Bank will give you the first currency in the pair in exchange for the second currency at the given ask 
FX rate)  
 
You may see just a single number to represent an exchange rate, this is the midpoint of the best bid 
(highest bid) and best ask (lowest ask).  
 
 
 
5. Leveraged Spot Trade Mechanic 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
50 
In the case where we want to short (sell) e.g. USD/INR. To sell 1 USD for INR, it is not necessary that 
we own USD in the first place. Instead, we can borrow 1 USD (and pay some interest on it of course) 
and then exchange it with 80 INR (buying INR). As you can see, during this trade not a single dollar 
leaves our account, and we have access to INR at the USD borrowing rate.  
 
Finally, to close the trade. We exchange the INR back into USD and give back the USD that we owe. 
We get to keep any USD more than the amount owed if the USD/INR exchange rate falls. 
 
 
 
6. Market Movers 
 
Technical Analysis: Chart patterns, Correlations between currencies 
Fundamentals: Purchasing power parity, Inflation differentials, Interest rate differentials 
Economics and Stats: Measures of economic growth (GDP), Measures of inflation (CPI), Balance of 
payments (trade flows), Financial statistics (government debt, fiscal measures) 
 
E.g., for trade flows, If the Pound were to appreciate against the Euro to e.g. EUR/GBP = 0.5 (£0.5 
buys €1, rather than £0.8 buys €1), UK importers can cough up fewer GBP to pay for something in 
EUR and are inclined to import more. On the other hand, other countries will have to pay more in their 
local currency to import UK goods. Hence, net importers may want a strong currency while next 
exporters may not.  
 
Politics: Public policy preferences, Current and planned economic policy (e.g. Lizz Truss crashing 
markets with mini budget) 
Central Banks: Market operations (fiscal policy), Interest rates (fiscal policy) 
Sentiment: World news (e.g. wars), confidence and stability (‘bull’ market vs. ‘bear’ market) 
 
 
7. Exchange Rate Systems 
 
Sometimes, a government might wish to be directly in charge of making sure an exchange rate that 
involves its own currency is fixed at a specific value. These types of exchange rate systems are fixed 
exchange rate systems. 
— 
An alternative sort of exchange rate system is a managed exchange rate system. There’s two 
mechanisms at play. The first is that the market is free to set the price through the forces of demand 
and supply. However, sometimes the government might want to step in and sort things out directly to 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
51 
push the exchange rate either up or down in price through selling its central bank’s own gold and 
foreign currency reserves.  
 
An adjustable peg system allows the price of a currency to fluctuate between two hard limits set 
artificially such that if the price falls outside of the limits specified, the central bank will undergo some 
buying or selling of its own currency in order to bring it back within the tolerance we want.  
 
In a floating exchange rate system, we simply let the free-market supply and demand forces dictate 
the price of the currency itself.  
 
A crawling peg system is a bit of a hybrid between an adjustable peg system and a floating 
exchange rate system. It still is a peg system (like we mentioned above) but we move the limits slowly 
to allow the central bank to buy or sell the currency over time in response to economic sentiment. If the 
price of the currency is increasing over the last 3 months, we move the limits up by a small amount 
(hence the ‘crawl’) and establish a new set of limits that the currency has to live in so that it reflects the 
recent market and economic momentum.  
 
A managed float has all the features of a floating exchange rate system but the government can 
intervene and buy or sell the currency in order to change its price. 
  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
52 
Reading List 
 
The best info that I have learned is through reading so thought I would add some recommendations to 
kickstart an interest in finance, or if you are already on the finance ladder some of these should be 
interesting.  
 
Quant 
- Fixed Income Relative Value Analysis, [D. Huggins, C. Shaller]

- Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives, [Hull] 
- An Introduction to the Mathematics of Financial Derivatives, [Hirsa, Neftci] 
- Term-Structure Models, [D. Filipovic] 
- Interest Swaps and Other Derivatives, [H. Corb] 
- The Concepts and Practice of Mathematical finance, [M. Joshi] 
- Algorithmic and High Frequency Trading, [Cartea, Jaimungal, Penalva] 
- FX Trader Derivatives School, [G. Jewitt] 
- Systematic Trading, [R. Carver] 
- Paul Wilmott on Quantitative Finance 
[https://github.com/PlamenStilyianov/FinMathematics/blob/master/Paul%20Wilmott%20on%20
Quantitative%20Finance.pdf] 
 
History/Biography 
- When Genius Failed, [R. Lowenstein] 
- Reminiscences of a Stock Operator, [Lefevre] 
- Liars Poker, [M. Lewis] 
 
Trading 
- Fed Up!: Success, Excess and Crisis Through the Eyes of a Hedge Fund Macro Trader, [C. 
Lancaster] 
- Market Wizards / Hedge Fund Market Wizards, [J. Schwager] 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certiﬁcate 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
53 
- Alpha Trader, [B. Donnelly] 
- What I Learned Losing $1 million Dollars, [J. Paul] 
 
Investing 
- The Intelligent Investor, [B. Graham] 
- Common Stocks and Uncommon Profits, [P. Fisher] 
- One Up on Wall Street, [P. Lynch] 
- The Little Book that Beats the Market, [J. Greenblatt] 
- The Most Important Thing, [H. Marks] 
- The Alchemy of Finance, [G. Soros] 
 
Economics 
- Yield Curve Modelling [Yolanda] 
 
Machine Learning 
- Neural networks and deep learning, [M. Nielsen] 
 
General Finance 
- Flash Boys [M. Lewis]

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
4 
Fixed Income 
Notes by Don-Angelo Sfeir 
Lecturer, Securities Education Certificate 2022/23. 
Introduction 
Fixed income is the world’s largest asset class. With an estimated value of over $200T globally, it is almost 
three times the size of combined global equity market valuation. Fixed income refers to any type of investment 
under which the borrower or issuer is obliged to make payments of a fixed amount on a fixed schedule. For 
example, the borrower may have to pay interest at a fixed rate once a year and repay the principal amount on 
maturity. 
 
The foundational fixed income security is the bond. There are many different types of bonds with various 
structures and uses. Fixed income is much less risky than equity investments and generates a steady source 
of income, depending on the terms bond holders may also have complete downside protection. 
What are bonds? 
Bonds essentially represent a loan that has been turned into a security which can be traded. A government or 
corporation borrows money from investors and issues bonds in return. The bonds represent a commitment by 
the issuer to repay the principal (amount back) at maturity (expiry) as well as interest payments at fixed 
intervals. 
 
Bonds fall under two main categories: Sovereign and Corporate. 
Sovereign Bonds 
A sovereign bond is a debt security issued by a national government to raise money for financing government 
programs, paying down old debt, paying interest on current debt, and any other government spending needs. 
Sovereign bonds can be denominated in a foreign currency or the government's domestic currency. 
Corporate Bonds 
A corporate bond is a bond issued by a corporation in order to raise debt. Companies use the proceeds from 
bond sales for a wide variety of purposes, including buying new equipment, investing in research and 
development, buying back their own stock, paying shareholder dividends, refinancing debt, and financing 
mergers and acquisitions. 
Equity and/or Debt – what’s the alternative? 
I think by this point, the picture is quite clear. If you own a company and you want to raise funds to kick-start or 
continue the company’s operations, you’re either going to sell shares in the company or take out a loan in the 
form of a debt.  
 
There are, however, two immediate issues when taking either of these approaches: 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
5 
➔ Selling equity means selling ownership of assets. In turn, since income can be converted into cash 
(which is a business asset that shareholders can claim), this results in shareholders staking a claim into 
the company’s profits through e.g. retained earnings. If you are the owner of the company, you could 
lose out on potential future profits if you choose to sell shares in order to raise funds.  
➔ Securing a loan i.e. debt results in you having to pay interest payments on the debt. This would of 
course mean that the net income of the company decreases and hence the company makes a smaller 
profit. 
◆ Remember, the interest rate is NOT determined by you but rather by the lender of the debt. 
They choose the percentage rate that you have to pay back on the debt every year, so you 
have no room to negotiate on those matters.  
 
You can sort of see the problem: either you give away ownership of future company profits or you get shafted 
by the interest rate you are charged on a loan.  
 
However, there is a third way! 
What if there was a way where you could raise funds from third parties WITHOUT selling any equity in the 
business and you could set the interest rate yourself on the money you are given? Wouldn’t that be great news 
for you since you’re the one in control of the lending of cash to yourself! 
 
That is a bond.  
 
A bond is essentially just a loan that an investor makes to a borrower (e.g. a company or an individual) at the 
terms of the borrower, NOT the lender. The borrower sets the terms and the investor has to meet those terms 
if they want to loan the money. It’s as simple as that! Often, I like to think about the word ‘bond’ as a binding 
agreement between the lender and the issuer although in this instance, the issuer is the one that is in the 
driving seat when it comes to lending negotiations.  
 
Let’s think about this a bit in terms of the actual underlying mechanics a bit more so that we can really see 
what’s going on under the bonnet.  
How does a bond work? 
Let’s start with this country’s biggest and most famous company… the UK government. Some of you are going 
to be thinking “how on Earth is the UK government going to be considered a company?” Really, it’s not 
(probably) but it’s good to think of it as one. Think about it: they earn money through taxes and other business 
that it does and spends that money on projects, teachers and lots of other interesting stuff that gets them on 
the front cover of the Daily Mail every single day.  
 
Sometimes, the Government doesn’t have the money to e.g. build a new bridge. What the Treasury (i.e. the 
business team of the Government) does is that it goes around to different potential investors and says “We’re 
looking to issue bonds in order to finance this bridge. We need £1 billion, so we’re going to issue 1 million 
bonds where each of you investors pay us £1000 and we’ll pay you back 3% every year for 10 years. When 
the bridge is built after 10 years, we’ll pay you back that £1000 after those 10 years.” 
 
That is a very simple and easy way to understand the bond. That’s all it is and that’s all that it’ll ever be. Bond 
issuing often happens with the Government when they are experiencing a budget deficit i.e. they haven’t got 
enough money coming in to pay out all the projects taking money out of the government. Yes, they could go 
and get one massive loan from Wonga, but why would they? There are keen investors who think the 
Government is a safe bond issuer.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
6 
 
One misconception I’d like to clear up is that Governments are not the only entities that can issue bonds. 
Large firms e.g. big industrialised companies can do so. There’s a lot of legal broo-ha-ha about what 
companies can issue bonds in terms of credit ratings (i.e. how likely they are to pay back the money). The 
bottom line is that governments and firms can issue bonds as much as they please.  
 
Right. Onto the mechanics of bonds! 
Setting up a bond 
Let’s look back at that example earlier.  
➔ Initial payment  £1000 
➔ ‘Interest Rate’  3% 
➔ Duration of bond 10 years 
 
Simple stuff. In ‘Bond Theory’, these terms have unique names: 
➔ Bond Principal £1000 
➔ Coupon Rate  3% 
➔ Maturity Date  10 years (from issue) 
 
Rather than leaving you with the definitions and running away, I’ll cover brief definitions of each term so that 
you’re safe in the knowledge that you know what you are learning about! 
 
➔ The bond principal is the amount of money the lender pays to the borrower. An alternative definition 
you’ll see is the amount of money an issuer is borrowing by the terms of the bond. Sometimes it’s 
known as the ‘nominal value’ i.e. the number that you see printed on the front of the bond when it 
comes out. It’s the same thing, honestly.  
 
There are two parties at play here: the borrower is the bond issuer and the lender is the bondholder. If 
that doesn’t make sense, essentially the bond issuer creates the bond contracts and the bondholder is 
the one who pays into the bond and holds the contract until it expires.  
➔ The coupon rate is simply the interest rate paid on the bond. In this case, it’s 3% of the bond principal 
i.e. £30. Often, this coupon rate is paid annually i.e. every year. Sometimes, it’s paid semi-annually i.e. 
every half a year (6 months). 
➔ When a bond is paid off and all finished, we often say that the bond has matured i.e. grown up. Once a 
bond is matured, the principal’s been paid back to the lender with the coupon payments and everyone 
is happy. Sometimes, we say the bond expires at maturity – literally, it means the exact same thing.  
 
In the olden days, a bond would be issued to the bondholders through a fancy certificate with coupons 
attached on the bottom of the page. Once a year would pass, the bondholders would snip off a coupon and 
travel to the bond issuer to demand coupon payment. That’s where the idea of a coupon essentially comes 
from! 
Picturing the bond’s cash flows 
Really, this is best done with a diagram. Let’s try one.  
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
7 
 
 
The top half of this diagram shows the bond issuer and the bottom half shows the bondholder. The bond 
holder delivers the principal of £1000 to Her His Majesty’s Government. In return, the Treasury pays £30 to the 
bondholder every year for 10 years, earning a nice sum of £30 × 10 = £300. After 10 years have passed, the 
bondholder receives the final coupon of £30 along with the original £1000 he paid in the first place. ‘Cash 
money.’ 
 
I do hope you’ve taken a brief insight into the world of bonds here using a bond from the Government with 
coupons attached. One thing to bear in mind is that Government Bonds (with the UK bonds uniquely named 
‘gilts’) are considered to be the safest investment in finance since it’s unlikely a Government can just run out of 
money to pay off its bondholders – a simple tax hike can fix anything. Don’t however be fooled by the rate of 
return on a bond being coined a ‘risk-free return’ – there is certainly a finite chance that Governments can go 
bankrupt although very few have… yet.  
Bond characteristics 
Due to the nature of bonds and their issuance, each bond will be different from the next in terms of legislation 
and terms & conditions. However, all bonds have the following characteristics (among others) that help 
investors and institutions understand the risk and return potential of a given bond. 
Maturity 
This is the date when the principal of the bond is paid back to bond holders and the company's bond obligation 
ends. Therefore, it defines the lifetime of the bond. A bond's maturity is one of the primary considerations an 
investor weighs against their investment goals and horizon.  
 
Maturity is often classified in three ways: 
➔ Short-term: Bonds that fall into this category tend to mature within one to three years. 
➔ Medium-term: Maturity dates for these types of bonds are normally over ten years. 
➔ Long-term: These bonds generally mature over longer periods of time. 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
8 
Secured/Unsecured 
A bond can be secured or unsecured. A secured bond pledges specific assets to bondholders if the company 
cannot repay the obligation. This asset is also called collateral on the loan. So if the bond issuer defaults, the 
asset is then transferred to the investor. A mortgage-backed security (MBS) is one type of secured bond 
backed by titles to the homes of the borrowers. 
 
Unsecured bonds, on the other hand, are not backed by any collateral. That means the interest and principal 
are only guaranteed by the issuing company. These bonds return little of your investment if the company fails. 
As such, they are much riskier than secured bonds. 
Liquidation Preference 
When a firm goes bankrupt, it repays investors in order of the Capital stack as it liquidates. After a firm sells off 
all its assets, it begins to pay out its investors. Senior debt is debt that must be paid first, followed by junior 
(subordinated) debt. Equity holders get whatever is left, this includes investors holding shares.  
 
Bonds are typically a senior debt hence their low relative risk compared to other derivatives. 
Coupon Rate 
The coupon amount represents interest paid to bondholders, normally annually or semiannually.  
 
To calculate the coupon rate, divide the annual payments by the bond principle: 
𝐶𝑜𝑢𝑝𝑜𝑛 𝑅𝑎𝑡𝑒=𝐴𝑛𝑛𝑢𝑎𝑙 𝐶𝑜𝑢𝑝𝑜𝑛
𝑃𝑎𝑟 𝑉𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒 𝑜𝑓 𝐵𝑜𝑛𝑑 
Tax Status 
While the majority of corporate bonds are taxable investments, some government and municipal bonds are 
tax-exempt, so income and capital gains are not subject to taxation. Tax-exempt bonds normally have a lower 
coupon rate than equivalent taxable bonds. An investor must calculate the tax-equivalent yield to compare the 
return with that of taxable counterparts. 
 
Callability 
Some bonds can be paid off by an issuer before maturity. If a bond has a call provision, it may be paid off at 
earlier dates, at the option of the company, usually at a slight premium to the principle. A company may 
choose to call its bonds if interest rates allow them to borrow at a better rate. Callable bonds also appeal to 
investors as they offer better coupon rates. 
Risks of Bonds 
Bonds are a great way to earn income because they tend to be relatively safe investments. But, just like any 
other investment, they do come with certain risks. Here are some of the most common risks with these 
investments. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
9 
Prepayment Risk 
Prepayment risk is the risk that a given bond issue will be paid off earlier than expected, normally through a 
call provision. This can be bad news for investors because the company only has an incentive to repay the 
obligation early when interest rates have declined substantially. Instead of continuing to hold a high-interest 
investment, investors are left to reinvest funds in a lower interest rate environment. 
Interest Rate Risk 
Interest rates share an inverse relationship with bonds, so when rates rise, bonds tend to fall and vice versa. 
Interest rate risk comes when rates change significantly from what the investor expected. If interest rates 
decline significantly, the investor faces the possibility of prepayment. If interest rates rise, the investor will be 
stuck with an instrument yielding below market rates.  
 
The greater the time to maturity, the greater the interest rate risk an investor bears, because it is harder to 
predict market developments farther out into the future. 
Credit/Default Risk 
Credit or default risk is the risk that interest and principal payments due will not be made as required. When an 
investor buys a bond, they expect that the issuer will make good on the interest and principal payments—just 
like any other creditor. 
 
When an investor looks into corporate bonds, they should weigh out the possibility that the company may 
default on the debt, this includes looking at the bond rating. Safety usually means the company has greater 
operating income and cash flow compared to its debt. If the inverse is true and the debt outweighs available 
cash, the investor may want to stay away. 
Bond Ratings 
Most bonds come with a rating that outlines their quality of credit. That is, how strong the bond is and its ability 
to pay its principal and interest. Ratings are published and are used by investors and professionals to judge 
their worthiness. 
Agencies 
The most commonly cited bond rating agencies are Standard & Poor’s, Moody's Investors Service, and Fitch 
Ratings. They rate a company’s ability to repay its obligations. Ratings range from AAA to Aaa for high-grade 
issues very likely to be repaid to D for issues that are currently in default. 
 
Bonds rated Aaa to Baa or above are called investment grade. This means they are unlikely to default and 
tend to remain stable investments. Bonds rated BB to Ba or below are called junk bonds—default is more 
likely, and they are more speculative and subject to price volatility. 
 
Firms may not have their bonds rated, in which case it is solely up to the investor to judge a firm’s repayment 
ability. Because the rating systems differ for each agency and change from time to time, changes in some 
bond’s rating can happen leading to a significant price change of the bonds being traded.  
 
Grade Description Category 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
10 
Aaa Prime 
Investment-grade 
Unlikely to default 
Tend to remain stable investments 
Aa1 High grade 
Aa2 
Aa3 
A1 Upper medium grade 
A2 
A3 
Baa1 Lower medium grade 
Baa2 
Baa3 
Ba1 Non-investment grade speculative 
Non-investment grade 
AKA high-yield bonds 
AKA junk bonds 
Default is more likely 
More speculative 
Subject to price volatility 
→ Higher coupon rates 
Ba2 
Ba3 
B1 Highly speculative 
B2 
B3 
Caa1 Substantial risks 
Caa2 Extremely speculative 
Caa3 Default imminent with little prospect for recovery 
Ca 
C In default 
 
Bond Yields 
Bond yields are all measures of return. Yield to maturity (YTM) is the measurement most often used, but it is 
important to understand several other yield measurements that are used in certain situations. 
Yield to Maturity (YTM) 
Yield to maturity (YTM) is the most commonly cited yield measurement. It measures what the return on a bond 
is if it is held to maturity and all coupons are reinvested at the YTM rate. Because it is unlikely that coupons will 
be reinvested at the same rate, an investor’s actual return will differ slightly. Calculating YTM by hand is a 
lengthy procedure, and should satisfy the below equation: 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
11 
 
𝐶𝑢𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝐵𝑜𝑛𝑑 𝑃𝑟𝑖𝑐𝑒=∑ 𝐶
(1+𝑦)𝑛+𝐹(1+𝑦)−𝑁
𝑁
𝑛=1
 
 
 
 
 
It is important to note the difference between yield to maturity and the coupon rate. The coupon rate is an early 
interest rate that is agreed upon between the buyer and the seller of the bond at issuance and remains 
constant throughout the life of the bond. The yield however can change as the price of the bond changes. 
Hence, at the moment of issuance (when the bond price has not changed at all), the yield is exactly the same 
as the coupon rate. During the lifetime of the bond (due to reasons explained earlier), the price of the bond will 
change and as a result the yield of the bond will change, as governed by the above equation. However, the 
coupon rate will stay the same, throughout the life of the bond. 
Current Yield 
The current yield can be used to compare the interest income provided by a bond to the dividend income 
provided by a stock. This is calculated by dividing the bond's coupon by the bond’s current price.  
 
Keep in mind, this yield incorporates only the income portion of the return, ignoring possible capital gains or 
losses associated with the principle. As such, this yield is most useful for investors concerned with current 
income only. 
 
𝐶𝑢𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝑌𝑖𝑒𝑙𝑑= 𝐴𝑛𝑛𝑢𝑎𝑙 𝐶𝑜𝑢𝑝𝑜𝑛 𝑖𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒
𝐶𝑢𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝑃𝑟𝑖𝑐𝑒  
Nominal Yield 
The nominal yield on a bond is simply the percentage of interest to be paid on the bond periodically. It is 
calculated by dividing the annual coupon payment by the principal value of the bond. 
 
It is important to note that the nominal yield does not estimate return accurately unless the current bond price 
is the same as its principal value. Therefore, nominal yield is used only for calculating other measures of 
return. 
 
𝑁𝑜𝑚𝑖𝑛𝑎𝑙 𝑌𝑖𝑒𝑙𝑑= 𝐴𝑛𝑛𝑢𝑎𝑙 𝐶𝑜𝑢𝑝𝑜𝑛 𝑖𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒
𝑃𝑟𝑖𝑛𝑐𝑖𝑝𝑎𝑙  
Yield to Call (YTC) 
A callable bond always bears some probability of being called before the maturity date. Investors will realise a 
slightly higher yield if the called bonds are paid off at a premium. An investor in such a bond may wish to know 
𝐶 = 𝑉𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒 𝑜𝑓 𝐶𝑜𝑢𝑝𝑜𝑛 𝑃𝑎𝑦𝑚𝑒𝑛𝑡 
𝐹 = 𝑃𝑎𝑟 𝑉𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒 𝑜𝑓 𝐵𝑜𝑛𝑑 
𝑁 = 𝑁𝑢𝑚𝑏𝑒𝑟 𝑜𝑓 𝐶𝑜𝑚𝑝𝑜𝑢𝑛𝑑𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝑃𝑒𝑟𝑖𝑜𝑑𝑠 
𝑦 = 𝑌𝑖𝑒𝑙𝑑 𝑡𝑜 𝑀𝑎𝑡𝑢𝑟𝑖𝑡𝑦 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
12 
what yield will be realised if the bond is called at a particular call date, to determine whether the prepayment 
risk is worthwhile.  
(Annual) Realised Yield (ARY) 
The realised yield of a bond should be calculated if an investor plans to hold a bond only for a certain period of 
time, rather than to maturity. In this case, the investor will sell the bond, and this projected future bond price 
must be estimated for the calculation. Because future prices are hard to predict, this yield measurement is only 
an estimation of return.  
 
Bond Spreads 
The yield curve 
For a bond that has a lifetime of 1 year after issue, the yield is quite low. This is because the bond doesn’t last 
very long so the issuer has little incentive to compensate the bondholder for a year’s worth of debt. For 
successively longer bond lifetimes, the yields tend to increase. We can plot hypothetical bond yields for the UK 
Gilt as an example of this: 
 
 
These types of yield curves are known as normal yield curves. You would expect most yield curves to curve 
upwards since the longer you hold a bond, the greater the return and hence the greater the YTM. 
 
Sometimes, this might not be the case. Instead, the yield curve could flip, giving instead an inverted yield 
curve: 
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
13 
 
 
Inverted yield curves indicate recessions. Investors want to be guaranteed a safe return on their investment in 
bonds so they’ll buy long term bonds if they believe a recession is coming. Even if a recession does happen, 
the central banks might have to lower interest rates and that would make the current long-term bonds even 
more desirable due to their higher coupon rate. To compensate for this, the central bank would have to 
negotiate higher yields in a bid to ensure people buy up their bonds and the government has enough money 
on its hands to deal with an oncoming recession, hence the inverted yield curve.  
 
Because Governments can easily pay off any bonds it issues owing to its taxation system, corporations have 
to entice investors with yields much higher than those that the Government is offering. As a result, corporation 
bonds exist on a yield curve much higher than treasury bonds. For this reason, we also name treasury bonds 
‘benchmark yield curves’: 
 
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
14 
Yield spreads 
Corporate bonds versus treasury bonds can cause a few questions including the way we compare them. 
Investors tend to use two metrics to solve this problem: G-spreads and Z-Spreads. Let’s discuss these in turn 
so we know what they mean.  
G-spread 
G-Spreads are the simpler ones to explain. Essentially, you are measuring the difference in yields between the 
corporate bond you are looking at and a corresponding treasury bond for a given duration before expiry. The G 
in G-spread simply stands for Government spread. Often, this spread can be written can be written as: 
 
G-spread = Yc – Yg 
 
where Yc is the corporate bond yield and Yg is the government (i.e. treasury) yield.  
 
Sometimes, it is possible that the maturity of a corporate bond doesn’t match any government bonds that you 
find. In this case, you would have to use linear interpolation to get around the problem in finding a value for Yg 
(as you shall see in the following example below).  
 
Take a corporate bond with a 12 year duration until maturity and two U.S. T-bills as treasury bonds: 
 
 
To find the YTM of the U.S. T-bill at 12 years, we would need to do some linear interpolation: 
 
3.5 + 2
5×0.8 = 3.82% 
 
Which gives us a hypothetical YTM of the U.S. T-bill for a 12-year lifetime of 3.82% assuming that the YTM of 
T-bills can be modelled linearly between those two points.  
 
This then helps to calculate the G-Spread of the corporate bond: 
 
5.2 – 3.82 = 1.38% 
Z-spread 
Remember calculating present values of equities in lecture 3? You can do the same with bonds too. The 
trouble is that the numbers we might use as discount rates will vary as the rate of return changes in line with 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
15 
the interest rates offered by treasury bonds. To solve this, we might match the discount rates with the YTM of 
zero-coupon bonds (known as spot curves). 
 
However, this can lead to another problem. Sometimes, the spot rates of e.g. T-bills may misprice a bond such 
that the present value of a bond is far higher than the market rate. Take the current spot rates of T-bills from 
the graph presented below: 
 
 
 
Say that there existed a 4-year 10% coupon rate £100 corporate bond which was trading on the market for 
£127. If we used the current spot rates above to calculate the present value for the bond, we’d get something 
completely different: 
𝑃𝑉 = 10
1+1%+ 10
(1+1.5%)2+ 10
(1+1.7%)3+ 10
(1+1.8%)4=£131.53 
 
Clearly, the present value of the bond here is too high using the spot rates of the T-bills. If we, however, 
boosted these spot rates artificially so that the discount rate was higher, we might end up closer to the £127 
that we’re looking to match.  
 
Let’s try adding a constant 1% (i.e. 100 bps) to each of the spot rates: 
 
 
You can see that there’s a constant shift of 1% for all the spot rates.  
Let’s try again: 
 
𝑃𝑉 = 10
1+2%+ 10
(1+2.5%)2+ 10
(1+2.7%)3+ 10
(1+2.8%)4=£127.05 
 
Now, we’ve got a much better discount rate that tells us the market expects a rate of return of 1% more than 
each of the treasury spot rates! 
 
Looking at this more generally, we can write: 
 
𝑃𝑉 = 10
1+𝑟+𝑍+ 10
(1+𝑟+𝑍)2+ 10
(1+𝑟+𝑍)3+ 10
(1+𝑟+𝑍)4 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
16 
 
where r is the spot rate and Z is the constant we added to the spot-rate. This is the Z-spread.  
Professionally, it is referred to as the spread that must be added to each spot interest rate to cause the present 
value of the bond cash flows to equal the bond’s price. 
 
Now you can see why it makes sense.  
N.B. You won’t be expected to calculate Z-spreads in the exam at all!  
It cannot be solved algebraically but instead through numerical methods i.e. trial and error.  
Why Bonds over Equities 
In general, investing in debt is relatively safer than investing in equity, and in turn the returns differ 
considerably. That’s because debtholders have priority over shareholders in the capital stack, that is if a 
company goes bankrupt most if not all funds accrued through the liquidation of assets will go to repaying debt 
holders, including bond holders, leaving little not no reimbursement for the equity partners, share holders for 
example. 
 
In terms of risk, bonds from developed governments, Treasury bonds, are considered risk-free as the entire 
economy of the country who issued the bonds would have to collapse and the nation declare bankruptcy for 
the bonds to default. While not exactly yielding high returns, in 2020, a 30-year US treasury bond yielded an 
interest rate of about 1.7%, there are cases where bonds yield much higher rates and are a more attractive 
investment. For example the current yield of the same 30 year US treasury bond is about 3.79%, over double 
that of 2020. This is because of the inflation crisis we are facing and how that translates to rising interest rates. 
 
When a government wants to bring down or control inflation they raise interest rates, due to Covid, the energy 
crisis, and the Russo-Ukrainian war 2022 has seen unprecedented soars in inflation across the globe with 
most countries being hit regardless of their economic size or position. In an attempt to control this inflation 
governments, most notably the US, UK, EU, and Brazil, have hiked their interest rates by massive amounts 
almost monthly leading to some of the highest interest rates ever. 
 
Bond prices are inversely proportional to interest rates, when interest rates rise bond prices drop. That is why 
we are seeing such unusually high yields in the bond market, as discussed in previous sections current yield 
would rise if bond prices drop. Aside from making bond prices low and yields high, rising interest rates raise 
the cost of capital so other investment tools become less attractive due to rising costs and higher risks. 
 
Occasionally, yields can spike to very high levels due to the underlying bond price crashing. This happens at 
times of uncertainty and instability in the market. Most recently the UK’s 10-year Gilts saw yields spike 
tremendously due to the crashing of the pound in late September and the new government's controversial tax 
cut plans. 
More predictable returns 
Stocks will outperform bonds in the long run. However, bonds outperform stocks at certain times in the 
economic cycle. It’s not unusual for stocks to lose 10% or more in a year, so when bonds make up a portion of 
a portfolio, they can help smooth out the bumps when a recession comes along.  
 
Also, in certain life situations, people may need security and predictability. Retirees, for instance, often rely on 
the predictable income generated by bonds. If your portfolio consisted solely of stocks, it would be quite 
disappointing to retire two years into a bear market. By owning bonds, retirees can predict with a greater 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
17 
degree of certainty how much income they’ll have in their later years. An investor who still has many years until 
retirement has plenty of time to make up for any losses from periods of decline in equities. 
Better Than the Bank 
The interest rates on bonds are typically greater than the deposit rates paid by banks on savings accounts. As 
a result, if you are saving and you don’t need the money in the short term (in a year or less), bonds will give 
you a relatively better return without posing too much risk. 
Bonds from stable governments and large corporations, Apple, Google, Amazon, are the best investment tools 
for capital preservation. However, keep in mind that although bonds are safer (as a rule) that doesn’t mean 
they are all completely safe. There are also very risky bonds, which are known as junk bonds. 
Asset Backed Securities 
Asset-backed securities allow their issuers to raise cash. The underlying assets of an ABS are often illiquid 
and can't be sold on their own due to their risk. So, pooling assets together and creating a financial instrument 
out of them—a process called securitization—allows the issuer to make illiquid assets marketable to investors. 
The underlying cash flow obligations can come from many different industries and types, usually a security is 
made up of only one family of underlying assets. 
Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLO) 
A Collateralized Loan Obligation (CLO) is a type of security that allows investors to purchase an interest in a 
diversified portfolio of Corporate loans. The company selling the CLO will purchase a large number of 
corporate loans from borrowers such as private companies and private equity firms, and will then package 
those loans into a single CLO security.  
Collateralized Mortgage Obligation 
Collateralized mortgage obligations consist of several tranches, or groups of mortgages, organised by their risk 
profiles. The trenches typically have different principal balances, interest rates, maturity dates, and potential of 
repayment defaults. Collateralized mortgage obligations are sensitive to interest rate changes as well as to 
changes in economic conditions, such as foreclosure rates, refinance rates, and the rates at which properties 
are sold. Each tranche has a different maturity date and size and bonds with monthly coupons are issued 
against it funded by the mortgage repayments made on the underlying asset.  
The 2008 Financial Crisis and CMOs 
CMOs were complex and involved many different mortgage types. For many reasons, investors focused on the 
income streams offered by CMOs rather than the health of the underlying mortgages themselves. As a result, 
many investors purchased CMOs full of subprime mortgages, adjustable-rate mortgages, mortgages held by 
borrowers whose income wasn't verified during the application process, and other risky mortgages with high 
risks of default. 
 
The use of CMOs has been criticised as a precipitating factor in the 2007-2008 financial crisis. Rising housing 
prices made mortgages look like fail-proof investments, enticing investors to buy CMOs and other Mortgage 
backed securities, but market and economic conditions led to a rise in foreclosures and payment risks that 
financial models did not predict. The aftermath of the global financial crisis resulted in increased regulations for 
mortgage-backed securities. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
18 
Collateralized Bond Obligation 
A collateralized Bond Obligation (CBO) is an investment-grade bond that is backed by a pool of junk bonds. 
Because the pool includes several types of credit quality bonds together from multiple issuers, they offer 
enough diversification to be structured as "investment grade." 
The role of Bonds in a Portfolio 
Investors include bonds in their investment portfolios for a range of reasons some of which we have already 
briefly touched on… 
Income generation 
Bonds provide investors with a source of income in the form of coupon payments. The investor can use the 
income generated by their investments for spending or reinvestment. Shares also provide income in the form 
of dividends: however, such payments are less certain and tend to be less than bond coupons. 
Capital preservation 
Unlike stocks, the principal value of a bond is returned to the investor in full at maturity. This can make bonds 
attractive to risk-averse investors who are concerned about losing their capital. 
Capital appreciation 
Although bonds are often viewed as a capital preservation tool, they also offer opportunities for capital 
appreciation. This occurs when investors take advantage of rising bond prices by selling their holdings prior to 
maturity on the secondary market. This is often referred to as investing for total return and is one of the more 
popular bond investment strategies. 
Hedge against economic slowdown 
While investors in stocks typically do not welcome a slowdown in economic growth, it can be a good thing for 
bond investors. This is because slower growth usually leads to lower inflation, which makes bond income more 
attractive. An economic slowdown may also be negative for company profits and stock market returns, adding 
to the attractiveness of bond income during such a time. 
Diversification 
Bonds are considered a defensive asset class because they are typically less volatile than some other asset 
classes such as stocks. Many investors include bonds in their portfolio as a source of diversification to help 
reduce volatility and overall portfolio risk. 
Credit Default Swaps 
Credit default swaps are commonly referred to as CDS and are critical to the current climate of fixed income 
and bond trading in particular. They were created in 1994 as a means of insurance, similar to how an 
insurance plan works, in the case of an unlikely event, in this case a credit default.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
19 
How do credit default swaps work? 
A credit default swap works in an inverse manner to a bond. When you buy a bond, you take on the risk that 
the company issuing the bond is unable to pay you back the money you lent them (called a default). So as a 
reward for taking this risk you receive payments until the bond matures and you hopefully receive your 
principal back. 
 
If you buy a CDS, you pay money to the seller, such that if the company issuing the bond defaults on its debt, 
then you get paid the principal. You are in essence paying someone for taking on the risk, in other words 
swapping the risk away for annual payments. Let’s look at an example of what happens when the underlying 
company defaults: 
 
Investment banks usually issue CDSs and so in this scenario, we are buying a CDS on ABC debt, with a 
principal of £1000 with a 3% spread and 10-year maturity. This means that every year we (the buyer) must pay 
the seller 3% on the principal (£30). However, suppose that in year 8, ABC defaults on its debt. Then by the 
agreement of a CDS contract, the investment bank must now pay us the principal value on the CDS and so we 
receive £1000 and the CDS ends. 
 
 
 
 
Therefore, the profit gained from this trade is 7 x -£30 + £1000 = £790.  
 
However, suppose ABC did not default on its debt, then we would have had to make the annual payments 
every year until the CDS matures. This would mean our final profit would be 10 x -£30 = -£300, since the CDS 
has 10 years till maturity. 
Why are CDSs useful? 
CDS allow the buyer to offload default risk from a bond and in exchange they make payments to the seller of 
the CDS. CDS are usually first issued by an investment bank and are then bought by buy-side firms for a 
multitude of reasons. The main reason why CDS were invented is to hedge your credit portfolio against the risk 
TOTAL return = 7 × -£30 + £1000 = £790 (on principal of £1000) 
NOTE £1000 is the 
principal value 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
20 
of the underlying party defaulting on their debt. CDS has similar characteristics to bonds in the sense that they 
have a payment, principal value and maturity.  
 
CDS are typically priced on spread, the amount the buyer pays to the seller for protection or insurance against 
default. The buyer then makes this payment usually on an annual basis to the seller as long as the underlying 
company can still pay off its interest. However, in the event the company defaults on its debt, then the seller 
must pay the buyer the principal value of the debt. 
 
CDSs can also be used as a form of speculation on how companies will perform in the future. If you believe a 
company is performing well and likely to meet all their debt obligations, then you can sell a CDS and receive 
the annual payments. However, if you believe a company is going to default on its debt, then you can buy a 
CDS and hope that they do default. Then you will receive the principal and make a net profit. 
 
In some cases, CDSs can be used for arbitrage as a bond and CDS with the same maturity and on the same 
principal value should in theory be trading with the same spread as the default risk is the same. However, 
there are a multitude of reasons why the spread on the CDS won’t be equal to the spread on a bond. This is 
mainly due to the effects of supply and demand as there may be alternative reasons for trading each 
instrument resulting in the spreads not converging to the same value. 
What sort of impact have CDSs had on markets? 
The most notorious impact CDSs have had on the market was probably its influence during the 2008 Financial 
Crash. As discussed earlier, during 2008 the market for mortgage-backed securities was incredibly inflated 
and a bubble formed as investors were enticed by the income streams on offer without properly scrutinizing 
the health of the underlying mortgages. A few market players, most famously Michael Burry, noticed the 
inaccurate credit rating of these mortgages and decided to buy credit default swaps, essentially betting that 
these homeowners would be unable to keep up with their mortgage payments. Eventually, some investment 
banks were willing to take the other side of this trade (selling the CDS) as they thought the probability of 
default on these mortgages was tiny. 
 
However, when the housing bubble burst and it was realised that homeowners did not have the income to 
sustain these mortgages and had to default. This was essentially the straw that broke the camel’s back, and 
led to a wave of payments from CDS sellers (investment banks) to CDS buyers which many banks were 
unable to afford. The biggest culprit of this was Lehman Brothers which owed $600 billion in debt of which two-
thirds were CDSs. The American government had to intervene to bail them out, however the damage was 
already done and a lot of banks around the world ended up going bankrupt and ceased to exist. 
 
In 2008, the notional value of the CDS market was a lot larger than others, sitting around $57 trillion in June 
2008 and is currently around $3.8 trillion, showing a massive drop-off in interest from investors. However, 
CDSs are still a popular way for large asset managers to hedge some of the risk in their massive bond 
portfolios.

documents.title: Lecture notes 2025(12)
documents.text:
Analysis I — Lecture Notes
Marie-Amélie Lawn mlawn@ic.ac.uk
Oﬃce hour: Thursday 13:00–14:00 (Hx1 408)
1 Numbers: a few more results
You saw in IUM that, starting from the Peano axioms, we takeN as the basic number system.
From None can construct the integers and rationals via equivalence classes: deﬁneZ = (N×N)/∼
with (a, b)∼ (c, d) ⇐⇒ a + d = b + c, and deﬁne Q ={(a, b)∈ Z× (Z\{ 0})}/∼ with
(a, b)∼ (c, d) ⇐⇒ ad = bc. You also saw how to axiomatiseR as a complete ordered ﬁeld.
In this course we will revisitR from a constructive viewpoint: we will build it fromQ using
sequences (Cauchy completion) and brieﬂy relate this to the alternative approach via Dedekind
cuts. For this purpose we introduce and recall a few concepts about numbers.
Countability. To compare these number systems we need a notion of size. Countability
provides it: N, Z, and Q are countable (they admit explicit listings), whereas(0, 1)⊂ R is
uncountable (Cantor’s diagonal argument, discussed below). Practically, this means there
are far more reals than rationals. Rationals can approximate values like
√
2 or non-repeating
decimals arbitrarily closely but need not equal them; the reals are obtained by adding all such
missing limits so that no gaps remain. Equivalently—this is the completeness property you have
met—every bounded set of real numbers has a least upper bound, a property that fails inQ.
Deﬁnition 1.1(Countably inﬁnite set). A set S is countably inﬁniteiﬀ there exists a bijection
f : N→ S.
Intuitively, this means that iff is bijective, we can listS as
S ={s0, s1, s2, . . .}, s n := f (n), s i̸= sj (i̸= j).
Proposition 1.2. If S⊆ N is inﬁnite, thenS is countably inﬁnite.
Proof. By the well–ordering principle (every nonempty subset ofN has a least element), deﬁne
f : N→ S recursively by
f (0) = min S, f (n) = min
(
S\{ f (0), . . . , f(n− 1)}
)
(n≥ 1).
Because S is inﬁnite, the setS\{ f (0), . . . , f(n− 1)} is nonempty for anyn, so f is well deﬁned.
Injectivity. At each step we remove the previously chosen values, sof (0) < f (1) < f (2) <··· ;
hence the values are all distinct.
Surjectivity. Suppose some element ofS is not in the image off. Let s = min
(
S\ Im(f )
)
.
Pick n with f (n) < s as large as possible (suchn exists since f (0)≤ s and the f (k) increase).
By the deﬁnition off (n + 1) as theleast element of S not yet chosen and larger thanf (n), we
must have f (n + 1)≤ s. Since s is the least missed element,f (n + 1) cannot be < s, hence
f (n + 1) = s, a contradiction. Therefore no element is missed.
Thus f is a bijectionN→ S, so S is countably inﬁnite.
Proposition 1.3(Characterisations for inﬁnite sets). Let S be inﬁnite. Then the following are
equivalent:
1
(1) S is countably inﬁnite (i.e. there exists a bijectionN→ S);
(2) there exists an injectioni : S→ N.
Proof. (1)⇒ (2). If f : N→ S is a bijection, then its inversef−1 : S→ N is injective (indeed
bijective).
(2)⇒ (1). Assume i : S→ N is injective andS is inﬁnite. Let A := i(S)⊆ N be the image
of i. Then i : S→ A is surjective by deﬁnition of image and injective by hypothesis; hence
i : S−→ A is a bijection. (1.1)
Since i is injective andS is inﬁnite, A is inﬁnite. Because A⊆ N and N is well ordered, every
nonempty subset ofA has a least element. Deﬁne a sequence(an)n∈N recursively by
a0 = min A, a n+1 = min
(
A\{ a0, . . . , an}
)
(n≥ 0).
This is well deﬁned (the set remains nonempty becauseA is inﬁnite). By construction,
a0 < a 1 < a 2 <··· ,
and the map
h : N−→ A, h (n) = an,
is bijective; record this as
h : N→ A is a bijection. (1.2)
Finally, combining (1.1) and (1.2), we obtain a bijection
N h−−→A i−1
−−−→S,
so S is countably inﬁnite. This proves(2)⇒ (1).
Proposition 1.4. Z is countably inﬁnite.
Proof. Deﬁne f : N→ Z by
f (0) = 0 , f (2k− 1) = k, f (2k) =− k (k≥ 1).
This listsZ as 0, 1,−1, 2,−2, 3,−3, . . .. Each integer appears exactly once, sof is a bijection.
Proposition 1.5. The set of nonnegative rationalsQ≥0 is countably inﬁnite.
Proof. First, we show thatN2 is countable. For this purpose we use a diagonal listing in order
of p + q: (0, 0), (0, 1), (1, 0), (0, 2), (1, 1), (2, 0), . . .. Let
C ={(p, q)∈ N× N : q≥ 1, gcd(p, q) = 1}∪{ (0, 1)}.
Then ψ : C→ Q≥0, ψ(p, q) = p/q, is a bijection (unique reduced form withq≥ 1, 0 = (0 , 1)).
Since C⊆ N2 and N2 is countable, Q≥0 is countable.
Remark 1.6. Hence Q is countably inﬁnite (account for signs).
Proposition 1.7. The open interval(0, 1) is uncountable.
2
Cantor’s diagonal argument.Suppose, for contradiction, that(0, 1) is countable. Then we can
list all its elements
x1, x2, x3, . . .
and write eachxn in decimal form
xn = 0, dn1dn2dn3 . . . , d nk∈{ 0, 1, . . . ,9}.
For deﬁniteness, choose for eachxn the decimal expansion that doesnot end with an inﬁnite
tail of 9’s (this is always possible since, e.g.,0.5000 . . . is preferred to0.4999 . . .).
Now build a new numbery∈ (0, 1) by deﬁning itsk-th digit ak from the diagonal digitsdkk:
ak =
{
1, if dkk̸= 1,
2, if dkk = 1.
Set y := 0.a1a2a3 . . .. By construction ak∈{ 1, 2} for all k, so y has a valid decimal expansion
that does not end in9’s and hence represents a unique number in(0, 1).
For eachn, the n-th digit ofy is an̸= dnn, so y diﬀers from xn in the n-th decimal place.
Therefore y̸= xn for all n, which contradicts the assumption that(0, 1) was completely listed
by (xn)n∈N.
Hence (0, 1) is uncountable.
Remark 1.8. The same proof shows thatR is uncountable.
Completeness.
Deﬁnition 1.9(Bounds). Let X be an ordered set. A nonempty setS⊆ X is bounded above
(resp. bounded below) if there existsB∈ X such that x≤ B (resp. x≥ B) for allx∈ S. Any
such B is called anupper bound(resp. lower bound) for S.
Remark 1.10. Upper or lower bounds, when they exist, are not unique and need not belong toS;
they may also fail to exist. For example, the setS :={ n∈ N : n is positive and even}⊆ N is
bounded below (e.g. by0, and indeed by any negative real number), but it has no upper bound
in R.
Deﬁnition 1.11 (Supremum/inﬁmum). Let X be an ordered set, S⊆ X be nonempty. A
number s∈ X is aleast upper boundor supremum of S (resp. agreatest lower boundor inﬁmum)
if
(i) s is an upper bound (resp. a lower bound) forS, and
(ii) for every B < s (resp. every B > s ), the numberB is not an upper bound (resp. not a
lower bound) forS.
In particular, when it exists, the supremum (and inﬁmum) is unique.
Deﬁnition 1.12 (Completeness). An ordered ﬁeld F is complete if every nonempty subset
S⊆ F that is bounded above has a supremumsup S lying in F.
This axiom is a key property distinguishingR from Q. For example, the set
A ={ x∈ Q : x2 < 2}
is nonempty and bounded above inQ (e.g. by 3/2), but it has no supremumin Q (its least
upper bound inR is
√
2 /∈ Q).
In the IUM you saw that the real numbersR can be deﬁned axiomatically (up to isomorphism)
as the unique ordered ﬁeld which is also complete. Completeness is one of the most important
concepts in analysis and we recall some consequences without proofs. We will see later that it is
equivalent to the concept of Cauchy completeness, once we we have deﬁned Cauchy sequences.
3
Proposition 1.13 (Archimedean property). For all x, y∈ R with x > 0, there existsn∈ N
such that nx > y.
There are several equivalent formulations that are useful in diﬀerent contexts.
Corollary 1.14. The following are equivalent to the Archimedean property:
(a) For every x∈ R there exists n∈ N such that n > x.
(b) For every x∈ R with x > 0 there exists n∈ N such that 0 < 1
n < x.
Proposition 1.15(Q is dense inR). For all x, y∈ R with x < y, there existsr∈ Q such that
x < r < y .
Example 1.16. Let A1 ={ x∈ R : 1 < x < 2}. Then 2 is an upper bound and1 is a lower
bound. We claimsup A1 = 2 and inf A1 = 1.
For the supremum, suppose there is an upper boundM with 1 < M < 2. By Proposition 1.15,
there exists r∈ Q with M < r < 2. Then r∈ A1, contradicting thatM is an upper bound.
Hence sup A1 = 2. The argument for the inﬁmum is analogous.
2 Sequences
Deﬁnition 2.1. A sequence is a mapf : N→ R.
Equivalently, a sequence is an inﬁnite list of real numbers
a1, a2, a3, . . . ,
with a real numberan for eachn∈ N (starting at1). We denote a sequence by(an), or sometimes
just by an.
Example 2.2. 1. Specifying the nth term: an = n2 gives 1, 4, 9, 16, . . ..
2. an = a + nb with a, b∈ R is anarithmetic sequence.
3. an = aqn with a, q∈ R is ageometric sequence.
4. Recursive deﬁnition: a1 = 1, a2 = 2, and an+2 = an + an+1 gives theFibonacci sequence
1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, . . . .
5. If an = 0 for n even and an = 1 for n odd, we get0, 1, 0, 1, 0, . . ..
6. If an is the nth prime, we get2, 3, 5, 7, 11, . . ..
7. For a ﬁxedC∈ R, an≡ C for all n is aconstant sequence: C, C, C, . . .
We will focus on thelong-term behaviour of sequences. The ﬁrst few terms never matter;
what counts is what happenseventually. Before that, we recall some facts about absolute value.
4
2.1 The triangle inequality
Recall that the distance between two real numbersx and y is|x− y|, where the vertical bars|·|
denote absolute value:|w| = w if w≥ 0 and|w| =−w if w < 0.
Proposition 2.3(Triangle inequality). For all real numbersx, y, z we have
|x− y|≤| x− z| +|z− y|.
Proof. Setting z = 0 and replacing y by−y reduces the claim to
|x + y|≤| x| +|y|. (2.1)
If x and y have the same sign then(2.1) is an equality. Ifx≥ 0 and y≤ 0 then y≤ x + y≤ x,
so|x + y|≤ max(|x|,|y|)≤| x| +|y|.
A useful corollary is:
Corollary 2.4. For all x, y∈ R we have
⏐⏐|x|−| y|
⏐⏐≤| x− y|.
Proof. Assume|x| ≥ |y|. From (2.1) with x ↦→ x− y we get|x| ≤ |x− y| +|y|, hence
|x|−| y|≤| x− y|.
2.2 Convergent sequences
We ﬁrst formalise “eventually close to a ﬁxed value”.

every ε > 0 there exists N∈ N such that for alln≥ N,

We writean→ L or limn→∞ an = L. If (an) converges, it is aconvergent sequence, and L is its
limit.
To showan→ L you must handleevery ε > 0; to showan ↛ L it suﬃces to ﬁnd asingle

If a sequence (an) tends to a limitL we sometimes writean→ L or limn→∞ an = L. A
sequence isconvergent if it has a limit inR; otherwise it isdivergent.
An important stability fact: changing ﬁnitely many terms does not aﬀect convergence.
Deﬁnition 2.7. Let (an) be a sequence andS∈ N. The shift of (an) by S is bn := an+S.
Lemma 2.8. Let (an) be a sequence,L∈ R, and S∈ N. Then an→ L iﬀ an+S→ L.
5
Proof. If an → L, then for any ε > 0 there is N so that|an− L| < ε for n≥ N. Then
|an+S− L| < ε for n≥ N. The converse is similar: if |an+S− L| < ε for n≥ N, then
|an− L| < ε for n≥ N + S.
Because∞ is not a real number, we deﬁne separately what it means totend to inﬁnity.
Deﬁnition 2.9. Let (an) be a sequence.
• For R∈ R, we say(an) is eventually bigger than R if there existsN such that an > R for
all n≥ N.
• We sayan→∞ if for everyR∈ R, (an) is eventually bigger thanR.
• Similarly, an→−∞ if for everyR∈ R, (an) is eventually less thanR.

We also need boundedness.
Deﬁnition 2.11. Let (an) be a sequence. We say:
• (an) is bounded aboveif there existsB∈ R with an≤ B for all n.
• (an) is bounded belowif there existsC∈ R with an≥ C for all n.
• (an) is bounded if there existsD∈ R with|an|≤ D for all n.
Clearly, bounded⇐⇒ bounded above and below.
Example 2.12. Let an = 0 if n is odd andan = n if n is even: 0, 2, 0, 4, 0, . . .. Then (an) is
not bounded above, but it also does not tend to∞ (it is not eventually> 0).
2.3 Some basic results on convergent sequences

2.5 Monotonic sequences
Deﬁnition 2.25. We say a sequence(an) is...
1. ... strictly increasingif an < an+1 for all n.
2. ... increasing if an≤ an+1 for all n.
3. ... strictly decreasingif an > an+1 for all n.
4. ... decreasing if an≥ an+1 for all n.
5. ... monotonic if it is increasing or decreasing.
Warning: you’ll ﬁnd some texts in which monotonic meansstrictly increasing or strictly
decreasing.
If a sequence(an) is increasing, it need not converge — for example, it could tend to inﬁnity.
If it is increasing and bounded above, then it must converge : we will see what the limit is.
Proposition 2.26. Let (an) be increasing and bounded above. Then(an) is convergent and
an→ sup{an|n∈ N}.
Proof. Consider the set S ={a1, a2, ...} of all the terms in(an). This set is bounded above
(because the sequence is bounded above) so it has a supremums. We claim thatan→ s.
Pick anyϵ > 0. Then s− ϵ is strictly less thans, so it is not an upper bound forS. So there
exists an N such that aN > s− ϵ. Then for anyn≥ N we have
s− ϵ < aN≤ an≤ s
because (an) is increasing, so for alln≥ N we have|an− s| < ϵ. Thus an→ s.
Similarly we can that a sequence which is decreasing and bounded below converges to its
inﬁmum.
Putting these two results together gives:
Theorem 2.27. Any bounded monotonic sequence is convergent.
This result is nice as you can use it to prove that a sequence is convergent without knowing
in advance what the limit is.

for all n. We will show thatan is bounded above by3. Indeed assume thatan < 33 for some n,

Then since an is monotonic and bounded, it is convergent.

2.6 Subsequences
Informally, a subsequence of(an) is a sequence obtained by missing out some of the terms
of (an). So 3, 4, 5, . . . is a subsequence of1, 2, 3, 4, 5, . . . and 1, 1, 1, 1, . . . is a subsequence of
1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, . . .. It is "a sequence inside of a sequence".
Deﬁnition 2.29. Let (an) be a sequence. Asubsequence of (an) is a sequence
af (1), af (2), af (3), ...
where f : N→ N is a strictly increasing function, i.e. such that f (n) > f (m) whenever
n > m.
Example 2.30.
1. Let an = n, and letf : N→ N be the functionf (n) = n2. Then the subsequence(af (n))
is a1, a4, a9, . . ., that is1, 4, 9, 16, . . ..
2. Let an = cos(n), and letf (n) = 2 n. Then the subsequence(af (n)) is a2, a4, a6, . . .which
is cos(2), cos(4), cos(6), . . ..
Another notation for subsequences is to writeni for f (i), so n1 < n 2 < n 3 <··· is a strictly
increasing sequence of integers, and the corresponding subsequence(ani) is
an1, an2, an3, ...
Proposition 2.31. Suppose an→ L. Then any subsequence of(an) tends to L too.
Proof. Let f : N→ N be strictly increasing, so that(af (n)) is a subsequence. Sincean converges,
for all ϵ > 0, there exists an integerN such that for all integersn≥ N,|an− L| < ϵ. But
N≤ f (N )≤ f (n) as f is stricly increasing. Hence|af (n)− L| < ϵ.
A similar result holds for a sequence tending to±∞ (exercise).
Corollary 2.32. A sequence having subsequences converging to two diﬀerent limits is not
convergent.
This gives an easy way to show that some sequences are divergent.
Example 2.33. Let an = (−1)n. If we extract the even terms we get the subsequence(a2n)
which is 1, 1, 1, ..., and if we extact the odd terms we get the subsequence(a2n−1) which is
−1,−1,−1, . . .. Since the ﬁrst subsequence tends to 1 and the second subsequence tends to−1
the sequence (an) cannot be convergent.
Proposition 2.34. Any sequence has a monotonic subsequence.
Proof. Consider the set of integersS ={n|am > an,∀m > n}. We have two cases.
Case 1: The setS ={n1 < n 2 . . .} is inﬁnite: in fact ifS is inﬁnite you can pick the smallest
element n1 in the set. By deﬁnitionam > an1 for all m > n 1. Among these integersm, pick
the next elementn2 > n 1 in the set. Thenak > an2 > an1 for all integersk > n 2. Iterating the
process we get a strictly increasing sequencean1 < an2 < . . ..
Case 2: S is ﬁnite. Then there exists an integerN such that no integern > N is in S. Pick
n1 > N. Since n1 is not inS, there exists an integern2, such thatn2 > n 1, but an2≤ an1. Now
since n2 is not inS either, there exists an integern3 > n 2, such thatan3≤ an2≤ an1. Iterating
the process we get a decreasing sequencean1≥ an2≥ an3≥ . . ..
Note that it is certainly not true that any sequence contains an increasing subsequence, for
example if (an) is strictly decreasing then this is not possible. The statement is that either we
can ﬁnd a increasing subsequence,or we can ﬁnd a decreasing subsequence.
10
Theorem 2.35. (Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem) A bounded sequence has a convergent subse-
quence.
Proof. Pick a monotonic subsequence, which you can do by Proposition 2.34. It is monotonic
and bounded, hence convergent by Theorem 2.27.
Example 2.36. Consider the sequence an = sin(n). This sequence is fairly erratic, but the
terms are never bigger than 1 or less than−1. Hence sin n is bounded by1. Therefore sin n has
a convergent subsequence. It is hard to write this subsequence explicitly. We can taken1 = 1,
i.e a1 = sin 1, and ni =any integer k such that k≥ ni, and sin k≥ sin ni−1. Note that we have
not prove that it is always possible to ﬁnd such an integerk. It is, but the proof is beyond the
scope of this course.
2.7 Cauchy sequences
We will now study a way to look at convergent sequences without mentioning their limits using
the idea of aCauchy sequence.
Deﬁnition 2.37. A sequence (an) is Cauchy if for any ϵ > 0 there exists an N such that
m, n≥ N implies that|am− an| < ϵ.
This deﬁnition is named after the French mathematician Augustin-Louis Cauchy.
So in a Cauchy sequence, if you go far enough along the sequence then any two terms are
within ϵ of each other.
We are now going to prove some facts about Cauchy sequences.
Proposition 2.38. A Cauchy sequence is bounded.
Proof. Let (an) be Cauchy and letϵ = 1. Then by deﬁnition there is anN such that m, n≥ N
implies|am− an|≤ 1. In particular if we setm = N then we must have|an− aN|≤ 1 for all
n≥ N. Hence
|an|≤| aN| + 1.
if n≥ N. Thus
|an|≤ max{|a1|, . . . ,|aN−1|,|aN| + 1}
for all n.
Lemma 2.39. Let (an) be a convergent sequence. Then(an) is Cauchy.
Proof. Let L be the limit of(an). Let ϵ > 0, and setϵ′ = ϵ/2. Then there is someN such that
if n≥ N then|an− L| < ϵ′. So if we taken and m both bigger thanN the triangle inequality
implies that
|an− am|≤| an− L| +|am− L| < 2ϵ′ = ϵ.
Theorem 2.40. A Cauchy sequence is convergent.
Proof. Let (an) be a Cauchy sequence. By Proposition 2.38 it is bounded, so by the Bolzano-
Weierstrass theorem (Theorem 2.35) it has a convergent subsequence(ani). Let L be the
limit of this subsequence. Pick anyϵ > 0, and setϵ′ = ϵ/2. Then there exists an integerN1
such that ni≥ N1 implies|ani− L| < ϵ′ But since (an) is Cauchy, there is anN2 such that
m, n≥ N2 implies|am− an| < ϵ′. Set N = max(N1, N2). Then for any integern≥ N we have
|an− ani| < ϵ′ and
|an− L|≤| an− ani| +|ani− L| < 2ϵ′ = ϵ.
11
Note that it is not enough to look at consecutive terms of a sequence, i.e at the expression
|an+1− an| in order to prove that the sequence is Cauchy as we see in the following example.
Example 2.41. Let an = log(n). This sequence is not bounded above, so it is not Cauchy.
However, if we look at successive terms of this sequence we have
an+1− an = log(n + 1)− log(n) = log
( n + 1
n
)
.
This sequence tends tolog(1) = 0 and therefore|an+1− an| < ϵ for n big enough. .
Remark 2.42. Our previous result shows that a Cauchy sequence is actually the same as a
convergent sequence. But this comes from the fact that we are dealing with sequences ofreal
numbers! This is for example not true for sequences of rational numbers (i.e. a sequence for
which the numbers involved,an, L, ϵ, etc. all lie inQ). In this case it still makes sense to deﬁne
Cauchy sequences. However, now it is not true that every Cauchy sequence is convergent. For
example, take an = (1 + 1
n )n. This sequence is obviously rational, but it converges to the Euler
number e, which is irrational.

The axioms of the real numbers allow you to add two numbers together and get another real
number, and so a sum of anyﬁnite list of numbers always makes sense. But at the moment, there
is no formal meaning to “inﬁnite sums” like these ones — we will deﬁne it using the machinery
for limits of sequences we developed in the last section.

4.2 Convergence tests for series
It is not alway easy to tell if a sequence is summable or not. In this section we will develop some

4.3 Absolute convergence of series
If a sequence has both positive and negative terms, it is even harder to see if it is summable.
For example, is the sequence
1, − 1
2 , 1
3 , − 1
4 , ..., (−1)n+1
n , ...
summable? We know that the harmonic series(1/n) is not summable, but now when we take
partial sums the negative terms will cancel out part of the positive terms, so it is possible that
this sequence is summable.
If you are faced with a sequence with both positive and negative terms, you can make the
problem easier by just ignoring all the minus signs,i.e. replace an with|an|. The sequence(|an|)
should be easier, for example we can now apply the comparison test.
Deﬁnition 4.14. A sequence (an) is calledabsolutely summableif (|an|) is summable.
Lemma 4.15. If (an) is absolutely summable, it is summable.
Proof. Observe that for for anyn we have
0≤ an +|an|≤ 2|an|.
The sequence (|an|) is summable, so the sequence(2|an|) is also summable by the algebra of
limits. Hence (an +|an|) is summable by the comparison test. But
an =

an +|an|
)

so (an) is summable by the algebra of limits.
The converse is not true!! ifan is summable, it does not follow thatan is absolutely summable.
We will see a counter-example after our next proposition.
Proposition 4.16 (Leibniz test). Suppose (an) is a weakly decreasing sequence andan→ 0.
Then the sequencebn = (−1)n+1an is summable.
For example if we setan = 1/n, this shows that the sequence(−1)n+1/n is summable.
Proof. Let sn be the nth partial sum of(bn). We will consider the odd-numbered partial sums
(s2n+1) and the even-numbered partial sums(s2n) separately.
Start with the odd-numbered partial sums. We have
s2n+1 = (a1− a2) + (a3− a4) +··· + (a2n−1− a2n) + a2n+1.
Every term in this sum is≥ 0, because an≥ an+1 for all n and a2n+1≥ 0. Therefore s2n+1≥ 0
for all n. If we look at two successive partial sums we have
s2n+3− s2n+1 = a2n+3− a2n+2≤ 0
so the sequence(s2n+1) is weakly decreasing. It’s bounded below by zero, so it must tend to
some limit: let’s call this limitL.
Now look at the even-numbered partial sums. We have
s2n = a1− (a2− a3)− (a4− a5)−···− (a2n−2− a2n−1)− a2n≤ a1
and s2n+2− s2n = a2n+1− a2n≥ 0, so (s2n) is weakly increasing and bounded above. Hence it
converges to some limit which we’ll callM.
However, sincea2n+1 = s2n+1− s2n and a2n+1→ 0, we must haveM = L by the algebra of
limits. So for anyϵ > 0, if n is large enough then|sn− L| < ϵ, whether n is odd or even. So
sn→ L and (bn) is summable.

4.4 Power series
Deﬁnition 4.23. Let (an) be any complex sequence. Thepower seriesassociated to (an) is

Some comments on this deﬁnition:
• Up until now we have been starting our sequences from 1,i.e. writing them asa1, a2, a3, ....
We could have been starting from zero and writinga0, a1, a2, ...instead, it doesn’t make any
diﬀerence. For this section it’s a bit more convenient to switch to the second convention.
• A power series is not a single sequence, but rather an inﬁnite number of diﬀerent sequences,
one for each complex numberz∈ C. It can be seen as a function f : C→ C, z ↦→∑n
k=0 akzk.
• Power series can be seen as a generalization of polynomials. In fact let(an) be a sequence
such that for all integersn bigger than some integerN, an = 0. Then the power series
associated to (an) is the polynomialP (z) =∑n
k=0 akzk of order n.
We are interested in when a power series is convergent,i.e. when the sequence(anzn) is
summable. In fact a power series can be convergent for some values ofz but divergent for others.

number r such that|z| < r ≤ R, such that P (r) is convergent. The result follows
immediately from Proposition 4.25.
2) Assume that P (z) is convergent. Then there exists a real numberr such that|z| > r > R .
Hence P (r) is convergent by Proposition 4.25. This is a contradiction to the deﬁnition of
