5 Continuity
We now stop discussing sequences(an) of real numbers, and move on to thinking aboutfunctions
f : R→ R.
The main aim of this section is to deﬁne a “continuous” function. Roughly, a function from
R to R is continuous if we can draw its graph without lifting our pen oﬀ the paper. Of course
this is not a mathematical deﬁnition. Before giving a rigorous deﬁnition, let us look at some
examples.
Example 5.1. 1. Let f : R→ R be the functionf : x↦→ x3. This is continuous.
2. Let
H : R→ R, H (x) =
{
1, x ≥ 0,
0, x < 0.
This is called theHeaviside step function. This function is not continuous, because it
suddenly jumps in value when we crossx = 0.
3. Let
f : R→ R, f (x) =
{
sin(1/x), x ̸= 0,
0, x = 0.
As x gets close to zero, f (x) oscillates faster and faster; in fact it oscillates inﬁnitely
many times in any interval(0, ε). It is not clear at all whether this function should be
“continuous”.
However, all the strange behaviour off is at x = 0. On the subsetx̸= 0 this is a perfectly
reasonable function, and it is continuous. We will see later that it is not continuous at
x = 0.
4. Let
f : R→ R, f (x) =
{
1, x ∈ Q,
0, x /∈ Q.
This function is not continuous anywhere.
24
5.1 Limits of functions
Before giving the deﬁnition of continuity for functions we will need to look at the deﬁnition of
limit. If (an) is a sequence, we now know the precise deﬁnition of the statementan→ L, or
an→∞ . We want to adapt this deﬁnition for functions.
Deﬁnition 5.2. Let f : R→ R be a function. ForL∈ R, we sayf (x) tends to L as x→∞
iﬀ for anyϵ > 0 there is someR∈ R such that ifx > R then
|f (x)− L| < ϵ.
We might also write “f (x)→ L as x→∞ ”, orlimx→∞ f (x) = L.
Deﬁnition 5.3. Let f : R→ R be a function. We sayf (x) tends to∞ as x→∞ iﬀ for any
M∈ R there is someR∈ R such that ifx > R then f (x) > M.
We might also write “f (x)→∞ as x→∞ ”, or limx→∞ f (x) =∞. There is an obvious
variation for the deﬁnition oflimx→∞ f (x) =−∞.
These deﬁnitions are very similar to the corresponding deﬁnitions for sequences. We have
just replaced the bit that says “forn large enough” (there existsN∈ N such that ifn≥ N then
··· ) with something that says “forx large enough” (there existsR∈ R such that ifx > R then
··· ).
Notice that these deﬁnitions work perfectly well if the functionf is not deﬁned on the whole
of R, but only on an interval(b,∞) for some b∈ R.
Example 5.4. 1. Let
f : (0,∞)→ R, f (x) = 1
x .
We claim thatlimx→∞ f (x) = 0. To check this, pick anyϵ > 0, and setR = 1/ϵ. If x > R,
then|f (x)| =|1/x| < ϵ, as required.
2. Let
f : R→ R, f (x) = x2.
We claim thatlimx→∞ f (x) =∞. To check this, pick anyM∈ R, and setR =
√
M (or
R = 0 if M < 0). If x > R, then f (x) = x2 > M, as required.
For sequences, there is only one “direction” in which you can go to inﬁnity: you can only
consider letting n get very large. For functions there is a second option: instead of letting
x→∞ , we could letx→−∞ and study the limit off (x). This is an obvious variation on the
above deﬁnitions, and we leave it as an exercise.
Much more importantly, instead of lettingx→∞ , we could choose a ﬁnitea∈ R and study
what happens whenx→ a.

x ,

To come up with a deﬁnition of “limx→a f (x) = L”, we are going to need to replace “forx
large enough” with something that means “forx close enough toa”. Also, it is going to matter
whether we approacha from the sidex > a or from the sidex < a.
Deﬁnition 5.6. Take a∈ R, and a functionf : ( a,∞)→ R. We say f (x) tends to L as
x→ a+ iﬀ for anyϵ > 0 there is someδ > 0 such that ifx∈ (a, a + δ) then
|f (x)− L| < ϵ.
We might also write “f (x)→ L as x→ a+”, orlimx→a+ f (x) = L. Two things to note:
• In this deﬁnition we have formalised “forx close enough toa” as “there is someδ > 0 such
that if x∈ (a, a + δ) then··· ”.
• We do not need the actual valuef (a) to be deﬁned in this deﬁnition. Also, it works
perfectly well iff is only deﬁned on an interval(a, b) for some b > a.
The “a+” notation is indicating that we are approachinga from the sidex > a. To approach
from the other side, we make the obvious modiﬁcations:
Deﬁnition 5.7. Take a ∈ R, and a function f : ( b, a) → R for some b < a . We say
limx→a− f (x) = L if and only if for anyϵ > 0 there is someδ > 0 such that ifx∈ (a− δ, a) then
|f (x)− L| < ϵ.

Like limits of sequences, limits of functions are unique:
Lemma 5.9. Suppose f : (a, b)→ R is a function, and we have

Proposition 5.22. Let I⊂ R be an open interval, and letf, g : I→ R be two functions. Then:

lim
x→a
(f + g)(x) = f (a) + g(a),
so f + g is continuous ata. Similarly, the other parts follow directly from the algebra of limits
of functions.
Note that in (3), iff (x) = 0 for some x then the function1/f is not evendeﬁned at x, so it
is meaningless to ask if1/f is continuous onI.
Example 5.23.We have checked that constant functions are continuous, and that the function
f : x↦→ 2x is continuous, so by Proposition 5.22(2) the functionx↦→ x must be continuous (of
course it is easy to check this directly). Then applying Proposition 5.22(2)n times tells us that
the function
g : R→ R, g (x) = xn
is continuous. If we apply parts (1) and (2) of the same proposition, we can conclude that any
polynomial
P (x) = anxn + an−1xn−1 +··· + a1x + a0
deﬁnes a continuous function fromR to R. Using part (3), we see that ifQ(x) is another
polynomial, and I⊂ R is an open interval on whichQ is never zero, then the quotient function
P/Q : I→ R, x ↦→ P (x)
Q(x)
is continuous. This kind of function is called arational function.
31
Proposition 5.24.Let I⊂ R be an open interval, and letf : I→ R a function. Now letJ⊂ R
be an open interval such thatf (I)⊂ J, and letg : J→ R be a function. If bothf and g are
continuous then
g◦ f : I→ R
is continuous.
This means that the composition of continuous functions is always continuous.
Proof. Pick any a∈ I, and let b = f (a)∈ J. We must show thatg◦ f is continuous at a.
Pick any ϵ < 0. Since g is continuous atb, there exists someδ such that ify∈ (b− δ, b + δ)
then|g(y)− g(b)| < ϵ . Also, since f is continuous at a, there exists some δ′ such that if
x∈ (a− δ′, a + δ′) then|f (x)− b| < δ. So:
|x− a| < δ′ =⇒ | f (x)− b| < δ =⇒ | g(f (x))− g(b)| < ϵ
Since g(b) = g(f (a)), this says thatg◦ f is continuous ata.
Remark 5.25. The familiar functionsexp(x), sin(x), cos(x) are all continuous on the whole ofR,
but this is not so easy to prove. We have to start with a precise deﬁnition of these functions,
which means deﬁning them using power series.
5.3 Sequential Criterion for continuous functions
We will now look at a very important result known as the Sequential Criterion, which makes a
connection between continuous functions and limits of sequences.
Proposition 5.26 (Sequential Criterion for continuous functions). Let I ⊂ R be an open
interval, f : I→ R be a function. Thenf is continuous ata∈ I if and only iff (an)→ f (a) for
all sequences (an), such thatan→ a.
Proof. Let f be continuous ata, and an be in I for all n. Moreover letan→ a. Then
∀ϵ > 0,∃δ > 0, such that∀x∈ I,|x− a| < δ⇒| f (x)− f (a)| < ϵ
and
∀ϵ′ > 0,∃N∈ N, such that∀n≥ N,|an− a| < ϵ′.
Now with δ = ϵ′, we get sincean∈ I,
∀n≥ N,|an− a| < δ,⇒| f (an)− f (a)| < ϵ.
Conversely suppose that for all sequences(an), such thatan→ a and f (an)→ f (a), f is not
continuous in a.Then there exists anϵ > 0, such that for somex∈ I,
∀δ > 0,|x− a| < δ and|f (x)− f (a)|≥ ϵ.
Especially this should hold for allδn := 1
n. For eachn there exists for this particularδn a real
number x =: an such that|an− a| < δn and|f (an)− f (a)|≥ ϵ. But this means exactly that
the sequence f (an) does not converge tof (a). This is a contradiction.
Example 5.27. Let f (x) =
{
x2, x∈ Q
−x2, x̸∈ Q . We prove thatf is continuous ata if and only if
a = 0.
Consider two sequencesan∈ Q and bn̸∈ Q, such thatan, bn→ a. Assume that f is continuous
at a, then we have by the sequential criterion
f (an) = a2
n→ a2 = f (a), f(bn) =−b2
n =−a2 = f (a)
32
Hence f (a) =−f (a) hence f (a) = 0 which is only true whena = 0.
It remains to show thatf is continuous in0. f (0) = 0. Let ϵ > 0. Take xn an arbitrary sequence
such that xn→ 0. Then there exists anN∈ N, such that∀n≥ N,|xn| < ϵ, and therefore
|± x2
n| <|xn| < ϵ. Therefore f (xn)→ 0.
Example 5.28. Consinder again f (x) =
{
sin 1
x , x̸= 0,
0, x = 0 . We will show using the sequential
criterion that the function is not continuous in0. Assume f is continuous in 0. Consider
the sequences an = 1
2πn and bn = 1
2πn+π
2
. Note that both an, bn→ 0. So we should have
f (an), f(bn)→ 0. But f (an) = sin(2πn) = 0 and f (bn) = sin(2πn + π
2 ) = 1, for alln. Therefore
we get a contradiction andf is not continuous in0.
5.4 Continuous functions on closed bounded intervals
In this section we are going to prove a few important results about continuous functions deﬁned
on closed bounded intervals, which means intervals of the form
K = [b, c] ⊂ R
for some real numberb≤ c. So far we have only deﬁned continuous functions on open intervals.
We therefore need the following
Deﬁnition 5.29. Let K = [ b, c]⊂ R be a closed bounded interval, and letf : K→ R be a
function. We say thatf is continuous if and only iff is continuous ata for all b < a < c , and
also:
lim
x→b+
f (x) = f (b)∧ lim
x→c−
f (x) = f (c)
Lemma 5.30. Let I⊂ R be an open interval and letK⊂ I be a closed bounded interval. If
f : I→ R is continuous thenf|K : K→ R is continuous.
Proof. This follows immediately from the deﬁnitions.
In practice, this is mostly how we will produce continuous functions on closed bounded
intervals.
It’s not hard to check that all our previous results about continuous functions still apply to
functions deﬁned on a closed bounded intervalK, the proofs need only trivial modiﬁcations.
Deﬁnition 5.31. Let S⊂ R be any subset, and letf : S→ R be a function. We sayf is
bounded if the set of values{f (x), x∈ S} is bounded,i.e. if there exists someB∈ R such
that|f (x)|≤ B for all x∈ S.
Continuous functions on open intervals do not have to be bounded, for example the function
f : (0 , 1)→ R, x↦→ 1/x is not bounded above. However, on a closed bounded interval, this
cannot happen.
Proposition 5.32.Let K = [b, c] be a closed bounded interval, and letf : K→ R be continuous.
Then f is bounded.
Proof. Suppose that f is not bounded. Then for anyn∈ N, there exist some numberxn∈ K
such that|f (xn)| > n. We consider the sequence(xn). This sequence is bounded (sincexn∈ [b, c]
for all n), so by the Bolzano-Weierstass Theorem it contains a convergent subsequence(xni),
tending to some limitL. We havexni≤ c for all ni so we must haveL≤ c, and similarly we
must have L≥ b, so L∈ K. Since f is continuous, by the sequential criterion the sequence
f (xni) tends to f (L). However, the sequencef (xni) is not bounded, so it cannot be convergent.
This is a contradiction.
33
Deﬁnition 5.33. If S⊂ R is any subset, andf : S→ R is a bounded function, then we can
consider the least upper bound and greatest lower bound of the values off:
sup(f ) = sup{f (x), x ∈ S} and inf(f ) = inf{f (x), x ∈ S}
Example 5.34. Let f be the function: f : [1 ,∞)→ R x↦→ 1/x. Then sup(f ) = 1 and
inf(f ) = 0 . This function does take reach value 1, at the pointx = 1, but it never actually
reaches the value 0, becausef (x) > 0 for all x in the domain.
Another nice feature of continuous functions on closed bounded intervals is that they always
reach their supremums and inﬁmums at some point.
Theorem 5.35. Let K = (b, c) be a closed bounded interval and letf : K→ R be continuous.
Then
1. there exists anx∈ K such that f (x) = sup( f ), and
2. there exists anx∈ K such that f (x) = inf( f ).
Note thatf must be bounded by Proposition 5.32, sosup(f ) and inf(f ) do exist. This result,
together with the previous one, say that a continuous function on a closed bounded interval is
bounded and attains its bounds.
Proof. We’ll just prove (1), as the proof for (2) is essentially identical.
Suppose that f (x)̸= sup(f ) at any point. Then the functiong : K→ R, x↦→ 1/(sup(f )−
f (x)) is well-deﬁned andg is continuous by Proposition 5.22, henceg is bounded by Proposition
5.32.
By the deﬁnition ofsup(f ), for anyn∈ N the number sup(f )− 1/n is not an upper bound
for f. So for eachn, there must be some pointxn∈ K such that f (xn) > sup(f )− 1/n. But
then g(xn) > n, which shows thatg is not bounded above. This is a contradiction.
Theorem 5.36(Intermediate Value Theorem). Let K = [b, c] be a closed bounded interval and
let f : K→ R be continuous. Then:
1. If f (b)≤ f (c) and A is any real number such thatf (b)≤ A≤ f (c), then there exists a
point a∈ [b, c] such that f (a) = A.
2. If f (b)≥ f (c) and A is any real number such thatf (c)≤ A≤ f (b), then there exists a
point a∈ [b, c] such that f (a) = A.
Proof. Again we’ll only prove (1), because case (2) is essentially identical.
Let S⊂ K be the subset:
S ={x, f (x)≤ A}
Then S is not empty becauseb∈ S, and S is bounded above byc, so S has a supremumsup(S).
Set a = sup(S). We’re going to show thatf (a) = A.
Suppose that f (a) < A. Then we can’t havea = c, because f (c)≥ A by assumption. So
f is deﬁned on the interval(a, c), and it makes sense to ask about the limit off as x→ a+.
Moreover since f is continous we must havelimx→a+ f (x) = f (a). Setting ϵ = A− f (a), this
means that there is someδ > 0 such that ifx∈ (a, a + δ) then f (x) < f (a) + ϵ = A. This says
that any pointx∈ (a, a + δ) lies in S, which contradicts the fact thata is an upper bound forS.
Now suppose f (a) > A . Then we can’t have a = b because f (b)≤ A, and we have
limx→a− f (x) = f (a). Copying the argument of the previous paragraph, this implies that there
is some δ > 0 such that ifx∈ (a− δ, a) then f (x) > A. But then f (x) > A for every x > a− δ,
so S is contained in the set{x≤ a− δ}. So a− δ is an upper bound forS, contradicting the
fact that a = sup(S).
34
The following corollary is immediate:
Corollary 5.37. If f : [ b, c]→ R is continuous, and we havef (b) < 0 and f (c) > 0 (or vice
versa) then f (a) = 0 for some a∈ (b, c).
Example 5.38. Consider the polynomialP : R→ R where:
P : x↦→ x3 + x− 3
We’ve seen that all polynomials are continuous, soP deﬁnes a continuous function from the
closed bounded interval[1, 2] to R. Since P (1) =−1, and P (2) = 7, this polynomial must have
a root lying between 1 and 2.
We can generalize the previous example to get a nice result, which should be intuitively
clear:
Corollary 5.39. Let P : R→ R be any polynomial of odd degree. ThenP has at least one root.
Proof. We haveP (x) = adxd + Q(x) where d∈ N is odd, ad̸= 0, and Q(x) is a polynomial of
degree at mostd− 1. Let’s assumead > 0, if not then replaceP with the polynomial−P, which
has a root iﬀP does.
Consider the sequenceQ(n) obtained by evaluatingQ on the natural numbersn = 1, 2, 3, ....
Our usual toolkit of results on sequences shows thatQ(n)/nd→ 0, so ifn is big enough then
|Q(n)/nd| < ad and so|Q(n)| < adnd. Hence:
P (n) > adnd−| Q(n)| > 0 and P (−n) <−adnd +|Q(n)| < 0
So P deﬁnes a continuous function on the closed bounded interval[−n, n], with P (−n) < 0 and
P (n) > 0, so P has a root in(−n, n).
Another nice application of the Intermediate Value Theorem, which is slightly less obvious:
Corollary 5.40. Let K = [b, c] be a closed bounded interval, and suppose we have a continuous
function f : K→ K. There there is somea∈ K such that f (a) = a.
Such an a is called aﬁxed pointof f. If you’re worried that we haven’t deﬁned what it
means for a function fromK to K to be continuous, just think off as a function fromK to R
whose image happens to be contained inK.
Proof. We can assume thatf (b) > b and f (c) < c, otherwise eitherb or c is a ﬁxed point off.
Now consider the functiong : K→ R deﬁned by:
g : x→ f (x)− x
This function is continuous (by Proposition 5.22), and we haveg(b) > 0 and g(c) < 0, so by the
previous corollary there is somea∈ (b, c) such that g(a) = 0. Then f (a) = a.
5.5 Compact sets and continuous complex functions
Deﬁnition 5.41. A subset A⊂ C is calledclosed if for every convergent sequence(an) in A
we have
lim
n→∞
an∈ A.
Example 5.42. The sets [a, b]⊂ R, R,{z∈ C :|z|≤ r}, and C are closed. The sets [a, b),
(a, b), and{z∈ C :|z| < r} are not closed.
35
Proposition 5.43. Let A1, . . . , Ak⊂ C be closed. Then
k⋃
j=1
Aj
is closed. Moreover, the intersection ofany family of closed sets is closed.
Proof. Exercise.
The union of inﬁnitely many closed sets need not be closed.
Example 5.44.
∞⋃
n=1
[ 1
n , 1
]
= (0, 1]
is not closed inR.
Deﬁnition 5.45. A subset K⊂ C is calledcompact if
1. K is closed;
2. K is bounded, i.e. there existsR∈ R>0 such that
K⊂{ z∈ C :|z|≤ R}.
Example 5.46. For a, b∈ R, the interval[a, b] is compact. The setR is not compact.
Proposition 5.47. Let K1, . . . , Km⊂ C be compact subsets. Then
m⋃
j=1
Kj
is compact. Moreover, the intersection of any family of compact subsets ofC is compact.
Proof. This follows from the corresponding statements for closed sets and the fact that a
union of ﬁnitely many bounded sets is bounded, and any intersection of bounded sets is again
bounded.
Theorem 5.48 (Bolzano–Weierstrass characterisation of compact sets). A subset K⊂ C is
compact if and only if the following holds:
For every sequence(zn) in K there exists a subsequence(znj ) and a pointz∈ K such that
lim
j→∞
znj = z.
Proof. Suppose ﬁrst thatK is compact, i.e. closed and bounded. Let(zn) be any sequence inK.
Since K is bounded, the sequence(zn) is bounded. By the (real or complex) Bolzano–Weierstrass
theorem there exists a convergent subsequence(znj ) with limit z∈ C. Because K is closed, the
limit of any convergent sequence inK lies in K, so z∈ K.
Conversely, assume that every sequence inK has a convergent subsequence whose limit lies
in K. We show ﬁrst thatK is bounded. If K were unbounded, we could choose a sequence
(zn) in K with|zn|→∞ as n→∞ . Such a sequence has no convergent subsequence inC,
contradicting our assumption. HenceK is bounded.
Next we show that K is closed. Let (zn) be a sequence in K with zn → z ∈ C. By
assumption, (zn) has a subsequence(znj ) converging to somew∈ K. On the other hand, a
convergent sequence has a unique limit, sow = z. Thus z∈ K, and K is closed. Therefore K is
compact.
36
Theorem 5.49. Let K⊂ C be compact andf : K→ C continuous. Then the image
f (K) :={f (z) : z∈ K}⊂ C
is compact.
Proof. Let (wn) be a sequence inf (K). By deﬁnition of f (K) there is a sequence(zn) in K
with wn = f (zn) for all n. By compactness ofK and the Bolzano–Weierstrass characterisation,
there is a subsequence(znj ) and a pointz∈ K such that znj→ z as j→∞ . By continuity off
we have
wnj = f (znj )−→ f (z).
Thus (wn) has a convergent subsequence whose limitf (z) lies in f (K). By the Bolzano–
Weierstrass characterisation again,f (K) is compact.
Theorem 5.50(Extreme value theorem). Let D⊂ C be compact andf : D→ R continuous.
Then there existz1, z2∈ D such that
f (z1)≤ f (z)≤ f (z2) for all z∈ D.
In other words,f attains both its minimum and its maximum onD.
Proof. By the previous theorem,f (D)⊂ R is compact, hence bounded. Therefore the numbers
m := inf f (D), M := sup f (D)
exist and are ﬁnite. Sincef (D) is compact, it is also closed, som, M∈ f (D). Thus there exist
z1, z2∈ D such that f (z1) = m and f (z2) = M, as claimed.
Corollary 5.51. Let D⊂ C be compact andf : D→ C continuous. Then f is bounded, i.e.
there exists a constantC > 0 such that
|f (z)|≤ C for all z∈ D.
Proof. The function|f| : D→ R is continuous. By the extreme value theorem there exists
z0∈ D such that
|f (z0)|≥| f (z)| for all z∈ D.
Setting C :=|f (z0)| gives the result.
Remark 5.52. Both the compactness ofD and the continuity off are essential.
Example 5.53. 1. Let f : [−1, 1]→ R be deﬁned by
f (x) =



1
x , x ̸= 0,
0, x = 0.
Then [−1, 1] is compact, butf is not continuous atx = 0, and f is not bounded.
2. Let f : (0, 1]→ R be deﬁned by
f (x) = 1
x .
Here f is continuous, but the domain(0, 1] is not compact, and againf is not bounded.
Deﬁnition 5.54(Uniform continuity). Let D⊂ C and f : D→ C. We say thatf is uniformly
continuous on D if
∀ ε > 0∃ δ > 0∀ z, w∈ D : |z− w| < δ ⇒ |f (z)− f (w)| < ε.
37
For ordinary continuity at a pointz0, the δ may depend on z0 (we write δ = δ(ε, z0)).
Uniform continuity says something stronger: for eachε > 0 we can choose a singleδ that works
simultaneously for all pointsz0 in the domain. So the function does not suddenly oscillate very
fast when you move to a diﬀerent part of the domain.
Example 5.55. The function f (x) = 1
x on (0, 1] is not uniformly continuous.
Intuitively, near0 the graph off (x) = 1 /x becomes extremely steep: very small changes in
x can cause very large changes in1/x. This suggests that no singleδ can work for all points in
(0, 1] at once. Let us make this precise.
Suppose, for contradiction, thatf is uniformly continuous on(0, 1]. Then there exists a
δ > 0 corresponding to ε = 1
4 such that for allx, y∈ (0, 1]:
|x− y| < δ ⇒
⏐⏐⏐⏐
1
x− 1
y
⏐⏐⏐⏐ < 1
4 .
Now choose a natural numbern so large that 1
n < δ. Set
xn := 1
n , y n := 1
2n .
Then
|xn− yn| =
⏐⏐⏐⏐
1
n− 1
2n
⏐⏐⏐⏐ = 1
2n < 1
n < δ,
so the condition|xn− yn| < δ is satisﬁed. But
⏐⏐⏐⏐
1
xn
− 1
yn
⏐⏐⏐⏐ =|n− 2n| = n≥ 1 > 1
4 ,
which contradicts the assumed implication. Hencef (x) = 1 /x is not uniformly continuous on
(0, 1].
Note that f is continuous at every point of(0, 1]; the problem appears only when we try to
control all points of the interval with thesame δ.
Theorem 5.56(Heine). Let D⊂ C be compact and letf : D→ C be continuous. Thenf is
uniformly continuous onD.
Proof. Assume, for a contradiction, thatf is not uniformly continuous. Then, by negating the
deﬁnition, there exists someε0 > 0 such that:
for every δ > 0 there exist z, w∈ D with|z− w| < δ but|f (z)− f (w)|≥ ε0.
For eachn∈ N, apply this withδ = 1
n. We obtain pointszn, wn∈ D such that
|zn− wn| < 1
n and |f (zn)− f (wn)|≥ ε0 for all n∈ N.
Since D is compact, the sequence(zn) has a convergent subsequence: there exist integersnk
and a pointz0∈ D such that
znk−→ z0 as k→∞ .
Moreover,
|znk− wnk| < 1
nk
≤ 1
k ,
so also wnk→ z0 as k→∞ .
Now use the continuity off at z0. By the sequence criterion for continuity (Theorem 6.12),
we obtain
lim
k→∞
f (znk ) = f (z0) and lim
k→∞
f (wnk ) = f (z0).
38
Therefore
lim
k→∞
(
f (znk )− f (wnk )
)
= 0,
so in particular
|f (znk )− f (wnk )| < ε0
2 for all suﬃciently largek.
But this contradicts the construction of the sequences, which satisﬁes|f (znk )− f (wnk )|≥ ε0 for
every k. Hence our assumption was wrong, andf must be uniformly continuous onD.
Remark 5.57. Heine’s theorem is one of the main reasons compact sets are so useful: You can
think of compact sets as “closed and bounded” sets with no points missing at inﬁnity and no
limit points missing on the boundary. Heine’s theorem says that on such “nice” sets, continuity
is automatically as good as possible: you can choose a singleδ that works uniformly over the
whole set. In particular, every continuous function on a closed interval[a, b]⊂ R is uniformly
continuous. not just near one point at a time.
Deﬁnition 5.58. Let A⊂ R. A function f : A→ R is calledLipschitz continuous (or just
Lipschitz) if there exists a constantL≥ 0 such that
|f (x)− f (y)|≤ L|x− y| for all x, y∈ A.
Any such L is called aLipschitz constantfor f.
Geometrically, a Lipschitz function has its slope bounded in absolute value byL: you can
never be steeper than a straight line with slopeL. This automatically prevents wild oscillations
and gives uniform control everywhere.
Proposition 5.59. If f : A→ R is Lipschitz, thenf is uniformly continuous onA.
Proof. Let ε > 0 be given. Choose
δ = ε
L if L > 0, δ = 1 if L = 0.
If|x− y| < δ, then
|f (x)− f (y)|≤ L|x− y| < L δ≤ ε.
The key point is that thisδ depends only on ε and on L, not on x, y. Hence f is uniformly
continuous on A.
Example 5.60. Let f (x) = x2.
(a) On the closed intervalK = [−R, R], the set K is compact and f is continuous, so by
Heine’s theoremf is uniformly continuous onK. Intuitively, on a bounded set the function
cannot “run oﬀ to inﬁnity” too fast.
(b) On all ofR, the same functionf (x) = x2 is not uniformly continuous. Indeed, if it were,
there would be aδ > 0 such that for allx, y∈ R,
|x− y| < δ =⇒ | x2− y2| < 1.
Take y = x + δ/2. Then
|x2− y2| =|x2− (x + δ/2)2| =|(x + y)(x− y)|.
For large|x|, the factor|x + y| becomes arbitrarily large, while|x− y| = δ/2 stays ﬁxed,
so we can make|x2− y2|≥ 1 even though|x− y| < δ. This contradicts uniform continuity,
so f is not uniformly continuous onR.
39
Example 5.61. Consider f : [0, 1]→ R given by
f (x) =√x.
The function f is continuous on the compact interval[0, 1], so by Heine’s theorem it is uniformly
continuous.
However, f is not Lipschitz on [0, 1] (see the deﬁnition below). Indeed, suppose there were a
constant L≥ 0 such that
|√x−√y|≤ L|x− y| for all x, y∈ [0, 1].
Taking y = 0 and x = 1
n2, this would give
⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐
√
1
n2− 0
⏐⏐⏐⏐⏐ = 1
n≤ L
⏐⏐⏐⏐
1
n2− 0
⏐⏐⏐⏐ = L
n2 for all n∈ N.
Rearranging,
1
n≤ L
n2 =⇒ n≤ L for all n,
which is impossible for largen. So no suchL exists, and f is not Lipschitz. This shows that
uniform continuity is weaker than the Lipschitz condition.
Remark 5.62. Many familiar functions are Lipschitz on bounded sets (for example, polynomials
on a closed interval), so they are automatically uniformly continuous there. Heine’s theorem is
more general: it applies toany continuous function on a compact set, even when no Lipschitz
estimate is available, as in the examplef (x) =√x.
40

Welcome
These are lecture notes for the second part of Calculus and Applications first year
module at the Department of Mathematics, Imperial College London. The notes
are split into three parts on Fourier Transform, Ordinary Differential Equations
and Introduction to Multivariate Calculus. Please refer to course Blackboard
for additional materials recommended text books for further reading.
These lecture notes are adobted from existing courses in our department. Part
I of the course is based on the old M2AA2 course (Andrew Walton) and Part II
and III are based on the old M1M2 course (Frank Berkshire, Mauricio Barahona,
Andrew Parry). Some examples and ideas from the old mechanics course M1A1
is included as well.
These notes are produced with accessibility in mind and I hope it meets your
requirements. You can experiment with the controls in the toolbar at the top of
the html version of the notes. You can search for a word, adjust typeface, font
size, font and background color. You can also download a copy of these notes
in differnt formats, if you wish for offline use. I hope you enjoy this course and
let me know if you have any comments or questions by email.

Part I: Fourier Transforms
7

Chapter 1
Fourier Transforms
Last term, we saw that Fourier series allows us to represent a given function,
defined over a finite range of the independent variable, in terms of sine and co-
sine waves of different amplitudes and frequencies. Fourier Transforms are the
natural extension of Fourier series for functions defined over ℝ. A key reason for
studying Fourier transforms (and series) is that we can use these ideas to help
us solve differential equations as seen in this course regarding ordinary differen-
tial equations and more extensively next year in relation to partial differential
equations. There are also many other applications for Fourier transforms in
science and engineering, particularly in the context of signal processing.

Chapter 2
Properties of Fourier
Transforms
In the following we present some important properties of Fourier transforms.
These results will be helpful in deriving Fourier and inverse Fourier transform
of different functions. After discussing some basic properties, we will discuss,
convolution theorem and energy theorem. Finally, we introduc Dirac delta
function.

Introduction to ordinary
differential equations
Differential equations are very important in science and engineering. In this
course, we focus on a specific class of differential equations called ordinary dif-
ferential equations (ODEs). Ordinary refers to dealing with functions of one in-
dependent variable. We initially focus on scaler functions of the form 𝑓 ∶ ℝ → ℝ .
Later in the course, we discuss systems of ODEs and consider vector functions
of the form 𝑓 ∶ ℝ → ℝ 𝑛. We assume 𝑓(𝑥)is differentiable upto order 𝑘. An ODE
is an equation for the function 𝑓(𝑥)that involves the function, its derivatives
and the independent variable. A general form for an ODE of order 𝑘 is
𝐺(𝑥, 𝑓(𝑥),𝑑𝑓
𝑑𝑥, ..., 𝑑𝑘𝑓
𝑑𝑥𝑘 ) = 0,
where the highest derivative present in the equation 𝐺 is of the order 𝑘. The
degree of the ODE is the power of highest derivative (when fractional powers
have been removed). The ODE is called linear if G is a linear function of 𝑓(𝑥)
and its derivatives. This form for the ODE is the so called implicit form. In
an explicit form for an ODE, the highest order derivative is given as function of
the lower derivatives:

Example 3.2 (Second Newton Law) .
Mechanics: A very short introduction
Kinematics is a branch of mechanics that describes the motion of points (objects)
without considering the forces that cause them to move. In one dimension 𝑥(𝑡)
denotes the position of a particle at time 𝑡. Then 𝑑𝑥
𝑑𝑡 = ̇ 𝑥 = 𝑣 is defined as
the velocity of the particle and 𝑑2 𝑥
𝑑𝑡2 = ̈ 𝑥 = 𝑎 is defined as acceleration. This
can be generalised to higher dimensions using vectors of location, velocity and
acceleration.
Dynamics is the branch of mechanics concerned with the study of forces and
their effects on motion. Isaac Newton came up with the fundamental physical
laws, which govern dynamics in physics:
-First law an object not acted upon by any force either remains at rest or
continues to move at a constant velocity
-Second law the vector sum of the forces 𝐹 on an object is equal to the mass
𝑚 of that object multiplied by its acceleration 𝑎: 𝐹 = 𝑚𝑎

𝑑𝑡 )
This is a second order ODE for the position of the object 𝑥(𝑡).
-Third law when one body exerts a force on a second body, the second body
exerts a force equal in magnitude and opposite in direction on the first body.
Mechanics used to be thaught until recently in our first year Mathematics course
as it provides many links to different areas of mathemaitcs. If you have any
doubts, look at this video, for a very cool counting problem for colliding particles
and its very unexpected solution and link to mathematics.

preys as one goes up and the other goes down.

Example 6.4 (SIR model of an epidemic) .
One of the simplest but influential models of an epidemic is the compartmental
SIR model. In this model, disease propagates between the population compart-
ments through susceptible individuals ( 𝑆) becoming infected ( 𝐼) after encoun-
tering other infected individuals with rate 𝛽 and finally moving to a recovered
population ( 𝑅) with rate 𝛾. The following simple system of ODEs describes the
dynamics of this compartmental model.

This model and its variations are the basis of a lot of mathematical modelling
that has been performed for predicting the effect of different interventions in
the Covid-19 world-wide pandemic. If you like to learn a bit more about the
SIR ODE system above check out this video. Also, the SIR model can be
modeled using a so-called agent-based stochastic approach, where the individuals
and their random interactions are specifically followed. You can check out this
interesting video from the 3blue1brown series to see some cool exploration of
this approach to SIR models.

Qualitative analysis of
ODEs
So far we have focused to obtain analytical solution to ODEs, but this is not
always possible. Even, when it is possible, it is not always very insightful. In
this section, we will focus on qualitative analysis of ODEs and as before we
mostly focus on linear ODEs. We’ll discuss asymptotics behavior, fixed points
(and their stability) and phase plane analysis.

Stability of fixed points
Informally, a fixed point is stable if whenever the initial state is near that point,
the state remains near it, perhaps even tending toward the equilibrium point as
time increases. Formally, we have two types of stability as described below.
Lyapunov stability
A fixed point ⃗ 𝑦∗ is said to be Lyapunov stable , if for every 𝜖 > 0 , there exists a
𝛿 > 0 such that, if ‖ ⃗ 𝑦(0) − ⃗ 𝑦∗‖ < 𝛿 , then for ∀𝑡 ≥ 0, we have ‖ ⃗ 𝑦(𝑡) − ⃗ 𝑦∗‖ < 𝜖 .
Intuitively, it means the solution does not blow up but also does not necessarily
approach to the fixed point.
Asymptotic stability
7.2. PHASE PLANE ANALYSIS 63
A fixed point ⃗ 𝑦∗ is said to be Asymptotically stable , if it is Lyapunov stable and
there exists a 𝛿 > 0 such that, if ‖ ⃗ 𝑦(0) − ⃗ 𝑦∗‖ < 𝛿 , then we have
lim
𝑡→∞
‖ ⃗ 𝑦(𝑡) − ⃗ 𝑦∗‖ = 0.
Intuitively, in this case the solution does approach to the fixed point over long
times.
7.2 Phase plane analysis
The general solution of systems of ODEs is given by the family of parametric
curves, specified by the initial condition:
⃗ 𝑦(𝑡; 𝑐1, ⋯ , 𝑐𝑛) ∈ ℝ𝑛.
These solutions represent trajectories in ℝ𝑛 for a system of 𝑛 dimensional ODEs.
For a 2 dimensional systme the family of solutions starting from different initial
conditions can be represented in a so called phase plane as illustrated in Figure
7.1.
Figure 7.1: Illustration of phase plane for a 2 dimensional system. Two solutions
starting at different initial conditions are shown (blue). Also, some representa-
tive velocity vectors from the vector field are drawn (red).
One can interpret the phase plane in terms of dynamics. The solution ⃗ 𝑦(𝑡)
corresponds to a trajectory of a point moving on the phase plane with velocity
𝑑 ⃗ 𝑦
𝑑𝑡(𝑡). For a system of first order ODEs of the form:
𝑑 ⃗ 𝑦
𝑑𝑡 = 𝐹 ( ⃗ 𝑦),
64 CHAPTER 7. QUALITATIVE ANALYSIS OF ODES
where, there is no explicit dependence on the independent variable (time) on
the right hand side, the velocity is a vector defined at every point of the phase
plane and is tangent to the trajectory. This is called the vector field. See Figure
7.1.
Uniqueness of solutions of ODEs
Solutions of ODEs are uniquely defined by initial conditions except at some
special points in the phase plane (no proof now, you will see in the second year
rigorous proof). Trajectories in the phase plane cannot cross (except at some
special points) as this would be equivalent of non-uniqueness of solutions. The
special points are fixed points or singular points where trajectories start or end.
Phase plane analysis for the linear systems of first order ODEs

7.4 Phase plane analysis for 2D nonlinear sys-
tems
Phase plane analysis is a very useful tool for 2 dimensional nonlinear systems and
the insights obtained from the 2D linear case is highly relevant. One obtains the
special points (fixed points) and could linearise the equations in the vicinity of
these points to get insight from the analysis of the corresponding linear system.
Vector field and asymptotic behaviour is useful to identify the trajectories in
the phase plane.

paper by Gardener, Cantor and Colins, Nature 403:339-342 (2000). Consider
two genes 𝑢 and 𝑣, which inhibit expression of one another. The following
system of nonlinear ODEs characterises the dynamics between the two genes.

The approach directly generalises for the linear systems as the solutions are

these directions and grow or decay depending on the sign of the corresponding
eigenvalue.
Stability of linear 𝑛 dimensional systems For the 2 dimensional case we
had stability where 𝜏 ≤ 0 and Δ ≥ 0 . In terms of eigenvalue characterization
this meant the real part of the eigen values are negative. Similarly, for the
general 𝑛 dimensional linear systems of ODEs we have stability if the real part
of all the eigenvalues are negative.
Lorenz system (1933, Edward Lorenz)
More complex dynamics in phase planes are possible. Lorenz proposed a system
of 3 nonlinear equations that is a model of atmospheric convection. This rather
simple model for certain values of parameters (e.g. 𝜎 = 10, 𝛽 = 8
3 and 𝜌 = 28),
exhibits a complex non-periodic dynamics that is an example of chaos. This
dynamical behavior is characterised by the divergence of trajectories in the

Introduction to Bifurcations
Bifurcations in a dynamical system (system of ODEs) describe the qualitative
change in behavior under a variation or change of some parameters of the system.
Parameters are constants that are tunable.

8.2.1 Saddle-node bifurcation
This is a basic mechanism for creation and destroying fixed points. The proto-
typical example of saddle-node bifurcation is given by:
𝑑𝑦
𝑑𝑡 = 𝑟 + 𝑦2,

For 𝑟 < 0 we have 2 fixed points (one stable, one unstable), at 𝑟 = 0 we have
one half-stable fixed point and for 𝑟 > 0 we have no fixed point. There is a

the fixed points can be summarised using the bifurcation diagram, which is a

8.2.2 Transcritical bifurcation
In certain systems a fixed point must exist for all values of a parameter. The
prototypical example of this form of bifurcation is given by:
𝑑𝑦
𝑑𝑡 = 𝑟𝑦 − 𝑦2,
where 𝑟 ∈ ℝ . This ODE has up to two fixed points 𝑦∗ = 0 and 𝑦∗ = 𝑟. Figure
8.6 using the vector field, illustrates number and stability of these fixed points
for different values of 𝑟.
Figure 8.6: The plot of 𝑓(𝑦)vs 𝑦 for different values of 𝑟, illustrates the vector
field and the stability of the fixed point for transcritical bifurcation.
For 𝑟 < 0 we have 2 fixed points (one stable, one unstable), at 𝑟 = 0 we have
one half-stable fixed point and for 𝑟 > 0 go back to two fixed points. At the
84 CHAPTER 8. INTRODUCTION TO BIFURCATIONS
bifurcation point 𝑟 = 0 an exchange of stabilities takes place between the two
fixed points. The changes in the number and stability of the fixed points is
summarised in the bifurcation diagram (see Figure 8.7).
Figure 8.7: The bifurcation diagram (plot of fixed points and their stability vs
the tuning parameter 𝑟) for transcritical bifurcation.
8.2.3 Pitchfork bifurcation
This kind of bifurcation is common in physical systems that have a symmetry.
There are two subtypes of pitchfork bifurcation.
Supercritical pitchfork bifurcation
The prototypical example of supercritical pitchfork bifurcation is given by:
𝑑𝑦
𝑑𝑡 = 𝑟𝑦 − 𝑦3 = 𝑓(𝑦; 𝑟)
Note that the equation is invariant under the change of variable 𝑦 → −𝑦, which
signifies the symmetry. The system has up to three fixed points 𝑦∗ = 0 and
𝑦∗ = ±√𝑟. Figure 8.8 using the vector field, illustrates number and stability of
these fixed points for different values of 𝑟.
For 𝑟 < 0 we have 1 stable fixed point, at 𝑟 = 0 we have still one stable fixed
point and for 𝑟 > 0 we have three fixed points (two stable and a middle one
that is unstable). At the bifurcation point 𝑟 = 0 an exchange of stabilities takes
place between the fixed points. The changes in the number and stability of the
fixed points is summarised in the bifurcation diagram (see Figure 8.9).
Subcritical pitchfork bifurcation

The system again has up to three fixed points 𝑦∗ = 0 and 𝑦∗ = ± √−𝑟. Figure

For 𝑟 < 0 we have we have three fixed points (two unstable and a middle one

point 𝑟 = 0 an exchange of stabilities takes place between the fixed points. The

8.2.5 Impossibility of oscillations for one dimensional sys-
tems
Fixed points dominate the dynamics of first-order systems. The trajectory in
one dimension phase plane never reverses direction and the approach to equi-
librium is always monotonic, hence there is no over-shoot, damped oscillations
or periodic solutions. This is a topological constraint, if you flow monotonically
on a line, you’ll never come back to your starting point. Of course, if we were
moving on a circle rather than a line, we could eventually return to starting
point. For example the following ODE 𝑑𝜃
𝑑𝑡 = 𝜔 for the angle 𝜃 has the following
periodic solution 𝜃 = 𝜔𝑡 + 𝜃0.

In Chapter 9, we introduced multivariable functions and notions of differentia-

10.1 Change of Coordinates

could be a nonlinear transformation. In this section, we focus on the familiar
example of the change of coordinates from polar coordinates ( ⃗ 𝑥 = (𝑟, 𝜃)) to
cartesian ( ⃗ 𝑥 = (𝑥, 𝑦)) and vice versa to illustrate the concepts. We generalise

10.4 Sketching functions of two variables
Similar to the sketching of functions of one variable, we will use the following

• If 𝜆1, 𝜆2 ∈ ℝ −, we have Δ𝑓 < 0 as we move away from the stationary

1 The SpaceR n: Introduction to matrices and
vectors
‘Linear algebra’ is the theory and practice of manipulating vectors and matrices.
It’s one of the foundation stones of pure mathematics, but it’s also one of the
most widely applicable bits of mathematics there is: every scientific or technical
subject uses it somehow.

2.1 Row operations
Definition 2.10.Arow operationis one of the following procedures we can
apply to a matrix:
1.r i(λ): Multiply each entry in theith row by a real numberλ̸= 0.
2.r ij: Swap rowiand rowj.
3.r ij(λ): Addλtimes rowito rowj.

Notice that every row operation is invertible, its effect can be undone by
performing another row operation. In fact each operation can be undone by
performing a second row operation of the same type: for type 1 repeat the
operation replacingλbyλ −1, for type 2 simply repeat the operation, and for
type 3 repeat the operation replacingλwith−λ.
Proposition 2.12.LetAx=bbe a system of linear equations in matrix form.
Letrbe one of the row operations from Definition 2.10, and let(A ′|b′)be the
result of applyingrto the augmented matrix(A|b). Then a vectorvis a solution
ofAx=bif and only if it is a solution ofA ′x=b ′.

But theith andjth equations of the systemA ′x=b ′ tell us

Subtractingλtimes the first equation from the second gives us what we need.
The conclusion is that doing row operations to the augmented matrix does
not change the set of solutions for the corresponding system of equations. It can
moreover be shown that those are the only operations possible. This suggests
a good method for solving linear systems: do row operations on the augmented
matrix, until you get a system where the solutions are obvious.
2.2 A systematical way of solving linear systems
Let us first introduce some terminology.
Definition 2.13.The left-most non-zero entry in a non-zero row is called the
leading entryof that row.
Definition 2.14.A matrix is inechelon formif
1. the leading entry in each non-zero row equals 1, and
2. the leading 1 in each non-zero row is to the right of the leading 1 in any
row above it, and
11
3. the zero rows are below any non-zero rows.
Definition 2.15.A matrix is inrow reduced echelon form(RRE form for
short) if
1. it is in echelon form,
2. the leading entry in each non-zero row is the only non-zero entry in its
column.

The point of RRE form is that if we have a system of equations
Ax=b,
andAis in RRE form, then we can easily read off the solutions (if any). This
works as follows. We distinguish two cases: whether or not there are zero rows.
•In case there are no zero rowsthere is going to be either one solu-
tion or infinitely many, which we can read off as follows. Recall that the
ith column corresponds to the variablex i. If theith column contains a
leading 1 then we callx i abasic variable, otherwise we call it afree
variable. As there are no zero rows, every row contains a leading 1 and
will thus correspond to a basic variable. Using the entries in the row we
can then write that basic variable in terms of the free variables and the
corresponding value inb. For example, if we have a row
�0 1 0 5 3 
and the corresponding entry inbis 8 then this corresponds to the equation
x2 + 5x4 + 3x5 = 8, and we can thus writex 2 = 8−5x 4 −3x 5. The basic
variable here isx 2, andx 4 andx 5 are free variables.
There is a special case when every column ofAcontains a leading 1. In
this case we must have thatA=I n is the identity matrix. This means
that every variable is a basic variable and there is one solution given by
x1 =b 1, x2 =b 2, . . . , xn =b n.
12
If we are not in this special case we have at least one free variable, and
any value for that free variable will give a different solution, which means
there are infinitely many solutions. We say that the system isunderde-
termined.
•In case there are zero rowswe have to look at the corresponding entries
inb. Each zero row corresponds to an equation 0 =b j, wherejis the
number of the row. It should be clear that there can only be a solution if
bj = 0. So there are two possibilities.
1. Every entry inbthat corresponds to a zero row is 0. In this case we
can ignore the zero rows and just follow the previous case for when
there are no zero rows.
2. There is a non-zero entry inbthat corresponds to a zero row. In
this case the system is inconsistent, i.e. it has no solutions.

It is also true (although we won’t show this) that the RRE form of a matrix
is unique; if you apply any sequence of row operations which puts your matrix

Now we have a systematic procedure for solving a system of simultaneous linear
equationsAx=b

1. Form the augmented matrix (A|b).
2. Apply the algorithm above to put the augmented matrix into RRE form
(A′|b′).
3. Read off the solutions toA ′x=b ′.
In fact it’s not necessary to get the whole matrix (A ′|b′) into RRE form,
you can stop when the left blockA ′ is in RRE form. Doing further operations
to adjust the final column will not help you read the solutions.So whenever we
talk about bringing an augmented matrix into RRE form, we really just mean to
bring the left hand side (i.e. theA) into RRE form and everything to the right
of the vertical bar will then be used to read off any solutions.
Proposition 2.20.The number of solutions to a systemAx=bis always
either 0,1 or∞.
Proof.Suppose that the number of solutions is not 0 or 1, so that there are
at least two solutionsv̸=u. ThenAu=Av=b, soA(u−v) =0. Write
k=u−v, and note this is not the zero vector. Now for any numberλwe have
A(u+λk) =Au+λAk=b+0=b, sou+λkis a solution. Since we have
infinitely many possible choices forλ, there are infinitely many solutions.

As we already discussed, a system of linear equations can always be expressed
as a matrix equation

The caseb=0is especially important. If you think about it, a lot of the row
reduction algorithm only depends onA, and we would do most of the work by
just solving
Ax=0.
Systems of equations of this form (ie with allb i = 0) are sometimes called
homogeneous (can you think of why?). Note that these systems are always
consistent, becausex=0is a solution. So they always have either 1 or infinitely
many solutions.

Lemma 3.23.IfAis ann×n-matrix, andA ′ is obtained fromAby a row
operation, thenA ′ is invertible if and only ifAis.
Proof.AssumeAis invertible. SinceA ′ is obtained fromAby a row operation,
by our previous considerationsA ′ =RAwithR, for some elementary matrixR.
ButRis invertible, andAas well, hence so is there product andA ′ is invertible.
Conversely ifA ′ is invertible, sinceA ′ =RAandRis invertible,A=R −1A′
and by the same argument,Ais invertible.
Lemma 3.24.Ann×n-matrixAin RRE form is invertible if and only if it
has no zero rows.
Proof.One direction is given by Corollary 3.19. Conversely IfAhas no zero
rows then every row contains a leading 1, butAis square so also every col-
umn contains a leading 1. ThereforeAhas to be the identity matrix, which is
invertible
Lemma 3.25.Ann×n-matrixAis invertible if and only if its RRE form is
the identity matrixI n.
Proof.LetA ′ be the RRE form ofA. By Lemma 3.23 we have thatAis
invertible iffA ′ is invertible. IfA ′ is the identity matrix then it is invertible
and henceAis then invertible as well. For the other direction: assumeAis
invertible, thenA ′ is invertible. By Lemma 3.24 we have thatA ′ has no zero
rows. Then every row inA ′ contains a leading 1. AsAis square this means that
it has a leading 1 in each column as well. ThereforeA ′ has to be the identity
matrix.
Proposition 3.26.Ann×nmatrixAis invertible if and only if there is no
non-zero vectorv∈R n such thatAv=0.
Proof.We’ve already seen (Lemma 3.17(1)) that if we can find such avthen
Ais not invertible. So we just need to prove the converse statement.
SupposeAis not invertible, and consider the system of linear equations
Av=0. The augmented matrix is (A|0), and the RRE form of this matrix is
(A′|0) whereA ′ is the RRE form ofA. By our previous results the matrixA ′
has zero rows, so we know that the systemA′v=0has infinitely-many solutions
(since the vector on the right-hand-side has all entries zero). In particular there
is a solution withv̸=0.
We know have a good algorithm to compute inverses of matrices.

SinceR n . . . R1A=I n, applyingA −1 on both sides yieldsR n . . . R1 =

4 Vector spaces
Avector spaceis a kind of abstract algebraic structure. Just to name two
questions that motivate the study of vector spaces:
•Can we talk about vectors and matrices in a way that doesn’t involve any
choice of coordinates?
•In what way is solving a linear ODE similar to solving a system of simul-
taneous linear equations?
4.1 Axioms and examples
Consider the setR n with the binary operation + of vector addition. This allows
us to add vectors together, but there is more structure here, for example if we
take a vectorv∈R n and a numberλ∈Rwe can scalevbyλto get a new
vectorλv. This defines a function
R×R n →R n
(λ,v)7→λv
which we callscalar multiplication(in this context we refer to the real number
λas ascalar, partly to distinguish it from a vector). A vector space is an
abstract version of this structure.
Definition 4.1.Avector spaceis the following data:
•A setV. We will refer to the elements ofVasvectors.
•A binary operation + :V×V→V, which we calladdition.
•A functionR×V→V, which we callscalar multiplication. We don’t
usually write a symbol for scalar multiplication, we just writeλvfor the
result of applying this function to (λ,v).
We require that the following axioms hold:
1. The binary operation + behaves like addition onV. More precisely, for
anyv,w,u∈Vwe have:
(a)v+w=w+v,
(b) (v+w) +u=v+ (w+u).
Furthermore, there is an element0 V , called thezero vector, such that
for anyv∈V:
(c)0 V +v=v,
26
(d) there is somev ′ such thatv+v ′ =0 V .
2. For anyv∈Vwe have 1v=v.
3. For anyλ, µ∈Rand anyv∈Vwe haveλ(µv) = (λµ)v.
4. For anyλ∈Randu,v∈Vwe haveλ(u+v) =λu+λv. (“scalar
multiplication distributes over addition”)
5. For anyλ, µ∈Randv∈Vwe have (λ+µ)v=λv+µv. (“scalar
multiplication distributes over scalar addition”)
Remark 4.2.Some consequences of axiom 1 of vector spaces allow us to sim-
plify the notation, which we will do from here on out.
1. Due to 1(b) it does not matter how we place the brackets, so we just leave
them out. That is, we will writev+w+ufor(v+w)+uandv+(w+u),
which are the same anyway.
2. The vectorv ′ in 1(d) is called theadditive inverseofvand we just
write−vfor it. For vectorsvandwwe writev−was an abbreviation
forv+−w.
Remark 4.3.The structure we’ve just described is a vector space overR, or a
realvector space. If you take exactly the same definition but replaceRwithC
then you get acomplexvector space. In fact (if you know what afieldis) you
can define a vector space over any field, e.g. the rationalsQ, or a finite field
Fp. However you cannot useZ, the axioms do make sense (and will result in
something called amodule), but we will sometimes need to divide by non-zero
scalars.
Example 4.4.1. For anyn, the setR n is a vector space. This was our
prototype example!
In particularRitself is a vector space, since we can think of it as column
vectors of height 1.
2. LetR[X] ≤2 be the set of all polynomials of degree at most 2, so
R[x]≤2 =
 
aX 2 +bX+c:a, b, c∈R
	
.
Polynomials can be added together, and they can be multiplied by real
numbers. Also, the sum of two polynomials inR[X] ≤2 will always lie in
R[X] ≤2 (although the sum of two quadratics might be linear, or constant).
It’s pretty obvious that all the axioms hold, soR[X] ≤2 is a vector space.
Adding together polynomials, or scalar-multiplying them, is the same as
adding or scalar-multiplying their coefficients:
(aX 2 +bX+c) + (a ′X 2 +b ′X+c ′) = (a+a ′)X 2 + (b+b ′)X+ (c+c ′)
and
λ(aX 2 +bX+c) = (λa)X 2 + (λb)X+ (λc).
So this vector space looks a lot like the vector spaceR 3. In a similar way
we could consider the setR[X] ≤d of all polynomials of degree at mostd,
this is a vector space, and it looks a lot likeR d+1.
27
3. Now consider the setR[X] ofallpolynomials. This is still a vector space,
but it doesn’t ‘look like’R n for any finiten.
4. Consider the set of all possible functions fromRtoR, this is sometimes
denoted
RR ={f:R→R}.
Given two functionsf, g∈R R, we can define a new functionf+g∈R R
by
f+g:x7→f(x) +g(x).
Similarly if we haveλ∈Rwe can defineλf:R→Rbyλf:x7→λf(x)
(these operations are calledpoint-wiseaddition and scalar multiplica-
tion). It’s easy to check that all the axioms hold, soR R is a vector space.
Note that a polynomial is a particular kind of functionR→R, soR[X] is
a subset ofR R, and the rules for addition and scalar multiplication agree.
5. Let Mat n×m(R) be the set of alln×mmatrices with real number entries.
Matrices can be added together, and multiplied by scalars, and it’s easy
to see that all the vector space axioms hold. So Mat n×m(R) is a vector
space.
This example can be confusing because we usually think of a matrix as
something that operates on vectors. However if we only consider ad-
dition and scalar multiplication and ignore matrix multiplication, then
Matn×m(R) looks a lot likeR nm; we’re just writing ournmentries in a
rectangular grid instead of in a vertical column.
6. LetVbe a set with a single element, which we’ll denote by0 V . There is
a unique binary operation onVdefined by0 V +0 V =0 V . We can define
a scalar multiplication onVby declaring thatλ0 V =0 V for allλ∈R
(which is the only possibility), then all the vector space axioms hold. This
is called thezero vector space, and sometimes writtenR 0.
Now we have a decent store of examples we can proceed with some general
theory. We start with some simple consequences of the axioms.
Lemma 4.5.LetVbe a vector space.
(a) The zero vector is unique. That is, ifv∈Vis such thatv+w=wfor
allw∈Vthenv=0 V .
(b) The additive inverse of a vector is unique. That is, if for anyv,w∈V
we havev+w=0 V thenw=−v.
Proof.(a) For anyw∈Vwe havev+w=w. We pickw=0 V so that
we getv=v+0 V =0 V . The first equality is due to0 V being the zero
vector and the second equality is due to our assumption onv.

4.2 Subspaces
In Example 4.4 we have already seen examples of vector spaces being contained
in one another. Such as the spaceR[X] ≤2 of polynomials of degree at most 2,
this is contained in the spaceR[X] of all polynomials. This will be a useful
concept in general, so we make the following definition.
Definition 4.7.LetVbe a vector space. A subsetU⊆Vis called asubspace
ofVif:
1. Ifx,y∈Uthenx+y∈U.
2. The vector0 V lies inU.
3. Ifx∈Uthenλx∈Ufor anyλ∈R.
Remark.1) Condition 2 yields automatically thatUis not empty.
2) It follows from condition 3 that wheneverx∈Uthen−x∈U, because
−x= (−1)xas we have seen in Lemma 4.6(c).
All the vector space axioms hold automatically, because they hold inV. So
we can always think of a subspace as a vector space in its own right.

4.3 Spanning sets
In this section we will think about the ‘smallest’ subspace containing a given
subsetS ⊆V.
Definition 4.11.LetS ⊆Vbe any subset. Alinear combinationof elements
inSis a vectorx∈Vwhich can be written as
x=λ 1v1 +λ 2v2 +· · ·+λ nvn
wherev 1,v 2, . . . ,vn are vectors inS, andλ 1, λ2, . . . , λn are real numbers.
Note that in a vector space we can add together any finite number of vectors,
just by using + repeatedly. But we have no way of adding together aninfinite
collection of vectors. So even if the setSis infinite, any linear combination from
Scan only involve a finite number of elements.
We can now rephrase our definition of subspace.
Lemma 4.12.A non-empty subsetUof a vector spaceVis a subspace if and
only if every linear combination of elements ofUis again inU.
Proof.If every linear combination of elements ofUis again inUthen in par-
ticular we must haveu+v∈Ufor anyu,v∈U, and we must haveλu∈U
for anyu∈Uandλ∈R. This takes care of conditions 1 and 3 for subspaces
in Definition 4.7. AsUis non-empty it must contain somex∈U. So0 V = 0x
is also inU, taking care of condition 2. SoUis a subspace.
Conversely, supposeUis a subspace. Then a linear combination involving
only one element ofUis a vectorλuwithu∈U, and this lies inU. A linear
combination of two elements ofUis a vectorλ 1u1 +λ 2u2 whereu 1,u 2 ∈U,
and this lies inUsince bothλ 1u1 andλ 2u2 lie inU. Now an easy induction
argument shows that for anyn, a linear combination ofnelements ofUagain
lies inU.

4.4 Linear independence
Example 4.24.Recall from Example 4.17(2) that the subsetS={e 1,e 2,v= ( 1
1 )} ⊆
R2 is a spanning set. This spanning set is not as small as possible, the reason
is that
v=e 1 +e 2
so we don’t actually need the vectorv. Alternatively we could say that
e1 =v−e 2
so we don’t need the vectore 1, the setS ′ ={e 2,v}is still a spanning set. A
more symmetrical way to express these equations ise 1 +e 2 −v=0.
Definition 4.25.A subsetLof a vector spaceVis calledlinearly dependent
if we can find distinct vectorsv 1, . . . ,vn ∈ Land non-zero scalarsλ 1 ̸= 0, λ2 ̸=
0, . . . , λn ̸= 0 such that
λ1v1 +· · ·+λ nvn =0 V .
IfLis not linearly dependent we say that it islinearly independent.
34
A subset being linearly dependent means that there is some vector that can
be written as a linear combination of some other vectors in the same subset.
A few things to note about this definition:
•The empty subset∅ ⊆Vlinearly independent, because we cannot find
any vectors in this set at all.
•The word ‘distinct’ is important in this definition, otherwise we could just
takev 1 =v 2, and write
v1 −v 2 =0 V
and then every (non-empty) subset ofVwould be linearly dependent.
•IfLis a finite subset{u 1, ...,u k}, thenLis linearly dependent iff we can
find a set of scalarsλ 1, ..., λk such thatλ 1u1 +· · ·+λ kuk =0 V , and not
all of theλ i’s are zero. It’s not necessary for everyλ i to be non-zero,
because the definition only involves asubsetofL.
•IfL ′ ⊆ LandL ′ is linearly dependent, thenLis also linearly dependent
(just use the samev i’s andλ i’s). So ifLis linearly independent then any
subset ofLis also linearly independent.

Here is another important way to characterise a basis.
Proposition 4.30.LetB={v 1, ...,v n} ⊆Vbe a (finite) basis of a vector space
V. Then every vector inVcan be written as a linear combination of elements
inB, in a unique way.
Conversely, any finite subsetBwith this property is a basis.
Proof.SupposeB ⊆Vis a basis. ThenBis a spanning set, so certainly any
x∈Vcan be written as a linear combination of elements ofB, but we must
show that this can only be done in one way. So, suppose that we can write
x=λ 1v1 +...+λ nvn andx=µ 1v1 +...+µ nvn.
It follows that
(λ1 −µ 1)v1 +...+ (λ n −µ n)vn =0 V
but sinceBis linearly independent, we must haveλ i −µ i = 0 for eachi. So
λi =µ i for eachi.
Conversely, suppose thatBhas the stated property. ThenBis a spanning
set. To see thatBis linearly independent, observe that we can write
0V = 0v1 +...+ 0v n
so ifλ 1v1 +...+λ nvn =0 V then uniqueness says thatλ i = 0 for alli. Hence
Bis a basis.
The proposition is also true for infinite bases, but the argument needs a few
modifications.
Example 4.31.The setS={e 1,e 2,v= ( 1
1 )} ⊆R 2 is a spanning set, but it is
not linearly independent. We observed in Example 4.17(2) that every vector in
R2 can be written as a linear combination of elements inS, but not in a unique
way. The non-uniqueness is precisely due to the fact thate 1 +e 2 −v=0.
So if we find a (finite) basisB={v 1, ...,v n}, then any vectorx∈Vcan be
uniquely expressed as
x=λ 1v1 +...+λ nvn.
37
The scalarsλ i in this expression are called thecoefficients ofxwith respect
toB. Every vectorx∈Vcorresponds to a unique set of coefficientsλ 1, ..., λn.
To put it more formally, the basisBallows us to define a function
FB :R n − →V

(strictly speaking we also need to choose an ordering of the elements ofBbefore
we can define this function). What Proposition 4.30 says is that this function
FB is a bijection. So if we have a basis forV, then we get a bijection betweenV
andR n, wherenis the size of the basis. Notice that the inverse functionF −1

sends a vectorxto its coefficients with respect toB.
In factF B is not just a bijection, it also preserves all the vector space struc-
ture, because adding or scalar-multiplying vectors is the same as adding or
scalar-multiplying their coefficients. This kind of bijection is called anisomor-
phismof vector spaces, but we will come back to this later (see Proposition
5.18).
Lemma 4.32.LetS ⊆Vbe a spanning set, and suppose thatSis not linearly

spanning set.
Proof.SinceSis linearly dependent, it is possible to find distinct vectors

Corollary 4.33.Any finite spanning set contains a basis.
Proof.LetS ⊆Vbe a finite spanning set. IfSis not linearly independent,
then we can remove a vector fromSand still have a spanning set. Repeat this
procedure until we have a basis.
Corollary 4.34.Any finite-dimensional vector spaceVhas a basis.
Proof.The definition of ‘finite-dimensional’ is thatVcontains a finite spanning
set.

4.5 Bases and dimension
As you may have guessed, a basis is exactly a spanning set with the minimal
possible size. So every basis ofVmust have the same size, and that size is the
dimension ofV. In this section we will prove this fact, first by row reduction,
then again by thinking about ways in which we can modify spanning sets.
Theorem 4.35.LetVbe a finite-dimensional vector space withdimV=n.
Then any basis ofVis finite and has sizen.
So you candefinethe dimension ofVto be the size of any basis ofV: the
theorem shows that this is equivalent to Definition 4.22.
Example 4.36.In Example 4.28 we saw many examples of bases, so for each
one we can now deduce the dimension of the corresponding vector space.
1. The dimension ofR n isn, because the standard basis hasnvectors in it.
2. The dimension ofR[X] ≤d isd+ 1, because we have a basis{1, X, ..., X d}.

sisting of four matrices (see Example 4.28(4)). You can generalize this

4.5.1 First way to show dimension is well-defined: row reduction
We’ve already gone through a lot of work learning about the row reduction
algorithm, we it turns out we can apply all that to prove Theorem 4.35.
This proof starts by assuming the two bases are finite, which is slightly cheat-
ing. But you can think about how to prove any basis for a finite dimensional
vector space must be finite (it’s tricky but not that bad!).
Matrix proof of Theorem 4.35.LetVbe a vector space, and letB={b 1, ...,b n}
andC={c 1, ...,c m}be two bases forV. We want to show they are the same
size.
The change-of-basis matrix fromCtoBis then×mmatrix

So the columns are the coordinates ofc 1, ...,c m in the basisB. We will meet
this matrix again later in section 5.
Let’s analyze the equationAx=0. If you expand this out it says
x1FB(c1) +· · ·+x mFB(cm) =0
Taking coordinates preserves linear combinations (check!) so this becomes
FB(x1c1 +· · ·+x mcm) =0.
39
This says that theB-coordnates ofx 1c1 +· · ·+x ncm are all zero, i.e.
x1c1 +· · ·+x mcm = 0b1 +· · ·+ 0b n =0.
We already proved that theCcoordinates are unique, so there must be only one
possibly choice ofx i that makes this work, and that is
x1 =· · ·=x m = 0.
So, we proved that the equationAx=0has only one solution. This means
there are no free variables when we row reduceA.
Now is the important observation: the only way a matrix in REF can have
no free variables is if it has at least as many rows as columns (draw a picture!).
ButAhasnrows andmcolumns, son≥m.
We can finish with the mathematician’s favorite trick: if we did all the same
things as before, switching the roles ofBandC, then we would end up with
m≥n. Hencem=n.
4.5.2 Second way to show dimension is well-defined: the Steinitz
exchange lemma
There is also an abstract way of doing this, just vector space manipulations. This
is nice for various reasons. It means we don’t need to rely on using matrices and
row reduction, and the fact that our row reduction algorithm terminates and
does what we say it does. For the sake of time, I’ll skip this in lectures, which
means this subsectionisn’t examinable. But you might prefer this approach,
so feel free to read it.

Corollary 4.39.LetVbe a finite-dimensional vector space, letS ⊆Vbe a
finite spanning set and letL ⊆Vbe a linearly independent subset. ThenLis
finite and#L ≤#S.
Proof.Assume for a contradiction that #L>#S. Then there must be a finite
L′ ⊆ Lsuch that #L ′ >#S(ifLis finite we can just takeL ′ =L, ifLis
infinite just pick a big enough finite subset). By the previous Proposition 4.38
there must beT ⊆ Ssuch that #T= #L ′, which gives a contradiction as then
#S ≥#T= #L ′ >#S.
Abstract proof of Theorem 4.35.By definition,Vhas a spanning setSof size
n, and no smaller spanning set. LetBbe a basis forV. ThenBis a spanning
set so #B ≥n, butBis linearly independent so by the previous corollary
#B ≤#S=n. Hence #B=n.

Lemma 4.41.IfVis not finite-dimensional, then for anyn∈Nwe can find

Proof.We will do an induction onn. The casen= 0 works, because the empty

Lemma 4.42.IfVis finite-dimensional anddimV=n, then any linearly
independent subsetL ⊆Vof sizenmust be a basis.
Proof.Assume thatLis linearly independent but not a basis. Then it does not

Lemma 4.43.IfVis finite-dimensional then any linearly independent subset
is contained in a basis.
Proof.LetL ⊆Vbe a linearly independent subset which is not a basis. Then
#L<dimV=n(if #L= dimVit is a basis by the Lemma 4.42, and we must
have #L ≤dimVby Corollary 4.39). ThenLcannot be a spanning set, so
we can add a vector toLto get a larger linearly independent subset. We can
repeat this until we have a linearly independent subset of size dimV, this must
be a basis by Lemma 4.42.
Note that all three lemmas are based on the same idea: ifL ⊆Vis linearly
independent and does not spanV, then we can find a vectorv∈(V\spanL)
and add it toLto formL ∪ {v}and by our previous results this larger set is
still linearly independent.
Lemma 4.43 says that given a linearly independent subsetL={x 1, ...,x k}
of a (finite-dimensional) vector space, we can always add in more vectors and
end up with a basisB={x 1, ...,x k,x k+1, ...,x n}. This procedure is called
extending to a basis.

Proposition 4.45.LetVbe a finite-dimensional vector space and letU⊆V
be a subspace.
(a)Uis finite-dimensional.
(b)dimU≤dimV.
(c) IfdimU= dimVthenU=V.
Proof.(a) SupposeUis not finite dimensional. Then by Lemma 4.41 we can

Example 4.46.What are the possible subspaces ofR 2? Since dimR 2 = 2, a
subspaceU⊆R 2 can only have dimension 0, 1 or 2. If dimU= 0 thenU={0}
(see Example 4.23(1)) and if dimU= 2 thenU=R 2. So a proper subspace
must have dimension 1, and then it has a spanning set consisting of a single
non-zero vectorv. In this case
U={λv:λ∈R} ⊆R 2
which is a line through the origin.
Example 4.47.What are the possible subspaces ofR 3? Repeating the argu-
ment of the previous example, we have{0}, andR 3, and all lines through the
origin, but there are also proper subspaces of dimension 2. In this caseUis
spanned by a pairu,vof linearly independent vectors, so
U={λu+µv:λ, µ∈R} ⊆R 3.
This is a plane through the origin.

5.2 Linear maps and bases
From now on we will assume all our vector spaces are finite-dimensional.
We’ve seen that ann×kmatrixAdefines a linear mapT A fromR k toR n.
In fact, these are the only possible linear maps fromR k toR n. This is one of
the main important results in linear algebra.
Proposition 5.12.Letf:R k →R n be linear. Thenf=T A for some matrix
A∈Mat n×k(R).
Proof.Consider the standard basis vectorse 1, ...,e k ∈R k. Then we get the
followingk-vectors inR n
v1 =f(e 1),v 2 =f(e 2), ...,v k =f(e k)∈R n.
We construct now ann×kmatrixA, so that thei-th column ofAisv i. Then
TA(ei) =Ae i =v i for eachi.
We will now show thatf=T A. Take any vectorx∈R k, we can write it as
x=λ 1e1 +...+λ kek
47
(theλ i’s are just the entries inx). Since bothfandT A are linear maps, it
follows that
f(x) =λ 1f(e 1) +. . .+λ kf(e k)
=λ 1v1 +. . .+λ kvk
=λ 1Ae1 +. . .+λ kAek
=A(λ 1e1 +. . .+λ kek)
=Ax=T A(x),
so the functionsfandT A are identical.
The proof is based on the fact thatT A(ei) =f(e i) for eachi, i.e. they agree
on the special choice of the standard basis. Then it followed by linearity that
f=T A. An important remark is that we could have taken any other basis. In
fact we can repeat this idea in a more abstract way:
Proposition 5.13.Letf:U→Vandg:U→Vbe two linear maps, and
letB={b 1, ...,b k}be a basis forU. Suppose thatf(b i) =g(b i)for each
i= 1, ..., k. Thenf=g.
Proof.Any vectorx∈Ucan be written as a linear combination
x=λ 1b1 +...+λ kbk.
Since bothfandgare linear it follows that
f(x) =λ 1f(b 1) +...+λ kf(b k) =λ 1g(b1) +...+λ kg(bk) =g(x)
sofandgare the same function.
This means that if two linear maps agree on a basis, they are actually equal!
The next natural question is the following. LetB={b 1, ...,b k}be a basis
of a vector spaceU, and suppose we take any list of vectorsv 1, ...,v k in another
vector spaceV. Can we find a linear mapf:U→Vsuch that for eachi
the basis vectorb i maps to the vectorv i? The answer is yes as the following
proposition shows.
Proposition 5.14.LetUandVbe vector spaces. LetB={b 1, ...,b k}be a
basis forU, and let{v 1, ...,v k}be any set ofkvectors inV. Then there is a
unique linear mapf:U→Vsuch thatf(b i) =v i for eachi.
Proof.SinceBis a basis forU, any vectorx∈Ucan be written as a linear
combination
x=λ 1b1 +...+λ kbk
in a unique way. Recall that these numbersλ 1, ..., λk are called the ‘coefficients
ofxwith respect toB’. Define a functionf:U→Vby
f:x7→λ 1v1 +...+λ kvk
whereλ 1, ..., λk are the coefficients ofxwith respect toB.
Obviouslyf(b i) =v i for eachi(takex=b i, thenλ i = 1 andλ j = 0, for
allj̸=i).
48
We need to show thatfis linear. Let
x=λ 1b1 +...+λ kbk andy=µ 1b1 +...+µ kbk,
then
x+y= (λ 1 +µ 1)b1 +...+ (λ k +µ k)bk
and
λx= (λλ 1)b1 +...+ (λλ k)bk
for anyλ∈R. It follows that
f(x+y) = (λ 1 +µ 1)v1 +...+ (λ k +µ k)vk =f(x) +f(y)
andf(λx) = (λλ 1)v1 +...+ (λλ k)vk =λf(x).
Moreoverfis the only possible linear map with this property, ifg:U→V
is another linear map satisfyingg(b i) =v i for alli, thenf=gby Proposition
5.13.
So we can define a linear map unambiguously by specifying what it should
do to a basis—this trick is calledextending linearly.
Example 5.15.There is a unique linear mapf:R[X] ≤2 →R[X] ≤1 such that
f:X 2 7→3X+ 2, f:X7→0,andf: 17→2X.
We getfby taking these values and then extending linearly, so
f:aX 2 +bX+c7→a(3X+ 2) +c(2X)
= (3a+ 2c)X+ 2a.
5.3 Isomorphisms
Definition 5.16.A linear mapf:U→Vbetween two vector spaces is called
anisomorphismiffis bijective. If there exists an isomorphism fromUtoV
we say thatUisisomorphictoV, and writeU ∼= V.
Iff:U→Vis an isomorphism then sincefis a bijection there is an
inverse functionf −1 :V→U. It’s not hard to check thatf −1 must be linear
(exercise), sof −1 is also an isomorphism. This shows that ifU ∼= Vthen
V ∼= U. Furthermore, iff:U→Vandg:V→Ware both isomorphisms then
g◦f:U→Wis an isomorphism (exercise), so ifU ∼= V ∼= Wthen alsoU ∼= W.
Finally, the identity function id V :V→Vis an isomorphism, soV ∼= V. We
thus see that the relation ∼= of being isomorphic is an equivalence relation on
the class of all vector spaces.
Also note that from Lemma 5.9, a linear mapf:U→Vis an isomorphism
if and only if Imf=V(sofis surjective) and Kerf={0 U }(sofis injective).

Is it possible to find an isomorphism betweenR2 andR 3? The answer is no, if
two vector spaces are isomorphic then they must have the same dimension. This
is not instantly obvious, but it does follow quickly from the following lemma.
Lemma 5.20.Letf:U→Vbe a linear map, and letB={b 1, ...,b k}be a
basis forU. LetC={f(b 1), ..., f(bk)} ⊆V. Then:
(i)Cis a spanning set ifffis surjective.
(ii)Cis linearly independent ifffis injective.
(iii)Cis a basis ifffis an isomorphism.
Proof.(i) Any vectorx∈Ucan be written uniquely as a linear combination
x=λ 1b1 +...+λ kbk, and then
f(x) =λ 1f(b 1) +...+λ kf(b k).
So if a vector lies in Imfthen it can be written as a linear combination
of elements fromC, and every linear combination occurs in this way, so
Imf= spanC. Hencefis surjective iffCis a spanning set.
(ii) The setCis linearly dependent iff we can find scalarsλ 1, .., λk which are
not all zero, such that
0V =λ 1f(b 1) +...+λ kf(b k) =f(λ 1b1 +...+λ kbk).
This is the same thing as finding a non-zero vectorx=λ 1b1 +...+
λkbk ∈Usuch thatf(x) =0 V . SoCis linearly independent if and only
if Kerf={0 U }, which is true ifffis injective by Lemma 5.9.
(iii) Combine parts (i) and (ii).
51
Corollary 5.21.U ∼= ViffdimU= dimV.
Proof.We first prove the left to right direction. Letf:U→Vbe an isomor-
phism. Pick a basisB={b 1, . . . ,bk}forU, and letC={f(b 1), . . . , f(bk)} ⊆
V. Proposition 5.20 says thatCis a basis forV, and sincefis a bijectionB
andChave the same size. So dimU= dimVby Theorem??.
Conversely, letn= dimU= dimV. Then by Proposition 5.18 we have
U ∼= Rn ∼= V, and thusU ∼= V.
Corollary 5.22.Letf:U→Vbe a linear map, and suppose thatdimU=
dimV. Then the following are equivalent:
(i)fis injective.
(ii)fis surjective.
(iii)fis an isomorphism.
Proof.By definition an isomorphism must be both injective and surjective, so
we only need to prove that (i) =⇒(iii) and (ii) =⇒(iii). Letn= dimU=
dimV. Pick any basisB={b 1, ...,b n}forU, and letC={f(b 1), ..., f(bn)} ⊆
V.
(i) =⇒(iii). Supposefis injective. ThenCis linearly independent, and
also #C= dimV. SoCis a basis for V by Lemma 4.41(b), hencefis an
isomorphism.
(ii) =⇒(iii). Supposefis surjective. ThenCis a spanning set forV. If
Cwas not a basis then we could remove a vector and still have a spanning set
(Lemma 4.32), but this is impossible because #C ≤dimV. SoCis a basis and
hencefis an isomorphism.
We’ve seen that choosing a basis forVgives us an isomorphism fromR n to
V. Another consquence of the above lemma is that we can reverse this:
Corollary 5.23.Iff:R n →Vis an isomorphism, then the set
C={f(e 1), ..., f(en)}
is a basis forV.
So choosing a basis forUisthe same thingas finding an isomorphism from
Rn toU.
5.4 The Rank-Nullity Theorem
The Rank-Nullity theorem is a very useful result about linear maps.
Definition 5.24.LetUandVbe vector spaces and letf:U→Vbe a linear
map. Then
•therank off, written rankf, is the dimension of Imf
•thenullity off, written nullf, is the dimension of Kerf.
52
Remember that ifAis ann×mmatrix then there is a transformation
TA :R m →R n and Im(T A) = col(A) is the column space ofA. Similarly
Ker(TA) = Ker(A) is the null-space ofA.
Since rank and nullity are especially important for matrices, we will write
rank(A) = rank(TA) and null(A) = null(TA), by definition.
Proposition 5.25.LetAbe ann×mmatrix. Then
1. The columns ofAin positions corresponding to leading columns of the
REF ofAform a basis forcol(A). Therefore the rank ofAis the number
of leading1s in the REF.
2. The basic solutions toAx= 0from a basis for the null-space ofA. There-
fore the nullity ofAis the number of free columns in the REF ofA.
Proof.For 1, letBbe the RREF ofA. Using elementary matrices to represent
row operations, we know that there is an invertible matrixRsuch thatRA=B.
By definition of RREF, the leading columns inBare just standard basis
vectorse 1, ...,e k for somek≤n, and all the entries in rows belowkare zero.
This impliese 1, ...,e k is a basis for col(B) = span(e 1, ...,e k).
Sincee 1, ...,e k are columns ofB(in the positions of leading 1s)R −1e1, ..., R−1ek
are columns ofA=R −1B(in the corresponding positions). The vectors
R−1e1, ..., R−1ek are linearly independent sincee 1, ...,e k are linearly indepen-
dent.
Ifv∈col(A) thenRv∈col(RA) = col(B) = span(e 1, ...,e k) and applying
R−1 shows that col(RA) = col(R−1B) = span(R−1e1, ..., R−1ek), so the vectors
span the column space ofA. This means they are a basis of col(A), which is
what we wanted to show!
Part 2 is explained in Example 4.29.
Theorem 5.26(Rank-Nullity theorem).LetUandVbe vector spaces and let
f:U→Vbe a linear map. Then
rankf+ nullf= dimU.
The proof is fairly similar to the proof of Lemma 5.20.
Proof.Pick any basisK={v 1, ...,v k}for the vector space Kerf, sok= nullf.
ThenKis a linearly independent subset ofU, so by Lemma 4.43 we can extend
it to a basis
B={v 1, ...,v k,u 1, ...,u r}
forU. Then dimU=k+r, so we need to prove thatr= rankf. To do this,
we will show that the set of vectorsC={f(u 1), ..., f(ur)}forms a basis for the
space Imf.
Any vectorx∈Ucan be written as a linear combination
x=λ 1v1 +...+λ kvk +µ 1u1 +...+µ rur
for some unique scalarsλ i, µj ∈R. Then
f(x) =µ 1f(u 1) +...+µ rf(u r)
53
by the linearity off, and the fact thatv i ∈Kerffor alli. This shows that
every element in Imfis a linear combination of elements inC, soCis a spanning
set for Imf.
Now suppose thatCis not linearly independent, so we can find scalars
µ1, ...., µr, not all zero, such that
µ1f(u 1) +...+µ rf(u r) =0 V .
Sety=µ 1u1 +...+µ rur, then this says thatf(y) =0 V soy∈Kerf. Hence
we can expressyas a linear combination
y=λ 1v1 +...+λ kvk
of elements ofK, but this says that
λ1v1 +...+λ kvk −µ 1u1 −...−µ rur =0 U
which is impossible sinceBis linearly independent. This is a contradiction, so
Cis linearly independent, henceCis a basis for Imf.

Linear Algebra and Groups Part 2
Marie-Amelie Lawn
based on notes by Matthew Towers, edited by Alexei Skorobogatov
Contents
1 Introduction to determinants 1
2 Eigenvalues and eigenvectors 4
2.1 Basic definitions . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 4
2.2 Diagonalisation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 7
2.3 Gram–Schmidt process and orthogonality . . . . . . . . . . . . . 11
2.4 Real symmetric matrices and orthogonality . . . . . . . . . . . . 13
1 Introduction to determinants
We have seen the definition of determinant for a 2 × 2-matrix and the fact that
it is closely related to the existence of an inverse. We are going to generalise
this notion to n × n-matrices. Let us first look at 3 × 3-matrices.
Let A =

invertible, then the 2×2-submatrix ˜A is also not invertible. Hence A is invertible
if and only if det ˜A ̸= 0. But det ˜A equals
a11a22a33 − a11a23a32 + a12a23a31 − a12a21a33 + a13a21a32 − a13a22a31 (1)

(2)
These two lines give rise to two equivalent definitions for the determinant.
Let us start with the second line (2). This is the definition of the determinant
of a 3 × 3-matrix you probably know, using the expansion along the first row.
In fact, it does not matter which row or column you use for your expansion; you
will get the same answer.
Definition 1. Let A be an n × n-matrix, and Aij be the submatrix obtained by
deleting the i-th row and j-th column of A. Aij is called the minor of A.
Example 1. Let A =


1 2 3
4 5 6
7 8 9

. Then A11 =


, A32 =

4 6

.
Definition 2. The determinant of an n × n-matrix A = (aij) is given by
det A =
nX

(−1)i+jaij det Aij =
nX

The first sum is called the expansion along the i-th row ( i is fixed), the
second sum is the expansion along the j-th column ( j is fixed). This is a
recursive definition: the same rule can be used to calculate det Aij in terms of
(n − 2) × (n − 2)-matrices, and so on.
Example 2. We compute the determinant of A =



2 2 1 0


. Note that
since it does not matter which expansion we choose, it is better to choose an
expansion along a row or a column which has a lot of zeros. In this case we
choose the first row.
det A = a11 det A11 − a12 det A12 + a13 det A13 − a14 det A14
= det
