where we have expanded along the first row for the first 3 × 3-matrix, and along
the third row for the other one.
Let us now go back to equation (1). We notice that each term is of the form
±a1ia2ja3k
where the numbers i, j, k are all different. We choose three entries from A, one
from each row and one from each column, then we sum all such monomials with
some signs. Another way to say this is that ( i, j, k) is a permutation of (1, 2, 3),
and we sum over all permutations. By the Group Theory part of the course
that the set of all permutations of (1 , 2, 3) is a group with 6 elements called the
symmetric group S3.
Now we define the sign of a permutation.
3
Definition 3. Let σ in Sn be a permutation. An inversion (i, j) of σ is a
pair of positive integers i, j such that i < j , but σ(i) ≥ σ(j). The sign of a
permutation is defined to be
sgn(σ) :=
(
1, if the number of inversions in σ is even .
−1, if the number of inversions in σ is odd .
Now we can rewrite the sum in equation (1) as follows:
det(A) =
X
σ∈S3
sgn(σ)a1σ(1)a2σ(2)a3σ(3)
This suggests a second definition for the determinant of an n × n matrix:
Definition 4. Let A = (aij) be an n × n-matrix. The determinant of A is
det(A) =
X
σ∈Sn
sgn(σ)a1σ(1)a2σ(2)...anσ(n)
Each term in the sum is a product of n entries, and there are n! terms, so for
large matrices it may be difficult to compute the determinant. However, there
are some easy special cases.
Example 3.
1. Let A be the identity matrix In, so aij = 1 if i = j and 0 if i ̸= j. If
σ ∈ Sn is not the identity permutation, then there is some 1 ≤ i ≤ n with
σ(i) ̸= i, and then aiσ(i) = 0 hence a1σ(1)a2σ(2)...anσ(n) = 0. So all but
one terms in det(A) are zero, thus
det(A) = a11a22...ann = 1.
2. The same argument shows that if A is a diagonal matrix, then det(A) is
the product of the entries on the main diagonal.
3. A square matrix A is called upper-triangular if aij = 0 when i > j , that
is, all the entries of A below the main diagonal are zero. If σ ∈ Sn is a
non-trivial permutation, then i > σ (i) for some i (Problem Sheet 6, Q2).
Now the same argument as above gives that det(A) is the product of the
entries on the main diagonal.
4. Suppose that A has a row of zeroes, so for some i we have ai1 = ai2 =
... = ain = 0. Then for any σ ∈ Sn we have aiσ(i) = 0, so every term in
det(A) is zero thus det(A) = 0.
5. Suppose that A has a column of zeroes, so for some j we have a1j = a2j =
... = anj = 0. For any σ ∈ Sn we have an inverse permuation σ−1, and if
we set k = σ−1(j) then akσ(k) = akj = 0. So again every term in det(A)
is zero and we have det(A) = 0.
Note that our two definitions are equivalent.
We now state without proof some of important properties of the determinant.
The following proposition describes the behaviour of the determinant under
elementary row operations.
4
Proposition 1. Let A and B be square matrices of the same size. Then
1) If B is obtained from A by swapping two rows of A, then det B = − det A.
2) If B is obtained from A by multiplying a row of A by a scalar λ, then
det B = λ det A.
3) If B is obtained from A by adding a multiple of a row to another row, then
det B = det A.
The next proposition is very important.
Proposition 2. Let A be a square matrix. Then
1) det A = det AT
2) det AB = det A det B.
Proposition 3. An n × n-matrix A is invertible if and only if det A ̸= 0.
Proof. If A is invertible, then there exists a matrix A−1 such that AA−1 =
In. By Proposition 2 (2) we have det( AA−1) = det A det(A−1) = 1, hence
det A ̸= 0. Conversely, let A be a square matrix with det A ̸= 0, and let A′ be
a matrix obtained from A by row operations. Then Proposition 1 implies that
det(A′) ̸= 0. In particular, if A′ is the reduced row echelon form of A, then A′
is obtained from A by a sequence of row operations, so det(A′) ̸= 0. The matrix
A′ is upper-triangular, thus det( A′) is the product of diagonal entries. Since A′
is in reduced row echelon form, det( A′) ̸= 0 implies that A′ = In. Thus A is
invertible.
2 Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
2.1 Basic definitions
These are some of the most important concepts in linear algebra, both for
applications and for pure mathematics. We shall give all definitions and results
for real matrices, but keep in mind that we could replace R by the field of
complex numbers C everywhere in this section without changing the definitions
and most of the statements. We will point out the few differences between the
real and complex cases along the way.
Definition 5. Let A be an n × n matrix, and let λ ∈ R be a number. We say
that λ is an eigenvalue of A if there exists a non-zero vector v ∈ Rn satisfying
Av = λv.
We call such a v an eigenvector of A (for the eigenvalue λ).
So eigenvectors are certain vectors on which A acts in an especially simple
way: by a scalar multiplication. They are useful — if we want to understand a
complicated matrix, knowing its eigenvectors and eigenvalues goes a long way
toward this.
Remark 1. Let A be an n × n-matrix. Obviously, the non-zero vector v is an
eigenvector of A with eigenvalue λ if and only if (A − λIn)v = 0.
5
Example 4. 1. Any vector v ∈ Rn satisfies the equation
Inv = v = 1v
so 1 is an eigenvalue of the identity matrix In, and any non-zero vector is
an eigenvector for this eigenvalue.
Now take any λ ̸= 1, and try to solve the equation (In − λIn)v = 0. The
matrix In − λIn is diagonal with all its diagonal entries non-zero, so it is
an invertible matrix, and the equation has a unique solution v = 0. This
shows that 1 is the only eigenvalue of the matrix In.
2. Any vector v satisfies
0n×nv = 0 = 0v
so 0 is an eigenvalue for the zero matrix, and any non-zero v is an eigen-
vector for this eigenvalue. A similar argument to the previous example
shows that 0 is the only eigenvalue.
3. Let A =

2 1


of A (for any eigenvalue). For example

4. Let A = diag(d1, ..., dn) be a diagonal matrix. Then the standard basis
vectors satisfy
Aek = dkek
so each dk is an eigenvalue of A.
6
Proposition 4. Let A be an n × n-matrix. Then λ is an eigenvalue of A if and
only if the matrix A − λIn is not invertible.
Proof. This is just a restatement of proposition from last term, because λ is
an eigenvalue of A if and only if we can find a non-zero vector v satisfying
(A − λIn)v = 0.
Hence to find out if a matrix A has eigenvectors to the eigenvalue λ, just
solve the linear system ( A − λIn)v = 0.
Example 5. Let A =
1 6
5 2

. We will show that 7 is an eigenvalue of A.
We write the augmented matrix (A − 7I2|0). Its RRE form is
1 −1 0

Remark 2. By our previous results a scalar λ is an eigenvalue of a 2×2-matrix
if and only if λ is a root of the equation det(tI2 − A) = 0. But
det(tI2 − A) = det
t − a11 −a12
−a21 t − a22

= t2 − (a11 + a22)t + a11a22 − a21a12
= t2 − trAt + det A,
where the trace of trA is defined to be the sum of the diagonal entries of a square
matrix A. This polynomial is called the characteristic polynomial of A. Its
roots are exactly the eigenvalues of A. Note that therefore a 2 × 2-matrix can
have at most 2 distinct eigenvalues.
For example the characteristic polynomial of the matrix A =
1 2
2 1

is
det(tI2 − A) = t2 − 2t − 3.
The roots of this polynomial are λ = 3 and λ = −1, so the only eigenvalues of
A are 3 and −1.
Similarly we have the following
Definition 6. Let A ∈ Mn×n(R). The determinant
pA(t) := det(tIn − A)
is called the characteristic polynomial.
It is easy to see that it is indeed a polynomial of order n. Hence the maximal
number of distinct eigenvalues for an n × n-matrix is n (see problem sheet).
Note that consequently for a real matrix doesn’t necessarily have real eigen-
values. On the contrary, we will see later that a complex matrix has at least
one complex eigenvalue.
7
2.2 Diagonalisation
Definition 7. Let A and B be n × n-matrices. We call A similar to B if there
exists an invertible matrix P , such that A = P −1BP .
Similarity is an equivalence relation. This says that the following three
properties hold.
Lemma 1. Let A, B, C be n × n-matrices.
(i) Reflexivity: A is similar to A.
(ii) Symmetry: if A is similar to B, then B is similar to A.
(iii) Transitivity: if A is similar to B, and B is similar to C, then A is
similar to C.
Proof. (i) is clear: take P = In.
(ii) A = P −1BP implies P AP −1 = B.
(iii) A = P −1BP and B = Q−1CQ imply A = P −1Q−1CQP = (QP )−1C(QP ).
Proposition 5. Similar matrices have the same characteristic polynomials,
hence the same eigenvalues.
Proof. Let A and B be similar matrices. Now by the rules for determinants we
have
det(tIn − B) = det( tIn − P −1AP )
= det( P −1(tIn − A)P ) = det P −1 det(tIn − A) detP
= det P det P −1 det(tIn − A) = det(tIn − A).
Similar matrices do not necessarily have the same eigenvectors but there
is a simple connection between them. Namely if v is an eigenvector of A
with eigenvalue λ so that Av = λv, then P −1A(P P −1)v = P −1λv. There-
fore BP −1v = λP −1v, hence w := P −1v is an eigenvector of B.
Definition 8. A matrix A is called diagonalisable if it is similar to a diagonal
matrix.
Lemma 2. Let A be an n × n matrix. Suppose that we can find n eigenvectors
v1, v2, ...,vn of A, with corresponding eigenvalues λ1, λ2, ..., λn. Let
P :=
�
v1|v2|...|vn

,
i.e. the matrix whose columns are the vectors vi. If P is invertible, then A is
diagonalisable and the corresponding diagonal matrix is given by
P −1AP = diag(λ1, . . . , λn).
Proof. Write D = diag(λ1, . . . , λn). The equation P −1AP = D is equivalent
to the equation AP = P D. The kth column of the matrix AP is Avk = λkvk
(since vk is an eigenvector of A with eigenvalue λk), and the kth column of P D
is P (λkek) = λkPek = λkvk. So AP = P D.
8
Remark 3. 1) The same calculation shows that, conversely, if A is diago-
nalisable, i.e. P −1AP = D for some invertible matrix P and diagonal
matrix D = diag(λ1, . . . , λn), then the columns of P are the eigenvectors
of A so that λi is the eigenvalue of the i-th column of P .
2) Not every matrix is diagonalisable, because it may be impossible to find n
appropriate distinct eigenvectors of A.
Example 6. Let A be the matrix A =
1 2
2 1


1

P −1AP =


A =
0 1

The characteristic polynomial of A is det

and this is never invertible. So A is not diagonalisable.
Theorem 1. If an n × n-matrix A has n distinct eigenvalues, then A is diago-
nalisable.
Proof. Let λ1, . . . , λn be the distinct eigenvalues of A. For each λi there is an
eigenvector vi with this eigenvalue. By Lemma 2 it is enough to show that the
matrix P whose columns are v1, . . . , vn is invertible. An equivalent property is
that v1, . . . , vn are linearly independent vectors.
Method 1: Suppose they are linearly dependent and choose a linear relation
µ1vi1 + . . . + µrvir = 0
involving the least number of eigenvectors with all coefficientsµi non-zero. Thus
r ≥ 2 is the smallest positive integer for which such a relation exists. Applying
A and using Avi = λivi we get
µ1λi1 vi1 + . . . + µrλir vir = 0.
9
Subtracting from the second relation the first relation multiplied by λi1 we
obtain
µ2(λi2 − λi1)vi2 + . . . + µr(λir − λi1)vir = 0.
Since all eigenvalues are distinct, all coefficients of the last relation are non-zero.
It involves r −1 eigenvectors, which contradicts the minimality of r. This proves
the theorem.
Method 2: Take a1, · · · , an such that a1v1+· · ·+anvn = 0. Then by applying
Am to this equation for 0 ≤ m ≤ n − 1, we see that
�
a1v1|a2v2|...|anvn

Q is the
zero matrix where
Q =



1

... ... ...

Hence
�



Diagonalizing a matrix can be useful for many things, here is one amusing
application.
Example 8. Let A be the matrix A =
1 2
2 1

again. Suppose we wish to
calculate the matrix An, i.e. the result of multiplying A by itself n times. We
know that P −1AP = D, where P is the matrix P =

and D is the
diagonal matrix D =

. It follows that A = P DP −1, and hence
A2 = (P DP −1)2 = P D(P −1P )DP −1 = P D2P −1.
More generally, for any n we have
An = P DnP −1.
Now the point is that D is diagonal, so computing the matrix Dn is trivial:
Dn =

Hence


We saw at the end of last term (see the lecture notes for term 1) that
diagonalisation is actually a special case of the change-of-basis formula. Let
f : V → V be a linear map. If we have a basis B ⊂ V , we can represent f by
the matrix B[f]B. In another basis B′, we write f as the matrix B′[f]B′. If we
10
let P = B′[idV ]B be the change-of-basis matrix from B to B′, then P −1 is the
change-of-basis matrix from B′ to B, and the change-of-basis formula is
B′[f]B′ = P
�
B[f]B

P −1.
Now let B′ be the standard basis of Rn and A = B′[f]B′. If B is a basis of
eigenvalues of A, then D := B[f]B is diagonal. Thus the last equation becomes
A = P DP −1, where P is the matrix of the change of basis from the basis of
eigenvectors to the standard basis.
Definition 9. Let A ∈ Mn×n(R). The eigenspace Eλ of A is the set
Eλ := {v ∈ Rn|Av = λv}.
Note that this definition includes the zero vector so that Eλ is the set of
eigenvectors of with eigenvalue λ together with the zero vector.
Proposition 2.1. Any eigenspace Eλ of a matrix A ∈ Mn×n(R) is a subspace
of Rn.
Proof. We just need to check the three properties for eigenspaces. Obviously
0Rn is in Eλ, so we just need to check that the set is closed under addition and
scalar multiplication. But for any vectors u, v ∈ Rn and µ ∈ R, we have
A(u + v) = Au + Av = λu + λv = λ(u + v),
and
A(µu) = µAu = µ(λu) = λ(µu),
which finishes the proof.
Remark 4. This result is not surprising because Eλ = Ker(A − λIn) is a vector
subspace of V .
We can state a necessary and sufficient condition of diagonalisability of a
square matrix in terms of eignespaces. Note that Eλ ̸= 0 if and only is λ is an
eigenvalue.
Proposition 2.2. A matrix of size n × n is diagonalisable if and only if n is
the sum of dimensions of eigenspaces: P
λ dim Eλ = n.
Proof. Choose a basis in each eigenspace Eλ. Arguing as in the proof of The-
orem 1 we show that the union of these bases is a linearly independent set of
eigenvectors in Rn. Indeed, otherwise we can choose a linear relation
µ1vi1 + . . . + µrvir = 0
involving the least number of eigenvectors with all coefficientsµi non-zero. Thus
r ≥ 2 is the smallest positive integer for which such a relation exists. Applying
A and using Avi = λivi we get
µ1λi1 vi1 + . . . + µrλir vir = 0.
If all λij are equal, then vi1 , . . . , vir are contained in the same eigenspace, but
this is not possible because then these vectors form a subset of a basis, hence
11
are linearly independent. Thus we can find at least two different values among
λij. We can assume that these are λi1 and λi2. Subtracting from the second
relation the first relation multiplied by λi1 we obtain
µ2(λi2 − λi1)vi2 + . . . + µr(λir − λi1)vir = 0.
The first coefficient is non-zero. After forgetting all coefficients equal to zero,
we get a linear relation with all coefficients non-zero involving less than r eigen-
vectors, which contradicts the minimality of r. This proves the claim.
The number of elements in a linearly independent set in Rn is at most n, so
we deduce that P
λ dim Eλ ≤ n.
Now we complete the proof. If our matrix is diagonalisable, there is a basis
of eigenvectors. In this basis, the eigenvectors with the same eigenvalue λ form
a linearly independent set in Eλ. Suppose there are mλ of these. We have
mλ ≤ dim Eλ, and thus n =P
λ mλ ≤P
λ dim Eλ ≤ n, henceP
λ dim Eλ = n.
Conversely, suppose that P
λ dim Eλ = n. Then, by the claim, there is a
linearly independent set of n eigenvectors in Rn, so it is a basis.
2.3 Gram–Schmidt process and orthogonality
Definition 10. A set of non-zero vectors S = {v1, . . . vk} in Rn is called an
orthogonal set if every two vectors in the set are orthogonal:
vi · vj = 0, for all i, j, i ̸= j.
The set S is called orthonormal if it is orthogonal and all vectors have length 1.
Note that it is always possible to construct an orthonormal set from an
orthogonal set by dividing each of the vectors by its length, i.e. if {v1, . . . vn} is
orthogonal, then { v1
∥v1∥ , v2
∥v2∥ , . . . , vn
∥vn∥ } is orthonormal.
Example 9. {


3
1

thonormal.
Proposition 2.3. An orthogonal set is linearly independent.
Proof. Assume {v1, . . . , vn} is orthogonal and linearly dependent. Then there
exist real numbers λ1, . . . , λn, not all of them zero, such that
λ1v1 + λ2v2 + · · · + λnvn = 0.
Now take the dot product with vi on both sides for some i ∈ {1, . . . , n}. Since
vi · vj = 0 for all i ̸= j, all term on the left hand side vanish except the i-th
term, and we get
λivi · vi = 0.
But vi·vi = ∥vi∥2 ̸= 0 since vi ̸= 0. Thus λi = 0 for all i, which is a contradiction.
Note that the converse is not true. For example v1 = (1, 1)T and v2 = (0, 1)T
are linearly independent but not orthogonal. The next question is therefore if
we can make an orthogonal basis out of an arbitrary one. For this purpose we
introduce the following tool.
12
Definition 11. Let {v1, . . . , vk} be an orthogonal set of vectors in Rn and U =
span{v1, . . . , vk}. Let y ∈ Rn. The orthogonal projection of y onto U is the
vector given by
pU(y) := y · v1
∥v1∥2 v1 + y · v2
∥v2∥2 v2 + · · · + y · vk
∥vk∥2 vk.
This a linear map pU : Rn → U such that y ·vi = pU(y)·vi for all i = 1, . . . , k.
Note that there is only one vector u ∈ U such that u · vi = y · vi for all i, so this
vector must be pU(y). (Indeed, if u·vi = u′·vi with u, u′ ∈ U, then (u−u′)·vi = 0
for all i. But v1, . . . , vk is a basis of U, so we can write u−u′ = x1v1+. . .+xkvk.
Since v1, . . . , vk is an orthogonal set, (u − u′) · vi = 0 implies xi(vi · vi) = 0 hence
xi = 0.) We conclude that every vector y ∈ Rn is uniquely written as y = u+ w,
where u ∈ U and w is orthogonal to U, moreover, we have u = pU(y).
Looking at examples in R2 and R3, we see thatpU(y) is indeed the orthogonal
projection of the vector y onto U.
Theorem 2.4 (Gram–Schmidt process). Let {v1, . . . , vk} be a linearly indepen-
dent set of vectors in Rn. Define inductively, for i ≤ k:
w1 := v1
w2 := v2 − pspan{w1}(v2) = v2 − v2 · w1
∥w1∥2 w1
w3 := v3 − pspan{w1,w2}(v3) = v3 − ( v3 · w1
∥w1∥2 w1 + v3 · w2
∥w2∥2 w2)
. . .
wi := vi − pspan{w1,w2,...,wi−1}(vi) = vi − ( vi · w1
∥w1∥2 w1 + vi · w2
∥w2∥2 w2 + · · · + vi · wi−1
∥wi−1∥2 wi−1).
Then
1. {w1, . . . , wk} is an orthogonal set.
2. Let ui = wi
∥wi∥. Then {u1, . . . , uk} is an orthonormal set.
3. span {w1, . . . , wk} = span{u1, . . . , uk} = span{v1, . . . , vk}
Proof. Note that (2) follows immediately from (1) as we discussed before. The
rest of proof is by induction. If i = 1, (1) and (3) are clear. Assume now that
for some i such that 1 ≤ i ≤ k we have
span{w1, . . . , wi−1} = span{v1, . . . , vi−1}
and w1, . . . , wi−1 is an orthogonal set. Note that vi is not in span{v1, . . . , vi−1},
because the vectors {v1, . . . , vk} are linearly independent. Then wi ̸= 0. For
j < i we have
wi · wj =


∥w1∥2 w1 + vi · w2
∥w2∥2 w2 + · · · + vi · wi−1
∥wi−1∥2 wi−1)

so w1, . . . , wi is an orthogonal set. We have
span{w1, . . . , wi} = span{v1, . . . , vi−1, wi} = span{v1, . . . , vi−1, vi}
13
Definition 12. A matrix is called orthogonal if its columns are an orthonormal
set.
Note that since all columns of an orthogonal matrix are orthogonal, they are
linearly independent, and therefore an orthogonal matrix is always invertible.
Proposition 2.5. A matrix A ∈ Mn×n(R) is orthogonal if and only if AAT =
In.
Proof. Exercise!
Thus A is orthogonal if and only if A is invertible and A−1 = AT .

2.4 Real symmetric matrices and orthogonality
As we have seen, we may not always be able to diagonalise a matrix. However,
there is a particular type of matrices for which this is always possible and for
which moreover this diagonalisation is particularly nice. This is the topic of this
last section.
Definition 13. A matrix A ∈ Mn×n(R) is called symmetric if A = AT .
Example 11. The zero matrix and the identity matrix are symmetric. The
matrix
1 2
2 1

is symmetric.
In this section, we need to consider complex matrices. As we mentionned
before, all our definitions are still valid in this case. First we recall a very
important theorem. You will see the proof of this statement next year in your
complex analysis module.
14
Theorem 2.6 (Fundamental Theorem of Algebra) . Every non-constant poly-
nomial p(x) with complex coefficients has at least one complex root: there is a
complex number x0 such that p(x0) = 0.
Proposition 2.7. Let A ∈ Mn×n(R) be a symmetric matrix. Then A has at
least one real eigenvalue, and all the eigenvalues of A are real.
Proof. The characteristic polynomial pA(x) = det(xIn − A) is monic of degree
n, so it has at least one complex root λ. This is a complex eigenvalue of A,
so there is a non-zero column vector v = (z1, . . . , zn) with complex coordinates
such that Av = λv. Let ¯v = (¯z1, ¯z2, . . . ,¯zn) be the complex conjugate of v.
Write ¯A for the matrix whose entires are the conjugates of the entries of A.
That is, if A = (aij), then ¯A = (¯aij). Since A is symmetric and real, we have
A = AT = ¯A = ¯AT .
Let ¯vT be the row vector with coordinates (¯z1, ¯z2, . . . ,¯zn). We calculate the
complex number ¯vT Av in two different ways. The first way is
¯vT Av = ¯vT λv = λ(¯v · v).
The second way is
¯vT Av = (¯vT ¯AT )v = ( ¯A¯v)T v = (Av)T v = λv
T
v = ¯λ¯vT v = ¯λ(¯v · v).
Since v is non-zero, we have ¯v · v = |z1|2 + . . . + |zn|2 > 0. In particular, this is
non-zero, so we obtain λ = ¯λ hence λ ∈ R.
We will now link symmetric matrices with the notion of orthogonality.
Lemma 3. Let A ∈ Mn×n(R) be a symmetric matrix. Let v1 and v2 be eigen-
vectors of A with distinct eigenvalues. Then v1 and v2 are orthogonal.
Proof. For any matrix A such that A = AT we have
(Av1) · v2 = (Av1)T v2 = vT
1 AT v2 = vT
1 Av2 = vT
1 (Av2) = v1 · (Av2).
Since v1 and v2 are eigenvectors of A, this gives λ1(v1 · v2) = λ2(v1 · v2). If
λ1 ̸= λ2, we must have v1 · v2 = 0.
Definition 14. A symmetric matrix A ∈ Mn×n(R) is called orthogonally diag-
onalisable if there exists a diagonal matrix D and an orthogonal matrix P such
that A = P DP −1.
Theorem 2.8 (Spectral Theorem) . A matrix A ∈ Mn×n(R) is orthogonally
diagonalisable if and only if A is symmetric.
Proof. It is easy to show that orthogonally diagonalisable matrices are symmet-
ric. Suppose that D ∈ Mn×n(R) is diagonal, P ∈ Mn×n(R) is orthogonal, and
A = P DP −1. By Proposition 2.5 we know that P −1 = P T . Then
AT = (P DP −1)T = (P DP T )T = (P T )T DT P T = P DP T = A.
To prove that symmetric matrices in Mn×n(R) can be orthogonally diago-
nalised, it is enough to show that there is an orthonormal basis of eigenvalues.
We argue by induction in n. The case n = 1 is clear.
15
Suppose that the statement is proved in dimension n−1. By Proposition 2.7
our matrix A has a real eigenvalue λ. Choose an eigenvector v1 of length 1 with
this eigenvalue. Using Gram–Schmidt we extend v1 to an orthonormal basis
v1, . . . , vn. Then the matrix P whose columns are v1, . . . , vn is an orthogonal
matrix. Consider the matrix P −1AP = P T AP . It is symmetric: ( P T AP )T =
P T AT P = P T AP . The (1 , 1)-entry of this matrix is
vT
1 Av1 = vT
1 λv1 = λ(v1 · v1) = λ.
The other entries in the first row are
vT
1 Avi = vT
1 AT vi = (Av1)T vi = λ(v1 · vi) = 0
for i > 1. By symmetry, all entries in the first column except the (1 , 1)-entry
are zero. It remains to repeat the process for the symmetric matrix of size
(n − 1) × (n − 1) obtained by removing the first row and the first colum from
P −1AP = P T AP , that is, to use the inductive assumption. (Recall that A and
P −1AP have the same eigenvalues.)
The method to orthogonally diagonalise a matrix uses the Gram–Schmidt
process. We first diagonalise the matrix, which means that we get a basis of
eigenvectors for each of the eigenspaces Eλi, i = 1, . . . k. Then we use Gram-
Schmidt to orthogonalise each of theses bases.
Example 12. Diagonalise the matrix A =


1 −1 −1
−1 1 −1
−1 −1 1

 by constructing
an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.
The eigenvalues of A are −1 and 2. We compute the eigenspaces E−1 =
span{(1, 1, 1)T } and E2 = span{(1, −1, 0)T , (0, 1, −1)T }. We have dim E−1 = 1
so we just choose an eigenvector v1 = 1√
3(1, 1, 1)T of length 1. Apply Gram–
Schmidt to find an orthonormal basis of E2. We get
v2 = 1√
2(1, −1, 0)T , v 3 = 1√
6(1, 1, −2)T .
Then P = ( v1|v2|v3) is orthogonal and we have A = P DP −1, where D is
diagonal with diagonal entries −1, 2, 2 on the diagonal.
Remark 5. 1. Any real symmetric matrix has a basis of eigenvectors. If
all the eigenvalues are distinct, by Lemma 3, we get immediately that the
eigenvectors are pairwise orthogonal.
2. Not every diagonalisable matrix is orthogonally diagonalisable. For exam-
ple the matrix
1 1
0 0

is diagonalisable, but one cannot find an orthonor-
mal basis of eigenvectors. (Otherwise, it would be symmetric.)

LOGIC
Proposition Logic
Propositional formulae definition:
Binding Conventions:
The principal/main connective in a formula is the weakest 
connective (the one at the top of the tree).`
The overall logical form for a formula is the principal 
connective (say, →) applied to generic formulae (so  → ).φ ψ
Logical forms technical terms:
– Atomic formula - ,  or p for an atom p.⊤ ⊥
– Negated formula - of the logical form ¬ .φ
- Negated atomic – ¬p, ¬ , or .⊤ ⊥
– Conjunction –   .  and  are its conjuncts.φ ∧ ψ φ ψ
– Disjunction –   .  and  are its disjuncts.φ ∨ ψ φ ψ
– Implication –  → .φ ψ  φ is the antecedent,  is the consequent.ψ
– Bidirectional implication –   φ ↔ ψ
Literal – atomic or negated-atomic formula.
Atomic evaluation function – a specific assignment of truth-
values to each propositional atom in in a set of propositional 
atoms. (e.g. v :  → {tt, ff}).Α
Evaluation function – the funciton which states how a formula 
is evaluated based on its subformulae.
Sheffer stroke (↑) - |  ↑ | = tt iff | | = ff or | | = ff, aka φ ψ φ ψ
‘NAND’
all boolean connectives can be defined in terms of the sheffer 
stroke.
Arity – the number of inputs for a connective (2-ary connective 
has 2 inputs). There are 2^(2^n) n-ary connectives. Unary is 1-
ary, binary is 2-ary.
Functional completeness – a set of boolean connectives with 
which any connective of any arity can be defined. e.g. {↑}
Valid arguent – φ1, φ2, ... φn therefore  is valid if  is true for all ψ ψ
situations where all  are true. (φ φ 1, φ2, ... φn  )⊨ ψ
 is the ‘double turnstile’, also ‘⊨ logically entails’, ‘logically 
implies’ and ‘semantically entails’.
Valid formula – a propositional formula is logically valid if true 
in every situation. Aka a tautology.
Satisfiable formula – a formula true in at least one situation. A 
non-satisfiable formula is a contradiction.
Equivalent formulae – formulae are logically equivalent if they 
are true in exactly the same situations.   φ ≡ ψ
Each row means the same
Implication formula – for argument φ1, φ2, ... φn  , the ⊨ ψ
corresponding implication formula is φ1∧φ2 …∧ ∧φ n→ .ψ
An argument is valid iff its corresponding implication formula 
is a valid formula.
Law of the excluded middle (LEM) – any propositional atom or 
formula is always true or false.
Showing argument validity with equivalences – convert the 
argument into its implication formula, and simplify the formula 
to  using equivalences.⊤

Using equivalences:
Name the law used/its logical form next to the result
Apply one at a time
You may apply one law more than once in one step
Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF) – disjunction of conjunctions of 
literals. (‘OR’s applied to formulas made up of ‘AND’s and 
literals)
Conjunctive normal form (CNF) - conjunction of disjunctions of 
literals. (‘AND’s applied to formulas made up of ‘OR’s and 
literals) 
DNF/CNF IMPORTANT INFO:
We use equivalences to rewrite formulae into normal form for 
easier computation. DNF and CNF cannot be further simplified 
using equivalences without leaving their forms.
DNF can be a conjunction of literals as it is a disjunction of a 
single conjunction. Similar for CNfF
Direct argument and natural deduction key terms:
Premises – the formulae taken as truth at the beginning (φ1, …, 
φn)
Conclusion – the formula reached by direct argument ( )ψ
Sequent – the statement something has been proved by direct 
argument (φ1, …, φn  )⊢ ψ
The single turnstile characters are used to show sequents. ( ) ⊣⊢
is used to show sequents in both directions (provable 
equivalence).
Formulae are provably equivalent iff they are semantically 
equivalent.
Natural deduction rules:
(I is introducing a connective, E is eliminating it)
You must list the lines used in brackets after the name of the 
rule.
• I –    from  and ∧ φ ∧ ψ φ ψ
• E –  or  from   ∧ φ ψ φ ∧ ψ
• →I –  →  from a box with asm  and conclusion φ ψ φ ψ
• →E –  from  →  and ψ φ ψ φ
• I –    from  or ∨ φ ∨ ψ φ ψ
• E –  from    and boxes with assumptions, , ,∨ ρ φ ∨ ψ φ ψ  
which each conclude ρ
• ¬I - ¬  from a box which assumes  and concludes φ φ
⊥
• ¬E -  from  and ¬⊥ φ φ
• ¬¬I - ¬¬  from φ φ
• ¬¬E –  from ¬¬φ φ
• E –  (where  is any formula at all!) from ⊥ φ φ ⊥
• I -  from  and ¬⊥ ⊥ φ φ
• LEM (law of excluded middle) –   ¬ , no φ ∨ φ
requirements
• MT (modus tollens) - ¬  from ¬  and  → φ ψ φ ψ
• PC (proof by contradiction) –  from a box with asmφ  
¬  and conclusion φ ⊥
• ←>I –  ←>  from  →  and  → φ ψ φ ψ ψ φ P
Predicate Natural Deduction Rules
• I - x  from [t/x]∃ ∃ φ φ
• E –  from x  and a box with assumption [c/x] ∃ ψ ∃ φ φ
and result ψ
• I – x  from a box starting term c (write I const) ∀ ∀ φ ∀
and result [c/x]. Remember you cannot introduce aφ  
variable into another  or ’s scope∀ ∃
• E – [t/x] (for any t!) from x∀ φ ∀ φ
• →E – [t/x] from x( (x) → (x)) and [t/x]. This ∀ ψ ∀ φ ψ φ
can be applied for different variables in one 
application
• reflexivity (refl) – t = t for any closed L-term t (e.g. a 
Skolem constant c).
• Substitution of equals (=sub) – [u/x] from [t/x] φ φ
and t = u
• Symmetry (= sym) – d = c from c = d
Stuck on natural deduction?
Try a proof by contradiction (assume ¬  and prove ) or use φ ⊥
LEM.
Soundness – a proof system is sound if every theorem is valid. 
(φ1, …, φn   then ⊢ ψ φ 1, …, φn  ).⊨ ψ
Completeness – a proof system is complete if every formula is a 
theorem. ( φ1, …, φn   then  ⊨ ψ φ 1, …, φn  ).⊢ ψ
Natural deduction is sound and complete. This means we can 
use natural deduction to check validity of formulae.
Satisfiability – a formula is satisfiable if it is true in at least one 
situation.
Consistency – a collection of formulae is consistent if their 
negations are not valid.
Predicate Logic
Predicates – take arguments e.g. sister(Bob, Mary), these have 
arities. Predicate symbols are often capital letters (P, Q, R, S, ...)
Constants – Bob, Room_308 etc. Constants are often lower-case 
letters (a, b, c, d, ...)
Variables – x, y, z etc...
Quantifiers -  (for all),  (there exists)∀ ∃
Functions – father_of(_), +, -. Functions are often lower case 
letters (f, g, h, …), these have arities.
Equality - =
Atomic formulae – a predicate applied to a constant is atomic 
e.g. student(Arron), friends(Arron, Russell).
Signature – specific to a given predicate logic. A triple of 〈K, F, 
P〉where K is a set of constants (can be empty), F is a set of 
function symbols (can be empty) and P is a set of predicate 
symbols. e.g. K = frank, susan, c. F = father _of. P = PC/1, 
Human/1, Bought/2. Arities are noted with a / followed by the 
degree of arity.
L-term – in a signature L, the following are L-terms: any 
constant in L; any variable, in L or not; f(t1, … tn), where f/n is in 
L and t1 … tn are L-terms.
Closed/ground L-term – an L-term which doesn’t involve a 
variable.
L-formula - in a signature L, the following are L-formulas: R(t1, 
… tn) is an L-formula iff R/n is a predicate in L and t1, … tn are L-
terms; t = t’ if t, t’ are L terms; , ; logic statements made ⊥ ⊤
from other L-formulas.
Atomic L-formula – a predicate symbol with arguments filled in 
with terms.
Literal – atomic L-formula or its negation
Sentence – a formulal with all variables in scope of a quantifier 
( , )∀ ∃
Domain of discourse - a non-empty set of objects. Denoted D. 
Cartesian power - For a positive whole number n, the n-ary 
Cartesian power of a set D, written Dn or Dx,..., xD, is the set of 
all n-tuples that can be constructed from its members. 
A relation R of arity n over D is a subset of Dn, so R is a subset 
Dn, with n >= 0.
Unary relation - a relation of arity 1.
Binary relation - a relation of arity 2.
A function of arity n over D is a one-to-one or a many-to-one 
mapping from all of Dn to D, with n > 0. 
A many-to-one function means that dierent n-tuples of objects 
in D may be mapped to the same object in D, but each n-tuple is 
mapped to exactly one object.
L-structure – a pair M = 〈D, I〉where D is a domain fo 
discourse of M aka, the universe of M. I is an interpretation that 
specifies the meaning of each symbol in L in terms of the objects 
in D (written I(c) = cM, I(f) = fM …)
Bound/free variables – an occurrence of a variable in a formula 
is bound if it is within the scope of a quantifier with its name ( ,∀  
). All other variables are free. ∃
Variable assignment - An assignment over an L-structure is a 
function that assigns an object D to each variable. e.g. 
assignment h, variable x -> h(x). With an L-structure and an 
assignment we can evaluate any L-term to an obkect in the 
domain of the L-structure and any L-formula with no 
quantifiers to true or false.

Variable-equivalent assignments – g and h are x-equivalent g =x 
h if all the assignments they make are the same except those for 
x. assignments are always variable-equivalent to themselves.
Sorted FOL
Sort – applied to each variable and constant. We cannot derive 
new sorts in sorted FOL, sorts like (s → s’) must be declared 
explicitly.
Sorted functions – functions are defined so each input and the 
output have defined sorts, e.g. f : (s1, …, sn) → s, where all sk, s are 
declared sorts. (s1, …, sn) → s is not a sort. Same for sorted 
relation symbols.
 and  should be used with a sort noted e.g. x : s∃ ∀ ∀
[t/x] φ – the formula  with all free occurences of x replaced by tφ
REASONING
Semi-structured proof
Rules:
1. Write out and name (e.g. (1)) each given formula.
2. Write out and name each formula to be shown.
3. Plan out the proof and name intermediate results.
4. Justify each step of the proof.
5. Vary the sizes of each step appropriately
e.g.
Induction
Induction can be used to prove statements of the form x : ∀
S.P(x) where S is an enumerable set and P  S.⊆
For any P  S, where S⊆ 0 is the ‘base case’ of S, P(S0)  k : S.∧ ∀
[P(Sk) → P(Sk + 1)] → n : S.P(n).∀
Proof schema:
Base case
To show P(S0)
…[show it here]...
Inductive Step
Take k : S, arbitrary
Inductive Hypothesis P(Sk)
To Show P(Sk + 1)
…[show it here]...
Mathematical Induction “Technique”
Exact same as normal induction but can use condition k >= m
For any P   and m : , ⊆ ℤ ℤ
P(m)  k >= m.[P(k) → P(k+1)] → n >= m.P(n).∧ ∀ ∀
Proof schema:
Base case
To show P(m)
…[show it here]...
Inductive Step
Take k : S, arbitrary
Assume that k >= m
Inductive Hypothesis P(k)
To Show P(k + 1)
…[show it here]…
Strong induction
aka complete induction
For any P  , ⊆ ℕ
P(0)  k : .[ j  [0..k].P(j) → P(j+1)] → n : .P(n)∧ ∀ ℕ ∀ ∈ ∀ ℕ
Proof schema:
Base case
To show P(0)
…[show it here]...
Inductive Step
Take k : , arbitraryℕ
Inductive Hypothesis j  [0..k]. P(j)∀ ∈
To Show P(k + 1)
…[show it here]…
Structural Induction over Haskell Lists
for any type T and P  [T]:⊆
P([])  vs:[T]. v:T.[P(vs) → P(v:vs)] → xs:[T].P(xs)∧ ∀ ∀ ∀
Proof schema:
Base case
To show P([])
…[show it here]...
Inductive Step
Take arbitrary v : a, vs : [a]
Inductive Hypothesis P(vs)
To Show P(v:vs)
…[show it here]…
Structural Induction over Haskell Trees
our tree structure is:
data Tree a = Empty | Node (Tree a) a (Tree a)
Principle:
P(Empty)  t1,t2 : Tree T. x : T.[ P(t1)  P(t2) → P(Node t1 x t2 )∧ ∀ ∀ ∧  
] → t : Tree T.P(t)∀
Structural induction tips for if you can’t solve an initial proof
1. Invent an auxilliary lemma (often proved by 
induction)
2. Prove a stronger property
Steps for induction over Haskell and logic stuff:
1. Inductively defined sets/relations/functions 
introduce a successor relation.
2. Successor relation gives rise to a (generic) inductive 
principle.
(of form Pbase  (P∧ k → Pk+1) → P)
3. Inductive principle can be specialized for specific 
predicates.
4. The generic/specialized inductive principle gives 
rise to proof schema.
Of form:
Base case
To show Pbase
…[show it here]...
Inductive Step
Take k : S, arbitrary
Inductive Hypothesis Pk
To Show Pk+1
…[show it here]...
Kotlin Reasoning (aka   pain  , pure   pain  )  
Every line has a pre and post condition (mid conditions are pre 
and post for the lines it is between). In order to show they are 
valid, a post condition must be shown from the pre condition 
and the line. These are proof obligations.
Main notation explained:
// PRE: P
// POST: Q
somecode
 { P } somecode { Q }≡
 P  somecode → Q≡ ∧
expresses that if the state satisfies P (pre-condition), then after 
executing somecode, the state will satisfy Q (post-condition).
// PRE: P
// POST: Q
code1
// MID: R
code2
 { P } code1 { R }   { R } code2 { Q }≡
 { P } code1 ; code2 { Q }≡
X old:
xold is  defined as the value of x in P (before the code is executed).
e.g. { P(x) } x++ { Q(x) }  { P(x≡ old) } x = xold + 1 { Q(x) }
 P[x → x≡ old]  x = [x → x∧ old] x + 1 → Q
DO NOT WRITE X OLD IN ANY PRE, MID OR POST 
CONDITIONS, IT IS FOR WHEN WE ARE PROVING THINGS 
ONLY!
X pre:
xpre is defined as the value of x before any code is executed at all 
(in the pre condition for the entire program).
Return x is written as r = x in proofs
Specification of addition of variables (let u, v be arbitrary 
integers)
// PRE: a = u  x = v∧
val y = a + x
// POST: a = u  x = v  y = u + v∧ ∧
we assume u, v have universal quantification over the whole 
specification (around the whole program there would be a u, ∀
v.)
The Mod function definition
Mod shows which variables are changed by a line
Arrays
v  a[x..y)  i  [x..y).[ 0 ≤ i < a.size  a[i] = v ]∈ ≜ ∃ ∈ ∧
this is an array slice and is the subarray in the indices [x..y)
a[..i) is array slice from 0 up to but not including i
a[i..) array slice from i up to its end
a[..) is the whole array
NrOccurs(a[..), v) = [the number of occurences of v in a]
a[..)  b[..)∼  means a is a permutation of b (share the same 
elements)
a[..) ≈ b[..) means the contents of a and b are identical (including 
ordering) aka deep equality.
a[x..y)Σ  is the sum of all elements in a from x up to but not 
including y
a[x..y)Π  is the same but product
Sorted(a[..)) means that the array is ordered (increasing)
Swapped(a[..) b[..), i, j) means that a and b are identical, save 
elements at indexes i, j, which are swapped.
min(a[..)) is the smallest element in a
max(a[..)) is the largest element in a
b[..) : c[..) is the concatenation of b and c
a[..) ≈ b[..) : c[..)  a[..b.size) ≈ b[..)  a[b.size..) ≈ c[..)≜ ∧
Deep Equality Lemmas: 
• ≈Symm - a[..) ≈ b[..) → b[..) ≈ a[..)
• ≈Trans - a[..) ≈ b[..)  b[..) ≈ c[..) → a[..) ≈ c[..)∧
• ≈Size - a[..) ≈ b[..) → a.size = b.size
• ≈IsPrm - a[..) ≈ b[..) → a[..)  b[..)∼
Permutation Lemmas:
• Symm - a[..)  b[..) → b[..)  a[..)∼ ∼ ∼
• Trans - a[..)  b[..)  b[..)  c[..) → a[..)  c[..)∼ ∼ ∧ ∼ ∼
• Size - a[..)  b[..) −→ a.size = b.size∼ ∼
Swapped Lemmas:
• ≈Swpd - Swapped(a[..), b[..), i, i) −→ a[..) ≈ b[..)
• Swpd - Swapped(a[..), b[..), i, j) −→ a[..)  b[..) ∼ ∼
• SwpSm Swapped(a[..), b[..), i, j) ←→ Swapped(a[..), 
b[..), j, I)
←→ Swapped(b[..), a[..), i, j) 
Ranges Lemmas: 
• RngWeak - b[..) ≈ a[0..i) : b[i..j) : a[j..)  x ≤ i  j ≤ y ∧ ∧
−→ b[..) ≈ a[0..x) : b[x..y) : a[y..)
• RngPrm - b[..) ≈ a[0..i) : b[i..j) : a[j..)  a[i..j)  b[i..j) ∧ ∼
−→ a[..)  b[..)∼
• RngSwap - a[..) ≈ b[..)  c[..) ≈ c[0..i) : a[i..j) : c[j..) −→ ∧
c[..) ≈ c[0..i) : b[i..j) : c[j..)
Conditionals (if statements)
{ P } if(cond) { code1 } else { code2 } { Q }
 { P  cond } code1 { Q }   and   { P  ¬cond } code2 { Q }≡ ∧ ∧
Function calls (bits in green are for returning values
for function f
{ R }   fun f (x: type)   { S }
and code

we must show
P → R[x → v]

Partial correctness - if the code is executed in a state satisfying 
PRE, if it terminates it must reach a state satisfying Q
Total correctness - Partial correctness + it will terminate
Recursion – you may use your PRE and POST for your function 
within itself for recursive calls!
Termination – prove using mathematical induction!

code

we must show M1 → I, I  condition  code → I and I ^ ¬cond → M∧ ∧ 2

decreases every iteration and is always larger than some value.

Question 1
(a)

0≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|=

(𝑥𝑛−𝑢)2+(𝑦𝑛−𝑣)2≤|𝑥𝑛−𝑢|+|𝑦𝑛−𝑣|.
The right hand side (RHS) tends to0, hence via the squeeze principle
|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|→0.
(⇐=)Suppose|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|→0. Since|𝑥𝑛−𝑢|≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|and|𝑦𝑛−𝑣|≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|,
both coordinate differences tend to0. Hence𝑥 𝑛→𝑢and𝑦 𝑛→𝑣. This
proves the equivalence.
(b)
Let𝑝 𝑛 =(𝑥𝑛 , 𝑦 𝑛).
Since𝑥 𝑛∈[𝑎, 𝑏], the sequence(𝑥𝑛)is bounded. By Bolzano-Weierstrass
inR, there exists a subsequence(𝑥𝑛𝑘)converging to some𝑥∗∈[𝑎, 𝑏].
Consider the corresponding subsequence(𝑦𝑛𝑘)⊂[𝐴, 𝐵]. It is bounded,
so by Bolzano–Weierstrass we can extract a further subsequence(𝑦𝑛𝑘ℓ
)con-
verging to𝑦∗∈[𝐴, 𝐵].
Note that(𝑥𝑛𝑘ℓ
)is a subsequence of the convergent sequence(𝑥𝑛𝑘), so it
also converges to𝑥∗(a subsequence of a convergent sequence converges to
the same limit).
Then
𝑝𝑛𝑘ℓ
=(𝑥𝑛𝑘ℓ
, 𝑦 𝑛𝑘ℓ

because𝑥 𝑛𝑘ℓ
→𝑥∗and𝑦 𝑛𝑘ℓ
→𝑦∗, and by part (a) this implies convergence
inR 2. Therefore𝑄is sequentially compact.
(c)
Assume𝑓:𝑄→Ris continuous on𝑄(meaning sequentially continuous
as defined in the question). We will prove that𝑓is uniformly continuous
on𝑄. Assume𝑓is not uniformly continuous. Then there exists𝜀0 >0and
sequences(𝑝𝑛),(𝑞𝑛)in𝑄with

1

the Triangle Inequality
|𝑞𝑛𝑘−𝑝|≤|𝑞𝑛𝑘−𝑝𝑛𝑘|+|𝑝𝑛𝑘−𝑝|≤1

𝑓(𝑝), so|𝑓(𝑝𝑛𝑘)−𝑓(𝑞𝑛𝑘)|→0; this contradicts the uniform lower bound𝜀0.

(d)

we have𝜑(𝑥𝑛)→𝜑(𝑥)and𝜓(𝑦𝑛)→𝜓(𝑦).
Now use the algebra of limits: the product of convergent sequences
converges to the product of the limits. Therefore
𝐻(𝑥𝑛 , 𝑦 𝑛)=𝜑(𝑥𝑛)𝜓(𝑦𝑛)−→𝜑(𝑥)𝜓(𝑦)=𝐻(𝑥, 𝑦).
This shows that𝐻is continuous on𝐼×𝐽.
(ii)

Question 1

(⇐=)Suppose|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|→0. Since|𝑥𝑛−𝑢|≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|and|𝑦𝑛−𝑣|≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|,
both coordinate differences tend to0. Hence𝑥 𝑛→𝑢and𝑦 𝑛→𝑣. This
proves the equivalence.
(b)
Let𝑝 𝑛 =(𝑥𝑛 , 𝑦 𝑛).
Since𝑥 𝑛∈[𝑎, 𝑏], the sequence(𝑥𝑛)is bounded. By Bolzano-Weierstrass
inR, there exists a subsequence(𝑥𝑛𝑘)converging to some𝑥∗∈[𝑎, 𝑏].
Consider the corresponding subsequence(𝑦𝑛𝑘)⊂[𝐴, 𝐵]. It is bounded,
so by Bolzano–Weierstrass we can extract a further subsequence(𝑦𝑛𝑘ℓ

verging to𝑦∗∈[𝐴, 𝐵].

Question 1
(a)
Let𝑝 𝑛 =(𝑥𝑛 , 𝑦 𝑛)and𝑞=(𝑢, 𝑣).

0≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|=

The right hand side (RHS) tends to0, hence via the squeeze principle
|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|→0.
(⇐=)Suppose|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|→0. Since|𝑥𝑛−𝑢|≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|and|𝑦𝑛−𝑣|≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|,
both coordinate differences tend to0. Hence𝑥 𝑛→𝑢and𝑦 𝑛→𝑣. This
proves the equivalence.
(b)
Let𝑝 𝑛 =(𝑥𝑛 , 𝑦 𝑛).
Since𝑥 𝑛∈[𝑎, 𝑏], the sequence(𝑥𝑛)is bounded. By Bolzano-Weierstrass
inR, there exists a subsequence(𝑥𝑛𝑘)converging to some𝑥∗∈[𝑎, 𝑏].
Consider the corresponding subsequence(𝑦𝑛𝑘)⊂[𝐴, 𝐵]. It is bounded,
so by Bolzano–Weierstrass we can extract a further subsequence(𝑦𝑛𝑘ℓ
)con-
verging to𝑦∗∈[𝐴, 𝐵].
Note that(𝑥𝑛𝑘ℓ
)is a subsequence of the convergent sequence(𝑥𝑛𝑘), so it
also converges to𝑥∗(a subsequence of a convergent sequence converges to
the same limit).
Then
𝑝𝑛𝑘ℓ
=(𝑥𝑛𝑘ℓ
, 𝑦 𝑛𝑘ℓ

because𝑥 𝑛𝑘ℓ

→𝑦∗, and by part (a) this implies convergence
inR 2. Therefore𝑄is sequentially compact.
(c)
Assume𝑓:𝑄→Ris continuous on𝑄(meaning sequentially continuous
as defined in the question). We will prove that𝑓is uniformly continuous
on𝑄. Assume𝑓is not uniformly continuous. Then there exists𝜀0 >0and

the Triangle Inequality
|𝑞𝑛𝑘−𝑝|≤|𝑞𝑛𝑘−𝑝𝑛𝑘|+|𝑝𝑛𝑘−𝑝|≤1

(d)

we have𝜑(𝑥𝑛)→𝜑(𝑥)and𝜓(𝑦𝑛)→𝜓(𝑦).
Now use the algebra of limits: the product of convergent sequences
converges to the product of the limits. Therefore
𝐻(𝑥𝑛 , 𝑦 𝑛)=𝜑(𝑥𝑛)𝜓(𝑦𝑛)−→𝜑(𝑥)𝜓(𝑦)=𝐻(𝑥, 𝑦).
This shows that𝐻is continuous on𝐼×𝐽.
(ii)

showingℎ◦𝐺is continuous on𝐼×𝐽.

Question 1
(a)
Let𝑝 𝑛 =(𝑥𝑛 , 𝑦 𝑛)and𝑞=(𝑢, 𝑣). Recall

0≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|=

The right hand side (RHS) tends to0, hence via the squeeze principle
|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|→0.
(⇐=)Suppose|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|→0. Since|𝑥𝑛−𝑢|≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|and|𝑦𝑛−𝑣|≤|𝑝𝑛−𝑞|,
both coordinate differences tend to0. Hence𝑥 𝑛→𝑢and𝑦 𝑛→𝑣. This
proves the equivalence.
(b)
Let𝑝 𝑛 =(𝑥𝑛 , 𝑦 𝑛).
Since𝑥 𝑛∈[𝑎, 𝑏], the sequence(𝑥𝑛)is bounded. By Bolzano-Weierstrass
inR, there exists a subsequence(𝑥𝑛𝑘)converging to some𝑥∗∈[𝑎, 𝑏].
Consider the corresponding subsequence(𝑦𝑛𝑘)⊂[𝐴, 𝐵]. It is bounded,
so by Bolzano–Weierstrass we can extract a further subsequence(𝑦𝑛𝑘ℓ
)con-
verging to𝑦∗∈[𝐴, 𝐵].
Note that(𝑥𝑛𝑘ℓ
)is a subsequence of the convergent sequence(𝑥𝑛𝑘), so it
also converges to𝑥∗(a subsequence of a convergent sequence converges to
the same limit).
Then
𝑝𝑛𝑘ℓ
=(𝑥𝑛𝑘ℓ
, 𝑦 𝑛𝑘ℓ
)→(𝑥∗, 𝑦∗)∈𝑄,
because𝑥 𝑛𝑘ℓ
→𝑥∗and𝑦 𝑛𝑘ℓ
→𝑦∗, and by part (a) this implies convergence
inR 2. Therefore𝑄is sequentially compact.
(c)
Assume𝑓:𝑄→Ris continuous on𝑄(meaning sequentially continuous

documents.title: Mondelez stock pitch 1
documents.text:
BUY Mondelez International Inc. (MDLZ)
Thesis
Catalysts Risks
1. Analysts project low cocoa and sugar 
prices in 2026. 
2. Clear strategic roadmap presented at 
recent CAGNY conference:
- 3-5% organic net revenue growth
- Increase in EPS 
3. Growing demand
- Brand loyalty and habitual consumption
- Mondelez brands entering the "Better-
for-you" market.
4. Emerging Market Expansion
- 40% of Revenue generated in AMEA
5. Low Price-Elasticity
- 6.6% mean price increase→ 2.2% 
decrease in volume/mix (2024)
Validation
Mondelez owns a portfolio of snacking 
brands. The market is pricing in past margin 
compression driven by elevated cocoa costs 
from 2024. However , Mondelez’ s stablecash 
flows enable a confident recommendation. 
The main risk is further deterioration in cocoa 
supply due to adverse weather.
1. Weather disruptscocoa/sugar yields
-  Exacerbated by 70% of cocoa yield coming 
from 2 countries.
2. Tariffs – particularly US/Mexico
- Mitigated by USMCA framework and regional 
manufacturing model. 
3. Competition with retailers e.g. Tesco
- Retailers simultaneously sell Mondelez 
products as well as private-label competition.
4. Elasticity underestimated
Horizon 12 months
Entry $58.80
Target $75.00 
Stop loss $52.00 
Risk/Reward 1:2.4
P/E falls from 28x (2025) to 22.7x (2026E) as earnings 
recover - valuation reset creates upside.
EV/EBITDA holds ~20x in 2026E, reflecting resilient cash 
flow despite prior margin pressure.
EPS Growth inflects from -13% to +2.6%, signaling 
earnings trough and recovery.

class QueueNode<T>(
12:     private val queue: Queue<T>,
13:     private val successor: Acceptor<T>,
14: ) : Acceptor<T>,
15:     Forwarder {
16:     override 
fun accept(item: T) {
17:         queue.enqueue(item)
18:     }
19:
20:     override 
fun forward() {
21:         val item = queue.dequeue()

23:             successor.accept(item)

26: }

class AQueue<T> : Queue<T> {
18:     public val items = mutableListOf<T>()

fun enqueue(item: T) {
21:         items.add(item)
22:     }
23:
24:     override 
fun isEmpty(): Boolean {
25:         if (items.isEmpty()) {
26:             return true
27:         } else {
28:             return false
29:         }
30:     }
31:

fun dequeue(): T? {
33:         if (items.isEmpty()) return null
34:         val rv = items[0]
35:         items.removeAt(0)
36:         
return rv
37:     }
38:
39:     override 
fun size(): Int = items.size

42:         override fun enqueue(item: T) {

44:         }
45:

fun peek(): T? = items.firstOrNull()
47:     }

50:         override fun enqueue(item: T) {

fun peek(): T? = items.firstOrNull()

val items = PriorityQueue<T>(comparator)

64:         }
65:
66:         override 
fun peek(): T? = items.peek()

documents.title: Notes Lecture 5
documents.text:
Securities Education Certificate 
 
Exclusive SEC Cartoon by Nicolas Geiseler Toran (Mechanical Engineering, 2022) [4/7] 
Lecture 5 
Derivatives I 
Lecturers 
Krish Nigam     krish.nigam21@imperial.ac.uk 
Adam Snelling    adam.snelling21@imperial.ac.uk 
 
For the Securities Education Certificate 2023/24 Series 
Imperial College Investment Society 
 
Lead Lecturer 
Adam Snelling adam.snelling21@imperial.ac.uk  
 
Notes by Ahmad Danesh (Lead Lecturer 2022/23) and Oscar Wooding (Lecturer 2022/23)

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
2 
Table of Contents 
Derivatives 3 
Introduction 3 
Definitions 3 
The what and why of derivatives 4 
How can someone hedge their risks and therefore minimise their exposure to an investment? 5 
What types of derivatives exist in financial markets? 5 
Types of derivatives 6 
Swaps 6 
Classifying derivatives 11 
Forward Rate Agreements (FRAs) 11 
Futures and Forwards 12 
How do futures and forwards differ? 14 
Over-the-Counter Markets (OTC) – Forwards 16 
How big are these markets? 16 
How are these contracts normally traded? 17 
Payoffs 18 
Pricing 19 
Example 1: Pricing non-dividend-paying non-storable asset futures 22 
Tricks to understand futures pricing 24 
Another mindset which can help you to understand futures pricing is to think: 24 
If I have $100, what would I prefer as a trader, to receive a set asset now for $100, or to receive the 
asset in the future for $100? An accurate price will make this decision impossible. 24 
- If I receive the asset now for $100, in one years time I’m left with no money and the asset (let's 
say a bar of gold for example) 24 
Example 2: Pricing income yielding and storable asset futures 24 
Example 3: Pricing FX futures 25 
 
  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
3 
Derivatives 
Introduction 
Before anyone is fooled, myself and Oscar can assure you that derivatives are inherently an abstract idea 
which might seem completely off-putting to learn at first. However, once you realise why we need them, you 
can then have a better understanding as to how they work and the motivations behind why an investor would 
choose to work with derivatives through their career. That’s how we plan to teach this lecture: we begin with 
the ‘why’ and then work towards the ‘what’ with regards to derivatives. Here, we only discuss the preliminary 
basics since there is so much more to derivatives, with a lot of maths surrounding their behaviour if you 
choose to study the more advanced types of derivatives. So, without any further ado, let’s crack on! 
 
The first thing I want you to understand is this: if there is money to be made, then anything can be 
arranged. When you read these notes and you think “why on earth would anyone want to arrange such 
a deal?!”, remember that phrase… either a buyer or seller will win in any deal! 
 
So far, you’ve got an idea as to what financial markets are all about (Lecture 1), who is involved in them 
(Lecture 1 + 2), what drives the markets on a daily or annual basis (Lecture 2) and what sorts of assets people 
can purchase (Lecture 3). 
 
Now, it’s not a huge surprise that people that work in the financial markets have one objective only… make as 
much money as you can and lose as little of the firm’s capital as possible. There are many ways that financial 
players (or actors, however you wish to call them) can invest their cash to maximise their returns, but any 
investor will tell you there is a possibility that their investments could fall in value and hence they’ll recoup less 
money back if they choose to sell the assets they acquired – who knows, a freak environmental disaster or an 
economic recession could happen in the near future. 
Definitions 
Before we discuss the idea of derivatives, it’s important to define a few rigorous terms associated with the bad 
outcomes that could arise from an investment.  
● A risk (n.) is simply the possible negative outcomes that arise from a given event happening. A risk is 
often measured by two things: 
○ How likely is the event to happen? 
○ How bad are the effects going to be as a result of the event taking place? 
A simple example – a homeowner defaulting on their mortgage. Banks do a background check on a 
customer’s credit history to see if they have a history of paying back credit loans on time and hence 
minimise the risk of the loan not being repaid (this is frequently referred to as a default [n.]). If they also 
don’t pay back the loan on the house, the bank repossesses the house and sells it to try to recover the 
money owed. If the housing market is plummeting as well, they probably won’t be able to recover the 
full value of the loan that they sold to the customer for the house to be purchased, which in itself is 
another risk to the bank. Notice here that the effect on the bank is that they’ll be out of money and have 
to ‘balance the books’ to stay afloat, which was a huge issue back in 2008 when the housing market fell 
apart as the banks issued thousands of NINJA loans (No Income, No Job, no Assets) and were 
surprised that the customers in question couldn’t pay back the loans they’d taken out… What a 
surprise!  
● A business deal can be simplified to a buyer and a seller (as we all probably are aware…). It’s better to 
think of both of them as two pieces that together form the complete puzzle of a sale. A counterparty in 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
4 
a deal is either of these two pieces of the deal i.e. either the buyer or the seller. This might not make 
sense from the formal definition, so the following example will help alleviate the definition’s abstract 
nature. If you are the buyer, the party on the other side of the deal is your counterparty i.e. the seller. 
Likewise, if you are the seller, the buyer is your counterparty in the deal. The counterpart of a deal that 
you’re in is – quite literally – the counterparty. Why might this be relevant? Often, you will hear the 
concept of counterparty risk. Google defines it as the likelihood or probability that one of those 
involved in a transaction might default on its contractual obligation. Simply put, it’s the risk that the 
party on the other side of the deal refuses to honour the deal and deliver the goods or the money, 
based on whether they are the buyer or the seller. Counterparty risk forms the basis of the motives 
behind derivatives.  
● To hedge (v.) a risk is to take precautionary action so that if a bad event occurs, there is a way for the 
risks to be minimised. Airlines often buy aviation fuel a year ahead of time at current prices so that if 
the fuel prices increase a year from now, they can load fuel into the planes in 12 months time at the 
cheaper prices offered today, hence minimising the risk of having to pay increased prices (how 
convenient for today’s world, eh?). Conveniently, the same idea is incorporated into the term ‘hedge 
funds’ – you’ll come onto them at a later date if you choose to attend one of the society’s career talks in 
the coming weeks.  
● When you invest in an asset, you gain something called exposure (n.). In short, exposure is basically 
how much capital you stand to lose if your investment fails. If you pay £1 million for a house and 
tomorrow the housing market collapses, your exposure is exactly £1 million because tomorrow your 
house could be worth £0. Some exposures can be greater than the price you pay for a given asset 
(have a guess how this can be possible… negative pricing) – this is often the case in commodity 
pricing, which you will see in Lecture 7. 
● The spot price of an asset is how much someone is willing to buy the asset for right now i.e. on the 
spot. The spot price of an asset can be found on exchange boards. For example, you could go and buy 
Tesla stock right now for a spot price of $622. This price represents the amount that a trader will pay 
for instant delivery of the asset (in this case, a stock) and will constantly fluctuate depending on supply 
and demand. The spot price is largely responsible for determining the price of a derivative as we will 
discover later. Notice here the price of the stock is quoted in U.S. Dollars (USD) – that’s because Tesla 
stock are traded only in America at an exchange called the NASDAQ (the National Association of 
Securities Dealers Automated Quotations – these acronyms really are bizarre at times).  
The what and why of derivatives 
Lovely. So, we now know that all investments have an intrinsic risk (by intrinsic, I mean a built-in, natural risk 
that we can’t avoid – we buy an asset for a price that by definition can lose us money if its value falls to 0). We 
want to hedge these risks so that we don’t lose any money, which will help us minimise our exposure to capital 
loss if things go awry. Thankfully, for hundreds (if not thousands of years), investors have developed specific 
contracts containing mechanisms that help them hedge risk and minimise exposure whenever they invest… 
This is what a derivative does. In mathematics, derivatives help you calculate the rate of change of a function 
f(x) when evaluated at a point x. In finance, derivatives help you manage risk and minimise exposure alongside 
a few other niche benefits – they are NOT the same by any stretch of the imagination, although you might 
notice they are named the same because of the underlying idea.  
 
Derivatives answer the following question for investors: 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
5 
How can someone hedge their risks and therefore minimise their exposure to an 
investment? 
‘Derivatives’ is an umbrella term for a range of different types of contracts that offer the possibility for an 
investor to hedge a risk or make money from something called ‘speculating’ – believing that an asset is going 
to go up in price in the future. We should be aware of what a contract is but for those that aren’t sure, a 
contract simply involves two or more different ‘parties’ (which can be people, institutions etc.) and binds them 
to an agreement with specific terms and conditions attached. A derivative is a contract of which its value is 
based on an underlying asset. More generally, it gets its value from what is otherwise known as a financial 
product. 
 
The underlying asset (often denoted as UL – UnderLying) can be confusing at first to wrap your head around, 
but it’s quite simple to understand with some real-life examples. Most of you have come across derivative 
contracts based on an underlying asset before. When you buy insurance for your car or home, the insurance 
company quotes you a specific price based on the condition of the car or the location of your home. The car 
and the home is the underlying asset here – it’s an asset that’s taken into consideration by the insurance 
company to sell you a contract promising protection and cover if it crashes or goes up in flames (touch wood). 
Alternatively, if you don’t own a car or home, you might be familiar with a sports bet. If Arsenal are playing 
Manchester United on Sunday, a bookmaker or betting shop might quote you betting odds based on the result 
of the football game once it’s played. They might offer you 10/1 odds if you think Manchester United will win 3-
0 but instead offer 2/1 odds if you think Arsenal will win 1-0 (although we don’t advise you to get bogged down 
on how betting odds work – just the idea that the predicted result of the game is used as a basis to quote odds 
and prices is the point of this example). It’s a good idea to first think of options as bets although you will find 
that idea will become more blurred when we discuss specific derivatives later on, such as futures and forwards 
(but hey, it’s a good start either way.) 
 
There are a wide variety of underlying assets that can be used to form the basis of a derivatives contract, 
including stock indices, bonds, interest rates – even the weather conditions could be the basis of a derivatives 
contract. You might now start to realise how different underlying assets might be used by an investor to hedge 
risk at this point, but if you don’t, you’ll find out later once we get into the specifics.  
 
Note: Some of you might have realised, for instance, that we compound the concepts of the weather and 
interest rates with the idea of an underlying asset. You’d also be right to question this idea… Of course, you 
can’t own the weather nor can you own the interest rate. However, if you are e.g. a farmer, you would grow 
crops of which its value depends on the state of the climate in the growing season. If you base a derivative off 
the price of a crop (which is linked to the weather, which in turn influences supply – Lecture 2), you would have 
a derivatives contract that is indirectly linked to the weather in the end anyway. Likewise, treasury bonds (see 
Lecture 6) also are linked inversely with interest rate, so if the interest rate set by a CB falls, treasury bonds 
increase in price and then you can have a derivative linked to the price of a treasury bond, which depends on 
the CB interest rate! As a result, you have a derivative indirectly linked to something you can’t really own but 
want to monitor to hedge your risk. I personally dislike the idea of a UL and instead like to think of a ‘contract 
driver’ to eliminate all ambiguity as to what really affects the derivative itself, but it’s all personal preference in 
the end.  
What types of derivatives exist in financial markets? 
There are several types that you will come across if you choose to work in financial markets. We’ll only be 
covering a few of them, which you see listed below in the order that they’re featured in the notes: 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
6 
➔ Futures 
➔ Forwards 
➔ Options 
➔ Swaps 
 
Shorting 
You may have heard the terms selling and shorting used interchangeably, but there is in fact a subtle 
difference. Selling an asset involves exchanging an asset that you own for an agreed payment. Shorting 
involves the process of borrowing an asset and selling it on the market for cash now with the hope that you 
can buy the same asset back later at a cheaper price and return it to the lender. An example of this kind of 
trade is, 
 
1. Borrowing a share of a company worth $100 now that you think is going to go down in price.  
2. Selling it immediately on an exchange to gain $100.  
3. At a later date, buying the same share back for a lower price of $90.  
4. Returning the asset back to the lender and pocketing the difference between what I sold it for and what 
I bought it for ($10 profit) 
 
Of course, the price of the share could go up in value and we would instead make a loss.  
 
Because none of your own capital is used to borrow, the only money required to initiate a short trade is a small 
amount that acts as margin to the lender of the asset. margin is an amount of money held to cover the risk of 
you not being able to buy the asset back. If you make a loss it is taken out of the margin account and if the 
stock price stays the same, you get your margin back when the trade is closed. As only margin is used, traders 
can leverage their short positions by e.g. putting down $10 to have exposure to price movements in a stock 
worth $100. If the stock price increased by 10%, their total profit would be $110 - $100 = $10. This is  a 100% 
increase from the $10 initially provided. By putting down only a small amount of money, but still getting all of 
the profit and loss on a much larger underlying value, they are creating leverage. 
 
Shorting is a part of most FX trades done to hedge or speculate - more detailed diagrams of this can be seen 
in the slides. 
 
 
Types of derivatives 
Swaps 
A swap is an agreement between two parties (e.g. Two companies) to exchange cash flows (e.g. loan 
interest or coupons on a bond) on different bases (e.g. a fixed interest rate vs a LIBOR calculated rate) on 
particular dates in the future.  
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
7 
If this doesn’t make sense at first, don’t worry, we will quickly get into an example which should make the 
mechanics clear.  
 
In this definition, a cash flow is any movement of money into or from someone's account such as interest 
payments and ‘different bases’ means that the cash flows are calculated on different underliers such as 
interest rates, equity prices, or inflation or it is simply fixed at the start of the contract. There are many different 
variants of swaps which allow parties to swap the payments that they are receiving or making with another 
party based on different underlying cash flows.  
 
Before we look at the different types of swaps, it is important to understand two key terms: floating and fixed 
payments.  
 
● Fixed applies to payments that will not fluctuate over the payment period. These payments are known 
at the start, for example receiving 5% interest per year for the next 2 years. You can calculate exactly 
how much you need to pay in the future. The fixed rate may also be quotes as a spread above 
government bond yields, e.g. T + 35/40 basis points (where T is the prevailing yield for the current 
benchmark Treasury of the same maturity).  
 
● Floating applies to payments that can fluctuate during the payment period. For example this may 
include paying interest calculated as the current LIBOR rate (i.e. rate at which banks lend to each 
other). As you can imagine, the amount to be paid each month differs so that there is uncertainty in the 
future payments.  
 
A swaps contract can include any combination of these two payment specifications depending on the needs of 
the two parties involved. The parties involved in these types of transactions typically include:  
- Banks 
- Corporations 
- Institutional Investors 
- Hedge Funds 
- Government Entities
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
8 
 
The following example will give a basic idea of how these identities may use swaps to hedge risk or speculate. 
 
Example 5 - Interest Rate Swaps 
 
 
Consider that you are CEO of a large company and recently issued £10 million in short term bonds with an 
expiry in 3 years with a variable coupon rate paid semi-annually and calculated as the current London 
Interbank Offered Rate plus 2%. As you can imagine in the current economic climate, the future of interest rate 
rises is highly unpredictable and therefore you may be liable to pay larger amounts of interest (coupons) on the 
bonds if interest rates rise more than expected. To hedge against this risk and lock in the interest paid, you 
would enter a swap.  
 
The diagram below shows your future payments before getting a swap.  
 
 
 
The dotted lines here represent the cash flows from the company paying investors coupons of an amount 
determined at the date that the coupon is to be paid - the floating rate. As the coupons are paid semi-annually, 
the interest paid at each arrow is calculated as: Notional Principal x Interest Rate x Day Count Fraction. 
The Day Count Fraction in this case is equal to 180/360 (semi-annual - US convention). Note also the principal 
payments at the start, receiving investor funds, and at the end, paying back bond principal at expiry.  
 
To hedge the floating rate risk, the company enters a fixed-for-floating swap so that they can guarantee their 
future liabilities and pay a fixed rate. You find a bank who is willing to pay you LIBOR + 2% on £10 million 
notional and in exchange you pay a fixed rate of 4% for the next three years to the bank.  
 
Now each month you still pay the LIBOR + 2% to investors but immediately receive LIBOR + 2% from the 
swaps contract you entered. The two cancel out. You are still left to pay the 4% fixed fee to the bank so overall 
you are not at risk if LIBOR increases. The new payment structure can be seen below.  
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
9 
 
As you can see, the two floating rates cancel to give simply a 4% fixed payment removing any risk of interest 
rates on the future payments on the bond.  
 
 
You now make a ‘profit’ (compared to an unhedged position) if the floating payment exceeds 4% as you locked 
in a rate of 4% through the swaps contract.  
 
 
 
There are a number of different variations on the mechanic described above including fixed-for-fixed 
payments, floating-for-floating payments (basis swaps) and even fixed-for-fixed payments. These core 
mechanics can be applied to a wide range of underliers with the main contracts being:   
 
Interest Rate Swaps - the size of the cash flows are linked to the interest rates in the same country. Here the 
floating payments are usually linked to a market standard rate like LIBOR or SOFR.  
 
Currency Swaps - the size of the cash flows are linked to interest rates in different currencies 
 
Asset Swaps - the fixed coupon of a specific bond is exchanged with a benchmark floating rate plus a margin 
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate

To understand how a company may profit from a swap we can look at an example. Imagine you start a 
company, Simperial Ltd. and need some funds to run the business. Finally, your company decides on issuing 
£1 million in five-year bonds with a variable annual interest rate of 2% plus the federal funds rate. The federal 
funds rate fluctuates but now sits at around 1.75%. This means that you receive £10 million in funds 
immediately but have to pay back a rate of 2% plus the fed funds rate each year to the bond holders.  
 
To hedge the risk faced by the fed funds rate increasing, and therefore increasing your interest payments, you 
find a company ABC Inc. willing to enter into an interest rate swap contract with you. ABC Inc. also owe £10 
million on a loan they have but the interest that they pay is fixed at 4.5% per year. ABC Inc. likes the idea of a 
lower interest rate and can handle more risk than your company so you agree to swap cash flows. ABC Inc. 
pays your interest of 2% plus the fed funds rate and you pay their interest rate of 4.5%. Both debts are still 
paid but you now pay a fixed 4.5% while ABC Inc. covers your repayments. Table 2 shows how this scenario 
would play out if the fed funds rate happened to increase by 0.5% per year.

As you can probably see, if the fed funds rate increases above 3%, Simperial Ltd. will start saving money on 
the money repaid monthly to bond holders. A swap may also be favourable as lenders may be more likely to 
lend if your current loan obligations have fixed rates rather than floating rates. 
 
Finally, the swaps contract that is famous for providing major profits to those who foresaw the 2008 financial 
crisis: credit default swaps. A credit default swap (CDS) is an agreement by one party to pay the lost principal 
and interest of a loan to the CDS buyer if a borrower defaults on a loan. More simply, this contract acts like 
insurance on a debt obligation, e.g. a bond. You can enter this contract with a financial institution OTC who will 
pay you the entire value of a bond’s principal and future coupon payments if it defaults. In return, you pay a fee 
each set number of days that the bond has not defaulted.  
 
CDS contracts can be very profitable as Michael Burry found in 2008 when his fund Scion Asset Management 
entered these agreements against mortgage backed securities. Who would’ve predicted that the strongest 
market in the world, real estate, would collapse as the majority of homeowners couldn’t pay back their interest 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
11 
repayments? Obviously the banks that Bury entered these contracts with couldn’t imagine this and so his fund 
only paid a low interest rate in comparison to the huge amount of assets he was able to ‘insure’. Eventually, 
when the housing market did crash, these contracts paid out and he secured over $700 million in profit for his 
investors.    
 
Classifying derivatives 
Futures and forwards are called vanilla derivatives because the price, expiry and quantity of underlying assets 
are fixed as soon as the contract is entered. It may help to remember this by thinking of vanilla having an 
etymology for basic, implying that futures and forwards are the simplest contracts traded. This is not the case 
for all derivatives as we will see with exotic contracts where any one of these variables may change throughout 
the contracts lifetime before expiry.  
 
We can split the available derivatives into several types; vanilla, simple exotics, and complex exotics. The first 
class of vanilla derivatives are characterised by fixed terms that do not vary throughout the life of the contract. 
The terms that outline a derivative include:  
 
- The time period over which it acts (or expiry) 
- The price at which it is delivered for 
- The quantity and quality of the underlying asset traded 
 
A rule of thumb to follow is that even if just one term of the traditional vanilla structure is broken, then the 
derivative becomes an exotic. Simple exotics typically have only one term altered, for example an options 
contract which can be activated over a range of dates rather than just one. For more than one of the terms 
altered, the derivatives contract is known as a complex. 
 
Forward Rate Agreements (FRAs) 
We now move our discussion on to another type of interest rate derivative – Forward Rate Agreement.  
 
It is an OTC product between parties that determines the rate of interest to be paid by both parties on an 
agreed date in the future. The two parties will exchange different interest rates at a date in the future – for 
instance fixed against floating. 
 
We can see an example of an FRA in the example below:

As you can see, we are ‘swapping’ two interest rates between two parties, so this is kind of like an interest rate 
swap. In fact, an interest rate swap is just a series of FRAs at many different points in time in the future – 
hence why these two products are covered together here. 
 
Many large corporations will use FRAs to hedge future interest rate exposure. In our particular example, the 
company enters an FRA with the FRA desk at an investment bank. They agree that the company will pay a 
fixed 5.26% rate and receive a floating rate of SOFR. The company can then use this to pay their debt/loans, 
which have an interest rate of SOFR + 150bps. 
 
Therefore, we see the company’s net position is an interest rate of 5.26% + 150bps = 6.76%. This means they 
have changed their interest rate from floating to fixed and are no longer exposed to movements in SOFR. 
 
Futures and Forwards 
To get a basic understanding of derivatives we can start with the two simplest types: futures and forwards. 
The difference between these two derivative contracts is essentially that futures can be bought by anyone, 
forwards can only be bought by institutional investors dealing with a trader at a bank. This will be discussed 
later, but they function largely identically so for now we will look at both. 
 
Both futures and forwards contracts allow someone to buy or sell (depending on if they are long or short the 
contract, respectively) a set amount of an underlying asset, for a set price at a set point in the future (e.g. 
you have to buy 100 barrels of oil for $100 per barrel in 3 months time, referred to as the expiry or settlement 
date). At the agreed set point in the future, the buyer/seller is obliged (i.e. they have to do it) to buy or sell the 
agreed amount of an underlying asset for the agreed price. Note that goods and cash are only exchanged at 
the settlement date. One of the parties to a forward or futures contract assumes a long position and agrees to 
buy the underlying asset on a certain specified future date for a certain specified price. The other party 
assumes a short position and agrees to sell the asset on the same date for the same price. It can help to look 
at an example to get a better understanding.  
 
Let’s consider an example of a futures contract in the context of agricultural livestock.  
 
> Example 1 - Futures and Forwards 
 
You enter a futures contract for live cattle that expires (or ‘reaches the agreed transaction date’) in 2 months 
for $53,500 (this price is roughly accurate and will get you 50,000 pounds or around 60 live cattle!). 2 months 
pass and the spot market price (i.e. current price) of cattle is now $60,000. If the cows are sold immediately 
after delivery, what is your profit/loss? To be clear here, when we mention delivery in finance, we are talking 
about the cows being transferred i.e. delivered from the seller (probably a farmer in this case) to you, making it 
your responsibility to look after them however you wish once the contract’s been agreed.  
 
To answer these sorts of questions, let’s look at the question from the perspective of our cash flow.
