If you think this example was pretty easy, you’d be right – because it was. However, more complicated 
situations require intricate analysis of each thing involved so breaking it down in an appropriate structure saves 
you lots of stress, time and your proprietary trading account. Of course, this could go the other way and the 
spot market price of cattle could go down leading to a loss. If supply of cattle increases whilst demand does 
not increase (or even decrease), the price could then fall to e.g. $40,000 and you’d be facing a loss of – 
$13,500 (that is, – 53,500 + 40,000 = – 13,500). Like we mentioned before, derivatives are great when used 
right to give you huge profit. but dangerous when you’re not careful of their risks.  
 
So, the above example makes sense but why would anyone use a forward or futures contract instead of 
buying the underlying asset straight away? They have the same outcome, right? Either way, I get to own 60 
live cattle after paying the appropriate price(s) involved. In theory, yes you’re right. But, do you really want to 
look after oil/cattle/gold for 2 whole months? Think about it – if you buy cattle today, where are you going to 
store it? You might impulsively buy the cattle today as you believe that today’s price will be the cheapest for 
the next 12 months, but where do you store it? Do you own a farm to place all these cattle? If you’re a trader, 
you probably don’t. Can you feed them for 2 months before selling them on at a higher price? Probably not, 
especially if you need to fund their food and other associated costs (you don’t want to sell cattle that’s 
underfed or even worse, dead). Futures and forwards avoid this issue by allowing you to lock-in i.e. fix a 
certain price for something you need in y months time in the future without the need to purchase it now, paying 
high rates of storage costs to store it for several months before using the asset itself. 
 
Where are forwards and futures used? The aviation industry makes heavy use of futures and forwards to fill up 
their planes with aviation fuel. Imagine that you own an airline and don’t want to risk the price of oil going up 
over summer when you have a large amount of flights to run. You could either buy jet fuel now for $x and have 
to store it until summer, or you could buy a forward contract. This allows you to fix the current price of jet fuel 
and not have it delivered until summer when it’s needed. Jet fuel melts steel beams (probably), so you 
wouldn’t want to put it in a warehouse for a year or two before you plan to use it because if it catches fire, the 
only place your investment is headed is into the sky in a puff of smoke and you certainly can’t pump that into a 
Boeing 737 to make it fly.  
 
As a trader, you may also prefer futures/forward to buying the underlying asset as you don’t actually have to 
keep the asset but instead to benefit from its price movements in addition to being able to access large 
amounts of leverage unavailable when buying solely the underlying asset. You’ll see this being mentioned 
further in these notes, but there are possibilities in finance where you can enter a derivatives contract without 
actually having the underlying asset delivered - this is called cash settlement. Essentially, you would own the

14 
UL for a set period of time and profit from any change in price when the contract expires. There’s more on this 
later, so if you don’t understand now then do not worry!  
 
Let’s take an example for starters. Oil is a common topic when it comes to derivatives, so let’s add some 
realism to our notes. For example, the image below shows the January expiry oil futures contracts on one of 
the most popular derivatives exchanges, the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME).  
 
 
Figure 1 - Crude Oil Futures Contract (Jan expiry) 
 
If you go long on this contract the price at the current time is locked in. At first no money is exchanged, and 
then on the 2nd of January, you pay the seller the quoted price upon entering the contract and in return get 
1000 actual barrels of oil, or more commonly a cash settlement equivalent to the profit you would’ve made.  
 
Remember, longing a contract means you’re in a position to buy it but shorting a contract means you’re in a 
position to be selling it. This is of course not ideal if you don’t actually want a physical barrel of oil so most 
contracts are cash settled i.e. you instead get the difference between the price you are forced to pay (strike 
price) and the current price of oil. 
 
Notice how the quoted contract price fluctuates over time, this can be attributed to changes in the underlying 
price of spot oil (i.e. oil for delivery now) as well as how long the contract has until it expires. We will cover this 
more closely later but it’s good to realise that this type of derivative can be actively traded just like a stock or 
commodity.   
How do futures and forwards differ? 
As promised, we will now look at the difference between the seemingly identical futures and forwards. The 
main difference is how the two contracts are traded. Forwards are traded over the counter (OTC) and 
futures are traded on an exchange. 
 
Let’s start with futures, and how an exchange actually is involved.  
 
Exchange traded markets - Futures 
Starting with the type of trading that you may be familiar with, exchange trading. A derivatives exchange is a 
market where individuals can trade contracts that have terms defined by the exchange - standardised 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
15 
contracts. This means that everyone on the exchange will have access to a basket of the same type of 
contracts (e.g. 1000 barrels of oil expiring in September may be one standardised contract or expiry in October 
may be another) and so there are generally lots of buyers and sellers for the same contract. This creates what 
is called liquidity; how easy it is to unload (buy or sell) a given contract. You can see this in Figure 1.1 above, 
there are specific contracts that can be traded rather than tailored products.  
 
Initially, the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) was created in 1848 to bring farmers and merchants together 
with a main focus of standardising the quantities and qualities of grains being traded. Farmers used to trade 
any quantity of a commodity e.g. 1, 32 or 69 tonnes of wheat and potatoes. This became an issue as buyers 
wanted a more consistent system where they could be quoted specific quantities of a commodity e.g. multiples 
of 100 tonnes, which is what standardisation helped to achieve. Not long after, the CBOT introduced the first 
futures-type contract, the to-arrive contract. This is indicative of the scheduled delivery date of a set amount of 
standardised grain. The CBOT no longer exists and instead merged with the Chicago Mercantile Exchange to 
form the CME Group which is one of the largest derivatives exchanges in the world.  
 
Let’s go back to the mechanics of the exchange. Once two traders have agreed on a trade, it is handled by the 
exchange clearing house. The clearing house acts as a middleman between traders and manages the risk 
to each party. This ensures that when trading on an exchange, each trader does not need to worry about 
the creditworthiness of the other trader. This is possible as the clearing house requires each party to 
provide collateral in the form of ‘margin’. Margin is a set amount of money that each trader has in their trading 
account that can be used to pay any debts which may arise when a trade goes against them.  
 
Let’s try another example to make this clear. If Ahmad enters a futures contract on an exchange for oil, the 
exchange quotes he may receive are below: 
 
 
Figure 2 - Futures exchange snapshot

Crude and Dubai oil as you saw in the commodities lectures) traded on the Chicago Mercantile Exchange. 
Along the left edge, you can see the different expiry times for each contract. Looking across to the right you 
can then see the Last price obtained (i.e. last trade to happen), the Prior Settle (i.e. the price traded at the end 
of the last trading period), and the open, high and low. The open price is the price of the contract at the start of 
the current trading window, high is the highest value trade that occurred in the window and low is the lowest 
value trade that occurred. The volume gives the number of each contract that was traded in the day. Each of

these contracts has a price which represents the price per barrel, however each contract is specified for 1000 
barrels. So if you entered one of these contracts, your exposure would be the price x 1000.  
 
Let's say he picks the Dec contract, so the settlement occurs on the 14th of December. Each contract is for 
1,000 barrels and he ‘locks’ in a price of roughly $93.40 by entering this contract (assuming that he can get the 
contract for close to the last traded price). His exposure is therefore $93400. 
 
Exchanges typically ask for a 3-12% margin depending on the volatility of the underlying asset and so Ahmad 
would have to give $2,802 - $11,208 as margin to ensure that, if the trade goes against him, there is money 
that can be used to settle the trade debt. Remember, volatility is how much the price of an asset tends to 
change in a given time period so we often use the standard deviation to calculate it.  
 
The image above of futures price quotes shows an electronic exchange market, where trades can be agreed 
and entered through a computer. Traditionally, derivatives exchanges have used an open outcry system. This 
required traders to physically meet on the trading floor or just shout or signal trades that they would like to 
complete across the room. Exchanges have now largely been moved from this system to electronic trading for 
one main reason: it’s much faster and less likely to go wrong, making sure all the participants are ‘heard’ 
equally online.  
Over-the-Counter Markets (OTC) – Forwards 
These are the markets which you will not see until you’re working in finance yourself. Only the biggest players 
such as banks, other large financial institutions, fund managers and corporations are the main participants in 
the OTC derivatives markets. This market primarily consists of bank traders quoting prices for certain financial 
instruments that the bank may offer, often described as ‘bespoke’ financial products. 
 
Traditionally, participants in the OTC derivatives markets will contact each other directly by phone and email, 
or will have counterparties for their trades using an interdealer broker. Here the participants (perhaps a hedge 
fund looking to speculate on the price of gold) will be able to phone up their designated trader at a bank and 
get a quote on the bid (short position price or ‘how much the bank will pay’) and offer (long position price or 
‘how much the bank will sell for’) for a particular forward contract. Banks often act as market makers for the 
more commonly traded instruments.  
 
Once an OTC trade has been agreed, the two parties can either settle (execute) the trade with a central 
counterparty (a CCP – basically, just an intermediary between the two parties) or clear (ensure the trade 
happens) the trade bilaterally. A CCP stands between the two parties to the derivatives transaction so that one 
party does not have to bear the risk that the other party will default. Alternatively, when trades are cleared 
bilaterally, the two parties have usually signed an agreement covering all their transactions with each other. 
This agreement covers most importantly, how much collateral must be posted by each side of the trade and 
under which circumstances the outstanding trades can be terminated. 
How big are these markets?  
Huge! Well, at least the value of the underlying assets which derivatives are based on are. For example, an 
over-the-counter agreement may be to buy 100 million US dollars with euros but typically only 1 – 2% of this 
amount would ever change hands as the cash flows cancel out and only the profit is transferred from the trader 
who lost on the trade. The frequency of OTC derivatives trades is smaller than the number of exchange 
derivatives trades. This does make sense when you consider the ease of trading electronically and high 
frequency trading compared to phoning or emailing a bank to ask for a quote on an OTC-traded forward. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
17 
However, the average size of an OTC trade dwarfs the average exchange trade alluding to a much larger OTC 
derivatives market (by market cap or total underlying assets) than the exchange traded derivatives market. 
This also makes sense if you consider why you wouldn’t want a computer to be handling deals worth tens of 
millions or billions of GBP each.  
 
The figure below compares the estimated total principal amounts underlying outstanding transactions in the 
over-the-counter and exchange markets between June 1998 and December 2021. This demonstrates the 
explosion of derivatives into the 21st century. Around 2008, you can see a decline after the financial crisis 
caused primarily by the danger of leveraged derivatives. Today, the total derivatives OTC market is estimated 
to have an underlying principal (assets combined value) of over $1 quadrillion!  
 
Figure 1.1 Estimated size of underlying assets for derivatives markets 
 
The main reasons for the huge success of the derivatives market include the ability for traders to access large 
amounts of liquidity (lots of buyers and sellers) as well as the wide range of different payoffs (profit and 
loss behaviour) that can be synthesised with just one underlying asset.  
How are these contracts normally traded?  
Imagine if every time that you entered one of these derivatives contracts, you had to take delivery of the 
underlying asset. As one junior trader in a large investment bank found out, this would be highly impractical for 
the majority of players in the financial system looking to profit from these contracts. It is reported that one 
trader forgot to sell on one of their forward contracts with cows as the underlying asset and subsequently was 
forced to take delivery and leave the office to sort out the cattle storage. To avoid this situation happening 
each time, there are a few ways that profit can be made without taking delivery:  
 
➔ Buying a contract with the opposite characteristics (e.g. if a trader is long they can close the trade by 
opening a short position for an equal amount of the underlying asset) to secure an exit strategy. 
➔ Only entering cash settled contracts. 
 
Most of the time, a bank will close a forward contract position by either entering an equivalent short contract if 
they are long or an equivalent long contract if they are short. This moves the responsibility outlined by the 
initial contract onto another party so that the bank only receives cash incoming and outgoings. For example, if 
a trader is long on 1000 ounces (oz) of gold forward contract, they will close the trade using a short position on 
a 1000 oz. gold forward contract. When the initial long position expires, they get delivery of the gold (after 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
18 
paying the amount outlined in the first contract) which they then need to deliver to another party whom they 
made the second closing position with (who will then pay the trader for the gold delivered at the price specified 
in the second short forward contract). 
 
For most traders this can be inconvenient so exchange traded futures contracts are normally cash settled. A 
cash settlement is a settlement method used in certain futures and options contracts where the seller of the 
financial instrument does not deliver the actual underlying asset but instead transfers the associated cash 
position. This cash settlement enables investors to trade more often as the contracts require less time and cost 
to deliver upon expiration, therefore resulting in more liquidity being bought into derivatives markets by this 
mechanism.  
 
Now that you have a firm understanding of how forwards and futures contracts work, we can start to look at the 
payoff characteristics of these derivatives.  
Payoffs 
The term ‘payoff’ is one that you’ll frequently come across when reading derivatives. We’re going to explain the 
concept of payoffs separately for each derivatives contract that you see in this lecture as we feel that each 
derivative behaves differently, so trying to do a ‘one size fits all strategy’ would make things confusing.  
 
A payoff in the context of forwards and futures is simply the amount of money an investor has lost or gained by 
preparing a long or short position on a futures or forwards contract – it’s a bit like gross profit. Forwards and 
futures contracts have the same linear payoff characteristics. When we mean linear, the payoff is literally just a 
straight line. To start, we will look at how a forward contract behaves assuming that the contract is at expiry 
and the contract is cash settled (these payoffs also apply to delivered contracts but the profit is not realised in 
cash - i.e. you instead own the asset for cheaper/more expensive than without the contract) . When we say the 
payoff has been ‘realised’, we simply mean that the profit (or loss, noting it as negative profit) has been 
confirmed and has been accounted for in the investor’s cash flow statements. 
 
In general, the payoff from a long position in a forward contract on one unit of an underlying asset is    
 
𝑃 =  𝑆 −  𝐾 
 
where P is the payoff realised, S is the spot price of the underlying asset at maturity of the contract and K is 
the delivery price (the price to be paid for the asset upon delivery outlined in the forward contract). Similarly, 
the payoff from a short position in a forward contract on one unit of an asset is 
 
𝑃 =  𝐾 −  𝑆 
 
noting that the investor would be paid the delivery cost K and have to buy the asset back at S. 
Because it costs nothing to enter into a forward contract, the payoff from the contract is also the trader’s total 
gain or loss from the contract.

Pricing 
So, now you have a firm grasp of how futures and forwards work as well as how they differ, there is one key 
part missing… How are they priced? Both futures and forwards have prices which are heavily reliant on the 
current price of the underlying asset – think back to what we considered as our model of a derivative. 
Standardised, exchange traded contracts such as futures are normally quoted in specific units where the final 
cost of the contract is a multiple of this base unit. For example, cattle futures are quoted normally in terms of 
cents per pound of cow, or oil futures are quoted in terms of price per barrel. The actual contract however 
specifies how many of this base unit must be purchased upon expiry, e.g. 40,000 pounds of cattle or 1000 
barrels of oil.  
 
There are a number of factors that will determine the price of a futures contract, most importantly the spot price 
of the underlying asset when the contract is entered. Other factors listed in the table below also have a role in 
determining futures prices as well for different underlying assets. 
 
Factor Comments 
➔ Spot price of the underlying asset. The MOST important factor. 
➔ Interest rates 
➔ Dividend income 
➔ Storage costs 
➔ Risk-Free rate 
➔ Convenience yield 
They don’t apply to all types of underlying assets as 
the types of ULs can vary, but they cover (broadly) 
most of the ones you’ll see in the world of finance.

There are a few key ideas to understand here, but don’t be worried if these concepts aren’t immediately clear 
as it often takes a while for some mechanisms to sit comfortably before considering more complex trade ideas! 
Just quickly we will cover how the interest mechanics work and then look at some pricing examples.  
 
 
 
Interest Conventions 
 
As most of you may know, we can find simple interest (i.e. no compounding) as  
 
𝑃 =  𝑋 ×  (1  + 𝑡 × 𝑟) 
 
Where r is the interest rate over one period, X is the value initially invested and P is your 
final payout after t periods. You may also be familiar with compounded interest, found as 
 
𝑃 =  𝑋 × (1  + 𝑟)𝑡  
 
This is where the gain over each investment period is then reinvested and so earnings also 
start bearing interest. In the financial markets there are odd conventions whereby spans 
shorter than a year are calculated using simple interest but spans over a year are found 
using compound interest. This has something to do with how lending parties either apply 
interest annually, or if the term is shorter than a year, then they apply interest at the end of 
the term. With this in mind, we can look at how interest rates impact futures prices. 
 
 
 
 
We’re going to be talking about the following 4 examples listed below, so familiarise yourselves with them 
thoroughly as on the next page, we’ll be diving deeper to discuss the intricate mechanics behind each of these 
examples in finer detail: 
 
1. EXAMPLE 1, Page 21 
The futures price of a non-dividend-paying and non-storable asset (e.g a stock that pays no dividends) 
is the function of the risk-free rate (or interest rate if we’re talking about currencies), spot price and time 
to maturity.  
 
2. EXAMPLE 2, Page 23 
Assets that are expected to pay an income through dividends will decrease the price of the futures.  
 
3. EXAMPLE 2, Page 24 
Storage costs always increase the futures price as the seller of the futures incorporates the cost into 
the contract. 
 
4. EXAMPLE 3, Page 24 
Convenience yields, which indicate the benefit of owning another asset rather than the futures, 
decrease the futures price.  
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
21 
Before we look at how to calculate futures delivery prices, it is important to understand why the above 
mentioned factors actually impact the prices paid for a futures contract. As with the rest of the market, futures 
prices are driven by supply and demand. The current price presented at an exchange represents how much 
the highest bidder will pay. The reason that something such as an interest rate will impact this amount is 
because markets are generally very efficient in ensuring there are no arbitrage (‘riskless return’) opportunities.  
 
Arbitrage is the process of finding a guaranteed profit. That’s the financial definition in terms of what benefits 
arbitrage can reward you with, but more specifically arbitrage occurs when the same asset is sold in two 
different places at two different prices. Think of something as simple as a stock. Carnival Corporation & plc is 
traded on the London Stock Exchange (CCL:LSE) and on the New York Stock Exchange (CCL:NYSE). In 
theory (noting all prices as USD), CCL:LSE could trade at $20.00 (that’s £16.66 in GBP) whilst CCL:NYSE 
could trade at $20.50 at any one moment in time. Noting the $0.50 difference, a trader could buy 100 shares of 
CCL at the LSE and sell them at the NYSE and pocket a cool $50 (0.50 × 100, noting we trade 100 shares) 
profit in difference before costs are paid for – this is achieved simply by buying in one place at a cheaper price 
and selling it elsewhere at a more expensive price. It’s rare for this to occur in stock markets as algorithmic 
trading is faster than any human trader could mash a stock ticker into Bloomberg. 
 
Let’s illustrate arbitrage in the finance world. For example (ignoring interest for now), if we imagine 
hypothetically that an Apple stock (NASDAQ:AAPL) futures contract expired tomorrow and the delivery price 
was $110 but the spot price for the stock right now was $100. We could buy Apple stock now:  
 
Losses Gains 
– $100 + 1 Apple Share 
 
Then, we short (remember, sell) the futures contract. Now, when we wait until tomorrow and the contract 
expires, the following occurs: 
 
Losses Gains 
– 1 Apple Share + $110 (This is how much the buyer of the forward 
contract pays us, the seller, for 1 Apple share as 
agreed) 
 
So, now we are left with no apple shares and $10 out of thin air. See how easy it is to make free money! Well, 
lots of traders in the market did and now the competition to find these trades is incredibly fierce, it would be 
almost impossible for a retail trader to find an arbitrage opportunity making even $0.01 on the above example. 
This is because each time someone executes the arbitrage trade, they sell the futures contract so its highest 
available bid decreases (price decreases) and the price of the shares increases and closes the price gap 
between the two. This happens continuously until there is no arbitrage opportunity left to take and the price 
stabilises. In this case, there are no factors impacting the price other than the underlying asset (Apple stock) 
price, therefore the futures contract would settle at a price of $100.  
 
Now that you understand why futures prices move to consider certain factors, let's look at some arbitrage 
examples which explain how each type of underlying asset may allow the futures contract to settle at a certain 
price. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
22 
Example 1: Pricing non-dividend-paying non-storable asset futures 
Let’s revisit the above example with the Apple stocks but now consider the impact of interest rates. Consider 
the same Apple stock that pays no dividends (in reality, the dividend is 0.63% but ignore this for now) and is 
worth $100. We also know that you can borrow or lend money for 1 year at 5%. What should the 6 month 
futures price of the stock be? 
 
The answer is the initial $100 with simple interest applied monthly for 6 months at an annual interest rate of 
5%: 
 
𝑃 =  $100 ×  (1 + 0.05 × 180
360)  =  $102.53 
 
Here we use the annual interest rate multiplied by the number of days where interest applied over the base. 
The base is the quoted number of days for the given interest rate of 5%.  
 
 
 Be careful when assuming a base of 365 days, this may seem like common sense, but if 
working with most interest rates the base is actually 360. This is a very odd convention with 
the UK being part of only a handful of countries to use 365 days.  
 
 
More generally, the formula we use is given as: 
 
𝑃 =  𝑆 ×  (1 + 𝑟 × 𝐷
𝐵) 
 
where P is the futures price, S is the spot price of the underlying asset, r is the interest rate achieved (quoted 
as an annual amount without compounding), D is the days until trade expiry and B is the base used (usually 
365). Note that the above equation only applies for periods under a year! If we instead wanted to find the 
future value for a period over a year, we would use the following formula which assumes compounded interest 
 
𝑃 =  𝑆 ×  (1 + 𝑟)𝑡 
 
Think about what a trader would do if the futures price was lower than the spot price for a stock that they 
owned – this is known as futures contract mispricing. Understanding why the futures delivery price increases 
with positive interest requires us to consider e.g. if the quoted futures price was instead $95. In this scenario, 
an investor who already owns the stock could sell it for $100 and enter into a long forward contract to buy it 
back for $95 in 6 months. The proceeds of selling the stock could be invested at 5% to earn $2.47 (as seen 
above – the same calculation) and after a year, the investor would pay $95 to get the Apple stock back. This 
process would guarantee a profit of $100 - $95 + $2.47 = $7.47 despite any moves in the underlying stock. 
 
This is summarised in the table below, showing the relevant cash flows and overall total profit. 
 
Event Net Cash Flow ($) 
1. Short the underlying AAPL stock. + 100.00 + ( you owe 1 Apple share) 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
23 
2. Invest the proceeds i.e. loaning it at 5% 
interest rate for one year, monthly 
compounded.  
100.00 × (1+0.05 x 180/365) 
= 102.47 
i.e. + 2.47  
3. Long a share of AAPL 1 year later at $95 by 
exercising the futures option. 
– 95.00  + (you receive an apple share and 
immediately give it to the person you owe) 
TOTAL PROFIT + 100.00 + 2.47 – 95.00 = 7.47  
 
As we learned earlier, this is an arbitrage opportunity and in reality, the price would move to ensure this would 
not occur and the future contract is correctly priced. At a futures price of $102.47, the invested income from 
selling the stock in steps 1,2 would be cancelled out by the higher delivery price. i.e. + $100 from selling the 
stock + $2.47 interest - $102.47 delivery price = $0. Note here that the reason why I get $0 overall is that I sell 
the underlying stock today at $100 and exercise a futures contract at the same time from another seller. I 
invest the $100 I earnt to earn another $2.47 thanks to the magic of interest, but then I will end up buying back 
an AAPL stock through the futures contract at a price of $102.47, leaving me with… nothing. This is a simple 
example although arbitrage works more commonly when sources of credit e.g. loans are involved. 
 
If the price of the futures contract was instead higher than $102.47, e.g. $110, the reverse operation could be 
used to make risk-free profit from arbitrage.  
 
1. We could sell the forward contract for e.g. $110 (such that we owe one share in 6 months time). Here 
no money is moved immediately. 
2. Borrow money from a bank and buy the underlying asset (AAPL share) at its spot value for $100.  
3. 6 months later, we then transfer this AAPL stock to the buyer and pocket the $110 for the price of the 
futures contract. We return the $100 we borrowed, along with an additional $2.47 interest rate to give 
us an overall profit of $7.53. 
 
Event Net Cash Flow ($) 
1. Sell a futures contract for 6 months time 
at a price of $110 
0 - no money moves yet 
2. Simultaneously borrow $100 from a bank 
and buy an AAPL share at the spot price 
of $100 
- $100 + 1 AAPL share  
3. 6 months later, transfer the AAPL stock to 
the buyer of the futures contract you sold 
and receive $110. Here you would also 
pay the interest due on the initial loan.  
- 1 AAPL share + $110 - $100 (loan) - $2.47 (interest) 
TOTAL PROFIT - 100.00 + 110.00 – 2.47 = 7.53  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
24 
 
Tricks to understand futures pricing 
Another mindset which can help you to understand futures pricing is to think:  
If I have $100, what would I prefer as a trader, to receive a set asset now for $100, 
or to receive the asset in the future for $100? An accurate price will make this 
decision impossible. 
- If I receive the asset now for $100, in one years time I’m left with no money 
and the asset (let's say a bar of gold for example)  
OR 
 
- If I have to only pay $100 in the future I can simply invest my $100 now, earn 
interest, and then buy the asset in the future for $100. This leaves me with the 
asset, plus some extra income in one year's time.  
 
The second choice here is obviously more appealing, therefore we expect that we would 
have to also lose the potential interest if purchasing the asset in the future (through an 
inflated futures price) for both outcomes to be equal.  
 
A  futures price is always the future value of the underlying asset if it was bought 
today 
 
Example 2: Pricing income yielding and storable asset futures 
Let’s take our model from example 1 a bit further. Again, we can build on this model by including any dividends 
that Apple may pay to its shareholders. It may not seem intuitive but stock dividends do the opposite to what 
interest rates do in terms of the value of our futures contract. First, we’re going to present to you the equation 
used to price income-yielding futures contracts and then justify why it is written the way it is. So, we can 
incorporate dividends into the above equation as  
 
𝑃 =  𝑆 × (1 + (𝑟 −  𝑑) × 180/365) 
 
*Please be aware that this equation only applies to periods below a year - a derivation of the pricing equation 
for periods over a year is given in the slides* 
 
For time horizons over a year, the pricing equation is given as 
 
𝑃 =  𝑆 × ((1 + 𝑟)𝑡  − (1 +  𝑑)𝑡  −  1) 
 
The derivation of this equation can be found in the slides 
 
where d is the percentage (%) dividend paid on the stock per year. This formula assumes that the dividend is 
paid out semi-annually, such that in this scenario the dividend compounded income can be neglected. The 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
25 
futures price decreases when there is a known interest income because the long side buying the futures does 
not own the asset and, thus, loses the interest benefit. Otherwise, the buyer would simply buy the underlying 
asset for a year instead of the forward/future. Why would you buy a futures contract in AAPL and wait a year 
when you could just buy the AAPL stock itself and benefit yourself to the 0.63% dividend rate that it offers. In 
the case of stock, the long side loses the opportunity to get dividends by entering a forward so a compensation 
must be made in the forward/futures price to deter traders from instead buying the underlying stock.  
 
Now that we’ve ‘priced in’ the dividend rate, one more important aspect in pricing considers assets with 
storage costs. The long side does not incur any storage costs until it actually owns the asset. Therefore, the 
short side charges the long side for the compensation of storage costs in the futures price. This makes sense 
– someone has to pay the warehouse for area to store the underlying asset. If the futures price was not 
increased to account for storage, it would be far more beneficial to enter the futures contract rather than buy 
the asset now and store it for a year. Storage costs are usually given as a single figure per given amount of 
storable asset rather than a percentage, therefore our pricing mode for periods below a year can be adapted 
as: 
 
𝑃 =  𝑆 × (1 + (𝑟 −  𝑑) × 180
360) +  𝑘 × 𝑡 
 
where k is the cost to keep (store) the underlying asset per unit time and t is the number of units of time until 
contract expiry. All commodities require storage and therefore often have higher futures prices than their spot 
prices. 
Example 3: Pricing FX futures 
Some of the most commonly traded types of futures contract are based on currency pairs (e.g. EUR/USD). As 
currencies don’t require storage (because they’re electronic, unless you want to take delivery of bank notes or 
coins) and provide no income such as dividends, the futures price is impacted only by the interest rates which 
apply to each currency.  
 
As we found in prior lectures, buying a currency pair requires borrowing the second currency and using it to 
buy the first currency, e.g. for a long position in EUR/USD, you would borrow USD and exchange it for EUR. 
Therefore, we earn interest on our EUR and pay interest on our USD. 
 
Now imagine that we want to buy a 2 year forward for GBP/USD, how would we price it? We know that the 
current spot price of GBP/USD is 1.18, i.e. 1 pound exchanges for 1.18 dollars, and the annual US interest 
rate is 2.50% and the UK rate is 1.75%. 
 
Remembering that a futures price is always the future value of the underlying asset if it was bought today, we 
can calculate the future outcome of buying this currency pair today given that the exchange rate doesn’t move.  
 
We will be buying pounds and so, at the current interest rate, £1 now  will be worth (1 + 1.75%)2  ×  1 = 
£1.0353 in 2 years time. Conversely, we will be borrowing dollars and so, at the current interest rate, $1.18 
borrowed now will be (1 +  2.5%)2 × 1.18 =  $1.2397 owed in 2 years. Therefore, our debt in 2 years will be 
worth $1.2397 and our assets will be worth £1.0353. To avoid any arbitrage opportunity, this is what we must 
therefore pay for the 1 year maturity forward contract.  
 
This leaves our quoted exchange rate as 
$1.2397
£1.0353= 1.1974. 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
26 
It is common to find this kind of thought process very confusing so one way of finding the price of a forward or 
future can be to find the future value of the underlying asset as if you had instead bought the asset at spot 
value, i.e. today on the market and waited until the maturity of the forward/futures contract.  
 
The general formula for finding the forwards price of a given currency pair is given as 
 
𝐹 =  𝑆  × (1 +  𝑟2)𝑡 / (1 + 𝑟1)𝑡 
 
where F is the quoted currency pair ratio, S is the current spot exchange rate, r1 is the interest rate which acts 
on the first currency in the pair (the one being bought) and r2 is the second currency in the pair (i.e. the one 
being borrowed). t represents the number of time periods that the interest acts over. This equation can be 
used for positions held over a year, for shorter positions a similar simple interest version can be used,  
 
𝐹 =  𝑆  × (1 +  𝑟2 × 𝐷/𝐵)/ (1 + 𝑟1 × 𝐷/𝐵) 
 
Where D is the number of days until expiry and B is the base (e.g. 360 days in US and 365 days in the UK - 
odd convention I know!) 
 
Typically, futures contracts have higher settlement prices than their spot prices, therefore the further away a 
contract expiry, the more expensive it is. This is known as contango. Under some conditions, however, the 
opposite situation might occur, and the futures price could be lower than the spot price. This could be due to a 
temporary scarcity of the commodity on the spot market, for example, due to a restricted current supply, 
making the price of the commodity unusually high and expected to decrease in the future - why would I agree 
to buy a barrel of oil in a year's time at today's price if I know the price is going to decrease? This situation is 
known as backwardation. If the market is in backwardation, the fair value of a futures contract is more difficult 
to calculate. 
 
The examples that we have looked at so far find the fair value of futures and forwards contracts at any time 
before the contracts expires. Using the above equations we can also see that as 𝑡 −>  0 the contract prices 
become equal to the spot price of the underlying asset. We can see the relation between the contract and spot 
price as the contract approaches maturity in Figure 3 below.  
 
 
Figure 3: Spot price vs futures price as the contract approaches maturity

documents.title: Notes Lecture 6
documents.text:
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
2 
Table of Contents 
Options 3 
Call options 3 
Put options 4 
The mechanics behind options 5 
Intrinsic Value 6 
In the context of call options 6 
Piecewise functions 7 
In-the-money, out-of-the-money and at-the-money 8 
In the context of put options 8 
Combined options position strategies 9 
Payoff diagrams 9 
Profit & Loss graphs 10 
Short Straddle 11 
A long straddle 14 
A long strangle 16 
A short strangle 17 
Case study: Nick Leeson 17 
A gentle introduction to the Black-Scholes model 19 
The Greeks 23 
Origins from the Black-Scholes equation 23 
A simple interpretation of the Greeks 24 
Objectives of using derivatives 26 
Non-examinable material 28 
History of derivatives 28 
Options notation at an exchange 29 
Exotic options 30 
Barrier Options 30 
Swaptions 30 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
3 
Options 
So, let’s summarise futures and forwards. Both involve an agreement between the buyer and seller to buy or 
sell an asset at an agreed price in the future. The method of settlement and standardised terms of the contract 
differ between the two, but generally the concept is clear – you agree to buy an asset at some point in the 
future. One thing to note here: with futures and forwards, you are obligated to complete the transaction – you 
don’t have the option to choose whether or not buy or sell the asset at the expiry date (or ‘expiration’, 
whichever sounds better to you). Some of you might see where this is going, but if you’re still not seeing where 
this is headed, consider asking yourself: 
 
What if I want the option to exercise the contract at expiration? 
Well, you’d be in luck – that’s where options come in! 
 
Starting with the formal definition, an option is a derivative i.e. a contract that offers the buyer (of the 
derivative) the right but not the obligation to buy or sell the underlying asset at a certain date in the future 
(expiry date), at a specified price (the strike price). More informally, if you buy an option, you get to choose 
whether you want to buy or sell the asset rather than being forced to buy it at expiration i.e. you get the option 
to buy or sell (how conveniently named, eh?).  
 
If you’ve read the section on hedging risks, you might realise that options let you walk away from a deal if you 
feel like you changed your mind or if the market experiences a huge downturn such that prices are either too 
cheap to make a profitable sell or too expensive to make a worthwhile investment. Given the greater variability 
in possible outcomes, options do have more ‘strings attached’ by having more features associated with the 
way they work. We discuss here the most basic types individually first but we will later build upon the 
foundational knowledge of options to see how they work mathematically and how they can be combined in 
complex ways later to benefit us if the market goes up or down.  
Call options 
First, let’s list out the two main types of options available – call options and put options. Let’s start with call 
options. Call options are simple – they are contracts that offer the buyer the right but not the obligation to buy 
the underlying asset at the expiry date, at a predefined strike price. One way to best think about a call option is 
to imagine picking up the phone to call your stock broker to buy the stock that you’re interested in. You would 
lock in a strike price today and then be able to purchase the underlying asset in the future at that strike price, 
irrespective of the market price. Ideally, you would want the UL’s market price to go up so that you could 
choose to exercise the option i.e. buy the asset at a cheaper price and then sell it onwards to pocket the 
difference. However (and this is where you should pay attention), if the UL’s market price goes down, your 
strike price would be more expensive than the market price and you wouldn’t want to buy the option’s 
underlying asset at the strike price, since it’s cheaper to go into the market and pay less. As a result, you do 
not choose to exercise the option and only lose the amount you pay to secure the option in the first place. 
 
I want to give you an example using a different currency since we’ve been playing with USD the whole time. 
There are more currencies than the GBP and USD, so let’s try something a bit less vanilla.  
Consider this example: 
 
I’ve loved Nissan cars as a kid, but recently I heard that they’re going to replace the acclaimed GT-R series 
with a new model called the Super GT. They’ve had to stop selling the GT-R due to noise regulations in its 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
4 
European export markets but I think that the next model is going to be very popular. Nissan cars are reliable 
(they’re Japanese), more affordable and have one of the best brand reputations in the world. Currently, 
Nissan Motor Co. Ltd trades on the Tokyo exchange (7201:TYO) at 515.50 Japanese Yen (JPY, ¥). I think the 
price of the stock is going to hit ¥550 this time next year (24/07/23) but I want to buy it this time next year 
around the current price of ¥515.50 (NOTE – this is just an example and not actually my OWN opinion.) 
 
Right, that’s a mouthful there for an example. So, what I want to do is buy Nissan stock in 12 months time at 
today’s price of ¥515.50. What I want to do here is buy a call option that expires on the 24th of July 2023 at 
around ¥515.50. If I look at an options listing online, I might find an options call being traded at ¥10 for a strike 
price of ¥516. So, when the 24th of July comes around next year, if I’m correct in my assessment, the market 
price of Nissan stock is at ¥550 or higher whilst I can be safe and sound in the knowledge that I can buy the 
stock cheaper at ¥516.  
 
Now, before we draw out a table and do some calculations, I’m going to be clear on a few things here. Options 
prices are quoted per share. Why is this important? Options contracts are standardised, which means that the 
contract will sell me a specific amount of the UL – in the case of shares, this is 100 shares that each options 
contract lets me buy. I will put this in bold text to make this clear: I DO NOT PAY ¥10 FOR THE PRIVILEGE 
TO BUY 1 SHARE. I HAVE TO PAY ¥10 FOR EACH UNIT OF THE UL I PAY FOR! So, I have to pay ¥10 × 
100 = ¥1000, noting that I’m paying for 100 shares of the underlying stock. To the person who’s selling me this 
option call, this “discount-access” privilege fee I pay is called the premium of the option call. The trader who’s 
offered me this contract can charge me ¥10 for every share I wish to buy at a discounted price. Futures and 
forwards have no costs associated with setting them up, but options do – and they’re quoted per share! A lot of 
people forget this when they open up their Trading212 account and realise they have to pay 100x the price 
they’re quoted for because of the nature of a call contract.  
 
Once I’ve paid the premium for the contract, I then have to pay for the 100 shares at the discounted ¥516 I’m 
quoted on the options contract. That comes to a total of ¥51,600. In total, I’ve spent ¥52,600 to buy 100 shares 
of Nissan (in a year’s time) but this is better than waiting a year and having to spend the market price of 
¥55,000 (¥550 × 100), so I’ve saved myself ¥2,400 (¥55,000 – ¥52,600) that I can use for something else. One 
more thing… because this is an options contract, I might change my mind and decide “actually, I don’t want to 
buy the 100 shares of Nissan anymore”. Of course, you’d first pay the premium to earn the rights you’re 
entitled to in an options contract, but after that ¥2,400 have been paid for, you can choose not to buy the 
Nissan stock at ¥55,000 if for instance the price per share goes to ¥450. Why would you exercise a contract if 
you can just pay for the cheaper stock and admit you got it wrong? In that case, you just take the ¥2,400 yen 
loss, which is purely the premium you paid to get the contract in the first place.  
Put options 
On the flip side, what are put options? Put and call options are complements i.e. they are the opposites of 
each other! If you inverse everything you read about call options, you have a put option… how wonderful. For 
completeness, put options are contracts that offer the buyer (of the put option, remember!) the right but not the 
obligation to sell the underlying asset at the expiry date, at a predefined price. Think of a ‘put’ as you putting a 
stock on the table for someone to buy off you – that’s how it’s best to remember the concept. A strike price and 
expiry date is set for you to sell the underlying asset to a willing customer. Ideally, you would want the UL’s 
market price to go down so that while everyone else around you has to sell for cheaper (otherwise known as a 
‘discount’), you can sell your asset at the higher, pre-agreed strike price and not make a loss. Of course, if the 
market price does go up in the end, your strike price ends up being lower than the market price so the 
customer will exercise the option to buy the underlying asset at a discount to them and at a loss to you. 
Options really are a two-sided game of chess – one side will always win and the other will always lose so you 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
5 
really want to be sure that you’re well prepared to be on the winning side with detailed analysis and an eye for 
forecasting future shifts in market price and market opinion.  
 
Consider this example: 
 
I think that because of inflation and increasing interest rates, share prices are going to go down such that 12 
months from now, my 100 shares of Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) is going to go down in price. This analysis is 
also supported by the fact that I think the XboX Series X and Windows 11 aren’t going to be very big sellers 
since market sentiments suggest the PS5 and the new Mac M2 machines are going to gain market share 
respectively. Right now (06/07/22, 13:00), MSFT is trading at $262.86. I think that in a year’s time (06/07/23, 
13:00), it will be at $230.00 (NOTE – this is just an example and not actually my OWN opinion.) 
 
I want to sell my 100 shares of MSFT in a year’s time at around $262.86 while everyone else might probably 
be selling and buying shares at $230.00 on the market. How do I do this? I go on the exchange and buy 
myself a put options contract for MSFT at $265.00 that expires in a year’s time on 06/07/23. Yahoo tells me I 
can buy myself a call option for MSFT at $30.00 per share, which I think is a reasonable price. I buy the call 
option and pay a $3,000 premium ($30.00 × 100) to the trader offering me the quote and wait a year. After 12 
months, the price of MSFT is now $230.00, but I can sell my shares to the trader at $265.00 each, which he 
has to accept since I own the contract. He pays me $26,500, giving me an overall profit of $23,500 after 
premium costs have been deduced. If I’d gone to the exchange to sell my 100 shares of MSFT, I’d have sold it 
for $23,000, leaving me $500 shorter – that’s the value of 2 shares of MSFT. Thankfully, my trade paid off.  
 
Two small things about options that should be clarified before we talk about the maths behind them: 
● Options are standardised to multiples of 100 shares, as explained above. You know that it means there 
are rules surrounding how they are traded and the conditions that you have to meet in the contract. 
Those terms and rules are normally set by the exchange on which the option is traded on.  
● Options do have a price attached to them unlike futures and forwards. You first pay to buy an options 
contract (known as a ‘premium’) and then you get to have the chance to buy or sell the underlying 
asset in question – we’ll come onto this again with another example later when we discuss the maths 
behind options contracts.  
The mechanics behind options 
This section is to do with the maths behind a lot of the contracts that you saw above. A preamble before we 
start: if you don’t like maths, we sympathise! A Civil Engineering student and Chemistry graduate like 
ourselves don’t enjoy maths either but there’s a reason for it… maths is one of those subjects that’s just 
terribly explained throughout life and lacks the visual understanding that many of us are used to. We are going 
to try and do a deep dive and make the maths straightforward but if things don’t click for you by that point, we 
recommend you speak to one of us after the lecture or try going online (somewhere like StackExchange) to 
ask your questions. We’ve tried our best here to make it simple although we’re sure you’ll understand that it is 
easier said than done to make maths easy for everyone.  
 
I’ll be clear here: we will teach you these diagrams in detail and we will help you if you do get stuck – we often 
find this to be a weakness in students throughout the SEC and as a result, we aim to make sure this is well 
understood this year. They will appear on the exam and although they aren’t easy to wrap your head around 
them at first, we will do our best to go through them step-by-step, in an ordered and linear fashion. 
 
Right, rant over. Let’s crack on.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
6 
Intrinsic Value 
I do hate this phrase because it has many concepts in many different areas. I would make the effort of unifying 
it all under one umbrella and describe the general notion, but I think that would be too much of a faff. I’ll try 
describing it after I’ve given its practical definition in the context of options but for now let’s focus just on 
options for a second.  
In the context of call options 
We’ll first wrap our heads around call options, and then move towards put options later.  
 
Let’s try another simple example.  
Sony trades at $90 at the NYSE. I like Sony, so I’d like a call option that expires in 3 months time with a strike 
price of $95 (Notice here I haven’t included the call’s premium). 3 months later, the stock price reaches $100. 
I get to pay $95 for a stock that’s worth $100. Fabulous.  
 
So, hypothetically speaking, if everything in the example scenario works out, I get $5 free profit! Wow. Why 
does this matter? I mean, we’ve been discussing similar examples a few times now.  
 
The value of this call option (to me) is, at most, $5. I would be prepared to pay up to $5 as a premium to buy 
this option contract so I can make some profit from immediately selling the underlying Sony stock. Notice here 
I say ‘up to’. If the options contract is priced at $5, then I pay a $5 premium to buy a $95 share, totalling $100 
per share – that’s the same as buying the underlying Sony share itself. If the premium was $4, I would be 
paying $99 in total per share, which I could then sell for $100 each to make $1 profit each. 
 
In options trading, the difference between the underlying asset price and the strike price is called the intrinsic 
value. Here, the intrinsic value of the call option was $5, or in other words as I said… I am prepared to pay 
anything up to $5 to buy this call option and still make a profit. Pay attention here – the trader also knows that 
the intrinsic value i.e. the benefit of this options contract is, at most, $5. If there was no premium i.e. the call 
option had no cost, I get full access to the $5 benefit that it offers. A trader being a money-making person 
would want to maximise their own gains whilst minimising my profits, so they’ll try to set the premium to as 
close as $5 as possible whilst still enticing me to buy the option contract they’re offering.  
 
Mathematically speaking, we denote the intrinsic value as: 
 
IV = S – K 
 
where IV is the Intrinsic Value, S is the price of the underlying asset and K is the strike price (notice where 
you’ve seen this before? That’s right, futures and forwards!) 
 
However, let’s go one step further. Remember with the example of Nissan when the share price fell to ¥450? I 
chose not to exercise the option since the strike price of the call option was far higher than the share price. 
One way to think about this scenario is to consider that the call option had no value – of course, I’m not going 
to exercise it am I? (assuming I don’t go on to sell the contract to someone else, but that’s a story for another 
time). So, what we say if the strike price is higher than the underlying price (i.e. S < K) is that the intrinsic value 
of the option is 0. 
 
Mathematically, we now know that the intrinsic value of a call option is either 0 or the value of S – K, provided 
that S > K (i.e. the inequality S ≤ K is not true). We want to be able to select either of these two values when

functions.

In-the-money, out-of-the-money and at-the-money 
Covering the concept of intrinsic value was important for defining in-the-money and out-of-the-money 
options. If the intrinsic value for the call option is positive, we say that it is in-the-money i.e. the owner of the 
option will make a profit if he chooses to exercise his SONY $95 call option in 3 months if the price of the UL is 
at $100. On the other hand, if the price of the Sony stock is at $90, the call option described in the example 
above would be out-of-the-money as the intrinsic value of the option is 0 i.e. S – K is negative. Interestingly, if 
the price of the underlying stock was the same as the strike price, S – K would be 0 and we would say the 
option is at-the-money. All of these terms we covered so far are given one specific name… ‘moneyness’. Yes, 
it’s an odd name but it’s how they’re defined in the world of finance.  
 
Notice here that we haven’t discussed the option premium. You have to take the premium you pay for the right 
to an options contract into account, so even if you’re at-the-money or in-the-money, you’d have to still account 
for the option premium before calculating your overall profit so do not mix up the ideas of in-the-money and 
profit! 
In the context of put options 
Everything you read about call options can be flipped towards put options. They’re always complements of one 
another, which is why learning call options makes learning put options very easy.

1st of January 2023, Glencore hit rock bottom at £3.00. That’s a shame, but I can sell each of my 100 shares 
at £4.00.  
 
Now, we can speed up our analysis. Notice here that the strike price K is greater than our underlying asset 
price S (K, S = 4.00, 3.00). In this case, our formula for intrinsic value is reversed: 
 
 
 
So, our put option here has an intrinsic value of £1.00. Writing it as a piecewise function is also simple, noting 
the reverse changes mean that if the price of GLEN goes higher than our value of K at £4.00, our option is 
worthless and we choose not to exercise it since we could just go on the exchange and sell our shares directly 
for the higher price.

Combined options position strategies 
Payoff diagrams 
Now things are getting sophisticated! One thing I forgot to mention in the diagrams above is that they’re also 
called payoff diagrams. I chose not to mention it at the time since I don’t think it captures the true meaning of 
the graph – the definitions are definitely synonymous (i.e. the same) but the concept of intrinsic value is a 
better approach to understand the piecewise functions and the associated maths. In essence, from the context 
of payoff, the graphs show how much money you’ll get from the options at certain values of the underlying 
asset price S.  
 
Another reason that I didn’t mention ‘payoff’ is that thanks to the wonderful world of mathematics, economics 
and anything numbers-related, if you google ‘payoff diagrams’ you will get results that are inconsistent and in 
some cases, completely wrong. How could that be? I don’t know, but I certainly feel it’s better to be strict on 
the terminology on a case-by-case basis.  
 
Let’s briefly sit in the perspective of payoff when we look at these graphs. If we go back to our put option, we’re 
saying that if the underlying stock is valued at £2, our option ‘pays us off’ (ha, see what I did there) £2 – we 
notice that by reading the y-axis or observing the linear 1:1 relationship of the line. However, do we actually

buy the option to sell our shares at £4 compared to everyone else who has to sell at the market price of £2. 
So, how does our so-called ‘payoff diagram’ actually look when we consider the cost of an option and turn our 
y-axis into Profit & Loss?

The same effect has been achieved. Notice that due to the vertical translation of the graph downwards, our 
strike price has not moved – it’s still in the same place on the x-axis as before. Where our options strategy falls 
below the y-axis, we would be seeing a loss per share sold if we sold it at that value S. There’s a lot that you

Short Straddle 
We’ve discussed put and call options separately so far, but they don’t have to work in isolation separately. In 
fact, they are often combined to make new strategies with unique payoff diagrams. The first option strategy 
we’ll be discussing is the idea of a short straddle.  
 
Picture yourself as a trader in a bulge bracket firm. You think prices for a commodity such as oil won’t 
change over the course of the next 3 months. You want to take advantage of this assessment using 
options to earn a nice premium knowing that the buyers that you’re selling this contract to won’t be 
exercising it to purchase the underlying 100 or so barrels of oil.  
 
What you can do here is sell a 3 month call option AND a 3 month put option at the exact same time, hoping 
that the price of oil will stay the exact same. 1 barrel of oil (bbl) trades at $100 but the call and put options you 
trade cost a premium of $0.75, with the underlying size of 100 bbl and strike price of $100 for both options 
contracts (remember, you believe the price of a 1 bbl will not change). You collect $1.50 ($0.75 × 2) in 
premiums per bbl, so in total you make $150 in premiums (the size of the UL is 100 bbl for both contracts). If 
the price of a barrel of oil is unchanged, congratulations! You made $150 in premiums and neither of the two 
counterparties exercised the option.  
 
However, IF there is a freak accident and the price of a barrel of oil changes significantly, ONE of the 
counterparties will exercise their option and you will be out of pocket having to buy the 100 barrels and sell it to 
them at a discount compared to the going market rate. If the price of oil drops to $90/bbl, the owner of the put 
exercises their contract and you’re forced to buy their 100 barrels of oil at your strike price of $100. If the price 
of oil rises to e.g. $120/bbl, you have to purchase $12,000 worth of oil and sell it at a discount of $100 each to 
the owner of the call option. Bad times indeed.  
 
Still, using the same techniques as we mentioned when we analysed put and call options separately, we can 
analyse the payoff and the P&L diagrams to see what would happen if the price of the underlying asset S 
changes. One thing you will realise here is we have to take the view of a trader. Both of the previous call and 
put options are drawn from the perspective of a buyer e.g. a retail investor. A trader however isn’t going to 
profit from selling a call option with a strike price below the market price. Instead, the trader will be making a 
loss as the buyer of the option will exercise the contract and the trader will be out of pocket to buy the UL if it’s 
a call option, and vice versa. It’s not too hard to think about the payoff diagram of a call option from the 
perspective of a trader: if the strike price is above the market price (S < K), the trader loses no money as the 
counterparty will not exercise the contract. However, if the strike price is below the market price (S > K), the 
trader will start to lose money. I’m going to keep the notation of ‘IV’ here although it doesn’t really serve a 
purpose other than consistency with the other diagrams we discussed.  
 
 
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate

axis i.e. a reflection. Try comparing the two piecewise functions for a trader and a buyer’s call option IV and 
you might see why.  
 
Let’s continue this idea for the trader’s IV for a put option. If the strike price K is above the underlying price S, 
the trader would be made to buy 100 barrels of the underlying oil as the buyer of the option exercises the 
contract. Else, if the strike price K is below the underlying price S, the option isn’t exercised by the buyer so 
the trader doesn’t lose any money.

What if we add both graphs together? 
Let’s try that.  
 
 
Well well well, we have a triangle of sorts. This here is what we actually call a straddle. Honestly, I have no 
idea where the name comes from although one definition of it is when you sit on a chair and put one of your 
legs on either side of the chair feet. Here, it sort of looks like the same idea – on both sides of the strike price 
K, you have a leg that tails off perpendicular to the other leg.  
 
We haven’t considered the cost of the contract. Of course, the trader sells this contract to 2 different 
counterparties, so we add two units of the cost (which is the total premium to the trader) to get the profit.  
 
 
 
If that doesn’t make sense to you, you can also prove the total premium to be 1.50 through some simple 
algebra: 
 
PLcall = IVcall + 0.75 
PLput = IVput + 0.75 
PLcall+put = PLcall + PLput = IVcall + IVput + 0.75 + 0.75 = IVcall + IVput + 1.50

A lot of work for one simple graph. You can also see here the sensitive nature of the short straddle. It only 
takes for the price of oil to change by $1.50 either way for the trader to start making a loss. Some of you might 
be wondering “but wait, if the price of the oil is at $101, that’s higher than the strike of $100 so how can the 
trader not be making a loss? The holder of the call option would be exercising the contract, no?” You would be 
right, as the holder would be netting a profit of $0.25 per barrel if they choose to exercise their option. 
However, as the trader owns two units of premium (due to two option contracts being traded), the trader still 
wins by earning $0.50 (red dot) in profit per barrel of oil compared to the call holder’s $0.25 (blue dot).  
 
 
 
Later on, you’ll be reading about a very famous case study where a short straddle took down one of Britain’s 
oldest banks, highlighting the dangers of combining options if you don’t know what you’re doing! 
 
We’ll cover one more options strategy before moving on to other types of derivatives.  
A long straddle 
Again, a long straddle is the opposite of a long straddle. I think it’s clear that there’s a pattern now emerging 
about how long and shorts, calls and puts etc. seem to reflect one another. If some of you are thinking “hmm, 
what if I flip the short straddle on the x-axis? Does that give me the long straddle P&L shape?”, you’d be right 
as well! However, we don’t want this lecture to be a simple memory exercise so we’re going to go through the 
identical analysis (albeit a bit faster) to reach the long straddle shape.  
 
A long straddle involves the simultaneous purchase of a call and a put – that’s basically a long call and a long 
put combined together. We don’t actually need to reinvent the wheel like we did when we analysed a short call

and the Dutch choosing to make cheese instead of milk over the course of 2023 (purely hypothetical, of

swiss might repopulate their grassland with cows and increase the supply of milk into the european dairy 
market. Whatever happens, you want to profit off the large swings in prices compared to the idea of predicting 
no change in price with a short straddle. You set a strike price of €4 and pay for a call and put option, both at 
€0.30.

Wonderful. The long straddle. You can see here as well the potential profits if the price of the underlying milk S 
goes either side of the $4 to make the trader very rich. What we haven’t discussed yet is the potential losses 
from a short or a long straddle. Let’s look at the simpler case first: the long straddle. If you notice, we only go 
below y = 0 at the x-values of €3.40 and €4.60. So, if the price of milk ends up in between either of those 
values i.e. 3.40 < S < 4.60, the trader ends up losing money on the strategy. Even then, the most that the 
trader can lose is the combined premium that he paid for both options contracts. In this case, the trader’s 
losses are theoretically limited, but their gains are unlimited. For all other values of S (even negative ones, as 
you will discover in lecture 8), the trader makes huge sums of profit per unit of the underlying stock.  
 
However, a short straddle is far riskier. Notice both sides of the short straddle go downwards, down into the 
negative values of the y-axis. This isn’t by coincidence: the most the trader can gain from setting up a short 
straddle is the combined premium cost of the two options they sold. The losses, however, can be unlimited. In 
fact, so unlimited that it can take down an entire institution, just like Nick Leeson found out in 1995.  
 
A long strangle 
A slightly more general version of a straddle is called a strangle. We call this a more general version as the 
strike prices of the two legs of my options don’t have to be the same.  
 
Like the long straddle, the buyer enters both a long call and a long put on the same underlying with the same 
expiry. However, the strike of the long call option must be greater than the strike of the long put option.  
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
17 
You would enter a long strangle for similar reasons you would enter a long straddle. However, because the 
strike prices are further away from one another, you find that in reality the premium cost of this strategy is 
cheaper than that of a straddle. This is because we require greater price movements in the underlying in order 
to achieve a profit.

A short strangle 
A short strangle is the is just a long strangle, but we enter short positions instead (as the name suggests). 
 
Therefore, we short a call option and put option, but the strike of the call option still remains above that of the 
put option. Similar to a short straddle, we enter this sort of trade as a sort of volatility trade, essentially betting 
that the price of the underlying asset is going to remain stable around some price range and we would like to 
make a profit there. 
 
Of course, as the range of values for which you have positive P&L is wider in a short strangle, you typically 
receive a lower premium per option contract as compared to a short straddle. We can see what an example
