Case study: Nick Leeson 
Before you continue to read this, please note this is NON-EXAMINABLE. We won’t be asking a single 
question about this story – this is only for your interests to read about one of the oldest banks in English history 
to go into liquidation thanks to the trades of one young derivatives trader.  
 
S 
P&L 
S 
P&L 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
18 
Nick Leeson was born in February 1967 in Watford, England. He was described as “an average student” at 
school who found himself working in the futures and options division at Morgan Stanley in 1987, helping the 
frontline traders at the bank clear successful derivatives transactions.  
 
After being rejected for a promotion as a stock trader at Morgan Stanley, he joined Barings bank two years 
later in 1989, which was one of England’s oldest banks – founded in 1762. Barings was a boutique bank, 
catering to High Net Worth Individuals (HNWIs) and high value institutions. Being an old bank, it was used to 
old-style paper-based settlement of transactions in contrast to the modern technological advances in the 
trading floor and back offices of Morgan Stanley which Leeson was accustomed to. Leeson was responsible 
for back office accounting where he found the old practices of Barings flawed and easy to exploit.  
 
Impressing his bosses with his confidence and ability to ‘get the job done’, he was assigned a desk in the 
Hong Kong office to track down debts of close to £100 million (actually, £200 million when you adjust for 
inflation). Spurred on by his success to track down the lost debts, Barings offered him a job as a trader in the 
Singapore’s office to be a part of Baring’s futures division, trading on the Japanese Nikkei 225 (basically, the 
FTSE 500 for Japan).  
 
Strangely, Barings bank allowed Leeson to be the head of the bank’s Singapore subsidiary whilst also having 
the ability to access the bank’s accounts to his own liking. Leeson’s downfall all started when one of his traders 
mistakenly bought a put option instead of a call option for a client, losing the client £20,000 and putting the 
bank £20,000 out of pocket in an error account (which Leeson had conveniently numbered #88888 in homage 
to the lucky number in the eastern markets.) At first, he racked up tens of millions in losses through margin 
calls to secure his futures trades alongside generating a list of fake clientele to convince auditors his trades 
were legitimate. Remarkably, he was able to cancel out his losses by July 1993, which only made him try to 
test his luck more with futures trading.  
 
Believing he was a lucky trader out of his own merit, he then racked up losses of £94 million hidden in his 
secret error account #88888, growing up to £160 million later on through further futures trading. After securing 
75% of the bank’s capital at around £350 million to help pay off outstanding fees he owed on his futures, he 
thought he could again cancel out the debt by betting on the Japanese political and economic landscape to 
remain stable and unchanged over the course of the coming years. So, he organised a short straddle on the 
Japanese stock market where he believed that prices wouldn’t move and he could use the premiums paid to 
wind out the debts he’d build up. Unfortunately for Leeson, Japan was hit by a 6.9 magnitude earthquake the 
very next day, causing the Nikkei exchange prices to spiral downwards and Leeson’s short puts were landing 
him in bigger debts that he had to find money for. The short straddle strategy was risky – so risky in fact that it 
had only ballooned his losses to £850 million – that’s twice the amount of money that Barings bank had in 
capital. Leeson did try to long futures in an attempt to stem the spiralling debt, believing that the Nikkei 
exchange would recover from the effects of the earthquake – again, to no avail.  
 
He then fled Singapore the same night after discovering his office had found discrepancies in the accounting 
books, owing to the secret #88888 that he hadn’t declared to auditors now containing an £850 million debt. 
Barings found itself in immediate liquidation after the Sultan of Brunei pulled out of a deal to rescue the bank 
alongside a refusal from the Bank of England to bail the bank out of its misfortunes. Leeson was later arrested 
in Germany and served nearly 5 years in both Germany and Singapore before being released on good 
behaviour and a cancer scare. Barings, however, was sold to the Dutch investment bank IAG for £1 before 
being asset-stripped with its various functions sold off in an attempt to cover the losses.  
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
19 
Leeson was then (funnily enough) still allowed to be involved in financial markets, although he later had a film 
made about him starring Ewan McGregor in 1999. He also became CEO of an Irish football club and even 
featured on Celebrity Big Brother in 2018, finishing in 4th place whilst bearing witness to the infamous Roxanne 
Pallett incident that year. During his time in prison, Leeson claimed he’d never heard of Barings bank before 
applying and revealed that he maintained an intense dislike for his colleagues such that he was only working 
with them as a “means to an end” and refused to go to after-work drinks with them. Leeson now offers dinner 
talks and presentations to companies about risk management, alongside other media appearances.  
 
How mismanagement of funds does pay! 
A gentle introduction to the Black-Scholes model 
One thing we haven’t mentioned yet is how the price of an option is actually calculated. I’ve been quite 
arbitrary in choosing an options price that’s a bit less than the option’s intrinsic value, although that is a 
predictive measure at best and is never how options are actually calculated in financial markets. No trading 
floor of a major investment bank is going to be making up option premiums on the spot, are they? Especially if 
you consider the volume of trades that go through their respective desks every single hour, several hours a 
day, 5 days a week.  
 
So, you might ask “how are options prices calculated?” Well, here comes the most famous formula in all of 
financial mathematics… the Black-Scholes formula. It’s a Nobel Prize winning formula used to measure the 
market value of an option leading up to expiration. I’d like to say one or two things about this formula before 
we discuss it analytically. First of all, it’s a very advanced and complex formula to derive so we don’t expect 
anyone to go and read on how it’s derived – it’s a graduate mathematic topic, so unless you’re a mathematics 
graduate, you won’t have come across or be expected to write out its derivation. Second of all, it’s a formula. 
Yes, formulae dictate much of the world around you but the Black-Scholes formula doesn’t work all the time for 
reasons you’ll discover in a few moments. We are going to give you a basic background to the formula itself 
and its associated parameters, but don’t feel like it’s the holy grail of all mathematical finance. Even Warren 
Buffet in 1998 said that no matter how well you understand the equation, you still need a back-up grounding 
on options in the first place since things can go wrong.  
 
Before we discuss the Black-Scholes equation, there are two types of options you need to know – European 
Options and American Options. They’re actually not too dissimilar, apart from one major feature: European 
options cannot be exercised until their expiration date. If the strike price favours the holder of the option before 
the expiry date, the holder can do nothing to exercise the option to buy or sell the underlying and so they have 
to wait until the expiration date to do anything about it. However, an American option is more favourable to the 
holder – they can actually exercise the option before the expiry date.  
 
Think of this simple example: If a call option has a premium of £3 and a strike price of £50, the holder might 
want to exercise the option if the price of the underlying is £60 a month before expiration. They get a stock 
that’s undervalued by £10, that they can immediately sell on for up to £7 profit (noting the cost of the option i.e. 
the premium). If the option in question is a European call option, they can’t exercise that option until it expires 
after a month’s time, at which point the underlying price could fall to e.g. £52. However, with an American 
option, they can immediately exercise the option at any time they wish, leaving the holder with an option that 
they can sell on immediately for up to a £7 profit. Why is this relevant, you might ask? The Black-Scholes 
option works with the much simpler European-style call option, as you will see in a moment.  
 
Right, here is the equation for all of you that are desperate to find out: 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
20 
 
 
C is the price of the European Call option, S is the stock price (i.e. the price of the underlying), K is the strike 
price, r is the interest rate which we use here as a discount rate and t is the time to expiration. 
 
The formula uses two parameters, d1 and d2: 
 
 
 
 
which it passes through a cumulative distribution function N(x): 
 
 
 
which many of you might realise is the normal distribution function that you covered in your first years of A-
level statistics.  
 
Now, that’s a lot of terms and equations which I’m aware can look daunting. Let’s start with the main equation, 
C(s, t). Some of you might see that if we hide the N(d1) and N(d2) that we have: 
 
C = S – K 
 
Well, isn’t this something we’ve seen before? Yes. It’s the intrinsic value that we discussed above. All that the 
N(d1) and N(d2) terms do is multiply both terms by a number between 0 and 1 (because that’s what the normal 
distribution does) to weight the terms appropriately. That’s all it is. It’s our standard equation to calculate the 
cost of an option using the intrinsic value but weighted appropriately using a bit more maths to make the 
calculations more rigid! 
 
There are 5 things that we need to be able to calculate the cost of an options contract, which I’ve already 
mentioned in a few cases but I’ll repeat again: 
1. The stock price, S.  
2. The exercise price, K.  
a. We use both S and K to calculate the intrinsic value, S – K.  
3. The risk-free interest rate, r. We use this to discount the strike price to its present value today using 
another term, which I’ll mention next.  
4. The time to exercise the option, t. Yes, that’s what we use. Note that we use the exponential function, 
exp(–rt) to discount it. You may need to revise your properties of exponentials to see why the 
exponential would discount or ‘divide’ the value of K (hint: it’s –rt, not +rt).  
5. The standard deviation of the option’s previous returns, σ in log form. This is a new term that we 
haven’t discussed before, but essentially it looks at the previous prices of the stock and works out how 
much they deviate from the standard mean (hence, the standard deviation). If the price of a stock 
deviates a lot, the price of the option could end up being much higher than K and hence is more likely 
to be in-the-money at expiration. If it’s a stable-price stock, its standard deviation would be lower and 
would be less likely to end up jumping to a high price S at expiration. 
 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
21 
Now, I mentioned that if S is very high compared to K, the cost of the option C would reflect it by being higher. 
This is also reflected in the terms d1 and d2. Take a look at the logarithm bracket inside both terms. We see a 
term present inside. If we think about it, if this ratio of S0 to K is large because the price of the underlying S is 
so high compared to the fixed options price K, the values of d1 and d2 would be higher, and hence the normal 
distribution function would output a value close to 1, leaving the terms S and K in our original equation largely 
unchanged. Another way to think about it is that if S is so large compared to K for a call option, the holder of 
the option would be extremely likely to exercise that option at expiration and hence the cost of the option is 
priced to take advantage of that certainty since it’ll almost certainly happen. If the option was unlikely to be 
exercised (say, because the strike price and underlying price were so similar), the holder of the call option is 
unlikely to exercise it as their P&L graph would show a loss and hence the call option would have to be priced 
much lower in order to reflect its unlikelihood of being exercised and be competitive to be bought.  
 
Of course, time does play a part in the value of an option. The closer the time to expiration for an option, the 
more worthless the option is i.e. the cheaper it becomes. That might seem counterintuitive to you at first but 
think about it – if you know that a huge event would have to occur in the next 24 hours to jolt prices up 
significantly, you would largely know what the price of a stock is going to be tomorrow, right? If you leave a 
stock at market close at $50, at market open you’d be very surprised that it jumps straight up to $60 or $70 
dollars, right? So, there’s no point buying an option the night before it’s due to expire since the likelihood of a 
huge price change is rather small. Who knows, there might be a small chance that an earthquake might take 
place in Tokyo the next morning? This is reflected in the discounting term e-rt term, such that it implies that if t 
is small, e-rt turns out to be large, scaling up the overall size of the N(d2)Kexp(–rt) term to make C0 very small.  
 
The final term to consider is the standard deviation of the option returns, σ. We actually use the log returns to 
make the data behave better during the modelling process whilst also being easier to deal with when doing 
calculations. Like we mentioned, if the value of σ is large (implying large standard deviations in the option’s 
prices), we’d expect the cost of the option to be higher than if σ was small. You can actually see that behaviour 
a bit if we look at d1 and d2. Notice the existence of a  term in both terms. The fraction containing σ is 
significant to this behaviour of the option cost. In d1, the term exists as  but in d2, it exists as . Why does 
this matter? If σ is large, d1 gets bigger, returning a value close to 1 for the normal distribution function and 
hence preserving the value of S. However, for d2, the presence of the negative sign means that as σ gets 
bigger, d2 gets smaller and the normal distribution function returns a value further away from 1, diminishing the 
size of the –K term. This means that the calculated option cost is closer to S – K and is therefore more 
expensive, as we would expect.  
 
The Black-Scholes equation behaves as any maths equation does. We input our values for S, K, r, t and σ to 
get a value for C. However, what if we had the market price for C, and by doing a bit of googling, we had the 
values of S, K, r and t to hand. Could we reverse engineer the standard deviation of log returns for a stock call 
option that we’re looking at? Well, yes we can! We can rearrange the Black-Scholes equation and get a value 
for σ, which we call the implied volatility. The reason we call it ‘implied’ is that it’s the value generated by the 
Black-Scholes equation and it’s the value it would use to price that option and output the market price. Of 
course, it doesn’t give you any information about the nature of the future prices that we’re predicting – only the 
standard deviation of them, but traders do like to use it as a guideline for when they’re looking at derivatives 
such as European call options on the derivatives markets around the world.  
 
The Black-Scholes equation also gives us another set of quantities that we can use to assess the behaviour of 
options, called the greeks. We’ll cover them later on towards the end of the notes, but for now we’d like you to 
make sure that you understand the nature of the Black-Scholes equation and all the maths of options that 
have led us up to this point in the lecture. We also haven’t discussed the issues with this equation. First of all, 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
22 
the equation is based on a partial differential equation (PDE). For those of you who have never met PDEs, 
they’re basically a way of expressing how a variable x, y, z etc. are equal to a rate of change, which we might 
denote as dy/dx. In actual fact, PDEs are a lot more complicated than that so I’ve left the definition of a similar 
branch of rate of changes called ODEs in the previous sentence as a starting point for you to discover PDEs 
through some independent research, although it’s not the focus of this lecture at all. The ‘founders’ of the 
Black-Scholes equation, Fischer Black and Myron Scholes solved this equation to give the one you saw 
earlier: 
 
  
 
It comes from an idea in Physics known as Brownian motion, which basically is a branch of physics and maths 
that tries to predict the random motion of particles e.g. gas particles in a given volume. In maths, this is 
generalised to an idea known as a Wiener process, named after American mathematician Norbert Wiener in 
his research of time-dependent random events known as stochastic processes. There are a whole list of 
issues with the Black-Scholes equation that could take a whole day to list and explain in detail, but we’ll list 3 
of them here that we feel are the most important to remember: 
 
1. Remember that for r and σ, we had to plug in values to get the value of the call option, C? We assumed 
that over the course of the option’s lifetime, they won’t change. Well, if you’re in the real world, you 
might realise that they most definitely do change. The risk-free interest rate definitely changes, which 
you’ll discover is linked rather indirectly to the interest rate you get from a government bond. The value 
of σ is pre-calculated before the option is sold, but over time the price of the option will be changing 
and that will definitely change the value of σ as well. In essence, you have to assume two constants in r 
and σ which you know won’t be constant in the real world, and the Black-Scholes equation fails to 
account for that.  
2. Another one for σ: it’s calculated based off the previous prices of the call option it’s trying to generate a 
price for. One thing you have to bear in mind in finance is that past performance does not indicate 
future results. It might work most of the time in fairness, but would anyone have guessed in the run up 
to January 2021 that GameStop options were going to fly through the roof based on the price of the 
underlying and their premium costs? The Black-Scholes equation fails to account for that because one 
of its basic parameters, σ, uses past data as a guideline to predict the current price of the option in the 
run-up to expiry. If the option price wasn’t volatile but you could see demand swinging upwards pretty 
quickly soon and hence drive up the price, the Black-Scholes equation would be underpricing your 
option by a lot and leave you open to being arbitraged by your counterparties. 
3. The really obvious one – it’s a European call option. You can’t use the Black-Scholes model for an 
American call option or even a Bermuda call option at that. It’s pretty useless if the world’s most 
complex and most valuable equation can only help you price 1 out of 1000 stocks, although there’s 
definitely other models that try and price them effectively – just do remember that these ‘exotic’ options 
with lots more features such as dividends and the ability to exercise early will require much more 
intricate maths, which can definitely go badly wrong if the model isn’t close to perfection. 
 
Right, that’s now out of the way. Onto the greeks! 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
23 
The Greeks 
Origins from the Black-Scholes equation 
Most of you might remember your A-level studies when you covered the first derivative of a function f(x), which 
gives the gradient of the tangent at a point x for the function f(x). You then will have covered the second 
derivative, which shows how fast or slow that gradient changes, often given the term d2y/dx2 or f’’(x). Further 
derivatives simply describe the rate of change of the function above it, which seems fairly straightforward for 
dny/dxn and f(n)(x) respectively. Those of you who study mechanics are aware of position, the first derivative of 
speed being the velocity, the second derivative being acceleration followed by jerk, snap, crackle, pop and all 
the other relevant derivative jargon used to describe a motion of a particle.  
 
The Greeks are principally the analogue of the mechanics derivatives although they’re applied to the Black-
Scholes equation we studied earlier. First, I’m going to show you the notation and the corresponding quantity 
in a table. Then, I’ll briefly discuss each of the quantities in words to show what they measure and give you a 
few examples so you’re comfortable with them.  
 
Here are the Greeks, along with their derivative notation (otherwise known as Leibniz’s form).  
 
Name Derivative Symbol 
Delta 
  
Gamma 
  
Theta 
  
Vega 
 V 
Rho 
 ρ 
 
Some of you who are new to Imperial might be wondering “what’s that funny looking ∂ symbol?” It’s a good 
question, to be fair. That’s known as a partial derivative, and the symbol is called ‘del’. Sadly, I can’t explain it 
in this lecture because otherwise these notes would be 60 pages long but a few videos on the internet from 
Sal Khan at KhanAcademy would help you understand it better. Just remember that a lot of functions in the 
real world use several variables e.g. f(x, y, a, …) compared to what you know about a single variable f(x), so 
you would need a way of just getting the derivative for x while leaving everything else unchanged – that’s what 
partial derivatives do. They only differentiate with respect to one part of the variables involved.  
 
The Greeks are named after a subset of the Greek alphabet. To be honest, we’re not quite sure why. Some 
speculate that it was an idea by Mathematicians to sound noble and prestigious when they discussed finance-
related concepts back in the day. Another theory is that it’s a nod to the concrete birthplace of finance back in 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
24 
Ancient Greece. Whatever the reason is, we don’t know. However, we do expect you to be familiar with each 
of the Greeks and what they mean, as we’ll discuss now.  
A simple interpretation of the Greeks  
Let’s discuss each of the Greeks in a bit of detail.  
1. Delta, Δ. The point about delta is it tells you how much the price of the option changes if the price of the 
underlying changes by $1. If we’re talking about a European call option, a delta has a value between 
0.0 and 1.0. So, if we have a stock with an option delta of 0.4, that means that for every $1 increase in 
the price of the underlying, our option price increases by $0.40 PER SHARE! Since options contracts 
are standardised to 100 shares, the total premium would go up by $40 for every $1 increase in the 
price of the underlying. For a European put option, delta would be between 0.0 and –1.0. If the 
underlying price falls by $1, the option premium decreases by $40. If our stock moves from $10 to $11, 
our option price might increase from $1 to $1.40, for instance.  
 
One way to think about deltas is the price sensitivity of the option to the movements in price of the 
underlying. A higher Δ means that the price of the option is affected more than if Δ is a lot smaller. For 
call options, if the option is deep out-of-the-money (OTM), Δ is typically quite low but if the option is in-
the-money (ITM), Δ is typically quite high. As a result, people use Δ as a probability gauge to guess if 
the option is going to land ITM or OTM at expiration. A low Δ ~ 0.3 means it has around a 30% of 
ending up ITM, but for a Δ ~ 0.9, it has a 90% of ending ITM. However, as the price of the underlying 
moves, it affects whether the option will finish in-the-money or out-of-the-money. As a result, Δ will 
change with time, depending on the value of S. To measure the rate of change of Δ, we use Gamma, Γ 
which is what we discuss next.  
 
2. Gamma, Γ. In fact, I’ve already mentioned gamma just in the previous line. It’s basically Delta's best 
friend. I like to think about it in the same way people think about Kobe and Shaq – both needed each 
other to work well if you’re going to trade options. In principle, Γ measures how much Δ changes if the 
price of the underlying increases by $1. If we take our previous example, for our first $1 increase in the 
underlying price, our option price C ended up at $1.40 with a Δ of 0.40 and a Γ of 0.10. If the underlying 
increases again from $11 to $12, Δ first increases by 0.10 to 0.50 (i.e. 0.40 + 0.10), and then the option 
price C increases by $0.50 to $1.90 (1.40 + 0.50).  
 
Now, how does theta, θ come into play? 
 
3. Theta, θ is actually considered to be an option’s biggest enemy. It’s a bit like the effect of Shaq 
deciding not to show up to training and the LA Lakers as a result losing their play-offs against the 
Clippers. Essentially, θ is what we consider as the option’s time decay. Remember, options don’t have 
a fixed value for their price. Options do end up expiring at the expiry date and the closer we get to the 
expiry date, the more useless the option becomes and hence the lower the option price – we pretty 
much are near-certain what the price of the underlying is a day before the option expires. Theta 
basically is a negative decimal that tells you how much the option price decreases every day the owner 
holds onto it. If we have a θ of 0.2, it means that for every share of the option, the contract loses you 
$0.20 a day. For 100 shares, you’re losing $20 every day by holding onto the option. This isn’t good if 
you a buyer of a contract as you might want to sell it on to someone else, but great if you’re selling the 
contract as you know the option is becoming more worthless per day until the value of the option 
eventually hits 0 and no one is going to exercise it close to expiration provided it’s out-of-the-money. 
One way to think about θ is to think of it like rent when living at home. You have to ‘pay’ θ to get access 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
25 
to the benefits of Γ and Δ.  
 
Okay, what’s next. Ah, yes. Vega.  
 
4. Vega, V. Do you remember what σ was all about? If you don’t, σ was the standard deviation of the 
option’s price over a given time period e.g. over the last year. We then said that if we didn’t know what 
σ was for a given market price C, we could reverse engineer the Black-Scholes equation to give us a 
value for σ, which we called implied volatility. If you’re using a strategy such as a long straddle, you 
want to bet on an underlying such as a stock with high volatility since if the price swings large either 
way, you’ll profit from the call or put option you bought at the exact same strike price K. As a result, if 
implied volatility is high, the options price C is generally higher as a result.  
 
Now, how does implied volatility affect the price of our call in a way that we can measure? That’s what 
vega, V does. V measures how much the price of an option will change if the implied volatility changes 
by 1% either up or down. Consider our original example for an option with a premium of $1, an implied 
volatility of 8% and a value for V at 0.04. If implied volatility goes up to 9%, the premium goes up to 
$1.04. If implied volatility goes down to 6%, the option premium goes down to $0.92 (remembering 2% 
gives a decrease of $0.08, twice that for the 1% definition). Vega is also time dependent. Think about it 
– an option that’s got another 40 days to run compared to an option with only 10 days left is going to 
have more opportunity to experience larger swings in option price than one that’s close to expiration.  
 
Vega is useful as well if you are trying to work out what could increase or decrease the option’s price 
over a given time period. Things such as a natural disaster (like we saw in Japan earlier), a general 
election or a huge company announcement could instantly change the price of the underlying stock, 
which in turn affects the options price and the implied volatility hugely. As a result, V can shift up or 
down significantly to reflect this.  
 
Last one coming up… Rho, ρ.  
 
5. Rho, ρ. On reflection, Rho is pretty useless but for some case-specific circumstances. In short, it 
measures how much an option premium changes if the interest rate increases by 1%. However, 
interest rates rarely change unless there’s something drastic such as inflation on the horizon. Even 
then, ρ is little affected as the interest rate r is adjusted very rarely for it ever to be of any significance. 
EDIT: I wrote these notes in July 2022 and now it’s November the 12th, 2022… this didn’t age 
well!  
 
So, there we go. That’s all the 5 Greeks that come as a result of the Black-Scholes equation. Greeks help you 
hedge your options positions and be able to find options that are undervalued yet have the potential to return 
huge payoffs.  
 
Options and Greeks are related in the same way a football shoe and football technique are related. Learning 
how a football shoe works in terms of its shape and construction will help you understand where you have to 
kick a ball to get the most power into the back of the net whereas the technique you develop through practice 
in training and drills helps you adjust your game so that you can kick a ball from anywhere on the pitch in any 
given high-pressure situation.  
 
Now onto some more discussions about derivatives before we call it a day! 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
26 
Objectives of using derivatives 
The customisable aspect of derivative contracts allows them to be utilised for a wide range of trader objectives.  
The strategies used fit into three main types of trading; speculating, hedging and arbitrage. We will now put 
ourselves in the shoes of each of these types of traders to get a better understanding of how the different 
derivatives described are really used in industry.  
 
Hedgers use derivatives to reduce the risk faced from potential future movements in the market. For example, 
this may be done by purchasing forward contracts for a certain commodity that may be required by a company 
in a year's time. As we discussed, this has the effect of locking in a price for future payments that a company 
may not want to risk increasing. Similarly, a hedger may use options to provide insurance against risks. Unlike 
futures and forwards, options contracts offer a way for investors to protect themselves against adverse price 
movements in the future while still allowing them to benefit from favourable price movements.  
 
Speculators act with opposite motives to hedgers, instead of diminishing market risk, a speculator wishes to 
take a position in the market betting that the market will either go up or down. Futures and options both provide 
a way in which a type of leverage can be obtained to magnify a speculator's potential returns. When a 
speculator uses futures, the potential loss as well as the potential gain is very large. When options are used, 
the speculator’s loss is limited to the premium paid for the options. Some notable derivatives speculators 
include gamestop retail traders and the legendary Michael Burry (see The Big Short).  
 
We have seen how futures, forwards and options are priced assuming no arbitrage opportunities. This is 
usually true, but there is in fact a third type of trader with the capabilities to find market inconsistencies and 
profit from arbitrage opportunities occurring in derivatives markets. Arbitrage involves locking in a riskless profit 
by simultaneously entering into transactions in two or more markets. Generally there is very little room for 
small investors to utilise arbitrage opportunities as transaction costs wipe out any profit. Therefore, these 
traders are only really profitable for large investment banks who can access very low transaction fees. 
 
To wrap up, we will now look at one of the most famous cases of derivatives speculation gone wrong leading 
to a loss of over €4.9 bn all because of one trader at french bank Societe Generale.  
 
 
Case Study: SocGen’s rogue trader 
 
One of the risks faced by a company that trades derivatives is that an employee who may be 
responsible for finding arbitrage trades with very low risk may become a speculator in search 
of large returns to boost their bonus. Jerome Kerviel worked for Societe Generale and in 
2005 was promoted to become a junior trader in the bank’s Delta One products team. His job 
was to look for arbitrage opportunities across equity indices. For example if a futures contract 
on an equity index was trading for a different price on two different exchanges, or was 
mispriced compared to spot prices as we looked at earlier. An arbitrage may also arise if 
equity index futures prices were not consistent with the prices of the shares constituting the 
index.  
 
Kerviel used his knowledge of the bank’s procedures to pretend to be arbitraging while in 
reality he was placing huge bets on his own directional views. He took large positions in 
equity indices with accompanying fake trades to make it appear as if he was hedged. This 
position grew over time to over €20 billion.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
27 
 
In January of 2008 his large positions were uncovered by SocGen and were unwound over a 
3 day period. This was at the time the biggest loss created by fraudulent activity in the history 
of finance and cost the bank over €4.9 billion.  
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
28 
Non-examinable material 
The material BELOW is NOT going to be examined (hence the italics, bold and underlined red text 
illustrating the point), so you can relax and read it for your own interests. We thought this would be cool to 
include for anyone doing interviews or for someone who wanted to get a finer understanding on how 
derivatives are denoted in the real world. Have a read if you wish and hopefully appreciate the examples we’ve 
discussed with regards to derivatives in general.  
History of derivatives 
The most interesting aspect of history is that not one historian can ever truly be sure of the first mention of 
something like derivatives, although the earliest given use is attributed to the use of clay tokens in around 
8000 B.C. (around 10,000 years ago, apparently) by the people of Sumer (the earliest known civilisation) with 
Iraq now in its place – in fact, Iraq seems to be at the centre of much of ancient B.C. history. Back then, when 
humans first got to grips with the concept of ‘money’ and a new idea of thinking called ‘Mathematics’, 
merchants used clay tokens to give to buyers as a promise that they would deliver a specific quantity of goods 
at a certain date in the future. These clay tokens would then insert into an envelope, much like a children’s 
wooden slot puzzle and have the settlement date baked into the token for confirmation – these are basically 
believed to be the first forwards contract that existed in pre-Jesus times.   
 
After that, the standardisation of derivatives with specific rules came around the 1700s B.C. in Mesopotamia, 
where contracts were specifically written out to tie merchants and buyers together on the specific terms of a 
deal. Back then, the main things that were traded were agricultural goods such as grain harvests and livestock 
but the main notion was that anything that could be traded between merchants and buyers could have a 
derivatives contract derived from it to protect against counterparty risk. The first ever recorded derivatives 
contract actually appears to be written by Thales, a notorious flat-earther and Greek astronomist who in 500 
B.C. successfully negotiated the first ever put options contract for olive oil presses. Although the story has 
different variations, the most accepted version is that Thales purchased the lease to olive oil presses 
(basically, just machinery) at low prices and knowing the demand for these presses would skyrocket thanks to 
him predicting a successful harvest, he could then sub-lease the presses for even higher prices and pocket the 
difference to make him huge amounts of profit. Some sources claim that Thales predicted (and allegedly 
influenced) the olive oil prices to also increase in price, furthering his own success by raising the lease prices 
above normal levels although such sources are few and far between to confirm this narrative.  
 
There are some further developments in the area of derivatives after Jesus was born in 0 B.C. but they aren’t 
really of any interest, although one big use of derivatives was in the Dutch ‘tulip mania’ era of 1600s, a time 
when dutch tulip bulbs became so expensive as a symbol of wealth and stature that it is now used to reference 
any modern-day economic bubble that’s imminent to explode after its conception (a bit like NFTs in 
cryptocurrency, for example). In the 1600s, derivatives contracts to trade tulips were at an all-time high and 
were actually one of the few ways (allegedly) for anyone to even get their hands on a dutch tulip in the first 
place. Of course, this only lasted for three years from 1634 such that by 1637 by which point the demand and 
prices of the tulip crashed completely, although historians often cite the Dutch Stock Exchange in Amsterdam 
Market as the first ever modern financial stock exchange that behaves identically to those that we have today.  
 
Derivatives continued to exist until their most significant development in the early 70s and 1993. First came the 
Black-Scholes model, an equation derived from the random behaviour of molecular motion in Physics to 
calculate prices of derivative contracts. It was one of the biggest contributions that mathematics gave to 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
29 
economics and finance, earning Myron Scholes a Nobel Prize for Economics in 1997 as they could now write 
equations for the price of an options based off how much it changes in price over time with relatively 
successful accuracy, although the equation only really worked when certain conditions (or ‘constraints’) were 
applied. Also, since the formula made use of the normal distribution from statistics and probability, the formula 
was never accurate when compared to the real-world results – it’s a bit like modelling the mechanics of a car 
without air resistance… it works, but it’ll never actually behave like the real world that a car is actually driven in.  
 
1993 served a more interesting time in finance as it is often cited as the birth of quantitative finance, an area 
where advanced mathematics aims to try and find marginal gains in the interest of profit and efficiency. What 
sparked exponential growth in the area of derivatives was the cancellation of a $4 bn Superconducting Super 
Collider in Texas by US Congress under the Clinton administration in October 1993, citing spiralling costs (like 
most things in debate between academia and politics). As a result, the large number of theoretical and nuclear 
physicists put out of work were then reportedly assembled by Goldman Sachs to become quants, applying 
their knowledge of string theory and stochastic brownian motion to generate turnover for the bulge bracket in 
financial markets such as options trading. Nowadays, derivatives are complex with advanced mathematics 
dominating the subject in many fronts. Critics such as Nassim Taleb (a famous financial statistician) have 
argued for the omission of Physicists from financial markets due to their inability to develop accurate models in 
financial markets where models and theories are rarely replicated in reality, although the philosophical debate 
of quantitative finance is still up for debate to this day. 
Options notation at an exchange 
There is a convention to write out an option before searching it on an exchange or online. If we take the 
Microsoft example from earlier, we can write out the following features: 
 
● Ticker – MSFT 
● Year of expiry –  2023 
● Month of expiry – 07 (July) 
● Date of expiry – 06 
● Type of option – Put option 
● Strike Price – $230.00 
 
Notice the underlined characters in the bullet points above. We use this in the the notation used in options 
trading is to package all of these features above onto one line: 
 
MSFT230706P002300000 
 
Reading this in reverse yields us the same features: 
 
● The first 4 letters are the stock ticker – MSFT 
● The next 2 digits are the year of expiry – 23 
● The next 2 digits are the month of expiry – 07 
● The next 2 digits are the date of expiry – 06 
● The single letter denotes the type of option – a Put 
● The first 5 digits denote the price of the option to the left of the decimal point i.e. 00230 implies $230 
● The final 4 digits denote the price of the option to the right of the decimal point i.e. 0000 implies .0000 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
30 
Exotic options 
 
Barrier Options 
A barrier option behaves very similarly to what we call a ‘vanilla’ option, however with one extra caveat, there 
is a barrier level affecting the ability to exercise the option. What do I actually mean by this? Well, in order for 
the option to become ‘live’ then the price of the underlying asset of the option must break through some 
predetermined barrier. 
 
When I say the option becomes ‘live’ I mean it can be exercised. Suppose we are long a call option with strike 
of $100 on an ABC share and the spot rate was $95 when I entered the contract. However, in order for me to 
have the right to exercise the option, the price of ABC shares must break through $105 during the life of the 
contract. Therefore, even if the price of the underlying is $104 at expiry as long as the price didn’t break 
through $105, I cannot exercise the option. 
 
This seems weird, because the intrinsic value of my option is $104 - $100 = $4, but I cannot take advantage of 
this positive IV. This is where barrier options differ from vanilla ones and is why commonly they are cheaper 
than equivalent vanilla options, as an extra requirement needs to be met before the option can be exercised. 
 
In general, there are 4 types of barrier options, an up-and-in option is what we just went through, where the 
spot price starts below the barrier and the option initially starts dead. The spot must then go up for the option 
to be ‘knocked in’. 
 
A ‘down-and-out’ option is where the spot starts above the barrier level and the option starts ‘live’ i.e. can be 
exercised. However, if the spot price goes down and breaks through the barrier, the option is ‘knocked out’ and 
is ‘dead’. Therefore, because of this extra requirement to exercise barrier options, they are usually cheaper 
than their vanilla option counterparts. 
 
Swaptions 
A swaption, also known as swap option, refers to an option to enter into an interest rate swap (or some other 
type of swap for that matter). They are an OTC product similar to swaps themselves and can be highly 
customised to suit the needs of both counterparties. 
 
There are two distinct aspects to a swaption, the option contract to enter the swap, and the swap itself. 
 
The option givers he purchaser the right but not the obligation to enter into a swap contract where they pay 
fixed and receive floating, or the other way around. The best way to think about these products is just a swap 
contract, but the buyer has the added bonus of deciding whether or not to enter the swap based on 
movements in the reference floating rate during the life of the option. 
 
They are generally used to hedge options positions on bonds or to aid in restructuring current positions of a 
portfolio. In this case, your underlying asset will not be something like equity or a commodity, but instead some 
reference interest rate like SONIA or SOFR.
