Real Estate 
Notes by Lasen Wanni Arachchige 
Lecturer, Securities Education Certificate 2022/23. 
Head of Events and Marketing, Imperial College Investment Society (2022/23) 
Introduction 
The real estate market is worth $217 trillion – making it the world’s most important asset class (by value). In fact, 
global real estate is a more valuable asset class than all the stocks, shares and bonds combined. Real estate is 
something we all experience on a day-to-day basis and will continue to play a vital role throughout our lives. 
Whether you are renting property or own property yourself, it is important to understand the basics behind the 
real estate industry. This section of the securities education certificate will take a deep dive into multiple different 
types of real estate investment including topics such as purchase of physical property, mortgage -backed 
securities, and real estate investment trusts. 
Defining Real Estate 
We will begin by defining real estate. 
The basic definition is as follows: 
Real Estate – A property consisting of land or buildings. 
However, we can take this a step further to include any changes made to the land, so we can define real estate 
as: 
Real Estate - Land or any permanent improvements to the land whether natural or man-made.  
Some examples of what can be considered as permanent improvements to the land include water, trees, 
minerals, buildings or fences. We also need to differentiate between real property and personal property when 
defining real estate. Real property is property that is permanently attached to the land such as the things listed 
above. However, personal property is not included in the definition, hence things such as jewellery or vehicles 
are not considered as real estate. 
Types of Real Estate 
Having considered the definition of real estate we can break this down further into the different types of real 
estate. 
Residential 
Residential real estate is property used for habitation purposes. Examples of residential real estate include 
Family residences, townhouses, detached houses and apartments.  
Commercial 
Commercial real estate is any property that is used exclusively for business purposes. Properties such as office 
buildings, hotels, shopping centres and parking facilities all come under commercial real estate. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
6 
Industrial  
Any property used for manufacturing, production, distribution, storage, or research and development. Examples 
of industrial real estate would include factories, power plants, warehouses, and data centres.  
Vacant Land 
This can simply be defined as any undeveloped land that has no buildings on it and land that is not being used. 
This includes raw land, natural fields, and forests. 
Special Purpose 
Finally, special purpose land is any property used by the public. Government buildings, public libraries, 
cemeteries, and places of worship all come under special purpose real estate.  
Historical Prices of Real Estate 
Before we start our dive into the various types of real estate investments, I would like to highlight the previous 
performance of the industry by looking at the average UK house price since 1990.  With the major exception of 
the 2008 financial crisis, house prices tend to demonstrate continuous year on year growth and can be one of 
the safest assets to invest in. Despite the onset of COVID-19 causing house prices to take a small hit, the 
vaccine rollout and reduction in pandemic concerns cause prices to accelerate to reach an all-time high in 2022. 
Factors such as supply of housing, interest rates, and general market sentiment are the main contributors to this 
price rise. 
Figure 1: Average UK House Price from 1990 - 2022 
 
 
 
Purchase of Physical Property 
The first method of real estate investment we are going to cover in this part of the SEC is the purchase of physical 
properties. This involves the concept of home ownership, rental properties, and house flipping. The way that 
profit is generated from these methods involve revenue from rent/ leases or appreciation of property value or a 
combination of both. 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
7 
Homeownership 
Purchase of residential property for the owner to live in. 
 
Owning a home is something we all strive towards and is one of the most common forms of real estate 
investment. Despite having a place to live being the major reason for purchase of residential real estate, 
homeowners benefit from the appreciation of the assets value too. In addition to this, diversification of the 
homeowner’s portfolio and the fact property can be bought using leverage (mortgage) are also considered as 
advantages. Furthermore, the inflation hedging characteristics of real estate can help homeowners reduce the 
impact an overheating economy has on their personal finances. As we have seen over the past year, high 
inflation causes the cost of living to rise with the rental market seeing a considerable increase in rental prices.  
This extra cost is not something homeowners face thus reducing the impact high inflation has on them. However, 
purchase of residential real estate does not come without its disadvantages. Price increases year on year tend 
to be quite small with an average percentage increase of 10.1%. There is no guarantee that the property will 
remain at the price it was bought at since geographical properties can have a considerable impact on property 
prices. Many first-time buyers are realising the large initial capital required to purchase a property with the 
average down payment on a mortgage of 12%. This factor also makes property markets illiquid since large 
amounts of paperwork and fees can be associated with selling/ buying property.   
Rental Property 
Purchase of property with the interest of leasing it out to tenants. 
 
Rental property (also called buy to let property) involves the owner of the property becoming the landlord and 
generating income through the collection of rent. This rental income is considered as regular passive income 
which can diversify the owner’s inco me sources. Like homeownership, the landlord benefits from the 
appreciation of value and the fact that the property can be bought using leverage (mortgage).  Again, rental 
property can also have negatives associated with it. An obvious disadvantage is the fact a landlord is unable to 
generate income when the property is vacant. In addition to this, management of tenants can be tedious and 
any damage caused by tenants will be an extra cost incurred by the landlord.  
House Flipping 
Purchase of a property with the intention of holding it for a short period of time (3 – 4 months) and quickly selling 
it. 
 
This method of real estate investment is far less common than the two outlined above due to the increased 
knowledge required and risk associated with it. House flipping can be done in two ways; repair and update or 
hold and resell. Repair and update involves the landlord purchasing the property, paying for renovations or any 
repairs needed, then selling the property off at a higher price. The profit generated by this is equal to the 
difference in resale and purchase price minus the cost of any repairs and updates done. In essence the investor 
is hoping that the changes made to the property will add more in value than the cost required to carry out the 
works. Hold and resell involves an investor purchasing a property then simply holding the property for a certain 
time period with the hope that property prices increase. Once the price has increased, the investor will sell the 
property above the initial purchase price. Both these methods come with the benefit of tying up capital for a short 
period of time and offering quick returns. However, strong market knowledge is required since accurate 
valuations are vital to ensure this method is profitable. Furthermore, there is always the risk that the property 
market cools, and the property cannot be sold at a higher price than what it was purchased at.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
8 
Property Value 
Property Value – The estimated fair market value (FMV) of a real estate property.  
 
Property value of a real estate asset is the estimated price at which a property can be sold in the open markets. 
Like most goods, the market value of a property is determined by the market demand and supply available at 
the present date. Market demand for a property can be classified as the amount of interest from potential 
investors and buyers in the market to purchase real estate in a given market. Supply can be classified as the 
amount of real estate (e.g. properties, land) available for sale in the market. There are many factors which affect 
supply and demand for real estate, therefore property values constantly fluctuate based on the current balance 
between market demand and supply.  
 
Commercial Real Estate Appraisal Methods 
Ultimately as a buyer/ investor of real estate, you must appraise properties to understand if you are overpaying/ 
underpaying for a property. There are numerous methods for appraising a property, but we will cover the most 
common methods below.  
Sales comparison  
The sales comparison method (SCA) works on the same premise that comparable analysis when it comes to 
stocks and shares. By compiling data regarding recent prices paid to purchase comparable properties in similar 
locations, an benchmark for pricing can be formulated. Comparing factors such as size of the property, number 
of bedrooms and bathrooms, amenities, property condition etc can be considered and used to either increase/ 
decrease the estimated property value.  
Income Approach – Direct Capitalisation Method 
The income approach or capitalisation approach is one of the most commonly used appraisal methods in 
commercial real estate (CRE). Property values are estimated based on their implied income potential and a 
capitalisation rate (or “cap rate”). Initially, the net operating income (NOI) of a property is calculated and the 
property’s NOI is divided by the market cap rate to arrive at property value. There is a large overlap between 
this method and the sales comparison method as property value is heavily influenced by the capitalisation rate 
used.  
 
𝑃𝑟𝑜𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑡𝑦 𝑣𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒 = 𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝑂𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 (𝑁𝑂𝐼)
𝐶𝑎𝑝𝑖𝑡𝑎𝑙𝑖𝑠𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑒 (𝐶𝑎𝑝 𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑒) 
Where  
𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝑂𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 (𝑁𝑂𝐼)=𝐸𝑓𝑓𝑒𝑐𝑡𝑖𝑣𝑒 𝐺𝑟𝑜𝑠𝑠 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 (𝐸𝐺𝐼)−𝐷𝑖𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑡 𝑂𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝐸𝑥𝑝𝑒𝑛𝑠𝑒𝑠 
And: 
𝐸𝑓𝑓𝑒𝑐𝑡𝑖𝑣𝑒 𝐺𝑟𝑜𝑠𝑠 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 (𝐸𝐺𝐼)=𝑃𝑜𝑡𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑖𝑎𝑙 𝐺𝑟𝑜𝑠𝑠 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 (𝑃𝐺𝐼)−𝑉𝑎𝑐𝑎𝑛𝑐𝑦 𝑎𝑛𝑑 𝐶𝑟𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡 𝐿𝑜𝑠𝑠𝑒𝑠 
 
Net Operating Income (NOI) 
Net operating income (NOI) is a real estate metric that measures the profitability of income-generating 
properties.  
 
𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝑂𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 (𝑁𝑂𝐼)=(𝑅𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒+𝐴𝑛𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑙𝑎𝑟𝑦 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒)−𝐷𝑖𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑡 𝑂𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝐸𝑥𝑝𝑒𝑛𝑠𝑒𝑠 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
9 
The NOI formula aims to isolate the core operating profits of real estate assets to avoid misleading operating 
profits with non-operating items such as corporate overhead and non-cash items such as depreciation. By 
summing up rental income (income generated directly from letting out space) and ancillary income (income 
generated form the property that is not form rent (e.g. parking fees, laundry fees) then subtracting direct 
operating expenses (expenses related directly to the operation of the property), NOI can be calculated. Effective 
gross income is equal to the sum of rental and ancillary income as they both take into account any potential 
vacancy and credit losses associated with the property. Net operating income is particularly  important if a 
company owns multiple properties as it isolates property level profitability as opposed to company profitability.  
 
Net operating margin can also be calculated using the following formula: 
 
𝑁𝑂𝐼 𝑀𝑎𝑟𝑔𝑖𝑛 (%)= 𝑁𝑂𝐼
𝑇𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝑃𝑟𝑜𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑡𝑦 𝑅𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑢𝑒 
NOI margin represents the proportion of revenue maintained as income – i.e., how much of each dollar earned 
in revenue is kept as NOI. In general, it can be said that a higher NOI margin can be attributed to a more 
efficiently run property but the average NOI margin changes based on the sector and property type. 
Capitalisation Rate % (Cap Rate) 
The cap rate is an estimate of the return on a real estate rental property investment based on the perceived 
income and risk profile. It represents the ratio between the net operating income (NOI) of a rental property and 
its fair market value (FMV) as of the present date.  
 
𝐶𝑎𝑝𝑖𝑡𝑎𝑙𝑖𝑠𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 𝑅𝑎𝑡𝑒 (%)= 𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝑂𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 (𝑁𝑂𝐼)
𝑃𝑟𝑜𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑡𝑦 𝑉𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒  
Generally, the cap rate is derived using a sales comparison analysis and then the market cap rate is applied to 
the NOI of a property to determine the FMV of said property. This will give an idea of a fair price that should be 
paid for the property given the market conditions. Higher cap rates usually imply higher risk since the market 
requires a higher rate of return in order to invest in that particular market. Likewise lower cap rates generally 
mean a lower risk investment. Generally speaking, a good  cap rate ranges between 4% and 10% in the 
commercial real estate market however it is dependent on a range of factors such as property type, location 
and current market conditions.   
Income Approach – Gross Rent Multiplier (GRM) Method 
The other income approach is the gross rent multiplier (GRM) method and uses the following formula: 
 
𝑃𝑟𝑜𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑡𝑦 𝑉𝑎𝑙𝑢𝑒=𝐴𝑛𝑛𝑢𝑎𝑙 𝐺𝑟𝑜𝑠𝑠 𝑅𝑒𝑛𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 𝑋 𝐺𝑟𝑜𝑠𝑠 𝑅𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝑀𝑢𝑙𝑡𝑖𝑝𝑙𝑖𝑒𝑟 (𝐺𝑅𝑀) 
 
Unlike the capitalisation approach, the GRM method uses the property’s annualised gross rental income, 
rather than the net operating income (NOI). This means that operating expenses incurred b the property are 
neglected. The GRM of the particular property segment is calculated using comparable analysis but this 
method of property appraisal is used mostly a77777s a back of the envelope calculation to get a quick idea of 
the property value.  
Cost Approach – Replacement Cost Method 
Finally, the cost approach method, approximates the value of a property by estimating the total cost incurred to 
replace or rebuild the assets from scratch. The included costs are items such as land costs, labour expenses, 
and material costs. In comparison to the income approach, the cost approach is less common, as the method 
is usually only used of recent sales data is limited, or if the property is very unique/ has been non-operational.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
10 
 
 
Calculating Return on Investment  
Return on investment (ROI) – the money earned on an investment after all expenses are deducted. 
 
ROI is a key figure used by real estate investors to determine whether a particular investment is worth the costs 
associated and is a metric used to compare one strategy to another.  There are two main ROI calculation 
methods: cost method and out-of-pocket method. In this course we are just going to cover the cost method. This 
method will then be applied to both homeownership and rental properties. For rental properties, return on 
investment tends to be calculated on an annual basis. 
Cost Method 
ROI using the cost method is found by dividing the investment gain on a property by the property’s initial cost. It 
also makes the key assumption that the property is purchased outright with cash and without financing. The 
equation below is used to calculate ROI using the cost method: 
𝐶𝑜𝑠𝑡 𝑀𝑒𝑡ℎ𝑜𝑑 𝑅𝑂𝐼 (%) = 𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝐼𝑛𝑣𝑒𝑠𝑡𝑚𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝐺𝑎𝑖𝑛
𝐼𝑛𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑎𝑙 𝐶𝑜𝑠𝑡  × 100 
Cost Method for Homeownership/House Flipping Example 
Take a property with initial value £100,000 to which the homeowner adds repairs and improvements costing 
£50,000. This increases the market value of the house to £200,000 and we are going to calculate the return on 
investment. 
First step is to calculate the net gain from the investment: 
 
𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝐼𝑛𝑣𝑒𝑠𝑡𝑚𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝐺𝑎𝑖𝑛 = £200,000 − (£100,000 + £50,000) 
                                                                                          = £150,000 
 
Next, we need to calculate the initial cost: 
 
𝐼𝑛𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑎𝑙 𝐶𝑜𝑠𝑡 = £100,000 + £50,000 
                                                                                                 = £150,000 
 
Finally, ROI can be worked out buy substituting net investment gain and initial cost into the ROI equation: 
 
𝐶𝑜𝑠𝑡 𝑀𝑒𝑡ℎ𝑜𝑑 𝑅𝑂𝐼 (%) = £50,000
£150,000 × 100 
                                                                                                                 = 33.3 % (𝑡𝑜 3 𝑠𝑓) 
Cost Method for Rental Property Example 
Take a property with initial value £100,000 to which the homeowner adds repairs and improvements costing 
£10,000. Monthly expenses (bills/maintenance) costs £250 and the rent charged by the landlord is £1200 per 
month.  
 
Calculating annual net gain from the investment: 
𝐴𝑛𝑛𝑢𝑎𝑙 𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝐼𝑛𝑣𝑒𝑠𝑡𝑚𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝐺𝑎𝑖𝑛  = (12× £1,200) − (12× £250) 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
11 
                                                                                                        = £11,400 
Next, we need to calculate the initial cost (same as for homeownership/ house flipping): 
𝐼𝑛𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑎𝑙 𝐶𝑜𝑠𝑡 = £100,000 + £10,000 
                                                                                                 =  £110,000 
Finally, ROI can be worked out by substituting net investment gain and initial cost into the ROI equation: 
 
𝐶𝑜𝑠𝑡 𝑀𝑒𝑡ℎ𝑜𝑑 𝐴𝑛𝑛𝑢𝑎𝑙 𝑅𝑂𝐼 (%) = £11,400
£110,000×100 
                                                                                                                           = 10.4 % (𝑡𝑜 3𝑠𝑓) 
 
Mortgage-Backed Securities 
Having discussed one method of real estate investment, the purchase of physical properties, we can move to 
another method called mortgage-backed securities (MBS).  
Before Mortgage-Backed Securities 
 
Figure 2: Transactions before MBS 
 
To understand how mortgage-backed securities work we will need to take a step back and investigate the 
transactions involved between parties when purchasing a property using leverage. If we completely ignore the 
idea of an MBS, three main parties are involved: the seller, the buyer and the bank. Firstly, the buyer and seller 
will agree on a price for which the property can be purchased, and the buyer will go to the bank and apply for a 
mortgage. Once the buyer’s credentials have been approved, a legally binding contract will be signed, and the 
transaction process begins. The seller receives the payment from the bank and hands over the keys to the 
house. This is where the seller leaves the picture and the transactions occur purely between the buyer and the 
bank. Each month (or whenever is agreed on the contract) the buyer will make a payment of the principal, 
amount that you borrow from the lender, and interest, the cost of borrowing money. For the duration of the 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
12 
mortgage (typically 20 – 30 years), the mortgage is kept on the bank’s books and the bank continues to receive 
the principal and interest each month. 
 
The issue with this way of managing mortgages is that it ties up the bank’s resources and capital for the duration 
of the loan. This results in an opportunity cost, the potential benefits lost out when choosing an alternative over 
another, due to the lack of funds available to the bank to use in other transactions (e.g. other mortgages). 
After Mortgage-Backed Securities 
To avoid the problem outlined above, the concept of a mortgage-backed (MBS) security was established with 
the first MBS issued in 1968. A mortgage-backed security is a type of bond representing an investment in a pool 
of real estate loans.  
 
Figure 3: Transactions with MBS 
 
With mortgage-backed securities, the initial part of the transaction remains the same. The seller receives the 
payment for the house and the buyer continues to pay principal and interest each month. The difference is what 
the bank decides to do with the mortgage. This time instead of keeping the mortgage on the bank's books it 
decides to bundle lots of mortgages, usually mortgages with the same investment rating, and sells this to 
investment banks in the form of a bond. The investor receives the principal and interest paid to the bank by the 
seller. By doing so, the investor who buys a mortgage-backed security is essentially lending money to home 
buyers. The MBS turns the bank into an intermediary between the homebuyer and the investment industry. This 
process frees up the bank’s capital (possibly allows the bank to issue more mortgages) and allows the bank to 
make money from originating and servicing the mortgage as well as any other associated fees. In addition to 
this, the investors make money from the interest, principal and changes in the value of the MBS. This process 
works well for all concerned as long as all parties involved do what they are supposed to do. This means that 
the bank keeps to reasonable standards when granting mortgages, homeowners continue to make payments 
on time and credit rating agencies that review MBS perform due diligence. 
Financial Crisis 
Now that we understand the concept of MBS, we can take a look at the central part it played in the 2008 financial 
crisis. With house prices continuing to increase year on year and the growing demand for mortgages and MBS, 
banks began lowering their lending standards to cope with demand. By doing so, a large number of people were 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
13 
granted mortgages despite low credit ratings and high risk of default (subprime mortgages). Despite this, the 
banks knew that with house prices rising rapidly if a person were to default, they could recoup any losses by 
taking the house as collateral and selling it on the market. As subprime borrowers began to default on loans, the 
housing market was flooded with homes causing prices to decrease rapidly resulting in the housing collapse 
seen from 2006 to 2008. As a result of the housing collapse, people began walking away from their mortgages 
because their homes were worth less than their loans. The sheer amount of non-payments meant that many 
MBSs, and collateralized debt obligations based on pools of mortgages were very overvalued. Huge losses piled 
up as institutional investors and banks tried and failed to unload bad MBS investments. Credit in the economy 
tightened leaving many banks on the brink of insolvency. Lending as a whole was disrupted so badly that the 
entire economy was on the brink of collapse. 
Real Estate Investment Trusts 
Now that we have discussed two out of the three major methods of real estate investment, what's left to cover 
is the concept of real estate investment trusts or REITs for short. The real estate sector is known for having 
very high barriers to entry with the average house price at £299,249 and average commercial real estate cost 
at £2,190 / m2. With such high barriers to entry, it can be difficult to finance real estate investments meaning that 
most people will miss out on the benefits from investing in real estate. 
 
A real estate investment trust is a company that owns, operates or finances income producing real estate. The 
concept of a REIT was established by Congress in 1960 to make the benefits of income producing real estate 
accessible to anyone and everyone. Today, approximately 145 million Americans own REIT stocks either directly 
or indirectly through mutual funds, ETFs or target date funds. Investing in a well-diversified REIT is like owning 
a small part of all of the REIT’s assets such as office buildings and hotels. It allows easy entrance to the real 
estate market and can be invested in with very small amounts of money so it is easy to finance.  
REIT Qualification 
In order to be classified as a REIT, the company must follow certain rules outlined and enforced by the Securities 
and Exchange Commission (U.S.) or Financial Conduct Authority (U.K.). When established as a REIT, the 
company does not need to pay corporation tax on any profits arising from the property business within a REIT. 
 
A company must follow the rules outlined below to be classified as a REIT: 
● Invest in at least 75% of total assets in real estate, cash, or U.S. Treasuries. 
● Derive at least 75% of income from rents, interest on mortgages that finance real estate property, or real 
estate sales. 
● Pay out a minimum of 90% of taxable income in the form of shareholder dividends each year. 
● Be an entity that is taxable as a corporation 
● Be managed by a board of elected directors or trustees 
● Have at least 100 shareholders after its first year of existence 
● Have no more that 50% of its shares held by five or fewer individuals 
 
If, and only if, the company follows these key rules can it be classified as a REIT and reap the benefit of being 
such a status. 
Types of REITs 
REITs can be split into three types: equity, mortgage and hybrid REITS.  
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
14 
Equity REIT 
An Equity REIT is where the REIT itself owns income producing real estate. This is the most common type of 
REIT where revenue is generated from rent paid by the REIT’s tenants. An example of a REIT is Prologis, which 
mainly focuses on industrial and logistics investment and owns over 1 billion square feet in 19 different countries. 
An investor can own a small part of each one of these buildings by investing in Prologis REIT. This outlines the 
benefit REITs bring to small investors especially with exposure to the commercial real estate sector. 
Mortgage REIT 
Mortgage REITs finance real estate owners as opposed to owning income producing real estate themselves. 
This type of REIT can finance owners directly, through loans, or indirectly, through investment in mortgage -
backed securities. Its main revenue source is from interest charged on loans. Annaly Capital Management is the 
world's largest mortgage REIT with over $89 billion in total assets. Its primary focus is on borrowing via short 
term security repurchase agreements and reinvesting proceeds into asset backed securities. The dividend yield 
of the company is currently at 10.68% which means that for every £100 pounds invested, you will receive £10.68 
in dividends each year. Such high dividend rates is a key feature of REITs and one that we will understand later. 
Hybrid REIT 
Finally a Hybrid REIT, as the name suggests, is a combination of both an equity and Mortgage REIT. This means 
that it owns income producing real estate and also finances real estate owners both directly and indirectly. Link 
REIT is the largest hybrid REIT in Asia. The company manages a diverse portfolio of retail facilities, car parks, 
offices, and logistics centres.  
Classification of REITs 
In addition to the type, REITs can be classified depending on how their shares are traded. 
 
Public REIT – Shares for public REIT are listed on a national securities exchange and are regulated by the 
Securities and Exchange Commission (US) or Financial Conduct Authority (UK). 
 
Publicly Non-Traded REIT – This does not trade on a national securities exchange but is regulated by the 
Securities and Exchange Commission (US) or Financial Conduct Authority (UK). 
 
Private REITs – This type of REIT is not traded on a national securities exchange or regulated by either the 
SEC or FCA. Shares of private REITs tend to be quite illiquid. 
Evaluation of REITs 
Having understood the concept of REITs and established the different types of REITs we can look into the 
advantages and disadvantages in investing in real estate investment trusts. 
 
The main advantage of a REIT, and also the reason why they were created, is the low entry price to the real 
estate market (commercial real estate in particular). It allows everyday people to gain access to an industry that 
was previously reserved for large institutional investors.  In addition to diversifying an investment portfolio, the 
high liquidity of publicly traded REIT shares means that all kinds of investors can benefit from a real estate 
investment. Due to the fact that a REIT needs to distribute 90% of its taxable profits as dividends, it can be a 
source of stable low risk cash flow for the investor. 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
15 
Despite the numerous advantages, there are also some negatives associated with an investment in a REIT. The 
key benefit of high dividends also means that there is very little money left over to reinvest into the company and 
therefore REITs tend to grow at a much lower pace when compared to alternative investments. Due to this 
characteristic, REIT shares are best purchased with a long term time horizon in mind. Another disadvantage is 
the fact that dividend yield is taxed as regular income unlike corporation tax. Although you as the investor gain 
access to the real estate sector, you have very little control over the returns and performance of your investment 
and you are paying for management fees in addition to the real estate purchase price when investing.  
Valuating REITs 
With REITs being such an easy way to invest in the real estate market, we need to establish some methods 
both quantitative and qualitative to understand what makes investing in one REIT better than another.  
Quantitative methods 
One of the best metrics to use when evaluating most companies is the price to earnings ratio (P/E). The P/E 
ratio can be used to compare companies within a particular sector and can indicate if a company is overvalued 
(high P/E ratio) or undervalued (low P/E ratio). However, it is NOT a good metric to use to evaluate a REIT and 
we will explore why below.  
𝑃/𝐸 𝑅𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜 = 𝑆ℎ𝑎𝑟𝑒 𝑃𝑟𝑖𝑐𝑒
𝐸𝑎𝑟𝑛𝑖𝑛𝑔𝑠 𝑝𝑒𝑟 𝑆ℎ𝑎𝑟𝑒 
If we break down how earnings per share is calculated: 
𝐸𝑎𝑟𝑛𝑖𝑛𝑔𝑠 𝑝𝑒𝑟 𝑆ℎ𝑎𝑟𝑒 = 𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 − 𝐷𝑖𝑣𝑖𝑑𝑒𝑛𝑑𝑠
𝑂𝑢𝑡𝑠𝑡𝑎𝑛𝑑𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝑆ℎ𝑎𝑟𝑒𝑠 
Looking further into how Net Income is derived: 
𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 = 𝑅𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑢𝑒 − 𝐸𝑥𝑝𝑒𝑛𝑠𝑒𝑠 
 
Figure 4: Example Balance Sheet & Income Statement 
 
The issue we have when it comes to REITs is the way expenses are calculated on the income statement. When 
calculating expenses for the income statement, any assets the company owns must be depreciated and is written 
as the annual depreciation in the income statement. This is then taken away from revenue to get the company's 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
16 
net income. The issue is properties tend to appreciate, not depreciate. Therefore, calculating price to earnings 
ratio in the method explained above will give an inaccurate indication of the company’s performance since the 
amount of depreciation included in the expenses will be very high for a company holding predominantly real 
estate assets.   
Funds from Operation 
In order to mitigate the problem associated with using price to earnings ratio we use a metric called Funds From 
Operation (FFO). The equation for calculating FFO is show below: 
 
𝐹𝐹𝑂 = (𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒 +𝐷𝑒𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 + 𝐴𝑚𝑜𝑟𝑡𝑖𝑠𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 + 𝐿𝑜𝑠𝑠𝑒𝑠 𝑜𝑛 𝑃𝑟𝑜𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑡𝑦 𝑆𝑎𝑙𝑒𝑠) 
− (𝐺𝑎𝑖𝑛 𝑜𝑛 𝑆𝑎𝑙𝑒𝑠 𝑜𝑓 𝑃𝑟𝑜𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑡𝑦) − (𝐼𝑛𝑡𝑒𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑡 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑚𝑒) 
 
Depreciation and amortisation are the expensed portions of a company’s tangible (physical) and intangible 
assets for the year. FFO adds these expenses back into net income to determine the companies actual incoming 
cash/ revenue from operations. Any losses on sales of business property, normally including long-term assets, 
are considered as one-time and non recurring so are not a part of normal operations. For the same reason any 
gains on property sales are taken away from FFO. Finally, we subtract interest income as it is not generally a 
regular part of the business’s normal operation thus should not be included in the funds from operation 
calculation. Funds from operation can be used to give an idea of cash generated by the REIT and can be 
converted into metrics such as P/FFO which can be used to compare different REITs with each other.  
Adjusted Funds from Operation 
Depreciation and amortisation are the expensed portions of a company’s tangible (physical) and intangible 
assets for the year. FFO adds these expenses back into net income to determine the companies actual incoming 
cash/revenue from operations. Any losses on sales of business property, normally including long-term assets, 
are considered as one-time and non recurring so are not a part of normal operations. For the same reason any 
gains on property sales are taken away from FFO. Finally, we subtract interest income as it is not generally a 
regular part of the business’s normal operation thus should not be included in the funds from operation 
calculation. Funds from operation can be used to give an idea of cash generated by the REIT and can be 
converted into metrics such as P/FFO which can be used to compare different REITs with each other.  
 
𝐴𝐹𝐹𝑂 = 𝐹𝐹𝑂 + 𝑅𝑒𝑛𝑡 𝐼𝑛𝑐𝑟𝑒𝑎𝑠𝑒𝑠 − 𝐶𝑎𝑝𝑖𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝐸𝑥𝑝𝑒𝑛𝑑𝑖𝑡𝑢𝑟𝑒 − 𝑅𝑜𝑢𝑡𝑖𝑛𝑒 𝑀𝑎𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑛𝑐𝑒 𝐴𝑚𝑜𝑢𝑛𝑡𝑠  
 
AFFO gives a full picture of a company and can help shareholders get an idea of any residual cash flow left to 
pay out as dividends. It also takes into consideration additional costs and income sources giving an overview of 
future capability to pay dividends.  
Net Asset Value 
NAV or Net Asset Value assesses the fair market value of a REIT’s real estate assets. The use of NAV is to 
bypass book value in favour of providing a more accurate estimation of the actual market value for a REIT’s 
holdings. Calculation of NAV can be very subjective since it requires you to determine the value of each of the 
company’s assets. One way of doing this is capitalization of the operating income, basing it on market rates. A 
cap rate for the present market is determined and used to divide a property’s operating income, with the resulting 
amount being the estimated market value. The market value minus any mortgage liabilities gives the NAV. This 
is another metric that can be used to compare similar sized REITs. 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
17 
Qualitative methods 
In addition to the quantitative methods outlined above we can use qualitative methods to ensure the stability of 
a REIT in a range of economic climates. 
Types of Property 
Diversity of the property sector a REIT owns is very important to its success. For example, a REIT which is 
diversified in the residential, retail, hospitality and office buildings may have more stability than a REIT purely 
focused in hospitality for example. If the economy were to experience a shock, the well diversified REIT is likely 
to have a more resilient performance over the single market REIT. As well as this, knowing the sectors a REIT 
is involved in can be a good way to predict future performance in various economic climates. The diagram below 
shows the breakdown of the properties held by AEWU REIT which is a UK based REIT. 
 
 
Figure 4: AEWU REIT Sector Weightings 
Types of Tenants 
By exploring the types of tenants that lease, property owned by the REIT can be very important since the tenant’s 
performance can sometimes have a significant impact on the price of a REIT. The key points to look out for is 
the company having large reliable tenants but also a large pool of tenants so that the REIT is not adversely 
affected if one tenant were to have sub-par performance. If we take a look at the diagram below we can see that 
RioCan, the second largest REIT in Canada has a very diverse range of tenants as well as large reliable tenants 
such as Walmart and Shell.  
 
Figure 5: RioCan REIT Tenant Breakdown 
Geographic Location 
The geographic location of the company’s real estate portfolio can have a huge impact on how a REIT performs. 
For example, just because one area is facing a property recession, other areas of the country or world may be 
experiencing a property boom. Therefore, in order for a REIT to perform well through a multitude of economic 
climates, geographic diversification is a very important factor. The ideal situation would be for a company to be 

Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
18 
geographically diversified both within a country (e.g. a range of states in America) and internationally (own 
property in multiple countries). 
Occupancy and Rent 
Finally, it is important to look into the occupancy and rent characteristics of the REIT. The occupancy rate  
measures how much of a company's property is vacant as a percentage of total property owned. High occupancy 
rates (95%+) is a good sign for a company. As always this is very dependent on the sector, for example the ideal 
occupancy rate for a hotel may be very different to the ideal rate for warehouse space. In addition to this, the 
average lease length of a REIT’s tenants can be a great measure for the stability of the REIT. In general long 
lease lengths demonstrate tenants have confidence in the company but also provide a secured source of income 
for the future.

Commodities 
Notes by Joseph Guillon 
Lecturer, Securities Education Certificate 2022/23. 
Secretary, Imperial Blockchain Group (2022/23) 
Introduction 
What are commodities? 
Commodities can formally be defined as basic goods possessing fungibility .  
 
Not getting very far with this definition are we… 
 
More practically, basic goods are the raw materials that we use as humans to live our daily lives and keep up 
with our standards of living. Hence, You can imagine from examining your lifestyle that these goods are 
numerous. From the natural gas used to heat your shower in the morning, the rice you eat for lunch, to the steel 
used to construct the very building in which you are – these are all examples of the basic goods that we are 
talking about. These goods are usually either seen as a safe store of value or are integral to the functioning of 
our modern industries. 
 
As for fungibility, it designates the interchangeability of the good produced, meaning the good is the same 
whether it is produced by producer A or producer B. For example, gold is a commodity as it is a basic good/raw 
material/natural resource that is fungible – a gold bar from Peru is that same as a gold bar from Australia of the 
same purity. This allows for a singular price to be attributed to the same commodity of different provenances 
and reassures the investor in what exactly they are buying into when investing in a commodity security (more 
on that later). 
Classification 
Commodities can be grouped into hard commodities and soft commodities. Hard commodities are extracted 
from the earth and include energy and metals, whilst soft commodities are ‘grown’ and include agricultural 
commodities as well as livestock and meat.  
 
The metal commodities can be sub-categorised into precious metals and base metals depending on the nature 
through which they derive their value 
Examples of commodities 
Energy: Crude oil, Natural gas, Coal 
Precious metals: Gold, Silver, Platinum, Palladium 
Base metals: Steel, Nickel, Aluminium, Copper 
Agricultural: Corn, Rice, Wheat, Coffee, Sugar, Cocoa 
Livestock and Meat: Live Cattle, Feeder Cattle, Juicy Bacon (I’m not joking – they’re also known to traders as 
frozen pork bellies. It has the electronic ticker of GPB on the CME Globex) 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
20 
Okay… enough with my obsession that some traders are getting paid seven figures to hoard bacon on the daily. 
Now that we know what commodities are, let’s look at why we should even be interested in investing in 
commodities in the first place.  
 
NOTE: You may hear some people refer to currencies, bonds and equities as ‘commodities’. In this lecture, I 
will only be referring to non-financial commodities as… well, commodities.  
The Commodity Market Players 
It should first of all be noted that there are two types of players that invest in the commodities market: hedgers 
and speculators. 
Hedgers 
Hedgers are commercial players who are either a producer or consumer of a commodity. Hedgers enter the 
commodities market in order to reduce price volatility on the commodities that they buy/sell as part of their core 
business. Hedgers are thus protected from the daily fluctuation of prices by ‘locking-in’ a price months or even 
years in advance with a futures contract. 
Buy-side hedging 
An airline company’s business depends on the price of fuel. To protect itself from the erratic fluctuations, it buys 
fuel contracts in bulk, in advance. 
Sell-side hedging 
A farmer wants to guarantee the return that he/she will get from selling the corn that he cultivates. He is satisfied 
with the current market price of corn but is afraid that corn prices may drop in a month when he will have 
harvested his corn. He thus sells a corn contract at a predetermined price and date to protect himself from the 
risk. 
 
The buyer of a futures contract is referred to as the holder, and the seller as the underwriter. 
Speculators 
Speculators are also referred to as traders/investors. These players don’t need the commodities they are trading. 
They buy and sell contracts with the sole purpose of profiting from the price fluctuations of the commodities 
market.  
 
Examples include savvy investors like you all (or Ahmad and Oscar). Or, just your typical commodities trader at 
a desk in Morgan Stanley. 
 
Thus, hedgers try to protect themselves from the price fluctuations while speculators try to profit from it. 
Why Invest in/Trade Commodities? 
Historically overlooked by investors, the commodities bull-run of the 90s and 00s attracted a lot of attention to 
its market as speculators sought to profit from it. Numerous investment instruments were thus created which 
made it easier for investors to buy into the commodities market.  
 
There are numerous reason why investors have been increasingly interested in commodities as an asset class: 
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
21 
Strong long-term fundamentals of commodities 
Population Growth 
It is a well-known trend that the population on this planet is booming. Yes, we did just reach 8 billion people on 
the 15th of November 2022! The UN forecasts show that by 2055, we will have reached 10 billion people. These 
people all need resources like you and me – food to eat, clothes to wear, buildings to live in, fuel for heating and 
transport – and this all requires raw materials. As we compete to get our hands on the world’s finite resources 
(peacefully or not), the fundamentals of supply and demand mean that the prices of commodities will only 
increase as demand increases. 
Urbanisation 
Urbanisation is another significant demographic phenomenon. In the early 20th century, according to the UN, 
less than 15 percent of the population lived in cities, this has since increased to 55 percent in 2022 and is 
forecast to reach 68 percent in 2050.  
 
Urbanisation is highly significant for commodities because people living in cities, through their urban lifestyle, 
consume a lot more than their rural counterparts. Think more miscellaneous shopping, more imported 
consumption rather than local production (e.g. food, textiles), more fuel needed for transport etc. In addition, the 
expansion of cities requires new roads, new rails, new buildings which all consume resources. 
Industrialisation 
Lastly is the large-scale industrialisation that is happening across developing countries. Though many 
developing countries are industrialising at lightning speed, five of them are known as the BRICS (Brazil, Russia, 
India, China, and South Africa) which account for a majority of the world’s economic growth. As these countries 
fight to secure supplies of key resources for industrialisation (e.g. steel, oil, gas, aluminium), this will only 
continue to put an upward pressure on commodity prices.  
Hedge against inflation 
There are two main reasons why people invest their money: to make money provide returns and to beat inflation.  
 
Inflation eats into your investments and reduces the real purchasing power of your assets. If you have been 
reading the news these past few months, you will have noticed that we have entered a period of high inflation, 
with inflation currently sitting at 11.1 percent in the UK, compared to the central bank’s target of 2 percent (Nov 
2022). Central banks usually tame inflation through increased interest rates which causes economic activity to 
fall and stocks to underperform as costs increase for businesses. Commodities are one of the only asset classes 
that benefit from inflation, as ironically, the increase in their prices is usually a large contributor to inflation itself.  
Safe haven of value 
In times of turmoil and market volatility, some investors turn to precious metals such as gold and silver for 
financial safety. These investors either view precious metals, especially gold, as a safe haven of value, or 
anticipate an increase in its price due to past correlation. Recent situations include the covid pandemic, the war 
in Ukraine, and the loss in value of currencies.  More on that in the gold section below. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
22 
Inelastic nature 
In economics, elasticity is defined as the influence price has on supply and demand. Elasticity seeks to quantify 
the effect that incremental change in price has on supply and demand. Elastic goods react to price changes 
whereas inelastic ones don’t (as much). Being necessity goods, many commodities fall into the latter category 
of inelasticity. Even as the price of commodities increase, many people will continue to desire them as they will 
still need that gas for heating, and that food to eat.  
Commodity diversification 
Between 1971 and 2007, the commodity markets posted positive returns in six of the years where the returns of 
the S&P 500 index were negative. Due to the different fundamentals that drove the prices of commodities 
compared to stocks and bonds, commodities were deemed a hedge against general market downturns and a 
necessary asset to achieve a diverse portfolio. This theory was largely true until substantial speculatory funds 
flowed into the commodities market in the 2000s, causing as a result a correlation to appear between the equity 
and commodities markets – with both rising when global prospects improved, and monetary policies were eased. 
A more refined theory since, explains that both markets are correlated when driven by demand changes (e.g. 
both dropping with reduced demand during pandemic), but uncorrelated when driven by supply changes (the 
war in Ukraine’s effect on energy and grain commodities as opposed to the S&P 500). 
 
Now that we are convinced as to why commodities make good investments, let’s look more closely at some 
specific commodities that we can invest in. We will be examining the big boyz in each commodity category. 
Commodity Focus: Crude Oil 
The energy commodities are mainly composed of the three ways through which humans still obtain the majority 
of their energy: crude oil, natural gas and coal. As part of the energy commodities category we will delve into 
the king of commodities: crude oil. 
 
Providing 40% of the world’s total energy needs whilst being the most traded commodity on the market, crude 
oil truly is the king of them all. Its production value and central role in our modern economy only cements that 
title.  
 
Before investing in any commodity, just like any other financial security, one must fully understand the 
fundamentals that drive its prices. For commodities, here are several questions that you need to ask yourself:  
 
➔ Where are the reserves located?  
➔ What companies are exploiting it?  
➔ What are its uses?  
➔ What has been the historic performance 
◆ What is the outlook for the future considering current supply and demand trends?  
➔ How is it affected by macroeconomics? 
 
In Figure 1, you can see a graph from BP showing the world consumption of energy and how oil is a big driver 
in the way the world functions as we see today.  
 
So, let’s answer some of these questions for our case on crude oil.

How do you invest/trade in commodities? 
There are many investment vehicles through which investors can gain exposure to commodities. As I am aware 
that you have already been exposed to most of these, you can skip sections you are already familiar with. 
However, they are still here in case you would like to read this document independently of the others. 
Futures 
Futures contracts are the most direct way of getting exposure to commodities. They are used to assume or shift 
price risk and can be settled either via delivery or offset. Futures allow you to purchase a specific underlying 
commodity at a mutually agreed upon price at a specific time in the future, hence the name.  
 
In addition, the quality, quantity and delivery location of the commodity are also specified and standardised. 
Futures contracts trade on designated commodity exchanges such as the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) 
and the London Metal Exchange (LME). The exchange provides liquidity and transparency to all market 
participants through its regulatory framework and thus increases investor confidence. 
Forwards 
The nature of the futures market means that only 20% of market activity happens through the exchange. 
Forwards are just like futures in the sense that they represent a contractual obligation to purchase/sell a specific 
underlying commodity at a mutually agreed upon price at a specific time in the future. However, the transaction 
does not happen through the exchange but rather through the Over -the-Counter (OTC) market – directly 
between two market participants. This contract is understandably much more used by hedgers as being outside 
the exchange and free from its standardisation of contracts, it provides the flexibility of tailoring the quality, 
quantity and delivery location of the commodity to the specific needs of the producer/consumer. 
 
Yeah mate, don’t get involved here, unless your grandma has suddenly planted too many potatoes in her 
backyard and wants to get a good cash bonus on top of her retirement when the harvest comes in next year. 
Options 
Options are just like futures, being standardised and trading through exchanges. However, one big difference 
distinguishes the two. While the holder of a futures contract has both the right and the obligation to fulfil the 
contract’s obligations, the holder of an option has the right but NOT the obligation to fulfil the option’s obligations. 
For this privilege, the holder of an option pays a premium. 
 
The technically correct way of thinking about it would be that commodity options give you the option to 
purchase/sell commodity futures. Just like stock options give you the option to purchase/sell stock.  
 
We won’t go into specific examples here as it has already been covered in previous parts of the course. 
Indexes through ETFs and Mutual Funds 
Commodity indexes track a basket of underlying commodities futures contracts, with the composition and 
weighting of each differing from one to another. The price of an index is essentially governed by an algorithm 
that seeks to mimic the returns of the underlying contracts. Indexes are a good way of determining how the 
commodity market is doing in general due to the broad exposure it has. Thus, as well as being a model of a low-
risk investment vehicle for the investor that is unsure of which specific commodity to invest in, they are also a 
benchmark and indicator of the commodity market as a whole. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
27 
NOTE: Note: Due to futures contracts having an expiration date, these are rolled regularly within an index. 
Contracts such as the crude oil futures are rolled monthly because they expire every month. However, other 
contracts that possess expiration dates on only certain months of the year are rolled on according to the available 
monthly contract trade. 
 
Here are some commodity Indexes to look into: 
➔ The S&P Goldman Sachs Commodity Index (S&P GSCI) is one of the most closely watched indexes in 
the market. Tracking 24 commodity futures contracts, the index is production -weighted – assigning 
different weights to different commodities proportional to their current global production quantity, e.g. 
crude oil has a higher weighting than cocoa. 
➔ The Reuters/Jefferies Commodity Research Bureau Index (CRB) is another commodity index that is 
closely followed by institutional investors. Tracking a basket of 19 commodities, this index is unique in 
that it uses a tiered methodology to distribute weightings. The components of the weightings are reviewed 
annually. 
➔ The Dow Jones – AIG Commodity Index places a premium on liquidity and chooses commodities based 
on their production value. The index is one of few that places a weighting floor and ceiling such that no 
commodity may account for less than 2 percent of more than 33 percent of weighting. This greatly 
reduces the weighting of crude oil to half of what it usually is in other commodity indexes and allows for 
greater exposure to other commodities.  
 
It should be noted that you cannot invest into a commodity index directly. Just like you can’t directly invest in 
other indexes such as the famous S&P 500 as these are trackers with no value behind them. You can however 
invest in a fund that tracks the performance of an index. This can be either through an exchange-traded fund or 
a mutual fund. 
 
A quick reminder (although you will be able to find them again in the notes for Lecture 3): 
➔ ETFs – a new breed of investment that tracks the performance of a fund with the convenience of trading 
a stock. 
➔ Mutual funds – an actively managed fund that purchases securities with pooled investor capital. 
 
Alternatively, you could just own the futures tracked by index or even more simply, by ironically adding a layer 
of complexity to our financial instrument (structurers love it), investors can also invest in index futures. You 
guessed it! An index future is a futures contract that tracks the performance of an index (that tracks the 
performance of specific commodity futures…). These are, however, more capital intensive as futures tend to 
trade for quite large sums of money due to the quantity of a commodity they represent. 
Stocks and MLPs 
By buying shares of a company involved in the commodities industry, you can gain indirect exposure to its 
market. Continuing with our main example of crude oil, the commodity needs to be found, drilled, refined, and 
distributed throughout its lifecycle. Thus any company involved in these activities would provide an investment 
exposure to the price of crude oil.  
 
If you are interested in investing in companies involved in the production, transformation, and distribution of 
commodities, one of the best ways to do so is to invest in a master limited partnership (MLP). The advantage 
being the tax structure that allows for higher cash payouts.  
 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
28 
MLPs are public entities that trade on public exchanges. Just like a public company, an MLP issues units (shares) 
that can be freely purchased by the public, which is why they are also termed publicly traded partnerships (PTP). 
This public partnership is run by a general partner for the benefit of the unit holders who are also called limited 
partners.  
 
MLPs benefit from the tax advantage of only being taxed once. This pass-through tax structure means that all 
earnings from the partnership are transferred untaxed to investors who then pay income tax on the distribution 
received. This is in contrast to corporation dividends that are taxed both at the corporation and investor level, 
allowing for MLPs to give higher payouts. 
 
Avoiding tax, this all sounds very nice, but be careful, however! Investing in companies also exposes you to 
additional corporate risk: managerial, administrative, operational… 
CTAs and CPOs  
Now, if you’re really a hands-off investment guy and would rather trust someone else (which may after all know 
a bit more than you) with your hard-earned money, you can go to a commodity trading advisor (CTA) or a 
commodity pool operator (CPO).  
 
CTAs are just basically conventional stockbrokers who specialise in the futures market. They are qualified to 
help you open a futures account, help you develop a commodities investment strategy, as well as trading futures 
contracts on your behalf. Each CTA will have their own investment strategy and philosophy and it is important 
to have done due diligence before trusting anyone with your capital. 
 
Another way to get access to commodity futures is to join a commodity pool. This designates a pool of funds 
that trade commodities futures. This pool is managed by a commodity pool operator (CPO), who just like the 
CTA needs to be licensed to do so.  
 
Investing in commodity pools has three main advantages: 
 
1. Diversification. Futures contracts cost a lot, mainly because of the quantity of the commodity they 
represent, as you saw above in the crude oil section. With more funds available, more futures can be 
purchased to thus create a more diverse commodity portfolio.  
2. Leverage. Once again as discussed above, one of the main characteristics that make commodity futures 
so unique is the leverage that it allows for. More funds, means much more leverage can be taken on, 
and thus potentially increase returns by manyfold.  
3. Reassuring considering the massive leverage that is usually taken on, your maximum loss at any given 
time is only your principal – the entirety of the money you gave the CPO. Now this may sound like a 
horrible deal but let me explain why it may be an advantage.  
 
When trading with leverage on an individual account, you can purchase securities on margin, i.e. borrow funds 
(usually from the broker) to fund the purchase of the futures contract. Now what happens when the position 
entered does the opposite of what you speculated? You get margin called (see Lecture 4 notes). Not only have 
you lost your principal, but you still owe money on top of that as you also need to pay the broker back for the 
money he lent you.  
 
CPOs on the other hand are registered as limited partnerships. This means that even if leverage is used to get 
into bad trades that result in the pool going bust, you will not be personally liable to the debt. Hence, you only 
lose the amount you initially invested into the pool i.e. your principal. 
Imperial College Investment Society | Securities Education Certificate 
 
  
29 
Now, hopefully, you would have performed the necessary due diligence before trusting a CTA or CPO with your 
money in order to minimise such situations. Important topics to inquire about should include: 
➔ Track record 
➔ Past disciplinary actions 
➔ Fees (management, performance and miscellaneous) 
➔ Minimum investment sum 
 
Important Commodities Market Indicators 
Simply looking at the price charts of commodity futures will not help you in making a decision on whether a 
commodity is a good buy. In order to assist you in investment decisions, here are several crucial market 
indicators you should consider: 
 
➔ Consumer Price Index (CPI) 
➔ EIA Inventory Reports 
➔ Federal Funds Rate 
➔ Gross Domestic Product (GDP) 
➔ London Gold Fix 
➔ Nonfarm Payrolls 
➔ Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) 
➔ Reuters/Jefferies CRB Index 
➔ US Dollar 
➔ WTI Crude Oil

documents.title: Pandemic Recovery Research Document
documents.text:
Sales & Trading
A PANDEMIC RECOVERY?
MACRO RESEARCH

HEDGE FUND: BUY-SIDE OBJECTIVES
● Investment Strategy: Multi Asset, $30 million capital. Use both long and 
short strategies. All assets are priced in US dollars.
● Investment Time Horizon: 30 minutes simulation time will be equivalent 
to investing through a ﬁctitious 3-month time period. 
● Investment Objective: To generate as high a return on investment as 
possible.
● Execution: Avoid large market impact on the exchange. Achieve more 
competitive prices by trading through the investment banks.
INVESTMENT BANK SALES TRADER: SELL-SIDE OBJECTIVES
● Client Relationships: Communicate regularly with your clients in order to 
form and maintain strong relationships. 
● Maximise Commission: Facilitate as many client trades as you can.
● Competitive Prices: Provide tight bid/offer spreads for clients when they 
ask for quotes. Bad/wide spreads will mean clients will be unlikely to trade 
with you.
● Manage Risk: When a client accepts a trade with you, the bank will 
automatically be put into the opposite position. Trade out of these 
positions of risk by using cross-trades with other investment banks, or by 
using the exchange..
● Proﬁt and Loss: Proﬁt is risk for an Investment Bank. Ensure that your PnL 
does not exceed your ﬁnal amount of commission.
● Manage Market Impact: When trading out of risk positions on the 
exchange, break positions up into smaller execution sizes to avoid PnL 
losses through market impact.

MACRO OVERVIEW
Government-mandated lockdowns aimed at controlling the pandemic led to an 
unprecedented halt in consumer activity, triggering both severe economic contractions 
and rapid shifts in market dynamics. Global markets experienced signiﬁcant divergence; 
the retail, hospitality, and travel sectors saw revenue contractions of up to 40-50% in some 
regions, whilst the technology sector surged in response to accelerated digitalization 
trends, with many tech ﬁrms reporting revenue increases of 20% or more. 
Heading into a new year, analysts are observing government and central bank policies 
intended to stabilise and foster economic recovery. A key concern is further government 
stimulus amid record-high debt levels, with government debt-to-GDP ratios exceeding 
100% in several advanced economies. Additionally, central banks face limitations using 
traditional monetary policy tools, as interest rates are already near zero in many regions.
 E-Commerce: Alibaba (BABA) 
Alibaba Group: Chinese multinational tech 
company specialising in e-commerce, retail, 
internet and technology. 
The last 12 months has seen the Alibaba 
share price ﬁnally break above the stubborn 
$200 resistance level with price then quickly 
rising to test the $300 handle. This 
acceleration higher is a function of global 
consumers being forced to increase their 
online spending due to Covid lockdowns. As 
we enter the new year analysts are hoping 
for new product offerings to take Alibaba’s 
business to the next level.
Oil & Gas: British Petroleum (BP)
BP: Multinational oil and gas company, one 
of the world’s seven oil & gas “supermajors”. 
BP’s share price took a major drop last year 
with more than half of its value being wiped 
out. The downside pressure was driven by 
both a collapse in oil demand due to the 
global lockdowns implemented to stop 
Covid-19 spreading, but also an increase in 
supply as OPEC and Russia failed to agree 
on extending production cuts. Analysts are 
hoping for an agreement to further cut 
production at their next meeting.

Semiconductors: Intel Corp. (INTC)
A multinational technology company, one of 
the world’s largest and most well-known 
semiconductor manufacturers. 
Intel’s share price experienced signiﬁcant 
volatility in 2020 largely due to supply chain 
disruptions caused by the global Covid-19 
pandemic. Despite these challenges, Intel 
has continued to beneﬁt from strong 
demand for semiconductors and data 
center processors, driven by remote work 
and online activities. Many analysts question 
whether they can stem competition from 
AMD and NVIDIA.
Technology: Microsoft (MSFT)
The company has enjoyed a fantastic 
growth trajectory with the share price more 
than tripling over the last three years. The 
transition to work-from-home and online 
schooling has meant that Microsoft’s 
products and services have experienced a 
sudden increase in demand. Analysts expect 
the growth story to continue but are also 
looking for additional revenue streams to be 
introduced for the business to sustain it’s 
recent rate of growth.
Automotive: Volkswagen (VW)
A global automaker headquartered in 
Germany who produces cars from popular 
brands including VW, Audi, Porsche.
Their share price took an initial sharp 
correction lower during last year's crisis 
lockdown, but this correction has been 
recovered surprisingly well in recent months 
as record low interest rates continue to make 
ﬁnancing deals very attractive. Analysts are 
now looking for further signs that the global 
economic rebound can be sustained before 
the share price can return above $75.

documents.title: Problem Sheet 4 Solution
documents.text:
Problem Sheet 4
MATH40012 - Linear Algebra and Groups - Spring 2026
1. Let G = R∗ × R where R∗ = R \ {0}. For (a, b), (c, d) ∈ G, we define (a, b) ⋆ (c, d) =
(ac, ad + b). Prove that G is a group with respect to the operator ⋆. What is the identity of G
? Given (a, b) ∈ G, what is inverse of (a, b) ?
Solution:
The associativity of ⋆: Given (a1, b1), (a2, b2) and (a3, b3), then
((a1, b1) ⋆ (a2, b2)) ⋆ (a3, b3)
=(a1a2, a1b2 + b1) ⋆ (a3, b3)
=(a1a2a3, a1a2b3 + a1b2 + b1)
and
(a1, b1) ⋆ ((a2, b2) ⋆ (a3, b3))
=(a1, b1) ⋆ (a2a3, a2b3 + b2)
=(a1a2a3, a1a2b3 + a1b2 + b1).
This proves the associativity.
The identity of G is e = (1, 0). Indeed, for every (a, b) ∈ G, we have
(1, 0) ⋆ (a, b) = ( a, b) ⋆ (1, 0) = ( a, b).
The inverse of an element (a, b) ∈ G is (a−1, −a−1b). Indeed, this is well-defined since
a ̸= 0, and we have
(a−1, −a−1b) ⋆ (a, b) = (1 , a−1b − a−1b) = (1 , 0).
2. Let G be a group, with identity element e ∈ G. Let g ∈ G. The order of g is the smallest
integer n > 0 such that gn = e (we say that g has infinite order if no such n exists). Suppose
that the order of g is d.
(a) Show that gk = e if and only if d divides k.
Solution:
Assume to the contrary that d does not divide k. Then there are integers p, r such that
1 ≤ r ≤ d − 1 and k = r + pd. By associativity we deduce
e = gk = gr+pd = gr(gd)p = gr(e)p = gr.
However this contradicts the minimality of d. We conclude the proof.
1
(b) Let h ∈ G be another element, whose order is c, and suppose that g and h commute.
Show that the order of gh is less than or equal to the lowest common multiple of c and
d. Solution:
Let D be the lowest common multiple of c and d. By Question 2(a), it is enough to
prove that (gh)D = e. Since g and h commute, we deduce
(gh)D = gDhD.
Since D is a multiple of d, we may write D = dq for some integer q > 0. Then we
deduce
gD = gdq = (gd)q = eq = e.
Similarly, since D is a multiple of c, we deduce hD = e. Thus we have
gDhD = e.
This concludes the proof.
3. Let G be any group, and let g ∈ G be an element. Define the set
CG(g) = {h ∈ G | hgh−1 = g}
Prove that CG(g) is a group with respect to the operation of G.
Solution:
First, since the operation on CG(g) is the restriction of that of G to CG(g), and that G is a
group, then the operation on CG(g) is associative.
Let e ∈ G be the identity. We have ege−1 = ege = g. Thus e ∈ CG(g). It is clear that e is
also the identity in CG(g).
Let h ∈ CG(g), then we have hgh−1 = g. Mulitiplying h on right we obtain hg = gh. Then
multiplying h−1 on the left of this last equality we obtain g = h−1gh. Since the inverse of
h−1 is h, we obtain h−1 ∈ CG(g).
Thus CG(g) is a group.
4. Let G be any group. Define the set
Z(G) = {h ∈ G | hgh−1 = g for every g ∈ G}
Prove that Z(G) is a group with respect to the operation of G.
Solution:
By definition, we have
Z(G) = ∩g∈GCG(g).
2
By the same argument as in Question 3, we deduce that the operation on Z(G) is associative.
Since e ∈ CG(g) for every g ∈ G, thus e ∈ Z(G). Take an arbitrary h ∈ Z(G). We see that
for every g ∈ G we have h ∈ CG(g), and we have seen that this implies h−1 ∈ CG(g) for
every g ∈ G. Thus h−1 ∈ Z(G). We have verified all the group axioms. Thus Z(G) is a
group.
3

documents.title: Problem Sheet 5 Solution
documents.text:
Problem Sheet 5
MATH40012 - Linear Algebra and Groups - Spring 2026
1. Show that if G = ⟨g⟩ is an infinite cyclic group, then g−1 is the only other generator.
Solution:
By definition, for every integer n, we have gn = (g−1)−n. Thus g−1 is a generator of G.
Now assume that gm is a generator of G. Then there is an integer k ∈ Z such that (gm)k = g.
Multiplying g−1 on both sides, we obtain gmk−1 = e. Let l = |mk − 1|. Then we have either
gl = gmk−1 = e or gl = ( gmk−1)−1 = e. Since clearly l > 1, we deduce that g has finite
order. Then G = ⟨g⟩ = {e, g, · · · , gl−1} which is a finite set. This is a contradiction.
2. Let p, q be two distinct prime numbers. Let k, l ≥ 1 be integers.
(a) Write down the elements in (Z/pk)∗. Compute |(Z/pk)∗|.
Solution:
We have seen that [a] ∈ (Z/pk)∗ if and only if gcd(a, pk) = 1 . It is clear that
gcd(a, pk) = gcd(a, p). For any 0 ≤ a ≤ pk − 1, this is equivalent to say that a ̸= pm
for some 0 ≤ m ≤ pk−1. Thus
(Z/pk)∗ = {[a] | a = pm + r with 0 ≤ m ≤ pk−1 − 1, 1 ≤ r ≤ p − 1}.
Thus |(Z/pk)∗| = pk − pk−1.
(b) Write down the elements in (Z/pq)∗. Compute |(Z/pq)∗|.
Solution:
We have seen that[a] ∈ (Z/pq)∗ if and only if gcd(a, pq) = 1 which is equivalent to that
gcd(a, p) = gcd(a, q) = 1 . For any 0 ≤ a ≤ pq − 1, this is equivalent to say that both
p and q do not divide a. The set of such a that is divisible by p are 0, p, · · · , p(q − 1).
The set of such a that is divisible by q are 0, q, · · · , q(p − 1). In summary, we have
(Z/pq)∗ = {[a] | 0 < a ≤ pq − 1, a /∈ {p, · · · , p(q − 1)} ∪ {q, · · · , q(p − 1)}}.
Then |(Z/pq)∗| = pq − p − q + 1 = ( p − 1)(q − 1).
(c) Write down the elements in (Z/pkql)∗. Compute |(Z/pkql)∗|.
Solution:
We have seen that [a] ∈ (Z/pkql)∗ if and only if gcd(a, pkql) = 1 . This is equivalent to
that gcd(a, p) = gcd(a, q) = 1 . For any 0 ≤ a ≤ pkql − 1, this is equivalent to say that
both p and q do not divide a. Thus
(Z/pkql)∗ = {[a] | 0 < a ≤ pq − 1, a /∈ {pm | 0 ≤ m < p k−1ql}
∪ {qm | 0 ≤ m < p kql−1}}.
Then |(Z/pkql)∗| = pkql − pk−1ql − pkql−1 + pk−1qk−1 = (pk − pk−1)(ql − ql−1).
1

divisible by pi for every 1 ≤ i ≤ m. We can compute |(Z/ Qm

4. (Harder) Given an example of an infinite abelian group without any infinite order element.
Solution:
Let Q > 1 be an arbitrary integer. We let
G = {e2πin/Qk
| k ∈ N, n ∈ Z}.
It is straightforward to verify the group axiom holds for (G, ×) where × is the multiplication.
Then clearly G is an infinite group, since {e2πi/Qk
| k} is already an infinite set. For any
element g = e2πin/Qk
∈ G, we have gQk = 1 which is the identity of G.
5. Let G be a group of order n. Let g ∈ G be an element of order d. Prove that d divides n.
Solution:
Given h1 and h2 in G, we denote h1 ∼ h2 if there is an integer n ∈ Z such that h1 = h2gn.
It is straightforward to see that this relation is: 1. reflexive: h ∼ h, 2. symmetric: h1 ∼ h2
implies h2 ∼ h1, 3. transitive: h1 ∼ h2 and h2 ∼ h3 implies h1 ∼ h3. Hence this is an
equivalence relation.
G is divided into equivalence classes. To show d divides n, it suffices to prove that each
equivalence class has exactly d elements. Clearly, the set {h, hg, · · · , hgd−1} is contained in
the same equivalence class. If there exist 0 ≤ i < j ≤ d − 1 such that hgi = hgj, then we
have e = (hgi)−1hgj = g−ih−1hgj = gj−i. Since 0 < j − i < d , this contradicts the fact that
the order of g is d. This proves the statement.
2
