documents.title: Problem Sheet 6 Solution
documents.text:
Problem Sheet 6
MATH40012 - Linear Algebra and Groups - Spring 2026
1. Let
σ =
    
1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
8 4 5 2 3 1 7 6
     and τ =
    
1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
5 7 8 3 6 2 1 4
    
(a) Compute στ .
Solution:
We write the two permutations on top of each other, whereτ goes on the top because
στ is the permutation where σ is applied after τ:
      
1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
5 7 8 3 6 2 1 4
3 7 6 5 1 4 8 2
      
.
So now we find στ by deleting the middle row:
στ =
    
1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
3 7 6 5 1 4 8 2
     .
(b) Write σ, τ and στ as products of pairwise-disjoint cycles.
Solution:
We run through the algorithm from the lectures to write σ as a product of pairwise
disjoint cycles. We first pick 1 and find its corresponding cycle (186). Next we pick 2
and find its corresponding cycle (24). Then we pick 3, which yields the cycle (35). The
only number left is 7, but that number is left fixed by σ, so we omit the corresponding
1-cycle. We thus have
σ = (186)(24)(35).
Similarly, we find τ = (15627)(384) and στ = (13645)(278).
2. Let σ, τ and ν denote the following elements of S3:
σ = (123), τ = (12), ν = (132)
Compute the composition ντ σ.
Solution:
Direct calculation
(132)(12)(123) = (132)(23) = (13) .
3. (a) Show that
V = {e, (12)(34), (13)(24), (14)(23)}
is a group under the operation of S4. Is V cyclic?
1
Solution:
We first check that V is closed under the group operation of S4. Each element squares
to the identity. In particular we see that each of the elements of V is its own inverse.
For the remaining ways to multiply the elements in V we calculate
(12)(34)(13)(24) = (14)(23) .
We can then apply a trick to find (13)(24)(12)(34), namely:
(13)(24)(12)(34) = ((13)(24)) −1((12)(34))−1 = ((12)(34)(13)(24)) −1 = ((14)(23)) −1 = (14)(23).
So after doing two more calculations like this we find that V is closed under the group
operation of S4. We already argued that every element in V is its own inverse. Finally,
clearly e ∈ V and associativity is inherited from the group operation on S4. So V is a
group.
The order of every element is at most 2, so V is not cyclic as the order of V is 4.
(b) Is
H = {e, (1234), (1243), (1324), (1342), (1423), (1432)}
a group under the operation of S4?
Solution:
No, H is not closed under the group operation of S4. For example:
(1234)(1243) = (132) ̸∈ H.
4. Express the following permutation (composition of cycles) in S7 in disjoint cycle notation
and give its cycle-type
(135)(263)(4712).
Solution:
We run through the algorithm from the lectures. First we pick 1 and we see that the corre-
sponding cycle is (165). Then for 2 the corresponding cycle is (2473). So we see that
(135)(263)(4712) = (165)(2473) ,
and its cycle-type is (3, 4).
2

documents.title: Problem Sheet 7 Solution
documents.text:
Problem Sheet 7
MATH40012 - Linear Algebra and Groups - Spring 2026
1. Recall that GLn(R) is the group of all invertible n × n (real) matrices, with the operation of
matrix multiplication. Let SLn(R) ⊂ GLn(R) denote the subset:
SLn(R) = {A ∈ GLn(R) : det( A) = 1 }
(a) Show that SLn(R) is a subgroup of GLn(R).
Solution:
Let A, B ∈ SLn(R). Since B ∈ GLn(R), B−1 ∈ GLn(R). Thus we have
det(AB−1) = det( A) det(B−1) = det( A)/ det(B) = 1 .
Thus AB−1 ∈ SLn(R). By Subgroup Test, we see that SLn(R) is a subgroup of
GLn(R).
(b) Fix a matrix B ∈ GLn(R). Which matrices lie in the left coset B SL n(R)? Can you
describe the set of all left cosets?
Solution:
A matrix C ∈ BSL n(R) if and only if B−1C ∈ SLn(R). This is equivalent to that
det(B−1C) = det( B)−1 det(C) = 1 . Thus the equivalent condition for C ∈ BSL n(R)
is that det(C) = det( B).
2. Let H ≤ Sn be such that |Sn : H| = 2. Show that H contains every 3-cycle in Sn.
Solution:
Let σ ∈ Sn be a 3-cycle, and assume for a contradiction that σ ̸∈ H. We thus have H ̸= σH.
Note that σ−1 = σ2, so we must have σ2 ̸∈ H and hence H ̸= σ2H. As |Sn : H| = 2 we
then must have σH = σ2H. We would thus have σ2 ∈ σH, and hence σ2 = σh for some
h ∈ H. However, then we get σ = h ∈ H, a contradiction. So we conclude that we must
have had σ ∈ H to begin with.
3. Let G = (Q∗, ·)
(a) Show that the set (Q∗)2 = {r2 : r ∈ Q∗} is a subgroup of Q∗.
Solution:
Clearly (Q∗)2 is non-empty. Let now a, b ∈ (Q∗)2 and write a = q2, b = r2 for
q, r ∈ Q∗. Then ab−1 = q2(r2)−1 = (qr−1)2 ∈ (Q∗)2. So by the subgroup test (Q∗)2 is
a subgroup of Q∗.
(b) Show that the there are infinitely many left cosets of (Q∗)2.
Solution:
We claim that p(Q∗)2 ̸= q(Q∗)2 for any two distinct primes p and q. Suppose otherwise,
then p(Q∗)2 = q(Q∗)2 and so q/p ∈ (Q∗)2. Hence q/p = ( a/b)2 for some integers a
and b. Rewriting this yields a2p = b2q. Counting prime factors we see that the p appears
an odd number of times on the left-hand side, while it appears an even number of times
on the right-hand side. We thus arrive at a contradiction and conclude p(Q∗)2 ̸= q(Q∗)2.
1
4. Let G be a group, H and K be subgroups of G, and S the smallest subgroup of G containing
H and K.
(a) Show that H ∩ K is a subgroup of G.
Solution:
As e ∈ H and e ∈ K we have e ∈ H ∩K, so H ∩K is non-empty. Now letx, y ∈ H ∩K,
then xy−1 ∈ H and xy−1 ∈ K, so xy−1 ∈ H ∩ K. By the subgroup test we conclude
that H ∩ K is a subgroup of G.
(b) (harder) Show that |K : H ∩ K| ≤ | S : H|.
Solution:
We define the following map
f : (K : H ∩ K) → (S : H),
x(H ∩ K) 7→ xH.
That is, f send a left coset x(H ∩ K) of H ∩ K to the left coset xH of H. We first
verify that this map is well-defined. If x(H ∩ K) = x′(H ∩ K) then we have that
x−1x′ ∈ H ∩ K. So definitely x−1x′ ∈ H and hence, xH = x′H. So whatever
representation for the input we choose, we get the same output. The domain and
codomain of f are also correct: if x ∈ K then definitely x ∈ S, as K ⊆ S, so xH is
indeed in S : H.
We claim that f is injective, from which |K : H ∩ K| ≤ | S : H| follows. So let
x, x′ ∈ K be such that f (x(H ∩ K)) = f (x′(H ∩ K)). That is, xH = x′H. Then,
we have that x−1x′ ∈ H. As x, x′ ∈ K we also have that x−1x′ ∈ K, and thus
x−1x′ ∈ H ∩ K. So x(H ∩ K) = x′(H ∩ K), and we conclude that f is injective.
2

documents.title: Problem Sheet 8 Solution
documents.text:
Problem Sheet 8
MATH40012 - Linear Algebra and Groups - Spring 2026
1. For each of the following parts, show (if we haven’t done so yet) that G is a group, H is a
normal subgroup of G and describe what the quotient group G/H ‘looks like’.
(a) G = (C∗, ·) and H = S1 = {z ∈ C : |z| = 1}.
Solution:
Checking that H ≤ G is straightforward and because G is Abelian it is in fact a normal
subgroup. We write Cr = {z ∈ C : |z| = r} for the circle with radius r, where r > 0
is a real number. We claim that the cosets are exactly the sets of the formCr and that
coset multiplication is given by Cr · Cs = Crs, so G/H looks like (R>0, ·).
We have that z and z′ are in the same coset of H iff z−1z′ ∈ H. So z and z′ are in the
same coset iff |z−1z′| = 1, which happens iff |z| = |z′|, and the claim follows.
(b) G = {f : Rn → Rn | f (x) = Ax + b, A ∈ GLn(R), b ∈ Rn} and H = {t : Rn →
Rn | t(x) = x + c, c ∈ Rn}, where the group operation is function composition.
Solution:
First, we verify that G is a group. Let f, g ∈ G, so they are of the form f (x) = Ax + b
and g(x) = Bx + c. Then
f (g(x)) = f (Bx + c) = A(Bx + c) + b = ABx + Ac + b,
and we see that f g ∈ G because AB ∈ GLn(R) and Ac + b ∈ Rn. So function
composition is indeed a binary operation on G. The identity function is in G, as it is
given by Id(x) = Inx + 0. The inverse of f (x) = Ax + b is f −1(x) = A−1x − A−1b,
as indeed:
f −1(f (x)) = g(Ax + b) = A−1(Ax + b) + A−1b = x + A−1b − A−1b = x,
and
f (f −1(x)) = f (A−1x − A−1b) = A(A−1x − A−1b) + b = x − b + b = x.
We conclude that G is a group.
Next we check that H is a subgroup. Clearly H is non-empty, so we can and will use
the subgroup test. Let f, g ∈ H and write f (x) = x + a and g(x) = x + b. By the
above, we have that g−1(x) = x − b. We compute
f (g−1(x)) = f (x − b) = x − b + c.
So f g−1 ∈ H and we conclude that H is a subgroup.
1
To see that H is normal we verify the conjugacy criterion. Let h ∈ H and g ∈ G, and
write h(x) = x + b and g(x) = Ax + a, so g−1(x) = A−1x − A−1a. We compute
g(h(g−1(x))) = g(h(A−1x − A−1a))
= g(A−1x − A−1a + b)
= A(A−1x − A−1a + b) + a
= x − a + Ab + a
= x + Ab.
So ghg −1 ∈ H and we conclude that H is normal.
For a matrix A ∈ GLn(R) we write GA = {f ∈ G : f (x) = Ax + b, for some b ∈
Rn}. We claim that the sets GA are the cosets of H. Indeed, let f, g ∈ G then f and g
are in the same coset iff f −1g ∈ H. Writing f (x) = Ax + a and g(x) = Bx + b we get
f −1(g(x)) = f −1(Bx + b) = A−1(Bx + b) − A−1a = A−1Bx + A−1b − A−1a.
So f −1g ∈ H iff A−1B = In, which happens exactly when A = B and the claim
follows. Finally, we will show that coset multiplication is given by GA · GB = GAB and
so G/H looks like GLn(R). Indeed, for matrices A and B we have that f (x) = Ax
and g(x) = Bx are representatives of GA and GB respectively. So GA · GB is the coset
that is represented by f (g(x)) = ABx, and hence GA · GB = GAB.
(c) G = Sn and H = An = {σ ∈ Sn : sgn(σ) = 1 }, for n ≥ 2.
Solution:
We know that Sn is a group. We claim that for any σ, τ ∈ Sn we have that sgn(στ ) =
sgn(σ) sgn(τ ). Indeed, we have seen in the lectures that for any permutation σ, if we
can write σ as a product of k 2-cycles then sgn(σ) = ( −1)k. So, if we decompose σ
and τ in n and k 2-cycles respectively then we have a decomposition of στ in n + k
2-cycles. We thus see that sgn(στ ) = ( −1)n+k = (−1)n(−1)k = sgn(σ) sgn(τ ).
From the claim it now quickly follows thatAn is a subgroup. It is non-empty, asId ∈ An.
Furthermore, for any permutation τ we have that if τ = τ1 ◦ . . . ◦ τk is a decomposition
in 2-cycles then τ −1 = τ −1
k ◦ . . . ◦ τ −1
1 and we conclude that sgn(τ ) = sgn( τ −1)
using the result from the lectures quoted before. So for any σ, τ ∈ An we have
sgn(στ −1) = sgn( σ) sgn(τ −1) = sgn( σ) sgn(τ ) = 1 . So στ −1 ∈ An and we conclude
that An is a subgroup.
We claim that|Sn : An| = 2, from which normality ofAn immediately follows. Consider
the coset (12)An. Then any σ ∈ (12)An will have sgn(σ) = −1. This is because it is
of the form (12)τ for some τ ∈ An. Hence sgn(σ) = sgn((12)) sgn( τ ) = −1 · 1 = −1.
Conversely, if sgn(σ) = −1 we have that sgn(σ) = sgn((12) σ) = sgn((12)) sgn( σ) =
−1 · −1 = 1 . So (12)σ ∈ An and hence σ = (12)(12)σ ∈ (12)An. We thus see that
(12)An = {σ ∈ Sn : sgn(σ) = −1}.
As 1 and −1 are the only possible signs for a permutation, we see that An and (12)An
partition Sn, so indeed |Sn : An| = 2.
2
It now also quickly follows that Sn/An looks like Z2 (or any 2-element group). As we
have seen there are only two cosets, and there is only one group structure possible on a
set of 2 elements (after we have fixed one element to be the identity).
2. Show that any quotient group of a cyclic group is cyclic.
Solution:
Let H ⊴ G and let g ∈ G be such that G = {gn : n ∈ Z} (such g exists because G is cyclic).
We will show that G/H is cyclic. In particular, we claim that G/H = {(gH )n : n ∈ Z}.
Let g′H ∈ G/H be any element of the quotient group. Then g′ = gn for some n ∈ Z, so
g′H = gnH = (gH )n, and the claim follows.
3. Consider the set Z × Z and define a binary operation + on this set by (n, k) + ( ℓ, m) =
(n + ℓ, k + m).
(a) Show that (Z × Z, +) is a group.
Solution:
Associativity is straightforward from associativity of + on Z (write this out to check for
yourself). The identity element is (0, 0), as indeed
(0, 0) + (n, k) = (0 + n, 0 + k) = ( n, k) = ( n + 0, k + 0) = ( n, k) + (0, 0).
Finally, the inverse of (n, k) is (−n, −k), as indeed
(n, k) + (−n, −k) = ( n − n, k − k) = (0 , 0) = ( −n + n, −k + k) = ( −n, −k) + (n, k).
(b) Let H = {(n, k) ∈ Z × Z : n = 0} and prove that H is a normal subgroup of Z × Z.
Solution:
Clearly H is non-empty. The subgroup test then tells us that H is a subgroup, as for any
(0, n), (0, k) ∈ H we have (0, n) + (0, −k) = (0 , n − k) ∈ H. Finally, (Z × Z, +) is
Abelian because
(n, k) + (ℓ, m) = ( n + ℓ, k + m) = ( ℓ + n, m + k) = ( ℓ, m) + (n, k).
So H must indeed be a normal subgroup.
(c) Describe Z × Z/H, what known group does this look like?
Solution:
Write Xn = {(n, k) ∈ Z × Z : k ∈ Z}. We claim that the cosets of H are exactly
the sets Xn for n ∈ Z. Using the fact we described at the start of the solution to
problem 1, we have that (n, k) and (ℓ, m) are in the same coset iff (ℓ − n, m − k) =
(−n, −k) + (ℓ, m) ∈ H, which happens iff ℓ − n = 0. So these are indeed the cosets.
We now claim that the group operation on Z × Z/H is given by Xn · Xm = xn+m, so
Z × Z/H looks like Z. Note that (n, 0) and (m, 0) are representatives of Xn and Xm
respectively, so Xn · Xm is the coset represented by (n, 0) + (m, 0) = ( n + m, 0), which
is indeed Xn+m.
3
4. Let G be a group and let ∼ be an equivalence relation on G. Write G/∼ for the set of
equivalence classes of ∼, and write [a] for the equivalence class of a ∈ G. Define a binary
operation ⋆ on G/∼ as follows [a] ⋆ [b] = [ ab] and assume that this operation is well-defined.
(a) Show that (G/∼, ⋆) is a group.
Solution:
By assumption ⋆ is a binary operation on G/∼. Associativity is straightforward: let
[a], [b], [c] ∈ G/∼, then
([a] ⋆ [b]) ⋆ [c] = [ ab] ⋆ c = [(ab)c] = [ a(bc)] = [ a] ⋆ [bc] = [ a] ⋆ ([b] ⋆ [c]).
The identity element is [e], where e is the identity element in G. Indeed, for any
[a] ∈ G/∼:
[a] ⋆ [e] = [ ae] = [ a] = [ ea] = [ e] ⋆ [a].
Finally, the inverse of [a] ∈ G/∼ is [a−1]:
[a] ⋆ [a−1] = [ aa−1] = [ e] = [ a−1a] = [ a−1] ⋆ [a].
We conclude that (G/∼, ⋆) is indeed a group.
(b) Find a normal subgroup H ⊴ G such that (G/H, ·) = ( G/∼, ⋆), where · is the usual
coset multiplication.
Solution:
The identity element of (G/H, ·) will be H, so if we want (G/H, ·) = ( G/∼, ⋆) then
we must have H = [e]. We will show that for this choice of H we do indeed have that
H is a normal subgroup and that (G/H, ·) = ( G/∼, ⋆).
First we show that H is a subgroup by verifying the requirements of being a subgroup.
Clearly e ∈ H. Let a, b ∈ H, that is a ∼ e and b ∼ e. We assumed ⋆ to be well-defined,
so we can perform the following calculation:
[ab] = [ a] ⋆ [b] = [ e] ⋆ [e] = [ ee] = [ e].
We thus see that ab ∼ e,1 and hence ab ∈ H. Finally, for any a ∈ H we can again use
well-definedness of ⋆ to get
[a−1] = [ ea−1] = [ e] ⋆ [a−1] = [ a] ⋆ [a−1] = [ aa−1] = [ e].
So we have that a−1 ∼ e and hence a−1 ∈ H. We conclude that H is a subgroup.
Next we verify that the equivalence classes of ∼ are precisely the cosets of H. To show
this, we claim that for any a, b ∈ G we have a ∼ b iff a−1b ∼ e. If a ∼ b then [a−1b] =
[a−1] ⋆ [b] = [ a−1] ⋆ [a] = [ a−1a] = [ e], so indeed a−1b ∼ e. Conversely, if a−1b ∼ e
then [a−1b] = [ e], so [a] ⋆ [a−1b] = [ a] ⋆ [e] and hence [b] = [ aa−1b] = [ ae] = [ a], so
indeed a ∼ b. Using the claim we now have b ∈ [a] iff b ∼ a iff a−1b ∼ e iff a−1b ∈ H
1Alternatively, just in terms of ∼, we have that ab ∼ ee = e by well-definedness of ⋆.
4
iff b ∈ aH, where the final equivalence is the fact mentioned at the start of problem 1
on this solution sheet.
We thus have H is a subgroup and its cosets are precisely the equivalence classes of ∼.
So the ⋆ operation is coset multiplication, which we assumed to be well-defined. From
the lectures we know that coset multiplication is well-defined iff H is normal. So we
conclude that H is a normal subgroup and (G/H, ·) = ( G/∼, ⋆).
5

6. LetI= (x, y) be an interval. Show that iff:I→Ris continuous and injective on
I, thenfis either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing onI.
Solution.Suppose for the sake of contradiction thatfis neither strictly increasing
nor strictly decreasing. Sincefis injective, it cannot be constant on any sub-
interval. Therefore, there must exist pointsa < b < cinIsuch thatf(b) is not
betweenf(a) andf(c). This leaves us with two main cases:
(a)f(b) is a local maximum:f(b)> f(a) andf(b)> f(c).
(b)f(b) is a local minimum:f(b)< f(a) andf(b)< f(c).
Let’s consider the first case (the second is analogous). Choose a valueksuch that
max(f(a), f(c))< k < f(b).
•Sincefis continuous on [a, b] andf(a)< k < f(b), the Intermediate Value
Theorem (IVT) implies there existsx 1 ∈(a, b) such thatf(x 1) =k.
•Sincefis continuous on [b, c] andf(c)< k < f(b), the IVT implies there exists
x2 ∈(b, c) such thatf(x 2) =k.
Thusf(x 1) =f(x 2) withx 1 < b < x 2, which meansx 1 ̸=x 2. This contradicts the
assumption thatfis injective.
Therefore, for any three pointsa < b < c,f(b) must lie strictly betweenf(a) and
f(c), which impliesfis strictly monotonic.

Supposeϕ 1 :I 1 →[a, b] andϕ 2 :I 2 →[a, b] are two functions such that bothγ◦ϕ 1
andγ◦ϕ 2 are arc length reparametrizations.

(b) Using the fact that a functionf:R→Rwith|f ′|= 1 must be of the form
f(u) =±u+C, prove that the two reparametrizations are the same up to a
time shift and a possible reversal of direction.
Solution.(a) By definition,ϕ 2 =ϕ 1 ◦ψ. Differentiating the arc length condition
forγ◦ϕ 2, we have:

Applying the Chain Rule to the composition (γ◦ϕ 1)◦ψ:

Sinceγ◦ϕ 1 is also an arc length reparametrization, we know that∥(γ◦
ϕ1)′(v)∥= 1 for allv. Therefore, the equation reduces to:
1 = 1· |ψ ′(u)|=⇒ |ψ ′(u)|= 1.
(b) From the result proved in class,|ψ ′(u)|= 1 implies thatψ(u) =u+Cor

relationϕ 2 =ϕ 1 ◦ψ, we find:
•Ifψ(u) =u+C, thenϕ 2(u) =ϕ 1(u+C). This corresponds to a translation
in the parameter (a shift in the starting “time”).
•Ifψ(u) =−u+C, thenϕ 2(u) =ϕ 1(−u+C). This corresponds to a
translation followed by a reversal of the direction in which the curve is
traversed.
In either case, the image of the curve is identical, and the speed remains con-
stant at 1, confirming that the arc length parameter is unique up to these
transformations.

r(the rolling circle) as it rolls without slipping around the exterior of a fixed circle
of radiusR(the base circle). Assume the base circle is centered at the origin and
the rolling point starts at (R,0).

m1 =m 2. Thus all elements inAare distinct. By the Bolzano-Weierstrass
property (or simply the fact thatA∩[0,1] is infinite), for anyϵ >0, there exist
distinctx, y∈Asuch that|x−y|< ϵ. Letδ=x−y∈A. Sinceδ̸= 0, the set
{nδ:n∈Z} ⊂Aconsists of points spaced by less thanϵ. By the Archimedean
property, any interval of lengthϵmust contain at least one suchnδ. ThusA
is dense inR, and the contact angles are dense in [0,2π].
8

Problem Sheet 7
(solutions)
(a) (Addition formulas.) Fixy∈Rand define
eC(x) :=C(x)C(y)−S(x)S(y), eS(x) :=S(x)C(y) +C(x)S(y).
Show that eS′ = eCand eC ′ =− eS. Compute eC(0) and eS(0), and use uniqueness
to deduce
C(x+y) =C(x)C(y)−S(x)S(y), S(x+y) =S(x)C(y) +C(x)S(y)
for allx, y∈R.
(b) (A first positive zero ofC.) Using only the properties already proved, show
that there existsr >0 such thatC(r) = 0.
Hint:Recall thatC ′′ =−C,C(0) = 1,C ′(0) = 0, andC(x) 2 +S(x) 2 = 1 for
allx∈R. Argue by contradiction: what would happen ifCnever vanished on
(0,∞)?
(c) (Definition ofπ.) Letr >0 be the smallest such thatC(r) = 0 (which exists
by the previous item). Defineπas the number:
π:= 2r.
wherer >0 is given by the previous item. ThereforeC(π/2) = 0 andS(π/2) =
±1. Prove thatS(π/2) is in fact positive, therefore equal to 1.
(d) (Values atπ,2π.) Use the addition formulas to compute
C(π), S(π), C(2π), S(2π).
(e) (Periodicity.) Using the addition formulas and your values in (d), prove that
C(x+ 2π) =C(x), S(x+ 2π) =S(x) for allx∈R.
Solution.(a) Fixy∈Rand define
eC(x) :=C(x)C(y)−S(x)S(y), eS(x) :=S(x)C(y) +C(x)S(y).
UsingS ′(x) =C(x) andC ′(x) =−S(x), we compute
eS′(x) =S ′(x)C(y) +C ′(x)S(y) =C(x)C(y)−S(x)S(y) = eC(x),
eC ′(x) =C ′(x)C(y)−S ′(x)S(y) = (−S(x))C(y)−C(x)S(y) =− eS(x).
Also, usingC(0) = 1 andS(0) = 0,
eC(0) =C(0)C(y)−S(0)S(y) =C(y), eS(0) =S(0)C(y)+C(0)S(y) =S(y).
Now defineF(x) :=C(x+y) andG(x) :=S(x+y). Then
G′(x) =S ′(x+y) =C(x+y) =F(x), F ′(x) =C ′(x+y) =−S(x+y) =−G(x),
andF(0) =C(y),G(0) =S(y). By uniqueness (from the previous exercise,
with initial time 0),
C(x+y) =F(x) = eC(x) =C(x)C(y)−S(x)S(y),
S(x+y) =G(x) = eS(x) =S(x)C(y) +C(x)S(y),
for allx, y∈R.

some positive point.

Now apply (a) withx=y=π:

4. (Inverse Trigonometric Functions)
(a) Prove that the following functions are well-defined:
•Define arcsin : [−1,1]→[−π/2, π/2] to be the inverse of sin restricted to
[−π/2, π/2].
•Define arctan :R→(−π/2, π/2) to be the inverse of tan on (−π/2, π/2).
•Define arccos : [−1,1]→[0, π] to be the inverse of cos restricted to [0, π].
Use the inverse function theorem to prove:
i. arcsin is differentiable on (−1,1) and
(arcsinx) ′ = 1√
1−x 2 (x∈(−1,1)).
ii. arctan is differentiable onRand

1 +x 2 (x∈R).
iii. arccos is differentiable on (−1,1) and
(arccosx) ′ =− 1√
1−x 2 (x∈(−1,1)).
Solution.(a)Well-definedness.
•On (−π/2, π/2) we have (sinx) ′ = cosx >0 (since cosx̸= 0 there and
cos 0 = 1 withπ/2 the first positive zero), hence sin is strictly increas-
ing on [−π/2, π/2]. Also sin(−π/2) =−1 and sin(π/2) = 1. Therefore
sin| [−π/2,π/2] is a bijection onto [−1,1], so its inverse
arcsin : [−1,1]→[−π/2, π/2]
is well-defined.
•On (−π/2, π/2) we haveT ′(x) = 1 +T(x) 2 >0, hence tan is strictly
increasing on (−π/2, π/2), so it is injective there. Moreover tanx=
sinx/cosxand cosx→0 + asx→π/2 − while sinx→1, hence tanx→
+∞asx→π/2 −. Similarly tanx→ −∞asx→ −π/2 +. Thus tan maps
(−π/2, π/2) bijectively ontoR, and its inverse
arctan :R→(−π/2, π/2)
is well-defined.
•On (0, π) we have (cosx) ′ =−sinx <0 (since sinx >0 forx∈(0, π)),
hence cos is strictly decreasing on [0, π]. Also cos 0 = 1 and cosπ=−1.
Therefore cos| [0,π] is a bijection onto [−1,1], so its inverse
arccos : [−1,1]→[0, π]
is well-defined.

6. Assumef n →funiformly andg n →guniformly on [a, b]. Prove thatf ngn →f g
uniformly on [a, b]. (Hint: add and subtractf ng.)
Solution.Sincef n →fandg n →guniformly on [a, b], the functionsf, gare
bounded on [a, b], and moreover (f n) and (g n) are uniformly bounded. Indeed,
chooseN 0 such that
∥fn −f∥ ∞ ≤1,∥g n −g∥ ∞ ≤1 (n≥N 0),
where∥h∥ ∞ := supx∈[a,b] |h(x)|. Then forn≥N 0,
∥fn∥∞ ≤ ∥f∥ ∞ + 1,∥g n∥∞ ≤ ∥g∥ ∞ + 1.
Now fixn≥N 0 and add/subtractf ng:
fngn −f g=f n(gn −g) + (f n −f)g.
Hence, for allx∈[a, b],
|fn(x)gn(x)−f(x)g(x)| ≤ |f n(x)| |gn(x)−g(x)|+|g(x)| |f n(x)−f(x)|.
Taking suprema over [a, b] gives
∥fngn−f g∥∞ ≤ ∥f n∥∞ ∥gn−g∥∞+∥g∥∞ ∥fn−f∥ ∞ ≤(∥f∥ ∞+1)∥gn−g∥∞+∥g∥∞∥fn−f∥ ∞.
Asn→ ∞, both∥g n −g∥ ∞ →0 and∥f n −f∥ ∞ →0, so the right-hand side tends
to 0. Therefore
∥fngn −f g∥ ∞ − →0,
i.e.f ngn →f guniformly on [a, b].

documents.title: Revision Exercises SOLUTIONS(1)
documents.text:
Analysis I - Revision Exercises
(this copy includes HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
Last Updated: January 29, 2026
HOW TO USE THIS PRACTICE SHEET
Step 1. Choose a problem (you do not have to follow any particular order, but you might
find it helpful that the problems are organised by sections).
Step 2. Without looking at the HINT or the SOLUTION, make an honest attempt at
writing down a careful formal answer for the problem in final form, i.e. as in an exam.
Step 3. If you do not have an idea on how to start, then read the HINT and try to write
down your formal answer again.
Step 4. Finally, compare your formal answer with the SOLUTION (keep in mind that
there are many correct ways of answering the same question and your formal answer might
be correct while differing from the one in the SOLUTION).
If you find any typos or mistakes, please let me know via the Ed Forum.
Basic Exercises
1. Let A ⊂ R be a non-empty bounded set. Prove the following fundamental properties
of the supremum:
(a) There exists a ∈ R, such that a = sup A.
(b) Given δ > 0, there exists x ∈ A such that a − δ < x ≤ a.
(c) There exists a sequence xn ⊂ A, such that xn → a.
Hint. Does (a) remind you to an axiom? For (b), can a − δ be an upper bound for
A? Finally, for (c) note that part (b) is true for any positive δ of your choice. Use
that freedom to construct a sequence.
Solution.
(a) There is nothing to prove as this is the statement of the supremum axiom. It
holds because A is non-empty and bounded from above.
1
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
(b) If δ > 0, then a − δ < a . Since a is the smallest upper bound for A, it follows
that a − δ cannot be an upper bound. In particular, there must be an x ∈ A
such that a − δ < x .
(c) Given n ∈ N, let xn be the element of A given by part (b) when applied for
δ = 1/n. Applying (b) for all natural numbers we define a sequence {xn}n∈N
satisfying a − 1/n < x n ≤ a. Therefore xn → a.
2. Show that if a sequence is convergent, then any of its subsequences must converge
to the same limit.
Hint. Have you checked the precise definition of a subsequence?
Solution. yn is a subsequence of a sequence xn if there exists σ : N → N strictly
monotone such that yn = xσ(n). Let L be the limit of xn. Then, given ε > 0, there
exists N1 ∈ N such that n ≥ N1 implies |xn − L| < ε . Now, since σ is strictly
monotone, there exists an N ∈ N such that n > N implies σ(n) > N1. In particular,
n > N implies |yn − L| = |xσ(n) − L| < ε.
3. Show that the following two statements are equivalent:
Statement 1: “∀ε > 0, ∃δ > 0, such that |f(x) − L| < ε, whenever 0 < |x − a| < δ.”
Statement 2: “f(xn) → L for all sequences xn such that xn → a and xn ̸= a.”
Hint. For S1 = ⇒ S2 take an arbitrary sequence converging to a. Then combine
the definition of convergence of a sequence with statement one. To see S2 = ⇒ S1
is, perhaps, less direct. This is an important type of argument you must get yourself
familiar with. One argues by contradiction. Assume that S1 does not hold and use
its negation to construct a sequence xn converging to a, such that f(xn) does not
converge to L, contradicting S2.
Solution.
S1 = ⇒ S2: Assume S1 and xn → a with xn ̸= a. Fix ε > 0 and let δ > 0 be
the one given by S1. By the definition of convergence there exists N ∈ N such
that 0 < |xn − a| < δ whenever n > N . In combination with S1, we get that
|f(xn) − L| < ε whenever n ≥ N for some N depending on ε.
S2 = ⇒ S1: Assume S2 as well as the negation of S1 and reach a contradiction.
The negation of S1 is the statement:
“∃ε0 > 0 such that, ∀δ > 0, there exists x, which depends on δ, that satisfies
0 < |x − a| < δ and |f(x) − L| > ε0.”
Now, we can apply this statement for any δ > 0, in particular we can do it for all the
numbers of the form δ = 1/n, where n ∈ N. Note that we are effectively applying
the statement infinitely many times. This produces a sequence xn, such that 0 <
|xn − a| < 1/n, so xn → a. On the other hand, by construction, |f(xn) − L| > ε 0.
Therefore, f(xn) ̸→ L, contradicting S2.
2
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
4. Show that the following two statements are equivalent:
Statement 1: “∀ε > 0, ∃R > 0, such that |f(x) − L| < ε, whenever x > R .”
Statement 2: “f(xn) → L for all sequences xn such that xn → +∞.”
Hint. This is similar to the previous problem, and I recommend you do that one
first. Notice how the role of |x − a| < δ is replaced by x > R . Informally, we can
interpret the first as “ x is close to a” and the second as “ x is arbitrarily large, or
close to +∞”. The strategy of the proof is exactly the same as before in each case.
Solution.
S1 = ⇒ S2: Assume S1 and xn → +∞. Fix ε > 0 and let R > 0 be the one given
by S1. By the definition of convergence (at infinity) there exists N ∈ N, depending
on R, such that xn > R whenever n > N . In combination with S1, we get that
|f(xn) − L| < ε whenever n ≥ N for some N depending on ε, which is S2.
S2 = ⇒ S1: Assume S2 as well as the negation of S1 and reach a contradiction.
The negation of S1 is the statement:
“∃ε0 > 0 such that ∀R > 0, there exists x, which depends on R, that satisfies x > R
and |f(x) − L| > ε0.”
Now, we can apply this statement for any R > 0, in particular we can do it for
all the numbers of the form R = n, where n ∈ N. Note that we are effectively
applying the statement infinitely many times. This produces a sequence xn, such
that xn ≥ n, so xn → +∞. On the other hand, by construction, |f(xn) − L| > ε 0.
Therefore, f(xn) ̸→ L, contradicting S2.
5. Let σ : N → N be an injection. Show that if lim n→∞ xn = L then limn→∞ xσ(n) = L
(this is to say that any reordering of a subsequence of a convergent sequence is also
convergent and converges to the same limit).
Hint. Given N ∈ N, for how many values of n can the statement σ(n) ≤ N be true?
Solution. Let xn → L, i.e. given ε > 0 there exists an N = N(ε) > 0 such that
n > N =⇒ | xn − L| < ε . Now note that since σ is an injection, the statement
σ(n) < N is true for at most finitely many n’s. Therefore, there exists N2 such that
n > N 2 =⇒ σ(n) > N =⇒ | xσ(n) − L| < ε. This is the same as saying xσ(n) → L
6. Let A be a nonempty subset of the Real line. Suppose that A is both open and
closed. Prove that A = R.1
1A set X with this property is called connected, i.e, “the only non-empty subset of X which is both
open and closed is X itself”. In the the real line, connectedness is, for most relevant applications,
encapsulated in the Intermediate Value Theorem. However, when you study higher dimensions it will
become an important concept on its own.
3
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
Hint. Say there exists t ∈ R \ A. Then, at least one of the sets A− = A ∩ (−∞, t) =
A ∩ (−∞, t] or A+ = A ∩ (t, +∞) = A ∩ [t, +∞) is not empty.
Solution. Assume the t in the hint exists. Without loss of generality, assume that
A− is non-empty. This set is bounded from above by t, which by assumption is
not in the set. By the axiom of the supremum there exists x = sup A. Since A is
closed, it follows that x ∈ A. However, since A is open, there exists δ > 0 such that
(x − δ, x + δ) ⊂ A. Therefore, sup A ≤ x < x + δ/2 and x + δ/2 ∈ A. Which is a
contradiction. Therefore, there does not exist t ∈ R \ A.
7. Prove that Thomae’s function:
f(x) =
(
1
q , x = p
q rational in lower terms and q > 0
0, x irrational
has the following helpful property: given any ε > 0 and a < b ∈ R, the set A =
{x ∈ [a, b] : f(x) ≥ ε} is finite.
Hint. What can you say about p and q if a ≤ p/q ≤ b and ε ≤ 1/q?
Solution. Since q is an integer, the equation 0 < q ≤ 1/ε implies that there are at
most finitely many possibilities for q. Similarly, the equation aq ≤ p ≤ bq implies
there are at most finitely many possibilities for p as well.
Limits
8. Show that lim x→a f(x) = L if and only if lim h→0 f(a + h) = L.
Hint. Defining g(h) := f(a + h) might help you think in an organised way. The
second statement is then g(h) → L as h → 0.
Solution.
( =⇒ ): Let g be the function defined in the hint. The statement “lim h→0 g(h) =
L” can be translated into “given ε > 0, ∃δ > 0 such that 0 < |h| < δ implies
|g(h) − L| < ε ”. Once again, we can translate this one into a statement involving
f, i.e., “given ε > 0, ∃δ > 0 such that 0 < |h| < δ implies |f(a + h) − L| < ε”. Now,
denote x = a + h and note that |x − a| = |h|. Therefore, the statements 0 < |h| < δ
are equivalent 0 < |x − a| < δ. So, once more, we can translate our statement about
f into “given ε > 0, ∃δ > 0 such that 0 < |x − a| < δ implies |f(x) − L| < ε”. This
concludes the proof of ( =⇒ ). The other direction is left for you to complete.
9. Show that if lim x→a f(x) = L > 0, then, for any ε ∈ (0, 1), there exists a δ > 0 such
that x ∈ (a − δ, a + δ) \ {a} implies that f(x) > εL.
4
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
Hint. Think of an interval centered at L that does not intersect εL, then apply the
definition of limit to that interval.
Solution. Since L > 0 and 0 < ε < 1, the interval ( εL, 2L) contains the point L.
Therefore, lim x→a f(x) = L > 0, implies that there exists a δ > 0 such that f(x)
is in the interval ( εL, 2L) whenever 0 < |x − a| < δ . Which is what we wanted to
prove.
10. Show that if lim x→a f(x) = L ̸= 0, then lim x→a
1
f(x) = 1
L.
Hint. You will need to show that 0 < |f(x)| < M for all values of x sufficiently near
a and for some fixed M, for example M = 2|L| would work.
Solution. Without loss of generality we can assume that L > 0 (the case L < 0 is
analogous). By the definition of limit, there exists δ1 > 0, such that 0 < |x − a| < δ1
implies |f(x)| ∈ ( 1
2 |L|, 2|L|). For these values of x it follows that
    
1
f(x) − 1
L
     = |L − f(x)|
|L||f(x)| ≤ 2|L − f(x)|
L2 .
Now, fix ε > 0. Using the definition of limit once more, there exists δ2 > 0, such
that |L − f(x)| < ε L2
2 as long as 0 < |x − a| < δ 2. Choosing δ = min {δ1, δ2} we
obtain that     
1
f(x) − 1
L
     ≤ ε
as long as 0 < |x − a| < δ.
11. Prove that the following limits do not exist:
• limn→∞(−1)n
• limx→0
1
x
• limx→0 sin(1/x)
• limx→∞ sin(x).
Hint. Remember that you can use the uniqueness of limit and/or the sequential
definition of limit as you need. In all these cases you do not need to argue using ε
or δ.
Solution. For xn = (−1)n, the subsequences x2n = 1 and x2n+1 = −1 are constant
and different, so they converge to different limits. This implies the limit of xn itself
does not exist, as otherwise, the limits of the subsequence would coincide.
For f(x) = 1/x consider xn = 1/n → 0 since the sequence f(1/n) = n is divergent,
the limit of f as x tend to 0 does not exist.
For f(x) = sin(1/x) take xn = 2
nπ, then xn → 0 but f(xn) = (−1)n, which does not
have a limit.
Finally, for f(x) = sin(x) take x = nπ
2 , then xn → ∞ but its image is the periodic
sequence 0, 1, 0, −1, 0, 1, 0, −1, ..., which does not have a limit.
5
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
12. Assume f : R → R has the following property: for each open interval I ⊂ R and
every ε > 0 there are at most finitely many points in the set {x ∈ I, |f(x)| ≥ ε}.
Prove that for all a ∈ R, limx→a f(x) = 0. Does it follow that f is continuous?
Hint. Given a and ε > 0 can we always find I such that x ∈ I \ { a} implies
|f(x)| < ε?
Solution. Fix ε > 0 and let I be an open interval containing a. By hypothesis there
are at most finitely many points x ∈ I \ {a} for which |f(x)| ≥ ε, since I \ {a} ⊂ I.
Therefore, there exists δ > 0 such that ( a − δ, a + δ) \ {a} contains no point x with
|f(x)| ≥ ε. It follows that x ∈ (a − δ, a + δ) implies |f(x)| < ε.
In general, lim x→a f(x) = 0 does not imply that f is continuous at a. This would
only be the case if f(a) = 0.
13. Let f : [a, b] → R a non-decreasing function. Show that at every point in the interior
the one-sided limits of f exist and satisfy lim x→x−
0
f(x) ≤ f(x0) ≤ limx→x+
0
f(x).
Hint. Let yn = x0 − 1/n. Show that lim n→∞ f(yn) = ℓ ≤ f(x0) exists. Then
show that any other sequence xn → x−
0 satisfies the same, i.e. lim n→∞ f(xn) = ℓ.
Conclude that lim x→x−
0
f(x) = ℓ. Similarly, you can show lim x→x+
0
f(x) = L. for
some L ≥ f(x0)
Solution. Let yn = x0 −1/n. Since yn is an increasing sequence, by the monotonicity
of f it follows that f(yn) is non-decreasing. Similarly, the monotonicity implies
f(yn) ≤ f(b) so f(yn) is also bounded from above. It follows that ℓ = limn→∞ f(yn)
exists. Clearly, since f(yn) ≤ f(x0) for all n, we must have ℓ ≤ f(x0). Now, we
claim that lim x→x−
0
f(x) = ℓ. In fact, fix ε > 0. Now, let N ∈ N be such that
ℓ − f(yn) = |ℓ − f(yn)| ≤ ε for n ≥ N. Then, by monotonicity and the formula in
the previous sentence, x0 − 1/N < x < x 0 implies that ℓ − ε ≤ f(yN) ≤ f(x) ≤ ℓ.
Therefore, choosing δ = 1/N we get x0 − δ < x < x 0 implies |f(x) − ℓ| < ε, which is
what we wanted to prove. Note that we can handle the limit from above in exactly
the same fashion, but with reverse inequalities.
Continuity
14. Show that if f is continuous at a, and f(a) ̸= 0, there exists a small neighborhood
around a where f is bounded away from 0.
Hint. Find an open interval containing f(a) and that is away from 0, and apply any
of the equivalent definitions of continuity.
Solution. Without loss of generality assume f(a) > 0. Note that the point f(a) is
contained in the interval (1
2 f(a), 2f(a)). By the definition of continuity, there exists
δ > 0 such that x ∈ (a − δ, a + δ) implies that f(x) ∈ ( 1
2 f(a), 2f(a)). This is what
we wanted to show.
6
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
15. Show that the function f(x) = |x| is continuous for all x ∈ R.
Hint. The function |x| is defined by cases, i.e. if x ≥ 0 or if x ≤ 0. Consider all the
possible cases when you are trying to prove continuity.
Solution. Fix ε > 0 and δ > 0, where δ is set to be chosen later. Assume that
|x − y| < δ . From the triangle inequality ||x| − | y|| = |x − y| < δ , it follows we
choose δ := ε.
16. Show that f(x) = √x is continuous for all x ∈ [0, +∞).
Hint. This is almost an arithmetic exercise. Prove the inequalities you want by
playing around with standard algebraic identities.
Solution. We will prove first that f(x) is continuous at a > 0 and deal with the case
a = 0 at the end. So, for now let a > 0 and assume that |x − a| < δ for some δ to
be determined later. Consider the expression
f(x) − f(a) = √x − √a
= x − a√x + √a
Note that if δ < a
2, then x > a
2 and √x >
√a√
2. In this case, it follows that
|f(x) − f(a)| ≤ 1
(1 + 1√
2)√a|x − a| < δ
(1 + 1√
2)√a .
Since we want this to be less than ε > 0 and remembering that we are already using
the condition δ < a
2, it follows we get the desired inequality as long as we choose
some δ < min{ε(1 + 1√
2)√a, a/2}. Finally, for a = 0, note that 0 ≤ x < δ gives
|√x − 0| = √x < δ as long as 0 < δ < ε 2.
The Intermediate Value Theorem
17. Let f : [0 , 1] → [0, 1] be continuous everywhere on [0 , 1]. Show that there exists
t ∈ [0, 1] such that f(t) = t.
Hint. Consider the function g(t) = f(t) − t. Does it satisfy the hypothesis of the
Intermediate Value Theorem?
Solution. Let g(t) = f(t) − t. Note that the goal is to find t such that g(t) = 0. This
function is continuous on [0, 1] because it is the sum of two continuous functions. If
g(0) = 0 or g(1) = 0, then we are done. Otherwise, we must have, by hypothesis,
that g(0) > 0 > g (1). In this case, the Intermediate Value Theorem implies the
existence of t ∈ (0, 1) such that g(t) = 0, which is what we wanted to show.
7
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )

19. (a) Give an example of a function f which is not continuous but satisfies the
Intermediate Value Property.
(b) (*) Suppose that f satisfies the Intermediate Value Property and it attains
each value a finite number of times. Prove that f is continuous.
(c) Show with an example that those hypotheses are still not good enough as a
good definition of continuity.
Hint. If you need any help for (a) remember that derivatives always satisfy the
Intermediate Value Theorem even if they are not continuous. For (b) show that for
each ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that f(t) < f (a) + ε for all t ∈ (a − δ, a].
Solution. (a) Take f(x) = sin(1/x) for x ̸= 0 and f(0) = 0. Take a < b ∈ R. First
note that f satisfies the Intermediate Value Theorem on the interval [ a, b],
when 0 < a < b or a < b < 0, because it is continuous on each open half line.
Therefore, we just need to check that the property holds over intervals of the
form [a, b] with a ≤ 0 ≤ b. In fact, in this case f([a, b]) = [−1, 1], which is the
entire range of the function. This implies the desired property.

Repeating the argument three more times, but also using f(a)−ε and [a, a+δ).

f(a) + ε for all t ∈ (a − δ, a + δ).
(c) Take f(x) = x sin(1/x). It attains f(x) = 0 infinitely many times around x = 0
and it is continuous.

The Extreme Value Theorem
21. Given an example of a function f : (a, b) → R which is continuous and bounded but
does not satisfy the extreme value property. What fails?
Hint. Try a strictly monotone function, for example.
Solution. The identity function f(x) = x works, since sup {f(x) : x ∈ (a, b)} = b
which is never attained.
22. Show that if X ⊂ R is a compact set (not necessarily a closed interval) and f : X →
R is continuous on X, then f attains a maximum on X.
Hint. Let s = sup f(X), note that s could potentially be + ∞. Is there a sequence
xn ∈ X such that f(xk) → s? Is the sequence xn convergent? If not, what can you
replace it by a convergent one?
Solution. By the definition of supremum there exists a sequence xn ∈ X such that
f(xk) → s. Note that xn itself might not converge. However, since X is compact,
xn does have a convergent subsequence. To simplify the notation we can denote the
subsequence also as xn. Notice that even after this change we still have f(xk) → s.
Moreover, the limit limn→∞ xn = x is contained in X because X is compact. Finally,
by continuity f(xn) → f(x). Therefore, s = f(x) and f attains its maximum at x.
23. Show that if f : (a, b) → R is continuous on (a, b) and limx→a+ f(x) = limx→b− f(x) =
+∞ then f attains its minimum on ( a, b).
Hint. By assumption the values of f are arbitrarily large, if we get close enough to
a and b. Formalise this observation and use it to argue that to find the minimum
of f we just need to look at a closed interval contained inside of [ a, b].
Solution. Pick any x0 ∈ (a, b). By assumption, there exists δ > 0 such that f(x) >
f(x0) on ( a, a + δ) and ( b − δ, b). Therefore,
inf f((a, b)) = inf f([a + δ/2, b − δ/2]).
By the extreme value property, applied to f on [ a + δ/2, b − δ/2] there exists a
t ∈ [a + δ/2, b − δ/2] attaining this minimum, which is what we wanted to show.
10
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
24. Let p(x) be a polynomial. Show that |p(x)| attains a minimum in R.
Hint. If p is constant the result is trivial. If p is not constant, what can you say
about the limits lim x→±∞ |p(x)|?
Solution. If p has degree 0 then|p(x)| is a constant function at it attains its minimum
at every point. Otherwise, we must have lim x→±∞ |p(x)| = +∞. Now, we can argue
as in the previous problem:
Pick any x0 ∈ R. By assumption, there exists R > 0 such that f(x) > f (x0) for
|x| > R. Therefore,
inf f(R) = inf f([−R − 1, R + 1]).
By the extreme value property, applied to f on [ −R − 1, R + 1] there exists a
t ∈ [−R − 1, R + 1] attaining this minimum, which is what we wanted to show.
25. Let A ⊂ R be a non-empty closed set. Show that for any x ∈ R there exists a point
a ∈ A which is closest to x.
Hint. How do we measure the distance between two points x and y? By the number
|x − y|. The absolute value is a continuous function. So can we take the infimum of
all the distances for which y ∈ A?
Solution. Let D = {|a − x| : a ∈ A}. Since D is bounded from below by 0 and
non-empty, there exists d = inf(D). By the definition of infimum, there also exists a
sequence an ∈ A such that |an − x| → d. The sequence |an − x| is bounded, because
it is convergent. Therefore, an itself is bounded and, by Bolzano-Weierstrass, we
can replace it by a convergent subsequence. To simplify the notation, we denote the
subsequence also by an. Finally, since an ∈ A and A is closed its limit limn→∞ an = a
is contained in A. Finally, by continuity of | · |, |a − x| = limn→∞ |an − x| = d, so
a is a point in A which is closest to x. (Remark: in this case we write dist( x, A) =
d = |x − a|).
26. Let f be continuous everywhere on its domain. Prove that, in all the cases below,
the function attains its minimum.
• f : [a, b] → R.
• f : (a, b] → R and limx→a+ f(x) ≥ f(x0) for some x0 ∈ (a, b].
Hint. This is a very, very short exercise. The first case is direct. The second case
is almost direct.
Solution. For the first case we can directly apply the extreme value theorem, since we
are assuming that f is continuous on [a, b]. For the second case, ifL = limx→a+ f(x) >
f(x0), then by the definition of a limit, there exists a small interval ( a, a + δ) where
f(x) > f (x0). The minimum of f on (a, b] must then lie in the compact set [a+ δ, b].
By EVT, this minimum exists. If L = f(x0), a similar argument or an extension
argument works.
11

Uniform Continuity
27. Let f : X → R be continuous on X. Show that:
(a) If X = [a, b], then f is uniformly continuous on X.
(b) If X is compact, then f is uniformly continuous on X.
Hint. The proof in both cases is the same. Proceed by contradiction. Prove that
negating the statement implies the existence of ε > 0 and convergent sequences
xn, yn with |xn − yn| → 0 and |f(xn) − f(yn)| > ε. Then reach a contradiction.
Solution. We just deal with the compact case. By definition, f is uniformly contin-
uous on X means “∀ε > 0, ∃δ > 0 such that |f(x)−f(y)| < ε whenever |x−y| < δ”.
That is what we want to prove. We will proceed by contradiction so we must negate
this statement. In other words, we will assume “ ∃ε0 > 0 such that ∀δ > 0 there
are x, y ∈ X, satisfying both |f(x) − f(y)| > ε 0 and |x − y| < δ ”. We can apply
this assumption for all numbers of the form δ = 1 /n, where n ∈ N. Let xn and
yn be the points produced by the statement in each case, i.e. |xn − yn| → 0 and
|f(xn) − f(yn)| > ε0. Now, the sequences xn and yn might not be convergent. How-
ever, since X is compact, i.e. closed and bounded, we can replace them by conver-
gent subsequences. (We must do this carefully, first we select a subsequenceynk that
converges to y ∈ X. Now, from the sequence xnk we select a subsequence xnkℓ
that
converges to x ∈ X. The two subsequences we keep in the end are xnkℓ
and ynkℓ
). To
simplify notation we denote them again byyn and xn. Now, from |yn−xn| → 0 it fol-
lows that x = y. Therefore, by continuity limn→∞ |f(xn)−f(yn)| = |f(x)−f(y)| = 0,
contradicting the inequality |f(xn) − f(yn)| > ε0.

30. Prove that a periodic continuous function f : R → R is uniformly continuous on R.
Hint. Let T > 0 be the period of f. Then, the behaviour of f is determined by
what happens in the interval [0 , T]. Prove that if δ > 0 is small enough we can
translate any two points satisfying |x − y| < δ by the same multiple of T so that
the resulting points lie in [0 , 2T ]. Then use the fact that continuous functions are
uniformly continuous in closed intervals.
Solution. Let T > 0 be the period of f. Fix ε > 0. Choose δ ∈ (0, T /2) to be
determined later, we will prove that we can choose δ depending on ε > 0 so that
the inequality in the definition of uniform continuity holds. Take x, y ∈ R such
that |x − y| < δ . Since, by assumption, δ < T / 2, there exists m ∈ Z such that
x′ = x + mT and y′ = y + mT both belong to the interval [0 , 2T ]. Since f is
uniformly continuous on [0 , 2T ], there exists a δ1 such that |x′ − y′| < δ 1 implies
|f(x′) − f(y′)| < ε. Finally, let δ = min(δ1, T /2), then |x − y| < δ =⇒ | x′ − y′| < δ,
this implies ε > |f(x′) − f(y′)| = |f(x) − f(y)| since f(x′) = f(x) and f(y) = f(y′)
by periodicity.

it is bounded on ( a, b)

Then, note that uniform continuity implies that the intervals involved can be chosen

Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
Open, Closed and Compact Sets
32. Show that an infinite union of open sets must be open. Is the same statement true
for closed sets?
Hint. The argument for open sets is very short. For the case of closed set construct
a counter example.
Solution. Let {Ai}i∈I be a (possibly infinite) family of open sets. If x ∈ ∪ i∈IAi,
then there exists i ∈ I such that x ∈ Ai. Since Ai is itself open, there exists an open
interval J such that x ∈ J ⊂ Ai. Therefore, x ∈ J ⊂ ∪ i∈IAi, which implies that the
union is itself open.
The statement is not true for closed set. For example, the union∪n≥3[1/n, 1−1/n] =
(0, 1) is not closed.
33. Show that if A is not an open set then there exists a ∈ A and a sequence xn ∈ R \ A
such that xn → a.
Hint. Carefully write down the negation of being open and apply it for a sequence
of δ’s going to 0.
Solution. Assume A is not open, i.e. there is a ∈ A such that ∀δ > 0 there exists
x ∈ (R \ A) ∩ (a − δ, a + δ). We can apply this statement infinitely many times for
δ = 1/n with n ∈ N. In this way we obtain a sequencexn ∈ R\A with |xn−a| < 1/n.
Therefore, xn ∈ R \ A and xn → a.
34. Show that a set B ⊂ R is closed if and only if for each sequence xn ⊂ B which is
convergent, we have limn→∞ xn ∈ B
Hint. For ⇒ use the fact that a convergent sequence enters any interval containing
its limit. For ⇐ use the negation of R \ B being open infinitely many times in order
to construct a sequence that contradicts the assumption.
Solution.
(⇒): It is enough to show that no x ∈ R \ B can be the limit of a sequence xn ∈ B.
Since B is closed, it follows that R \ B is open. Therefore, ∃ε0 > 0 such that
(x − ε0, x + ε0) ⊂ R \ B. This implies that x cannot be the limit of a sequence
xn ∈ B.
(⇐): We proceed by contradiction. Assume R\B is not open, i.e. there is x ∈ R\B
such that ∀δ > 0 there exists b ∈ B ∩ (x − δ, x + δ). We can apply this statement
infinitely many times for δ = 1 /n with n ∈ N. In this way we obtain a sequence
bn ∈ B with |bn − x| < 1/n. Therefore, bn ∈ B and bn → x /∈ B, which is a
contradiction.
14
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
35. Show that the sets Q and R \ Q are neither open nor closed.
Hint. Use the density of both sets in R and the fact that they are complementary
sets.
Solution. Since Q and R \ Q are dense in R, it follows that every open interval
contains points of Q and R \ Q. Since any interval J around q ∈ Q contains points
of R \ Q, J is not contained in Q, so Q is not open. Since they are complementary,
it follows that they are also not closed.
The Derivative

37. Show with an example that f ′(x0) = 0 does not imply that x0 is a local maximum
or local minimum for f.
Hint. Try f(x) = x3.
Solution. Let f(x) = x3, then f ′(0) = 0 but f(0) = 0 is neither a local maximum
or a local minimum, as the function f is strictly increasing.
38. Let f and g be differentiable functions:
(a) Show that if f(a) = g(a) and f ′(x) ≤ g′(x) for all x > a , then f(x) ≤ g(x) on
[a, +∞).
(b) Is the converse true?
(c) Show with examples that both hypothesis f(a) = g(a) and f ′(x) ≤ g′(x) for
all x > a are necessary

Hint. Study the difference g − f. What can you say about its derivative?
Solution. (a) The function h(x) = g(x) − f(x) satisfies h(a) = 0 and h′(x) ≥ 0 for
all x > a . Fix x ∈ (a, +∞), by the Mean Value Theorem, there exists t ∈ (a, x)
such that h(x) = h′(t)(x − a) ≥ 0. It follows that g(x) ≥ h(x).
(b) The converse is not true. For example, take a = 0, f(x) = sin(1 /x)x2 and

40. Classify all the functions f : R → R which are bounded and convex.
Hint. There is only one type of function. Use the previous problem to show that if
there are a < b such that Q(a, b) = f(b)−f(a)
b−a ̸= 0, then f must be unbounded.
16

Solution. Let us assume that f is not constant, i.e. there are a < b ∈ R such that
Q(a, b) ̸= 0. Note that f(t) is convex if and only if f(−t) is convex. Similarly, f(t)
is convex if and only if f(t + c) +d is convex, for any fixed c, d ∈ R. In other words,
translations and reflections along the vertical axis, preserve convexity. Therefore,
without loss of generality we can assume 0 = a < b , f(0) = 0 and Q(a, b) > 0.
Then, 0 < Q(0, b) ≤ Q(nb, (n + 1)b), for all n = 0, 1, 2, . . .. This gives us
f((n + 1)b) − f(nb) ≥ bQ(0, b) > 0.
Finally, adding these inequalities from n = 0 to n = N, we obtain
f((N + 1)b) ≥ (N + 1)bQ(0, b),
which converges to +∞ as N → +∞. Therefore, if f is both convex and bounded,
we must have Q(a, b) = 0 for all a < b ∈ R, which implies that f must be a constant
function.
41. Show that if f : [a, b] → R is twice differentiable on ( a, b) and convex in [ a, b] then
f ′′(x) ≥ 0 for all x ∈ (a, b).
Hint. You can argue by contradiction. What would happen if f ′′(p) < 0 for some
p ∈ (a, b)?

follows f ′(p) > f ′(p + ε), for all ε > 0 sufficiently small. On the other hand, since
f is convex, we have

theorem, we obtain

42. Find two convex functions such that f(x) = g(x) if and only x is an integer.

43. (a) Prove that if f and g are convex and f is increasing, then f ◦ g is convex.
(b) Give an example where g ◦ f is not convex (in particular, convexity is not
preserved by function composition).
(c) Suppose that f and g are twice differentiable. Give another proof of the result
of part (a) by considering second derivatives.
Hint. For (a) remember that a function h is convex on an interval I, if and only if
for any x, y ∈ I, we have h(tx + (1− t)y) < th(x) + (1− t)h(y), for all t ∈ (0, 1). For
(b), g cannot be increasing because of part (a). For (c) just compute the formula
for the second derivative of f ◦ g.
Solution.
(a) Let x, y. Since g is convex, we have
g(tx + (1 − t)y) < tg(x) + (1 − t)g(y).
Now, since f is increasing:
f(g(tx + (1 − t)y)) < f (tg(x) + (1 − t)g(y))
< t(f(g(x)) + (1 − t)f(g(y))
where we have used convexity of g in the last line.
(b) For example f(x) = 1 + x2, x > 0 and g(x) = 1/x, x > 0.
(c) From the Chain Rule it follows:
(f ◦ g(x))′′ = (f ′(g(x)g′(x))′
= f ′′(g(x))(g′(x))2 + f ′(g(x))g′′(x)

44. (*) Let f be a twice differentiable function with the following properties: f(x) > 0
for x ≥ 0, f is decreasing, and f ′(0) = 0. Prove that f ′′(x) = 0 for some x > 0.
(Before starting, make some drawings to convince yourself this is an expected result).
Hint. Choose x0 > 0 with f ′(x0) < 0. Why is there such an x0? We cannot have
f ′(x0) ≥ f ′(y) for all x0 < y . Why not? So f ′(x0) < f ′(x1) for some x0 < x 1.
Consider f ′ on [0, x1].
18
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
Solution. Choose x > 0 such that f(0) > f (x). Then, the mean value theorem
implies that f ′(x0) < 0 for some x0 ∈ (0, x). If f ′(y) ≤ f ′(x0) for all y, this will
imply that f is eventually negative, contradicting the assumption f > 0. Therefore,
there exists x1 > x0 such that f ′(x1) > f ′(x0). Since we also have f ′(0) = 0 > f ′(x0)
this implies that the minimum of f ′ on [0, x1] occurs at an interior point. At that
point we must have f ′′ = 0.
45. We saw in class that a convex function on an open interval must be continuous.
Give an example of a convex function on a closed interval which is not continuous
and explain which types of discontinuities can occur.
Hint. Can the discontinuities be interior?
Solution. Let f : [ a, b] → R be a convex function. Since f must be contin-
uous on every open interval, then it must be continuous on ( a, b). Therefore,
the only discontinuities can occur at a or b. In which case the only possibility
is that lim x→b− f(x) < f (b) or lim x→a+ f(x) < f (a). For example, the function
f : [0, 1] → R with f(x) = 0 for x ∈ [0, 1) and f(1) = 1 is convex.

50. Answer each of the following questions providing a proof of your answer in each
case:
24
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
(a) Which functions have the property that every lower sum equals every upper
sum?
(b) Which functions have the property that some upper sum equals some (other)
lower sum? (i.e. “other” meaning we use a different partition).
(c) Which continuous functions have the property that all lower sums are equal?
Hint. On each case you must consider appropriate partitions. For (a) it is enough
to consider a partition with only to points. For (b) consider the union of both
partitions and then argue as in (a). Finally, use the usual properties of a continuous
function to show that, if it is not constant, then you can find a partition P with
lower sum far from the upper sum with respect to the partition which contains only
the end points.
Solution. In what follows, let f : [a, b] → R be the bounded function considered on
each item.
(a) We can take P = {a, b}. Since inf [a,b] f × (b − a) = L(f, P ) = U(f, P ) =
sup[a,b] f × (b − a), it follows that sup[a,b] f = inf[a,b] f, which implies f must be
constant.
(b) Let P and P ′ be partitions of [ a, b] such that U(f, P ) = L(f, P ′). Denote by
P ′′ = P ∪ P ′. We saw in the lectures that L(f, P ′) ≤ L(f, P ′′) ≤ U(f, P ′′) ≤
U(f, P ). It follows that U(f, P ′′) − L(f, P ′′) = 0. If P ′′ : a = t0 < t 1 < · · · <
tn = b, the equality implies that P(sup[ti,ti+1] f − inf[ti,ti+1] f)(ti+1 − ti) = 0.
Note that all the terms are non-negative, so we conclude that sup [ti,ti+1] f =
inf[ti,ti+1] f. Therefore, f must be constant on each of the intervals [ ti, ti+1].
Moreover, f(ti) = f(ti+1), for all i, which implies f is constant.
(c) This is clearly true if f is constant. We claim that this is the only possibility.
We prove it by contradiction: assume that f is not constant. Therefore, there
exists t ∈ (a, b) be such that min [a,b] f < f (t). In fact, by continuity, there are
ε, δ > 0, such that min [a,b] f + ε < f (x) for all x ∈ [t − δ, t + δ]. Let P = {a, b}
and P ′ = {a, t − δ, t + δ, b}. It follows that L(f, P ′) − L(f, P ) ≥ 2δε > 0.
Contradicting the property.

rational number satisfying a ≤ p/q ≤ b and ε ≤ | 1/q| = |f(t)|. Note that both of
this inequalities limit the values of p and q. First, there are at most finitely many
q’s satisfying |q| ≤ 1/ε. In turn, this implies that there are at most finitely many p’s
satisfying a ≤ p/q ≤ b. In other words, A must be finite. Let 0 ≤ a1 ≤ a2 ≤ · · · ≤
an ≤ b be the elements of A. Consider a partition P = {a = t0 < t1 < · · · < t k = b}
such that each interval [ ti, ti+1] contains at most one element of A. For example, if
ti+1 − ti < δ for δ > 0 satisfying δ < mini(as+1 − as). If an interval [ ti, ti+1] contains
an element of A, it follows that the upper sum there is bounded from above by
max f × δ = δ which happens at most n times. On the rest, f is bounded from
above by ε × (b − a). Therefore,
U(f, P ) ≤ ε(b − a) + nδ.
Since we can choose ε and δ arbitrarily small independently of n and ( b − a), we
conclude that f is integrable.
55. (**) Find f and g integrable, such that f ◦ g is not integrable.
Hint. Consider the Thomae and Dirichlet functions.
Solution. Let f(x) = 0 if x = 0 and f(x) = 1 otherwise. This function is integrable
because it is continuous expect at one point. Let g(x) be Thomae’s function, we
showed in Problem 54 that g is integrable. On the other hand f ◦ g is the Dirichlet
function, i.e. f ◦ g(x) = 1 if x is rational and f ◦ g(x) = 0 if x is irrational. This
function is not integrable.

Derivatives and Integrals
57. Let f be integrable on [ a, b], let c be in [ a, b], and let
F (x) =

for a ≤ x ≤ b. For each of the following statements, give either a proof or a coun-
terexample.
(a) If f is differentiable at c, then F is differentiable at c.
(b) If f is differentiable at c, then F ′ is continuous at c.
(c) If f ′ is continuous at c, then F ′ is continuous at c.
Hint. They are all very simple if you know the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Solution.
(a) Since differentiability implies continuity, f is also continuous at c. By the FTC
this implies that F is differentiable at c and F ′ = f.
(b) Note that the statement “ F ′ is continuous at c” is implicitly assuming that F ′
exists, not only at c, but in a neighborhood of c. One quick way of constructing
f’s that are integrable but such that the corresponding F ’s have points of
discontinuity is using step functions. For example f(x) = 1

exist for points arbitrarily close to 0.
(c) The statement “ f ′ is continuous at c implicitly assumes that f ′ exists every-
where in a neighborhood of c. In particular, f is continuous on a neighborhood
of c. Moreover, by the FTC F ′(x) = f(x) for all x in that neighborhood. This
implies the claim.

58. Find all continuous functions f satisfyingZ x

Hint. Show that if f(a) ̸= 0 then f is differentiable in a neighborhood of a. Differen-

On the other hand, we want F (x) = ( f(x))2 + C, which implies that ( f(x))2 is
differentiable, but maybe f(x) is not. We can divide the possibilities into cases:
Case 1 - there exists a such that f(a) > 0: Since x 7→ √x is differentiable on (0, +∞)
and f(x) > 0 in a neighborhood of a, it follows from the chain rule that f(x) =p

which implies f ′(x) is constant on that neighborhood. Let K > 0 be this constant.

30
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
Additional Challenging Exercises
60. (*) Show that a set of pairwise disjoint open intervals contains at most countably
many intervals.
Hint. Each open interval contains some rational numbers.
Solution. Let F = {Iα}α∈Γ be a family of pairwise disjoint open intervals, i.e. Iα ∩
Iβ = ∅ if α ̸= β. We would like to show that the index set Γ is at most countable.
Since Q is dense in R, there exists a rational number, qα ∈ Iα. Consider the map
σ : Γ → Q given by σ(α) := qα. Since the intervals are pairwise disjoint it follows
that σ is injective. This implies that Γ is either finite or countable infinite.
61. (**) Show that an open set of the real line is always a union of at most countably
many open intervals.
Hint. This is perhaps the most delicate exercise on this problem sheet. Let A ⊂ R
be an open set. Show that for each a ∈ A there exists a largest open interval Ia
such that a ∈ Ia ⊂ A. Then prove that given a, b ∈ A the intervals Ia and Ib are
either identical or disjoint. Finally, observe that A = ∪a∈AIa and use the previous
exercise to conclude there are at most countably many of them.
Solution. Let A ⊂ R be an open set. We claim that given any a ∈ A there exists a
largest open interval Ia such that a ∈ Ia ⊂ A. By largest we mean the following: if
J is another open interval such that a ∈ J ⊂ A, then J ⊂ Ia. In fact, let Ia be the
union of all open intervals containing a, i.e.
Ia = ∪{I ⊂ R, I open interval and a ∈ I}.
To prove the claim we must show that Ia defined this way is itself an open interval.
First, note that if x, y ∈ Ia, then the interval [x, y] is contained inIa. This is because,
by definition of Ia, there exist open intervals a, x ∈ J1 and a, y ∈ J2. Moreover,
from a ∈ J1 ∩ J2, it follows that [ x, y] ⊂ J1 ∪ J2. In addition, J1 ∪ J2 is itself an
open interval since the union of two overlapping open intervals is an open interval.
To conclude the claim, observe that with this property Ia = (inf Ia, sup Ia), where
the infimum and supremum could be −∞ and +∞ respectively.
Now that we have established that Ia is an open interval for all a ∈ A. We observe
the following property, given any a, b ∈ A, we either have Ia = Ib or Ia ∩ Ib = ∅.
This is easy to observe. Assume that Ia ∩ Ib ̸= ∅. Then by the same reason as
above Ia ∪ Ib is an open interval containing a. Therefore, Ia ∪ Ib ⊂ Ia, which proves
Ib ⊂ Ia. Since the argument is symmetric in a and b we conclude Ia = Ib.
Finally, from the simple equality A = ∪a∈AIa and the fact that the intervals Ia are
either disjoint or identical, we conclude that A is a union of disjoint open intervals,
and by the previous exercise it contains at most countably many.
31
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
62. (*) Prove that there does not exist a continuous function f : R → R which takes on
every value exactly twice, i.e. f such that for each y ∈ R the set f −1(y) consists of
exactly two points.
Hint. Make a drawing while you read the following. Take a < b such that f(a) =
f(b) = y. Prove that y cannot be both the maximum and minimum of f in [a, b].
Prove that without loss of generality you can assume y is not the minimum. Let
s < t ∈ (a, b) be the only two points where the minimum m = f(s) = (t) is attained.
Argue that there exists an ε > 0 such that values in (m, m+ ε) are attained at least
4 times.
Solution. Let x < y be such that f(x) = f(y) = 0. Then, f(t) ̸= 0 for all t ∈ (x, y).
Since f is continuous, the Intermediate Value Theorem implies that either f(t) > 0
for all t ∈ (x, y), or f(t) < 0 for all t ∈ (x, y). Without loss of generality, we assume
from now on that f(t) > 0 for all t ∈ (x, y). By the Extreme Value Theorem
and by the hypothesis above, there are a < b ∈ (x, y) such that m = f(a) =
f(b) = min {f(t) : t ∈ [x, y]}. Fix a 0 < δ < 1
2 min{a − x, b − a, y − b}. Let
k = min{f(a−δ), f(a+δ), f(b−δ), f(b+δ)} > m then. Finally, by the Intermediate
Value Theorem f attains all the values in the interval (m, k] in the disjoint domains
[a − δ, a), (a, a + δ], [b − δ, b) and ( b, b + δ], which contradicts the hypothesis.
63. (**) Prove that a monotone function f : R → R has at most countably many
discontinuities. In other words, there exists a countable set A ⊂ R, such that f is
continuous at every x ∈ R \ A.
Hint. Let A be the set of discontinuities off, i.e. A = {a ∈ R : f is not continuous at a}.
Since f is monotone, its one-sided limits exist at every point. Prove that for a ∈ A
we must have lim x→a− f(x) < limx→a+ f(x). Then, to each a ∈ A we can associate
an open interval (lim x→a− f(x), limx→a+ f(x)).
Solution. We have seen that since f is monotone, its one-sided limits exist at every
point. In fact, we must have lim x→a− f(x) ≤ f(a) ≤ limx→a+ f(x).
Let A be the set of discontinuities of f, i.e. A = {a ∈ R : f is not continuous at a}.
Since f is not continuous at a ∈ A, for those points we must have lim x→a− f(x) <
limx→a+ f(x). In addition, note that if a ̸= b ∈ A the intervals (limx→a− f(x), limx→a+ f(x))
and (limx→b− f(x), limx→b+ f(x)) must be disjoint. Therefore, the function σ : A →
Q given by σ(a) = qa, where qa is a rational number in (limx→a− f(x), limx→a+ f(x))
is injective.
64. (*) We say that a set K ⊂ R has the finite covering property if any family of
open intervals {Iλ}λ∈Λ that covers K, i.e. K ⊂ ∪ λ∈ΛIλ contains a finite subfamily
{I1, · · · , IN } ⊂ { Iλ}λ∈Λ that still covers K, i.e. K ⊂ I1 ∪ · · · ∪ IN.
Show that a closed interval [ a, b] ⊂ R has the finite covering property.
32
Analysis 1 - Revision Exercises ( HINTS and SOLUTIONS )
Hint. Argue by contradiction. Fix a cover {Iλ} for which the property does not
hold. Split [ a, b] by its middle point m = a+b
2 into two closed intervals [ a, m] and
[m, b]. Observe that for at least one of them the finite covering property does not
hold with respect to the same cover. Repeat this process to construct a nested
family of closed intervals [ ak, bk], whose extremes ak and bk converge to a point
x ∈ [a, b]. Obtain a contradiction in the following way: first, note that there is a
single open set I in the cover that contains x. Then, show that I must eventually
contain [ak, bk].
Solution. We argue by contradiction. Assume there is a cover F = {Iλ} such that
[a, b] does not have the finite cover property with respect to F.
Let [a0, b0] = [ a, b]. Given [ an, bn] define [ an+1, bn+1] = [ an, mn] if [ an, mn] does not
have the finite cover property with respect to F. Otherwise, we define [an+1, bn+1] =
[mn, bn]. By construction, at each step [an, bn] does not have the finite cover property
with respect to F. On the other hand, the constructed intervals form a nested family
of closed intervals, i.e.
[a0, b0] ⊃ [a1, b1] ⊃ · · · ⊃ [an, bn] ⊃ · · · .
We have seen that under these conditions the set X = ∩n∈N[an, bn] is not empty.
Let x ∈ X. Let I ∈ F such that x ∈ I (such an I exists because F is a covering).
Since an ≤ x ≤ bn and |bn − an| → 0, it follows that [ an, bn] ⊂ I for n large enough.
This contradict the assumption that [ an, bn] does not admit a finite subcover from
F.
65. (*) The Heine-Borel Theorem: A set K ⊂ R is compact (closed and bounded) if and
only if it has the finite covering property.
Hint. Note that K is inside of some closed and bounded interval. Start with a cover
of K, expand it to a cover of [ a, b] by adding just one more open set, and then use
the previous problem.
Solution. Since K is bounded, there exists a < b such that K ⊂ [a, b]. Let F = {Iλ}
be an open cover of X. Since the set K C = R \ K is open, the union F ∪ {K C} is an
open cover of [a, b]. Therefore, there exists a finite subset {I1, · · · , In, KC} that still
cover [a, b]. Since K C does not cover K ⊂ [a, b], it follows that K ⊂ I1 ∪ · · · ∪ In,
which is what we wanted to show.
33

We prove thatRis non-decreasing on (a,∞); the case (−∞, a) is analogous. Thus,

inversef −1 :J→Iis differentiable atf(a).

1: module Helpers where
2:
3:
import Data.Containers.ListUtils (nubOrd)
4: import Data.Set (Set)
5: import Data.Set qualified as Set
6: import Control.Monad (guard)
7:
8: mhead 
:: [a] -> Maybe a
9: mhead [] = Nothing
10: mhead (x:_) = Just x
11:
12: bundle 
:: Int -> [a] -> [[a]]
13: -- Pre: n > 0
14: bundle n xs
15:     | null rems         = [bund]
16:     | length rems <= n  = [bund, rems]
17:     | otherwise = bund : bundle n rems
18:     
where
19:         (bund, rems) = splitAt n xs
20:
21: unique 
:: Ord a => [a] -> Bool
22: unique xs = length xs == length (nubOrd xs)
23:
24: uncurry3 
:: (a -> b -> c -> d) -> ((a, b, c) -> d)
25: uncurry3 f (x, y, z) = f x y z
26:
27: getSingle 
:: Set a -> Maybe a
28: getSingle s = do guard (Set.size s == 1)
29:                  Set.lookupMin s

31: (Split n t1 t2 t3) ! i
32:   | i < interval  = t1 ! i
33:   | i < 2 * interval = t2 ! (i - interval)
34:   | otherwise = t3 ! (i - 2 * interval)
35:

44:   | i < 2 * interval = Split n t1 (update (i - interval) x t2) t3
45:   | otherwise = Split n t1 t2 (update (i - 2 * interval) x t3)
46:   
where

75:   pretty end bars (Leaf x)           = showsBars (node end) bars
76:                                      . shows x
77:                                      . showString 
"\n"
78:   pretty end bars (Split _ t1 t2 t3) = showsBars (node end) bars
79:                                      . showString "â\224\220\n"
80:                                      . pretty False (withBar bars) t1
81:                                      . pretty False (withBar bars) t2
82:                                      . pretty True (withEmpty bars) t3

"â\224\224â\224\200â\224\200"

:: String -> [String] -> ShowS
88:   showsBars node [] = id
89:   showsBars node (_ : tl) =

91:

:: [String] -> [String]
93:   withBar bars   = "â\224\202  ":bars
94:   withEmpty bars = "   ":bars

66: allRowsAndCols :: [(Row, Col)]
67: allRowsAndCols = [(Row r, Col c) | r <- [0..gridSize-1], c <- [0..gridSize-1]]
68:
69:
{-|
70: Converts a row and a column from the 2D grid into
71: a 1D index.
72: -}
73: toIndex :: Row -> Col -> Int
74: toIndex (Row i) (Col j) = i * gridSize + j
75:
76:
{-|
77: Finds the numbered box that a given cell occupies.
78: -}
79: boxOf :: Row -> Col -> Box
80: boxOf (Row r) (Col c) =
81:   Box ((r ‘div‘ boxSize) * boxGridSize + (c ‘div‘ boxSize))
82:
83:
{-|
84: Returns the indices of all cells in a given row.
85: -}
86: rowIndices :: Row -> [Int]
87: rowIndices (Row i) = [i*gridSize..i*gridSize+gridSize-1]
88:
89:
{-|
90: Returns the indices of all cells in a given column.
91: -}
92: colIndices :: Col -> [Int]
93: colIndices (Col j) = [j,j+gridSize..j+gridSize*(gridSize-1)]
94:
95:
{-|
96: Returns the indices of all cells in a given box.
97: -}
98: boxIndices :: Box -> [Int]
99: boxIndices (Box b) = [topLeft + i*gridSize + j | i <- [0..boxSize-1]
100:                                                , j <- [0..boxSize-1]]
101:   
where (br, bc) = b ‘divMod‘ boxGridSize
102:         topLeft = br*gridSize*boxSize + bc*boxSize
